A theologian they call ish ha-elohi [Hebrew], "a man of God." Theology, moreover, into two parts

the most learned of their theologians, Maimonides, divides it (in the second preface to the More Nebuchim [Guide of the Perplexed], and in the preface to the third part of the same work). For it is either ma'aseh bereshith, the "work of creation," that is, natural theology; or ma'aseh merkabah, the "work of the chariot," that is, mystical theology — the name taken from Ezekiel 1. The former, he teaches, is to be expounded commonly [to all], the latter to few. For the rest, in the Old Testament there is no word that can be rendered by "theology," nor any other term "analogous" [Greek: analogon] to it. Commonly, "theologos" [Greek] among Greek writers is one who discourses about God; whence "the Delphic theologians" [Greek: hoi Delphōn theologoi] in Plutarch, On the Cessation of Oracles [Greek: peri tōn ekleloipotōn chrēstēriōn]. "Theologoumena" [Greek] are "things disputed about God." Suetonius, in his Augustus, mentions the books of "theologoumena" [Greek] of Asclepiades of Mendes — in which he both brought to light a foul atheism, and an "unlawful coupling" [Greek: athemiton mixin; the Egyptian practice at Mendes with the worshiped he-goat]. Nicomachus of Gerasa also wrote two books of "arithmetical theologoumena" [Greek: arithmētikōn theologoumenōn]; "a work far removed from its title" [Greek: ergon porrō tēs epigraphēs dierrimmenon], says Photius (Bibliotheca, Codex 187) — inept, that is. But "theologia" [Greek], according to the usage of the Greek tongue, signifies rather "discourse about God" than "the discourse [word] of God"; and so Augustine expounds that word (On the City of God, Book 8, ch. 1): "It is," he says, "speech, or reasoning, about God." Yet there are not lacking examples of the other signification. "Theopropion" [Greek] is "an oracle of God," that which God utters; and "theosēmeia" [Greek] is "a sign which God displays." The most ancient Greek writers, who treated of the gods and their worship, were first called "theologoi," and their science "theologia." Lactantius (On the Anger of God, ch. 2) [says] these were the poets, and they accordingly were commonly distinguished by that name. [So also] Aristotle, Metaphysics 3; Clement of Alexandria, Stromata 2; Isidore, Book 5. Pherecydes the Syrian is said to have been the first among the nations to treat "theological" matters [Greek: theologika] in continuous prose; and the "symbolic" [Greek: symbolikēn] and cryptic, or "enigmatic" [Greek: ainigmatikēn], manner which the Pythagoreans afterward used, they affirm to have been that man's teaching. He himself was called the chief teacher of Pythagoras, and a "theologian." That Heraclitus wrote such "theological" works [Greek: theologika] is testified by Diogenes and Hesychius Illustrius, whence he is called "the obscure" [Greek: skoteinos]. Now this Pherecydes lived in the age of Cyrus — so utterly were the Greeks infants in all science, if they be compared with the Orientals. This was cast in Solon's teeth by a priest of Saïs: "O Solon, Solon, you Greeks are always children, and an old Greek there is none" [Greek: Ō Solōn, Solōn, Hellēnes aei paides este, gerōn de Hellēn ouk estin]. And when Solon, hearing this, asked "what do you mean by this?" [Greek], that old man, [Solon] said, answered: "You are young in your souls; for you hold in them no opinion old through ancient hearing [Greek: di' archaian akoēn palaian doxan], nor any learning hoary with time" [Greek: oude mathēma chronō polion ouden], Plato in the [Timaeus]. For that theology obtained its splendor — nay reached its "peak" [Greek: akmēn] — long before the times of Cyrus, is well known. See Pliny, Natural History, Book 7, ch. 1, "what things a man may discover in life." "Theopompus says this man was the first to write, for the Greeks, about nature and the gods" [Greek: Touton phēsi Theopompos prōton peri physeōs kai theōn Hellēsi grapsai]

— "Theopompus asserts that he [Pherecydes] was the first of all to write about nature and the gods," Diogenes [Laertius] in his life. Which also appears from the letter of Thales to him, of which this is the beginning: "I learn that you are about to be the first of the Ionians to publish to the Greeks discourses concerning divine matters" [Greek: pynthanomai se prōton Iōnōn mellein logous amphi tōn theiōn chrēmatōn eis tous Hellēnas phainein]. Hesychius Illustrius thinks that Pherecydes the Syrian was named from the island of Syros: "He was," he says, "[of] one of the Cyclades islands, Syra [Greek: mia tōn Kykladōn nēsōn, hē Syra], the country," in his life of him. Others will have it that he was a Syrian, which is more probable. He himself, in the proem of his work, affirmed that Musaeus, son of Eumolpus, "first composed a theogony [a generation of the gods]" [Greek: prōton theogonian poiēsai]. Him the Platonists emphatically called "the theologian" [Greek: ton theologon]; and he was always held the most excellent theologian. But concerning Homer, Philostratus (in the Heroica): "He surpassed Orpheus in many things pertaining to theology" [Greek: Orphea en pollois tōn kata tēn theologian hyperēre] — and added to [others'] inventions. Now their "theologia" was an exposition of the "theogonia" [Greek; the generation of the gods]; for they supposed all the gods to have been begotten. So Herodotus, of the Persians, in [Book 1,] Clio: "They did not consider the gods to be of human form [Greek: ouk anthrōpophyeas enomisan tous theous], as the Greeks do"; for the Persians worshiped natural gods. I speak of the Greeks after the introduction of Hellenism; for that the ancient Greeks, after the manner of the other nations, first worshiped heaven and the stars, Eusebius proves (Preparation for the Gospel, Book 1, ch. 9): "There was not, then" [Greek: ouk ara], "among the most ancient of men any account of a Greek or barbarian theogony, nor erection of carved images [Greek: oude xoanōn hidrysis], nor the now-abundant nonsense [Greek: hē nyn pollē phlyaria] of naming the gods male and female." And long before him, Plato in the Cratylus: "The first of the men about Greece seem to me to have held these only to be gods, as now many of the barbarians do — sun and moon and earth and stars and heaven" [Greek: hēlion kai selēnēn kai gēn kai astra kai ouranon]. But of these things more later. At what time this word [theology] was given citizenship in the Christian state is uncertain. In the title of the Apocalypse, John is called "the theologian" [Greek: ho theologos]. In the royal copy of Montanus it stands, "The Revelation of the holy apostle and evangelist John the theologian" [Greek: Apokalypsis tou hagiou apostolou kai euangelistou Iōannou tou theologou]. In the remaining copies it is so called absolutely. There is added, in the Ethiopic version, "which John was bishop of the metropolis of Constantinople when he was about to suffer persecution" — stupidly enough, and as became that translator. For the rest, since that inscription [Greek: epigraphē] is of most uncertain origin, and rightly "set aside" [Greek: oichetai; rejected], it may seem to be taken from a verse of the second chapter: "John witnessed the word of God" [Greek: emartyrēse ton logon tou Theou] — that is, [witnessed] the word of God, but not [a discourse] about God. But the other signification of the word is the more usual, as we have shown. Nor was it because he testified "the word of God" [Greek: logon tou Theou], but because he most clearly expounded "the Word [who is] God" [Greek: logon ton Theon], that the ancients called John "preeminently" [Greek: kat' exochēn] the theologian. The first who did this is Origen. Dionysius the Areopagite mentions "theologians," and [speaks] of theologians, so familiarly (ch. 3 of On the Divine Names) as if those "technical names" [Greek: onomata technika] had been in most frequent use in his time. But that that writer is "spurious" [Greek: hypobolimaion; a later forgery] almost all agree. And so these words, as far as concerns sacred Scripture, are "unwritten" [Greek: agrapha]. The evangelical doctrine, indeed, is called "the word of life" [Greek: logos zōēs], Phil. 2:16; "the word of the cross" [Greek: ho logos ho tou staurou], 1 Cor. 1:18; "the word of faith" [Greek: logos tēs pisteōs], 1 Tim. 4:6; "the word of Christ" [Greek: ho logos tou Christou], Col. 3:16; "the word of God" [Greek: logos Theou], 1 Thess. 2:13; with respect had to these, and

other such [expressions], we do not reject a sober use of words of this kind — although we are not unaware that their "technical" [Greek: technikon] sense cannot be retained without prejudice to truth. Let us therefore call that matter of which we are speaking, with Eusebius (in his letter to Marcellus), "ecclesiastical theology" [Greek: theologian ekklēsiastikēn].

Argument: The abstract notion of theology — In what sense the scholastics contend that it is a science — Their opinion rejected — That theology is alien to all human science the apostle proves, 1 Cor. 2 — Who is the Pauline theologian — The vain labor of very many in framing theology to the rules of the human arts and sciences — The ineptitudes and want of learning of the scholastics.

I. There is no reason why the things debated among very many about the "abstract" and "artificial" notion of theology (as they speak) should detain us, since they do not pertain to the exposition of its nature as we shall view it. For whether it be an art or a science; and if it is to be called a science, whether theoretical or practical, or rather mixed of both; what its proper and immediate object is, what its genus, its differentia, its end — and very many other utterly futile matters — theologians of every sort, mixing the philosophy of Aristotle's school with Christian simplicity, have disputed variously and subtly: so understanding the matter that they understand nothing. For since Aristotle made mention of "theology as a science" [Greek: theologikēs epistēmēs], and taught it to be the most excellent of "the theoretical sciences" [Greek: tōn theōrētikōn epistēmōn], the scholastics (those especially whose Thomas is next to God), for whom to dissent from him was a scruple of conscience, contended that theology is a Christian science, and that a speculative one. That the name "art" ought to be banished from this business is at last the consensus of all. Whether the definitions of "science" — by which what it is, is declared, differently by these men and by those, according to each one's pleasure — have anything in common with theology, will be seen. Since, then, the things themselves (namely, the arts and sciences) and their descriptions and definitions are uncertain and arbitrary, fabricated in diverse ways according to the diverse capacity of certain men; and since there is no habit of mind, whether theoretical or practical, that is the "imprint" [Greek: ektypōma] of any art (since all science, as Plato witnesses, Epinomis, is an "imitator" [Greek: mimētikē] of the things themselves for the most part) or of any science, which would exhibit the thing of which we treat — all labor and skill in reducing these so diverse things to order will be in vain. For the rest, in the sciences properly so called [Greek: technikōs], which regard conclusions [drawn] from better-known principles and take their name from their proper objects, attention is paid either to the doctrine itself (by which the things they treat of are skillfully arranged), or to the faculty or habit of mind by which we comprehend that doctrine. But of a doctrine which always rests on certain foundations and theorems that are not only consonant to human reason but akin to it — such as is all [human] science — since

with a mystery transcending every purely "natural" [Greek: psychikon] capacity there can be no fellowship — for that "God cannot rightly be known except through God" is the "first apprehension" [Greek: prolēpsis] of all. The "scientific" habit, as they call it, is the conformity of the mind to a doctrine of that [human] kind, born of the disciplinary action that flows back from kindred principles; so that it cannot express that divine power of the mind which is theology, and which rests on the truthfulness of the One who reveals. But the object of theology, since in some sense it is God himself, is infinitely more distant from the objects of all the sciences than those objects are from very nothing — so far is it from being the case that, by any account, in respect of that object, theology would agree with the other arts or sciences.

II. The apostle also, most plainly, in the passage where he treats of these matters on set purpose and expressly, renders the account of Christian theology as alien to all human wisdom and science: "My speech" [Greek: ho logos mou], he says, "and my preaching" [Greek: kai to kērygma mou] — that is, both the science and the doctrine — "were not in" (whether "in me" or "in your perception") "persuasive words of human wisdom" [Greek: ouk en peithois (peistois or pithanois) anthrōpinēs sophias logois] (in which consists all human art or science commonly so called), "but in demonstration of the Spirit and of power" [Greek: allʼ en apodeixei Pneumatos kai dynameōs] ("one through two" [Greek: hen dia dysin; a hendiadys]) — for as the mathematicians have their "demonstrations" [Greek: apodeixeis], and the dialecticians theirs, so this our doctrine also consists in "demonstration" [Greek: en apodeixei], but a spiritual and powerful one, which is as far from that "human wisdom" [Greek: anthrōpinēs sophias] as "heaven is from earth" [Greek: hoson ouranos estʼ apo gaias]. (Then he adds the reason and end of this distinction:) "That" [Greek: hina], he says, "your faith" [Greek: hē pistis hymōn] (that is, the assent given to our doctrine) "should not be in the wisdom of men" [Greek: mē ē en sophia anthrōpōn] (as it would be if theology consisted in the same or like principles, form, or end, just as the arts and sciences of the philosophers), "but in the power of God" [Greek: allʼ en dynamei Theou] (who works this wisdom efficaciously through his Spirit). "But we speak wisdom among the perfect" [Greek: sophian de laloumen en tois teleiois]; "yet a wisdom not of this world" [Greek: sophian de ou tou aiōnos toutou] (not the wisdom that proceeded from human learning); "but we speak the wisdom of God in a mystery, even the hidden wisdom" [Greek: alla laloumen sophian Theou en mystēriō apokekrymmenēn] — (but what fellowship of "the wisdom of God" can there be, "in a mystery" [Greek: mystēriōdei], with any human disciplines?) — "which God has revealed to us by his Spirit" [Greek: hēmin ho Theos apekalypse dia tou Pneumatos autou] (these things human wisdom teaches otherwise). — 1 Cor. 2:4, 5, 6, 7, 10. Whether, therefore, you regard the origin of theology, or its subject, or its end, or the mode of learning and teaching it — in short, its whole nature or use — it appears that it can in no way be so numbered among the human sciences, whether speculative or practical, that it ought to be bound to their rules or methods. Nay, the very words "method," "methodizing," "art" [Greek: methodos, methodeuein, technē], which are "proper" [Greek: oikeia] to the sciences, have here, according to Scripture, no place; "mystery" [Greek: mystērion] — and thence "the initiated" [Greek: from myeō], concerning which the apostle treats in a most holy sense — denote, even among the heathen themselves, something constantly quite alien from all the other sciences: it is a mystery. "Myeō" [Greek] is "I initiate"; "myoumai" passively. A little differently, but to the same sense, the author of the Etymologicum Magnum: "Mystēs," he says, "[is so called] from myō, to close [the eyes]; for closing the senses [Greek: myontes gar tas aisthēseis] and getting outside fleshly cares [Greek: exō tōn sarkikōn phrontidōn ginomenoi], they thus received the divine illuminations" [Greek: houtō tas theias analampseis edechonto]. And that "the mysteries [Greek: ta mystēria] are derived from the mice [Greek: apo tōn myōn]," Iamblichus (in Photius, Codex 94) says — but ineptly, as the etymological reasoning he brings forward shows.

III. Of Pythagoras, instructed in the wisdom of almost all nations, Laertius affirms that "he was initiated into all the rites both of the Greeks and of the barbarians" [Greek: pasas emyēthē tas te Hellēnikas kai barbarikas teletas]. For that wisdom which those ancient philosophers went to seek by their travels was nothing else than the method of religious worship, as Lactantius witnesses: "I am wont indeed," he says, "to wonder that, when Pythagoras and afterward Plato, from love of investigating the truth, had penetrated even to the Egyptians, the Magi, and the Persians, to learn the rites and sacred things of those nations (for they suspected that wisdom was conversant in religion), they nevertheless did not approach the Jews," Book 4, On True Wisdom. Hence, when Apollonius wished "to be initiated" [Greek: myeisthai] at Athens, "the hierophant was unwilling to grant the sacred rites, saying that he would never initiate a sorcerer, nor open Eleusis to a man not pure from demons" [Greek: ho hierophantēs ouk eboulato parechein ta hiera; mē gar an pote myēsai goēta, mēde tēn Eleusina anoixai anthrōpō mē katharō ta daimonia], Philostratus, Life of Apollonius, Book 4, ch. 6. Therefore "to be initiated" [Greek: myeisthai] is to be instructed in sacred things — which appears yet more from the answer of Apollonius: "Concerning the rite of initiation" [Greek: peri tēs teletēs], he said, "I know more than you" [Greek: pleion ē sy gignōskō]. And among the Fathers, as Budaeus witnesses, "one initiated" [Greek: memyēmenos], by itself and without addition, signifies one who is learned in the sacred letters. "Mystery" [Greek: mystērion] in Paul, says Chrysostom, [means] "that which is secret, and wonderful, and unknown" [Greek: to aporrēton, kai thaumaston, kai agnooumenon] — that is, naturally; others [say] "things hidden and known to few, and not to be communicated except to the initiated" — that is, "we speak wisdom among the perfect" [Greek: sophian laloumen en tois teleiois].

IV. Not as if among us the sacred things were secrets which it would be lawful to expound only under some oath and silence first imposed — since Christ took care that all things even which, by the measure of his ministry, he taught "most mysteriously" [Greek: mystēriotata] in private, he "revealed" to be openly published "in the light" [Greek: en tō phōti] and "upon the housetops" [Greek: kai epi tōn dōmatōn], Matt. 10:27.

V. To divulge the mysteries to the common people was indeed an impiety among the ancient Gentiles: "They think it not lawful to commit to writing the things which they teach about sacred matters," [Caesar] of the Druids, Book 6, On the Gallic War. Therefore everywhere those to be initiated were bound by oath not to blab anything out. Whence Herodotus, of himself, after he had learned the Egyptian sacred things from the men of Heliopolis: "But the divine things of the narratives [Greek: ta men nyn theia tōn apēgēmatōn], such as I heard [Greek: hoia ēkouon], I am not eager to set forth [Greek: ouk eimi prothymos exēgeesthai], beyond their names only [Greek: exō ē ta ounomata auteōn mounon], thinking that all men know equally about them" [Greek: nomizōn pantas anthrōpous ison peri auteōn epistasthai], Book 2 — that is, the initiated. Not, therefore, for a reason of this kind is the evangelical doctrine marked with the name of "mystery," but because the things themselves revealed in it exceed every purely natural human capacity, 1 Cor. 2:7, 14.

VI. In order, therefore, that anyone may receive and understand "the wisdom hidden in a mystery" [Greek: sophian en mystēriō apokekrymmenēn], it is necessary that he himself be "initiated" — that is, instructed in the secrets of the divine will by the Holy Spirit. Nor are any others "the perfect" [Greek: teleioi] in Paul than the initiated — that is, those instructed by the Holy Spirit, of whom he treats;

for "teleioō" [Greek] is "I consecrate" and "I initiate," whence among the ancients baptism also is called "an initiation" [Greek: teleiōtēs]. For consecration or dedication the Septuagint uses it (Exod. 29): "You shall consecrate their hands" [Greek: teleiōsai tas cheiras autōn], verses 33 and 35; nor does that word seem to be expounded otherwise in the Epistle to the Hebrews 2:10, "to make the captain of their salvation perfect through sufferings" [Greek: ton archēgon tēs sōtērias dia pathēmatōn teleiōsai]. And likewise "telē" [Greek] are mysteries, as Budaeus proves from Plato; and "teletai," sacred rites; and "tetelesmenoi," the initiated [and those expiated by sacrifices], in Philostratus, Life of Apollonius, Book 4, ch. 6. He, therefore, who is "perfect" [Greek: teleios], or instructed by the Spirit by which we are initiated into the Christian sacred things, and "initiated" [Greek: memyēmenos] by him who alone lays open "to us the mystery of his will" [Greek: hēmin to mystērion tou thelēmatos autou], and that through "the Spirit of wisdom and revelation" [Greek: pneuma sophias kai apokalypseōs], Eph. 1:9, 17 — he is the Pauline theologian. Of the use of the arts and sciences in learning divine truth it will be spoken later. Meanwhile, that theology is a certain "mystical" [Greek: mystēriōdē] and divine wisdom, and is not circumscribed by those terms or delivered by those rules whose barriers all the arts and sciences endure, did not escape the heathen themselves. And perhaps for no other reason has it come about that those who have spent much labor on this matter — namely, to describe theology "exactly" [Greek: akribōs] according to the rules and methods of the other sciences — are, concerning its definition, genus, differentiae, proper object, subject, immediate end, and very many other things of the kind (about which sacred Scripture [says] not even a "grunt" [Greek: gry; not even a jot]), most remotely sundered in their opinions, and wondrously fight among themselves. For while they study to render the mystery of divine truth, revealed from the bosom of God, hewn down to the flexible and arbitrary rules of the human arts and sciences, and hindered by who-knows-what philosophical terms (which, and by whose aid and help, all truth, both natural and supernatural, could most gladly have done without, had it not seemed otherwise to Aristotle) — terms with which it [the mystery] would have no community — they effect nothing else than that a thing in itself and by its nature most certain, most evident, and most open to the rational mind, should become most uncertain, ambiguous, manifold, and obscure. Learned men indeed, relying — in place of innate principles — on the divine revelation in sacred Scripture, and by a progress not unlike that of the other sciences (some discovering somewhat, others with easier labor adding a doctrine to their discoveries), have built up a certain doctrine, comprehended after the manner of the other sciences in principles, theorems, theses, and conclusions, which they call theology. But that doctrine — since as such it has no "self-evidence" [Greek: autopistian], nor needs supernatural faith by which it should be received, nor attains any end of theology, whether theoretical or practical — is not theology properly so called. From the investigation of this doctrine there is acquired a certain habit of mind, consonant with the impression received from it, which the scholastics contend is neither faith nor natural science. And this is the theology of the schools, of whose nature and use it will be spoken below. Let us therefore dismiss the innumerable, laborious, and (if the truth may be told) not-worth-a-farthing disputations, if only we are willing to allow theology
to enjoy the right of liberty with which the liberal hand of sacred Scripture has claimed it.

VII. By whatever words it has pleased the Holy Spirit to use for declaring this matter, the scholastics have rendered each and all of them, by their disputations, vexed more than enough. That Scripture calls "science," "prudence," "wisdom," "doctrine," that knowledge of himself with which God wills us to be imbued — directing men in the contemplation of God and his works, and in the obedience to be rendered to him — is acknowledged. Hence, carried away (as has been said) by the itch of disputing, they pertinaciously contend whether theology is a science, and that speculative or practical, or prudence, or wisdom. And whatever word it has seemed good to subject to examination, weighing all its philosophical aspects, they subtly inquire whether it can be fitted to theology. Those who will have theology to be wisdom (that from this one head all the labor of these men may be estimated) think this task is given them: to fish out all the things that, in the writings of the philosophers, are attributed to human wisdom, and to show that those fit theology aptly. But this work, whatever it be, when they suppose they have accomplished it through long circuits, subtle fictions, and most audaciously inept conclusions, then — as though the business were well done, and the opinions of all others routed (especially of those who lead or follow another school) — they conclude that theology is wisdom. And so, since they are utterly ignorant of the mind of the Holy Spirit in those words "science," "prudence," "wisdom," "doctrine," and the rest of the kind, they bend all the sinews of their wit (being, as most of them are, most ingeniously stupid) and their eyes, to change the wisdom of God hidden in a mystery into that wretched, imperfect, nay foolish wisdom of this world, and to circumscribe it within who-knows-what limits — than which figment nothing more dangerous, or more diametrically fighting against apostolic doctrine, could ever have been devised. Those thorny contentions, therefore, of the scholastics and others, which they build up in front of their theological treatises, fixing a torment upon their wretched readers — the candidates of sacred wisdom ought to despise them; for they would not wish to spend their study unprofitably, nay to err, when the very truth that is to be tracked out from them by infinite labors is, on account of the "alien kind" [Greek: allogenian] of the proposed subject, not even true [to the matter].

Argument: Between the intellect and truth there is a most close union — Theology denotes both a habit of mind and truth itself — The knowledge of God cannot be called the "archetype" [Greek: archetypos] of ours — Theology, as doctrine, is from God alone and immediately — In what sense the Word of God is our theology — The distributions of "theologians": angels, men — Men are either the God-man [Greek: theanthrōpos] or "mere men" [Greek: psiloi anthrōpoi] — Mere men are either wayfarers or possessors — Their theology to be investigated.

I. Dismissing those things which, although among some they fill both pages [occupy the whole discussion] in the inquiry into the nature of theology, in reality throw a hindrance in the way of those sincerely investigating the truth, let us look more closely at the general matter itself, as concerns its nature, on which we are bestowing this labor.

But since between any known thing whatever and that faculty and "power" [Greek: dynamis] of our mind by which we apprehend it — that is, the intellect itself, considered with its "disposition" [Greek: schesis] toward the thing — there is a close union and bond, arising from the intervening conformity of truth, such that the one communicates its names mutually with the other, so the affections which are proper to the one are often ascribed to the other. For often a thing, or doctrine, is called "certain," from that certitude which is an affection of the mind; and our apprehension is called "true," from the truth of the thing which we apprehend. Hence in definitions there arises no small confusion, while it cannot easily be discerned whether it is the doctrine, or the faculty of the mind (by which we apprehend the doctrine itself, by its own proper light, toward its own proper end), that is being defined. Since, therefore, it is acknowledged that by "theology" is denoted both the doctrine about God, his worship, and the obedience due to him, and the power and faculty of our mind by which we perceive it (the habit conformed to it) — to avoid the confusion arising from this "ambiguity" [Greek: homōnymia], they must be treated distinctly.

II. It pertains to the infinite "self-sufficiency" [Greek: autarkeian] of God that he alone perfectly knows himself: Ps. 147:5, "of his understanding there is no number." Therefore that knowledge by which God most perfectly knows himself and all his attributes, since it is infinite and necessary, is none other than God himself, infinitely knowing and wise. This knowledge, then — of the first Truth most perfectly comprehending and loving itself — is only improperly called by some the "archetypal" [Greek: archetypos] theology. But what they would have understood by "archetypal" [Greek: archetypon] theology, whether they themselves understand, I vehemently doubt. For neither is our knowledge of God the express character of that infinite divine knowledge (which is proper to the Son alone, partaker of the divine nature, Heb. 1:3), so that it could be called its "imprint" [Greek: ektypos] in respect of it; nor can we know anything of God by its force or power, except by the intervening free counsel of the divine will. Indeed, our mirror is not immediately God himself, but his word, or gospel, in which, "with unveiled face," through Christ "we behold the glory of the Lord," 2 Cor. 3:18. God has in his mind an eternal "idea" or conception of his truth, which he wills to be known by us. And on this all our theology depends — not immediately indeed, but on that act of the divine will by which it pleased him to reveal that truth to us: "For no man has seen God at any time; the only-begotten Son, who is in the bosom of the Father, he has declared him," John 1:18. The revelation, therefore, of the divine mind and will — that is, the Word of God — is that doctrine of which we treat, to which all the conceptions of our mind about God, his worship, and the obedience due to him ought to be conformed. And here again theology agrees little with all science that is the offshoot of human wisdom. For that "system" [Greek: systēma] of principles and conclusions by which any science is delivered is not the norm of the mind's conceptions, or of the acts

of the intellect about the proper object of that science; but, since it is posterior to them and their effect, it ought to be regulated by them. So the most excellent philosopher [Plotinus]: "The sciences in the rational soul [Greek: hai de epistēmai en psychē logikē ousai] — those of sensible things [Greek: hai men tōn aisthētōn] (if one ought to call the knowledge of these 'sciences' [Greek: ei dei epistēmas toutōn legein], whereas the name 'opinion' rather befits them [Greek: prepei de autais to tēs doxēs onoma]) — being posterior to the things [Greek: hysterai tōn pragmatōn ousai], are images of them" [Greek: eikones eisi toutōn], Plotinus, Ennead 5, Book 9. But the doctrine of which we treat outgoes [precedes] every conception of our mind about its peculiar and proper object, and is the infallible rule and immutable norm of all our science and knowledge, Ps. 19.

III. Theology, therefore, is the very divine truth itself, as it is revealed by God; namely, the Word, the doctrine, or light, "trustworthy and self-authenticating" [Greek: axiopiston and autopiston]. No one can worthily think or speak of God or things divine except through God. Nor does anyone rightly know God except from his own exposition through the Son; nor does he will to be worshiped except as he himself has prescribed. All our obedience also depends purely on his will. But all theology is conversant about God, his worship, and the obedience due to him. Not, therefore, except from God himself can that doctrine be. Since, then, theology is the principle and norm of all divine worship, of faith, and of obedience, it must accordingly be also of heavenly origin and of infallible certitude, "from heaven, not from men" [Greek: ex ouranou, not ex anthrōpōn], Matt. 21:26. For divine faith would not rest on any principle that is not in every way divine, "self-sufficient and trustworthy" [Greek: autarkes and axiopiston]. But the worship of God is so called no less from the command than from the divine object. Therefore, lest the whole human race should perpetually fluctuate in all its obedience, and fall into rough places, God willed that doctrine which is the norm of all his worship and of our obedience to be from himself. In this sense, then, theology is the doctrine of God concerning himself, his works, his will and worship, our obedience in every state, and the reward of the obedient and the punishment of the disobedient — revealed and expressed to the glory of his name; that is, the very Word of God itself. But since the Word of God is either "inward" or "uttered" [Greek: endiatheton or prophorikon], and this again either "unwritten" or "written" [Greek: agraphon or engraphon], how each of these stands in this business we shall expound severally hereafter. But what is to be thought of the "theological theses" (as they call them), catechisms, confessions, commonplaces, and farragoes of credible propositions of that kind — and in what place they are to be held, in the learning or even the teaching of which (unless I am mistaken) many suppose theology to consist — will be seen below.

IV. After this, then (to add here this "by way of anticipation" [Greek: kata prolēpsin]): since every word of God whatsoever that is committed to writing — that Scripture is our theology in such a way that we attribute authority to it as a whole, and to every part of it, and to each truth contained in it, not because one proposition

is built up from another by evident reasons adduced, or because the conclusions to which we assent are rightly gathered from any principles whatever, but [because] we embrace each truth, or proposition of truth, considered separately, with a certain faith, on account of its immediate divine revelation, on which all theology rests. But that habit which the scholastics call "discursive" — by which, from propositions (one of which is Scripture's, and therefore divinely revealed, the other of which shines by its own natural evidence), we elicit conclusions (about whose nature, namely whether they are "of faith," as they speak, or not, they keenly fight) — inasmuch as it is one thing (if indeed it be another) from the gift of interpreting the Scriptures, is alien to our design.

V. This being premised, then — namely, that theology, considered "objectively" (as they speak) and "in the abstract," is the pure, sheer Word of God, that light of the mind of the rational creature which is also called by that name — it now comes to be accurately weighed further. But that we may more easily arrive where we are tending, some distribution of the subjects capable of this light must be premised, and then the relation of that light to the doctrine of which we have spoken, and its union with it, must be explored. In the first place, then, in respect of its subject (as they speak), theology seems able to be commonly divided into that which is of the angels, and the human. For to angels and to men, because they are endowed with intellectual light, it is incumbent to know God and to obey God freely; and their ultimate end consists in that enjoyment of God to which the way is through obedience. But this "heavenly" [Greek: ouranidēs] doctrine requires a nature, state, and condition of that kind in all those who would perceive it, or use it to its proper end. But what sort of innate knowledge of God and of his will the angels had — "keeping their own beginning [first estate] and their proper habitation" [Greek: tēn heautōn archēn kai to idion oikētērion; Jude 6] — and with what light they were instructed, so that, in respect of increases, that light was capable of degrees as concerns the mysteries "hidden from the ages in God" [Greek: apokekrymmena apo tōn aiōnōn en tō Theō], at length "made manifest to the principalities and powers in the heavenly places through the church" [Greek: tais archais kai tais exousiais en tois epouraniois dia tēs ekklēsias], Eph. 3:10; and what sort [of knowledge] they have through the continual vision of God, especially now that they are possessed of their reward in Christ — it is not the place here to inquire.

VI. "The God-man" [Greek: Theanthrōpos] Jesus Christ, and "mere men" [Greek: psiloi anthrōpoi], express the whole human race. The things which are to be said of the theology of Jesus Christ — "in whom are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge," Col. 2:3 — and of that knowledge which he had, and has, through the personal union, and of the revelations given to him by the Father, Rev. 1:1, and how in him dwells "all the fullness" of the Spirit, John 3:34, whom he received "not by measure" [Greek: ek metrou], we have judged it advisable to omit entirely here, since they cannot be comprehended within the compass of the present dissertation. But those who are "mere men," and either still pass the mortal life, or at least can be considered as such, and are commonly called "wayfarers" (for "in all this life there is hope," Eccles. 9:4), or, loosed from the frames of the body, are called "possessors" — of these, who...
[The sentence continues into Chapter IV (the theology of the first man), which belongs to the next section.]

... of these [the "possessors," who, having run the course of faith and obedience toward the will of God, have mercifully obtained their end and rest in the enjoyment of God], the theology — to dispatch it in a word — is that light of glory by which, beholding divine things "face to face" [Greek: prosōpon pros prosōpon; 1 Cor. 13:12] and God himself "as he is" [Greek: kathōs esti; 1 John 3:2], suffused with ineffable joy, they are eternally free for [given over to] the praises of God and of the Lamb. And these things had necessarily to be premised, that we might arrive where we are tending.

VII. According to the various state of those who are called "wayfarers," their theology also is various; and here it is in general twofold: namely, [the theology] of integral nature, or of creation; and of fallen nature, or of sin. But other considerations and distributions of men fallen into sin must be applied below. Of the first man we must treat in the first place.

chapters.heading: Part One: On Natural Theology, or the Theology of the First Man.

blocks.text: Argument: The theology of the first man, here [treated] — Its description — In what sense it is natural — The degrees, force, and efficacy of the connate light — The over-curiosity [Greek: periergia] of the scholastics, the error of the Socinians and others — The origin and necessity of natural theology — The obedience of the first man — Exposition of the promised reward, of sin, and of punishment — The corruption of original theology — The manner of the corruption — The first covenant abolished, and with it the theology resting on it.

I. The theology of all men regards either the "inward word" [Greek: logos endiathetos], and is called natural (in respect of its greatest part, since it was never alone), or the "uttered word" [Greek: prophorikos], which was brought in to supply the place of the obliteration of that "inward" [Greek: endiathetou] word through sin, and is called supernatural, or [the theology] of revelation. Of the first we must treat first. It can, and ought to, be considered as pure, as corrupt, and as apostate.

II. The state of integral nature, then — of men placed merely under the law of creation — first presents itself to us. In that state theology was "inward and natural" [Greek: endiathetos et naturalis]; yet not altogether "innate" [Greek: emphytos]. For from the beginning it was augmented by revelation, and was moreover to be augmented. In that state it is called pure. And it was a salutary light, consisting in the knowledge of God as Creator, Lawgiver, Ruler, and Rewarder. We call it "light," since that befits all theology by the usage of Scripture. This light was implanted in man by God in the very creation; and, augmented at once by the externally made revelation of the divine will in the sacramental precept, it was to be increased day by day from the consideration of the works of God. And this [theology] was able to make him wise in rightly rendering obedience to God according to the covenant of works, unto his own felicity and unto the glory of the divine power, wisdom, goodness, grace, and justice. This was the theology of the first man.

III. This light (or spiritual wisdom) God implanted in the mind of man in his very creation. It was congruous to the condition in which, and the end for which, he was made — nay, altogether necessary,

Eccles. 7:29. Although, therefore, by its own nature, and as concerns its use and end, it was plainly supernatural (for that man should obey God according to the covenant promising an eternal reward was of God's most free constitution), yet, because it was "inborn and implanted" [Greek: endiatheton kai emphyton], we say that it was natural to him. That is properly called "natural" which necessarily accompanies nature itself, or takes its origin from the principles of nature. In that sense it is plain that this light was not natural to man. For it does not necessarily follow human nature, so as to be inseparable from it; nor can it be drawn out of the natural faculties of the soul by their own proper and native power. But since it pertained to the rectitude of nature in respect of its original state, and was implanted in the very creation in man placed under law, with respect had to his ultimate end, it cannot be denied to have been natural. But about these matters, against the papists, it has been disputed elsewhere more than enough.

IV. About this light, or knowledge, of man in the state of incorrupt nature — as to its degrees and efficacy — there is some battle of opinions. The scholastics extol it magnificently enough, being here (as everywhere) full of empty speculations and of audacity in curious determinations. The Socinians feign the first man to have been a blockhead and a stump, scarcely yet master of his reason; as though ignorant of God, of himself, of his wife, and of the other creatures, they serve him up, as it were, to be derided. They suppose that one single precept was set him by God, about not eating the fruit of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil. Whether he had a mind, or that conscience, or acknowledged any dependence on God, they know not — or at least feign not to know; for the fiction of "ignorance" is very often the color [pretext] for the most insolent boasting of knowledge. Thus it has seemed good to them to give patronage to the foulest errors which they cherish about the fall of the first man and the state of sin: "Adam, like an infant or a boy, did not know that he was naked" (Smalcius, On the Truth of the Deity of the Son, ch. 7, p. 2) — as though he were really blind before he ate of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil; and, "Of his own wife he knew nothing but what was obvious to the senses," as the very leader of the flock himself speaks (On the State of the First Man, ch. 4, p. 119); "Nor," he says, "did he have a clear view of the power of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil" (p. 147) — as though the whole effect of the first sin took its origin from the power of that tree; and again, "though he was mortal, yet he did not know that he was mortal" (ch. 4, p. 118): namely, a lie must be covered with lies, lest it leak through. That they may prove Adam to have lost almost nothing by sin, they affirm that he never had anything; since he could not lose what he never had. A lid worthy of the dish! We, with an even balance if it can be done, will weigh strictly the theology of the first man, inclining neither to the over-curiosity [Greek: periergian] of those [scholastics] in asserting, nor to the audacity of these [Socinians] in denying.
V. That God, by the right of creation, is the supreme Lord of all things, is acknowledged by all creatures. Hence the existence of all is necessarily

accompanied by a dependence on God; and that dependence is accommodated to the principles of nature which he has communicated to each. All creatures, therefore, such as they are, and according to the capacity and disposition with which they are furnished, celebrate the Creator and serve him: Ps. 145:10, "All thy works shall praise thee, O Lord"; whence the Psalmist many times, "by personification" [Greek: kata prosōpopoiian], exhorts each and all of them to render that duty rightly. This eternal and invariable ground of divine worship the holy choir of the church, whether militant or crowned, establishes by unanimous consent: Rev. 4:11 [quoted by Owen in Greek], "Thou art worthy, O Lord, to receive glory and honor and power, for thou hast created all things, and for thy pleasure they are and were created"; to which the catholic [universal] company of all creatures responds (ch. 5:13, likewise in Greek), "And every creature which is in heaven, and on the earth, and under the earth, and such as are in the sea, and all that are in them, heard I saying, Blessing, and honor, and glory, and power, be unto him that sitteth upon the throne, and unto the Lamb, for ever and ever." For since God has made all things for himself — that is, for his glory (Prov. 16:4) — it was right that all those things should render him glory and honor. And just as it belongs to that supreme right of God, and his dominion over all things, to assign to each its proper and ultimate end, so it pertains to that dependence that there be a law and rule for operations tending toward that end. All creatures enjoy a fixed end and a law of operation, because they are creatures. That law, therefore, was connatural to all. But the rational creature, since it bears a twofold respect — namely, as it is a creature, and as, being capable of reason, it is consecrated to eternity — obtains from God a twofold dependence: one common, or "natural," of which we have spoken; another proper, or "moral." This is the obedience due to God for the state of a creature. To exact this obedience from creatures, nothing more is required than that God be set before them as supreme and best. And accordingly he exacts fear and worship, since he is "Adon" [Hebrew: Lord], the Lord of all: Mal. 1:6, "If I be a Lord, where is my fear?" That this should not be known from the very creation was impossible. For rightly rendering that obedience, that connate law must have sufficed. So at last both the ultimate end, and the creature's dependence on God, are established. But about that innate law two things may be considered: the law itself, namely the norm of the required obedience, and the knowledge of the law. That this best and greatest Benefactor, supreme Ruler, and Rewarder is to be loved, worshiped, and feared — as the moral order of this dependence, which (since God the Creator is supremely just and holy) embraces all justice and holiness, and is to be continued until his ultimate end is reached — this is the sum of this law. And this law is that "inward word, self-authenticating" [Greek: logos endiathetos, axiopiston] and utterly infallible, which was, as it were, the doctrine in the theology of the first man. But the knowledge of this law did not really differ from the law itself. And by a salutary light of that kind, by whose force and guidance man could rightly

render the obedience due to God according to the law, so as to attain his own proper end — by such a light, it appears, [his theology] consisted. And this was the theology of the first man. Constituted in a state of moral dependence on God, and submitted to the command of God, and thereby capable of eternal blessedness in the enjoyment of the Creator, he had a connate and "implanted" [Greek: emphyton] law of those operations which were necessary for attaining that end; and furthermore he was instructed, in the knowledge of God and of all the means of his worship, with that wisdom by whose force — according to the will and mind of God shining in it — he could rightly render the obedience prescribed in the law. But since this "implanted" law [Greek: emphytos], which in this theology supplies the place of doctrine — that is, the "inward word" [Greek: logos endiathetos] — was connatural to the mind of man, it was not, as concerns the thing itself (or "materially," as they say), other than that light, or habit of mind, by which the doctrine about God and his worship is salutarily perceived; nor was it anything more than the fountain and origin of that order of the rational creature's dependence. But in respect of exercise, and of actual obedience, and "formally" speaking, it was distinct from it.

VI. Since, therefore, God made man upright (Eccles. 7:29), in his own image (Gen. 1:26, 27) — which consists in wisdom, justice, and holiness (Col. 3:10; Eph. 4:23, 24) — and commanded him to hold the other creatures under his dominion, to his [God's] glory (to which dominion he would be wholly unequal unless their natures had been thoroughly known to him); and exacted from him obedience under the penalty of eternal death, and with the promise of a reward of blessed life: it is plain that he was instructed with that wisdom and moral light by which he could both know God, and perfectly understand the "implanted" law, and the revelation of the properties of God in the creation of all things, and the sacramental "law-giving" [Greek: nomothesia]. And being instructed with this theology, nothing at all was lacking to him for rightly worshiping God, or for living well and blessedly.

VII. And hence an estimate can be made of the obedience of the first man, of the proposed reward, together with the nature of the sin itself, and also of the threatened punishment. For the obedience to be rendered by force of the covenant had to be conformable to his connate light, or theology, delineated above; and the covenant itself, as it was connatural to Adam, so it was known to him; for it is plain that by force of this theology he knew both his duty and his reward. But the superadded sacramental precept did not reveal the covenant, but opened the way to sealing it. The proposed reward consisted in nothing but the everlasting enjoyment of God. The voice of the law is still the same thus far: "Do this, and you shall live"; for God never appointed any other reward of the obedience to be rendered by force of the covenant. But after what space of time, finally, run out, it would have befallen Adam to enjoy God under the character of reward — since God himself never foreknew it as future — is a subtle and dangerous disputation. That the first man, if he had stood in his original state, would have obtained the reward, and that the reward itself would have been God,

is plain from the very nature of the covenant; the time and manner in which it would have befallen him so to enjoy God, neither the nature of the thing nor the sacred page indicates. The obedience of the first man would therefore have been a universal and express exemplar of the image of God in all things; the reward, God himself. But his first sin, since it was committed against the directing force of this theology, plainly consisted in the total subversion of the moral order of the creature's dependence on God; whence the punishment of that sin [was] eternal separation from God, with the additional infliction, moreover, of the sense of punishment, which vindicatory justice could not but exact. After, therefore, the body and soul of the sinner had experienced the first death, in their being torn from one another, they had to be united again — by the divine power serving justice thus far — to undergo the second [death], consisting in eternal separation from God under the gravest punishments; from which condition, namely, we have been freed through Christ. The reward, therefore, and the punishment annexed to the first covenant, as concerns their substance (so to speak), differed in nothing from those which follow upon the new covenant. God himself, and separation from God, contain the nature of each on both sides. Nor can any other reward or other punishment be assigned; though it can perhaps be said that each was increased by the accession of some degrees, by force of the new covenant. Hence the reward is now called "a far more exceeding and eternal weight of glory" [Greek: kathʼ hyperbolēn eis hyperbolēn aiōnion baros doxēs], 2 Cor. 4:17; and the punishment, "death unto death" [Greek: thanatos eis thanaton], ch. 2:16. Of the resurrection of Adam, "apart from the word of the incarnation" [Greek: ektos tou logou ensarkōseōs], Scripture is indeed silent; for it does not pursue empty things, nor curiously discuss matters on the supposition of a state that would never be. But of the state of those who are utterly strangers to the mediation of Christ — since they never believed in him, nor sinned against his grace, of which they never heard anything — it speaks openly enough. But to hold that Christ himself merited the resurrection of men from the dead unto condemnation, for sins committed without law and without any knowledge of Christ, is impious. Since, therefore, Adam's immortality in his original state in no way depended on the internal principles of nature, but on the sole and most free good pleasure of God — that external preserving cause being removed by sin — it was necessary that Adam should die at a certain time, and by force of the covenant be drawn out of the state of natural death to judgment.

VIII. And let it suffice to have discoursed these things about natural theology considered in its original and native purity; it remains that we briefly survey its "destruction" [Greek: aphanismos] through the entrance of sin.

IX. But the entrance of sin, and the abolition of the divine covenant through that entrance, are known from elsewhere, and are here presupposed; so here we must treat only of the removal of this theology through them.

X. That all theology ought to be sufficient for attaining its proper end will be granted. This [end] consists in the glory of God, and in the eternal felicity of the theologians themselves. The image of God in man being therefore abolished through the entrance of sin, that law of "the knowledge of God" [Greek: theognōsias], implanted, of which

we have spoken, is utterly deprived of the nature and use of theology, so that the whole human race must rightly be reckoned to have been divested of all true theology through sin: these things are to be taught. So close was the union between that original doctrine and the habit of mind, that, torn apart, they could not maintain their station. But that salutary light, by which (we have shown) the proper form, as it were, of this original theology consisted, was extinguished through sin; so theology itself suffered "destruction" [Greek: aphanismos]. Besides, no doctrine can properly be called theology which does not rest on some divine covenant, by whose force the theologians themselves may please God and at length enjoy him. For all the theology of wayfarers directs men in the knowledge of God, and goads them to render the obedience due according to the norm of the covenant which it has pleased God to enter into with them. But that covenant which was the foundation of that original theology was, as I said, abolished. For what would it now do? — since, although it commands all things well, yet there is no one who can obey. The doctrine of that covenant is indeed still most true: "for he that doeth the things of the law shall live in them." But the covenant itself, after the entrance of sin, retained no force by which it might bring men to God. Nor, because men are compelled to bear the command of his precepts, are they to be called "covenanted," since they are held by his bond only from the supreme right and dominion of God, which he cannot abdicate. To such a pitch of misery has it come through sin, that the very doctrine set forth in the law of nature — however it accompanied the original light — was nevertheless not sufficient to direct sinners in the way of life; nor, however much it might wish, could it save sinners at all. For to know God, and to obey him as Creator, Ruler, and Rewarder, unless a man be furthermore instructed in the knowledge of his grace and mercy through Jesus Christ, suffices no one for salvation after the entrance of sin. Truly, therefore, the "inward and connate" [Greek: endiatheton et congenitum] light lost, through sin, the true ends of theology, its salutary use, and its force efficaciously directing the theologians in the way of life.

XI. And this is the reason why, from the apostasy of the theologians, theology itself also was utterly abolished — which we shall not find to have happened afterward. For since all theology, as we have said, is founded on a covenant, the covenant being destroyed, theology itself must necessarily collapse. But this covenant entered into with Adam rested on a moral dependence on God that was absolutely universal. In no way, therefore, could it be that it should not be made void through the first sin. The matter stands in the same way in the new covenant. The theology of the covenanted rests on the covenant. Whether any of the covenanted can fall from that covenant, or be cast down, we do not now dispute. The covenant itself cannot be abolished, because it is ratified in and with Christ the Mediator; nor accordingly does the theology resting on it totter. However much, therefore, the theologians have fallen away from its doctrine (as we shall often see), yet theology itself has remained altogether stable and immutable, the nature of the covenant requiring it.
[Owen's sentence "So concerning this [theology]…" carries over to p. 45, where Chapter V begins, "On the corruption and loss of natural theology" — the next section.]
... we have treated; and let it suffice to have treated it in few words, since it lasted but a short time, and the doctrine about it is elicited only from very few passages of Scripture.

chapters.heading: Part Two: On the Corruption and Loss of Natural Theology.

blocks.text: Argument: How far natural theology survives after the entrance of sin — Its remnants [Greek: leipsana], what and of what sort, are proved — From the law of nature: what it is, and its force, Rom. 2:14, 15 — The force of conscience — Atheists — That God can be known from his works is shown.

I. That original and native light of which we have hitherto treated was, as we said, extinguished through sin. Hence all men are called blind, nay, darkness itself [Eph. 5:8]; for that "inward" doctrine [Greek: endiathetos], together with the aids by which (in respect of its object) it was to be enlarged, was not able to attain its proper end. Whether that original theology, though corrupt and laboring under its own viciousness, still survives, we shall now see.

II. That there is still implanted in the hearts of men a sense of God as Creator, Ruler, and Judge — with a certain indelible knowledge of good and evil in relation to God's government, and of the distinction between the honorable and the base — almost all confess, and we shall prove. The apostle expounds it, Rom. 2:14, 15. Moreover, sinners who possess reason still enjoy the benefit of considering the works of God. For to believe that the nature of men has been so overwhelmed that it seems to have utterly shaken off the law of dependence and subjection inscribed on its heart by the Creator, is not [the part] of a reasonable man. We do not say that men are born furnished with any [actual] knowledge of God; they have none at all. But we say [they are born] with the power of knowing; nor do they so [actually] know and feel by nature. That each man retains an implanted power of knowing God, prompting him in some way to render him worship — a power that will of its own accord exert itself in adults possessed of reason, no less certainly and necessarily than reasoning itself — there is no reason to doubt, since we feel it.

III. But yet these things must be proved, since it is long since the theologians taught us to distrust what we see and feel. First, then, [we shall show] that there survive in the minds of sinners very many "remnants" [Greek: leipsana] of natural theology; then, that these, whatever they are, are not true theology.

IV. We say, therefore, that human nature — though shattered by so great a calamity through sin, and almost the mere remainder of itself — is still furnished with the relics of this theology. That God is, and is such as the very nature of truth requires him to be (namely, best by reason of virtue, greatest by reason of benefits), does not escape it. Whatever knowledge it has [of itself] it reckons as received, under God the Creator. There still flourishes in our nature that law and reason which teaches and proclaims this truth. That there is some law of nature is the voice of nature; that is, [nature] bears witness about itself. But wherever there is a law, there some lawgiver must be acknowledged;

and the giver of the law of nature can be no other than the author of nature. For that law is distant from nature itself only by a relation to some object. That God therefore is, and is to be worshiped, and that right and justice are to be observed among men, this law teaches — as Scripture also testifies, Ps. 145:15; 141:7-9; Job 12:7-10; 37-39; Isa. 40:12; Matt. 6:26; Acts 17:26-28. Its first dictate about God is, Rom. 1:19, "That which may be known of God is manifest in them" — that is, in the Gentiles, given over to the discipline of nature alone. And this "that which may be known" [Greek: to gnōston] of God is manifest not only "among them" (that is, in some of them, namely the philosophers), but "in them" [Greek: en autois] — that is, in all, through an implanted "knowledge of God" [Greek: emphytos theognōsia]. The same apostle also arraigns all men as guilty because they "held the truth" (about the nature and attributes of God) "in unrighteousness" [Greek: en adikia], v. 18. But they retain some divine truth who unjustly detain it. Furthermore, this law is declared to be so engraved on the hearts of all — victorious over all vices and prejudices — that he affirms the judgment of God concerning the punishing of sinners does not escape even the most worthless and abandoned of all two-footed creatures, v. 32. But those who acknowledge God's right to punish sins cannot still be ignorant of that same right of his that exacts obedience, nor of God himself. Secondly: the same law exacts from all the moral obedience due to God. After it has shown God, it teaches that he is to be worshiped: Rom. 2:14, 15, "For when the Gentiles, which have not the law" (the written law, namely, in the manner it was given to the Jews), "do" (yet, by the force of the law inscribed on their hearts) "the things contained in the law" (which the written moral law prescribes), "these, having not the law" (that is, the written, or otherwise immediately divinely revealed law), "are a law unto themselves" (because, namely, they have that law connatural to them); "which show the work of the law written in their hearts." "The work of the law," that is, what the law effects — an effect like itself; this they show themselves to have written or engraved in their hearts; that is, they have a law implanted, the transcript [Greek: ektypōma] of the written law. That it answer exactly to the written law is necessary. But what the written law teaches, and in whose name and by whose authority, we know. And to it that law is "corresponding" [Greek: antistrophos] which they have who lack the [written] law. Since, therefore, all men whatsoever have this law — inasmuch as it is of nature and common to all from nature equally — and since this law expresses that divinely revealed law which commands and forbids in the name of God, it appears that all those who can in no way lead a life loosed from the command of this law cannot but have some knowledge of God, and must acknowledge the moral obedience due to him.

V. Of this truth, too, conscience (as they say) is a thousand witnesses. Hence the apostle, proceeding to complete the remaining argument: "Their conscience also bearing witness, and their thoughts the meanwhile accusing or else excusing one another" [Greek: symmartyrousēs autōn tēs syneidēseōs, kai metaxy allēlōn tōn logismōn katēgorountōn ē kai apologoumenōn; Rom. 2:15] — that conscience is men's judgment about themselves, with respect to the judgment of a Superior, the matter itself proclaims; whence "Conscience is a god to mortals" [Greek: brotois hapasi syneidēsis Theos], in Menander. But

he who is of such "preeminence" [Greek: hyperochē] that he has dominion and can exercise judgment in the hearts of men, is God. That natural "accusation or defense" [Greek: katēgoria or apologia], therefore, which is carried out in each man by the help of conscience [Greek: syneidēsis], regards none but God. But for anyone to snatch away all power from this Judge — who among all men discharges his office in the name of God — is utterly impossible. At what price would the Cains, the Judases, the Neros, the Caligulas, and the lesser monsters of crimes too, buy freedom from his scourges? It must be confessed that this condition of human nature is set beyond all the ways and reasonings of our counsel and will. None of the wise reckons conscience among those things which they contend are "in our own power" [Greek: eph' hēmin]. Those who would too much have man to be wholly his own supreme Lord turned conscience into Furies, Avengers, Eumenides — tormentors of their own right. Let sinners be secure from the judgment of men as to their condition; let them be untroubled by the ferocity of those present; let them so despise the last perils of life, and the height of temporal things, that they voluntarily take upon themselves the extreme penalty — yet this tribunal, while they are manifestly guilty of crime, they cannot flee. But of this argument we have treated elsewhere.

VI. But let the thing itself speak. Do we not all experience that the human mind is so prone to give assent to this truth — namely, that God is; so [prone] to rush into its embrace (a truth without which the mind would not only be thrown down from dignity and honor and cast into the lowest rank, but would even be ignorant of itself) — that, the terms of which its proposition consists being explained, it is not lawful to dissent from it? Whether this happens by a certain impulse and instinct of nature (which is itself an argument, and reason alone suffices for it), or whether [the mind] uses a most rapid discourse and does not perceive that it has reasoned before it has, with all its powers, conceded to the proposed opinion — experience testifies that the human mind rests immovably in this truth. Excellently Plotinus, Ennead 3, Book 7, speaking of eternity, which he does not distinguish from God himself: "When we say," he says, "that eternity and time are diverse from each other, and that the one is concerned with the everlasting nature, the other with that which comes to be; and we suppose this [notion] to be universal — we, as it were of our own accord, from a natural command and by a certain sudden intuition of the intelligence, have a manifest instinct about these things, deeply implanted in our minds, saying the same things about them always, and everywhere naming them." [Greek: Plotinus, Ennead 3.7; "ton aiōna kai ton chronon heteron legontes hekateron einai…"] Be it so: let there be impious men who would too much have God not to exist — nay, who have dared openly to profess that they believe God does not exist. Yet since this highest impiety and wickedness never advances unaccompanied by all kinds of other crimes, who will make me believe that those impure rascals, who dare to slander human nature (with all that partakes of it resisting),

do not also impudently lie about themselves? Whatever it be, what madness would it be to give credence to three or four disgraces of human nature, against the constant and consenting voice not only of all good men but of nature itself? "For he [Epicurus] alone saw, first, that there are gods, because nature herself had impressed a notion of them on the minds of all. For what nation, or what kind of men, is there which has not, without instruction, some anticipation of the gods? — which Epicurus calls 'prolepsis' [Greek: prolēpsis], that is, a certain conception of a thing antecedently grasped by the mind, without which nothing can be understood, or inquired, or disputed," says Velleius in Cicero, On the Nature of the Gods, Book 1, ch. 16; and Seneca, Epistle 117: "That there are gods we infer, among other things, from this — that an opinion about the gods is implanted in all, nor is there any nation anywhere so cast beyond laws and customs that it does not believe in some god." That there is some god [is] "an opinion and notion common to the whole human race, implanted by necessity and innate by nature in every mind, alike among Greeks and among barbarians," says Dio of Prusa [Chrysostom], Oration [Greek: doxan kai epinoian koinēn tou sympantos anthrōpinou genous, homoiōs men Hellēnōn, homoiōs de barbarōn, anankaian kai emphyton]. And hence Aristotle forbids disputation to be undertaken with those who doubt whether the gods are to be worshiped, since that ought to be set beyond controversy by the dictate of right reason. "Those," he says, "who doubt whether the gods are to be honored and parents to be loved, need chastisement" [Greek: hoi men aporountes poteron dei tous theous timan kai tous goneis agapan ē ou, kolaseōs deontai], Topics, Book 1; they must be coerced by penalties, not convinced by reasons. The whole chorus of the wise — all of them — feel one and the same thing in this matter. The philosopher [Aristotle] joins together the worship of God and the love of parents, which it is agreed are from nature. These notions Justin (in his Apology) calls "seeds of truth [implanted] in all" [Greek: spermata alētheias para pasi]. "No soul is without crime, because none is without the seed of good," says Tertullian, On the Soul. That they generally name "gods" in the plural [Greek: polytheōs], assenting [to many], must be ascribed to corrupt traditions and to prejudices prevailing against the [native] genius of human nature. So Lactantius: "What pardon for their impiety can they hope for, who do not acknowledge the worship of him whom it is utterly unlawful for a man to be ignorant of? For both when they swear, and when they wish, and when they give thanks, they name not Jupiter or many gods but God; so far does truth itself, nature compelling, burst forth from unwilling breasts." And it is most true that, in the midst of idolatries, this native and original light of reason exerted itself so efficaciously that it compelled most men — even those who were most lavish in idol-madness — to confess one God, supreme and greatest: "For this reason the first and greatest god, the framer who built [the world]" [Greek], in Dio, History, Book 56, [the words of] Tiberius Caesar. Aristotle, On the World, adds the reason for the multiplicity of names [Greek: polyōnymia]: "Being one," he says, "he is called by many names [Greek: polyōnymos], being named from all the conditions which he himself institutes." To this [sense] of human nature about God and his worship, [its] "anticipation" [Greek: prolēpsis], Julian bears illustrious testimony: "We are all, before instruction, persuaded that there is some deity; thither must we look, thither must we hasten; and I believe our souls are so disposed toward God as things endowed with sight are toward the light." [Greek: pantes adidaktous einai theion ti pepeismetha, kai pros touto aphoran…]

Hence that old formula, used by those set in dangers and crises, of appealing from the injuries of others — that the gods exist. It was the voice of nature crying to the God of nature: "While each for himself murmurs that the gods exist, and do not neglect human things," Livy, Book 3; and, "There is a heavenly deity; there is, great Jupiter," the same, Book 8. So too it was done by Sulla, fearing the treachery of Volux, son of Bocchus, in Sallust's Jugurthine War; and in Plautus, Captivi: "Surely there is a God, who both hears and sees what we do." Excellently Cyprian, On the Vanity of Idols: "This is the height of guilt — to be unwilling to acknowledge him whom you cannot be ignorant of." This opinion Eusebius also expounds excellently, Preparation for the Gospel, Book 2, ch. 9: "That is an excellent and especially useful thing which signifies the name and essence of God — which, led by nature itself, and by notions impressed on the soul of themselves (or rather divinely inspired), everyone understands; for all peoples have perceived this by a certain common sense of reason, since the same Maker of this universe has sown it, by certain natural thoughts, in every mind endowed with reason and intelligence" — thus he. [Greek: physei men oun kai autodidaktois ennoiais, mallon de theodidaktois, kalon te kai ōphelimon… pantes gar anthrōpoi koinois logismois proeilēphesan tou tōn holōn Dēmiourgou.] And Tertullian, Against Marcion: "We define God as first to be known by nature, then to be recognized by doctrine." And in the Apology: "Would you have us prove it from the testimony of the soul itself" (that God is, and is one) "— [the soul] which, though pressed by the prison of the body, though circumscribed by depraved institutions, though enervated by lusts and concupiscences, though enslaved to false gods, yet, when it comes to itself, as from drunkenness, as from sleep, as from some sickness, and enjoys its health, names God — for this only reason, that he is properly the true one, this one God, good and great; and 'what God may grant' is the speech of all. It also attests him as Judge: 'God sees,' and 'I commend it to God,' and 'God will repay me.' O testimony of a soul naturally Christian! Finally, in pronouncing these things, it looks not to the Capitol but to heaven. For it knows the seat of the living God, from whom, and from thence, it descended."

VII. But just as most of the wise had noted these "common notions" [Greek: koinai ennoiai], or "anticipations" [Greek: prolēpseis], so also they had found out that force of conscience which we have expounded. Menander: "Conscience is a god to mortals" [Greek: brotois hapasi syneidēsis Theos]. Speaking of Tiberius (Annals 6, ch. 6), Cornelius Tacitus: "So did his own crimes and disgraces turn to his punishment. Nor was it in vain that the most excellent of the wise was wont to affirm that, if the minds of tyrants were laid open, lacerations and blows might be seen; since, as bodies [are torn] by stripes, so the mind [is torn] by cruelty, lust, and evil counsels. For neither his fortune nor his solitudes protected Tiberius from confessing the torments of his breast and his own punishments." The fear arising from the con-

science of sins, Cicero calls "a most cruel master"; and, "the wicked," he says, "the furies harass and pursue, not with burning torches, as in the fables, but with the anguish of conscience and the torture of their own fraud," On the Laws, Book 1. Vexed by that continual anguish — which had spurned all remedies, whether of new crimes, or of withdrawal from the company of men, or even his fear of vengeance — Tiberius could not contain himself, but, weary of himself, publicly professed in a letter to the senate the torments he suffered in his breast: "What shall I write to you, conscript fathers," he says, "or how shall I write, or what shall I not write at all at this time? May the gods and goddesses destroy me worse than I feel myself perishing daily, if I know [what to write]," Suetonius, Tiberius, ch. 67. Otho too, Galba having been slain by a nefarious crime, was so terrified that he tried by every kind of expiation to propitiate the shades of Galba; by the same witness. Excellently the Satirist:
"He whose mind is cold with crimes — his silent heart sweats with hidden guilt." (Juvenal, Sat. 1)
And again:

"Yet why think you that those have escaped, whom a mind conscious of a dread deed holds astonished, and lashes with a soundless whip, the soul shaking the hidden scourge as tormentor? Nay, the punishment is grievous, and far more savage than those which grim Caedicius, or Rhadamanthus, devised — to carry, night and day, one's own witness in the breast." (Juvenal, Sat. 13)

A signal example in Jugurtha, after innumerable crimes had been perpetrated, the historian sets forth: "Nor after that time," he says, "was any day or night quiet for Jugurtha; he trusted neither place, nor any mortal, nor the hour enough; feared citizens and enemies alike; looked round on all things, and started at every noise; rested now here, now there, often against royal dignity, by night; sometimes, roused from sleep, seized his arms and made a tumult: so was he driven by a kind of dread and frenzy."
And this is the first foundation, or first part, of natural theology in the corrupt state and state of sin; whence he who has not ceased to be a man cannot utterly cease to be a theologian.

VIII. Furthermore: from the consideration of the works of God — both of creation and of providence — this residual knowledge of God can be cherished and increased day by day; for this theology, such as it is, rests not only on human nature, but on the whole world.

IX. First, nothing can be said more aptly to this matter than what the Psalmist has, Ps. 19:1-7: "The heavens declare the glory of the mighty God; and the firmament showeth the work of his hands. Day unto day uttereth speech, and night unto night showeth knowledge," etc. A twofold principle — or rather means — of God's revelation this Psalm celebrates:

namely, his works and his word. In comparing these with each other, he prefers the latter to the former in saving effects (which indeed he ascribes to the word alone). But the former [the works] he proves to declare the glory of God throughout the whole world. The thing revealed, of which there is here mention, is the glory of God, and also the power which he exerted in the creation of all things; that is, as the apostle says, "his eternal power and Godhead" [Greek: aidios autou dynamis kai theiotēs], Rom. 1:20. The means of revelation are the heaven and the sun, and the days and nights driven round by their circuit, succeeding one another in turn. The mode of this revelation is variously expressed: namely, they are said to declare, to show, to utter, to exhibit, to speak — all metaphorically, but [in terms] which sufficiently indicate efficacy toward the proposed end. In the place of an adjunct is [the fact] that this revelation is catholic [universal], namely, extended to all the extremities of the habitable world.
