The Psalmist adds, indeed, that this doctrine of the works of creation and of God's providence is not sufficient for anyone to know God rightly and worship him holily — since he claims those [saving] effects entirely for [God's] word, the doctrine contained in sacred Scripture; yet meanwhile he expressly affirms that the essence, glory, and power of God are so revealed through it [the creation] that all the inhabitants of the world learn from it both that God is, and that he is infinitely separated from all the idols of the Gentiles.

X. No less clearly does the apostle confirm the same truth, Rom. 1:18-20: "For the wrath of God is revealed from heaven against all ungodliness and unrighteousness of men, who hold the truth in unrighteousness; because that which may be known of God is manifest in them, for God hath showed it unto them. For the invisible things of him, from the creation of the world, being weighed by the mind, are clearly seen by the things that are made, even his eternal power and Godhead; to this end, that they may be inexcusable." I do not wish to involve myself much in the matters controverted in these words which lie outside our purpose. The rotten figment of the Socinians, who wrest the words of the twentieth verse to the doctrine of the gospel, has long since been hissed off. For the rest, the thing revealed is in general called "that which may be known of God" [Greek: to gnōston tou theou]; specifically, his eternal power, Godhead, and wrath, or vindicatory justice. The means of revelation are the works of God — the things he made: namely, [the works] of creation, which have existed from the founding of the world, and [the works] of providence, which he still works from heaven. The mode is through a certain laying-open of these things, connatural to them, and [through] the consideration of the mind, which they indelibly establish. And through these means this revelation attains this degree: that, in those to whom it is made, it is called a "manifestation" of the things revealed. The end is "inexcusability" [Greek: anapologēsia] — not that [end] absolutely, indeed, but as concerns the use of the benefit of this revelation. But whether "that which may be known of God" is rendered by "what can be known of God" or "what ought" [to be known], the matter comes to the same; for it is not said absolutely, but with respect to that [manifestation] which [comes] through the works of creation and providence of God; for thence it can be known that God is, that he is of eternal

power and just; and this ought to be known thence — for the apostle does not here extend that "that which may be known of God" [Greek] any further. Indeed, the things that are natural to God, and (an object being once posited) necessary in respect of exercise — whatever they be — fall no less exactly under this manifestation than those of which mention is made "by name" [Greek: kata lexin]. But of those things whose exercise, all objects being posited, depends on the most free will of God — such as grace and mercy toward sinners — the account is different. For of these there is no revelation except in Christ; which we have demonstrated more fully elsewhere.

XI. Nor less illustrious are those passages in which the Holy Spirit himself uses this very argument, and proves from his works that God is, and is to be known and worshiped. And who shall forbid us to be wise according to the dictate of the Holy Spirit? For the rest: Ps. 8; also 145:8-15; 147:4-6; Job 12:7-10; 37-39; Isa. 40:12; Matt. 6:26. This argument either God himself, or the prophets in his name, press with many [words]. To dwell on all would be too long, and alien to our purpose. Let us briefly run through two arguments of the same apostle to this end; one in Acts 14:15-17, the other in ch. 17:24-28. The first stands thus: "Sirs, why do you these things? We also are men, subject to the same affections as you, announcing that you should turn from these vain things to that living God, who made heaven, and earth, and sea, and all things that are in them; who, in times past, suffered himself not to be without testimony, in giving good things, giving us rain from heaven and fruitful seasons, filling our hearts with food and gladness." These are the words of Paul and Barnabas, defending themselves from the impious worship of the Lystrans. First they teach that both the worship itself, and the object of worship which those men had feigned for themselves, are "vain" [Greek: mataia], that is, foolish and empty; then they point out the true God, both by the attribute of life and by the effect of his works. For God is the living one who made heaven and earth. And they prove that he gave such testimony of himself to them through the works of providence that all might thence be able to know him, and easily to distinguish him from vain, dead, worthless idols. What could be said more clearly? "He left himself not without witness" [Greek: ouk amartyron heauton aphēken] — that is, he testified both that he is, and that he governs this world by his providence; and that in what way? Through his works, namely, of providence. Not indeed that he gave this testimony of himself with this end, that men might know him savingly and be converted (for how could he [have done] that seriously, when meanwhile he willingly suffered them to walk in their own ways?), but only to this end, that those who, despising him, attached themselves to vain idols, might be "without excuse" [Greek: anapologētoi].

XII. In the same manner of arguing the apostle proceeds, Acts 17:24-29. But this thread of the apostolic discourse — since, when we come to treat of the sufficiency of this revelation unto salvation, it must be discussed by us at greater length — we have not thought fit to expound further at present. And these teachers — namely, the works of God — even those celebrated for wisdom among the Gentiles acknowledged.

"Heaven never ceases teaching men [that there are gods]" [Greek: ouranos oudepote pauetai didaskōn anthrōpous], says Plato, Epinomis. "And who is so blind that, when he has looked up to the heavens, does not perceive that there are gods?" as Cicero, On the Response of the Haruspices. And Zaleucus put it into his laws: "All who inhabit the city and the country must first be persuaded" — and he willed [them to hold] — "that the gods exist; and, looking up to heaven and the world and the order in them, [to judge] that these are not the work of chance, nor of men," etc. [Greek: tous katoikountas tēn polin kai tēn chōran pantas prōton pepeisthai chrē… theous einai, kai anablepontas eis ouranon kai ton kosmon…]. To their mind Tatian: "This," he says, "we learn from his creation, and his invisible power we grasp by his works" [Greek: touto dia tēs poiēseōs autou ismen, kai tēs dynameōs autou to aoraton tois poiēmasi katalambanometha]. See Maximus of Tyre, Dissertation 1; Maimonides, More Nebuchim, Part 1, ch. 34. It cannot indeed be denied that all those things which by their own nature lead to the knowledge of God, and to some sense of his attributes, are drawn elsewhere by many, and interpreted otherwise than they ought. We have seen that very often none are more prone to atheism than those who profess to track out, by every trace, the knowledge of all things — especially of those which are hidden and lie concealed by nature; for they abuse the contemplation of all things only to satisfy the innate curiosity of the mind in considering the connections and knots of second causes, being secure and negligent of their God. So too, those for whom the vicissitudes of things have been pondered through a long series of years — so far is it often [from being the case] that they thence understand and fear the "manifold" providence of God [Greek: polypoikilos], that, from a native vice (namely the blindness of mind and malice of heart), they even slide down into atheism. Hence the proverb arose, "no old man fears Jupiter." But these vices of certain men must not be ascribed to human nature itself. Excellent indeed are the words of Aristotle in Cicero, On the Nature of the Gods, Book 2 — from what work of Aristotle they are taken is uncertain, for they nowhere appear in him: "If there were [men]," he says, "who had always dwelt beneath the earth, in good and well-lit dwellings, adorned with statues and pictures and furnished with all the things with which those who are thought happy abound, and yet had never gone out above the earth, but had learned by report and hearsay that there is a certain deity and power of the gods; and then, at some time, the jaws of the earth being opened, they could escape from those hidden seats into these places which we inhabit, and could come forth; when suddenly they had seen earth and seas and sky; had learned the magnitude of the clouds and the force of the winds; had beheld the sun, and learned both its magnitude and beauty and also its efficacy, in that it makes the day, light being diffused through the whole sky; and when night had darkened the lands, then beheld the whole heaven distinguished and adorned with stars, and the varying lights of the moon now waxing now waning, and the risings and settings of all these, and their courses fixed and immutable through all eternity — when they saw these things, surely they would judge both that there are gods, and that these so great works are the works of the gods." To which may be added very many of the things which Cicero himself disputes in that place. The sum is this: that there is some excellent and eternal nature, and that it is to be looked up to and admired
by the human race, the beauty of the world and the order of the heavenly things compel [us] to confess — which are the words of the same Cicero, in the second book On Divination.

[At the foot of p. 54 begins Chapter VI: "On the sufficiency of natural theology unto salvation" — including Owen's large digression on universal grace and the will of God to save those who lack the word. That chapter is the next section.]

Argument: The sufficiency of natural theology unto salvation is treated; that it is insufficient is proved by various arguments — The doctrine of this theology is imperfect, and the habit also — A digression on universal grace, and on the will of God to save those who lack the word.

I. Since the knowledge of God has been of this kind, and rests on these means — the [knowledge] which has flourished, and flourishes, among sinful men destitute of supernatural revelation — let us see further whether, by its force and help, those who were (or are) imbued with it could attain their ultimate end, so that it might still seem to merit the name of true theology. A twofold sufficiency of any doctrine can exist, in respect of God and of his supreme judgment upon men: one unto "inexcusability" [Greek: anapologēsia], the other unto salvation. The former the apostle ascribes to this knowledge, Rom. 1:20; to the latter we assert it unequal. For: (1) All those ages in which the greatest part of mortals lived destitute of heavenly revelation are called "times of ignorance" [Greek: chronoi agnoias], Acts 17:30. These God, as it were, overlooked by conniving, permitting the Gentiles to walk in their own ways, Acts 14:16, not announcing to them that they should repent. Through that ignorance they were, to a man, "alienated from the life of God" [Greek: apēllotriōmenoi tēs zōēs tou Theou], Eph. 4:18; so that, as concerns special grace and love, no communication intervened between them and God. (2) Next, all that knowledge of which men could be made partakers in that state pertained to the law; but for that [end] the law itself does not suffice, nor can the knowledge of the law suffice — especially knowledge that is imperfect; nor does the imperfect knowledge of a most absolute and most perfect law profit a whit, in respect of that law's end. But that the law itself is unequal to this thing — namely, that one should please God — the apostle shows at length, Rom. 3 and 4. (3) Also, "without faith it is impossible to please him" [Greek: chōris pisteōs adynaton euarestēsai], Heb. 11:6; but "faith comes by hearing, and hearing by the word of God" [Greek: hē pistis ex akoēs, hē de akoē dia rhēmatos Theou], Rom. 10:17. (4) "This is life eternal, that they" (men) "might know thee the only true God, and Jesus Christ whom thou hast sent" [Greek: hina ginōskōsi se ton monon alēthinon Theon, kai hon apesteilas Iēsoun Christon], John 17:3. "And there is salvation in none other" [Greek: kai ouk estin en allō oudeni hē sōtēria], Acts 4:12. But since the promise of Christ was made only after the fall — by which the mercy of God toward the human race first appeared — and since all the knowledge of God that men have without special revelation is only that particle of it which the first-formed [protoplasts] had in the state of integrity, it is utterly impossible that anything about Christ (all knowledge of whom is "by hearing" [Greek: ex akoēs]) should emanate thence. (5) This whole knowledge, however great it was, served only the covenant of works; whence, that covenant being made void through sin, it could direct men to no obedience except from fear of punishment.

And when anyone does a precept from fear of punishment, "not from love of justice, he does not do in his mind what he does in deed, and is therefore inwardly guilty of sin, however innocent he may seem to himself," as Augustine aptly [says].

II. There are, therefore, two things in the theology of fallen man: (1) the internal light still surviving, which is, as it were, the faculty of theology, or its "power" [Greek: dynamis]; and (2) the revelation of God through the works of creation and providence, which is, as it were, theology considered objectively, or its doctrine. But to neither of these do we dare ascribe the efficacy of theology unto salvation, in respect of the ultimate end. For the first we must needs convict of imperfection — both in respect of the doctrine which it reveals (which, not exceeding the compass of the law, as we said, contains nothing akin to the knowledge of Christ), and in respect of the mode of perceiving divine things, by which its efficacy is circumscribed; which mode, since the minds of men are utterly beset with darkness and blindness, cannot be spiritual. To that other [the objective revelation] also we ascribe the same [insufficiency], on the same grounds, since it neither exhibits the "outline" [Greek: hypotypōsin] of all the doctrine absolutely necessary, nor exerts itself with that efficacy by which saving light could be infused into a blind intellect; for Jesus Christ alone "brought life and incorruptibility to light, and that through the gospel," 2 Tim. 1:10.

III. Those who set up [the claim] that the revelation of the divine mind — and so of our obedience — through the works of creation and providence rests upon a general will of God to save all men, besides being compelled to ascribe to God useless desires, imperfect purposes, an inefficacious intention which he either will not or cannot render efficacious, and a mercy nowhere and never exerting itself, also use distinctions of such a kind, and confine their assertion within such limits, that they need not confess that revelation ever to have been, or to have been able to be, of saving use to any mortal. For just as that separation of this revelation from the illusions by which the prince of darkness wondrously made sport of the wretched manikins bound to him — a separation which they contend is necessary — cannot be made without the light of the word; so neither can that removal of darkness, blindness, and prejudices from the minds of men (without which all revelation must necessarily fall out in vain) be procured without the special help of the Holy Spirit.
That is, a dissertation on the sufficiency of natural theology, in the state of sin, unto salvation.

I. But since the opinion of all is not the same about these matters, it is well to trace this a little higher, and to subject to examination, "as in passing" [Greek: hōs en parodō], the opinion of certain most learned men. That this manifestation of God not only suffices sinful men unto salvation, but that the sinners themselves are endowed with that power by which they can so use that manifestation as at length really to obtain salvation — this the Pelagians once contended; and into their opinion almost the whole nation of the Arminians has gone over. With them I have no mind to engage at present; they have, in the writings of the ancients and of the more recent, wherewith to exercise themselves long enough. But a certain part of that controversy has lately begun to be agitated among brethren. They have chosen to call it "universal grace." And the hinge not only of truth, but among some of fraternal charity also, seems to turn in this dispute. For so it is that among Christians men live forgetful of meekness, nay of human frailty. Neither the duty which the nature of evangelical grace exacts, nor the mercy due to the weak human condition, avails anything to make us bear with an even mind those who are separated from us by opinion in trifling matters. We confess that the best of men know only "in part" [Greek: ek merous]; that any of the saints are so perfectly transformed, through the renewal of the mind, into the light of God that they are overspread by no relics of darkness and liable to no errors, we are unwilling to acknowledge. But this everyone thinks ought to be said of all others, not of himself. For when it comes to the thing itself and the test, there is no man who does not so bear himself as if he were the possessor of the best faith and of all truth, so that it is scarcely fair that anyone should enjoy this common light who dares to dissent from him. But between the "form" [Greek: morphōsis] of truth and its "power" [Greek: dynamis] there is this difference: the former only illuminates the mind with the knowledge of the true, and as it were compels it to give assent to evangelical doctrine; but the latter, over and above, induces its very stamp upon the whole soul, and renders the mind itself in all things conformable to it.

II. What battles and feuds arose not long ago, over this matter, between certain learned men in Belgium [the Low Countries] and some of better note in France, no learned man is ignorant who cares to know the affairs of religion and of the churches of Christ. To narrate the occasions, origin, and progress of that controversy, as it has been agitated among them for some years past — since these are more than enough divulged in the published exercitations, animadversions, apologies, and vindications [issued] on this side and that — would be nothing but to waste labor. For no one can spend his study more uselessly than in bringing to light things which are of themselves too well known, and which it were to be wished had never been known. Not indeed has weariness of peace, or of fraternal charity, so far seized me that I would wish to thrust myself importunately and rashly into the midst of a combat which is agitated with various motions of mind, arising from things that are really "beside the matter" [Greek: exō tou pragmatos]: I have other things to do. But if I shall candidly expound — without any lust of contradicting anyone — what I myself think about that part of this controversy which is not foreign to our present design (nay, is so framed that, unless the doubt about it be removed, I can scarcely safely proceed by the way I have begun), I hope it will be grievous or troublesome to no one.

III. I could indeed have wished that those most learned men had entered upon this contest with greater gentleness and more zeal for Christian meekness, with less haughtiness (pardon the word) and "self-display" [Greek: phantasia]. For I fear that, before fair judges free from party zeal, the excuses they will perhaps allege may not suffice — excuses which they could have done without, with not the least loss of the opinion they hold to be true, or of their reputation. For what? Is it all one with what counsel, or with what disposition of mind, we approach the handling of sacred things? Are we to believe that that Holy Spirit — without whose special help and guidance we can begin nothing piously or finish anything happily (for that "something" which we seem to perform by our own powers is really nothing) — would wish to mingle himself with the embittered affections of our minds, or with minds hindered by anger, hatred, vengeance, or desire of fame, by which he is most of all grieved? Must we not strive with all our might lest somehow, when we have announced the truth to others, we ourselves be, as concerns its "power" [Greek: dynamis], "disapproved" [Greek: adokimoi; reprobate, not standing the test]? Since it is also most certain that no one can ever rightly perceive divine truth as it is in Jesus, except he who has from the bottom of his heart denied himself, and laid aside, to the best of his power, all haughtiness and all prejudices — how is it lawful to expect that others will be taught by us that truth whose force we do not so experience in ourselves as to be "delivered into the mould [Greek: typos] of it"? For such is the nature of all evangelical doctrine, suffusing our minds with its own native light, that it renders us meek and humble, and most like to its author; namely, the wisdom which is from above, which "first indeed is chaste, then peaceable, gentle, compliant, full of mercy and of good fruits, without disputing, and not in the least feigned," James 3:17.

IV. But those who, on the other side, would bring brethren dissenting from them into peril of peace, or of their reputation among their churches — let them do it without me for a rival. To deprive learned, pious men — ministers of churches, rectors of schools — of the liberty of their own judgment in controversies of this kind, and, in the whole exercise of their office, to wish to send them under the yoke of precise [predetermined] opinions, would be nothing else than to bring an intolerable tyranny into the church of Christ. Nor indeed would I expect thence either any notable progress of truth, or that "building-up, in love, of those who profess the truth" [Greek: oikodomēn en agapē], where the dispensers of the divine word are compelled, like abject slaves, to be of another's judgment in all their work. Neither the rationale of the institution of the evangelical ministry, nor the nature of the gospel itself or of faith, nor the distinctions of the gifts of the Holy Spirit, nor fraternal charity, nor the edification of the church, nor the condition of the light of which we have been made partakers in this life, nor the innumerable divine precepts, nor the guidance of grace which we experience, will bear the imposition of a burden of this kind. But with how great a calamity that opinion of those who affect domination over their brethren — that no divorce of opinions ought to be tolerated in matters which do not immediately pertain to the foundation of faith — has long since well-nigh everywhere afflicted the Christian cause, is too well known to need our narration.

V. These things being premised, let us subject to consideration that thesis which some oppose to our [doctrine] of the total insufficiency of corrupted natural theology unto salvation. It is this: that God wills adults who lack his word to be saved. Whether those who holily weigh with themselves what it is to be saved, and what it is to lack the word of God, will be ready to give easy assent to that opinion, I vehemently doubt. Let party zeal be laid aside for a little; let the consideration of that connection which this thesis has with other opinions be dismissed; the matter will be plain. Let the disputants only beware lest they clamor with syllogisms against that communion which they themselves have graciously obtained with God through Jesus Christ in matters of eternal salvation, and perhaps these contests will cease.

VI. But the will of God concerning the salvation of those to be saved regards either the end and event — and is called intention, purpose, or decree — or the means which they ought to use who would be saved, and is called precept. But in what sense God wills those to be saved who in fact will never be saved — whom, willing it, he suffers to walk in their own ways, in which it is utterly impossible that they should be saved; whom he does not call, nor will to call, to repentance through that means by which alone it is possible that anyone be called to true repentance — such is my dullness, I confess, that I do not understand it. For he who grants only those means of salvation which can reveal neither who is the author of salvation, nor through whom it is to be obtained, nor what that salvation is, seems rather to be said to be unwilling than willing the salvation of those to whom he grants them. If this matter were brought to be decided by those who — instructed with spiritual "perception" [Greek: aisthēsis], and relying on the help of all saving means — even of the Holy Spirit himself "richly" [Greek: plousiōs] poured out through Jesus Christ — know what it is to serve God in the midst of temptations, so that at length, by the ineffable benefit of his grace, they obtain salvation: if it were brought to such men, set outside the cords of contention, the estimate of it would prove easy.

VII. Whoever would survey in mind those parts of the world which even now lack the word, and behold what is the condition — in respect of eternal salvation — of the wretched men passing their life "in iniquity" [Greek: en anomia], will doubtless hang in suspense of mind over that will of God which some learned men proclaim. Their most foul condition, I know, they will contend must be ascribed to "their own malice"; nor do I deny that it must absolutely be so. But that their malice exceeds that with which all those are naturally imbued to whom God has granted his word because he wills them to be saved — this I deny. In what sense, therefore, God is to be said to will those to be saved who will never be saved, and to whom he is unwilling to supply the means necessary to salvation, is a matter too entangled, and one that refuses easy explanation.

VIII. Some assert that "the word can be preached to them, but this is not done, through our negligence and sloth." That the gospel can indeed be preached even to those to whom it is not [now] preached, no one doubts; for what contradiction would seem to arise from the thing itself? But that it can be done in re-

spect of the event — [an event] which God from eternity willed should not be — this is not yet proved. That it is not actually preached to many, nor was once preached, Scripture ascribes to the will of God, Ps. 147:19, 20; Matt. 11:26; Acts 16:6, 7. That it falls out so through our negligence and sloth does not appear. Although, if it were so, the difficulty would not be removed; for that God could take that sloth from us if he would, no one doubts. But negligence and sloth have no place except where there is a duty; and all our duty depends on the will of God calling us to the duty. But how will they teach that we are called by God to the duty of preaching the gospel to those who hitherto, in the various parts of the world, have lacked it? Let us grant the learned men that there is no need of any ecclesiastical mission to undertake that office — for who is furnished with that catholic power, to send others thus? — yet I would wish to know what is that providence of God which makes us so certain of his will that we could undertake that work in faith; or who they are that are furnished with gifts rightly to accomplish it. That we are all too negligent and slothful in administering that province in the work of the gospel which God has graciously delegated to us, I confess; "and who is sufficient for these things?" [Greek: kai pros tauta tis hikanos]. But that the guilt of our sloth extends so far that it must be imputed even to this — that the gospel has not been preached to the inhabitants of America — to me, at least, free from all heat of disputation, is not credible.

IX. But it is well to look thoroughly into the opinion of those most learned men. First, then, they grant that "no pagan, without the preaching of the word, ever used, or could use, God's documents set before him by nature or by the works of providence, so as to repent and obtain salvation." So speak certain most celebrated men; and thus they show that in this matter they feel most differently from all the Pelagians, from most of the Arminians, and even from some petulant revilers here among us. And why should not the estimate of this whole dispute be made here? Have we nothing else, more proper to us and greater [to do], than to waste time disputing whether that can be done, or not, which neither ever was nor is to be — nay, which God purposed in himself should never be done? But it is another matter, as we shall see.

X. They add, therefore, that "aids of this kind were given to the pagans which in and of themselves lead to some degree of saving knowledge." So speaks a most learned man, of great name in theology. But every degree of saving knowledge is itself saving. And all saving knowledge consists in the knowledge of the covenant of grace. Unless, therefore, that degree of saving knowledge which can be procured through those aids be more plainly expounded, I may be permitted to profess that I neither grasp nor understand that degree. Even the least degree of saving knowledge requires a saving renovation of the mind. That in a blind mind there should be any degree of saving knowledge is impossible; for in saving knowledge consists its renovation. But where renovation of the mind is, there is the Holy

Spirit, who alone works and effects it. Those aids, therefore, which lead to some degree of saving knowledge, lead also to the obtaining of the Holy Spirit, the renovation of the mind, and true faith. But that this is the peculiar work of the gospel, [Scripture] everywhere teaches. But if by "saving knowledge" only saving doctrine is intended, and not renovation of the mind, then the sense of that statement is that those aids lead sinful men to the doctrine of the gospel, or at least to some degree of it. Whether that is true, we shall see hereafter.

XI. In that state, surely, we now are: that unless we know Jesus Christ, whom God sent, and God in him reconciling the world to himself, we cannot attain eternal life. But all that knowledge of God which sinful men can acquire without the word, from the works of creation and providence, is only the knowledge of God as Creator and Governor of all things; of redemption, the deepest silence. But they rejoin: "If these things are right, they bring it about that a man — even if, by an impossibility, he be supposed to be well and dutifully engaged in the school of nature, and to confer all his power of understanding upon weighing and learning the things he there hears or sees — can yet draw nothing else from these documents but this one thing: that there is some God, most holy, most just, most powerful, and most wise, who founded and governs this universe; and that he himself is a sinner, guilty of the violated divine law, and therefore subject to heavenly wrath, most certainly to be visited with death and malediction without any hope of pardon. So it must be that God sets all these things before men with this intent, that they should learn that they are certainly and infallibly damned, and that no place is left them either for salvation or for a repentance that would help them." But this speech sins in many ways; and so it happens when a matter is handled from party zeal, not from equity and good. For if it be lawful for learned men to suppose whatever they will — whether it be possible or impossible — without [our] being able to prevent them from inferring from it whatever they would, no one can hinder them. And if I am unwilling to suppose things that surely are not to be supposed, because they include a tacit "begging of the question" [Greek: to en archē, the petitio principii], the whole speech at once collapses. Next, who ever said that this one thing is taught through the works of God? More can be taught, yet so that Christ is not taught; that this God is supremely to be loved and worshiped, his works teach — namely, because they teach that he is God. And there are other heads of corrupt natural theology, as we shall show. Furthermore, those who affirm that God reveals himself through his works, that men may know him as the Creator, Ruler, Governor of all, most powerful, most holy, most just, do not say — nor, if they would be consistent, can they say — that he sets before men all the documents in the works of nature and providence with this intent, that they should learn that they are certainly damned. But, you will say, according to their opinion, as concerns their eternal salvation, sinful men can learn nothing else. Be it so! You will not quickly show that any evil arises from this opinion. For what? Will anyone bring

a suit against God, because he is just, and cannot but be just? Or is an accusation to be brought against him because men are sinners and cannot be ignorant of his right — namely, that those who had committed sins are worthy of death? Or because they have nefariously fallen away from the state in which they could have perceived saving things from that revelation of his? But [you say] he does not set forth his sparing mercy (that is, [the mercy] in the covenant of grace) through the works of creation and providence. What then? Does the very law of God — which contains a much clearer manifestation of God than those other aids and documents of which we speak — declare that mercy? But will any mortal dare, for that cause, I say, to dash himself against the law of God? Does not the apostle everywhere dispute that through the law is the knowledge of sin, but not of the remedy? Supposing, therefore, that a man rightly and diligently discharged his duty in the school of the law, yet he can learn nothing else in it but that he is a sinner, and certainly to be damned. Is the law therefore sin, or is God unjust who gave it? "Let it not be!" [Greek: mē genoito] — and yet as many as are of the works of the law are under the curse.

XII. But they will say: "God grants this manifestation of himself to men with this mind and will — not that it should be despised, but that they should use it rightly unto the knowledge of sin, repentance, and the worship of God according to the nature of those things." That God sets the documents of himself before men with the mind and will that they should be despised — whether that thought has befallen any mortal even in a dream, I vehemently doubt. But that he sets the works of his providence before those who lack the word with this mind and will, that they should use them rightly unto repentance (which he well knows they will never do) — since the learned men with whom we have to do know this also — will scarcely gain credence among those who, by God's benefit, do not lack the word. Nor would the learned men themselves so unwisely dispose their own rules. Next, what they understand by "the mind and will of God" in this question they do not teach accurately enough; but of these things hereafter.

[Part 1 ends here. Section XIII — where some place "the hinge of the controversy," whether any clemency of God toward sinners is manifested in the works of nature and providence — opens Part 2, continuing on p. 61.]

XIII. But some assert that the hinge of this controversy is placed in this: "whether, namely, in the works of nature and providence, any clemency and placability of God toward sinful men has been manifested." But, with their leave, the matter stands far otherwise. We know that some clemency of God toward sinful men is manifested through the works of providence: "For with much clemency he bears the vessels of wrath fitted to destruction" [Rom. 9:22]. But that clemency is not the one which consists in sparing mercy, whose effect is the remission of sins. And without the knowledge of this [sparing mercy], it is impossible that anyone should attain to saving repentance. Next, there is here no mention of the will of God, yet the question is about that alone — not about the nature of the things themselves, nor whether any degree of saving knowledge can be procured from this manifestation (although it would not displease me to treat of those too). But that pious and learned men, translated out of darkness into the marvelous light of Christ, who have purified their souls by hearkening to the truth through the Spirit, can speak so jejunely and languidly about the saving knowledge of God's sparing mercy — I almost marvel.

But in examining the words of the learned men, it is not necessary that I be further troublesome to them. I know that in this cause I am carried away by no party zeal, and am sworn to the words of no master or synod; I will therefore expound the state of the controversy with what faithfulness I can — first setting forth the things in which the parties agree, then the things controverted. I will state the whole matter perhaps more briefly than so great a matter ought to be stated, but, for our design, as I hope, plainly enough; for I have no wish to speak amply and loftily in a cause in which I think only humbly and lowly.
XIV. It is granted, therefore: —
1. That there is some natural knowledge of God, or "implanted knowledge of God" [Greek: theognōsia emphytos], which we have expounded above.
2. That through the works of creation and providence a revelation has been made of the eternal power and deity of God, the Creator and Ruler of all.

3. That whatever men know of God, or can know by force of the revelation granted them, they ought to use all of it to this end: that they may worship, love, and glorify him as God, and show themselves obedient to him in all things.

4. That no pagan — by reason of his native blindness and malice, and the craft of the devil ensnaring them on every side in depraved superstitions and nefarious idolatries — submitted himself to the command of God, or obeyed him, according to the measure of the knowledge which he had, or could have had, of him; and therefore that they were all utterly inexcusable.
5. That Christ the Mediator is not revealed through these means — not, at least, so far that he could be savingly perceived by those who obtained only these means.
6. That the revelation of Christ the Mediator through the gospel is absolutely necessary for obtaining salvation.

7. That, besides all external revelation of God and his will, made by whatever means, there is need of the efficacious power of the Holy Spirit suffusing the mind with saving light, that one may understand spiritual things spiritually, unto the glory of God and his own eternal salvation.
8. That God did not purpose from eternity, in himself, that any of those to whom he grants no other means of salvation besides the works of nature and providence should be saved.
About all these things there is scarcely any question among the orthodox.
XV. The hinge of the whole controversy, therefore, seems to turn on these two questions; for it is asked —

First, "Whether that manifestation of God which he grants, through the works of nature and providence, to sinners destitute of the word, contains in itself any degree of truly saving knowledge, by whose guidance they could arrive at true and God-pleasing repentance, and attain eternal salvation."
Secondly, "Whether God grants this manifestation of himself with that mind and will" (that is, purpose and intention) "that those to whom it is granted

(as it is to absolutely all men) should come to true and God-pleasing repentance, and so attain eternal salvation, although they be destitute of the word." That is: both the sufficiency of the means, and the intention of God, in respect of the salvation of those who lack the word, fall under controversy.

XVI. But it has seemed good to subject briefly to examination some arguments drawn from the Scriptures which appear in the writings of certain men in this dispute. The first is deduced from the Pauline words which we have cited before, from Acts 17:24–26, to which they add some things from ch. 14:16. From these passages, they say, it is established "that God brings forth all the works in nature and the government of nature with this mind, that sinful men should seek him and find him"; and this, they affirm, Paul most expressly testifies. But this affirmation scarcely touches the state of the controversy, much less settles it. What it is by which they try to build up the credit of their opinion from these passages, and then how they weave together their proofs, it remains for us to see.

XVII. First, then, having built up [the claim] that God administered the said aids to men destitute of the word with this mind, that they should seek him and find him, they conclude that in those [aids] are contained all the things necessary to be known, that God may be sought and found. "To seek God," they say, "is not to investigate whether he exists, but to flee to faith in him, and to seek the help of his grace. And to find God is to obtain and procure his sought-for help, and to experience his most holy deity propitious and benevolent." Response: To flee to faith in God is to trust in God; and those who do this are blessed, Ps. 2:12. To seek the help of God's grace includes self-denial and resignation into the grace of God, which always bring forth justification, Luke 18:13, 14. And this is to experience the most holy deity of God propitious and benevolent — which has never befallen anyone except in the remission of sins. And so, by these interpretations of the Pauline words, they seem to intend a saving belief in God, with a living and efficacious faith unto salvation.

XVIII. But how immensely they here enlarge the proposed thesis! Perhaps those who argue thus feel that they have advanced in arguing beyond their hope, so that they cannot restrain themselves enough to stay within the limits of the question as it is commonly proposed by them. For what? Is the manifestation of God through the works of providence to be judged, in the opinion of these greatest men, to contain all the things necessary to be known, that sinful men may flee to God with the whole confidence of their mind, experience him propitious, worship him, and believe in him? Why, then, do the same learned men deny that those works of providence reveal Christ? Or why do they assert that the knowledge of Christ is necessary for attaining living faith and salvation? Nay, if the matter were so, what need is there of the word? Or in what sense shall we say that Christ "brought life and incorruptibility to light through the gospel"?

XIX. But since it has come to this, let us see how they try to establish this opinion from this passage. Upon the construction of the apostolic "passage" [Greek: periochē], and the sense of the words "to seek" and "to find" (God, namely), depends the whole rationale of the argument the learned men use; they prove the mind and counsel of God from that structure of the words: namely, "to seek the Lord" [Greek: zētein ton kyrion], Paul said, for "on account of seeking" [Greek: heneka tou zētein] — that is, in order to proclaim the mind of God who sets his works before sinful men; "by a mode of expression" [Greek: tropō lexeōs], as they affirm, familiar to the Greeks. And they prove that "to seek God" ought to be taken in no other sense than that expounded by them, adducing all the passages of the Old Testament in which mention is made of seeking and finding God.

XX. And thus indeed the most learned men plead their cause; but whether they have grasped the mind of the Holy Spirit, or the force of the Pauline argument, a brief narration of the occasion and sense of the apostle's words will teach. Paul, therefore, when he was at Athens, his spirit being stirred because he saw that city full of idols (Acts 17:16), could not restrain himself but at once — contrary to what seems to have been his first intention — publicly set forth and announced the living God, in opposition to all those "vain" [Greek: mataia] idols by whose religion that most superstitious city was held. And since this was his purpose, he uses an argument most ready and most strong, drawn from the creation and government of all things. But that he might bind the idolaters the more tightly, he teaches them that they are utterly destitute of all excuse in the worship of idols, since these very works of God, which were daily set before them, plainly indicated their author — that God is, and is supreme, good, wise, powerful, and everywhere present; so that it would be of the highest madness to be willing not to know him whom they could not utterly be ignorant of. Nay, he says, that living God, the Creator and Ruler of all, sets the works of his nature and providence before the consideration of all with this mind: that they should seek their author through those aids, "if haply they might feel after him and find him"; so that those who, leaving him — or rejecting into the last place, as unknown, him who alone both is and can be known — betake themselves religiously to things which either are not, and never were, or are vain and foolish, cannot but be both "inexcusable" [Greek: anapologētoi] and "self-condemned" [Greek: autokatakritoi].

XXI. The apostle therefore proves that the gods of the Gentiles are vain idols, but that the true and living God, the one Maker and Ruler of this world, so set himself before them that they could most easily, and with no trouble, know him from a sure consideration of his works, which he daily set before them. If, therefore, they had taken heed, and from the aids of their knowledge which he abundantly supplied them had sought God — as it behooved to be done, and whom they could not deny to be — surely they would have found him, since he was not far from them, nor would they have transferred themselves to mute and vain idols. For the words of the apostle are not to be stretched beyond the subject matter and the occasion of the preaching.
XXII. Let us see further what those apostolic words mean —

"that they should seek the Lord, if haply they might feel after him and find him" — as they seem to regard the counsel and will of God. But since it was most superstitious idolaters with whom the apostle had to do, it appears that the event itself is not intended by them, nor any purpose of the divine will in respect of it (since God is destitute neither of power nor of wisdom for executing his purposes); it is certain that those words denote the very nature and character of the things [themselves], and accordingly that counsel of the divine mind by which he willed to set those things before all men so that they might be sufficient external means — that is, in their own kind — for attaining that proposed end; namely, that they should seek and find the Maker and Ruler of all. God, therefore, sets the works of nature and providence before men destitute of the word with this mind and will: that, following their guidance, they should seek and find their author and the Ruler of all — leaving the false gods, and all that "Lernean hydra" of idolatry which the blindness of the human mind and the craft of the devil had brought into the world; and beyond this, nothing seems able to be made out from those passages. Nay, the apostle really makes no words at all about the will and counsel of God in founding and ruling the world. He proves that the nations could have arrived at that knowledge of him which we have set forth above, through the bare consideration of the works set before them. But about the placability of God, about the remission of sins, about the bosom of mercy, about invocation and prayers, about saving repentance, about the zeal of living holily and rightly (which no one can have without faith), and many other things of the kind which certain learned men labor to carve out of these passages — [there is] deep silence.

[Part 2 ends here. Section XXIII — on the Greek grammar of Acts 17:27 (the particles εἰ ἄραγε, εἴπως), by which the learned men try to make the verbs of "seeking" express God's saving intention — opens Part 3, continuing on p. 65.]

XXIII. But let us see how the learned men strive to prove their thesis from these words of the apostle. First, then, they affirm that Paul said "to seek the Lord" [Greek: zētein ton Kyrion] for "on account of seeking" [Greek: heneka tou zētein]; but since no necessity or reason compels that interpretation of the apostolic speech, it must be reckoned to be said gratuitously. Yet there is no need at all that we stir up a quarrel about that phrase; let us therefore grant the learned men to use their right in tearing these words from the proper place which they occupy in the apostolic discourse (Acts 17:27), and applying them to other words of the same apostle in ch. 14:16 — if only they have the power to do so. Let us grant also that "to seek" [Greek: to zētein] ought to be taken in the same sense in which, in the preceding verse, "to dwell" [Greek: katoikein] is used, and so [grant that it] denotes the counsel of God in creating and ruling the world, in so far, namely, as that world is by its nature apt to manifest him. What then would seem to follow? Namely, that "if haply they might feel after him and find him" [Greek: ei arage psēlaphēseian auton kai heuroien] ought to be expounded in the same way, and so to denote the same intention or will of God. But why so, pray? Namely, [because] "if perhaps" [Greek: ei ara] and "if by any means" [Greek: eipōs] are taken for "in order that" [Greek: hina] and "so that" [Greek: hopōs]. But does this always happen, or only sometimes? I do not think the learned men will say it always happens so, since they can produce only a single example where "eipōs" seems able to be so taken; about "ei arage" they are silent. "Ei arage" perhaps occurs in this one place only of the New Testament; "eige" and "ei ara"

more often — Acts 7:1; 8:30; 2 Cor. 5:3; Gal. 3:4; Eph. 3:2; 4:21; Col. 1:23 — can serve as examples. Let the reader try whether, from even one of those places, he can wring that [supposed] sense and use of these particles. About "eipōs" there is no need for us to be anxious, nor to inquire carefully what Eustathius expounded of its use, since it is not found in our text. But that the apostle expounded the mind, counsel, and will of God in words which denote extreme uncertainty, is hard to believe. The sense of this place, then, is that God, in founding and ruling the world, gave such documents of his power and divinity that through them men could be roused to seek the Maker of all, and also to try their powers of finding him and of distinguishing him from all idols. For God, incorporeal and invisible, left in those works, as it were, traces of himself, such that, by clinging to them, he can be found.

XXIV. But in the sense of the words "seeking" and "finding," some think they have found a firmer support for their cause. That those means of the knowledge of God, of which we speak, are in some respect at least sufficient for seeking God in the way the apostle speaks of, and perhaps for finding him, cannot be denied; for otherwise the apostle must be judged unjust, who demanded it of those who had no other means. But they affirm that "to seek God, and to find him" is "not simply to investigate God, that men may know that he is and what he is" (although those words seem to express nothing else), "but to flee to faith in him, to acquiesce in him, to worship him with invocation and prayers, to embrace him with the whole confidence of the soul, and to experience him propitious; and accordingly that those means supplied to all are sufficient for these ends, and destined by God so to suffice" — namely, for men destitute of the word. But since those words — if only they are not used in another sense than that in which they are used in sacred Scripture (nor indeed does it become a theologian to use any words in a sense that shrinks from our one rule and norm of thinking and speaking about sacred things) — would accomplish the whole work of saving faith, and so would equate those means, as to the thing itself, with the very word of God, which "he has magnified above all his name" [Ps. 138:2], they will doubtless grant that they need the strongest arguments and proofs to establish that "paradox" [Greek: paradoxon]. To confirm this their interpretation of these words, some bring forward all the passages of the Old Testament in which mention is made of seeking and finding God, and affirm that those words, in those places, cannot be taken in any other sense than that expounded by them. And they asseverate that there is no doubt the sense of those words is the same in this place. That there is no doubt is easy to say; but I not only doubt it, I judge it plainly most false; nor can I sufficiently marvel how it could come into anyone's mind to assert it.
XXV. For in all those places

sacred Scripture speaks of God's covenanted people; but in this, only of those who were strangers as to the promises of the covenants. In those, of those who in fact, at least in profession, were faithful; in this, of idolaters. In those, of those who knew God in the word and abominated idols; in this, of those to whom he was utterly unknown. In those, it treats of seeking and finding the favor of the known God through Christ; in this, of God to be sought through the works of nature and providence, that he may be known. In those, it treats of God known, but for sin turned away for a moment in the external dispensations, and [sought] through the promise of finding him propitious in Christ; but in this, of an unknown God to be sought, and to be distinguished from idols. Shall we now think that these words are uttered in the same sense concerning these and concerning those? — concerning those, namely, who, being God's covenanted people, faithful, justified in Christ, instructed in the word, leaning on the evangelical promises, had need to seek the face and favor of God; and concerning those who, destitute of the word and of all revelation of Christ, exiles from the divine covenant, "without God" [Greek: atheoi] in the world, had set before them only the works of nature and providence, that they might know that God is, and that their vain idols were "by nature" [Greek: physei] not God? Nay, far, very far, be even a slight suspicion of this kind from every pious mind.

XXVI. Nor is it said absolutely of those whom the apostle arraigns as guilty of negligence and sloth, for not considering the works of God, that they should "seek" God, but that they should seek him in that [particular] way — namely, "if haply they might feel after him and find him." But whether that could be said of those saints of whom mention is made in the Old Testament, who, leaning on the most certain promises of God in Christ, were wont to approach him, I leave to the reader to judge. It is therefore one thing altogether to seek the favor and grace of God revealed through his word, by force of his promises in Christ; and another to seek an unknown God in the works of nature and providence, "if perhaps he might be found by feeling after him."

XXVII. But since all the theologians who follow what they call the "new method" think the firmest support of their cause is placed in a certain "placability" of God which they assert lies open in the works of providence, it will perhaps seem not useless if, tracing this matter a little higher, I expound in a few words what is to be thought of that placability — which in this disputation fills almost both pages [occupies the whole discussion] — and what the nations themselves, destitute of the word, perceived of that matter. And since, too, in all the arguments of those most distinguished men that placability everywhere meets us, whatever I shall say to dislodge it from its place, abridging [the labor], may seem able to be set down as gain.

XXVIII. The state and condition of sinful men destitute of the word, as concerns the knowledge of God, we have described above. What natural "power" [Greek: dynamis] of knowing God, exerting itself of its own accord in adults, and what "anticipations" [Greek: prolēpseis] and "common notions" [Greek: koinai ennoiai] they retained in that state, we have also shown. And that those connate notions about God can be cherished, increased, and drawn out through the consideration of the works of providence, we have likewise

demonstrated at length. And this manner of knowing God would, in the state of integrity, have abundantly sufficed for men to render all obedience rightly and to attain the reward of obedience. But through the entrance of sin it has been so disturbed that from it sinners hang in doubt of mind, and can have from it no explicit way of worshiping God. On the one hand, they could not but apprehend God as good, bountiful, patient, and gentle — which is also connatural to man. But on the other hand, tightly bound by the consciousness of sin, and by the sense of divine wrath and indignation, or of avenging justice, distracted with anxious care and various thoughts, they had to fluctuate, this way and that, most uncertainly. For if on the one side any hope ever shone, so that they seemed to have ascertained what they might expect from the goodness and bounty of God, on the other side a desperation mixed with horror at once rendered all the counsels first begun perpetually uncertain — so that, all hope being utterly given up (convicted by their own hearts), they were worn out, Heb. 2:15.

XXIX. But to these presumptions, fighting diametrically in the minds of sinners, the works of providence set before them were, as it were, an auxiliary to each hostile party. For while they pondered the rains and the appointed seasons given from heaven for bringing forth fruits, and the other benefits for which they solemnly named the deity "best," they were wont to be of confident mind, to call all things good, to celebrate their author, and to adorn him wonderfully with words. But when they also beheld the wrath of God revealing itself from heaven against the impiety and injustice of men, they could not but perceive that all that hope, with which they had falsely flattered themselves, was at an end.

[Part 3 ends here. Section XXX — that this knowledge regards only God's natural attributes (goodness and avenging justice), while the free acts of God's will, and the "placability" in question, are arbitrary and revealed only in Christ — opens Part 4, continuing on p. 68.]

XXX. But I do not speak only of that which is wont to happen in such a state, since it could not happen otherwise; for that knowledge of God of which we speak regards his natural attributes. So the apostle everywhere teaches us. The free acts of the divine will do not pertain to it, as we shall see. For those attributes are known partly from connate notions, partly from effects. Of any free act of the divine will there is no natural notion or presumption. Goodness, indeed, on the one hand, and what it necessarily posits, and avenging justice on the other, are natural to God. Of these, in all men, there is a certain "anticipation" [Greek: prolēpsis]. But the external effects of these are diverse and contrary. All the works of providence seem bound to be referred, respectively, to these fountains. There is no one, destitute of special revelation, who can refer them elsewhere. Of those things whose going-forth is not necessary, the objects being posited, the account is different. But it depends on God's most free choice that goodness and justice, in respect of their effects, should meet each other and mutually kiss — unless one supposes that God must be said to punish sin, and also to remit it, from necessity of nature. But that the "placability" of which we speak pertains here is plain — [a placability] which in God is wholly arbitrary, or

a most free act of his will, if indeed anything of this kind can be assigned to God: for it properly expresses neither any attribute of the divine nature, nor any act of his will. Driven, therefore, by these contraries, as by the blows of winds, they went off into contrary [directions], according as the one or the other notion prevailed in their minds.

XXXI. For some, changing the goodness of God into a certain perpetual lenity — which is either a vice, or at least set on the border of vices, and unworthy of the supreme Ruler — utterly cast away all thoughts of the wrath and severity of God, charging the foulest crime of superstition upon those who were touched by any care or zeal for them. That very many of the impious are of this mind, Scripture testifies (Ps. 50:21); and most of the wise trod this path, as will be shown later. Some, meanwhile, continually wasted themselves away with fear and a certain foreboding of the wrath and vengeance of God — whom Plutarch most wittily lashes in his little book "On Superstition" [Greek: peri deisidaimonias]. These too the sacred page depicts (Mic. 6:6, 7). There were also those who, relying on old traditions, suspected that the idols they worshiped were placable, and could be appeased by sacrifices — at whom, however, the chorus of philosophers laughed. But that the works of providence suggested to them any such placability of the deity, there is no ground from which even the slightest suspicion could arise; if anyone pleases to suspect it, we cannot prevent him, since suspicion is set in each one's power, but in this matter it lacks all proof. That the custom of sacrificing among the nations must be referred partly to inveterate traditions emanating from God's institutions, partly to the pride and craft of the old serpent wickedly drawing to himself the worship of God, is certain. For the wisest of the Gentiles handed down no other origin of it. That the origins of sacrifices are to be assigned to the one will and most free choice of God, we have taught before; that the knowledge and observance of them should emanate to nations destitute of supernatural revelation from any other fountain than those we have indicated, is impossible.

XXXII. But there were those who, in order to extricate themselves from the snares in which they felt themselves entangled by those contrary notions about God and his nature (which they had conceived from the diverse effects numbered above), feigned for themselves two principles of all things, two gods — one good, the author of all goodness; the other perverse, master of evils and punishments. And this handed-down persuasion still endures in a great part of the common people of America. Hence the Tlaxcallans sent envoys to inquire of the Spaniard Cortés — who was unmanning them with the terror of his cannon — which he was: a god? the good one, who gives fruits? or the bad one, who delights in human blood? — that they might know how he should be received. Nor is that a recently born persuasion; nay, it is very ancient. "Labeo, whom they proclaim most skilled in matters of this kind, distinguishes good deities from bad by a difference of worship; he asserts that the bad gods are propitiated by slaughters and gloomy supplications, but the good by glad and joyful observances," as it is in Augustine, On the City of God, Book 2, ch. 11.

XXXIII. Nor does it seem unworthy of note that, among the ancient idolaters, those who seemed to venerate the deity with some affection of mind above other mortals, relying on the deepest persuasion of his wrath and vengeance, always entered upon a horrible way of appeasing the gods — namely, "human sacrifices" [Greek: anthrōpothysias]. Let the Carthaginians be an example, whom Plautus more than sufficiently intimates to have been led, among the heathen, by the greatest reverence for the deity, when he assigns to the [Carthaginian] he brought on stage no other speech than one that seems to savor of an unusual sense of religion. But "Punic faith" is a Roman proverb; which [proverb], had Hannibal known not only how to conquer but how to use his victory, would have had no place — or, on the other side, would have stained the Roman name itself with no less a blot. But the abominable sacrifices of those same men are most well known.

XXXIV. And indeed, among the nations, the matter stood thus. For since, from the works of nature, nothing at all of placability toward sinners could lie open — inasmuch as those works, [being] absolute before all consideration of sin, were consigned to a plainly different end; and since the works of providence, whatever of goodness or patience they made manifest, they counterbalanced by the disclosure of wrath and indignation; and since the use of sacrifices was introduced only from a detestable tradition (because most foully corrupted) or by the craft of Satan — whatever becomes of that placability, it appears that the nations, destitute of the word of God, were condemned to the darkness of all knowledge of sparing mercy, and of [the mercy] that leads to saving repentance.

XXXV. Furthermore: all revelation of God pertains to some covenant. But there are two covenants between God and men: one of works, the other of grace. The means of revelation of which we speak must pertain to one of these. But if they pertain to the former, namely [the covenant] of works, how can they manifest any placability in God? By the tenor of that covenant, God is not placable. If they are to be referred to the latter, and the works of providence are to be held [as] means of administering the covenant of grace, then they ought to declare not God placable, but God appeased, reconciled, and reconciling the world to himself — if indeed they are to be thought to proclaim the truth itself. The revelation, therefore, of placability in God pertains neither to the covenant of works nor to the covenant of grace. That is, there is none at all. They can indeed reveal the goodness of God, and his patience and longsuffering, which God exercises toward the vessels of wrath; but placability, not so. Let the distinguished men see, then, in what sense they have so often feigned to God that placability which — since it can be said to be in God under no respect — surely can be revealed through no means. If only the heat of disputing should once cool down, and the pious soul should seriously weigh with itself what it is to deal with God in Christ about the remission of sins, ratiocinations of this kind will be found to vanish quicker than the word [is spoken].
[Part 4 ends here. Section XXXVI takes up the second proof-text, Acts 14:16–17 (Paul and Barnabas at Lystra), and opens Part 5, continuing on p. 70.]

XXXVI. Let us proceed to the consideration of another place in the same book — namely, Acts 14:16, 17 — whose whole force, however, such as it might seem able to have in this cause, we have [already] carried over into the preceding argument. The words of the apostles to the Lystrans are these: "Sirs,

why do you these things? We also are men, subject to the same affections as you, announcing that you should turn from these vain things to that living God, who made heaven, and earth, and sea, and all things that are in them; who in past ages suffered all nations to walk in their own ways. Nevertheless he left not himself without testimony, in doing good, giving us rain and appointed seasons for bringing forth fruits, filling our hearts with food and gladness."

XXXVII. After, therefore, they had announced the living and true God, the apostles show what his mind was toward the nations destitute of the word in past ages — that is, in all that time in which he left them without special revelation of himself, before the preaching of the gospel: namely, "he suffered them to walk in their own ways." Then they expound the manner and end of the divine dispensations toward those so left; for "he left not himself without testimony." From each of these two parts of the apostolic speech the learned men labor to fortify their opinion, chiefly from the last. For first they contend that God suffered the nations to walk in their own ways only so far [as this]: that he interposed no prophet's or apostle's preaching against their corruption. Then [they contend] that the testimony which, they affirm, the apostles say the true and living God bore to himself in the works of providence, does not testify of God only that he is, nor only that he is powerful and wise, but also that he is best — and indeed [best] toward sinners, with that kind of goodness and benignity which is called mercy (Luke 6:35, 36). And hence [they say] it follows that, by that testimony of God concerning himself, there was also in some measure laid open his placability and goodness toward sinful men — without which a sinful man cannot be brought to true repentance and the true worship of God.

XXXVIII. But from the things granted by us above on the one hand, and proved on the other — [namely] that unless they here intrude, by no legitimate consequence, mention of the true worship of God, of repentance, and of placability, the adversaries can carve nothing at all out of this place that would seem to favor their cause — a fair reader would easily perceive [the truth]. But that manner of arguing from the testimonies of Scripture does not please me: that, the occasion of the whole speech and the scope of the place being omitted, valid arguments can be drawn from words precisely [taken by themselves], seems hardly [right]; we shall briefly consider both.

XXXIX. Since, therefore, the crowd of the Lystrans, astonished at the miracle of the healing of him who had been lame from the womb, had suspected that Paul and Barnabas were gods — namely Jupiter and Mercury — and had driven the priests to sacrifice to them, the apostles, disturbed by their idol-madness almost no less than [the crowd was] stirred by the divine power that accompanied them, "rending their garments, leaped into that crowd, crying out and saying, Sirs, why do you these things?"
XL. But since the crowd was entangled and implicated in a twofold error, each most pernicious — one, namely, that Jupiter and Mercury were gods,

powerful in miraculous healings; the other, that Paul and Barnabas were that Mercury and Jupiter in disguise — the apostles meet both. And first, to clear their own credit at once, they cry that they are men, subject to the same affections as [the crowd] themselves; then they teach that Jupiter and Mercury, and all that worship which the stupefied crowd wished to render them under those names, are "the vain things" [Greek: ta mataia] — empty, fictitious, impious; but that they announce that living God who made heaven and earth. But since it would have been easy for the crowd to throw back at them, "If there be such a God as you announce, and he alone, so that our deities have plainly no place at all with him, how has it come about that we have hitherto never heard anything of him? and that he took no care to be preached, either to us or to our fathers in past ages?" — that they might meet this inveterate prejudice of the whole world of that time (on account of which the cause of truth was everywhere condemned as a thing unheard of), the apostles teach that this living God, by the dominion he has over all, in past ages suffered the nations (of which those Lystrans were some part) to walk in their own ways — that is, in the vanities of idolatry — and did not call them to the knowledge of himself and to true worship; and so they hint at the "economy" [Greek: oikonomian] of the New Testament (about which they knew they must at once contend, against the Jews also), by which, the middle wall of partition being broken, and every distinction of nations, peoples, and families taken away, God indiscriminately calls all to repentance. But lest by this speech they should seem to have given the Lystrans a handle for excusing themselves — that at least hitherto they had worshiped those idols, since that true God had not been revealed to them, nor could they be obliged to worship one whom they utterly knew not — they subjoin that "correction" [Greek: epanorthōsin] by which they show that [the Lystrans] were in their idolatry altogether "inexcusable" [Greek: anapologētous], namely on account of some revelation of the true God granted them through his works. For although (they say) it pleased him to suffer the nations to walk in their own ways, and not to be called to repentance through his word, yet thus far he always laid himself open to them through the works of his providence, and by that bore this testimony to himself, so that it was not without grave sin and signal folly that they — both these and other nations — turned themselves from him, so revealed, to idols; especially since those very works by which he bore testimony to himself were of such a kind that from them came all the good things of which the nations were made partakers.

[Part 5 ends here. Section XLI takes up what the learned men try to infer from this speech — that the works testify God to be not only wise and powerful but "best" — and opens Part 6, continuing on p. 72.]

XLI. Since, therefore, this is the scope and end of the apostolic speech — namely, to show that God revealed himself through the works of providence [only] so far, in those times in which he suffered the nations to walk in their own ways, that without any cause, and not without gravest sin, they had betaken themselves, leaving him, to vain idols — let us see what the learned men try to carve out of this in support of their cause. They say, then, first: "These documents which are set before the nations testify of God not only that he is wise and powerful, but moreover that he is best." Be it so; from whomever we receive any benefits, him we reckon to be good and beneficent; from whom [we receive] all,

[him we reckon] best. Nor have we ever said that all the nations were so brutalized as not to acknowledge the supreme giver of benefits to be best. The orator testifies the contrary: "Thou Capitoline [Jupiter]," he says, "whom on account of his benefits the Roman people named Best…" — Pro Domo Sua, 57.

XLII. But, they say, these documents prove God to be best toward sinners; and let this too be granted. For how far the nations acknowledged themselves sinners against God, it is not the place here to inquire. But since the apostle himself had not known sin "unless the law had said, Thou shalt not covet" (Rom. 7:7), it is plain that those who were destitute of that law labored under a sufficiently crass ignorance of the nature of sin. But let us grant that the nations knew they were sinners; let us grant that God was good toward them [even when] convicted of sin; what then seems to follow? "Namely, that God is good and gentle and merciful toward sinners; for Christ affirms this goodness toward sinners to be mercy" (Luke 6:35, 36): for after he had said, "The Most High is kind toward the unthankful and the evil," he at once subjoins, "Be therefore merciful, as your Father is merciful." Therefore God is merciful toward sinners; who denies it? — even toward the unthankful; who will gainsay it? — but even toward those about to perish; why not? — since we know that he "with much lenity bears the vessels of wrath fitted to destruction" (Rom. 9:22). For though he is daily provoked by them, yet he does not at once "cast them into Tartarus" [Greek: tartaroi], but heaps them even with many benefits, to be recalled at the appointed time (Hos. 2:9). But this benignity of God is called mercy broadly and improperly, [being] taken for any disposition of doing good; so he is called merciful not only toward men, but toward all his works — namely sheep and oxen and the other cattle of the field (Ps. 145:9, 15, 16). But that this mercy, or benignity, exerting itself in earthly things, or "in the things of [this] life" [Greek: biōtikois] — things which to very many are an occasion and enticement of sinning — is the foundation of true and saving repentance, the apostles neither say, nor is it true, nor can it be proved from their words by force of any legitimate consequence. Of that special mercy whence flows the remission of sins, not one word is ever made in all Scripture where the condition of those who are destitute of the word of God is set forth; for that [mercy] is in Christ alone; but that Christ is revealed through the works of providence, "those on the opposing side" [Greek: hoi ex enantias] deny.

XLIII. Since, then, only that mercy which Jesus Christ expounded from the bosom of the Father is the foundation of saving faith and of repentance, we can grant — without the least prejudice to our cause — that the knowledge of that other [benignity] was set forth through the works of providence; nay, since we have no cause but the one truth, we most gladly grant it. The things that can be added about that apostolic "correction," "Nevertheless he left not himself without testimony," can all be granted in a sound sense; for God did not so suffer the nations to walk in their own ways as to have cast off all care and command of them. Nay, the apostles say,
thus far he always bore testimony to himself through his works, so that it was by no means free for the nations to repudiate the knowledge of him and give themselves to idols.

[Part 6 ends here. Section XLIV takes up the inference the learned men draw from "he suffered the nations to walk in their own ways" — that this means only that no prophet or apostle was sent — and shows how much is contained in that "one thing" God withheld from the Gentiles (Ps. 19; 147:19, 20). It opens Part 7, continuing on p. 74.]

XLIV. But hence they proceed thus: "That Paul said God had hitherto suffered the nations to walk in their own ways signifies nothing else but this one thing: that God had hitherto interposed no prophet's or apostle's preaching against the corruption of the nations; that he had vouchsafed the nations no vocal testimony concerning himself and his affairs, with which honor he had dignified the one nation of the Jews." But this "one thing" is great. All the external means salutary for repentance and salvation are contained solely in that one thing which God did not vouchsafe the nations (Ps. 19:4, 9–11; 147:19, 20). That any saving knowledge of God, any calling to repentance, or saving faith can be had from any other source than that one means, has not yet been proved. But they proceed: "The following words," they say, "altogether signify that God, among those very nations, always testified, by the daily documents of nature and providence itself, what men ought to think about him, and in what manner they ought both to worship his deity and to order the rest of their life. By 'the works of nature,' the works of creation are understood." What those [works] would teach sinners about God, that would lead them to repentance — since they teach, and can teach, nothing else than what was apt to teach in the state of integrity — has not yet been established; let us therefore dismiss that in this disputation. In the works of providence is all the hope [of their argument]. That those [works] taught the nations what they ought to think about God — namely, so far as God laid himself open to them through them — we confess. But by what reasoning will it be proved that the nations could learn, from those same teachers, in what manner they should worship his deity and order the rest of their life? Six hundred things of this kind can be said gratis. For the rest, these things too are said far too loosely.
