Whoever would expound that manner of worshiping God and of ordering one's life, by which the nations could have approached God savingly, will do something worth the labor. That God is; that he is the Creator, Ruler, powerful, wise, the bountiful giver of temporal benefits; and therefore that idols are vain, and all worship of them foolish, inept, insane — this the apostles prove by the testimony drawn from those works of God; but of the true worship of God, and of the right ordering of life, [there is] deep silence among them. Some add: "Since the nations passed by all these admonitions with a deaf ear, and with horrible ingratitude cast off all worship and care of their Creator and Ruler, both best and greatest," the apostle signifies "that God now, more sharply and with a clearer voice, calls them back from vain and pestilent institutions, by the ministry of his apostles." But so far are the apostles from instituting any comparison between that calling which is made through the gospel and that other through the works of providence, that they are utterly silent about the gospel and all its doctrine. Nor indeed is the preaching of the gospel so to be reduced into order with those documents which God sets before men from the works of nature, that

it should be called only a sharper calling, made with a clearer voice — of the same kind of calling, namely. For that calling which is through the revelation of that mystery which was hidden from the ages and generations, and by which God made known what are the riches of that glorious mystery, is immensely distant from that testimony which he bore to himself, as Ruler of all and bountiful giver, through the works of providence. But [it would be only] that calling which is indeed of the same kind and nature as the other, yet differs from it in degree or mode of proposition, that could be called a calling "sharper" than another, and made with a clearer voice. But since the whole foundation of the evangelical calling is set in the laying-open of God the Father and the Son through the Holy Spirit, and almost all its force is set in the declaration of remission, reconciliation, and forgiveness of sins through the blood of the Mediator, the God-man [Greek: Theanthrōpou], and in the efficacious dispensation of the Spirit — all which, and other such things perfecting that calling, were utterly hidden in the bosom of God from the ages and generations in which he suffered men, as destitute of his word, to walk in their own ways — these are in no sense to be equated, through the works of providence (documents set before men), with that [evangelical calling], nor to be placed with them in the same series. And I marvel not a little that those who know what it is to repent savingly, and what it is to believe in God, can speak so.

XLV. They go on, and in perorating thus conclude this argument: "Since, then, that testimony of God which resounds everywhere on earth contains those things which pertained to recalling the nations from their ways, and to leading them across from idolatry to the worship of the one true God, from crimes and outrages to justice and honesty — it is altogether necessary that by that testimony of God there was also in some measure laid open his placability and lenity toward sinful men, without which it cannot be that a sinful man be brought to true repentance and the true worship of God." But this speech sins by a manifold "inconsequence" [Greek: anakolouthēsia]. For about recalling the nations from their ways through that testimony of God resounding everywhere on earth, there is no mention among the apostles; nay, they testify that God permitted them to walk in those ways. That the nations could be recalled from those ways only through the gospel, the apostle here clearly enough hints, and elsewhere openly teaches (Acts 17:18; Eph. 1:10; 3:9–11). It is one thing that God did not leave himself "without witness" [Greek: amartyron] among the nations; another, that he recalled them from idolatry to his true worship. Of the true worship of the true God, in that testimony resounding far and wide, [there is] not a word, because [there is] nothing about Christ, in whom alone we can approach God (Eph. 2:12–15, 18). Next, whence does it appear that God cannot bear such a testimony to himself, by which he renders idolaters inexcusable, unless he also laid open his placability?

XLVI. For what has that placability to do with convincing idolaters of error, madness, and crime? "But by the fact that God bestows all good things on sinners, it appears that he is placable, and shows himself placable to them through those good things." Since all good things are understood in one

kind only — namely, "of [this] life" [Greek: biōtikōn] or temporal — we deny that proposition. God bestows those good things many times on those whom he holds in hatred, toward whom he is endowed with an immutable mind of punishing, whom he has devoted to destruction, whom he has pronounced [must be] destroyed and tormented with eternal punishments (Ps. 73:4–12, 18–20). But those things, from which no one can discern love or hatred, cannot lay open that God is placable (Eccles. 9:1, 2). When God heaps impious men with benefits, that he may render them fattened with their own fat, and fed up for the day of slaughter, is he to be thought to bestow these on them with this mind — that he may show himself placable? That God certainly knew the nations destitute of the word would use those temporal benefits most ill, and could not use them rightly to his glory, is acknowledged. He also appointed them witnesses of his patience and wisdom (Job 38:25, 26). And he very often uses their accumulation to blind men in their sins. How, then, will those [benefits] show him placable — especially since, in their administration, there are present and intermingled innumerable [tokens] of wrath, and most certain indications of the divine "righteous judgment" [Greek: dikaiōmatos] against sinners? But let us grant — what the apostle does not affirm, nor what can be drawn from his words by any legitimate consequence — namely, that the nations could gather from God's works some placability of his toward sinners: are we therefore to think that they were called to true repentance? For since those same nations acknowledged his right — namely, that those who sinned are worthy of death — and could not understand, on account of that right of his, whether any possible way existed of actually appeasing and reconciling him (him whom they suspected to be only in some measure placable), how, pray, are they to be thought, through that knowledge, recalled to true repentance? Nay, that saving repentance cannot be without faith — since it pleases God, which cannot be done without faith — is plain (Heb. 11:6). But faith in God can be only through Christ; such faith, I mean, as is the foundation and companion of saving repentance. But the works of providence do not lay open Christ. Nor is God revealed placable in Christ, but appeased, and reconciling the world to himself (2 Cor. 5:19). There is, therefore, no [such] placability; nor, if there were, could it be revealed through the works of providence; nor, if it were revealed, would it be a foundation of either faith or repentance to any sinner.
[Part 7 ends here. Section XLVII takes up how the nations came to feign their idols "placable" — fashioning gods after their own corrupt image — and opens Part 8, continuing on p. 76.]

XLVII. The nations did indeed feign some of their idols placable. And why should they not feign the deity which they themselves had made to be easily favorable and accessible to them? For since [man] had of his own accord cast away that image of himself by which the true God had created him, and had thereby taken from himself all community with that true God his Creator, he set about to fashion, and as it were to create, new, fictitious gods, after that corrupt image in which he was constituted by his apostasy from the true God; and, entangled in vices, since he was [absorbed] in his delights, he daily set up in his mind a deity fashioned after his own likeness, and more concordant with himself. Hence he experienced nothing in his own nature but that he supposed the same thing forthwith

to have a place in those gods which he had feigned for himself at his pleasure. Hence each one, according to his own genius and disposition, invented them [as] irascible, placable, easy, or stern. But what is this to our God, the Father of the Lord Jesus Christ — "whom no one has seen; that only-begotten Son, who is in the bosom of the Father, he has expounded him to us" [John 1:18]?

XLVIII. There still remains an argument, which I have cast into this last place precisely because, had I not found that it pleased some learned men (carried away, I fear, by party zeal), I would have judged it a puerile sophism, and unworthy to occupy any place in this cause. It is wrung from the first chapter of Paul's Epistle to the Romans, verses 21, 22. "Since," some say, "men did not use 'that which may be known of God' [Greek: tō gnōstō tou Theou] as it became them, they were made inexcusable" — that is, since they polluted themselves with the crime of idolatry (of which the apostle there treats), although God had laid open his eternal power and truth through the works of creation. What then? "If they had used it well, they would not have been inexcusable; if they had not been inexcusable, they would have been excusable; if they had been excusable, they would have been excused." Response: This reasoning would have some plausibility if it had been equally easy for men destitute of the word to use "that which may be known of God" [Greek: tō gnōstō tou Theou] well as to use it ill. But that is presumed gratis, and is a "begging of the question" [Greek: en archē]. And yet the whole of this speech hangs on it. Because it is most certain that men could use "that which may be known of God" [Greek: tō gnōstō tou Theou] ill — and the apostle shows that they so used it — therefore that they could use it rightly is indeed supposed, but most falsely.

XLIX. Next, if they had used it rightly — namely, in that kind of use of which the apostle speaks — how far would they have been excused? So far as to be saved? But about their salvation nothing at all occurs in the apostle; nay, he proves on set purpose that no one, through the use of those means granted to the nations, could ever have obtained salvation without the saving grace of God in Christ. They could indeed have abstained from the foulest crime of idolatry so far that it could not deservedly be objected to them in the way it is here objected by the apostle. Therefore, of this enthymeme — "because they did not use 'that which may be known of God' well, they were made inexcusable; therefore, if they had used it well, they would have been excused" — if the consequence be reckoned legitimate (which, however, is most false, since the account of the ill use and of the right use of the means of salvation, or of the saving knowledge of God, is not the same), there is no other meaning than "they would not have polluted themselves with the crime of idolatry"; for those words, "excusable" and "excused," must necessarily be restricted to the subject matter of which the apostle treats — which is everywhere most true. But they go on arguing: "They would not have been excused," they say, "unless they had fled to the mercy of God; and they would not have fled to him, unless that manifestation under which they lived taught that they must flee to it." Response: They would have been "excused" so far as the apostle treats of them in that place, even though they had not fled to the divine mercy. That they could use that "which may be known" [Greek: tō gnōstō] further, remains to be proved.

L. And these things may suffice by way of a sample — at least that we may see of what kind are those arguments which men learned on every side use in this cause. To one pressing further, both the plan of the present work, and the lucubrations of others recently published in this very cause, stand in the way.

[Here the Digression on Universal Grace, and with it Chapter VI, end. There follows "On Natural Theology, Part Three, Which Concerns Its Total Corruption, Whereby It Became Apostate," beginning with Chapter VII — the next section.]

chapters.heading: On Natural Theology, Part Three: Which Concerns Its Total Corruption, Whereby It Became Apostate

blocks.text: Argument: The unprofitableness [Greek: anōpheleia] of natural theology in the state of sin — The means of its total corruption — The fatal superstition substituted in its place — What the knowledge of God among the nations destitute of the word was like — The corruption of theology through philosophy — The origin of philosophy — The two parts of natural theology — The first utterly corrupted through metaphysics and ethics — The origin, abuse, use, and restoration of ethics, and likewise of metaphysics — Then of astronomy and natural philosophy — Through these, natural theology corrupted.

I. Since the condition of natural theology, vitiated by sin and considered in itself, was such that nothing of spiritual good could ever be expected from it, let us see further in what way, by the craft of the devil and the vanity of the human mind, it was further corrupted — intending to treat, in the next place, of the new theology, and of those whom God enrolled, by its force, into a peculiar people. And here we have determined to transact only the heads of the matters, transferring the investigation of the origin and progress of idolatry elsewhere. That natural theology was corrupted, and well-nigh lost among most mortals, and that in its place, from the remotest records of things and the memory of men, a pernicious superstition was substituted, all know. By what way, and by what means, that fatal and universal metamorphosis was introduced into the world, we shall now narrate in a few words.

II. That some knowledge of God flourished among the nations destitute of the light of the divine word — emanating from that twofold fountain of which we have spoken, namely the internal natural light, and that revelation of him which is made through the works of God — we have shown and proved. That the event corresponded to those principles, Josephus, Justin Martyr, Athenagoras, Theophilus of Antioch, Origen, Clement of Alexandria, Athanasius, Theophilus of Alexandria, Chrysostom, Eusebius, Theodoret, Tertullian, Lactantius, Arnobius, Augustine, and others among the ancients, long since made plain, from the testimonies of the learned scraped together on every side from among those [nations]. Thomas [Aquinas], Steuco of Gubbio, Raymond Sebond, Ludovicus Vives, Mornay, Charron, Fotherby, Vossius, Stuck, Giraldi of Ferrara, Grotius, and writers of every kind in later ages, did the same. The works of Orpheus, Homer, Plato, Xenophon, Aristotle, Hesiod, Plutarch, Epictetus,

Arrian, Cicero, Seneca, Plotinus, Iamblichus, Proclus, Philostratus, and others, which still survive, prove the same clearly. The monuments and histories of all ages, nations, and peoples agree. For let us grant that "the many" [Greek: tous pollous] were always so hedged about with the veil of ignorance and darkness that they never even thought about the supreme deity, or the obedience due to him according to right reason; and granted that "the refined" [Greek: hoi charientes] were so empty of divine things that they held them in contempt: yet of "the wise" [Greek: sophōn] at least, and "the contemplative" [Greek: theōrētikōn], in whom a care of investigating the natures of things a little more deeply, and of advancing the "common notions" [Greek: koinas ennoias], had settled, the account is different. That these, while the first effort of struggling nature still endured, expounded many things excellently and elegantly about God and his worship, the obedience due to him, and the ultimate end and felicity of men, is well known. Let us see, then, how, and by what means, this theology was utterly corrupted, until it ended in a horrendous and total apostasy from God — in the monstrous hydra of idolatry and of the most impure vanities. And this evil of defection and of human misery seems to have flowed from a twofold fountain. For men, left to themselves and their own ways, either studied to advance the remnants of natural theology — and by that effort, from their native blindness, vitiated them the more — or set about, on set purpose, to corrupt them further. That each attempt succeeded perniciously enough is plain from the event. But the first was the nobler, and worthier of creatures endowed with reason. Of it, therefore, we shall treat in the first place.

III. That sinful men, destitute of all supernatural revelation, retain a natural power of knowing God, exerting itself of its own accord in adults, we have proved. We have also before ascribed to them a general knowledge distinguishing the honorable from the base, in relation to God and his government and decretory judgment — a knowledge of which, without the destruction of the practical intellect, they could not be utterly deprived. But from the perpetual unquiet and fluctuation of conscience by which they were pressed, and which those "common notions" [Greek: koinai ennoiai] begot, they easily perceived that this knowledge, or theology, did not suffice for them to attain their ultimate end. Instructed, therefore, by the benefit of the revelation of the eternal power and divinity of God through the works of creation and providence (which we have shown was also left to them), they strove with the utmost force to advance those innate principles. That they were themselves, for the most part, ignorant why they did this, is plain from the event. Instructed in the means of the intellect's operations, they set about to prove, as if by groping, what could be produced from them. And this is the origin of philosophy. Philosophy, I say, is the effect — and, as it were, the offshoot [Greek: surculus] — of that residual theology which the first man had connate in the state of integral nature, enlarged by the aforesaid revelation through the works of God. "To seek God by groping, who is not far from each one of us," to search out the "testimonies" of which he suffered himself not to be without in his works, and to weigh with the mind the natures of all things by which the "invisible things" of God are perceived — with the knowledge of all which

the first man, as necessary to the worship of God the Creator, was full — this, in short, is to philosophize. In this way the so-called "wise men" [Greek: sophoi] of the earlier ages tried to elucidate and advance those principles mentioned above, that thereby the mind of men might rise up to the origin and primeval state of those principles.

IV. That philosophy had no other origin, no other end, is certain. The Greeks, as though they had first cultivated it, always boasted of themselves most magnificently. Doubtless ineptly. Diogenes Laertius, in the proem of his work, taunting those who dared to say that "the work of philosophy began from the barbarians" [Greek: to tēs philosophias ergon apo barbarōn arxai], says: "They unawares attribute to the barbarians the achievements of the Greeks, from whom not only philosophy, but even the human race, took its beginning" [Greek: lanthanousi autous ta tōn Hellēnōn katorthōmata, aphʼ hōn mē hoti ge philosophia, alla kai genos anthrōpōn ērxe, barbarois prosaptontes]. Equally [absurd], no doubt.

V. Tatian, Clement, Theophilus, Eusebius, and others prove the contrary, clearer than light. There are also manifest traces of theft. For from the Chaldeans, Egyptians, Indians, Magi, Syrians, and Jews, there is nothing they did not borrow. But that the ancient Greeks much advanced the study of a philosophy devised by others cannot be denied. The things they had received by report, the things they had filched from the monuments of the Orientals, the things they had learned from living teachers by far travels undertaken for that end, and the "common notions" [Greek: koinas ennoias] about God and his works, about the base and the honorable, which they themselves had observed with nature as guide and companion — these very many of them labored to vend among themselves, first by living voice under the title of wisdom, then in writings marked [with the name] of philosophy. But I know not what an Iliad of evils accompanied the endeavor of these men from its very cradle. For the philosophers themselves, as they wished to be called, were for the most part rascals of proven lust; especially so servilely and openly greedy of "empty glory," and such show-offs, that they were themselves commonly a laughingstock and a byword.
VI. "They ceased to be upright when they turned out learned," says Seneca. And by Timon they are all, to a man, called —
"Wineskins full of empty conceit" [Greek: keneēs oiēseōs empleoi askoi]

— "skins full of vain opining (or of arrogant self-esteem)." And Euripides: "I hate the sophist who is not wise for himself" [Greek: misō sophistēn hostis ouch hautō sophos] — Fragment (uncertain) 111. For scarcely one out of very many was there who ever gave any specimen of true wisdom in human life. With these agrees that [saying] of Anaxippus the comic poet, in Athenaeus: "Alas, do you philosophize? But the philosophers I find to be wise only in words; in the doing of things I see them to be witless." [Greek: oimoi philosopheis? alla tous ti philosophous en tois logois phronountas heuriskō monon; en tois dʼ ergois ontas anoētous horō.]

VII. Ineptly, therefore, and proudly and contumeliously toward God, does Epictetus boast of these slaves of "vainglory" [Greek: kenodoxias], ch. 71: "The position and express image of a philosopher is to expect all utility and harm from himself" [Greek: philosophou stasis kai charaktēr pasan ōpheleian kai blabēn ex heautou prosdokan] — namely, he would have every philosopher be a god. The Brahmans among the Indians also openly boasted themselves to be gods. For when Apollonius inquired what estimation they had of themselves, their chief, Iarchas, answered "that they thought themselves gods." But in vain. In what place and at what price they ought to be, and to be held, Amithocrates, king of the Indians, judged more rightly, who (as Athenaeus, Deipnosophists, Book 14, witnesses) requested by letter from King Antiochus that there be sent to him sweet wine, dried figs, and a sophist — all bought with his own money.

VIII. It is shameful to relate what the same Athenaeus records (Book 11) about Plato himself. Let them, if they please, expect all things from themselves, provided others expect nothing from them. But of whatever sort they were, that they either lost or corrupted the true use and end of the common notions about God, about the true and the honorable, is plain from the event. That those [notions] should be the rule and index of human life, in respect of the obedience due to God and of that natural worship of him; that [they should be] means for obtaining the blessed condition in the enjoyment of God himself — it does not appear ever to have come into their minds. Hence, from that obscure and thin report or knowledge of the immortal state which they had retained, there flowed to the ordering of life according to right reason either no efficacy, or the very slightest. These, then, were they through whom all the remnants of natural wisdom were turned into empty sophistry. Of the attempt of certain Platonists, after the preaching of the Christian religion, to restore philosophy to its former place — as we shall see later — the account is different.

IX. The restoration, therefore, of the original theology, collapsed and lost through the entrance of sin — and no other end — philosophy had "from the beginning" [Greek: apʼ archēs]. And by this way the human race set about repairing theology.

X. But that there were two parts of that primeval theology has been shown above. The natural knowledge of God, and of his will concerning the obedience and worship due to him in that state of things; and the knowledge of the works of God, directing and goading the theologians to worship rightly the supreme God, the Creator of all — these completed it.

XI. Since, therefore, philosophy raised itself up — the rubble of theology being thrown down — to the same ends which that theology had set before itself, it strives to do vicarious service to sinful men, or else it does nothing at all. For first the "contemplative" [Greek: theōrētikoi] men, who first sweated in raising and polishing it, thought this task was given them: to cherish, draw out, and rouse the common notions and "anticipations" [Greek: prolēpseis] about God and his will, together with the connate dictate discriminating the accounts of good and evil, so that by their guidance, if it could be done, they might enjoy God.
[Part 1 ends here. Section XII begins to show how the first part of this philosophy — metaphysics and ethics — utterly corrupted natural theology; it opens Part 2, continuing on p. 81.]
XII. That such was the first attempt of those who philosophized among

the nations destitute of divine revelations, and [that it was] destined to that end which we have mentioned, appears not only from the nature of the thing, but from all the traces of their design and purpose that occur everywhere. That whole ancient wisdom, which Pythagoras and others investigated through Egypt and the Eastern parts of the world, consisted in the method of establishing religions and in the ceremonies of divine worship. Hence [its priests] were held [to be] the stewards of it. So, to Telesinus, asking what his wisdom or philosophy was, Apollonius — a follower of the ancient wisdom — answered, in Philostratus, Book 4, ch. 13: "[A wisdom] proceeding from divine inspiration [Greek: theiasmos], and such as may teach in what manner one ought to pray to the gods and to sacrifice to them" [Greek: theiasmos, kai hōs an tis euchoito kai thyoi]. Philosophers who were not priests, Greece first brought forth — after the new corruption of philosophy, of which we shall soon treat. That that whole philosophy savored of idolatrous superstition, I confess; but yet [a superstition] which retained certain obscure images and traces of truth.

XIII. To this excellent attempt, therefore, the event did not correspond. By its power and auspices natural theology was not restored — nay, rather corrupted. Whatever of philosophy anywhere [exists], drawn from the monuments of the most ancient and still surviving, testifies this. For the natural light — or what is left of it — the innate vanity of the human mind led variously around, in the investigation of the true, until, plunged into the quicksands and marshes of empty curiosities, infinite brawls, and useless speculations, it was well-nigh extinguished.

XIV. For after it pleased certain acute men, endowed with subtle wit — the consideration of the primary end of all this philosophy being set aside — to bind the universal knowledge of things, and the account of the obedience due to the deity, artificially to certain methods of teaching; and to compress them into the snares, fetters, and manacles of certain sciences, or rather "arts" [Greek: technōn]; and to load them with the chains of empty notions, subtle terms, and arbitrary signification — art shortly twisting nature elsewhere, away from its primitive design — this knowledge gradually ended in a certain ethics (a methodical doctrine of virtues and vices) and in metaphysics; of which we must treat in a few words.

XV. But since each of these doctrines — both ethics, which Cicero calls "the part of philosophy that pertains to morals," and metaphysics, which by some is called natural theology (although it exhibits a much lighter shadow of it than ethics does) — formed according to the norm of Aristotle's doctrine, has so obtained dominion everywhere over the judgments of learned men that it has not only occupied a conspicuous place in Christian schools for some ages now, but has also utterly mingled itself with that theology which is publicly taught in them, it is to be handled by me only courteously, and almost "with fine-linen words" [Greek: rhēmasi byssinois; that is, with kid gloves]. For I would not irritate the learned hornets, or stir up against me the fiery and eternal disputers; it is long since I have been thoroughly wearied of all controversies — especially

do I sorely dread lest I should fall upon those who have their wit so ready in cash that they can, at any hour, on any subject, produce fifty or a hundred arguments (most of them futile), and are well-nigh wholly transformed into syllogisms. And the race of men is irritable. But nothing, that I know of, hinders me from thinking it free for me to expound, in a few words, what I myself think about those arts, and about the place they hold in the schools. First, then, I shall subject ethics, then metaphysics, to examination.

XVI. Ethics is the science of morals. It was once a part of natural theology — namely, that [part] which prescribed the manner of living to God according to the norm of the law of creation, and the mode and rule of performing one's duties toward oneself and others. That the remnants of that knowledge clung to the human mind after the entrance of sin has been shown. To rouse those [remnants], to bring them forth, to elucidate them by reasoning, to distribute them into various classes, was the labor and work of the philosophers in ethics. When they had been brought forth, collected, and (such was his dexterity of wit) enlarged by him, Aristotle — that highest and admirable craftsman of compacting general notions into a systematic body — reduced them into a disciplinary science. But since both he and others who preceded him in that work were utterly ignorant of the true God and of the observance due to him, they moved out of place the whole account that discriminates the honorable from the base (which consisted of the remnants of natural theology), and twisted it to a plainly different end than that for which it was originally instituted by God. For which of the philosophers ever undertook to handle, or to teach, the doctrine of virtue and vice, of the base and the honorable, with this end: that it should be the norm of living to God, or a means by which men could at length, in a blessed condition, enjoy him eternally? Nay, that the knowledge of virtue and of moral duties should be in that very place, price, and degree, so as to have the same end in [this] philosophy which it obtained in the state of integral nature, was both impossible, and God himself denied it by the pact of the new covenant. Since, therefore, that doctrine of morals, or ethics, which Aristotle cultivated, seems at length to have been received, as if by "postliminy" [the right of return], into the church, let us see in a few words in what place it is to be held, or what use or end we can assign to it.

XVII. (1) That the end for which this doctrine — which in the state of integral nature was "inward" [Greek: endiathetos] — was designed was no other than to be a principle directing the minds of men in the worship and obedience due to God, that, walking before him according to the law of creation, they might at length live blessedly with him, we have proved before. If anyone will acknowledge that man was made for the glory of God, and capable of moral dependence on him, he will not, I think, deny that this was the end of the knowledge of right and wrong. But to which of the philosophers can we suspect that this came into mind? Nay, what Christian either ought, or has dared, to teach ethics to this end? For would not this be to bring wretched sinners again under the dominion of the natural covenant, where they must perish eternally? Therefore:

XVIII. (2) Let us grant that those who either learn or teach Aristotelian ethics do not intend that end; tell me [cedo] that other end at which they aim with their eyes. The end of moral science can be nothing but practice. But the end of the practice of moral duties is nothing but the glory of God, and our own enjoyment of him. Let that, then, be the end of ethics; what then afterward? Nothing — except that we wretched manikins are, by its help and benefit, brought again under the law of creation, as was said before. But we are far deceived; another end will be given — namely, that, instructed by this doctrine, we may pass our political life here, in the midst of the human race, rightly and usefully. Is it so indeed? But who is endowed with that authority, that he can pervert, at his pleasure, that doctrine which God instituted for one end, to another? Besides, neither is this another end; for no one ought to pass his life among men piously, soberly, usefully, except that thereby he may obey and live to God. Inasmuch, therefore, as it has departed from theology, and prepares its return into the church, this ethics pertains to the covenant of works.

[Part 2 ends here. Section XIX presses the point that true and solid knowledge of virtue and duty is revealed more plainly in Scripture than in all the philosophers — with the memorable image (from Philostratus) of birds taught to mouth human words they do not understand. It opens Part 3, continuing on p. 84.]

XIX. (3) But, after all, what need is there of this — whether theology or philosophy? That a true, solid, sincere knowledge of virtue, of vices, of the base, of the honorable, of all moral duties, is revealed and delivered more plainly and more fully in the sacred Scriptures than is contained in all the writings of all the philosophers, or in their archetype (namely, the remnants of the "inward" [Greek: endiathetos] theology), no Christian will deny. I mean the things themselves; for the "exactness" [Greek: akribeia] of notions, and the comprehension of the harmony of terms by which the knowledge of things is delivered "technically" [Greek: technikōs], are of no account with me — except in so far as I judge them bound to be set down as the greatest hindrances to true wisdom. One may see well-nigh innumerable men daily who, though they know all the definitions, divisions, distinctions, terms, and notions by which some science is delivered — and so the whole system of the doctrine — as [well as] their nails, so that they never triumph more seriously than when it comes to disputations, are yet really raw in the things themselves, and most learnedly stupid. Learned, indeed, in the manner in which Philostratus relates that Euxenus, the master of Apollonius, was instructed in the Pythagorean philosophy: "He knew the opinions of Pythagoras," he says, "just as birds, taught by men, [speak human words]; for the birds sometimes utter 'hail,' and 'fare well,' and 'Jupiter be propitious,' and such things, neither knowing what they say, nor disposed [in goodwill] toward men, but only [wagging] the tongue" [Greek: tas Pythagorou doxas eginōsken, hōsper hoi ornithes manthanousi para tōn anthrōpōn; to gar chaire, kai to eu pratte, kai Zeus hileōs, kai ta toiauta hoi ornithes euchontai oute eidotes ho ti legousin, oute diakeimenoi pros tous anthrōpous, alla [monon] tēn glōttan]. Nor indeed do very many seem to be wise, from the discipline of the sciences, otherwise. Nowhere does this vanity of the human mind appear more than when it is engaged in this moral science. The unjust, the rapacious, the drunken, the irascible, the cowardly — all are imbued, or can be imbued, with this science;

for not virtue, but the shadow of virtue; not duties, but the mask of moral duties, fill both pages in it. I would dare to say that not one true virtue is truly and certainly taught in all the Aristotelian books to Nicomachus; nor will anyone ever, from their doctrine, turn out just, good, or truly "earnest" [Greek: spoudaios], but [only] a masked hypocrite.

XX. (4) I will say, then, what the matter is. God placed the whole doctrine of duties, of virtue, of the observance of right reason, in another place, and another order, under the covenant of grace, than that in which it was disposed under the law of nature. But those natural notions, from which ethics arose and emerged, regard only that first, now-abolished order (described above). There is now no virtue but that which is graciously granted to the faithful by force of the new covenant. No moral act is good and useful to the agent unless it proceeds from a new principle, or spiritual habit graciously infused into the heart. And the reason is that God constituted all moral duties, of whatever kind they are, and all abstinence from vices, [to be] a part of the obedience which he exacts under the new covenant. Nor are virtues to be taught seriously otherwise (the school is child's play) if we wish to be Christians. That anyone be instructed in the knowledge of the general nature of virtue, but not at the same time in the knowledge of supernatural grace and of its respect to Christ the Mediator; or that anyone be provoked to elicit acts of virtue, without being at the same time taught whence the powers for performing them are to be expected by him — this I judge plainly impious. Moral duties are to be performed; acts of virtue to be exercised; we must be just, sober, temperate, brave; but only under the account of the obedience which God requires of us in the covenant of grace. The nature of virtue is to be taught, all moral duties explained, their practice assiduously urged, the knowledge of sin and the hatred of vices seriously inculcated; but that all these should regard Christ the Mediator, the Holy Spirit, the covenant of grace, and be the obedience due to God, is in no way to be omitted. But he would be inept who, in explaining that doctrine of morals which now obtains in the schools, should throw in mention of these or the like. And what darkness, what fluctuation and uncertainty, what confusion of things and notions, a doctrine so diverse about the same subject and the same things can, and is wont to, generate in the minds of men, anyone may conjecture.

XXI. (5) Furthermore: the doctrine of this moral philosophy — especially that which, from the genius and method of its author, now obtains everywhere — is false in many things, and corrupt, uncertain, utterly useless, nay, even pernicious. For: (1) It includes the end of virtue within the limits of this earthly life; which is nothing else than to destroy the whole nature of virtue, and to eradicate virtue itself, and to bind all its students with the horror and desperation of that great Brutus, crying, "O wretched virtue, thou art nothing else but a name!" [Greek: Ō tlēmon aretē, hōs sy mēden allo ei plēn onoma!]. (2) It teaches that the sufficiency of moral powers, for all that exercise of virtue

which is necessary to obtain its proper end, is in the power of all men; which opinion contains an open denial of the whole gospel. (3) It teaches man to trust himself, to expect all blessedness from himself — which is also the sum of Epictetan wisdom, and the highest contempt of God. (4) It asserts virtue to be a certain mean between extremes, than which nothing more inept can be feigned. (5) It proposes or acknowledges no certain, stable, universal rule of virtue: that which pleases the many, which pleases the wise, which most smiles upon those uncertainly divining, is virtue. (6) It completes moral duties without any respect to grace, justice, or divine providence. (7) It denies that there can be friendship between God and men. (8) It is ignorant of eternal reward and punishment.

XXII. But I do not wish to thrust myself into the midst of the controversies of learned men; I will only add: if this moral philosophy still seems to good and learned men [fit] to be retained in its place — which for my part I cannot but wish eliminated beyond the bounds of the church — I greatly desire that two evils be diligently met. One, namely, that the minds of the young be not occupied with prejudices against the purity, simplicity, and indeed the whole mystery of the gospel; how this can be done, when they are delivered into the form of this doctrine, I plainly know not. The other, that the principles on which it rests do not gradually creep into the theology of the schools — unless these things are thought to be said too late.

XXIII. And I indeed favor the attempts of those who have endeavored to forge new hypotheses in this moral science. For it must be confessed that they gave those enslaved to Peripatetic philosophy occasion to inquire further into the natures of these things. Whether they have attained the truth itself, let the judgment be with others. Nor, on account of the use of one or two obscure little words in Aristotle, are those to be said to embrace the more an opinion set forth to the human intellect, who [in fact] repudiate it. That man may dwell in tranquillity of mind and joy, without the stings of his conscience — this a certain learned man, in a book he published on duties, contends [to be] the end of moral philosophy. But we know, and that learned man knows, that such a state of mind can by no means be obtained by any performance whatever of duties. For we are sinners, and can be made partakers of no true tranquillity or joy except through the blood of Christ. From the entrance of sin, no other way to peace or tranquillity has been set forth. Further, to obtain this end, that same learned man bids each one studiously observe the laws and decrees of his own nature. But that this means by no means suffices for attaining that end, all Christians know; that there is some law of nature, we contend [together] with the learned man. But since human nature itself is so corrupted and vitiated through sin that it can neither
understand nor correct its own vice, we must necessarily deny that a law which obtains in it alone is fit for the use which is pretended.

XXIV. There are very few things which I please to add separately about metaphysics. The things we have said about the origin and corruption of ethics are all common to it also; there is no need to repeat them. But this science I judge plainly useless in human life, nay, harmful. For let there first be taken away those things which pertain properly and necessarily to logic on the one hand, and those which [pertain] to theology on the other; what remains will be nothing but a laborious and useless farrago of terms, notions, words, and abstractions, which will never make any man better, more learned, wiser, or more fit for his duty toward God or others. Let those who seem most exercised in it consult the caskets of their own breasts, and say sincerely whether, by its benefit and help, anything of piety, probity, wisdom, prudence, or any true doctrine ever accrued to them. Why, then, is there further need to fix a cross upon wretched manikins — who daily complain that life is short, art long — with this thorny farrago of useless and perplexed notions, to be handed over to oblivion most speedily, if they wish to be wise?

XXV. Next, the mixture of this science with supernatural theology — at the contemplation of which Scotus once exclaimed that at last "the theological doctors had mixed philosophy with theology with the greatest fruit" — brought well-nigh ruin upon evangelical truth. The fetters and chains of metaphysical notions and terms, spiritual things would not bear: the liberty also and amplitude of that spiritual intellect, which God freely gives us for rightly understanding the mystery of the gospel, perishes, while it is ensnared in the nets of this secular wisdom (for so it is, if indeed it ought to be called wisdom). The noble, sublime, spiritual, heavenly evangelical truth, full of a certain divine savor — bound by the rules of this science, and delivered in the words which it teaches — is rendered sterile, dry, thorny, jejune, meager, philosophical, and is cast down from its heavenly beauty and spiritual sweetness. The mystery of the gospel, and that doctrine which is according to godliness, has in it something higher, greater, more divine, than that it ought to be confined within the lattices of human wisdom. Since, therefore, [if] I wished to perpetuate theological quarrels, and to lead the souls of men away from the simplicity of the gospel, I would not hesitate to commend this cause, matter, and instrument of brawls to students. What Prudentius once sang, in his Apotheosis, of certain ancient philosophers, fits no less the innumerable theologians puffed up with this vain wisdom:

"They assail the state of almighty God with calumnious lawsuits; they dissect the faith with minute quibbles, each one as he is the more worthless in tongue; they loose and bind the chains of questions through plaited syllogisms."
[Part 3 ends here. Section XXVI turns to the corruption of natural theology through the other parts of philosophy — astronomy and natural philosophy — and opens Part 4, continuing on p. 88.]

XXVI. But we must return to the way. We have the origin, progress, and abuse of ethics and metaphysics. To use the remnants of the "inward" [Greek: endiathetos] theology for their original end, and accordingly to rouse, ventilate, and advance them, contemplative men had set out. Thence emanated all the doctrine of the nations about vices and virtues, and the contemplations about the nature and perfections of God. But, forgetting the origin, end, and scope of those contemplations, men sophistically learned and acute produced those arts which we cultivate under those names; and these are, in themselves indeed, certain masks of wisdom, but, mixed with supernatural theology, pernicious.

XXVII. Furthermore: since the works of God make manifest "that which may be known" of him [Greek: to gnōston], sinners, diligently and accurately scrutinizing their natures, sought to find by groping the very Lord and Creator of all. And this was the second part of natural theology. This too the human wit, laboring under its native vice, attempted to cultivate. To seek God himself from the works of God — both of creation and of providence — that was the inward [thing] which perpetually urged them. But the matter fell out the same way. For, the vanity of mind under which they universally labored growing strong, in natural philosophy and astronomy this attempt too gradually — and learnedly — vanished.

XXVIII. This, I say, was the origin of astronomy, and of that vanity which is called astrology. The contemplation and knowledge of the beauty, order, and course of the heavens, the stars, and all the heavenly bodies, was a part of natural theology. From it all men ought to have learned the magnitude, infinite power, wisdom, and goodness of the Creator, and so to have been roused to his natural worship. After the entrance of sin, and the universal apostasy of the human race from the true God, that contemplation gradually fell everywhere into disuse, and the science born of it vanished. For very many, being wise only in earthly things, had cast off all care of the things that were above them. Others were enticed by the craft of Satan into the worship of the stars. Nay, the stolid and crass contemplation of the order, course, and beauty of the heavens, which strike the external senses, was to those who had cast off the knowledge and discipline of the living God an incitement to idolatry, as we shall afterward prove at length. So at last, among many, the heaven itself was the first — if not the only — God. And the very name "God" [Greek: Theos] seems to be derived from no other source than "from running" [Greek: apo tou thein], from the course of the stars. Among those pursuing these extremes, there arose at length certain theoretical men who gave themselves to the contemplation of the heavenly bodies for plainly other ends. For they had it in mind neither to pursue them with religious worship, nor, from the knowledge of them, to investigate the living God and obey him: they seem to have taken this care upon themselves only that they might satisfy the innate desire of knowing the causes and natures of things. For when they observed that the heavenly bodies were presented to the eyes with a certain incredible variety and change of appearing things, [they investigated] what order they kept among themselves, and what were the hidden

causes and reasons of those "phenomena" [Greek: tōn phainomenōn]. From the observations and conjectures of such men — certain fictitious terms of arbitrary signification being employed in their explanation (for most of the things on which it rests are fictitious), which they tried somehow to apply to the things themselves — arose that art which we call astronomy; "the gold," namely, "was cast into the fire, and there came out this calf" [Exod. 32:24]. In the contemplation, indeed, of the heavens, the stars, all the heavenly bodies — in the investigation of the nature, order, course, influence, and law put into them by the Creator of all — I judge labor most excellently bestowed. For the bare and common sight of these things, which fall at once upon the eyes of all, strikes the minds of men forthwith with a certain silent admiration of the infinite wisdom and power of God. Far more efficacious is that contemplation which is joined with serious meditation and skillful investigation. And it were to be wished that the knowledge of the heavenly bodies, and of the perennial law by which they are driven — so far as anything certain can be known of it — were communicated to more [people] than it is nowadays. Yet some things, without anyone's offense (as I hope), it is necessary, adhering to my proposed design, that I add; I will say it briefly. As it is now taught, and has for some ages now obtained the right of a science among the learned, astronomy has lost its place, order, and well-nigh all its usefulness. Natural theology being removed, in respect of the end of true theology, how, pray, could astronomy guard its place? What part of it was it, if we men are created for the glory of God? Either, therefore, it is restored to another place through Christ (in whom all things are to be restored) under the new covenant, or it must be utterly cast off. Nay, that the whole "encyclopedia" [Greek: enkyklopaideia] of the sciences, directed to a new end by new means, ought to be subordinated to the glory of God in Christ, he does not deserve to be called a Christian who will gainsay it. But what that restoration of the science, or knowledge, of natural things — of the works of creation — is, [the restoration] made for the use of men sincerely worshiping God in Christ, which has been made and ought to be made; and what place all science has in supernatural theology, or [the theology] of revelation — especially this skill of the stars and their courses — is a longer history than I have leisure to narrate, or perhaps am sufficient to expound.

XXIX. In this manner, therefore, by an attempt truly vain, the human race strove somehow to restore that primeval theology of which we have treated above, and, in restoring it, lost it. This was the effort of the rational creature to extricate itself from the curse of ignorance and blindness incumbent [on it] because of sin; by which it effected nothing else than that it should be further miserably entangled. For since we are renewed to the image of God only in Christ, and that theology was an illustrious part of that image, it appears that every attempt to restore it, without that renewal, is utterly in vain. Hence, therefore, it came about that those who — being without that renewal, or ignorant what place philosophy ought to occupy in it — nevertheless set about to polish and teach it, twisting it from its proper

end and use, profited scarcely anything more than to fix a cross upon themselves and others with a farrago of thorny and useless questions and disputations. And so, from this first endeavor, natural theology appears not so much further corrupted as lost. Its total corruption, therefore, and how it ended in heathenism, is to be further investigated. But here let us see what sinners did amiss on set purpose.

[Here Chapter VII ends. Chapter VIII follows — "On traditions: their origin, use, and corruption; the attempt and success of the poets in corrupting natural theology…", treating Kronos/Chronos and Noah, the origin of human sacrifice, the lawgivers, political and natural theology, and the total destruction of the theology of the Gentiles — the next section.]

Argument: On traditions: their origin, use, and corruption — The attempt and success of the poets in corrupting natural theology — The horrendous calamity of the human race from ignorance of the true God — Kronos, Chronos, and Noah — The origin of human sacrifice [Greek: anthrōpothysias] — On the lawgivers — Political theology [Greek: theologia politikē] — The philosophers — Natural theology [Greek: theologia physikē] — On superstition — The contempt of all religions among the wise — The total destruction of the theology of the Gentiles.

I. As to how philosophy played its part in corrupting natural theology — or rather, was itself corrupted — I have expounded. There were also other causes of the total "apostasy" [Greek: apostasias] from all true knowledge of God. To narrate these here briefly — although they properly pertain to the corruption of religious worship, of which [we must treat] later, in the investigation of the origin of idolatry — has seemed good [to do] in a few words. And here, on set purpose, the human race fell away from natural theology. For whatever help God granted to sinners for obtaining the knowledge of themselves, of the right, and of the true, all of it the blindness of the human heart twisted to its own destruction.

II. That the knowledge which the most ancient mortals had of God was increased and heaped up by the traditions of those revelations which some had received from God through their parents, is plain. For when that [antediluvian] age was finished by the flood — in half whose span the common parent and instructor of all was present — from Noah and his sons there flowed, to their grandsons and to those born of them, no small report of divine things and knowledge of God: no one can doubt it. How far the efficacy of that knowledge extended, while the traditions flowed unsullied from the fountain, it is not the place here to inquire.

III. That the knowledge of many things pertaining to religion emanated from ancient times into the human race through traditions, is certain. But that the knowledge of ancient things pertaining to sacred matters was very obscure and uncertain among the nations, the wise themselves confessed. So Virgil:
"Open Helicon now, goddesses, and stir up song. For you, divine ones, remember, and are able to recall; to us scarcely a thin breath of the report glides down." (Aeneid 7.641, 645–646)
IV. Yet we shall see that they had some [knowledge]; "but whether those things which are excellently said about divine matters among the ancient philosophers were all drawn from the

writings of the Jews, I for my part vehemently doubt," [says] Scaliger. Eusebius indeed contends that all knowledge of divine things among the Greeks emanated from the Hebrews. That Plato too scraped together much from the theology of the Phoenicians, which he himself did not understand, Julius Scaliger writes (Exercitations 61); rightly or wrongly, I know not. That they retained some things by force of catholic [universal] tradition — things whose report had invaded the world from the very first beginnings — can be unknown to no one who has greeted their writings even from the threshold. Let us review a few, by way of sample. Very many acknowledged the Founder of all things, with no reasonings adduced to establish faith in him:
"There was once a time when all things had grown together at once" [Greek: ēn pote toi chronos houtos en hō hama pantʼ epephykei]

— ascribed to Linus, the most ancient stitcher-together of songs among the Greeks. The same origin of things all the poets celebrate. What Ovid wrote at the beginning of his work, boys know. Horace assents (Satires 1.3.99–104):

"When living creatures crept forth upon the first lands — a dumb and ugly herd — they fought, for acorns and lairs, with nails and fists, then with clubs, and so on with the arms which use afterward fashioned; until they found words by which to mark their voices and feelings, and names."
(With which the genuine [account] of Diodorus Siculus agrees, History, Book 1) — describing, after his manner, the origin of the human race. And Juvenal (Satires 6.11–13):
"For otherwise then, when the world was new and the sky fresh, lived men, who, born from the burst oak, or compounded of clay, had no parents."
And his contemporary [Greek: synchronos] Statius (Thebaid 4.275–281):

"To you the ancient Arcadians — older than the stars and the moon — give faithful troops; sprung, as fame tells, from the hard stock of the woods, when the earth, marveling, first bore the prints of feet: not yet were fields, or homes, or cities, nor measure in marriages. The oaks and laurels bore raw offspring, and the shady ash created peoples, and the green child dropped from the teeming mountain-ash."

V. Of the Brahmans, Strabo (Geography, Book 15): "[They agree] with the Greeks about many things — that the world is generated and perishable [Greek: genētos ho kosmos kai phthartos], and that God, who administers and makes it, has pervaded the whole of it" [Greek: hoti dioikōn auton kai poiōn theos diʼ holou diapephoitēken autou]. That the Greeks and barbarians agree in this — that the world was founded by God — that author [reports], namely from most ancient tradition. Aristotle doubts (Topics 19; On the Heavens 1.10), but well-nigh alone. That Hesiod, Orpheus, Apollonius, Aristophanes, Pliny, Diodorus Siculus, Numenius, Anaxagoras, and Megasthenes gave credence to the same tradition, it were easy to show. About Plato there is no question. That Thales,

Anaximenes, Xenophanes, Parmenides, Democritus, Empedocles, [and] Metrodorus held that the world had some kind of origin, Eusebius shows (Preparation for the Gospel, Book 1, ch. 7). The traditions of particular things — about Noah, the flood, Abraham — which Philo Byblius, Lucian, Strabo, and others mention, I pass over. With the founding of the world, the report that a judgment is to be exercised upon men after this life obtained a catholic fame. That persuasion was accompanied also by the presumption of the immortality of souls, which — although it rests also on reason — yet, since it always obtained most of all among the common people rather than the "wise" [Greek: sophous], is to be ascribed to nothing but tradition. Thales of Miletus defined the soul as "a nature ever-moving, or self-moving" [Greek: physin aei kinēton ē autokinēton]. And of him Diogenes: "Some say that he too was the first to say that souls are immortal" [Greek: enioi de kai auton prōton eipein athanatous tas psychas] — that is, he first treated philosophically of the "immortality" [Greek: athanasia] of the soul. That honor the consensus of very many gives to Pherecydes. "One thing," he says, "of those which the Druids teach, has flowed out into the common [knowledge] — namely, that souls are eternal." And of the same, Caesar (On the Gallic War 6.14): "Especially this they wish to persuade: that souls do not perish." And Valerius Maximus (Book 2, ch. 6.10): "There occurs that old custom of the Gauls, who, it is recorded, were wont to give loans of money to be repaid to them among the dead; because they were persuaded that the souls of men are immortal." About Plato, no one's house is better known. The Phaedo is witness.

VI. The same persuasion holds even the most barbarous nations. Through all America there was scarcely anyone who doubted [it] before the arrival of the Europeans on those shores. Some even received the report of "resurrection" [Greek: anastasias]. "Theopompus says, according to the Magi, that men will live again and will be immortal" [Greek: Theopompos kai anabiōsesthai kata tous magous phēsi tous anthrōpous kai esesthai athanatous], Diogenes Laertius, proem. The great Plato assents in the Phaedo, ch. 17; for he says, "But there really is both a living-again, and the living coming to be from the dead, and the souls of the dead existing; and for the good [souls it is] better, for the bad worse" [Greek: allʼ esti kai tō onti kai to anabiōskesthai, kai ek tōn tethneōtōn tous zōntas gignesthai, kai tas tōn tethneōtōn psychas einai; kai tais men agathais ameinon einai, tais kakais kakion].

VII. And these things, as we said, they believed — expecting some judgment to be exercised upon men, according to what they had done in this mortal life worthy or unworthy of right reason. Whence the same Plato (Epistle 7): "One must always believe the ancient and sacred discourses" (that is, the most ancient traditions), "which declare to us that the soul is immortal, and has judges, and pays the greatest penalties, when one is freed from the body" [Greek: peithesthai de ontōs aei chrē tois palaiois te kai hierois logois, hoi de mēnyousin hēmin athanaton psychēn einai, dikastas te ischein kai tinein tas megistas timōrias, hotan tis apallachthē tou sōmatos].
VIII. But to such a degree did that opinion obtain among very many,

and was esteemed of such moment for restraining the minds of the wicked, that the wisest of the heathen, Cato, did not hesitate, in the very senate, to ascribe the contrary opinion to Caesar, that he might create odium against him; for so he speaks in Sallust (Catiline 52): "Caius Caesar discoursed these things, thinking false, I suppose, the things which are recorded about the underworld — that the bad, by a different road from the good, have foul, wild, ugly, and dreadful places." This opinion the most learned of poets painted (Aeneid 6), imitating Homer (Odyssey 11), whence the papists drew [theirs], as is well known. The common people too were thoroughly imbued with that persuasion. Hence the things that happen in the underworld were committed to painters to depict — as is still done in those places where they adore the Roman pontiff, and elsewhere. Plautus (Captivi 5.4.1): "I have often seen many painted [scenes] of the torments that take place beneath Acheron." By these things they thought men could be held to their duty and drawn back from crimes. "They think [men] are especially roused to virtue, the fear of death being despised," [says] Caesar, of the Gauls. And Lucan (Book 1.469):

"Happy in their error, whom that greatest of terrors, the fear of death, does not press; hence the mind prone to rush upon the sword, and souls capable of death, and [counting it] cowardly to spare a life that will return."

And of the Germans, Appian (in the Celtica): "The Germans have most ferocious manners — signal boldness in attacking, the greatest contempt of death, because of the hope of living again." The same was the mind of the Britons, since Caesar testifies that the discipline of the Druids came forth from them. That the inhabitants of New England — [and] all the Americans — are persuaded that industrious, active, and temperate men, when they die, go to the southern regions of the world (which they believe the good God inhabits, since they feel that the winds which drive off the cold, and so all want, blow thence in springtime), but that all the slothful and idle go to the northern, or horrid and dreadful, places — the English report. Excellently Seneca: "Then our mind will have wherewith to congratulate itself, when, sent out from the darkness in which it wallows, it has looked upon clear things not with thin sight, but has admitted the whole day, and been restored to heaven; when it has received the place which it occupied by the lot of its birth." These things he [says], after his manner — splendidly, elegantly, magnificently.

IX. But, since they were utterly ignorant of the one way to true felicity, although they much preferred the good to the bad, yet they reckoned the condition of the best, in the underworld, [to be] uncertain and well-nigh wretched; and so "through fear of death were all their lifetime subject to bondage" [Greek: phobō thanatou dia pantos tou zēn enochoi ēsan douleias], as the apostle speaks, Heb. 2:15. So Achilles, though ruling widely over the dead, laments his lot to Ulysses:

"Do not speak soothingly to me of death, glorious Ulysses. I would rather be a hireling on the land, serving another man — one with no portion, whose living is not great — than rule over all the perished dead." [Homer, Odyssey 11.488–491]
[Part 1 ends here. Section X continues with the traditions concerning a future renovation of all things and the conflagration, and opens Part 2, continuing on p. 93.]

X. But all these things — some [of them], very many [of them] — flowed, it is plain, from most ancient tradition into the whole human race. I know indeed that among the wise very many are found to whom all these things about a future judgment, and the various — namely eternal — lot of men, were a laughingstock; nor does Lucretius the Epicurean alone cry out:
"And that fear of Acheron must be driven headlong out, which utterly troubles human life from its foundations." (Book 3.37–38)

XI. But Cicero himself, too, derides these things: "Tell me, pray," he says, "do those things terrify you — the three-headed Cerberus in the underworld, the roaring of Cocytus, the ferrying over Acheron, Tantalus killed by thirst, his chin touching the surface of the water? or that which —
'Sisyphus turns the stone, sweating with effort, and profits not a whit'?

perhaps also the inexorable judges, Minos and Rhadamanthus — before whom neither L. Crassus may defend you, nor M. Antonius; nor, since the matter will be pleaded before Greek judges, will you be able to employ Demosthenes: you yourself must plead your own cause before the greatest assembly. These things perhaps you fear, and therefore think death to be an everlasting evil. A.[uditor]: Do you think me so crazy as to believe that it is so? M.[arcus]: What, do you not believe these things? A.: By no means. M.: You tell me bad news, by Hercules. A.: Why, pray? M.: I could be eloquent, if I were to speak against them." (Tusculan Disputations 1.5) — If indeed he had only ridiculed the most vain additions of the poets, he would have acted according to the norm of right reason; but, as is plain from the following disputation, he opposed himself to the whole tradition, and so to the very truth of the thing. And out of the mind of others, Seneca the Tragedian (Troades, Act 1):

"After death there is nothing, and death itself is nothing — the last goal of a swift course. Do you ask where you shall lie after death? Where the unborn lie. And devouring time, and Chaos, swallow us. Death is indivisible, harmful to the body, and not sparing the soul."

XII. But let us grant that Cicero wished to dispute plausibly on each side, [and] to determine nothing; and that Seneca wrote anything poetically, at his pleasure. But Pliny not only rejects that persuasion of the common people, but tries to refute it by arguments (Book 7, ch. 56): "For all," he says, "from the last day, [there is] the same as before the first; nor is there any more sense, after death, to body or soul, than before birth. For the same vanity propagates itself even into the future, and even into the times of death lies to itself about life — sometimes by giving immortality to the soul, sometimes transfiguration, sometimes sense to those below, and by worshiping the shades, and by making a god

of one who has already ceased even to be a man. As if, forsooth, the manner of breathing in man differed in any way from the other animals; or as if many things were not found more long-lived in life, for which no one divines a like immortality. But what is the body of the soul by itself? what its matter? where its thought? how [its] sight, hearing — or what does it do? what use of it? or what good without these? Then what seat, and how great a multitude of souls — like shadows — through so many ages? These are the figments of childish ravings, and of a mortality greedy never to cease." And whatever of this was received among men, Strabo most openly calls fables (Book 15): "They weave in fables too, just as Plato does, both about the incorruptibility of the soul and about the judgments in Hades" [Greek: paraplekousi de kai mythous hōsper kai Platōn peri te aphtharsias psychēs, kai tōn kathʼ Haidou kriseōn]. He speaks of the Brahmans. "They have woven," he says, "fables too, as Plato did, about the immortality of the soul, and about the judgments which stand among those below." Along with Pliny and Strabo, without doubt, very many of the wise thought.

XIII. Relying on this persuasion, they exhorted one another to perpetrate every crime. Hence it passed into a proverb, "Let us eat and drink, for tomorrow we die" [Greek: phagōmen kai piōmen; aurion gar apothnēskomen], which the apostle mentions, 1 Cor. 15:32. Nothing occurs more frequently in the poet. Catullus (Carmen 5):

"Let us live, my Lesbia, and let us love, and value all the rumors of the more severe old men at a single penny. Suns can set and return; for us, when once the brief light has set, there is one perpetual night to be slept."
And Horace (Odes 1.4.15):

"Life's brief sum forbids us to begin a long hope. Soon night will press you, and the fabled Shades, and Pluto's meager house: whither, as soon as you have gone, neither will you cast lots for the kingdom of wine, nor admire tender Lycidas."
And Persius himself, though jesting (Satires 5.151):
"Indulge your genius; let us pluck sweet things; what you live is ours: you will become ash, and shade, and a fable."
Nearer to the words mentioned by the apostle [are] the Greeks. So Strato:
"Drink and take pleasure, Democrates; for not forever shall we drink, nor forever shall we have delight" [Greek: kai pine kai terpou Dēmokrates; ou gar es aiei piomethʼ oudʼ aiei terpsios hexometha].
And:
"Drink and be merry; for what tomorrow, or what the future, is, no one knows" [Greek: pine kai euphrainou, ti gar aurion, ē ti to mellon oudeis gignōskei].
But from that opinion which it had drawn from most ancient tradition, the common people never suffered itself to be removed.

XIV. The other particles of divine truth which the traditions also intimated, it were easy to recount. To use the benefit of those [traditions], I say, for advancing the remnants of the natural light, was the duty of all men. But it appears that those very traditions were corrupted well-nigh in the first place; and so the very remedy of the disease (and the antidote against eternal

death) was turned into a deadly poison. For we have shown that this "doctrine handed down from the fathers" [Greek: patroparadoton] brought no contemptible help to the natural light in seeking God; but, that [tradition] itself being at length corrupted, it gradually corrupted and overturned even that residual light, and all the "anticipations" [Greek: prolēpseis] of the human mind about God. How this was done, we shall expound in a few words.

XV. That traditions flit hither and thither "through the mouths of men" [per ora virum] is natural to them. In that state of things which we have described, each used them according to the store of which he was made partaker. Those who first dared to halt the dispersed and here-and-there wandering [traditions], to collect and expound them, were the poets. These first, modulating melody in hymns and little songs to catch the ears of men, undertook their province; then in writings stuffed, beyond what can be said, with fables of every kind, they gave it forth adorned. The foundation of the whole mass [was] the traditions. But gradually they utterly corrupted all that knowledge of God which the mortal race had drawn from the ancient traditions. We speak chiefly of Hellenism; for that manner of "apostasy" [Greek: apostasias] at length arrived at "Catholicism" [universal prevalence]. While, therefore, they either twisted what they had heard into foreign and fabulous senses, or augmented it with cunning fictions, or invented other things twin to those they had heard, or used ingeniously devised allegories for reading those things which they themselves had received by obscure report from their ancestors and in no way understood — they left nothing sound, solid, true; nothing not vain, fictitious, idolatrous, deadly, in the whole of natural religion.

XVI. Nay, they so confounded all things that what each one received, and whence, and from whom, by what report, tradition, occasions, [or] reasons led — what many things, very many, they embraced — it is utterly impossible to attain by conjecture. It appears that they mixed traditions interpolated with fables, natural and moral theology, [and] the worship of demons and of dead men. What each one drew from corrupt natural theology, what from perverted traditions, what from diabolical oracles, what from the usage of the common people, what from his own brain, is utterly impossible to investigate accurately. Nay, very often they set forth some obscure divine tradition, some excellent part of natural theology, the most inept fables, and Satanic worship, in the same words, with the same baggage of fictions. The same was the Sun, [and] Noah, [and] Saturn — nay, Jupiter, a Planet, a Hero, Heaven, Aether, and Satan himself — as we shall afterward see more fully.

XVII. But the human mind, laboring under native blindness, is greedy of fables and errors, and tenacious of them. After it had therefore been imbued with "superstition" [Greek: deisidaimonia] and polytheism, the natural light itself, unequal to removing prejudices, plainly succumbed; nor could it ever extricate itself from the hydra of fables. By one example this confusion can, it seems, be set before the eyes. Bacchus is the same as Adonis, Evius, and Sabus; so Plutarch (Symposiacs 4, question 5), where, investigating the origins of the Jewish ceremonies — all care of truth and candor being cast aside — most ineptly
[Part 2 ends here. Section XVIII continues the example of this confusion (Bacchus, Liber, and the Jewish rites misread by Plutarch and Tacitus) and opens Part 3, continuing on p. 96.]

XVII. (continued) ... [Plutarch] most ineptly sets about [it]. That the names Iacchus and Bacchus flowed from יְהוָה (YHWH), Sandford well-nigh proves — conjecturing, indeed, learnedly — in the first book On the Descent of Christ to Hell. And to that best and most learned man very many owe very much in the inquiry into the origins of polytheism [Greek: polytheotētos], idolatry, and Hellenism — some [owe] everything, I know; whom yet you will find rarely named by anyone, and praised by no one. But that Adonis [is] from אֲדֹנִי (Adonai), no one, I think, will deny; Sabus, from צְבָאוֹת (Tsebaoth); and Evius seems to express אֶהְיֶה (Ehyeh) — whence Plutarch dreams that the Levites among the Jews got their name. But these are either names or epithets of the living God. By those names, then — which had reached them, though by a thin report — the ancients understood the true deity. These being so received, they assigned to them other "divine attributes" [Greek: theia]. Among these, some were true, but others "falsely entitled" [Greek: pseudepigrapha], false, diabolical. First, what they had heard about Noah they referred to it [Bacchus]; and the name Bacchus seems to some to be drawn from נֹחַ (Noach), or from Noah. That Liber [Bacchus] filched Noah's vines and vineyard is not to be doubted. Hence Lactantius: "The lie of the poets is not in the fact, but in the name." See Vossius, On Idolatry, Book 1, ch. 19 and 25. Thus the true God and "the man of the soil" [Hebrew: אִישׁ הָאֲדָמָה, ish ha-adamah, Gen. 9:20] were confounded. Bacchus too is perhaps בַּר כּוּשׁ (Bar Cush), "son of Cush"; so Bochart holds (Sacred Geography, Book 1, ch. 2) — that is, Nimrod. Hence called Ζαγρεύς (Zagreus), or "the mighty hunter" (Gen. 10:9); and Νεβρώδης (Nebrodes), as all the Greeks call Nimrod. But that well-nigh the whole history of Moses was ascribed to Bacchus, Sandford shows by many examples (Book 1, ch. 1, sect. 18, 19); after him, and with the same instances, Vossius (Book 1, ch. 30); nor does Bochart think otherwise (Book 1, ch. 18). But whatever [is said] of Bacchus, all the same things are said of Osiris in Egypt, who, having been great among his own, after death obtained all the honors of the sun. But at length there came forth the Theban Bacchus, or Διόνυσος (Dionysus), so called from Mount Nysa; and that Nysa is nothing but Syna [Sinai], certain learned men suspect, by a "transposition" [Greek: metathesis] of letters. That Arabia is called India by the Greeks, it were easy to prove by many testimonies. But there is also a Mount Nysa in India beyond the Caucasus, not far from the river Cophen, as Philostratus shows (Life of Apollonius, Book 2, ch. 4) — which, he relates, men planted with vines in honor of Bacchus, who there, namely, worked wonders. But that that Bacchus was an Assyrian, and that the Theban Bacchus came to him, the inhabitants of that region maintained. Others [say] that the city set at the roots of the mountain is called Nysa, [and] the mountain itself, Meron. So Curtius (Book 8, ch. 10). But Μηρός (mēros) is "thigh." Hence the fable of Bacchus sewn into the thigh of Jove — unless you suspect a Hebraism, and that "to be born from the thigh of Jove" signifies nothing else than to be the son of God, as Heinsius holds (in his Aristarchus). And these things are not unworthy of the jeers with which Lucian receives them (Dialogues 9).
XVIII. For the rest, the origin and deeds of the Theban Dionysus, Eusebius relates from Diodorus (Preparation for the Gospel, Book 2, ch. 2). He, when perhaps the first of the Europeans

to make some excursions into the Asiatic shores, exhausted, among the fable-makers of the next age, all the ancient traditions about the Bacchuses mentioned above; and one man was all [those things] of which really there was none; for the "myth-makers" [Greek: mythopoioi] heaped upon him well-nigh all the "things handed down from the fathers" [Greek: patroparadota]. Nor did this marvelous frustration stop here. Because the ancients ascribed the discovery of the vine and of vineyards to Bacchus, the patchers-together of natural theology contended that by "Bacchus" only wine, or autumn, or that season of the year in which the vines ripen, was denoted. So Lucretius teaches us (Book 2.654–658):

"Here, if anyone resolves to call the sea Neptune, and the crops Ceres, and prefers to abuse the name of Bacchus rather than to utter the proper name of the liquid [wine], let us grant that he may keep declaring the orb of the earth to be the Mother of the gods, provided that in reality he be not [such] himself."

And Athenaeus (Deipnosophists, Book 1): "Some say that the flight of Dionysus into the sea signifies the making of wine, known long ago; for wine is sweet when sea-water is poured in" [Greek: enioi de kai tēn Dionysou phygēn eis tēn thalassan oinopoiian sēmainein phasi, palai egnōrizomenēn; hēdyn gar einai ton oinon parenchoumenēs thalassēs]. But that the fable was taken from the history of Moses is too well established.

XIX.
