That they feigned these things ineptly and unskillfully, Philo Byblius is witness, in the preface to the history of Sanchuniathon: "But," he says, "the more recent of the sacred writers, rejecting from the beginning the things that really happened, devised allegories and fables; and, having fabricated an affinity with the affections of the universe, set up mysteries" [Greek: all', hoi men neōtatoi tōn hierologōn, ta men gegonota pragmata ex archēs apopempsantes allēgorias kai mythous epenoēsan, kai, tois kosmikois pathēmasi syngeneian plasamenoi mystēria katestēsan]. And he indeed, since he had laid it down that all things said about the gods are to be referred to true histories, rejects both that whole natural theology on the one hand, and the "mythical" [Greek: mythikēn] [theology] of the poets on the other. But innumerable other examples of this confusion occur everywhere. The history of the origin of the world in Diodorus Siculus — especially in Sanchuniathon and that Philo Byblius — shows the same. The first chapter of Genesis is transferred into the history of many ages, about I-know-not-what kings: Heaven, Earth, Sun, Moon, מָוֶת (Maveth, "Death") — who is said to be Pluto — have a place in it. אֱלֹהִים (Elohim) himself is also introduced. Driven about among these rough places, how easy it was for the old serpent to impose on the human race nefarious opinions about God, and impious worship of himself, anyone can conjecture.

XX. Excellently Pausanias (in the Arcadica): "In all ages, many things that happened long ago, but no longer happen, have been made incredible to men by those who, building falsehoods upon true things" [Greek: en tō panti aiōni polla men palai symbanta mēketi de ginomena, apista einai pepoiēkasin eis tous, hoi tois alēthesin epoikodomountes epseusmena]. They rendered truth itself — so corrupted and laden with lies — incredible; how this was done, he shows a little later

[saying]: "Those who, hearing such things, take pleasure in mythological tales are themselves naturally inclined to contrive something; and so they have marred true things by mixing them with falsehoods" [Greek: hoti hosoi de mythologēmasin akouontes hēdontai pephykasi kai autoi ti epitēdeuesthai kai houtō tois alēthesin elymēnanto synkerannyntes auta epseusmenois]. So it is brought about that those who lend their ears to the portents of such fictions soon themselves add something, whereby it happens that truth, vitiated as if by a sewer of lies, utterly loses its authority.

XXI. By the just judgment of God, therefore — [God] delivering up men who detain the truth in unrighteousness "to a reprobate mind" [Greek: eis adokimon noun] — it came about that those to whom it seemed not [good] to retain God in their knowledge became vain in their reasonings; and so at once a kind of deluge of spiritual abominations, accompanied by all injustice, violence, and impurity, settled upon the world. This came about from the craft of the devil and the "evil emulation" [Greek: kakozēlia] of very many. For, polytheism growing strong, there was nothing so vain or worthless that it was not enrolled in the number of the gods. No stone, no wood, no virtue, no vice, nothing good, nothing bad, but it was worshiped. With the same "dishonor" [Greek: atimia] were all creatures affected, from the grass of the field to the highest stars of heaven. This the apostle teaches us: "They changed," he says, "the glory of the incorruptible God into the likeness of an image of corruptible man, and of birds, and of four-footed beasts, and of creeping things" [Greek: ēllaxan tēn doxan tou aphthartou Theou en homoiōmati eikonos phthartou anthrōpou, kai peteinōn, kai tetrapodōn, kai herpetōn], Rom. 1:23. By those words, it is plain, Paul lashes idolaters of every kind. Maddened by Hellenism, reckoning the gods "of human form" [Greek: anthrōpophyeas], they fashioned the effigies of the deity after the image of corruptible man. The worship "of birds, four-footed beasts, and creeping things" [Greek: peteinōn, tetrapodōn, kai herpetōn] took its origin from the Egyptians. "Making gods of dogs, wolves, lions, crocodiles, and very many other beasts, both aquatic and winged" [Greek: kynas, lykous, kai leontas, kai krokodeilous, kai alla pleiona thēria, kai enydra, kai ptēna theoplastountes], [says] Philo — "dogs, wolves, lions, crocodiles, and very many others, aquatic, terrestrial, and flying," they feign in the place of gods, and adore. And Theophilus of Antioch (To Autolycus, Book 1): "It remains for me to recount the multitude of animals which the Egyptians worship — reptiles, and birds, and beasts, and aquatic swimmers" [Greek: to moi loipon katalegein to plēthos hōn sebontai zōōn Aigyptioi herpetōn te kai ptēnōn kai thēriōn kai enydrōn nēktōn]. For in those who are thought to have first revolted from the true God, he willed that the greatest example of diabolical domination and tyranny should stand forth. It is permitted, therefore, to contemplate in passing their "objects of worship" [Greek: sebasmata] — as Paul did at Athens — especially since the Egyptian superstition by no means contained itself within the bounds of Egypt:
"We Romans have received into our temples your Isis, and the half-divine dogs,"
says Lucan (Book 8.831).

XXII. I know not what mysteries Athanasius Kircher lately tried to elicit from their sacred things, and labors to teach — with great and costly effort, but vain, not to say ridiculous — that those alone were wise whom the rest of the world had hitherto believed to have been foolish beyond [the bounds of] humanity. He saw in their hieroglyphics things which, beyond all doubt, neither did they themselves ever see, nor would anyone see who held the account of Christian modesty, or of truth, to be weightier than [that] of secular conceit. But —
"He did not see, but thought he saw the moon through the night." [Greek: ouk iden allʼ edokēsen idein dia nykta selēnēn.]
[Part 3 ends here. Section XXIII takes up Paul's verdict on the heathen mode of worshiping the gods, and opens Part 4, continuing on p. 99.]
XXIII. What Paul decreed about their manner of worshiping their religions, we have shown;

we have also produced the agreement of Philo the Jew, who passed his life in the metropolis of Egypt. In reproaching them with the same crime, Josephus too is copious (Book 1, Against Apion). In Exodus 12:12 God threatens "that he will execute judgments upon all the gods of the Egyptians." That he gave effect to that threat, whatever it was, Moses relates (Num. 33:4). That among the other beasts he slew Apis himself, and the other animals sacred to the Egyptians, is not to be doubted. What those who, among the heathen, [seemed] to be wise — [and who] seem to us, surely, to have raved less than the Egyptians — thought of the Egyptians' religious worship, is well known. The Satirist (Satire 15.1–6):

"Who knows not, Bithynian Volusius, what monsters mad Egypt worships? One part adores the crocodile; another dreads the ibis gorged with serpents. The golden effigy of the sacred long-tailed monkey gleams… Here they venerate the river-fish; there whole towns [venerate] a dog."
Anaxandrides, in Athenaeus (Deipnosophists, Book 7), thus addresses the Egyptians:

"I could not be your ally, for neither our manners agree, nor our laws; they stand far apart from one another. You worship the ox; I sacrifice it to the gods. You hold the eel to be the greatest deity; we [hold it] by far the greatest of dainties. You eat no pork; I delight in it… You venerate the dog; I beat him." [Greek]
And Antiphanes, in [the] Lycon:

"They say the Egyptians are clever in other things too — in counting the eel equal to a god; for it is far more precious than the gods. For the gods we can win by praying; but these [eels], one can scarcely get to smell, having spent at least twelve drachmas or more: so utterly holy is this beast." [Greek]
XXIV. This infamy Apollonius casts up to Thespesion, chief of the Gymnosophists, in Philostratus, treating of statues and images (Life of Apollonius, Book 6, ch. 19): "About the gods,"

he says, "I will first ask you: what did you learn, that you have handed down to the men here absurd and ridiculous forms of the gods, except a few? — for few, indeed very few, are set up wisely and in godlike fashion. The rest of your sacred things appear to be honors of irrational and inglorious animals, rather than of gods" [Greek]. Not unwelcome to the reader will be what Thespesion replies, if he will consult the said place of the author. But it appears from the same Philostratus that the Egyptians could give no account at all of their insane worship — however much Kircher, after so many centuries run out, feigns that he has detected, in those monstrous gods, the mysteries of all Christianity. One utility Tully thought they regarded: "The Egyptians who are laughed at," he says, "consecrated no beast except for some utility which they got from it" (On the Nature of the Gods, Book 1). To the Egyptians, after his manner playing the tragedian, Gildas joins the Britons (On the Ruin of Britain): "The portents of Britain," he says, "were diabolical themselves, well-nigh surpassing in number those of Egypt; some of which, with lineaments still deformed, within or without the deserted walls, stiffened in the wonted manner, we behold with grim faces." Freed by the grace of Christ, we all stand astonished at, and detest, these monsters of crimes, this tyranny of the old serpent; and so deservedly to be abominated is the impiety of those who — though they wish to be called Christians, and by that most holy name alone to be deemed worthy — we yet hold convicted, from their own confessions, of being driven by the same madness, if not a greater. "For more tolerable is the error of those who worship for God a golden or silver statue, or an image of other material (as the Gentiles venerated their gods), or a red cloth raised on a spear (which is related of the Lapps), or living animals (as once the Egyptians), than [the error] of those who worship a morsel of bread." These are the words of the Jesuit Coster (Enchiridion, ch. 8). Here surely, if we are men, if endowed with sense and reason, if any faith is to be given to the words of sacred Scripture, we have them confessing [themselves] guilty — that such idolatry is found among the sons of the most holy father the pope as, in the very darkness of heathenism, was never either seen or heard.

XXV. But since the world was overwhelmed by the number of the gods, and men knew not whom or what they worshiped, it was customary, in invocations, to use that [phrase]: "Whoever thou art." "Now, whoever the god is, I venerate him," Plautus (Rudens 1.3). And:
"We follow thee, holy one of the gods, whoever thou art." (Virgil, Aeneid 4.576)
And in the Capitol a shield was consecrated, on which was written: "To the Genius of the city of Rome, whether it be male or female."
XXVI. And hence that formula for calling forth the gods from places besieged in war,

which Macrobius records from the fragments of Samonicus: "If [it be] a god, if a goddess, in whose tutelage this people and city is — and thee especially, who hast taken up the tutelage of this city and people — I pray and venerate, and beg pardon of you, that you forsake this people and city; that you leave their places, temples, sacred things, and city; that you depart from these; and that you cast upon that people and city fear, dread, [and] forgetfulness; and, having betrayed [them], come to Rome, to me and mine; and may our places, temples, sacred things, [and] city be more acceptable [to you]; and may you be set over me also, and the Roman people, and my soldiers, that we may know and understand it. If you do so, I vow that I will make you temples and games."
XXVII. Hence, before any city was overthrown, the gods were believed to have deserted it. So Virgil, of the destruction of Troy:
"All the gods, by whom this empire had stood, have departed, the shrines and altars left behind." (Aeneid 2.351)

XXVIII. For the most ancient poets reported that the gods were seen carrying their images out of the temples, when the destruction now drew near. And, in general, Aeschylus (Seven against Thebes 219):
"But there is a saying that the gods of a captured city forsake it." [Greek: all' oun theous tous tēs alousēs poleōs ekleipein logos.]

XXIX. Hence it is that the Romans willed it to be concealed in whose god's tutelage the city of Rome was, lest the gods, called by their proper names, could be "unhallowed" [their consecration undone] by the enemy. This was provided for by pontifical law. And the pontiffs themselves prayed thus: "Jupiter, best and greatest! — or by whatever other name thou wilt be called."

XXX. That these empty figments ever pleased wise men — and [men] desiring to live according to reason — I would marvel beyond measure, were it not permitted to perceive, by daily proofs, how great a tyranny vain superstition exercises over the minds of men. So, in a manner not unlike that which we have related from Samonicus: when, battle having been joined at the shrine of Saint-Quentin, the Spaniards found it necessary to throw down and overturn a temple dedicated to Saint Lawrence, to which the enemy had betaken themselves, King Philip bound himself by a vow to the saint that — another and much greater temple being erected — he would compensate the damage done. This was the occasion of building the Escorial edifice dedicated to Lawrence, which an anonymous poet elegantly expounded:

"These most rich temples Philip set up for thee, Lawrence, after he had cast down by war, at Saint-Quentin, those which had been [there] — since the enemy, shut within the walls, had hidden in trembling beneath the altars, and [trusted] in the deity in vain. To whom the king [said]: Here too let it be permitted me to take vengeance, and to bury the hostile nation in sacred ruins; let it be right to defile the hearths with blood — for I will give greater [things]."
[Part 4 ends here. Section XXXI continues with the form of ancient vows (Statius), and opens Part 5, continuing on p. 102.]

XXXI. And [it is] plainly the form of the ancient vows which Statius celebrates in his Tydeus (Thebaid, Book 2.726–734): "[If I be borne into the ancestral arms of Parthaon, and Martian Pleuron lie open to my return,] then will I dedicate to thee golden temples in the midst of the city,

on the hills, where it is sweet to look down on the Ionian storms, and where turbid Achelous, lifting the sea with his tawny head, goes out past the opposing Echinades. Here will I fashion the battles of [our] ancestors, and the dread faces of great-souled kings, and will fix arms on the proud domes."

XXXII. But ignorance of the true God is the fount and origin of all evils. Hence those very [gods] which they had set before themselves to be worshiped, the impious and wicked manikins served up to be derided and imitated — foolish, fatuous, filthy, stained with robberies, brawls, wars, adulteries, [and] unnameable sins. And so the remnants of the natural light were extinguished; nor [was there] any greater care of the works of God, which manifest his eternal power and deity. So at last Hellenism grew strong — that is, the "mythical" [Greek: mythikē] theology of the Gentiles. Hence came forth all the gods — the greater, the middle, the lowest.

XXXIII. But with how great a calamity those portents of the poets afflicted the human race — as concerns honest morals, a tranquil life consonant with reason, the civil state, and the "anticipation" [Greek: prolēpsis] of eternal punishments — is well known: it will suffice to set forth one or two detestable examples. Saturn, who is Kronos [Greek: Kronos], was among them Chronos [Greek: Chronos], or Time. So, in natural theology, they "made a myth" [Greek: emythologēsan]. Noah also [was reckoned Saturn], from a most corrupt tradition. Time is completed through the motion of the heaven: hence Saturn [is] the son of Uranus, or of Heaven. So too Noah is reckoned [the survivor], all others being destroyed by the flood's "utter destruction" [Greek: panōlethria]. But they feigned that Saturn devoured his sons and vomited them up again. Whether [this is] because, by turns, time begets, consumes, reabsorbs, and casts out all things; or because Noah, having shut his sons up with him in the ark, gave them again to the world, as it were revived — is uncertain. It appears that the fable was forged from a most obscure tradition and an ingenious allegory. And hence at length, by the help of Satan, there came forth a horrible idol-madness; and with the nefarious superstition was mixed a monstrous cruelty. For the foolish manikins, employing — I know not what — unspeakable ceremonies for the solace of their misery, immolated their own children to Saturn. Hence the "mound of the children" was so called.

XXXIV. But this wickedness seems to have been older than all the Greek portents; that it took increase from them, no one doubts. Thence at length it became "catholic" [universal]. Nor is there any part of Satanic worship in which the human race will be found to have conspired more concordantly. Whether here the old serpent had it in mind to exalt himself magnificently, and to triumph over conquered sinners — he who was one day himself to be triumphed over on the cross of Christ; or whether [he had it in mind], by sacrifice, to do prejudice and contumely in the minds of men — it is plain that he poured out all his venom, far and wide, in advancing this crime. Let us review some examples. Of the Africans the matter is well known: "Infants were openly immolated to Saturn in Africa, right up to the proconsulship of Tiberius," Tertullian (Apology, ch. 9). The same things Arnobius has. When the Carthaginians had been conquered by Agathocles, king of the Sicilians — thinking God angry with them —

that they might more diligently pay the expiation, they immolated two hundred sons of the nobles to Saturn; Pescennius Festus is witness, in Lactantius. Pressed by the Roman war, they performed the same rites; the son of Hannibal himself, Aspar, was slain. Hence Silius brings in Hamilcar's wife bewailing this crime (Punica, Book 4):

"What piety, moreover, is this — to besprinkle the shrines with gore? Alas, the first cause of crimes to sick mortals [is] to be ignorant of the nature of the gods. Go, pray just prayers with pious incense, and avert the savage rites of slaughter. God is mild, and akin to man: thus far I pray — let it be enough to have seen bullocks slain before the altars; or, if so great an impiety be fixed and settled in your will, me, me, who bore [them], consume with your vows. Why does it please [you] to spoil the Libyan lands of this offspring?"

XXXV. Among the Romans, [it was] customary for living men to devote themselves to the gods below; the story of the Decii is well known: the father in the Gallic war, the son in the Samnite, devoted themselves in the same manner; whom Juvenal celebrates (Satire 8.254):

"Plebeian were the souls of the Decii, plebeian their names; yet these [men], for whole legions, and for all the auxiliaries, and all the Latin youth, suffice for the infernal gods and for mother earth; for the Decii [are] worth more than those who are saved by them."

XXXVI. That [it was] once the custom to worship Jupiter Latialis with homicide, Arnobius affirms. In the consulship of Cornelius Lentulus and Publius Licinius Crassus, a decree of the senate was made that no man should be immolated. And that, up to that time, prodigious rites had been openly celebrated, Pliny narrates (Natural History, Book 30, ch. 1). Thus, when the Gauls were rushing in, "the elders, who had held the highest honors, assemble in the forum, and there, the pontiff devoting them, consecrate themselves to the gods of the dead," in Florus (Book 1.13).

XXXVII. That the Gauls, too, were wont to appease the gods with these nefarious victims, Cicero relates (Oration for Fonteius, 10): "Who is ignorant," he says, "that they retain, to this day, that monstrous and barbarous custom of immolating men?" That a Gaul and a Gaulish woman were also wont to be sacrificed at Rome yearly, Plutarch notes. The Roman emperor Hadrian immolated his catamite Antinous to the gods in Egypt. "Antinous died in Egypt — either because he fell into the Nile, as Hadrian writes, or because he was sacrificed, which is the truth," says Dio, in Xiphilinus (Book 69) [Greek]. And he made the immolated [boy] a god. That the inhabitants of the island of Thule observed this custom of immolating men down to their own times, Procopius reports. The same was the custom of the Britons. The island of Mona [Anglesey] being conquered by Paulinus, "a garrison was imposed on the conquered, and the groves sacred to savage superstitions were cut down: for they held it right to drench the altars with

captive gore, and to consult the gods by the entrails of men," says Tacitus (Annals, Book 14, ch. 30). And, to that place, Horace: "I shall see the Britons, savage to their guests." Acron: "The Britons slew their guests as a victim." The gods to whom the Gauls offered with human blood, Lucan mentions (1.444):
"And those whom pitiless Teutates is appeased with dire blood, and dread Hesus with his savage altars, and Taranis, whose altar is not milder than [that] of Scythian Diana."

XXXVIII. But that all these were gods of the Britons, the most diligent Camden proves in his Britannia; and that the Gauls received the whole discipline of the Druids from the Britons, Caesar himself reports (On the Gallic War, Book 6). The same madness [belonged to] the Germans. "A man being publicly slain, they celebrate the horrid beginnings of [their] rites," says Tacitus. And again: "Of the gods they chiefly worship Mercury, to whom, on certain days, they hold it right to offer even with human victims" (On the Manners of the Germans, 9). That marble monuments of this impiety still survive among the Etruscans, and that he himself saw one of them at Perugia, Camerarius reports (Historical Meditations, Book 2). Of the Goths, Jornandes: "They always appeased Mars with a most savage worship, for his victims were the deaths of captives." The Greeks [were] bound by the same crime. Erechthonius, the most ancient king of Attica, they say, sacrificed two of his own daughters. And that Neanthes of Cyzicus devoted himself, Athenaeus shows (Deipnosophists, Book 13); and Epimenides expiated the whole land of Attica, for certain ancient crimes, with human blood — he who was the author, to the Athenians, of erecting an altar to the unknown God. Among the Thebans sprung from the Tyrians (from whom this superstition emanated), Menoeceus is celebrated, who devoted himself for the city, and slew himself, offering to the gods below with his own blood; whose death Papinius Statius excellently celebrates (Thebaid, Book 10.756–769):

"But pious Menoeceus, on a chosen part of the walls — now sacred in aspect, and more august than his wonted face, as if suddenly sent down to earth from the upper pole — stood, manifest to be known, his helmet taken off; and he looked down on the ranks of men, and with a deep cry turned the field [toward him], and commanded silence for the war. 'Gods of arms, and thou, O Phoebus, who grantest me to die by so great an end — give to Thebes the joys which I have pledged, and which, prodigal, I buy with all my blood… and to the Tyrians restore, by my death, temples, fields, homes, marriages, children, if I have pleased you [as] a peaceful victim.'"
By which words the poet wondrously painted the common feeling of the nations about expiatory sacrifices; as also afterward, in the mother's lament:
"Did I, then, vile mother, rear you, glorious boy, [to be] a lustral [victim] for savage Thebes, and a devoted head?" (793–794)
[Part 5 ends here. Section XXXIX continues the catalog (Tyrians, Egyptians, Menelaus in Egypt) and opens Part 6, continuing on p. 105.]

XXXIX. That the Tyrians immolated a freeborn boy to Saturn, Curtius shows (Book 4, ch. 3). That the Egyptians immolated living men to brute gods, Herodotus and Diodorus Siculus testify. That Menelaus, in Egypt, sacrificed two boys, the priests told Herodotus

(Clio [Book 1], p. 158). "The Thracians offer human blood to the gods," says Florus (History, Book 3, ch. 4). The Celts, immolating a man, strike him with a sword across a transverse partition, [as] Diodorus witnesses (Book 5). The Normans and Danes, every year in the month of January, immolated to their gods ninety-nine men, as many horses, with dogs, [as] Dithmar witnesses (Book 10). Of the Massilians, Lucan (3.303–305):
"Here [were] barbarous sacred rites of the gods after their manner, altars built for funereal rites, and every tree purified with human gore."

XL. "A man was sacrificed in Rhodes, on the sixteenth before the Kalends of November, to Saturn" [Greek: ethyeto kai en Rhodō Metageitniōnos hektē histamenou anthrōpos tō Kronō], says Theodoret (Book 7, Cure of Greek Affections). And: "The Phoenicians, in great calamities of wars, or droughts, or pestilences, sacrificed some one of their dearest to Saturn, appointing him to this by common votes" [Greek: Phoinikes en tais megalais symphorais ē polemōn, ē auchmōn, ē loimōn, ethyonto tōn philtatōn tina epipsēphizontes Kronō], says Porphyry (On Abstinence from Animals, Book 2). The catholic [universal] plague invaded even the people of God himself: "They sacrificed their sons and their daughters unto demons, and shed innocent blood, [even] the blood of their sons and of their daughters, whom they sacrificed unto the idols of Canaan" (Ps. 106:37, 38). Whoever wishes for more, let him add also the things we noted in the little book On Divine Justice, ch. 8. That some Christians — at least in name — defiled themselves with this crime, I would show, were I not ashamed.

XLI. The origin of these sacrifices Porphyry refers to Abraham (so Eusebius witnesses, Preparation for the Gospel, Book 1, ch. 9) — and [says] that he was called Saturn, [and] that his daughter, Gene, [when] immolated, had been begotten of Queen Anobret. That profane writers everywhere bring forth a shameful "unhistoricalness" [Greek: anistorēsia] of this kind, in investigating the origins of things and nations, is well known. Varro thinks the ancients devised this kind of sacrifice because the human race is the best of all seeds. The Druids judged that, unless a man's life were given for a man's life, the deity of the immortal gods could not be appeased (Caesar witnesses, On the Gallic War, Book 6). That the human race drew the use of sacrifices, for the expiation of sins, from ancient and catholic tradition, is clear. That a just and supreme God could be appeased, for the crimes of men, by the blood of cattle, anxious and doubtful minds — agitated with horror and the sense of sins — could never thoroughly persuade themselves; [and] the solicitous and well-nigh despairing Satan assails, in his manner. He easily persuades men that they have nothing more precious [than themselves]. Hence the mind to immolate themselves and others. That conscience, vexed and terrified with the sense of sin and the fear of punishment, was the bottom of this evil, the prophet shows (Mic. 6:7). That [conscience], Satan playing midwife, brought forth this monster into the light. Often they got no other response from the oracles — fearing, or about to dare, something great — than that [the gods] must be appeased with human blood: well known

are the stories of Menoeceus and Iphigenia. The custom of the Lacedaemonians, beating themselves with scourges in the worship of the gods, had no other origin, Apollonius relates in Philostratus (Book 6, ch. 10): "The custom of the scourges," he says, "is performed for the Scythian Diana, because, as they say, the oracles prescribed these things; but to make counter-laws against the gods is, I think, a kind of madness" [Greek: to de tōn mastigōn ethos tē Artemidi tē apo Skythōn dratai, chrēsmōn phasin exēgoumenōn tauta, theois dʼ antinomothetein mania, oimai]. And Pausanias (Boeotica): "The Delphic oracle answered that the Megarians must sacrifice a comely boy to Dionysus" [Greek: aphiketo hama ek Delphōn tō Dionysō thyein paida hōraion]. Things twin to these occur everywhere among the historians; to what height it would climb, Satanic malice has not [yet] found, having advanced thus far.

XLII. But of this impious heap there is no end. It is well known that the adulteries and debaucheries of the dung-gods were, to very many, an incitement and an apology for perpetrating all impure outrages — that I may add that example in the second place. For after they had feigned these gods for themselves, they at once also feigned [them] like themselves — that is, liars, perjurers, and stained with every impurity. Take away the brawls, lies, fights, perjuries, [and] adulteries of the gods: dwarfs will those certain ancient writers be, who now advance with giant step; with these, alas! Christian youth is still polluted, and is immolated to that Moloch of which John Comenius — a learned and pious man — complains. I do not wish, by repeating, to thrust upon chaste ears the filthiest crimes, eternally to be kept silent by the saints. To make plain to what end the devil suggested these things to the minds of nefarious men, a few things can be recalled: let that [saying] of Mercury about Jove, in Plautus (Prologue to the Amphitryo), suffice:
"For I believe you already know how my father is — how free he is in these unpunished matters, and how great a lover, when once something has pleased him."
And:
"How often does he himself, who leads the heaven and the clouds, put on lesser shapes." (Seneca, Hippolytus)

XLIII. Ah! how often does the wet and soft clay drink in blasphemies of this kind, the stupid molder applauding. Excellently Augustine: "All the worshipers of such gods, as soon as lust has impelled them, look rather (as Persius says, 'steeped in boiling venom') at what [the gods] have done basely, than at what Plato taught or Cato judged" (On the City of God, Book 2, ch. 7). Of the filthiest worship of the Mother of the gods, the reader will find more in that place. What minds and affections the human race — too headlong of itself into vices — conceived from those examples set before it, Terence sets before the eyes of all in his Chaerea (Eunuch, Act 3, Sc. 5.35):

"While [the bath] is being prepared, the maiden sits in the chamber, looking up at a certain painted tablet, where this picture was: how Jove, they say, once sent a golden shower into the lap of Danaë. I too began to gaze at it; and because he had played a like game long ago, my mind rejoiced the more exceedingly —

[to think] that a god had turned himself into a man, and through another's roof-tiles had come secretly, through the skylight, to play a trick on a woman. But what god? He who shakes the highest temples of heaven with his thunder. And I, a manikin, should not do this? I did indeed do it — and gladly."

[Part 6 ends here. Section XLIV continues — how men exhorted one another, by the gods' example, to the filthiest crimes (Catullus); temple prostitution; the wicked impersonating gods (the Mundus and Paulina affair from Josephus) — and opens Part 7, continuing on p. 108.]

XLIV. Would that, from dishonorable pictures and the reading of the poets, the same destruction were not still carried into the channels of youth! But this impiety advanced so far that, by the example of the gods, they openly exhorted one another to perpetrate the filthiest crimes. So Catullus (68.137–140):

"Lest, after the manner of fools, we be too troublesome — often even Juno, greatest of the heaven-dwellers, has blazed at her husband's daily fault, learning the very many thefts of all-desiring Jove."

XLV. To worship the gods even with the worst outrages was, for very many, a settled custom. That the Babylonians prostituted their own daughters in the temples, Herodotus relates (Book 1), and Strabo (Geography, Book 16).

XLVI. Another plague too, no less deadly, taking its origin from this impiety, settled upon the world. For after that nefarious persuasion about the gods had grown strong among the common people, all the most wicked rascals — especially those who belonged to the sacred [orders] of those gods — feigning their names and manners, committed and practiced innumerable outrages: rapines, adulteries, thefts, robberies. On mountains, in caves, woods, temples, at the banks of rivers, [and] the shores of the sea — all things were full of masks of this kind. Let an example be the most well-known story of the crime perpetrated in the temple of Isis at Rome, which Josephus relates (Antiquities, Book 18, ch. 4). Mundus, a Roman knight, after he had long tried in vain to storm the chastity of Paulina, a most honorable matron, was secretly led into the temple by the priests under the name of Anubis — the woman's husband consenting — and ravished her. Most of the ancient heroes were the bastards and base-born of robbers, brigands, tyrants, [and] priests.

XLVII. And this reason a noble historian gives — and not ineptly — why Romulus did not institute vestal virgins: "Neither did he establish virgin priestesses for the goddess, mindful (as it seems to me) of the maternal disgrace — [his mother], who in the ministry of that goddess had lost her virginity" (ravished, namely, by a priest) — "nor, if he had caught any priestess of the goddess violating her chastity, could he have punished her according to the ancestral laws, because of the memory of the domestic shame" [Greek], Dionysius of Halicarnassus (History, Book 2, ch. 65).

XLVIII. In the same manner, that the Roman sacrificers — while the authority of ignorance flourished everywhere, and the papacy held evangelical truth captive — committed, masked, every crime, no one among the papists themselves dares to deny, unless he be sturdily impudent. For after

they had persuaded the blind and stupid common people that certain placid "spirits" [genii], not very hostile to the human race, frequented their houses by night, and did things which it is shameful to relate, they themselves, masked under the name of those [spirits], really did all those filthiest crimes which they wished — falsely, most ineptly, ridiculously — to ascribe to those innocent spirits, forsooth. So our Chaucer, of a certain assembly of friars, most well known in his time:
"For there as wont to walken was an elf, / There walketh now the Limitour himself. / In every bush, and under every tree, / There nis none other incubus but he."

[Part 7 ends here. Section XLIX shows, from Iarchas in Philostratus and from Cicero, that the poets made virtue all but impossible by setting up the most wicked men as gods; it opens Part 8, continuing on p. 109.]

XLIX. Hence the Indian philosopher Iarchas, reproaching Apollonius with this impiety, affirms that the observance of virtue was rendered well-nigh impossible to the Greeks (Philostratus, Life of Apollonius, Book 3, ch. 7): "But," he says, "those who are held the wisest poets among you do not permit you to be just and good, even if you wished [it]" [Greek: hoi de ge sophōtatoi poiētai hēmōn, oude boulomenois dikaioi te kai chrēstoi einai synchōrousin hymin ginesthai]; and he adds the reason — that they held the most wicked men in the place of gods. And that Apollonius himself openly confessed (Book 5, ch. 5): "For the things feigned about the heroes," he says, "of which the whole material of the poets consists, corrupt the hearers; since the poets recount unnatural loves — marriages of brothers and sisters, and calumnies against the gods, and the devourings of children, and illiberal knaveries, and altercations [Greek]; for these things, when they are brought forth by the poets as if [really] done, lead men to lust, and to envy, and to the desire of riches and of a kingdom." For no one can think that he sins while he imitates the gods. Marcus Tullius too, in the first [book] On the Nature of the Gods (ch. 16), repeats the same: "I have set forth," he says, "not so much the judgments of philosophers as the dreams of madmen; for these are not much more absurd than those things which, poured out by the voices of the poets, have done harm by their very sweetness — [poets] who brought in gods inflamed with anger and raging with lust, and made us see their wars, fights, battles, wounds; their hatreds, moreover, dissensions, discords, births, deaths, complaints, lamentations, lusts poured out in every intemperance, adulteries, chains, couplings with the human race, and mortals begotten of an immortal." And what Plato discoursed to this end in the Timaeus, let the reader consult, for it is worth reading.

L. The lawgivers and the philosophers were brought in as substitutes for the poets. To those [lawgivers] it pleased to forge a certain natural theology — which really was nothing else than the idolatry of the first apostates, in the worship of the works of creation.

For, the benefit of the traditions being lost (utterly deleted, namely, by the fables of the poets), and the mind of the common people being besieged, moreover, by insuperable prejudices, they could never — scarcely even tried to — free natural theology from the impure and most pernicious fables, and from idolatrous worship; [free it] neither from these nor from that.

LI. But the lawgivers, in forging a theology, had well-nigh nothing else in mind than so to temper religion that nothing of disturbance or evil should arise from it in the civil state. The same is still the opinion of very many politicians about religion. They therefore held it necessary to inscribe in the tables of the laws all the errors and fables, and the whole idol-madness, with which the poets had infected the peoples — provided only they did not oppose, "with bare head" [Greek: gymnē tē kephalē; that is, openly], as they say, the public government of the cities, or honest morals.

LII. It is worth the trouble to behold Plato himself succumbing to the prejudices of the common people. So, then, he [says] in the Timaeus: "But concerning the other [gods] — those which we call δαίμονας — to speak of their generation, or even to conceive it in mind, is beyond our faculty or powers; but faith is to be given concerning them to those who in ancient times explained the nature of the gods to us, being themselves (as they affirmed) the offspring of the gods, and so knowing their own ancestors. For faith is not to be denied to the children of the gods, although they speak without probable signs or necessary arguments; but, because they profess to report their own affairs, known to themselves, the ancient law and custom must be obeyed, and faith given to them. So, then, as it was handed down by them, let the origin of these gods be held" [Greek]. Thus he — mindful, namely, of what had befallen Socrates his teacher, who was adjudged to death because he was unwilling to hold for gods those whom the city held for gods. He admits, therefore, the common gods — though not without open mockery of the credulity of the common people. He discourses the like in the second book On the Republic. That the lawgivers first, on set purpose, devised fables in religion, to restrain the minds of the peoples by a certain terror of invisible evils, Strabo teaches (Geography, Book 1): "The thunderbolt," he says, "and the aegis, and the trident, and the torches, and serpents, and thyrsus-spears are the arms of the gods — fables, and all archaic theology; and these the founders of the commonwealths set up as bugbears [Greek: mormolykas] for the childish-minded" [Greek]. And so it was once done by the sacrificers in the papacy. For after they had, with the grossest ignorance and wicked morals, extirpated all the efficacy of the Christian religion from the minds of the common people, they had to invent purgatory — and I know not what other scarecrows — to hold them somehow to their duty.
LIII. And this was the "political" theology [Greek: politikē] of the Gentiles; of whose sacred things

Seneca [says]: "All which the wise man will observe as commanded by the laws, not as pleasing to the gods." Since, therefore, the common people did not suffer the poetic frenzies to be snatched from them, and would rather be cast out of the state than from a wicked opinion about religion; and since the lawgivers were content to seize all the ancient fables for their own purpose — the wise repudiated this whole "political" theology as a fable, devised for preserving order among men in civil life. So the bad merchandise grew cheap, and was always held [as] of little worth.

LIV. The philosophers seemed more sharp-sighted. They indeed looked round on all things most skillfully, investigating the lost theology. But, to say it in a word, they could not extricate themselves from the corrupt traditions and the portents of the poets, except by the help of opinions drawn from the sinks of atheism. From these came forth the "natural" theology [Greek: physikē]. That this was the "invention" [Greek: heurēma] of men hardened in the greatest crime, and not knowing how to yield to truth, Eusebius so clearly shows in the books On the Preparation for the Gospel that there is no need to add anything to them. And so that kind of men too "became utterly vain, and their foolish heart was darkened" [Rom. 1:21].

LV. And by these degrees all knowledge of God was corrupted. But among the bad — not the worst — ways of cultivating religion, the common people seem to have retained [theirs], who were wise in nothing beyond obscure traditions interpolated with the portents of fables. Those who courted the praise of most skillful investigation and acumen in sacred things plainly "became fools" [Greek: emōranthēsan]. The old opinion about a judgment somehow to be exercised after this life, and the diverse state of men surviving among the dead, obtained among the common people; it never wished to dissent from itself in that matter. How much weight that persuasion brought toward restraining the malice and impiety rampant on earth, is plain. And even when at length all that theology of the former ages had been deleted everywhere among the nations, yet those who live outside the knowledge of Christ have some presentiment — I know not what — of that judgment and future state. But that whole opinion, as an empty scarecrow, the well-nigh entire senate of philosophers laughed at (I know not whether with "sardonic laughter" [Greek: gelōta sardonion]).

[Part 8 ends here. Section LVI takes up the superstition of the nations (δεισιδαιμονία) and the various etymologies of "superstition" (Cicero, the comic poets, Servius); it opens Part 9, continuing on p. 111.]

LVI. And this was the superstition of the nations. By that word it has pleased [me] to express "deisidaimonia" [Greek: δεισιδαιμονία]. But of [its] etymology [Greek: onomatologia] the account is various. That "superstition" was so called from those who prayed all day that their sons might "survive" [superstites] them, the parent of Roman eloquence [Cicero] affirms. That parents daily brooded over that vow, the comic poets witness:
"As you wish your only son to survive your life, safe and a survivor." (Plautus, Asinaria 1.1.6)
"By Castor, a clever boy is born to Pamphilus; I pray the gods that he may be a survivor." (Terence, Andria 3.2)
And in the Heautontimorumenos (5.4.7): "So may you be a survivor to me, and to him."
Servius [derives it] from the little old women who, since they are survivors through much age, "and addicted to vain things, while they wish to seem too religious, dote."

Nor otherwise Donatus: "The superstitious," he says, "are old men and old women, who, [being] survivors now through much age, dote"; whence also "the superstitious [are those] who fear the gods too much," which is a sign of doting. Hence the Satirist:

"Lo, a grandmother, or an aunt fearing the gods, has taken the boy from the cradle, and his forehead and moist little lips, with the infamous finger and with lustral spittle, she first expiates." (Persius, Sat. 2.31–34)

Which custom is retained in the papists' baptism. Lactantius will have "the superstitious" [to be] those who "cultivate the memory of the surviving dead" (Institutes, Book 2, ch. 28). The superstitious man is also he who is "inspired" [Greek: entheos] and "oracular" [Greek: thesphatos]. So Plautus: "This man is superstitious indeed; he speaks true things" (Curculio 3.27). But superstition denotes either a depraved affection of the soul about divine things, or a vain and inept worship. In the former [sense] Cicero and Plutarch said very many things most falsely — he [Cicero] here and there, [Plutarch] in a peculiar and elegant writing "On Superstition" [Greek: peri deisidaimonias]. In the other sense, Tacitus impiously calls our religion a "deadly superstition" (Annals, Book 15); and Suetonius [calls the Christians] "a race of men of a new and maleficent superstition" (in Nero). Some too rejected all religion under the name of superstition:
"Vain superstition; virtue alone [is] the goddess in the breast."

LVII. Which madness — would that today no one could be found who would so rave! But although the religion of the heathen was nothing else than a most pernicious superstition, they themselves yet acknowledged a distinction; and they asserted that this [superstition] was necessary on account of the promiscuous multitude, which would not be held [in check] without it. Excellently Strabo: "Lamia is a fable, and the Gorgon, and Ephialtes, and the bugbear [Greek: mormolykē]; and the many who dwell in the cities are led to exhortation [toward virtue] by the pleasant [parts] of the fables, when they hear the poets recounting fabulous deeds of prowess — such as the labors of Heracles or of Theseus, or honors bestowed by the gods; or, by Zeus, when they see paintings, or images, or molded figures intimating some such fabulous turn of fortune. But [they are led] to dissuasion [from vice] when they receive [accounts of] punishments from the gods, and fears, and threats — whether by words, or through untimely places — or even believe that some have fallen into them. For it is not possible, by a philosophic discourse, to lead on a crowd of women and every base multitude, and to call them to piety and holiness and faith; but one must [do it] also through superstition" (Geography, Book 1) [Greek]. Who would not think he hears the supreme pontiff speaking from his chair! This vice the great Plato, as [he does] all things, excellently explains: "It is a custom, especially with all women, and with the weak in every way, and those in danger, and those in want — in whatever way one is in want, and, on the contrary, when they obtain some abundance — to consecrate whatever is at hand, and to vow sacrifices, and to promise the founding of shrines to gods and demons and the children of gods; and, in apparitions when awake, through fears, and in dreams likewise, recollecting many visions

[Greek] — while they try to bring remedies for each, they have filled all houses, all streets, with altars and chapels, and have dedicated places, as it were pure, with these rites" (On the Laws, Book 10, near the end). With what other words anyone could "more emphatically" [Greek: emphatikōteros] paint that superstition which flourishes in the papacy, I plainly know not. So, of religion and superstition, Tully (in the second book On Divination, near the end): "Wherefore, as religion is to be propagated — [religion] which is joined with the knowledge of nature — so all the roots of superstition are to be cast out. For it presses and urges, and, wherever you turn yourself, it pursues: whether you have heard a soothsayer, or an omen; whether you have sacrificed, or beheld a bird; if you have seen a Chaldean, [or] a haruspex; if it has lightened, if it has thundered, if anything has been struck by [lightning from] heaven; if anything like a portent has been born or done — of which it is necessary that something happen for the most part — so that it is never permitted to stand with a quiet mind." These things I have related, that we may know that, under every profession of any religion whatsoever, superstition is everywhere like itself.

LVIII. For the rest, the things which pertain to the religion, or superstition, of the heathen have been handed down to memory by very many. What was, according to their mind, the method of cultivating religions, Varro, Cicero, Ovid, Festus Pompeius, Macrobius, Censorinus, Pliny, and most of the historians in the Life of Numa expound. That some "little fragments" [Greek: apospasmatia] of truth lay hidden in the "rites handed down from the fathers" [Greek: patroparadotois] is certain. But of those things which fell from [men] either unwilling, or doing something else, or [actually] investigating the truth itself — testimonies of a purer theology — we have an ample enough harvest in Josephus, Justin Martyr, Clement of Alexandria, Eusebius, Theodoret, Lactantius, Augustine, Steuco of Gubbio, Mornay, Grotius, and innumerable others among the more recent. The ineptitudes, folly, impiety, fury, [and] madness of the worship — and of the whole religion which flourished among them — after the apostle (in the first chapter of the Epistle to the Romans), Tertullian, Eusebius, Augustine, Theodoret, and others excellently lash; [while] Maimonides, Giraldi, Stuck, Vossius, [and] Selden expound the origin, practice, [and] secrets of idolatry — Hesiod, Cicero, Plutarch, Strabo, Pliny, [and] Seneca not disagreeing — elegantly and lucidly.

[Part 9 ends here. Section LIX shows how at length the wisest, ashamed of all the rites and worship of the gods, withdrew to the contemplation of nature and the observance of the right and honorable (Socrates in Cicero) — the chapter's close on the total destruction of Gentile theology. It opens Part 10, continuing on p. 113.]

LIX. But after religion among the nations, emerging out of an endless heap of fables, had grown into so great a mass of superstition and idolatry that nothing more foolish, or more abhorrent to all natural theology, could ever have been devised, at last every wisest man began to be so ashamed of all the rites and of the whole worship of the gods, that beyond the contemplation of nature and the observance of what is right and honorable they wished to have nothing further in common with the gods. So Cicero concerning Socrates (Academic Questions 1.4): "Socrates," he says, "seems to me to have brought philosophy down [ p. 114 ] to common life, so as to inquire about virtues and vices, and generally about things good and evil; but the heavenly things he judged either to be far removed from our knowledge, or, even if they were fully known, to [contribute] nothing to living well." And Tacitus, Annals 6.22: "As I listen to these and similar things, my judgment is uncertain whether the affairs of mortals are turned by fate and unchangeable necessity, or by chance; indeed you will find the wisest of the ancients, and those who follow their school, divided; and in many is planted the opinion that neither our beginnings, nor our end, nor in short men at all, are any care to the gods." And Seneca: "We shall worship in such a way as to remember that his worship pertains more to custom than to reality." To these answer the words with which Marcus Tullius closes his most learned books On the Nature of the Gods: "When these things had been said," he writes, "we parted in such a way that to Velleius the argument of Cotta seemed the truer, while to me that of Balbus seemed to lean nearer to the likeness of truth." And he had already set forth his mind concerning all divine things at the beginning of the first book (ch. 5): "We are not," he says, "men to whom nothing seems true; but men who say that to all true things certain false things are attached, with so great a likeness that in them there is no sure mark for judging and assenting."

LX. That they should drift in this way pleased Satan, because he had nothing certain to set in the place of the truths he had taken away. Hence when the Athenians consulted Pythian Apollo as to which religions they should chiefly hold, the oracle was given: those which were in the custom of their ancestors, as the same Cicero testifies in On the Laws. And so at length every good and wise man settled into that religion which Lucan ascribes to Cato:

"This was the school of hard, unmoved Cato: to keep the mean, to hold to the end, to follow nature, to spend his life for his country; to believe himself born not for himself but for the whole world. To him a banquet was to have conquered hunger; a great palace, to have shut out winter with a roof; costly raiment, to have drawn over his limbs the rough toga after the manner of a Roman citizen; the greatest use of Venus, offspring; for the city he is father, and for the city husband; a keeper of justice, a guardian of rigid honor; good for the common weal." (Pharsalia 2.381-391)
And Persius [spoke] excellently for that state of things, and that uncertainty by which all men were tossed:

"What good is it to bring our morals into the temples, and to infer the gods’ good pleasure from this sinful flesh? Tell me, you pontiffs, what is gold doing in a holy place? Just what the dolls do which a maiden has given to Venus. Why do we not give to the gods what the blear-eyed offspring of great Messalla cannot give from his great dish: right and duty well ordered in the soul, the holy recesses of the mind, and a breast steeped in noble honor? Let me bring these to the temples, and I shall sacrifice acceptably with plain meal." (Satire 2.62ff.)

LXI. At last, therefore, weary of the fables, they poured themselves out into contempt of the gods at whatever occasion offered itself. Let that most religious orator himself serve as an example, [in his speech] for his own house, before the pontiffs, against Clodius: "You say," he declares, "that the things I am accustomed to proclaim about myself are not to be borne; and, witty man that you are, you even put on urbane and elegant talk: that I am accustomed to say that I am Jupiter, and likewise to assert that Minerva is my sister. I am not so insolent in saying that I am Jupiter as I am unlearned in supposing Minerva to be Jupiter’s sister. But at least I claim a virgin for my sister; you did not allow your sister to be a virgin. But take care lest you ought to call yourself Jupiter, since you can rightly call the same woman both sister and wife." (On His House 34)

LXII. The Athenians even publicly betrothed their Minerva, with solemn ceremonies, to the drunken Antony when he pretended to be Bacchus, as Dio testifies (Histories, bk. 48). Nor was there any expectation of help or aid from [the gods] on the part of men placed in dangers. Hence king Porus, when his friends urged him to sacrifice to the river, that it might not bear up the rafts of the Macedonians nor grant Alexander an easy crossing, answered, "It is not for men who bear arms to make supplication" [Greek: ouk esti ton hopla echonton to katarasthai], as the wise men of India relate in Philostratus, Life of Apollonius 2.10.

LXIII. Let those who think Nero is to be numbered among men see in Suetonius how he treated the gods. Nor without cause did Seneca (in the tragedy Octavia, act 2) ascribe to him that utterance which, as the prophet testifies (Isa. 44), would suit all idolaters well enough: "Shall I foolishly fear the gods, when I myself make them?" Nor otherwise Achilles in the greatest of poets [Homer]: "You have wronged me, Far-worker, deadliest of all the gods; surely I would repay you, if only the power were mine." [The verses are from Iliad 22.15, 20; Goold’s text prints "Il. x."] And Propertius: "Good Mind, if you are a goddess, I give myself to your shrine: so many vows of mine had fallen on the deaf ears of Jove."

LXIV. But Tertullian, shrewdly, that is, in his own manner, lashes all this impiety in his Apology: "Your household gods," he says, "whom you call Lares, you handle with household authority, pawning them, selling them, changing them, sometimes making a cooking-pot out of Saturn, sometimes a ladle out of Minerva, as each one has been worn down and battered; each is worshipped just so long, until its owner has felt some more sacred domestic necessity."

LXV. Having set forth the cradle and the progress of the theology of the Gentiles, its catastrophe must now be dispatched in a word. God indeed determined, from the laying of the world’s foundations, to send at the appointed time "a light for revelation to the Gentiles" [Greek: phos eis apokalypsin ethnon; Luke 2:32]. But it pleased him to hide this mystery in himself for a while, having permitted all the nations to walk in their own ways until, covered with thickest darkness and every misery, that saving light [ p. 116 ] should at last shine forth the more welcome. Yet he did not so bury this counsel of his will in the secret chest of his infinite wisdom but that, out of supreme mercy toward the human race, and out of a most just and most holy wrath against false, empty, worthless gods and idols, there came forth both promises of this grace and horrible threatenings. For "Jehovah is the true God, the living God, and the everlasting king: at his wrath the earth shall be shaken, and the nations cannot endure his indignation. Thus," he says, "shall you say to them: these gods, who made not the heavens and the earth, shall perish from the earth and from under this heaven" (Jer. 10:10, 11). Likewise he threatens that he will famish all the gods of the earth (Zeph. 2:11). And it is certain that God aimed these threatenings at those fictitious gods and idols under whose auspices, and in obedience to whose instigation (that is, to the old serpent [working] in them), the four most famous empires had continually declared war upon him and upon his people. But concerning them all, the matter has long since been settled. They have departed from their shrines; their heaven and their sun, their mountains and islands, have fallen; they themselves, with all their worship, their pageantry, and their worshippers, have been removed out of the earth and from the regions that are under heaven. At the very first ray of the rising Sun of righteousness the idolatrous world felt, and grieved, that its gods tottered, nay, shuddered:
"The oracles at Delphi are silent, and darkness concerning the future condemns the human race." (Juvenal, Satire 6.554)

And nowhere do the wisest of the Gentiles trifle more than in assigning the causes of the cessation of the oracles. "It may be," says Cicero (On Divination 1.19), "that that power of the earth, which stirred the mind of the Pythia with divine breath, has vanished through age, just as we see that certain rivers have vanished and dried up, or have been twisted and turned aside into another course." Plutarch agrees with him. Nor is there anyone who offers anything better.

LXVI. But God, sending forth his saving light by degrees, at length so established his ancient oracles [that is, the prophetic Scriptures] that there is no mortal anywhere among the nations who makes use of that gentile theology, or who worships and venerates those ancient idols with the ancient rites. Thus all those vain, absurd, and blasphemous things with which the world, "lying in the evil one" [Greek: en to ponero; 1 John 5:19], through so many centuries of years and an unspeakable stretch of time, out of the cunning of Satan and the native blindness of the human mind, had befouled, polluted, and rendered useless natural theology, have utterly perished by the sentence of God’s avenging judgment.

Chapter IX. The power and efficacy of the gospel in overthrowing Gentilism — All the Bellarminian notes of the true church fit Gentilism — An examination of the prejudices they plant in the minds of men — Satan’s last effort on behalf of his worship.

I. Whoever weighs a little more attentively the dominion which old Gentilism held over the human race, what roots it had driven down through a long series of ages in the whole world, what fruits [ p. 117 ] it bore, and its total disappearance [Greek: aphanismos], will perhaps find his own mind not unmoved by those questions with which, in Cicero, Velleius the Epicurean harries the Platonic account of the origination of all things. "What machinery," he asks, "what tools, what levers, what workmen were there for so great a work?" (All of which, however, as Maffei and Ribadeneira relate, God the Father showed to Ignatius, father of the Jesuits.) [Owen’s irony: the Jesuit biographers claimed Ignatius was shown the mysteries of creation in a vision.] It is impossible, I say, that it should not occur to the mind to consider what and of what kind was that force, or hidden power, which God employed for removing so great a mass, which had grown up from immemorial origin, "like a tree through hidden age" [Virgil]. For to accomplish so great a work, and to cast down the ancient and catholic foundations of depraved superstition, there was plainly need of the finger of God.

II. That this may appear more clearly, I will show in few words what pretexts the human race had for retaining its catholic and most ancient superstition, and on what prejudices and reasonings it leaned in setting itself against divine truth. For prejudices give strength and sinew to error; from them, by the mediation of vain reasonings, arise pretexts by which opinionated men [Greek: doxastikoi] fortify the approaches of the mind against the light of truth in order to retain it. Now men who in their worship are pleasing to God are called the church. For that assembly, or community of men, which strives to please God in celebrating the worship which he himself has instituted, is the true church, and always has been. That this assembly, or community, can be distinguished from all others who would falsely arrogate that name to themselves by certain notes, is the opinion of many learned men. The most learned Bellarmine determined that the chief of these are fifteen in number, and showed at length that there is in them a certain incredible power for procuring credit to that assembly to which they properly belong. Those who judge by the evidence of those notes that they have found the true church will sooner suffer themselves to be cast out of life than out of its communion. But in very truth all those Bellarminian notes, or at least most of them, and those by far the most excellent, are fitted by nature rather to adorn heathenism with the title of true religion than to point out that divine truth, and the profession of it, which we have learned from the teaching of the very Son of God, or its professors. But if by far the greatest part of those who are called Christians, as they boast of themselves, is persuaded that it ought even now to trust those notes so far that, rejecting the most certain evidences [Greek: tekmeria] of divine truth, it believes them alone in the business of religion and commits to them the direction of souls into the truth, how much more rightly ought the heathen to have clung tooth and nail to that profession of divine worship which was fortified with those notes, since all other indications of heavenly truth were utterly unknown to them!

III. Now the first note of the true church for Bellarmine is the very name catholic; that church, namely, which is called catholic is the true church. This note, being almost worthless, the learned man without doubt sent ahead to perish, like a light-armed soldier. We know in what place and at what time the disciples of Jesus were first called Christians; but the origin of this name [ p. 118 ] catholic is as uncertain as the sources of the Nile. It does not occur in holy Scripture. Irenaeus, reciting the rule of faith, makes no mention of it (bk. 1, ch. 2); nor does Tertullian, in the Prescription against Heretics; and "by the appellation of a name nothing is judged either good or bad, apart from the actions that fall under the name," as Justin rightly says (Apology 2). The Roman church, indeed, calls itself catholic; it was not so called of old by holy men. To Ignatius it was the church "which presides in the place of the region of the Romans," if indeed that epistle is Ignatius’s. To Clement, "the church sojourning at Rome." And the pope is called "the bishop in Rome" by the first council (canon 6). And in what esteem both he and the Roman church were then held, the Council of Chalcedon sufficiently teaches. To very many Christians, dispersed for many ages through the whole world, it is the pontifical, papistical, apostate, idolatrous, Roman [church]. Since therefore the name is the invention [Greek: heurema] of uncertain men, let us dismiss the quarrel about it and look at the thing itself.

IV. From the time that this name, the catholic church, was devised by certain men, it began wonderfully to please all worshippers of true religion (since there were not lacking those who would most foolishly shut up the church of Christ within the limits of this or that particle or faction of Christians); and indeed not altogether without cause, since by that word they meant to express nothing but what our Savior himself had foretold would come to pass, namely, that the gospel was to be preached "in the whole inhabited world" (Matt. 24:14), or "in the whole world" (Matt. 26:13), or "into all the world" (Mark 14:9), and not to the Jews only. And the fame of that word, having quickly become celebrated, grew daily with praise. But when the Roman pope had climbed by evil arts to that summit of secular power, so that it was easy for him to draw flatterers to himself by the hope of plunder, he attempted to draw that title to himself and to his faction out of their flatteries (using also to his advantage the carelessness and even the humility of others); he meanwhile, with no illiberal hand, measuring back to them in return things which no sober man would believe were ever placed in his power. So it was done of old, as in Horace (Epistles 2.2.99-101): "I depart Alcaeus by his vote: and he by mine, who is he? Who but Callimachus? If he seems to demand more, he becomes Mimnermus, and grows by the chosen surname."

V. By the same right the Mohammedans are Mussulmans [that is, "the faithful"]; and Simon [Magus], of old, was "the great power of God" (Acts 8:10); and Diana, a great deity. Everywhere in the world even now those who are the more numerous adorn themselves with honorable titles. "Faction," of course, is the name given to the fewer, or to the oppressed party. Let us see, then, whether the heathen themselves did not arrogate to themselves alone those names which in those times were held among men of the best repute, while the worshippers of the true God were everywhere branded with reproachful names. Before all things they both constantly boasted and most fiercely contended that they alone cultivated faith and religion, and that all others were superstitious, ignorant of the ancient gods, and, so to speak, heretics and schismatics. Until the coming of our Lord, we know that the worship of the true God flourished among the Jews alone. And what [the heathen] thought of them, a most weighty author is witness, Cornelius Tacitus (Histories 5.4): "Moses," he says, "in order to bind the nation to himself for the future, introduced new rites, contrary to those of the rest of mortals. There everything is profane which among us is sacred; and again, things are permitted among them which to us are incestuous." That is, they are heretics and schismatics, because the lawgiver [Greek: ho nomothetes] taught them to observe rites abhorrent to the catholic usage of the human race. Plutarch has almost the same. And there is scarcely one of the historians who has not handed down to memory that they venerated the head of an ass in their temple. "The effigy of the animal," says Tacitus (that is, on his own showing, of an ass), "by whose guidance they had dispelled their wandering and their thirst, they consecrated in the inner shrine" (Histories 5.3, 4). And Democritus the historian, in Suidas: "They worshipped the golden head of an ass." That Posidonius and Apollonius Molon handed down the same, Josephus shows in the second book Against Apion, who raved with the same madness. I pass over the other monstrous lies; yet a comparison of them with the things which the pontificians, the Catholics forsooth, have invented about the Albigenses, the Waldenses, and other faithful servants of Christ, would not be unprofitable. With one mouth all declared that nation to be given over to superstitions, opposed to religion, and repugnant to the common judgment of mortals concerning the worship of God.

VI. At length the Christians arose; they also for some time were called Jews. Nor did the Gentiles long distinguish between the two peoples. "The Jews, who at the instigation of Chrestus were constantly raising tumults, he expelled from Rome," says Suetonius in his Claudius. And Arrian, in Epictetus (2.9): "Do you not see in what sense each man is called a Jew? And when we see someone halting between two positions, we are accustomed to say, He is not a Jew, but is acting a part. But when he takes on the disposition of one who has been dipped [baptized] and has made his choice, then he both really is, and is called, a Jew." Some adduce this passage in vain to prove that the Jews were accustomed to use the rite of baptism, since the philosopher is most plainly speaking of Christians. So too in Revelation 3:9 the Holy Spirit himself understands by "Jews" none other than Christians. And concerning these [the Christians], the same Tacitus sets forth the judgment of the world (Annals 15.44): "Nero inflicted the most exquisite punishments on those whom the common people, hating them for their abominations, called Christians. The author of that name, Christus, had been put to death in the reign of Tiberius by the procurator Pontius Pilate. And the deadly superstition, suppressed for the present, was breaking out again." And Arnobius teaches that the Christians were reproached with everything in their manners and sacred rites being contrary to the human race. But as to the name of religion and of the worship of the gods, [the heathen] were so secure that they determined that the very name Christian contained crime enough for inflicting the last punishment, or heretic-slaying [ p. 120 ]. Whence also Tertullian and others found it necessary to compose an apology for the name itself. "The circumcised," "Apella," Jews, Christians, heretics: these, among those Catholics, were the names of crimes. With equal step, therefore, each idolatrous people begins its march. If, then, there is any force or efficacy in honorable names, as the cardinal judges, for indicating the truth and confirming the mind in the opinion which they adorn, the worshippers of Gentilism used it most of all.

VII. The second place with Bellarmine is occupied by antiquity; not indeed that absolute antiquity which refers its origin to God alone, in which, if this controversy were made to rest, it would be all over with these Bellarminian notes; but that doubtful and merely probable antiquity which must be dug out of histories and other monuments of things past, and judged from them. Now upon this same argument, or prejudice, the whole of Gentilism once rested. For when Jesus Christ himself first came forth to declare the heavenly truth from the bosom of the Father, the Pharisees assailed him first of all with this question: "What new doctrine is this?" (Mark 1:27).

VIII. In like manner the philosophers assailed the apostle Paul as he preached the same truth at Athens. "May we know," they say, "what this new doctrine is, of which you speak?" (Acts 17:19). And in the whole of that contest which lay between the gospel and the superstition of the Gentiles, there was almost nothing which more violently stirred the minds of unbelievers into rage against the truth than that the Christian religion was new, that it was destitute of antiquity, that it endeavored to take away the ancient rites and the ancestral worship of God. And how great a prejudice that persuasion planted in their minds may easily be conjectured from the pontificians themselves, hardened as they are by the pleasure, or rather the profit, of their errors. But that the antiquity which the Romanists pretend is in all things doubtful, and in most things openly false, very many have shown. And it was evident that, those monuments of history alone excepted which (because they belonged [Greek: oikeia] to Jews, heretics, and schismatics) they thought themselves entitled to despise, there existed, since the birth of mankind, no record at all of events or religions which preceded the origin of their worship; so that none doubted that the religion they professed was eternal. Symmachus uses this argument in his epistle to Theodosius: "If long age," he says, "procures authority for religions, faith must be kept with so many centuries, and we must follow our fathers, who happily followed theirs. Let us imagine that Rome herself now stands beside us and pleads with you in these words: Best of princes, fathers of your country, reverence my years, to which pious rite has brought me; let me use the ceremonies of my ancestors." And Caecilius, in Minucius Felix: "How much more venerable and better it is to receive the teaching of your ancestors as the high priests of truth, to cultivate the religions handed down to you, to fear the gods whom your parents taught you, before you knew them familiarly, to adore; and not to pass judgment upon the deities, but to yield to your forefathers." Whence Augustine shrewdly: "This is the devil’s custom, that falsehood should be commended through the channel [ p. 121 ] of antiquity." Nay, the very Indians of America, as the Jesuit Xavier testifies (bk. 4, ep. 1), were accustomed to boast of the antiquity of their religion against the novelty of the gospel. I am not now arguing that Bellarmine fights against evangelical truth with the weapons of the heathen; I am showing how great an undertaking it was to overcome and abolish heathenism, when its professors were fortified with those reasonings which that most learned man judged to have so great a power for holding men’s minds in the opinions they have once taken up.

IX. Edmund Campion no doubt thought he had done something splendid when, to those arguments by which he tried to persuade the unskilled of the pontifical religion, he added as a finishing stroke: "Witness the universities, witness the tables of the laws, witness the native manners of men, witness the election and inauguration of the emperors, witness the rites and anointing of kings, witness the orders of knights and their very mantles, witness the windows, witness the coins, witness the city gates and the civic houses, witness the fruits and life of our ancestors, witness all things great and small, that no religion in the world but ours has ever settled down with deepest roots." Although these things are utterly trifling, yet they do show clearly that that religion had spread itself far and wide over no small space of time, had seized the minds of very many, and had corrupted their manners. But with how much greater pomp and show of argument all these things could be said of Gentilism, at the time when it was at war with evangelical truth, no one is ignorant. For the superstition of the heathen had not invaded Europe alone, or only some of its more celebrated parts, as that of the pontificians has, but from the most ancient ages, beyond all the credit of history and the memory of men, it had so occupied the whole world that there existed nothing at all in the whole earth which did not bear testimony to its dominion. The answers which Clement, Justin, Origen, Tertullian, Augustine, Ambrose, and others opposed to this pretext of antiquity are well known. If therefore that doubtful antiquity which can be traced out of the monuments of things and deeds among men is a presumption of truth, surely no mortals, in any age or in any cause, could ever produce it with greater evidence and a greater multitude of testimonies than the patrons of heathenism. Without doubt most men were not more persuaded that they were men, or that they were alive, than that the religious worship of their nations had flourished from the beginning of things, if indeed all things had a beginning, and that it was eternal if they themselves were eternal. Nothing therefore did they cry out more loudly than: everywhere, always, by all. [Owen turns the Vincentian canon, "quod ubique, quod semper, quod ab omnibus," into the heathen’s own plea.] Hence Radbod, king of the Frisians, when he was about to be baptized and had dipped one foot in the water, asked where the greater part of his ancestors were placed, in hell or in paradise. When it was said that the greater number were in hell, drawing back at once, "It is more proper," he said, "to follow the many than the few."

X. Next, the cardinal puts in the third place long and never interrupted duration. If he means times past, this note [ p. 122 ] really differs not at all from the one that went before, unless the difference lie in this, that here that duration drawn down from ancient times is called never interrupted and long-continued. But if he speaks of times to come, that note is nothing but the learned man’s conjecture; although I for my part could well believe that the Roman church will endure until he comes who shall destroy her with the breath of his mouth [2 Thess. 2:8]. But the heathen also destined perpetual duration for their worship in their prayers, and confirmed it by predictions. The poet, meaning to show that he had won himself an eternal name, says: "I shall grow ever fresh in the praise of posterity, so long as the pontiff climbs the Capitol with the silent virgin" (Horace, Odes 3.30.7). Or if he would play the historian, he is by no means to be compared with the Gentiles. The beginnings of idolatrous superstition are plainly unknown to this very day. But that at least two thousand years flowed by from its rise to the preaching of the gospel in the world is not doubted; nor was its profession ever interrupted. The thing is well known and needs no proofs. And this prejudice, arising from the antiquity and long duration of that religious worship which they observed, had driven such roots into their minds that it ought to be no wonder if they scarcely suffered themselves by any means to be torn away from a most pernicious opinion so fortified.

XI. In the fourth place the cardinal enumerates the multitude and variety of believers. That he should assign this as a note of his church, the sectaries, wherever they are, would doubtless pay a great price; since it runs directly against that note of the true church which Christ himself published, if indeed the flock of Christ be the true church ["Fear not, little flock," Luke 12:32]. But since the pope will have it to be a note, let it be a note. He who would contend that multitude is not a note of the true church, if this dispute is to be settled by the word of God, and that the Roman faction ought to be called neither absolutely nor comparatively great or numerous, would in my judgment have an easy case in hand. But that is not our business now. Very many of the pontificians are wise and learned, and most practiced in the use of affairs. They know thoroughly what things are apt and fitted to allure the minds of mortals to take up an opinion in religion, or to persuade them to cling to it tooth and nail. How great, therefore, the force of multitude is in this cause, they have well observed.
