Nay, it is now almost a long time since all the hope of their cause has seemed to them to rest on nothing else than that they are many; and therefore, dismissing all other arguments, they long ago resolved to decide the business of religion everywhere by the sword. Meanwhile we know that this note belonged most properly to Gentilism. They were the most; they were all; they were the whole world. The sons of the mathematicians affirm that the highest mountains do not at all hinder the earth from being called perfectly round. Much less could the tiny flock of the worshippers of the true God seem to stand in the way of its being said that the whole world lay in the evil of Gentilism [ p. 123 ]. For almost whatever of the human race existed anywhere had agreed with singular concord upon polytheism: and, good God! what men, what emperors, kings, generals, philosophers; wise, good, just, temperate, brave men, lovers of their country, and holders-fast of equity and goodness beyond what can be imagined; that men should suffer themselves to be torn and separated from the religion of these, that is, of absolutely all men, and especially of the best; what could be said or imagined more abhorrent to all reason than that, bewitched by the persuasion of a few men, they should renounce the conviction of the whole world held through so many ages? Look, pray, reader, at this corner of the world in which, by God’s gracious disposing, we pass our days: how few are there who would not count it the height of madness to depart from the most received opinion of the majority; especially when that departure lies exposed to some of those evils which rushed in throngs upon the first preachers of the gospel. And yet we all profess that we owe our hearing not to multitude, not to antiquity, but to the single word of Christ. But the catholicism of the pontificians will never equal the amplitude of Gentilism, no, not if it should burst itself.

XII. Next in Bellarmine comes the succession of bishops in the Roman church; the same argument holds among the Mohammedans for the caliphate. Old Rome likewise had her supreme pontiffs [pontifices maximi], whose title, name, place, office, and spirit that Bellarminian bishop has invaded, seized, and now possesses (Rev. 13:11-13). The succession of those pontiffs from the first consuls down to Tiberius Caesar, under whose rule the Christian name began to be known, was recorded in the public registers. And men believed that the supreme authority of these had been divinely constituted. So the orator, to the pontiffs, in the speech for his house (chs. 1, 12): "While many things, O pontiffs, were divinely devised and instituted by our ancestors, nothing is more excellent than their resolve that you, the same men, should preside both over the religions of the immortal gods and over the highest interests of the state." In which opinion all the Romanists persist to this day. He immediately adds words which even now fill both pages in the disputations of the pontificians about the church: "For what is so arrogant as to attempt to instruct the college of pontiffs concerning religion, divine things, and sacred ceremonies; or so foolish as to recount to you whatever someone may have found in your books; or so officious as to wish to know those things about which our ancestors willed that you alone should be consulted and you alone should know?" And not one of those pontiffs was a woman, or a magician, or a murderer, or incestuous, or sacrilegious, so far as we know from the records of those times. [The contrast with the papal succession, with its Pope Joan legend and its criminal popes, is left to the reader.] But to take up an opinion in the business of religion differing from these men seemed nothing short of utter madness.

XIII. Nay, so great was the authority of those supreme pontiffs that scarcely could anyone dissent from their judgment in religion with more impunity than now from that of the pope himself. For the historian so describes their authority that you can scarcely tell whether he speaks of old [ p. 124 ] or of new Rome. "The pontiffs," he says (by the institution, namely, of Numa), "hold the greatest authority among the Romans... they are also lords of the greatest affairs; for they judge all sacred causes, between private men, magistrates, and ministers of divine things" (which is still the solemn distribution of the Roman church into its various kinds of members), "and they make laws concerning whatever sacred things are unwritten and not settled by custom, judging which laws and usages seem suitable to them. All the magistracies to which sacrifice and the service of the gods are committed, and all the priests, they examine. Their servants also and ministers, whom they use for sacred things, they watch, that they transgress nothing of the sacred laws. To private men who do not know the observances belonging to the gods and divine powers they are expounders and interpreters; and if they perceive any not obeying their commands, they punish them, looking to each particular case. They are themselves liable to no judgment or punishment, nor do they render account either to the senate or to the people. When one of them dies, another is appointed in the place of the deceased, chosen not by the people but by the college itself." (Dionysius of Halicarnassus, Roman Antiquities, bk. 2.) Whether Dionysius could exhibit a more accurate description of the pontifical authority if he were living at Rome now, as he once did, may justly be doubted. "The pontiffs," he says, "are endowed with the highest authority and bear rule in the greatest affairs. For they judge all sacred suits between private persons, magistrates, and ministers of sacred things" (in the executing of which part of his authority the Roman pontiff has disturbed every Christian kingdom whatsoever, and has even ruined and overturned some); "but they also make laws concerning sacred things which are not yet written nor received by usage, if they seem worthy to be secured by laws and customs" (that is, they alone were judges of unwritten traditions, so that whatever they wished, and whenever, they ordained to be observed by laws and canons; which is the best-fortified citadel of the present pontifical power); "likewise they inquire into all magistracies to which sacrifices and the worship of the gods are committed, and into all priests; they also keep the ministers of sacred things to their duty, that they may commit no offense against the sacred laws" (they alone, forsooth, being the fountain and origin of the whole clergy of the Lord, of all ecclesiastical order and power); "to private men also they point out beforehand and interpret the worship of the gods or divine powers" (but for those private men, "idiots," it is enough if with implicit faith they reverently observe and embrace what these men prescribe in the worship of the divinities); "and if they observe anyone despising their prescriptions, they fine him according to the magnitude of the offense" (but that those punishments were capital, except in the case of the Vestal virgins, we do not read. Yet there is scarcely a region in all Europe which the second order of Roman pontiffs have not stained with deadly slaughters, blood, flame, and deaths of various kinds for the neglect of their prescriptions); "they themselves are exempt from judgments and fines, nor are they bound to render account either to the senate or to the people" (that is, the pontiff judges all men and is judged by no man. And sweetly indeed does "unaccountable" [Greek: anypeuthynos] sound in pontifical ears. But, will they nill they, they shall at last render account to the Judge of all); "and if [ p. 125 ] one of them departs from life, another is wont to be substituted in the dead man’s place, not by the votes of the people, but he who seems most fit to the college." Just as is done at Rome to this day. Let the reader learn from this whose successor and whose vicar the Roman pontiff is.

XIV. In the next place he sets forth agreement in doctrine with the ancient church. But since from that "ancient church" he excludes the apostles and their writings, he means nothing else than that that is the true church which teaches concerning sacred things just as the predecessors of those who compose it have taught. But this was the best-fortified citadel of the whole of Gentilism. They counted nothing more foolish, nothing more worthy of reproach, than to reject rites handed down from the fathers [Greek: patroparadotous; cf. 1 Pet. 1:18]. That was superstition, which was ignorant of the ancient gods. Socrates was condemned to death for no other cause than that he held those not to be gods whom the city from antiquity had believed to be gods. Against the Christians above all such cruelty raged because they were deserters of the ancestral and national religion. How much trouble the idolaters made for the professors of the gospel with this argument, Origen, Justin, Tertullian, Arnobius, Lactantius, Augustine, Clement of Alexandria are witnesses, and every single one of those who undertook the defense of the truth against their sophisms. Therefore neither were the heathen free from this prejudice against the gospel.

XV. The seventh Bellarminian note is the union of the members with one another, and with their head, the Roman bishop. One is ashamed of such insolent boasting; that union which flourishes among the Romanists is the greatest reproach of the Christian name; and unless that reasoning which pleads utility were the strongest with most of them, it would long ago have come almost to nothing. Take away force, the most savage tyranny, burning alive, ignorance, avarice, secular pride, the love of this present world, and that union will at once appear too watery to subsist. And of what sort it is, the wars, robberies, butcheries, slaughters, devastations of cities, and depopulations of fields bear witness, which are daily perpetrated and carried on among the members of that church, the Roman pontiff protesting in vain. Were they not stupid, blind, or the most shameless of mortals, and equally most ignorant of evangelical truth, they would long since have been ashamed of this most insane boast. For my part, I had rather the church were divided into a thousand pieces than papistically united. The discords, dissensions, schisms, political and ecclesiastical, of minds, of opinions, of writings, which flourish in that conspiracy of men which insists on being called the church, the whole world knows; only in this do these crafty men, bewitched by the things of this world, seem to agree, for various reasons, political, secular, Satanic: that they should strenuously oppose evangelical truth and the liberty procured for Christians by Christ himself. Yet I would not assert that there is no unity at all among the pontificians, since the Spirit foretold of the pope that he would "cause all, small and great, rich and poor, free and bond, to receive a mark in their right hand" (Rev. 13:16). Among the heathen most things were otherwise. That strong man armed kept his whole house, [ p. 126 ] the world, in peace and union [Luke 11:21]. There was a certain diversity in worship, not unlike that which is seen among the religious orders, of whom one prefers this saint, another that. As to the sum of the matter, the old idolaters cultivated peace among themselves most harmoniously under their head Satan and his vicar, the supreme Roman pontiff; especially after the world had been ceded to the city. Maecenas, in Dio, persuaded Augustus not to allow various religions to be introduced, nor other gods to be adored or preached than those he himself worshipped, as we have shown elsewhere; and the introducing of foreign gods was forbidden by the law of the Twelve Tables. That it was impious and unlawful for Romans to take up usages in sacred things foreign to those of the city, they argue against the doctrine of Paul (Acts 16:21). Egypt almost alone was accused of heresies. In crushing the truth there was no dissension. "The pagans," says Augustine in his Sermon on Fasting, "worshipping many diverse gods, gods even at law with one another and burning with hostile hatred among themselves, yet hold in them a kind of unity; while they go together to their temples, though their gods are angry with each other, they themselves are of one accord." And elated by the opinion of this concord they were accustomed to reproach the Christians with their dissensions, as the same Augustine testifies (On the Shepherds, ch. 15), and Clement of Alexandria (Stromata, bk. 7).

XVI. Let us go on with the cardinal. In the eighth and ninth places he mentions the sanctity of doctrine, together with its efficacy, and the holiness of its authors. Beyond all doubt the most holy doctrine is the most true. For the gospel itself is "the truth which is according to godliness," as the apostle testifies to Titus (1:1). And the same is most efficacious, since "the word of God is living and efficacious" (Heb. 4:12), "nay, the power of God unto salvation to those who believe" (Rom. 1:16). By it are regenerated "as many as are born of God" (1 Pet. 1:23). And its author is holy, since he was none other than the most holy Son of God (Heb. 1:1, 2; John 1:18). But what sanctity is, what is true or false, we cannot know unless taught from that word. Moreover, the sanctity which can be seen can be feigned; and that which cannot be feigned cannot be seen. Now the sanctity of the Roman church is attested by very many men of all orders within her, indeed by almost all: by the clergy especially; it is attested by the lives, manners, and deaths of the popes, such as even heathens would detest; by the pride, haughtiness, avarice, ignorance, sloth, cruelty, and rapine of the bishops; by the cunning, frauds, impurity, hypocrisy, and unnameable sins of the religious, as they are called; by the idolatry of them all; above all by the frauds, impostures, lies, treasons, murders, lusts, perjuries of the Jesuits, and their skill in extenuating the sins of men of every order. And the sanctity of that church has at last advanced so far that among the American Indians, where they ought most of all to have adorned it for the honor of Christ and the gospel, the very name Christian has been rendered utterly hateful and abominable; so that, whatever is at last to be concluded about them, they obstinately and stubbornly refuse to have anything in common with Christians or their religion. Luis [ p. 127 ] de Granada complains that the matter has been brought to this pass, "that among the barbarous nations the Christian name is so execrable that, when monks are sent to them, lest they detest them with a like hatred, it is necessary to say that they indeed are not Christians, but fathers, who have come to care for their salvation; for the Spaniards treated them with such cruelty that the most holy name of Christian was held to be a name not of piety and religion, but of cruelty and savagery." The most holy Lord Jesus will at his own appointed time exact the penalties of this sanctity. The fathers of the Society also acted holily when, in preaching the gospel among the Chinese, they studiously concealed that Jesus Christ had been crucified, and so persuaded them to put their faith in some other Jesus, I know not whom; which at last was prohibited at Rome. But it is a wonder that Bellarmine dared touch this topic, since he knew well that it would be the easiest thing in the world to compile, out of approved histories, out of the writers of his own church, out of daily examples, huge volumes most full of the execrable crimes, lusts, and impieties of the chief fathers of that church. But this is not our task. If the most learned man speaks of a sanctity that strikes the eyes of men, all confess that it must be measured by some rule, either divinely given or in some way received. But such was the doctrine of certain philosophers among the heathen, that many Christians even now are not ashamed to call it most holy. Its efficacy in changing the manners of men very many examples prove; and how great, among the heathen, was the sanctity of life according to the precepts of philosophy, is well known. Excepting faith alone, and the knowledge of the true God in Christ, which alone is eternal life, of those things which strike the eyes there seems to have been almost nothing lacking to some of them.

XVII. Next he reviews the miracles which he contends are wrought in his church, and not elsewhere. Nowhere do some of the pontifician writers bear themselves more magnificently than when it comes to miracles. Here, if anywhere, Valerianus Magnus shows himself a man in recounting the miracles of the Capuchin brethren; let the reader for whom the Golden Legend of Jacobus de Voragine is not enough consult him, and also the animadversions of that most learned man John Comenius upon that Valerian history of miracles. If anyone delights in juggleries, trifles, figments, lies, in things vain, useless, monstrous, diabolical, ridiculous, and blasphemous, let him go to the legends, the writers of the lives of the saints, the archives of the monks, the canonizations of the papal saints, and the compilers of the ecclesiastical annals after the fourth century, and they will supply him abundantly with whatever of that kind can anywhere be scraped together or invented. One only out of a thousand I have thought worth repeating here, by which judgment may be formed of the rest; it is recorded in the Book of Conformities. When Francis, says the author, "was once preaching, and an ass was making a tumult among the people, Francis said to him: Brother ass, be quiet until I have finished my sermon. Immediately the ass, obedient to the word, [ p. 128 ] settled himself with composed bearing at Francis’s feet." It is a wonder if there remains in the whole world an ass who would not at once surrender himself to the power of that church in which this whole efficacy of working miracles resides.

XVIII. Meanwhile, as all know that true miracles, wrought by the immediate power of God for the confirmation of heavenly truth, such as were those of our Lord Jesus Christ and of the apostles, had great force and efficacy for stirring the minds of men to listen to the truth itself and to believe it; so it is most certain that diabolical portents and wonders whose causes cannot be traced, and figments sold and believed as true miracles, are most powerful for ensnaring the common people in superstitious credulity, and for making them embrace with obstinate reverence the dogmas which seem to be confirmed by them. Now miracles of this sort were most frequent among the heathen. There is scarcely one among the more approved historians, Greek or Latin, who has not committed very many of them to writing. What Porphyry wrote of Pythagoras, and Philostratus of Apollonius, is well known; to such a degree that Hierocles impiously contended that that magician of Tyana should be compared with Christ himself in the brilliance of his miracles; whose wicked audacity Eusebius beat back in a separate book. But this indeed is nothing to the pontificians, with whom it is customary to swear that this or that friar wrought more miracles than Christ himself and all the apostles. Innumerable are the wonders of this kind which Diodorus, Livy, Polybius, Plutarch, Cicero, Pliny, Suetonius, Tacitus, Caesar, Pausanias, and Dionysius of Halicarnassus scatter through their pages. Valerius Maximus collected some of them (bk. 1, ch. 6); I do not wish to dwell on particulars, or to compare them with the miracles of the pontificians. Let it suffice to have pointed to that most celebrated history of the deliverance of the temple of Delphi from the sacrilege of the Gauls. Pausanias, among others, relates it in his book on Phocis. That Satan set his throne among men, above all at Delphi, for several ages, all know. Nowhere on earth did he more impudently sell himself as the god of this world. The Gauls, bursting into Greece under the leadership of Brennus, undertook to plunder the temple of Apollo built there, enriched with the votive offerings of almost all nations and peoples. When, having passed Thermopylae, Brennus made straight for Delphi, the townsmen, struck with fear, fled to the oracle; the god bade them be of good courage at once and fear nothing: "He promised that he himself would defend his own." Immediately all was full of prodigies: "Sudden portents from the god, the most manifest of any we know of, appeared against the barbarians. First the earth, as much of it as the army of the Gauls occupied, quaked violently for almost the whole day; thunderings [ p. 129 ] and lightnings came without ceasing, which not only terrified the Gauls, but so stunned them that they could not hear the commands given by their leaders. Moreover there were seen rising up against them the apparitions of heroes, Hyperochus, Laodocus, and Pyrrhus," and others. "With sufferings and panics of this kind the barbarians were gripped through the whole day; but things far more grievous were to seize them in the night. For there came a sharp frost, and snow with the frost; and great rocks slipping down from Parnassus, and crags breaking off, made the barbarians their mark; and not one or two only, but thirty and even more at a time, as they stood on guard together or took their rest together, were crushed in heaps by the falling cliffs." (Pausanias, Phocis [10.23].) Terrified in this manner, broken, and scattered, they were destroyed to a man. So it pleased God to avenge the wicked impiety of the human race, and its defection from the knowledge and worship of himself. For since they did not see fit to retain God in their knowledge [Rom. 1:28], God delivered them into the power of the prince of darkness, and permitted him, for their greater hardening, to put forth so many and so great prodigious miracles in defense of the citadel of superstition [Greek: deisidaimonia]. In the same manner he foretold that he would deal with the followers of antichrist, and so he has dealt. For because they did not receive the love of the truth, that they might be saved, "God sent them an efficacy of error, that they should believe a lie," and delivered them over to be deluded by him "whose coming is according to the efficacious working of Satan, with all power and signs and lying wonders," as the apostle teaches us (2 Thess. 2:9-11). Yet there is this difference between the degrees of the two efficacies, that the papal antichrist has never yet anywhere wrought so many miracles and prodigies, or so great, as Satan put forth in the patronage of heathen idolatry. For although myriads of such portents and prodigies, set against the single testimony of holy Scripture, are not to be valued at a straw, yet if any such things had ever anywhere happened in defense of the pontifical rites as occurred at Delphi, it is easy to guess how greedily the pope’s devotees would seize on them for their cause, and with what insolent boasting they would everywhere exalt themselves. Relying therefore on this prejudice also, namely of miracles, [the heathen] did not easily suffer themselves to be torn from the darkness of their errors. For what? Should they desert gods so powerful, so propitious, so present, of whose care and concern for them and for their worship they had such illustrious proofs, whose deity, power, supreme might, and benefits had been made manifest by so many wondrous prodigies, at the preaching of a new religion, unheard of before in the world, a work plied by a few unknown, superstitious, worthless men? That indeed seemed [ p. 130 ] abhorrent enough to all wisdom, constancy, faithfulness, and virtue. Should it be thought right to receive an unknown God, a crucified deliverer, a Jewish religion, abandoning their fathers’ gods, after they had used them in war and peace through an innumerable series of years? It would be better to die than to admit this supreme crime and disgrace. Yet how the gospel surmounted these mountains of prejudice is known to all.

XIX. The prophetic light is Bellarmine’s twelfth note; not indeed that which dwelt in the divine writers of the Old and New Testaments; not that of Jesus Christ and his apostles, on which all Christian faith rests; but the cardinal means a certain gift of foretelling things to come, which he says some persons in the Roman church have had. That false prophets have existed in every age, as much within as without the people of God, all know; and there is nothing by which Satan has more bewitched the minds of men, and made them more subject to himself, than by predictions of things to come. Among the gentiles lived the Sibyls, and gentiles they were; whose oracles were most celebrated, and all of them served for the confirmation of the foulest idolatry, as appears from all the prophecies taken out of their books which are extant in Livy, Cicero, and others. The whole world venerated the Delphic oracle and others. How far Satan can foretell things to come, and how far God may will to permit him, we do not now dispute. This is certain, that among the gentiles it was a most settled persuasion that the prophetic light resided with them alone, that their gods alone were foreknowers and foretellers of things future, and that they had communicated that gift of foreknowing to many; and so, possessed by this prejudice also, they stubbornly declared war on the truth.

XX. In his thirteenth note, which is the confession of adversaries, the cardinal extricates himself unhappily; had he not beforehand resolved to fill up the number of fifteen notes, he would beyond all doubt never have picked up this most foolish commonplace from the crossroads. But would that there had been none among the ancient fathers, the schoolmen, and others, who went so far in their concessions to gentilism as to affirm that its adherents, provided they lived according to reason [Greek: meta logou], could have been saved in that superstition; never will the cardinal wring a more shining testimony from the mouth of any adversaries of his church.

XXI. The unhappy end of those who have opposed themselves to the church constitutes the fourteenth note. This too the idolaters claimed for themselves. We have related the utter destruction [Greek: panolethria] of the sacrilegious Gauls. The gold of Toulouse passed into a proverb. [Aurum Tolosanum: the plundered sacred gold that brought ruin on all who touched it.] The unhappy ends of those who, neglecting or despising [ p. 131 ] the religions, undertook any business whatever, all the historians relate. And from this it may be gathered how much confidence the heathen placed in this note: that when the gospel first began to be known, the metropolis of all superstition [Rome] had grown, through various successes and prosperous events, to such a height of secular felicity that it had no higher point to climb. And thence they constantly affirmed that all unlucky events, all unhappy issues of wars, and all the evils with which the human race was in any way afflicted, were to be charged upon the Christians and their religion. Whence Tertullian: "They put forward, in defense of their hatred, this vanity also, that they consider the Christians to be the cause of every public disaster, of every popular misfortune. If the Tiber rises to the walls, if the Nile does not rise over the fields, if the sky has stood still, if the earth has moved, if there is famine, if plague, straightway the cry is, ‘The Christians to the lion!’" Cyprian has similar things in his letter to Demetrianus; Augustine, On the City of God 3.30 and 5.22; and the latter part of the hundred and twenty-sixth of the Questions to the Orthodox runs thus: "And how is it that Hellenism is not shown to be the holier, since, so long as it held the cities, all the cities and the fields possessed prosperity and abundance, though they were more often at war; but from the time the Christian preaching took hold of them, they have become destitute of houses, of inhabitants, and of all other abundance; and the cities, scarcely retaining the remains of the buildings raised of old by the Greeks, show what they once were, putting forward the two religions as the causes of the two states, the old abundance and the new desolation?" It is therefore most certain that Bellarmine here fights with none but the weapons of the Gentiles. Let the apologetic writings of the old Christians be consulted: Justin, Tertullian, Arnobius, Augustine, Orosius show clearly what the heathen concluded from this note, and what ought in truth to be concluded.

XXII. Temporal felicity divinely granted is the last Bellarminian note of the true church. Had he come sober to the destruction of the church, he would scarcely have employed this note. It is heathen, it is Mohammedan, and above all contrary to the word of God. With it, indeed, the idolatrous crowd of the Jews in Egypt contended against the prophet Jeremiah (Jer. 44:15-18): "Then all the men who knew that their wives burned incense to other gods, and all the women who stood by, a great assembly, indeed all the people who dwelt in the land of Egypt, in Pathros, answered Jeremiah, saying: As for the word which you have declared to us in the name of Jehovah, we will not listen to you. But we will certainly do whatever word has gone out of our own mouth, burning incense to the queen of heaven, and pouring out drink offerings to her, as we have done, we and our fathers, our kings and our princes, in the cities of Judah and in the streets of Jerusalem: for then we were filled with food, and were merry, and saw no evil. But since we ceased burning incense to the queen of heaven, and pouring out drink offerings to her, we have wanted all things, and have been consumed by the sword and by famine." Let the cardinal go now and teach us what difference there is between his note and this note of the Jews. On both sides they argue most fiercely, and affirm, [ p. 132 ] on the one hand, a felicity divinely granted on account of which they gave themselves to the worship of the heavenly ones, and on the other, that they have reaped the greatest misery for its neglect. But what are those things, I pray, bread, namely, food, and mirth, if they are compared with what the heathen orator brings forward in the assertion of this note? "Let us love ourselves as much as we please, conscript fathers," he says, "yet it is not in numbers that we have surpassed the Spaniards, nor in strength the Gauls, nor in cunning the Carthaginians, nor in arts the Greeks, nor, in short, in that native and inborn sense of this very race and land, the Italians themselves and the Latins; but in piety and religion, and in this one wisdom, that we have perceived that all things are ruled and governed by the divine power of the immortal gods: in this we have surpassed all peoples and nations" (Cicero, On the Responses of the Haruspices 9). If the cardinal were to hear a man of consular rank discoursing most gravely on the deeds, victories, and prodigious successes of the Romans, and abusing all these for the confirmation of the vainest superstition, what answer, pray, would he have to make? That these things furnish a note of the true church, and so of true religion and worship, he would not deny; whether he would surrender is uncertain; by what means he would protect himself and the truth, it is very hard to guess. So Dionysius of Halicarnassus (Roman Antiquities, bk. 2) affirms that he set forth the Roman rites "so that those to whom the piety which the Romans practiced has hitherto been unknown may cease to wonder that all their wars came to the happiest issue; for they will find that they entered upon them all most religiously and with the most just causes, and for that reason above all had the gods propitious to them in dangers."

XXIII. And all these things are said to this end, that that divine force and efficacy of the gospel, or of the word of the cross of Christ, by which it set up signal trophies over all idolatrous worship within a brief space of time, may shine forth the more: relying on that word alone, and on the presence and power of Christ in it, a very few men assailed numberless men, the whole world; the poor assailed the lords of the world; the simple, the wisest; the unlearned, the most learned, fortified as these were with prejudices, reasonings, most ancient traditions, miracles, and those very arguments which men who are Christians at least in name still count irrefragable; and they did not cease until, through a thousand perils, deaths, tortures, and innumerable hazards, they had subdued the whole world to God and to Christ.

XXIV. But as soon as the light of the gospel began to shine out, Satan seems to have had a presentiment, from many signs, of the ruin of his catholic and most ancient dominion. Yet, that he might not seem to flag, or to fail himself in his extremity, he left no stone unturned to fortify himself and his own against the heavenly truth, and to crush it. For besides driving his slaves with monstrous cruelty to rage against the professors of the gospel, that, if it could be done, he might smother the truth in the blood of those who embraced it, he also attempted in various ways to restore that superstition [ p. 133 ] in which he alone was all in all, and to give it new strength and new luster. For since there were two parts of Gentilism, namely, vain opinions about God and divine things, and idolatrous worship, there were in each part very many things most universally received among all men, from which human reason itself, when it called them to examination, could not but recoil. Lest therefore any from these things should first seize an occasion of doubting concerning that whole most vain superstition in which they had been ensnared, and so of searching out the truth, as soon as the fame of the gospel spread abroad in the world, he stirred up great and learned men who, preserving his autocracy [Greek: autokratoria], should devise and defend opinions about God and divine things more spiritual and less abhorrent to right reason, and should establish modes of celebrating worship at least less absurd and ridiculous than those which had been in common use. In the first part of the work Amelius, Eumenius, Plotinus, Proclus, Hierocles, Celsus, and others diligently served him. For these men, though they were most tenacious of idolatry, yet carefully set forth more sober opinions concerning God than the common run of philosophers had delivered before, and exploded vain, false, and noxious notions about the deity. And it is most certain that in this endeavor they availed themselves of the benefit of that very truth which they hated, which had already cast its rays into the minds of men. Of those who labored to varnish over the nefarious worship and its most wicked mysteries, the chiefs were Apollonius, Porphyry, Iamblichus, and Julian; and in these stood the last effort of collapsing Hellenism. But the word and Spirit of God prevailed; until, the malice and impiety of men growing strong, almost the whole world again basely fell away from the true and pure worship of God.
END OF BOOK I.

books.title: Of the Restoration of Theology after the Fall and the Entrance of Sin, and of Its Renewed Corruption; or, of the Adamic, Sublapsarian, Antediluvian Theology.

blocks.text: Chapter I. Theology corrupted by degrees, restored by degrees — Theology in the abstract, divine doctrine — The degrees of revelations — The rise of supernatural theology — The first degree in the promise of the seed of the woman — In that promise a new covenant given, entered, established — The force of the word berith [Hebrew: berit, covenant] — The nature of the covenant between God and men, Gen. 3:15, explained — Christ the promised mediator — The Lamb slain — The chief heads of the new theology — What the Targumists, and Paul (Heb. 2:14), adduce on Gen. 3:15 — What the last days are — Christ the seed of the woman — The malice of the more recent Jews — The a-theology of the Socinians — The notorious [ p. 134 ] blunder of the Vulgate interpreter — The blasphemy of certain commentators — The madness of Fabricius Boderianus — Adam’s repentance and thanksgiving — The precepts of the new theology, what and of what kind — The institution of sacrifices — The trifles of the pontificians, the Socinians, and Episcopius about their origin — The opinion of Smalcius refuted — That of Porphyry examined — The sum of the Adamic theology after the fall.

I. What belongs to the general notions of theology we have set forth in the preceding book. We have also shown the rise, use, and disappearance [Greek: aphanismos] of the implanted [Greek: emphytos], or original, theology. We must now proceed to treat of the institution of another, substituted theology in its place. But since neither was that natural theology corrupted all at once, nor was this new theology, which we now take up, restored all together [Greek: aollêdên] and in one form [Greek: monoeidôs], but the one suffered its corruption, and the other obtained its perfection, in many parts and in many ways [Greek: polymerôs kai polytropôs; Heb. 1:1], I have thought it well to set forth briefly the more notable degrees of each.

II. We have taught before that theology may be taken either in the abstract (as they speak) for divine doctrine, or in the concrete for a habit of mind in theologians, that is, a habit "for the discerning of good and evil" [Greek: hexis pros diakrisin kalou te kai kakou] (Heb. 5:14). So Aristotle of astrology: "Astrology is the name both of the science itself concerning heavenly things, and of the sailors’ practical skill in those things." For astrologos meant the same of old as astronomos; he who predicts I know not what effects of the stars is now so named. Now all postlapsarian theology worthy of the name is of the same kind. It has been renewed several times, objectively only, by various degrees of revelations. That habit of mind, therefore, by which that doctrine is comprehended and perceived, is altogether the same in all theologians of all ages. But since we shall have much to say of it in the consideration of theology properly so called, the Christian, we have decided to defer all the doctrine of it to that place. Of the various degrees and progress of supernatural revelation, or of the new theology, we shall first treat briefly. The apostle intimates all this doctrine in Hebrews 1:1. And since the original theology underwent degrees of corruption almost matching them, these too must be spoken of.

III. After the entrance of sin, and the consequent unprofitableness [Greek: anôpheleia] and disappearance of natural theology, there came forth, out of the infinite abyss of divine grace, wisdom, and love of mankind [Greek: philanthrôpia], the Adamic antediluvian theology, or the very first [Greek: pamprôton] evangelical promise. Here supernatural theology planted its first footstep; here was its rise and its first degree. This revelation, therefore, of the mind and grace of God must be related first; and then, how the corruption of the theologians overtook its progress, and afterward the catholic apostasy [Greek: apostasia], and the horrible universal destruction [Greek: panôlethria]. We have shown before that all true theology rests upon some divine covenant. And although berith [Hebrew: berit] sometimes signifies a bare promise, and is first used in that sense in the Scriptures (Gen. 6:18; Exod. 34:10; Isa. 59:21), yet, since a promise of that kind holds forth some good which demands a restipulation of duties, or "the answer of a good conscience toward God" [Greek: syneidêseôs agathês eperôtêma eis theon; 1 Pet. 3:21] (cf. Jer. 31:33), we may pronounce that every divine covenant has its precepts and its promises. When [ p. 135 ] therefore we say that God entered into a new covenant with fallen Adam, we understand a new prescription of obedience, furnished with gracious promises. For constituting and confirming a covenant between God and men, nothing further is required. And we say that that covenant was of grace, because it was freely founded in another, that is, in a mediator. For whatever covenant could be entered into with men immediately, even though it exacted obedience with equity [Greek: kat’ epieikeian] and without rigor, would be a covenant of works. This new covenant, therefore, was of grace, because founded in another, who was bound to perform all its conditions. And of the promises and precepts of that covenant this new theology consisted.

IV. Now the foundation and nature of this covenant may be contemplated in that most celebrated promise of the conquering seed, Gen. 3:15: "And I will put enmity between you and the woman, and between your seed and her seed; he shall bruise your head, and you shall bruise his heel." Here Christ was first promised; whence he was afterward called the Word [Greek: ho logos]; that is, he concerning whom that saving word of God, or word of promise, was made. For "word" is very often used absolutely for a promise; as we say in English, "I will give you my word for it; and I will make good my word; that is my promise"; which was observed by the most learned Cartwright in his Harmony of the Gospels. In this promise, too, that Lamb was slain: to be slain, indeed, with respect to the actual event; slain, with respect to the saving effect, namely, from the foundations of the world (Rev. 13:8). This, I say, is the sum of the new theology. And here this river of paradise (so to speak) parts itself into four heads. First, it is here first revealed that righteousness, and by virtue of it gracious acceptance, is no longer to be sought at home and in obedience rendered by native strength, but to be freely received from another, who would ward off the peril of eternal death. Already then the question between God and the first-formed pair [Greek: prôtoplastoi] concerned death, the desert of sin, and the justification of sinners before God. In verse 9 God calls Adam to plead his cause; in verse 11 he arraigns him for sin and convicts him; in verse 19 he pronounces death upon the guilty; for by that most weighty expostulation, "Have you eaten of the tree of which I commanded you that you should not eat?" he shows that Adam had made the covenant of works utterly void, and so had cut himself off from all communion with God. The renewal of communion, therefore, through another is here in question; for God had taught most clearly that for sinful man there was no hope placed in himself. Secondly, that this deliverer, or that other one, through whom all manner of salvation was thenceforth to be expected, was to be brought into the world by incarnation [Greek: ensarkôsis], being made, namely, of a woman (Gal. 4:4). Whence he is here called the seed of the woman; which bringing-in the apostle calls "the bringing of the firstborn into the world" [Greek: eisagôgê tou prôtotokou eis tên oikoumenên] (Heb. 1:6), and by which he is said "to come into the world" (Heb. 10:5). As therefore sin entered the world through the woman, at the persuasion and instigation of the serpent, so God promises that through her [ p. 136 ] the remedy also should come forth. Thirdly, that he might stand forth a perfect deliverer, he must both suffer and conquer the serpent; for the bite of the heel by the serpent inflicts a deadly wound. God therefore foretells that the deliverer from the seed of the woman would taste death for sin and for sinners (Heb. 2:9). And whereas Satan wished to pursue and press the victory he had lately won over the human race to the eternal destruction of all, God promises that that seed would not only wrest the victory out of his hands, and divide the spoils with that strong one (Isa. 53:12), but would also utterly subdue Satan himself and overthrow his works. Lastly, that this best Savior and supreme conqueror, together with that everlasting righteousness [Hebrew: tsedeq olamim] (Dan. 9:24) which he was to bring back, must be apprehended by faith. This both the nature of the thing and the manner of the revelation demand. For when to lost and accursed sinners all manner of salvation is announced as to be sought and expected from some other, absolutely nothing more is required than that they trust in him and, denying themselves, place their whole hope in him, that they may become partakers of that salvation. The manner of the revelation consisted in a promise, and this also demands faith; for it cannot be understood how anyone should set himself to obtain the thing promised except by exercising faith in the promise itself. And it would be easy to prove that in these heads is contained, however obscurely, all the doctrine concerning the person and office of the mediator, free justification, repentance, eternal death, life and reward, and the resurrection of the flesh.

V. That this promise looked to the Messiah, the older Jews acknowledged; the more recent deny. The Targum of Jonathan ben Uzziel: "They shall apply a medicine to the heel in the days of king Messiah." The Jerusalem Targum: "They shall secure soundness in the heel at the end of the extremity of days, in the days of king Messiah." And "He shall remember it against you," or "be mindful of you," is the speech of one threatening; as we say in English, "Remember this," when we threaten requital. But both paraphrasts teach that the wound of the serpent will be incurable. To the same sense Paul, dealing with the Jews, expounds this promise out of their own common faith of that time (Heb. 2:14, 15): "Since then the children have partaken of flesh and blood, he also himself likewise took part of the same, that through death he might destroy him that had the power of death, that is, the devil; and deliver those who through fear of death were all their lifetime subject to bondage." What else do these words declare, than that a medicine or remedy was prepared, through the Messiah, for the bite of the serpent? And this was to be done "in the last days" [Greek: ep’ eschatôn tôn hêmerôn], that is, at the end of the extremity of days [as the Targum speaks]. That the days of the Messiah are the last days, the apostle asserts elsewhere also (Heb. 1:2). Further, that no medicine is prepared for the serpent, which both Targums expressly affirm, the same apostle teaches (Heb. 2:16): "For verily he does not take hold of angels, but he takes hold of the seed of Abraham." So also in Midrash Tehillim, on Psalm 1: "All things shall be healed in the age [ p. 137 ] to come except the serpent." That the apostle in the Epistle to the Hebrews disputes from principles formerly conceded among the Jews, we shall show (God willing) in our commentaries on that epistle. The more recent Jews, out of hatred of Christ, are of another opinion. Rightly Fagius: "Hence we may see that there was greater sincerity among the ancient Hebrews in the interpretation of the Scriptures than there is today among the more recent Jewish writers, who often of set purpose deprave the clearest and chief passages of Scripture, and maliciously wrest them to their own sense." Josephus of old stuck in the bark of the words (Antiquities 1.3). Philo invents I know not what allegories. The Socinians deny that any promise of Christ is contained here. "If we deny that there is a promise there, but a curse, and that, like all the others, a temporal one, signifying the enmity of men and serpents, which before did not exist, what, pray, will Carolius bring forward to prove that the Logos is there promised?" (Johann Sommer against Peter Carolius, bk. 2, ch. 3, fol. 73). Hence, in expounding the degrees of the revelation of the divine will, Volkelius passes over this place in perpetual silence (On the True Religion, bk. 2, ch. 8, pp. 13, 14); and so does he who treads in his footsteps, Simon Episcopius (Theological Institutes, bk. 2, ch. 11). Valentin Smalcius expressly (Refutation of the Theses of Frantzius, disp. 4, p. 94): "That the gospel," he says, "took the beginnings of its constitution forthwith in paradise — these are the words of Frantzius — is confirmed by no authority of the sacred writings and by no reason. If he should say that those words, ‘The seed of the woman shall bruise the head of the serpent,’ can be understood of Christ and of the victory which he won over Satan, which is the only thing that is urged by all and that can be drawn from these words, I answer that it makes nothing to the purpose. For there are many sayings in the sacred writings, uttered concerning other things, which afterward under the new covenant can be referred to Christ and to the things belonging to the new covenant. But to hold those for prophecies and promises belongs to men who have nothing from which to prove their opinions openly. And surely, if the beginnings of the gospel had commenced in paradise, it seems scarcely credible that it could have come to pass that none of the sacred writers of the new covenant should have alleged that prophecy at least once; yet that this was nowhere done is clearer than light itself." We have seen what this man’s judgment is concerning this evangelical prophecy and promise. He denies, and that boldly, that it pertains directly to Christ, or that any mention of it is made in the New Testament. But not all [Socinians] are of the same judgment: Jeremias Felbinger calls Christ "that seed of the woman, promised by God in paradise to fallen men, which should bruise the head of the infernal serpent" (Demonstration of the Gospel, p. 17). But that mention of this promise is made in the new covenant, Smalcius denies with too much impudence. The serpent is the devil (2 Cor. 11:3; Rev. 12:9, 14; 20:2); the ungodly are "offspring of vipers" (Matt. 3:7), "of their father the devil" (John 8:44), who was "a murderer from the beginning" (John 8:44) and "sins from the beginning" (1 John 3:8), through the seduction of the common parents of mankind, by which he slew all. [ p. 138 ] Next, the seed of the woman is Christ (Heb. 2:14; Gal. 3:16; 4:4). And that he has bruised the head of that serpent, the Socinians themselves, I suppose, will not deny. See John 12:31; 14:30; Luke 10:18; 1 Cor. 15:54; Heb. 2:14; 1 John 3:8; but of these things elsewhere. The Vulgate interpreter has ipsa conteret, "she shall bruise"; and from this the commentators babble I know not what blasphemy about the blessed Virgin, with plainly asinine stupidity. Zera [Hebrew: zera, seed] is of the masculine gender; to it answers the pronoun hu, "he himself." Therefore although the Seventy render zera by sperma [a neuter noun], and not by sporos, yet in place of hu they have auton ["him," masculine], attending to the thing itself, not the words, after the manner of the ancients. So the comic poet: "Where is that crime [neuter] who [masculine] ruined me?" The same form of speech is not unusual in the New Testament: "Disciple all the nations [neuter], baptizing them [masculine]" (Matt. 28:19); and the converse form, "Some [seed] fell by the wayside, and the birds of the air devoured it" (Luke 8:5); and "Again a new commandment I write to you, which thing is true in him and in you" (1 John 2:8); and Rev. 17:16, "And the ten horns [neuter] which you saw upon the beast, these [masculine] shall hate the harlot." Why? Because those horns were in truth kings, of the masculine gender. Jerome rejects that reading [ipsa] as contrary to the Hebrew verity (Hebrew Traditions on Genesis). Detestable, therefore, was the audacity of Guido Fabricius Boderianus, a learned man indeed, but enslaved to superstitions, who in the Plantin interlinear edition undertook to substitute hi [the feminine pronoun] for hu. That there is no trace of a variant reading here appears from the most diligent searcher-out of such variants, Louis Cappel (Critica Sacra, bk. 5, ch. 11, sect. 4); but instead of hi there crept in, either by design, that the crime of falsification might betray itself, or by the carelessness of the printers, hih, which is no Hebrew word at all. And that Adam, having received this promise, repented and was delivered from sin, the author of the book entitled the Wisdom of Solomon shows (10:1): "She" (Wisdom, namely) "preserved the first-formed father of the world, who was created alone, and delivered him out of his own fall." And the Targum on the Song of Songs (1:1) affirms, from Jewish tradition, that Adam, after the remission of his sin, on the Sabbath day uttered a song to God. By this new revelation of grace, of redemption, and of the remission of sins to be obtained by the death of the Mediator, the whole special nature of the theology of sinful men was utterly changed. The ultimate end common to all theology remained indeed the same, which is the eternal enjoyment of God. But that the renewal was made not only in doctrines but also in principles and means, the fuller account of evangelical theology will show.

VI. The precepts belonging to this new theology were of two kinds. For, first, all the moral precepts, in which the implanted [Greek: emphytos] theology in great part consisted, were carried over into the use of this new theology. That native light, directive of moral operations, which God put into the mind of the first man in his very creation, remained by His gracious economy [Greek: oikonomia] even after the fall, corrupt though it was and weakened, in part [Greek: ek merous] in all his posterity, as we have shown more fully above. And God did not cease to cherish that light daily, by his Spirit, in those who embraced this new revelation by faith. Hence, although those precepts had not yet been sealed by the spoken word [Greek: logos prophorikos], they nevertheless became a perfect rule of obedience, faith supplying to them a new use, and to the theologians a new light. [ p. 139 ]

VII. No other precepts were given to Adam as a rule of living, beyond the remains of the natural light discerning the honorable from the base. These, stirred up and directed by faith, sufficed for that end. And there are very many antediluvian examples of obedience rendered according to this law (Gen. 4:4, 6, 7; 7:1, 2). Of the seven precepts which some affirm were delivered to Adam, others to Noah, we must treat hereafter. This is most certain, that every human duty rests upon the precepts of God.

VIII. And indeed that implanted light, or inward [Greek: endiathetos] theology, extended itself to all the precepts of the decalogue. But as it seemed good to God, in the very state of integrity, to explain and confirm the second precept, by which his will concerning the manner of all worship is eternally established, through an arbitrary command suited to that state, namely, not to eat the fruit of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil; so also after the entrance of sin, and the promise of the restoration of sinners through the seed of the woman, it pleased him to establish the same precept by a new command suited to that state, through obedience to which men could express both their faith in the promise and the new use of the implanted law. This was the institution of sacrifices, which completes the whole Adamic theology after the fall.

IX. The pontificians indeed assign the origin of sacrifices to the light of nature, and contend that this rite of worshipping God would have had place and use in the state of innocence: Gregory de Valentia, On the Sacrifice of the Mass, bk. 1, ch. 4; Bellarmine, On the Mass, bk. 1, ch. 24; Lessius, On Justice and Right, bk. 1; Suarez on the third part of the Summa, dist. 71, sect. 8. The mass has need of this figment and of very many others, being itself the most monstrous figment of all. The Socinians surmise that this rite drew its origin from right reason, by which man concludes that he ought to worship God with the best things he has in his possession; and so the Arminian Episcopius: "Abel," he says, "by faith alone, induced by no divine precept, that is, by the instinct of right reason alone, judged that God was to be worshipped with the best things he had in his own store; that is, with sacrifices of the firstborn of his flocks and of their fat" (Theological Institutes, bk. 1, ch. 8, sect. 3). Trash [Greek: lêroi]! As if anyone could do anything from faith induced by no divine precept; or as if to act from faith were the same as to act from right reason without any precept; or as if there were any faith that did not look both to a precept and to a promise; or as if God had granted to the will of his creatures liberty to invent new modes of his worship; or as if the whole reason of Abel’s sacrifice had been that he determined God should be worshipped with the best things he had in his store! All of which is plainly atheological [Greek: atheologa]. The same Episcopius, elsewhere investigating the origin of this rite, again boldly lays down this opinion: "It may be believed without danger," he says, "that this rite of sacrificing was introduced from the very beginning of the world out of a certain great zeal and desire of obliging and honoring God by that singular act and service, in which all the best, dearest, and most precious things were expended on the honor of God; and thus it was publicly demonstrated that all those things were owed by greater right to God than to men, whose use and eating, and so whose will, they daily served: and that God, although perhaps the rite did not seem best to him (for it is not credible that God is delighted with the blood and slaughter of innocent animals; which reason perhaps even suffices for our believing that this rite was not commanded by God himself), [ p. 140 ] yet, because it proceeded from so religious a mind, willed to hold it acceptable, and of his kindness permitted it to be used continually thereafter; and so at length afterward nothing forbids that he also enjoined and prescribed it to his own most beloved people." Nothing forbids, say you, Episcopius? Nay, whatever can be known of God naturally, whatever he himself has revealed of himself or of his will in the Scriptures, forbids us to give credit to this most absurd fable. For what? Is it right to make the wisdom and will of God such handmaids of the wisdom and will of miserable sinners, that what they invented in his worship, though it was neither the best that could have been invented nor pleasing to himself, he should nevertheless not only graciously permit and accept, but also most severely, under penalty of death and cutting off, enjoin upon his whole church to be observed for two thousand years? Will that which may be known of God [Greek: to gnôston tou Theou; Rom. 1:19], which cries out that nothing but what is supremely perfect is to be ascribed to God, permit us to believe this? Will Scripture permit it, where God himself so often rejects and condemns all human inventions in his worship? Was there nothing in the ancient sacrifices beyond a desire of expending the best and dearest things on God? Nothing in them pertaining to Christ and his sacrifice? No specimen of the divine wisdom and goodness in them, except that he permitted mortals to walk in their own ways, which he does only by way of a great curse? No divine instruction, no prop of faith in the Messiah to come, established in them? But one is ashamed to linger over such idle songs.

X. We know what the human race is, and what manner of men they are of whom it consists; nor is it hard to guess what would have become of divine worship if even the least particle of it had been indulged to human discretion. Nor is it right to judge that that solemn rite, by which it pleased God to set forth the whole mystery of the death and merit of his only-begotten Son, drew its origin from any other fountain than from the proper counsel of his own will. Upon the entering of the new covenant, remission of sins was granted. But that this cannot come to pass "without shedding of blood" [Greek: chôris haimatekchysias], the apostle teaches (Heb. 9:22). Therefore that covenant was ratified, by God’s institution, with sacrificial blood (perhaps of the beasts from whose skins God fashioned coats for the first-formed pair, Gen. 3:21).

XI. Porphyry alone among the philosophers set himself of purpose to investigate the beginnings of sacrifices. It is worth while, therefore, to relate his opinion in passing [Greek: hôs en parodô], especially since it contains some things the knowledge of which can bring some light to certain places of holy Scripture. He undertakes the work in the second book On Abstinence from Living Creatures [Greek: peri apochês empsychôn]. First, then, he assigns the origin of all sacrifices to the grateful mind of men, and to the purpose of worshipping God with the best things they had in their possession: "The returns and thanks for benefits," he says, "must be rendered to different beings differently, according to the worth of the benefaction; and the greatest, and from our most precious things, to those who have done us good in the greatest matters... and the best and most precious things with which the gods benefit us are the fruits of the earth; with these therefore we ought to honor them." What he affirms here is true, and agreeable enough to holy Scripture; see Prov. 3:9. That the rite of sacrificing [ p. 141 ] had this origin, he there sets himself to establish at length. "For," he says, "as to good men, so also to them" (that is, to the gods) "we think it right to pay the firstfruits of our goods." He teaches that the Egyptians used this custom from time immemorial, and elsewhere that all others did the same from the beginning (sect. 5). And he affirms that right reason, and the observance due from it toward the best benefactor, demanded this religious worship; in which opinion certain Christians agree with him, as has been shown. Further, serving his thesis [Greek: douleuôn hypothesei] about not eating flesh, he goes over to the side of Cain and contends that only the fruits of the earth should be employed in sacrifices. For he relates that from the beginning of things and of nations sacrifices were made of herbs, grain, and flowers, not mixed with myrrh or cassia or frankincense with saffron, but pure, just as the earth brought them forth; and that only many ages afterward, error growing heavier by degrees, were offerings made of all other kinds of things. The most ancient nation, he says, the Egyptian, "first began to sacrifice to the heavenly gods from the hearth, not firstfruits of myrrh or cassia and frankincense mingled with saffron; these they did not offer at first; but of green herbs, as it were, lifting in their hands some down of the generative nature" (sect. 5). And this opinion he relates and confirms through the greatest part of that book. But the offering of animals, or of blood, in sacrifices, he contends was brought into use only very late, as wickedness, impiety, and superstition grew strong; and that while very many oracles disapproved that worship, and at last permitted it only with much caution employed: "To an overseer of sacred things," he says, "who was of the family of seers, and who wished to offer firstfruits of his sheep, they say the oracle granted leave, but with much caution; for its words run thus: It is not lawful for you to slay by violence the race of sheep, you who are sprung from seers; but if one of itself shall nod assent to a willing death, that one, with hands washed, you may sacrifice, I say, justly." [ p. 142 ] And that caution is plainly contrary to the divine institutions, in which all things that died of themselves were altogether unclean (Deut. 14:21). Further, holding to his design, he teaches that there is some supreme deity which, since it is good and holy, does, and can do, nothing but what is good, and pleasing and profitable to men. This, he judged, is natural to it, so that it is impossible for it to depart from it: "It is impossible," he says (sect. 38), "that these" (the good gods, namely) "should both furnish benefits and again, in the same things, become causes of harm." To these he adds the good genii or angels, "announcing the things of men to the gods, and the things of the gods to men, carrying up our prayers to the gods as to judges." But the worship of the best and greatest deity, or of the good gods, he argues, is to be performed not with sacrifices but with the laying aside of the affections, the abstraction of the mind from corporeal things, the contemplation of heavenly things, and the love and pursuit of them, as reason, not fancy, dictates. Moreover, that there are evil and noxious spirits or demons, who, inhabiting the air nearest the earth, are the authors and causes of all evils, namely, of diseases, famine, plagues, earthquakes, wars. And these he determines to be liars and proud, eagerly aspiring to occupy the place and office of the best and greatest God with the human race: "Falsehood," he says, "is proper to these; for they wish to be gods, and the power that presides over them wishes to seem to be the greatest god." These things he drew from the sacred books, which none of the old philosophers turned over more diligently, or with a mind more possessed by malice. For that saying, "falsehood is proper to these," most plainly reproduces those words of our Lord Jesus Christ (John 8:44): "he speaks a lie of his own, for he is a liar, and the father of it." The rest, derived from the same fountain, is turned aside into Platonic pools. But to those demons, he teaches, men bound to corporeal and earthly things, of which the demons themselves are masters, and given over to depraved affections, find it necessary to offer sacrifices: "For, as the theologians say, for those who are in bondage to outward things, and have not yet mastered their passions, it is necessary to avert this power also; otherwise they will have no end of troubles." And he contends at length that to these, and to no others, were the sacrifices of animals offered, with whose smoke and savor that in them which is ethereal, and comes nearest to corporeal substance, is fattened: [ p. 143 ] "He who is careful of piety sacrifices nothing animate to the gods, but to demons, and to other powers whether good or bad." Here we have the defendant confessing: that the nations, namely, worshipped not God but demons with their sacrifices; which crime the apostle Paul had before charged upon them in almost the same words (1 Cor. 10:20): "The things which the Gentiles sacrifice, they sacrifice to demons, and not to God." And so Eusebius (Preparation for the Gospel, bk. 3), Augustine (City of God, bk. 10), and Theodoret (Therapeutics, serm. 3) use this testimony against the nations. So that Lucas Holstenius, in his Life of Porphyry, pronounced not undeservedly: "No one at all of those outside [the church] supplied the doctors of the church with more or stronger weapons, with which they cut the throat of the manifold error of the gentiles, than this Porphyry." Such is the force of truth, everywhere consistent with itself and inserting its particles through all the windings of errors, that it often escapes men against their will.

XII. And this was the sum of the Adamic theology after the fall. In those three parts which we have set forth consists all the theology of sinners. What followed was only a variation of degrees. The foundation of the whole is laid in the promise, which, drawn out through various increases of light for the use of the church, the gospel at length exhibited and fully expounded. The rule of obeying God, or the implanted law, the Decalogue expressed at the appointed time; its nature and use the prophets expounded. The pattern of external or ceremonial worship, and of the divine good pleasure, was fixed in the institution of sacrifices; and for all posterity this divine declaration [Greek: theopropion] was sacredly consecrated: No worship is acceptable to God but that which he himself has instituted.

Chapter II. The institution of the new church according to the rule of postlapsarian theology — Adam’s diligence and office in its building — How long it endured pure — The defection in Cain the apostate — The nature, degrees, and manner of that defection — Jehovah looked upon Abel and his offering — The oversights [Greek: paroramata] of some of the ancients — The obscurity of the Septuagint translation — Why the Cainite sacrifice was rejected by God — On the words of Moses, "And Cain said to Abel his brother" (Gen. 4:8) — The fables of the Targums — Cain, removed from the church, flees it, distrusting the promise of mercy — The reformation of the church in the ejection and cursing of Cain.

I. According to the rule of the theology which we have expounded, the first church of sinners was instituted in the family of Adam. This was its proximate end: that an assembly might be constituted of those worshipping God in the Mediator. The care of that work rested upon Adam. For since he was the father of the family of the whole human race, and had received the promise of the conquering Seed to be preached to all his posterity, he was bound to it by the bond of that office, both by the right of nature and by virtue of positive precept. [ p. 144 ] That he was not wanting in that duty, very many things persuade us. For that he should discharge the commanded office diligently was doubtless secured by the greatest benefit lately received from God, by love and compassion toward his own race, and by the tenderest sense of the state into which he had brought his miserable posterity by sin. His whole family, therefore, under his care and rule, that is, the entire human race universally, no one excepted, was the church of God, in which faith, observance, and religious worship flourished. In that state of things the church was absolutely catholic. But how long a space of time it endured blameless in the profession of the new theology is not altogether certain: it admitted the first stain of defection in the apostasy of Cain. Since that apostasy gave the first specimen of defection in the church of sinners professing the worship of God in the Mediator, it must be briefly related.

II. We have shown before that there were three parts of this theology. Against them all the apostate sinned. The foundation of the whole was the promise of the Mediator. Now that Cain’s defection began from unbelief, the apostle shows when he celebrates the faith of Abel (Heb. 11:4). Nor, when admonished by God, did he acknowledge his sin; which impenitence [Greek: ametanoêsia] sprouted from the same root. Next, he performed the instituted worship otherwise than he ought; and here the unbelief [Greek: apistia] that had long lain hidden within first put itself forth. Lastly, wrath and envy thrust him, already fallen from the faith, into fratricide, whereby he transgressed the dictate of right reason, or the implanted law. Thus he stood forth a sinner and a rebel against the whole divine covenant and each of its parts. Hence he was made the first of hypocrites, and the leader and type [Greek: typos] of all the rest to the end of the world (1 John 3:12).

III. Some of old supposed that God testified his acceptance of Abel’s sacrifice by sending fire from heaven to devour it. So Theodotion rendered the words of Gen. 4:4 ["And Jehovah looked to Abel and to his offering"]: "And the Lord kindled a flame upon Abel." Jerome commends that exposition (Hebrew Traditions on Genesis). Strange is the paraphrase of the Seventy at Gen. 4:6, 7, which drove many of the ancients into byways in expounding the sin of Cain. The words are: "And the Lord God said to Cain, Why are you grieved, and why is your countenance fallen? If you offer rightly, but do not divide rightly, have you not sinned? Be still: to you shall be his turning, and you shall rule over him." What those words mean, "If you offer rightly, but do not divide rightly," is altogether uncertain. They have nothing to do with the sacred text. Notable, on their occasion, are the hallucinations and over-curious labors [Greek: periergiai] of Augustine (City of God 15.7), of Chrysostom (Homily 18 on Genesis 4), of Cyril against Julian, and of others. Jerome (Hebrew Traditions on Genesis 4) sharply censures this oversight [Greek: parorama] of the Seventy; yet not without a slip of memory himself. "Chattath, sin," he says, "is among the Hebrews of the masculine gender, but in the Greek tongue of the feminine." For it is certain that chattath is of the feminine gender. What deceived Jerome is that in this place it is joined with robets ["crouching"], a participle of the masculine gender. But that is not unusual [ p. 145 ] among the Hebrews; and the anomaly of this place is removed by chet and aven, each signifying the same as chattath, in the masculine gender. The most learned Mercerus, on the passage, uncovers the source of the slip. I will say in brief what the truth is: Cain’s sacrifice was rejected, neither on account of its matter, nor because some portion of his possessions was wrongly consecrated, nor for the defect of any external rite, but on account of the sin and unbelief of the sacrificer.

IV. Rebuked by God, Cain concealed for a time his envy and wrath against his brother. This seems to be signified by the words, "And Cain said to Abel his brother" [Hebrew: vayyomer Qayin el-Hevel achiv]. Our countrymen render the words thus: "And Cain talked with his brother Abel." The Samaritan codex and the Septuagint version add, "Let us pass over into the field." The Vulgate has, "Let us go out of doors," doubtless from the Jerusalem Targum. In the Masoretic Bibles there is, after "his brother," a gap of a single small line. Lindanus, Morinus, and others cry out that the Hebrew text is corrupt. Jerome rejects the patch: "Cain said to his brother," he writes; "what is to be understood is, the things which the Lord had spoken; that, therefore, which is found in the Samaritan volume and in our own is superfluous" (Hebrew Traditions on Genesis). You may see also how much he valued that Samaritan codex, which some prefer to the Hebrew. Aben Ezra, on the passage, follows Jerome in supplying the sense: "He seems to me," he says, "to have told or related to him with what rebukes he had been received by God." Onkelos keeps to the words of the text. Jonathan, as also the Targum which is called the Jerusalem, relates a long disputation of the brothers: "And Cain said to Abel his brother, Come, let us both go out into the field. And it came to pass, when they had both gone out into the field, Cain answered and said to Abel, I perceive that the world was created by mercies" (the Jerusalem Targum otherwise: "that the world was not created by mercy"), "but it is not governed according to the fruit of good works, and there is respect of persons in judgment; wherefore your offering was accepted, but my offering was not accepted with good pleasure. Abel answered and said to Cain, The world was created in mercies, and it is governed according to the fruits of good works, and there is no respect of faces in judgment; and because the fruits of my works were better than yours, my offering was received with good pleasure. Cain answered and said to his brother, There is no judgment, nor judge, nor another world, nor will a good reward be given to the righteous, nor vengeance be taken on the wicked. Abel answered and said to Cain, There is a judgment, and there is a judge, and there is another world, and a good reward will be given to the righteous, and vengeance will be taken on the wicked. And on account of these matters they contended upon the face of the field." So Ben Uzziel, in his own manner, that is, foolishly. "For this was no place for these things" [Horace].

V. Philo the Jew says that Cain was a sophist, "and challenged his brother to dispute"; perhaps because he dealt captiously with God himself [ p. 146 ] (Gen. 4:8, 9). But the matter is plain; and our countrymen excellently intimate the sense of the words by their version: "And Cain talked with his brother." That is, after he had settled in his mind to take his brother out of the way, pressing deep in his heart the capital hatred which out of envy he nourished in his marrow, and feigning love with his countenance, he conversed with him in a friendly and brotherly way. That is the sense of the words. That is the manner of those who thirst for the blood of others. So afterward Joab treacherously dispatched Abner, and Absalom Amnon, under a pretended renewal of love. Allured by that pretext, the first martyr fell by treachery.

VI. Now of the perpetrating of this murder the devil was in a special manner the author to Cain. Therefore not only is Cain said, on account of this very murder, to have been "of the evil one" (1 John 3:12), but the devil also is "a murderer from the beginning" (John 8:44). For he is not called a murderer in that place only because he enticed the first men into sin, upon which death was to follow, but also because he actually procured the perpetration of this murder; just as, at his instigation, the Pharisees in their time wished to kill the Lord Jesus. An old poet, whose verses are extant in Epiphanius, expressed this elegantly: "O serpent, first origin and final boundary of disasters, and you, father of the treasury of evils, error, walking before blind ignorance, whose food is the tears and sighs of men: you, arming fratricidal right hands against their own kin with hatred not to be expiated, you drove on Cain to be the first to stain his wrath with gore; you stood forth the cause whereby the first-formed man fell from his eternal state to the depths of earth."

VII. But after the unbelief and hypocrisy of Cain had thus openly disclosed themselves, so that he ought no longer to be tolerated in the church, God, who still exercised an immediate care of the tender flock, lest it should be infected by the fellowship of the impious apostate and of those who clung to him in his crimes (whose number seems not to have been small, since by their help he set himself to build a city), banished him from the bounds of the church, loaded with a curse. This is the sense of those words, Gen. 4:12, "A fugitive and a wanderer shall you be in the earth" [Hebrew: na vanad tihyeh va’arets]; that is, "Go out upon the face of the world, lately cursed [ p. 147 ] for your sake, as being now heaped with a new defection from my rule; wander in it wherever at last you will; mind earthly things; you shall henceforth have nothing in common with my worship or my church."

VIII. This part of his punishment he himself repeats (v. 14): "A fugitive and a wanderer shall I be in the earth" (not "groaning and trembling," as the Septuagint); and, "From your face shall I be hid." The gracious face of God in the promised seed he had despised before. The hope of entering again into grace with God he casts away in other words: "My iniquity," he says, "is greater than that it may be pardoned," or "than I can bear" [Hebrew: gadol avoni minneso]. Jonas Schlichting denies that these words "are to be censured, as though they contained anything of evil in them" (On the Trinity, against Meisner, p. 119), as though they had proceeded from right reason. But it is certain they did not proceed from faith, and so were full of desperation. Further, he had neither hope nor fear that he could lie hidden from the providence of God. He acknowledges, therefore, that he was excluded from the communion of the saints and from the presence of God in solemn worship. And being driven from the church, he also fled from it (Gen. 4:16).

IX. Now this defection of Cain happened in the hundred and twenty-ninth year after the church was founded in the promise of the deliverer.
