For it is probable that in the year next after the death of Abel, Eve bore Seth. That was the hundred and thirtieth year of Adam’s life (Gen. 5:3). In that space of time the human race doubtless grew into number and multitude; and so likewise the church itself, which was still absolutely catholic, embracing that whole race. Therefore that first church of sinners professing faith in the mediator endured blameless one hundred and thirty years, or thereabouts, walking holily and blamelessly before God according to the rule of the restored theology.

X. Cain therefore rushed into apostasy when he was a hundred and twenty years old. For that is foolish which Gregory Abulpharagius, the Arabic chronologer, the beginning of whose history the famous Pococke published not long ago, reports from the opinion of a certain Theodosius: namely, "that Adam begot Cain only after thirty years had passed from his expulsion from paradise; and that he mourned Abel a hundred years before, returning to his wife, he begot Seth." But the sacred text expressly [Greek: rhêtôs] rejects these ravings (Gen. 5:3); and yet this most foolish rumor, arising I know not whence, has traveled through the whole East and stained the writings of very many.

XI. Hypocrisy, then, having advanced from unbelief toward God to the persecution of the saints, brought in both the occasion and the necessity of a reformation of the church; of which, in the ejection and cursing of Cain, we have the first divinely appointed specimen and exemplar.

Chapter III. The second reformation of the antediluvian church, Gen. 4:26 — Huchal [Hebrew: huchal, "it was begun"] — The onomastic blunders of some noted — The Enoshian idolatry; the opinion of Maimonides, of Selden, of Cyril — The total defection of the reformed church — The beginning, occasion, and manner of that defection, Gen. 6:1-5 — The sons of God and the daughters of men — The trifles of the Jews, and of certain old Christians — No antediluvian idolatry — The causes of the universal destruction by the flood, and the tradition of it among the nations — The end of the antediluvian church — In it a type of churches to come.

I. Cain, the standard-bearer of the corrupters of the Adamic antediluvian theology, being cast out of the bounds of the church, its force and energy [Greek: energeia], and the pure worship of God resting upon it, flourished down to the age of Enosh. A new corruption, as it seems, and one creeping far and wide, had by that time invaded the church; which a new reformation, following upon it, blotted out: "For then it was begun to call upon the name of the Lord" (Gen. 4:26). On which place some things must be added.

II. The words are: az huchal liqro beshem YHWH ["then it was begun to call on the name of Jehovah"]. There are those who think a reformation of the church is denoted in them; there are those who think its lapse into idolatry. The Seventy render thus: "He hoped to call upon the name of the Lord God"; deriving huchal, namely, not from chalal but from yachal ["to hope"]. From this arose the innocent error of some, who suspected that those words express the meaning of the name Enosh. Chrysostom’s interpretation of the place rests solely on that hallucination; for, thinking that Enosh signifies "he hoped," he comments at length on the pious hope of his parents.

III. And indeed, in rendering the reason of Hebrew names, the Greeks blunder everywhere. So George Cedrenus (p. 172): "He named his firstborn Cain, which is ‘zeal,’ ‘envy’; and he, being envious, slew his brother Abel, which is interpreted ‘mourning,’ for over him, the first to be murdered, his parents mourned." Foolishly, and against the reason of the naming assigned in the text itself (Gen. 4:1). Yet there is a show of color, for qinah is envy, and ebel mourning. So also Zonaras: "Abel, the second, and his name signifies mourning." But az, "then," I judge must be referred to the whole time of the life of Enosh: in the days of Enosh, whom Seth begot, these things were done. That the condition of the church was already wretched at the time Enosh was born, I would readily grant; whence his pious parents took occasion for the giving of his name [Greek: onomatothesia]. Enosh is "miserable man," from anash, "he was mortally sick, was calamitous" (cf. Ps. 9:20). But there is no reason that compels us to bind the things here recorded to the time of his birth. For these things happened perhaps three hundred years, or thereabouts, after the first reformation of the church by the ejection of Cain; whereas the birth of Enosh falls in the hundred and sixth year after his flight. For although the beginnings of that work, whatever at last it was, of which the Holy Spirit here makes mention, were perhaps laid at the very birth of Enosh, yet I do not believe [ p. 149 ] it was brought to effect until Enosh himself, who is pointedly noted, had for some time stood forth as head of a family; and in this opinion are almost all the Jews.

IV. The Targum ascribed to Jonathan asserts that the entrance of idolatry into the world is noted in these words: "That was the age in whose days they began to err, and they made themselves idols, and surnamed their idols by the name of the Word of the Lord." That is, they called their idols gods. So also the Arabic interpreter in Erpenius’s edition: "Then men began to fall away from the obedience of God"; otherwise than the codex published in the London Polyglot has it. Of the Targum of Onkelos there are two exemplars: one Arias Montanus printed in the Royal Bible, the other Buxtorf in his Basel edition; of which the latter affirms that "men desisted from calling upon," the former that "they began to call upon the name of the Lord." Maimonides fetches the beginning of all idolatry from here, at the opening of his book On the Worship of the Stars: "In the days of Enosh the sons of men erred with a great error, and the counsel of the wise men of that generation was made brutish, and Enosh himself was among the erring; and this was their error: they said, Since God created these stars and spheres to govern the world, and set them on high, and gave them honor, and they are ministers who minister before him, surely it is fitting to praise and glorify them and give them honor." The learned man had in view those words of Moses, Gen. 1:16: "And God made the two great lights, the greater light to rule the day, and the lesser light to rule the night, and the stars." From these he holds that the degenerate sons of the patriarchs snatched an occasion of adoring the sun, the moon, and the whole array of the heavens, to which dominion over days and nights, and so over the whole earth, had been committed. Of the same opinion is R. David Kimchi: "In the days of Enosh," he says, "they erred after idolatry, and the invocation of the name of God was then profaned; but there are those who expound the word huchal by ‘to begin,’ in this way: Then men began to call the names of images and idols by the name of God." Selden follows Maimonides (Prolegomena 3 to the Syntagma on the Syrian Gods).

V. But Josephus, older than all these, asserts that the worship of the true and one God endured down to the seventh generation (Antiquities, bk. 1). R. Eliezer agrees with him in Maase Bereshith, ch. 22, and proves that no mention of idolatry is made here, because no mention is made of the other things they were to have worshipped, that is, of the sun and stars. With these most of the old Christians agree. "The men," says Cyril against Julian, "who lived from Adam to Noah honored one God, by nature and in truth the maker of all things; but after the flood and the building of the tower they held a confused opinion about God, some surmising that heaven was god, others the sun and the moon." [ p. 150 ] And this opinion is the truest, in which also is Epiphanius (bk. 1, ch. 1, sect. 4), and Bede in his Chronicle. Chalal principally signifies "to begin"; in piel, "to profane"; in hiphil or hophal [in that sense] most rarely; whence techillah, "beginning," and chalal, "to enter publicly upon something." And this interpretation Jerome embraces. Nor did men then first begin to profane the name of the Lord, in the days of Enosh, since that had been done by the impious Cainites long before Enosh was born. There is therefore no cause why it should be pointedly said that men then began to profane the name of God; but the greatest cause why it should be said that they then began to call upon it in a peculiar manner.

VI. It is not, therefore, a corruption of religious worship, but a reformation of the church, that is indicated by these words. The prefixed particle ["then"] marks that something new and unusual is signified. Now while the church was confined within the limits of one family, its reformation was not difficult, namely, by the ejection of the contumacious. But the human race being increased beyond measure, and the borders of the church enlarged, another way had to be entered upon. For a multitude of sinners begets impunity and license of sinning. These words therefore denote two things: first, that the godly constituted separate assemblies for performing the solemn worship of God; next, that they took upon themselves the peculiar name of the worshippers, or sons, of God, which they used down to the next defection. Thus they solemnly called upon the name of God in their separated assemblies, and were called by the name of God, that is, worshippers, or sons, of God. Our country’s interpreters approve both senses; for as they read in the text, "Then began men to call upon the name of the Lord," so they add in the margin, "to call themselves by the name of the Lord." And this was the only way of reforming the church that remained. That this way must be entered upon when very many corrupt the true worship of God and persist obstinately in wicked manners (which was the state of the church in those times), both the nature of the thing itself and right reason demand. For what should they do who desired to present their consciences unimpaired and pure to God in religious worship? Should they suffer themselves to be mingled with the crowd of apostates and corrupted? That does not seem to be their duty. Should they cast the apostates out and banish them from the bounds of the church? But that cannot be done by the fewer upon the more numerous. There remained, therefore, the separation of the godly, and their gathering into distinct assemblies. That this was now done, these words record. For the name of the Lord was not then first profaned, which had been done by the impious Cainites long before; nor did men then first, absolutely speaking, begin to call upon the name of the Lord, since all the godly had given themselves to his worship from the foundations of the world; but then first did some, a visible separation from the rest of the world being made, solemnly perform religious worship among themselves.

VII. And this was the second reformation of the antediluvian church. The first defection was singular, in the person of Cain; in his ejection consisted the first reformation. By the time of Enosh hypocrisy had crept wider and infected more. That they might keep themselves free from the common stain [ p. 151 ] and preserve pure the worship of God in the promised Mediator, the godly Sethites, seceding from the crowd of apostates and instituting separate assemblies, were called "the sons of God." And here first the church stood forth visibly and voluntarily separated from the world.

VIII. By virtue of this reformation pure theology flourished, and by its force the visibly separated church endured for a thousand years, or thereabouts. In that course of time God put forth, in the translation of Enoch, a signal exemplar of obedience, a proof [Greek: tekmêrion] of true theology, and a pledge of eternal life (Gen. 5:24). But in process of time the impious Cainites, and those Sethites from whom the godly had seceded at the time of the Enoshian reformation, pretending, as it seems, that peace must be cultivated in common, were admitted into the fellowship of the godly through marriages and the other bonds of civil life, and well-nigh universally ruined all things divine and human. Such, for the most part, is the fatal outcome of corruption admitted anew into the church after reformation.

IX. But when the greatest throng of impious apostates had been admitted into the fellowship of the godly, and these could no longer free themselves either by the authoritative ejection of the others, as they speak, or by voluntary secession, the defection straightway became catholic, and the whole church came forth corrupted. God therefore put an end to that dispensation, and to the Adamic sublapsarian theology, in the universal destruction [Greek: panôlethria] of the apostate theologians by the flood.

X. Now the rise, progress, and end of this last defection of the antediluvian church its own most just avenger has himself committed to memory (Gen. 6:1-5). Verses 1 and 2 relate its rise: "And it came to pass, when men began to multiply on the earth, and daughters were born to them, that the sons of God, seeing that the daughters of men were fair, took to themselves wives of all whom they chose."

XI. Who these "sons of God" are, and who the "daughters of men," is disputed. The Jews trifle I know not what about good and evil angels; and even before the rabbis were born, Philo and Josephus did the same. So also the author of the books of Enoch, certain fragments of which are extant in Joseph Scaliger’s animadversions on Eusebius, and in Kircher’s Egyptian Oedipus (vol. 2, ch. 2). Very many of the ancient fathers favor that delirium: Justin (Apology 2), Clement (Stromata, bk. 3), Tertullian (On the Apparel of Women), Lactantius (Institutes 2.15), Eusebius (Preparation for the Gospel, bk. 5), Ambrose (On the Veiling of Virgins), Zosimus of Panopolis in Photius (Codex 191). But neither the nature or condition of angels (Matt. 22:30) nor the course of the Mosaic narrative allows that suspicion. Bene ha-Elohim, "the sons of God," Onkelos renders by "the sons of the mighty"; and benoth ha-adam, "the daughters of men," by "the daughters of the poor." The Seventy: "sons of God"; Aquila: "sons of the gods"; Symmachus, as Jerome testifies, "sons of the potentates." And all these understand an utter confusion of all ranks in the political state and in the common manner of living, as if men, with no regard to degrees or honor of dignities, had coupled with one another after the manner of cattle. Of this opinion is Rambam (Maimonides), Guide of the Perplexed, pt. 1, ch. 14: "Adam signifies," he says, "also ‘the common people’ and ‘the multitude of men’; so too ‘the sons of Adam,’ as also ‘the sons of man’ (Ps. 33:13); [ p. 152 ] and in this signification it is said, ‘And the sons of God saw the daughters of Adam.’"

XII. But the matter is plain enough of itself. The "sons of God" were those who, from the days of Enosh, instituted separate assemblies for celebrating the true worship of God, professing the adoption of sons [Greek: huiothesia]. For that name they were the hatred and the mockery of the ungodly world; for the world never was, nor will be, unlike itself in this matter. These, perhaps for some centuries of years, kept themselves separate from the communion of the wicked, which has always stood as the chief cause of ecclesiastical defection. In process of time they too were increased with a wicked offspring, possessed only of the form [Greek: morphôsis] of adoption. This offspring, driven by unbridled lust, joined to itself in marriage the daughters of the apostates. And these were the "daughters of men." That down to that time the godly Sethites held all their rights, both divine and human, separately from the Cainites, and even dwelt in places far distant, without any commerce of life or of the world, is an old tradition of the Jews; which, because it carries a show of truth, I would not rashly reject. Abulpharagius, in Pococke’s edition, reports it interpolated with fables: "Seth," he says, "the son of Adam: it is related that he was the first author of writing, and that he instilled into his offspring a longing for the blessed life which their parents had had in paradise; so that they withdrew to Mount Hermon, given to the worship of God, to piety, and to continence, in no wise going in to women; whence they were called Bene-Elohim. But in the fortieth year of Jared, coming down from Mount Hermon, when they despaired of a return into paradise" (that is, when holiness of life and separation from the world began to be a weariness to them), "they lusted after women; and when those joined to them by kinship refused to give them wives, holding them in contempt, the Cainites invited them to marriage, freely offering them their daughters; with whom consorting, they begot giants, excelling in wars and hostile incursions."

XIII. And these things he drew from the apocryphal book of Enoch On the Watchers [Greek: peri tôn egrêgorôn]; except that what he ascribes to the Sethites, that other trifler, the Jew, assigns to angels descending upon Mount Hermon. And the fragments which are still extant of those books On the Watchers and On the Apocalypse of Moses will abundantly supply fables of that sort to anyone who thirsts for them. Angels were called by the ancients egrêgoroi ["watchers"]. They snatched the handle from Daniel 4:13, where an ir, or "watcher," is said to have looked down from heaven. The common copies of the Greek text have eir, the Hebrew word being retained; a certain manuscript has egrêgoros.

XIV. From this fountain proceeded the lawless mixture [Greek: athemitos mixis] of all with all. At length both assemblies so coalesced into one that, the single family of Noah excepted, scarcely any distinction survived between the world and the church. This the Holy Spirit shows, where he affirms that "all flesh had corrupted its way" (Gen. 6:12). This, I say, was the origin of the catholic defection of the Enoshian, or Adamic, antediluvian church, reformed a second [ p. 153 ] time; in which we have an illustrious exemplar of the rise of every catholic apostasy. For it was always the fellowship of the wicked that ruined the church.

XV. Let us see, further, the progress of this defection. The Holy Spirit sets it forth in verses 11 and 12: "The earth was corrupt before God himself, and was filled with violence; and God, looking upon the earth, behold, it was corrupt, for all flesh had corrupted its way." It had advanced so far, namely, that it extended itself to all flesh, Noah alone excepted (v. 8; 7:1). But he was "a herald of righteousness" (2 Pet. 2:5); in whose ministry the Spirit of Christ "contended with the rebellious" (1 Pet. 3:19, 20) for a hundred and twenty years (Gen. 6:3). So the Targum of Ben Uzziel expounds that place; which yet, after its usual manner, trifles I know not what about Shamchazai and Uzziel, who at that time, falling from heaven, begot the giants.

XVI. Maimonides contends that the nature of this apostasy consisted chiefly in idolatry; into his opinion Selden has gone over. Mohammed, in the Koran (Azoara 81 [Sura 71]), furnishes us with the names of the gods they worshipped: Wadd, Suwa, Yaghuth, Yauq, and Nasr. To these, it seems, Shamchazai and Uzziel gave their service. But let us dismiss the trifles. What is said of antediluvian idolatry is destitute of all probability. For the Holy Spirit makes no mention of it, and he would by no means have passed it over, it being the gravest of sins, and the most effectual argument for carrying out the vindication of divine justice in destroying the world. Beyond doubt God willed to exhibit, in the diluvian extermination of the old world, a great prejudgment of the judgment to come, as Tertullian speaks, second only to the cross of Christ; can it then be supposed that, in setting forth the causes of so great a destruction and penalty, less only than the eternal one, he would pass over in silence the greatest sin of all, especially when he accurately recounts the other crimes and wickednesses deserving it? For the malice of men’s hearts, the universal corruption of life, violence and rapine, and unlawful marriages too, are expressly recorded (Gen. 6:2, 5, 11-13). The other vices and crimes of that age the Berosus found in Annius of Viterbo relates not unaptly, as does Ovid in the first book of the Metamorphoses: "These giants," [pseudo-Berosus] says, "trusting in the vastness and strength of their bodies, rushed in with arms and oppressed all men; and, serving their lust, they invented pavilions and instruments of music and every delight; they ate men..., and there was nothing which they did not allow themselves, despisers of religion and of the gods." But that the true Berosus also consecrated the flood to memory in his history, Josephus is witness (Antiquities 1.4 [1.3.6]).

XVII. The end or outcome of this catholic apostasy was the diluvian destruction of the apostates; namely, "the world that then was, being overflowed with water, perished" (2 Pet. 3:6). That the nations received this cause of the flood by tradition, Lucian, among very many others, is witness (On the Syrian Goddess, sect. 12): "This generation," he says, "the men who now are, were not the first; but that generation all perished, and these men are of the second race, which again from Deucalion grew into multitude. And concerning those former men these things are related: being exceedingly insolent, they wrought lawless deeds; for they neither kept oaths, nor received strangers, nor bore with suppliants; for which things the great calamity came upon them. Straightway the earth gave forth much water, and great rains came, and the rivers ran down greater, and the sea rose high, until all things became water and all men perished. But Deucalion alone of men was left for a second race, for his good counsel and his piety. And his deliverance came to pass thus: into a great ark which he had, he put his children and his wives, and himself went in. And as he was entering, there came to him swine and horses and the kinds of lions and serpents, and all the other creatures that feed on the earth, all in pairs; and he received them all, and they did him no harm, but a great friendship from the gods was among them; and in the one ark they all sailed, so long as the water prevailed. These things the Greeks relate concerning Deucalion." [ p. 154 ] All of which, as everyone sees, is taken from the sacred text. Horace also plays upon the matter: "He terrified the nations, lest the grievous age of Pyrrha should return, who complained of strange marvels, when Proteus drove all his herd to visit the high mountains, and the race of fishes clung to the elm-top, which had been the well-known seat of doves, and the timid deer swam on the overwhelming sea" (Odes 1.2). Similar things Josephus reports from Hieronymus the Egyptian, Berosus, Mnaseas, and Nicolaus of Damascus (Antiquities 1.4 [1.3.6]); and Eusebius in his Chronicle from Abydenus and Alexander Polyhistor.

XVIII. But most men confound the catholic flood with the local [Greek: topikos] flood of Deucalion, or with that which long preceded it, the Ogygian. Of all these a most ancient writer in Cedrenus speaks rightly: "There were two floods noised abroad in the land of Greece: the first under Ogyges in Attica" (though very many say Ogyges was king not of Attica but of Thebes); "and some time later, in Thessaly" (that is, the land afterward so called, for the name was not known even to Homer) "under Deucalion, when Cranaus, the second autochthon, was reigning at Athens. Of this flood, that under Deucalion namely, the Egyptians also seem to make mention, asserting that their own country was not flooded; and they rightly contend that Egypt was not then overflowed, for this flood was local. For the former, that is, the catholic flood, they do not even know; for their patriarch was not yet born — Ham, the son of Noah, the father of Mizraim, from whom come the Egyptians." But no one doubts that the first writers of myths so mingled the fame of the catholic flood with the history of the particular ones, that later men understood neither the one nor the other. Whoever wishes more concerning these floods, let him consult Augustine, City of God 18.9, 10; Nonnus, bk. 3; Orosius 1.9.

XIX. And this end the apostate antediluvian church obtained. Binding itself with the crime of a new defection after reformation, it came forth irreformable. This solemn period the supernatural theology of the first revelation underwent. But in it were established, as we shall see, the type and exemplar of all churches to be instituted, and of all lapsed churches to be reformed, in times to come. May God, according to his infinite mercy, avert the omen from the reformed Christian churches.
END OF BOOK II.

Chapter argument: 2 Pet. 3:5-7, a new heaven and a new earth; what the old world was; the new world, Isa. 51:15, 16; 65:17; 66:22; the age to come; the world to come; Noah a just man; he had faith before the flood; theology restored and increased in his family after the flood; the first express mention of a divine covenant; promise and restipulation belong to the nature of a covenant; in what respect a promise is called a covenant; whence the word berith [Hebrew: berith, "covenant"] is derived; it often signifies a bare promise, 2 Sam. 23:5; Jer. 31:31, 32; diatheke, syntheke [Greek terms for covenant]; whence "foedus" [covenant] comes; how covenants were ratified of old, Jer. 34:18; of the sign of the covenant; the bow in the cloud; why the sign of the covenant is called the covenant; the nature of the Noachic covenant; precepts added to theology; Gen. 9:5, 6, an exposition of the first postdiluvian precept, concerning [ p. 156 ] the not shedding of blood; the manner of taking vengeance through the magistrate established in it; the opinion of Onkelos; the origin of the law of retaliation; an exposition of the second precept, concerning not eating flesh with the blood, Gen. 9:2, 3; the seven precepts given to the sons of Noah; what the Hebrews understand by them; the fictitious oration of Noah to his sons in Inghirami; the restoration of the Noachic church; its duration; the sin of Ham; the trifles of the Jews; the origin of the fable of the mutilation of Saturn; why Canaan was cursed; new promises given to the reformed church; its new defection; the occasion of that defection; a narrative of the universal defection of the Noachic church.

I. In the preceding dissertation we set forth the rise and progress of that mixed theology which consisted partly of the law of nature, or the dictate of right reason, that is, of the remains of the innate [Greek: endiathetos] theology, and partly of the revelation made to Adam after the entrance of sin. We also briefly showed there the various degrees of its apostasy, which at length brought in a total defection from God of almost the whole world, together with the horrible punishment and utter destruction [Greek: panolethria] of the apostate theologians. We now apply ourselves, in the next place, to setting forth the restoration, increase, progress, and outcomes of that theology from the flood down to the calling of Abraham. Now the blessed Peter propounds this whole divine economy thus: "The heavens were of old, and the earth standing out of the water and in the water, by the word of God: whereby the world that then was, being overflowed with water, perished: but the heavens and the earth which are now, by the same word are kept in store, reserved unto fire against the day of judgment and perdition of ungodly men" (2 Pet. 3:5-7).

II. The apostle here makes mention of a twofold "world": of that old one which had perished by water, and of the one then present, which was to be consumed by fire; after whose destruction he foretells the entrance of a third, verse 13: "Nevertheless we, according to his promise, look for new heavens and a new earth, wherein dwelleth righteousness." In neither place does the apostle treat of the visible heaven or earth with respect to their substance. For when the old world was destroyed by water, the frame of the heavens and the earth nevertheless remained. That "world," therefore, was the men living in the world. When they had been blotted out by the flood, another world had to be raised up for the due performance of the worship of God. Now God laid the foundations of this world in the family of Noah, and the erection and adornment of the Jewish church completed the whole fabric. And this was the world which the blessed Peter foretold was presently to be dissolved by fire, speaking, namely, in the prophetic style. So we read in Isaiah, chapter 51:15, 16: "I am the LORD thy God, that divided the sea, whose waves roared: The LORD of hosts is his name. And I have put my words in thy mouth, and I have covered thee in the shadow of mine hand, that I may plant the heavens, and lay the foundations of the earth, and say unto Zion, Thou art my people."

III. At that time, therefore, when God, dividing the sea and leading his people out of Egypt, entrusted to them his word or law, with a solemn prescription of his worship, forming them into a church for himself, he instituted and completed this new world, that heaven and that earth. At the time when Peter wrote, this world, that is, the Jewish church, now apostate, [ p. 157 ] was hastening into the destruction of fire, no otherwise than as that old world had rushed headlong into the flood. By the burning of the temple and the city, the frame of that world was dissolved. But since the consummation of the age [Greek: tou aionos synteleia] was not yet at hand, the apostle bids the faithful look for another world, new heavens and a new earth, according to the promise of God. That promise stands in Isaiah chapter 65, verse 17, and in the same words in chapter 66, verse 22: "For, behold," says he, "I create new heavens and a new earth: and the former shall not be remembered, nor come into mind." In these places the prophet depicts the state of the church after the coming of Christ; namely, the time when God would take from among the Gentiles men for priests and Levites, as the words run in verse 21 of the last chapter; that is, when he would institute the evangelical ministry. Hence that state of the church before the conflagration of that second world was designated "the age to come" and "the world to come," as Paul teaches us in the Epistle to the Hebrews, chapter 2, verse 5, and also chapter 6, verse 5. That first or old world, therefore, perished by the flood of water; the second, present at that time, the apostle foretells would perish by fire; but the future one, he confirms, will endure to the very consummation of the age. But let us return from this byway to our road.

IV. It is evident from the manifold testimony of the Holy Spirit that Noah himself, of whom we are speaking, was a just man, pleasing and acceptable to God. Gen. 6:8, 9: "Noah was a just man, perfect in his generations, and Noah walked continually with God"; also chapter 7, verse 1: "Thee have I seen righteous before me in this generation." Moreover, honorable mention is made of him, conformable to this testimony, both in the Old and in the New Testament (which is rare and almost singular): Ezek. 14:14; 1 Pet. 3:20; 2 Pet. 2:5. And that he obtained righteousness by faith the apostle proves in the eleventh chapter of the Epistle to the Hebrews. All saving faith has respect to the promised Seed. Now this faith he had before the flood; for it was then that he was adorned by God with the testimony of righteousness. He was therefore just and pleasing to God, walking blameless before him, by virtue of that antediluvian Adamic theology which we set forth in the preceding dissertation. But for the sake of Noah himself, and of the church to be restored in his family, that theology was immediately after the flood augmented with various degrees of light, which I shall briefly run through.

V. We earlier divided the antediluvian theology of sinners into three parts: the promise of grace, the law of nature, and the prescription of instituted worship. By the new revelation made to Noah, the first was further enlarged, the second more fully expounded, the third established. For now for the first time God makes express mention of a covenant, Gen. 9:9: "And I, behold, I establish my covenant with you" [Hebrew: va'ani hineni meqim eth-berithi ittekhem]. That God ratified a gracious compact, exhibiting spiritual benefits in the Mediator, with Adam in that most [ p. 158 ] celebrated promise of the seed, we have proved before. For the solemnity of a divine covenant, indeed, there is required a promise of life, and of all the means necessary for obtaining it graciously, together with an exaction of obedience and an express restipulation. But it is not necessary that all these things be expressed together wherever a covenant alone is treated of.

VI. A promise always includes a requirement of obedience. For obedience is nothing other than the due observance of the means instituted for obtaining the promised life. But where the obedience required in the covenant is moral only, and therefore has its general foundation elsewhere than in the covenant itself, namely in the law of nature, and is only rendered accepted and pleasing to God through the covenant, while the promise belongs to pure revelation, the name of covenant is peculiarly ascribed to the promise. And so it is in this place: the promise is called a covenant. But for other reasons also "promise" and "covenant" sound the same; this is not the place to recount them: a few things may be added about the word itself. Some hold that berith [Hebrew: berith] is derived from barah or barath, "to cut down" or "to cut off." So Grotius, in his Annotations at the beginning of the Gospel according to Matthew, because in covenants victims were slain. Others refer it to bara, which also signifies "to choose" and "to dispose"; and this account of the name pleases Cocceius, a learned and pious man, in his work On the Covenant of God, chapter 1. Mercerus, under the root barath, derives it from barar, "to choose," "to purify." From whatever source it takes its origin, it often signifies a bare "promise," 2 Sam. 23:5; Jer. 31:31, 32; also a gratuitous, irrevocable "donation," Num. 18:19; and a "statute" or decree of God, Jer. 33:21. And it is very often employed where a solemn convention or compact of parties has no place at all. In the New Testament it is rendered by diatheke [Greek: diatheke], a word which extends just as widely as berith. It is used of laws, engagements, and testaments, as Grotius witnesses. The word syntheke [Greek: syntheke], which properly signifies a "covenant" or mutual pact, is not employed in the New Testament for the explanation of berith. The ancient Seventy [the Septuagint translators] constantly render that word by diatheke; otherwise Aquila and Symmachus, who also use syntheke. Our countryman Ball, in his treatise Of the Divine Covenant, chapter 1, affirms that the Seventy render berith by syntheke at Isa. 28:15. But the learned man is mistaken; for the word which they there translate by syntheke is chozeh, which properly signifies a "vision"; while at the beginning of the verse, in their usual manner, they put diatheke in place of berith. The reason of this matter the same Grotius renders excellently. "Synthekai," he says, "receive their binding power from the consenting testimony of two wills; but what Moses and others call berith is for the most part of such a kind as to require no consent of the other party, since the force of obligation belongs to it by the sole command and authority of God." So it is: the covenant of God is not suspended upon our will, nor upon any conditions to be performed by us; it has all its power and effect from the authority, grace, and faithfulness of God himself. For the promise of grace is absolute, nor is there any condition of the covenant which is not contained in the promise itself. They act ineptly and preposterously, therefore, who endeavor to expound the nature of the divine berith [ p. 159 ] from the nature of covenants customarily ratified among men. Nor does "foedus" among the Latins always denote a solemn "convention of parties," but sometimes a law or constitution; so in Virgil, Aeneid 1.62:

"And he gave them a king who, by a fixed covenant [foedus], should know both to tighten and, when bidden, to slacken the reins" (Virgil, Aeneid 1.62, 63). We acknowledge, however, that this promise had the true nature of a covenant; and therefore Chrysostom, in his twenty-eighth Homily on Genesis, interprets the diatheke of the Seventy in this place by synthekai [mutual compacts].

VII. Moreover, "foedus" [covenant] is said to come from "feriendo" [striking]; the origin of the name is from a foul superstition. The custom of entering into covenants, whence the origin of the name, is related among others by Polybius, book 3, chapter 25. "The fetial priest," he says, "taking a stone in his hands, after agreement had been reached between the parties concerning the covenant, spoke these words: If I make this covenant and this oath rightly and without evil deceit, may the gods grant me all prosperity; but if I either act or think otherwise, then, all others being safe in their own laws, their own homes, their own temples, their own tombs, may I alone perish, as this stone now falls from my hands." Others record that covenants were entered into otherwise, and that the fetial used these words: "So may great Jupiter foully smite me, as I foully slay this pig, if I shall not have kept the compact of the covenant." And so saying he struck the pig and killed it with a blow of the stone. Nearly so Livy, book 1, chapter 24: "'Hear, Jupiter; hear, pater patratus; as those terms, from first to last, have been openly recited from those tablets or wax without evil deceit, and as they have here this day been most rightly understood, from those terms the Roman people will not be the first to depart. If it shall first depart by public counsel, with evil deceit, then do you, O Diespiter, so smite the Roman people as I shall this day smite this pig: and smite so much the more, as you are the more able and powerful.' When he had said this, he struck the pig with a flint stone." To the same sense the poet: "Armed, before the altars of Jupiter, holding the libation bowls, they stood, and joined covenants over a slaughtered sow" (Aeneid 8.640, 641). And: "The priest in spotless garment brought the young of a bristly sow" (Aeneid 12.169, 170).

VIII. It is probable that this custom of ratifying covenants flowed, not without the intervention of depraved superstition, from that ancient usage of entering into fixed and solemn compacts by sacrifices, and by the division of the immolated victim into two parts, between which those who were to make the covenant had to pass. Homer pursues that custom at length, Iliad 3.252, in the covenant which preceded the single combat [Greek: monomachia] of Paris and Menelaus: "that you may cut trustworthy oaths" [Greek: hin' horkia pista tamēai]. On which words Eustathius: "To cut oaths, in Homer and Herodotus, signifies to sacrifice the victims slain over the oath." For tamnein [to cut] is said in Ionic for thyein [to sacrifice]. In that manner was established the covenant between the princes of Judah and the king of Babylon, which God, on account of the invocation of his name, calls his own, Jer. 34:18: "I will give the men that have transgressed my covenant, which have not performed the words of the covenant which they had made before me, when they passed between the halved parts of the calf which they cut in [ p. 160 ] two." And again, verse 19: "which passed between the halved parts of that calf." Passing between the parts of the victim, the covenanters loaded themselves with a curse, devoting themselves to a dissection of the same kind unless they should stand by the appointed compacts. So Agamemnon prays to the gods, Iliad 3.279: "Whosoever shall swear a false oath, be you witnesses, and guard the trustworthy covenants." After which he adds, verse 292: "He spoke, and cut the throats of the lambs with the pitiless bronze." Devoting perjurers to a like death, Neh. 10:29: "We made a covenant and an oath that we would walk in the law of God." Hence perhaps that threat against the unfaithful servant: "He shall cut him in two" [Greek: dichotomesei auton], namely the Lord, Matt. 24:51; on account of the neglected covenant he will divide him into parts. And from that cutting of the victim, "to cut" among the Hebrews is "to strike a covenant": 2 Chron. 7:18, "And I will raise up the throne of thy kingdom, according as I have cut [Hebrew: karatti] with David thy father"; "as I covenanted" [Greek: hos diethemen], render the Seventy; "I have promised," the Vulgate interpreter; "As I have covenanted," our vernacular translation. Literally, "I have cut off," "I have cut"; the same verb is used in the same manner in chapter 2 of the prophet Haggai, verse 5. But these things do not pertain to the simple berith.

IX. And this was the first step in the progress of postlapsarian theology. For by this revelation made to Noah, that communion which intervenes between God and sinners in the Mediator is for the first time expressly called a covenant. That name, most full of consolation, clearly proclaiming the divine love and faithfulness, is laid as a foundation for the new church to be erected in the family of Noah.

X. Further: God now for the first time appointed a visible sign of the covenant, namely "the bow in the cloud," Gen. 9:11-13. Not indeed that he now first set the bow in the cloud; but he now first appointed the bow in the cloud as a sign of the covenant. In this manner the sacraments of the New Testament may, it seems, be called othoth berith [Hebrew: othoth berith], or "signs of the covenant." For the sake of this sign also, the promise is called a covenant. For a promise with a visible sign has the proper nature of a covenant. Whence the sign itself is sometimes called the covenant: "This cup is the new covenant" [Greek: touto to poterion he kaine diatheke], Luke 22:20. It is true that express mention is here made only of things pertaining to this life [Greek: ton biotikon], and the sign of the covenant itself directly respects a temporal benefit. Nevertheless spiritual grace, or the gratuitous love of God toward the faithful, is chiefly intended. For such was the economy [Greek: oikonomia] of religious institutions before Christ was exhibited in the flesh. But all these things were a shadow of good things to come [Greek: skia ton mellonton agathon], Heb. 10:1, of which Christ is the high priest, chapter 9:11. And this berith olam [Hebrew: berith olam], or "everlasting covenant," existed only in Christ. A temporal benefit indeed flowed from it upon all; but the spiritual grace of the covenant ratified in Christ pertains to the elect alone.

[ p. 161 ] XI. And in this manner the antediluvian Adamic theology was enlarged both by a new revelation, and by the appellation of a covenant, and by a visible sign. Thence the faith and hope of the theologians were confirmed. Now the designation of the bow in the cloud as a sign of the covenant pertains to instituted worship. To the promise, therefore, was added the name of covenant; to instituted worship, a new sacrament; by which the two former parts of that theology were amplified.

XII. Further, for the enlarging of this theology, precepts also were added to it, so that after that notorious fall it might rise up brighter in every part. The first concerned homicide, or the not shedding of human blood. For life is the first and last care; all else is subservient to it. The precept stands at Gen. 9:5, 6: "And surely your blood of your lives will I require; at the hand of every beast will I require it, and at the hand of man; at the hand of every man's brother will I require the life of man. Whoso sheddeth man's blood, by man shall his blood be shed."

XIII. A solemn avenging of the violent shedding of human blood is here instituted. The shedding itself was prohibited by the law of nature. The crime and punishment of Cain teach as much. Indeed, all violence against others rises so directly against the dictate of right reason, that that unbridled fury by which the antediluvian sinners, as if with a concerted assault, crushed one another, stood as the weightiest cause of the flood itself. But a solemn manner of punishing violence through the magistrate, one that should extend itself beyond the bounds of private families by the mutual consent of the many, had not yet been constituted. God, therefore, granting the habitable world to the sons of Noah, laid this condition upon them all, namely, that for the sake of guarding political society and public peace among all, they should constitute a magistracy among themselves. Hence the magistrate is a human creation [Greek: anthropine ktisis]; yet not erected except upon a stable foundation, namely, the command of God.

XIV. The paraphrast Onkelos therefore rightly expounds those words: "Whosoever shall shed the blood of man, by witnesses, by the sentence of the judges shall his blood be shed." By ba'adam, "in man" [or "by man"], he understands suitable witnesses among men. Nor otherwise Jonathan: "He who shall shed the blood of man, by witnesses, the judges shall condemn him to death; but he who shall shed it without witnesses, the Lord of the world will take vengeance on him in the day of the great judgment." Grotius judges that the natural right of retaliation is here intimated, "because public judgments had not yet been constituted." But the natural right of retaliation did not first arise now. And although public judgments had not yet been constituted, nevertheless provision is here made that, as the human race increased, they should be constituted, lest, namely, that grievous antediluvian age, crammed with unbridled violence, should return. The magistrate, therefore, is instituted by God, and the sword is put into his hands, as Munster rightly notes on the place.

XV. To this precept was added that other also, concerning not eating flesh with the blood, which is the life of the flesh. Since the investigation of that matter [ p. 162 ] would involve more labor than profit, having spent a few words in its explanation, let us dismiss it.

XVI. Most affirm that men before the flood were not permitted to eat flesh. For whatever privilege there is in the eating of flesh seems to be conceded only now, Gen. 9:3: "Every moving thing that liveth shall be meat for you; even as the green herb have I given you all things" [Hebrew: kol-remes asher hu-chai lakhem yihyeh le'okhlah keyereq esev natatti lakhem eth-kol]. These words are joined to those by which dominion over all animals is bestowed on man, verse 2. This place, compared with that other in which God granted food to the antediluvians, plainly shows that they did not use flesh. Hence the report of abstinence from animals reached the Gentiles; Ovid, Metamorphoses, book 1, fable 3, verse 103:

"And content with foods produced with no man compelling, they gathered the fruit of the arbutus, and mountain strawberries, and cornel cherries, and blackberries clinging to the hard bramble thickets, and acorns that had fallen from the spreading tree of Jupiter" (Ovid, Metamorphoses 1.103-106). And again: "But that old age, to which we have given the name of Golden, was blessed with the fruits of trees and the herbs which the ground brings forth, and did not pollute its mouth with gore. Then birds moved their wings safely through the air, and the hare wandered fearless in the open fields; nor had its own credulity hung the fish upon the hook" (Metamorphoses 15.96-101). Virgil also: "Even before the scepter of the Dictaean king, and before an impious race feasted on slaughtered bullocks, golden Saturn lived this life on earth" (Georgics 2.536-538). And from our own writers, Tertullian, On the Foods of the Jews (if he is the author of that book), chapter 2: "The first food of men," he says, "was only the produce and fruit of trees." Hence the philosophers dispute at length how it came about that men were ever induced to eat flesh. Plutarch, after skillfully canvassing the fact, ascribes it to necessity, in his oration On the Eating of Flesh [Greek: peri sarkophagias]. Porphyry contends with many arguments that that custom flowed from participation in sacrifices, in book 2 of his treatise On Abstinence from Animate Creatures [Greek: peri apoches empsychon]. Hence Casaubon shows, in his Animadversions on Athenaeus, book 1, chapter 11, that the ancient Greeks called all animals that are slaughtered hiereia [sacrificial victims], because, namely, they ate no flesh except of those animals which had been slain for the use of sacrifices. Burnt offerings alone, therefore, were in use before the flood. When the eating of flesh was permitted, feasts upon the sacred things accompanied the sacrifices, which is probable even before the law, although Selden defends the contrary, On the Law of Nature among the Hebrews, book 3, chapter 8. On the beginnings of the eating of flesh, consult Bereshith Rabbah, Parashah 34, and Joseph Albo, book Ikkarim, part 3, chapter 14.

XVII. Flesh being permitted, the eating of it with the blood is prohibited, verse 4: "But flesh with the life thereof, which is the blood thereof, shall ye not eat" [Hebrew: akh basar benafsho damo lo tokhelu]. It is not the eating of the blood itself, but of flesh [ p. 163 ] with the blood, that is prohibited. It has been shown that the long-lived men of the primitive age were prone to cruelty. Hence the earth before the flood was filled with violence. Experience among the American Indians teaches that men are made savage by eating raw flesh, with the blood still almost throbbing with motion. To meet that vice, on the border of which, above all others, the human race destitute of the word of God seems to be placed, this prohibition of eating flesh with the blood was given. Some understand a limb torn from a living animal. They say the barbarous Americans still delight in that food. So Aben Ezra on the place: "Because I have granted you the eating of the flesh of animals, you ought not to be cruel toward the beasts themselves, so as to eat a limb from a living animal." Grotius is of opinion that the more recent Jews were carried into this interpretation by hatred of the Christians, that is, of the Greek Christians, who still abstain from sausages distended with gore, as Tertullian speaks. To me it scarcely seems that the eating of blood which has already grown cold is here prohibited. Nor can that be proved by any suitable argument from the text. Indeed blood is not primarily, directly, or at all forbidden; much less the eating of it separated from the flesh. The object of the prohibition is flesh, affected in a peculiar manner: benafsho, "in its life"; that is, while it is still animated by living blood. And Stephanus Curcellaeus seems to me to have wasted his labor in his Posthumous Diatribe on the Eating of Blood, in which he contends that that question is of some moment in Christian practice. Let Ludovicus De Dieu be consulted on the place. But whether what is prohibited is ever min hachai [Hebrew: ever min ha-chai], "a limb from the living," that is, with the blood still dripping, or dam min hachai, "the blood of an animal still living," or "blood from an animal" still throbbing in the flesh, is uncertain.
XVIII. To this head pertain also those seven much-discussed [Greek: polythrylleta] precepts which report says God gave to the sons of Noah. They are commonly enumerated thus:
1. Al avodah zarah [Hebrew: al avodah zarah], Of strange worship [the prohibition of idolatry].
2. Al birkat hashem [Hebrew: al birkat ha-shem], Of cursing the Name, or the Deity.
3. Al shefikhut damim [Hebrew: al shefikhut damim], Of the shedding of blood.
4. Al gilluy arayot [Hebrew: al gilluy arayot], Of the uncovering of nakednesses.
5. Al hagezelah [Hebrew: al ha-gezelah], Of rapine.
6. Al hadinim [Hebrew: al ha-dinim], Of judgments [the administration of justice].
7. Al ever min hachai [Hebrew: al ever min ha-chai], Of the limb of a living animal.

XIX. By the first of these every kind of idolatry is prohibited. Concerning the sense and interpretation of the second, the learned are not so well agreed; the dispute arises from the ambiguous use of the word barakh [Hebrew: barakh], for it signifies both to bless and also to curse. There are some who take the words affirmatively, and would have the blessing of the divine name, or true worship opposed to that strange worship, intended by them. More take them negatively, of not cursing the name of God. The third concerns homicide. The fourth concerns adultery and every unlawful sexual union. The fifth expresses the eighth commandment of the decalogue. Political government is instituted by the sixth; and the last teaches mortals to abhor all cruelty.

[ p. 164 ] XX. Genebrard asserts that these precepts were given to Noah and his sons in the first year after the flood, in his Chronicle, at the year of the world 1656. "Seven precepts," he says, "are given, which the Hebrews call partly the precepts of the sons of Noah, partly the precepts of nature, and which they hold to bind the Gentiles also." This persuasion, of course, flowed from the traditions of the Jews. In both Talmuds and in the writings of many rabbis mention of them is most frequent. But that the Jews affirm these precepts to have been given to Noah and his sons, that is, to those four men, immediately after the flood, is false. By the Noachidae, or sons of Noah, they understand all the nations distinct from themselves; while they call themselves Abrahamidae and sons of Israel, in opposition to the sons of Noah: absurdly, as if Abraham and Israel were not sons of Noah. But so they love to speak. By the Noachidae they understand all those who, descending from Noah, enjoyed no spiritual privilege. In affirming that these precepts were given to such men, they mean nothing else than that they are general dictates of right reason, or heads of natural law obtaining binding force toward all those who are destitute of supernatural revelation, while they themselves are bound in addition by the written law. Hence Seder Olam, chapter 5, and Sepher Melakim [the Book of Kings, i.e. Maimonides, Hilchoth Melakim], chapter 9, affirm that the six earlier precepts were delivered to Adam. By which, namely, they show that they are common dictates of nature, or universal heads of natural law. The last, they assert, was added for Noah; so they interpret that command about not eating flesh with the blood: Manasseh [ben Israel], Conciliator, question 2 on Deuteronomy. For the rest, it is well known that John Selden has illustrated these precepts with most learned commentaries, to which we remit the reader who is studious of these matters.

XXI. I do not wish to set down here the oration of Noah to his descendants, whom he brought with him into Italy, which Curtius Inghiramius brought to light among the fragments of Etruscan antiquities, as though it would occupy some place in this enlargement of antediluvian theology. For that whole drama about the discovered capsules, which were supposed to hold enclosed little histories of the antiquities of Etruria, from the arrival of Noah in Italy down to the Catilinarian war, seems to me, and not to me alone, a most laborious fiction of a man learned indeed, but abusing his leisure and his talent too much, nay impiously. But since in the oration which is ascribed to Noah the author of the drama has observed what is fitting [Greek: to prepon], I have thought it might be allowed a place here for the reader's judgment. The title of the capsule in which the oration was deposited is this: "Noa Vandimon." Then: "These are the words of the great Vandimon, which I, Ancus Cecinna, transcribed." And the oration is this: "Hear, my sons and grandsons, my words, and receive with your ears what your father speaks to you. Blessed be God, before whose face I have found grace, who reserved me, snatched out of the waters, for the repairing of the human race, which, God being angry, was destroyed by rains and showers, lest what God had made should utterly perish. Be not, therefore, ungrateful to the most great Aesar [the Etruscan name for God]; fear him alone, serve him from your minds, because he is worthy, and because he has done many good things for you, and [ p. 165 ] because he is God. If the most great Aesar shall always be your God, nothing shall be lacking to you. Let his altar not be polluted; let no one seek the life of his brother, as Cain did. Let there be among you a bond of peace, and let it never be broken. You have this city, which I have built on this mountain; when this shall not suffice you (for you must be multiplied), you will be able to build others on the same mountain; and if the mountain shall not suffice, choose healthful places in which you may be able to build others. And if there be no dissensions among you, all things shall prosper for you. He who does not fear God, and who dissents from his brothers, let him be cast out from among you, because the discord of a brother corrupts all things. Do not, my sons, again provoke God by fornication, by iniquity, and wickedness, but remember God, who made you, delivered you from the waters, created all things for your sake, and subjected all things to you. God exalts the good and destroys the wicked; love him alone, and do all things in his fear; for doing these things you shall be filled with abundance of grain, wine, and oil, and the sons of your brothers shall serve you. Your kingdom shall remain for ages. Keep always the sacred rites and ceremonies, and all things which I leave to you; and teach them to your sons. May God bless you and fill you with every blessing: and may he quickly send a king to deliver you from alien power." Thus he; and I marvel that his trifles ever pleased any learned men, since there is not a page of the whole book that does not sufficiently betray fraud and imposture.

XXII. And this was the postdiluvian theology: namely, that natural theology of Adam, destined to a new end through the promise of the Messiah and consigned to new uses, conformed to that state into which Satan by sin had with evil guile enticed all men, augmented and restored by a new revelation, by the renewal of a solemn covenant, by a visible sign, and by various precepts; which the church of eight persons held as the rule of faith, of obedience, and of all divine worship. That many, instructed by it, passed their lives with the highest reverence toward God, with piety, faith, love, and obedience, and toward their own kind with gentleness, charity, mercy, justice, and faithfulness, there is no reason for us to doubt. For holiness of heart, purity and integrity of life, beneficence toward others, simplicity and candor of all manners, and the other virtues by whose benefit men are renewed unto the image of God, could flourish even under the least degree of external revelation, provided the Holy Spirit were present, irradiating the minds of men with his saving light.

XXIII. By the benefit of this theology, the reformed church, as to external profession and confession [Greek: homologia], lived on earth pure and acceptable to God for forty years or thereabouts. For nothing that in any way defiled it is recorded, except after the birth, and perhaps after some years' growth, of Canaan, the fourth and youngest son of Ham. At length the long-concealed impiety of Ham broke out. Of what kind it was the Holy Spirit shows, Gen. 9:22. It was a sin against the dictates of right reason, which, as Aristotle witnesses, teach all men to honor parents [Greek: goneis timan]. [ p. 166 ] So also Plutarch, On Brotherly Love [Greek: peri philadelphias]: "All men say and sing that nature, and the law that preserves nature, have assigned to parents, after the gods, the first and greatest honor; and there is nothing that men do more pleasing to the gods than when they cheerfully repay to those who begot and reared them the old favors lent, with new ones added; nor again has there ever been a greater demonstration of an atheistic temper than contempt of parents and offenses against them. Therefore, while it is forbidden to do ill to others, with regard to one's own mother and father, even the failure to give oneself always to doing and saying the things by which they will be gladdened, even if no grief be added besides, is held unholy and lawless." Thus he, excellently. Hierocles has similar things, and almost the whole chorus of the wise. It is strange, therefore, that some, on account of those words, Gen. 9:24, "And he knew what his younger son had done unto him" [Hebrew: vayyeda eth asher-asah lo beno haqqatan], contend that not Ham but Canaan ought to be understood. "For what," they say, "did Ham do to his father? He was only a messenger to his brothers concerning his father's disgrace." These are the words of the great Scaliger in his Refutation of the Oration of David Pareus. That, I say, is what he did: he openly and publicly transgressed the law of nature, which is plainly evident from the deed of his brothers, set in opposition to his impiety. He "showed his father's nakedness to his brothers with mockery," as Josephus rightly says, Antiquities, book 1, chapter 1 [1.6.3]. He sinned, therefore, against the greatest principles of that theology which was the foundation and rule of the church, of worship, and of obedience.

XXIV. The sin of Ham, therefore, the sacred Scripture, as we have shown, expounds clearly enough. The things that are added to that narrative by some are conjectures, nay, the ravings of dreamers. The rabbis trifle that he not only saw, or gazed upon his father's nakedness with joy and a kind of insulting triumph, but that he cut off his genitals, or by magic arts bewitched him, that he might beget no sons thereafter: Rashi on chapter 11 [9] of Genesis. Boissard adheres to their fables in his Apollonius of Tyana, and Athanasius Kircher seems to give them credit, On the Origin of Letters and Obelisks, chapter 2. Hence almost all suspect that the fable arose of Saturn cutting off the genitals of his father Caelus, as Lactantius, book 1, chapter 12, or of Jupiter cutting Saturn himself with the sickle. And that indeed is very likely, although, to confess the truth, Porphyry, in his book On the Cave of the Nymphs at Ithaca, accommodates the whole fable so cleverly to a cryptic natural theology that I could almost believe the first fable-makers had something of that sort in mind. But the curse is expressly denounced against Canaan, [ p. 167 ] either because he was at that time especially his father's darling, being, namely, his youngest son; or because he stood by his father in the sin and the abominable spectacle; or because that family among the posterity of Ham was to be the first to depart from the true church and the worship of God; or for the consolation of the Israelites, who, about to enter the promised land, might understand that the nation with which they were to engage in war was accursed, and long since devoted to servitude and destruction. However it was, it is most certain that Ham himself was cursed in the person of his son, that is, cast out of the family of God. But wonderful, or rather laughable, are the things which some of the ancients trifle about the passion of Christ prefigured in the drunkenness of Noah, about the cross, the sacraments, the Gentiles, the Jews, the ministers of the word, all of which they fancied they saw in the wine, in Ham, in Japheth, and in the garment.

XXV. On account of this sin, therefore, by which his hypocrisy, concealed for some time, broke out to the gravest scandal of the church and openly displayed itself, Ham was cursed by his father, who still, under God, presided over the whole church by paternal authority. And in his ejection from the society of the godly we have another and that an illustrious example of ecclesiastical reformation. For the reformation of the church is first to be attempted by the removal of offenders from the assembly and communion of those who duly worship God by faith. We gave an example of this earlier in the postlapsarian Adamic church. But when the band of the contumacious grows strong, the separation of the faithful from their assemblies supplies its place.

XXVI. In this manner, then, the reformation of the new church was instituted. For such is the infirmity of all and the malice of many, that no church, from the first beginnings of the world, has ever been able to endure long without some signal reformation. Now to this church, recently reformed, new promises were given; partly for consolation in a most wretched lot, partly that they might be spurs to more exact obedience, and to a life to be spent before God with the utmost care and diligence. Gen. 9:26, 27: "Blessed be Jehovah, the God of Shem; God shall persuade Japheth, that he may dwell in the tents of Shem."

XXVII. That Ham, driven by the paternal curse from the solemn worship of God, also fled all the assemblies of the godly, there is no doubt. But the time was not yet at hand when the human race was to be dispersed into the various parts of the earth. Therefore Ham and his people continued to dwell under the same region of the sky with his brothers and their descendants. Hence arose a new stain of evil; hence the seat of defection sprouted again in a church not long since emerged from the flood and recently reformed. That Noah, Shem, Japheth, and their godly sons and grandsons endeavored to preserve intact in the church the purity of the worship of God according to the canons of that theology with which they were furnished, no one doubts. Both their own piety and the consideration of their duty toward others demanded it. But that very many of their descendants, carried away by the impious seductions and examples of the Hamites, since they themselves also had a nature headlong toward every crime, foully and almost universally fell away from the rule [ p. 168 ] and worship of God within a few years, we shall understand from the things that followed. For in the hundred and first year after the flood, the crowd of apostates, covered over with crimes, mad with pride, and contumacious, openly setting at naught the authority, discipline, and admonitions of Noah, was rushing headlong into every crime. And since the humble, peaceable, and pious manner of life of Noah displeased them above all things, they went off into everything contrary. Casting away, therefore, all reverence for the Deity, and lifted up with I know not what pride, they applied themselves to building a tower whence they might procure for themselves a name and glory. But as they were attempting great things, hateful to God, the horrendous judgment of the confusion of tongues seized them and scattered them over the surface of the whole earth. From that day until the coming of the promised Messiah, God permitted the greatest part of the human race to walk in their own ways.

Chapter argument: Noah, ish ha'adamah [Hebrew: ish ha-adamah, "a man of the ground"]; the fable of Saturn and Rhea born thence; Janus; a herald of righteousness; the Sibylline oracles fictitious; the authors of the Sibylline verses; whence the Sibyls were so called; traditions mingled with their oracles; a specimen; the builders of the tower of Babel; the manner and cause of its building; what the primeval language was; the opinion of Grotius weighed; whence the holy tongue was called Hebrew, Gen. 10:21, Josh. 24:2, 3; the most monstrous fables of the Targumist Uzziel; Abraham called a Hebrew from Eber; the time of the division of tongues.

I. Scripture notes that Noah was ish ha'adamah, that is, "a man of the earth," Gen. 9:20: occupied, namely, in a humble manner of living. This befitted the wretched condition of sinners on earth, and his own in particular, who had seen the horrendous judgments of God upon the antediluvian world. Hence the fable-makers called Saturn the man or husband of Rhea, that is, of the Earth. The conjecture is Bochart's, Sacred Geography, book 1, chapter 1. Whatever later men thought of him, it is plain that that manner of life displeased the impious of his age. Planting a vineyard also, he "drank of the yain," or wine [Hebrew: yesht min-hayyayin]; whence he was called Janus. That he was a herald of righteousness before the flood, the apostle affirms, 2 Pet. 2:5. Duly discharging that office, he condemned by faith and deeds the world which he could not heal, Heb. 11:7. His preaching is remembered in the verses which are called the Sibylline oracles, in these and other lines:

"Faithless men, smitten with great madness, your deeds shall not escape God; for the immortal Savior, the all-seeing one, knows all things, he who commanded me to announce them to you, that you may not perish in your hearts. Be sober, cut off your wickednesses, and do not fight violently with one another, having a blood-stained heart, drenching the broad earth with the blood of men." [Owen's Latin rendering: "Treacherous men, what madness has seized you? God is not ignorant of what you do; for the unquenchable Author of salvation sees all things and knows all things; who bade me bring back these words to you, [ p. 169 ] lest your mad sloth be your destruction. Speak righteousness, abstain from vices, and do not exercise hatreds among yourselves with bloody minds, staining the earth far and wide with human blood."] So speaks that Sibyl who a little later feigns herself to be Noah's daughter-in-law, and to have been in the ark together with him and her husband. But everything that is circulated under the name of the Sibyls is fabricated. I marvel that any mortal was ever of such abject credulity as to think otherwise. Their authors not only drew very much from the sacred Scriptures, but were Christians, and heretics. That Priscilla and Maximilla, the prophetesses of Montanus, composed those patchworks [centos], if women were the authors of the deed, is by no means improbable. It was only after the publication of their prophecies that the mysteries of the Christian religion came to be drawn out of the Sibylline oracles. That certain prophesying women, whom they called Sibyls, lived among the Gentiles, as though privy to the more secret counsels of the gods (for "to be possessed by a god," says Diodorus, book 4, chapter 61 [4.66], "is to sibyllize" [Greek: to gar entheazein, sibyllainein]), I would not deny. But that their lots and oracles were diabolical, the credit of all history teaches. Consult what Dionysius of Halicarnassus, Plutarch, Cicero, Varro, Livy, Tacitus, Zosimus, and others have handed down to memory as drawn from their books at various times by order of the Roman senate: everything savors of diabolical superstition and of paganism. Cornelius Tacitus gives the sum of them all, Annals 15, chapter 44: "By custom," he says, "expiations were sought from the gods, and the books of the Sibyl were consulted, in accordance with which supplication was made to Vulcan and to Ceres and Proserpina, and Juno was propitiated by the matrons, first on the Capitol, then at the nearest point of the sea." Perhaps, however, the ancient serpent mingled even some true things with the false, after his manner, whereby he might render the true suspect and procure credit for the false. Even those notions which, by faint report, from the tradition of the first promise and of other divine revelations, had invaded the earlier ages of the world concerning the future kingdom of the Messiah, drawn off into pagan channels, had been mingled with those vaticinations. Of such a kind seems to be that which Marcus Tullius mentions in book 2 of On Divination, chapter 54: "We attend," he says, "to the verses of the Sibyl, which she is said to have poured forth in frenzy; whose interpreter was lately thought, according to the talk of men, to be about to declare certain falsehoods in the senate, namely, that he whom we in fact had as king must also be called king, if we wished to be safe. If this is in the books, against what man and what time is it directed? For he who composed those verses contrived cunningly that, whatever happened, it should seem to have been predicted, all definition of men and times being removed." He marks Lucius Cotta, one of the Fifteen [the quindecimviri who kept the Sibylline books], who, at the instigation of Antony, attempted to gratify the ambition of Julius Caesar, who desired to add the very name of king to regal power, by means of the public report of those times concerning a most great king to come. The same oracle is set forth in almost the same words by the most renowned of the Roman historians, Suetonius and Tacitus; the former in the Life of Vespasian, the latter in the fifth book of the Histories. Suetonius calls it "an old and constant opinion"; Tacitus, "a persuasion from the ancient books of the priests." [ p. 170 ] Namely, it is, "Out of thee shall come forth the ruler" [Greek: ek sou exeleusetai ho hegoumenos]; that is, the East shall grow strong, and one set over Judaea shall gain possession of affairs. But these things have nothing to do with our verses.

II. But we showed above that Noah admonished his antediluvian contemporaries of their crimes. It would be wicked to suspect that he did not also strive, with yet more earnest labor, to restrain his sons and grandsons after the flood from impiety and from defection from God; both in vain. For although he was the father of the whole new people, yet he could not restrain his degenerate sons from the foolish attempt to make their name famous by the building of a huge tower, Gen. 11:3, 4.

III. The sacred Scripture clearly intimates that Noah himself and all the godly abstained from that insane undertaking of the apostates. Those who attempted that deed were benei ha'adam [Hebrew: benei ha-adam], Gen. 11:5; that is, "the sons of men." These are opposed to "the sons of God," that is, the godly and those fearing God. For "the sons of God," from the days of Enosh, both before and after the flood, were godly men adhering to the Adamic theology and to divine worship; "the sons of men" were deserters and apostates. The Holy Spirit, therefore, here points to the impious apostates, deserters of the church. That the fable of the giants was born hence, even boys know. Now they built the tower, not that by it they might ascend into heaven. Nor is it credible that those men raved with that madness, whom God hindered from completing what they had begun, verse 6. Nor did they attempt that deed in order to set themselves out of reach of another flood. They could not be ignorant that there would be no universal flood. Nor for that end would they have descended from the loftiest mountains of the whole earth into a most deep valley. But Moses expressly testifies, verse 4, that, lifted up with pride and vainglory [Greek: kenodoxia], they had resolved with themselves to procure a name and glory for themselves by that work.

IV. Concerning the confusion of tongues many things are disputed here and there which do not belong to this place. Let us add a few things concerning the primeval language, and the reason of the name which it afterwards obtained. We have said that "the sons of God" in no way showed themselves accessory to the crime of tower-building [Greek: pyrgopoiia]. Among them, therefore, and their descendants, the use of the primeval language remained. That this language was the Hebrew, namely, that which was afterwards so called, learned men are almost agreed. Rightly the celebrated Bochart, Sacred Geography, book 1, chapter 10: "The languages of the others being confounded, these" (that is, the godly partakers of the covenant) "retained their own, that is, that most ancient one, which from Eber was called Hebrew, because it remained whole and intact among the posterity of Eber." So far as I know, Grotius alone among the moderns is of another opinion, in his Annotations on Gen. 11:1, "The earth was of one lip." "That language," he says, "the Hebrews claim as their own; the Syrians as theirs." This is right; the Hebrews say so, and that constantly. So the Targum of Jonathan: "They spoke in the holy tongue, in which the world was created from the beginning." The Jerusalem Targum in the same words. Rabbi Solomon Jarchi [Rashi] on the place: "The one lip is the holy tongue." So also Aben Ezra, and all the ancient Christians, Theodoret excepted. "But the Syrians think it was their language," says Grotius. This also is true. The Maronites are of that opinion, [ p. 171 ] following Theodoret. But it is only too plainly evident that they think so absurdly. That the Syriac language took its origin from the Hebrew, not the Hebrew from the Syriac, is acknowledged by all who have any understanding of their analogy. The purity and simplicity of the Hebrew tongue cry it aloud. "The Maronite Syrians," says Joseph Scaliger, in his letter to Richard Thomson, "contend with any wager you please that Syriac is more ancient than Hebrew; which is the height of ignorance: for it is just as if they said the Italian idiom was older than the Latin. For certainly melekh [Hebrew: melekh, 'king'] is prior to malka [the Aramaic form], and we can bring many other things to overthrow this obstinacy." "But the Hebrew language," says Grotius, "is the language of the ivrim [Hebrew: ivrim], the newcomers from Chaldea. For this is the origin of the name of the Hebrews, not from Eber. See Josh. 24:2, 3. That Hebrew language, therefore, by the sojourn of Abraham and his posterity in Canaan, approached ever nearer and nearer to the Canaanite language." Thus he; nothing sound in it [Greek: ouden hygies].

V. I confess that that account of the imposition of the names [Greek: onomatothesia], namely, of "Hebrew" and "Hebrews," has pleased certain learned men, and still pleases them. Erpenius collected the arguments for that opinion in his Oration on the Hebrew Tongue. They are refuted by Fuller, Sacred Miscellanies, book 4, chapter 4, and by Rivet on Genesis, Exercitation 66; and we have shown above that Bochart rejects that derivation of those words. It was indeed accounted a great thing of old to have gone out of Mesopotamia, among the nations whom "the rivers hold shut in" [Greek: synkleisantes hoi potamoi echousin]. For "thinking themselves islanders, they say that they go down to the sea when they walk to the rivers, and they make the circuit of the rivers the boundary of their land," as Philostratus says in the Life of Apollonius, book 1, chapter 14 [1.20]. But that Abraham was thence called a Hebrew, and the holy tongue Hebrew, that is, he a crosser-over and it the language of those crossing over, is too incredible. Moses says that "Shem was the father of all the children of Eber," Gen. 10:21. And what else this should be than "the father of all the Hebrews" can scarcely be understood.

VI. Therefore the sons of Eber are the Hebrews; and the Hebrews are the sons of Eber. And this is the account of the naming [Greek: onomatologia]. Some say these words can signify that "Shem was the father of all those beyond the river." But that is said without any example, and is destitute of all verisimilitude. If indeed the Holy Spirit had said, "He was the father of all the ivrim" [Hebrew: hu avi kol ha-ivrim], that conjecture would not have been so futile.
