But as the words stand, namely, kol-benei-ever, "all the sons of Eber," it is clear from the common usage of the holy tongue that the posterity of Eber, and no others, are denoted by them. Nor can any probable reason be rendered why Shem should be called distinctively [Greek: diakridon] the father of all those beyond the river, since the greatest part of his own posterity always dwelt on this side of the river, while very many families sprung from Ham crossed over. And what we have said of Eber is expressly affirmed by Balaam in his last prophecy, Num. 24:24: "Ships from Chittim shall afflict Asshur, and shall afflict Eber"; where the interpreters agree that the prophet uses gentile and patronymic names for their cognates, [ p. 172 ] so that the Vulgate renders "the Assyrians and the Hebrews."

VII. But Grotius sends us to Josh. 24:3. Joshua does indeed say in that place that God took Abraham me'ever hannahar [Hebrew: me-ever ha-nahar], "from the crossing of the river," or from the place which was beyond the river. He supposes, therefore, that Abraham was thence called ivri, as you might say, a crosser-over; and that for this reason, namely, because Abraham crossed the river, that name was so given into use by the Holy Spirit that it became the surname of all his posterity, and that for all time they were called crossers, or the nation of those passing over. But no parallel or similar account of such an imposition of names exists anywhere among the nations in the records of history. Not a trace, therefore, of any ground for the conjecture appears here. Nor was Abraham the only one in that age who had crossed the Euphrates to migrate into the land of Canaan. On that reasoning, therefore, and because he had dwelt in Mesopotamia, he was no more to be called a crosser-over, or one from beyond the river, than very many others. Nay, it is most certain that most of the Canaanites, sprung namely from Ham, crossed the Euphrates not long after the Babylonian dispersion. They, therefore, on this derivation of the word, would no less deserve to be called Hebrews than Abraham himself and his posterity; nor could they any less than the apostle Paul have boasted that they were Hebrews of the Hebrews.

VIII. It is said gratuitously by some in this cause, nay, it is falsely presumed, that Abraham was first called a Hebrew by the Canaanites, as if, namely, they wished to denote by that name his crossing over, which, as they say, they had almost beheld with their own eyes. For it was by the Holy Spirit himself that he was first marked with that surname, Gen. 14:13. "There came one that had escaped," it says, "and told Abram the Hebrew." The words are the Holy Spirit's, not the Canaanites'. It is true that among the Canaanites he was first so named; but by the Canaanites, by no means. But the Seventy render those words "to Abram the crosser" [Greek: Abram to perate]. Hence Chrysostom, too rashly, supposed that he was also called Abram for that reason, and that the name has that signification, Homily 35 on Genesis. "From the beginning," he says, "his parents gave him this name, signifying beforehand his removal from thence. For since he was to cross the Euphrates and come into Palestine, for this reason he was also called Abram." Would that it were in this place alone that that most learned man blundered through ignorance of the Hebrew language. But that the signification of that name is plainly otherwise, even boys know. Some think that the Seventy called Abraham peraten [crosser] from Perath [Hebrew: Perath, the Euphrates]. But the force of the word openly deceives them. They rendered, then, an interpretation of the word, not the word itself. That this was customary with them, Jerome observes on Isa. 27:12, where conversely they translate nachal Mitsrayim [the brook of Egypt] by "Rhinocorura." "For the torrent of Egypt," he says, "the Seventy translated Rhinocorura, which is a town on the border of Egypt and Palestine, expressing not so much the words of the Scriptures as the sense of the words." [ p. 173 ] So, deceived by the similarity of the letters, they more than once translated "servant" for "Hebrew," reading, namely, Daleth in place of Resh: 1 Sam. 13:3, "Let the Hebrews hear" [Hebrew: yishme'u ha-ivrim]; the Seventy, "The servants have revolted" [Greek: ethetekasin hoi douloi]. What ethetekasin is supposed to mean, I do not know; douloi [servants] stands for ivrim. And Jonah 1:9, "I am a Hebrew" [Hebrew: ivri anokhi]; the Seventy, "I am a servant of the Lord" [Greek: doulos Kyriou eimi ego]; where they add "of the Lord" of their own, to give a color to their blunder. There is no reason, therefore, why their interpretation of this name should move us in the least. But if Moses had wished to call him a crosser-over and not a Hebrew, he would have named him not ivri, which is most plainly a patronymic, like Emori [Amorite] and Amaleqi [Amalekite], but over or overi, as Augustine, Steuchus, Drusius, and Buxtorf show. But the reason of the naming is at hand. The whole nation of the Canaanites, destined of old to be rooted out, had at that time been compounded out of many families which, driven from their eastern seats by various chances, or impelled by a certain impulse from God, had flowed together into that land under various leaders. And they were all distinguished among themselves by surnames taken from the best-known founders of their stocks (not from Ham, not from Canaan himself, just as neither was Abraham named from Shem), which Moses recounts, Gen. 10:15-18. This man was an Amorite, that one a Jebusite, another a Hivite, and a Hittite. Each was denominated from his stock, or from the founder of his stock. Abraham, dwelling among them, was called a Hebrew from Eber, the most famous founder of the family from which he sprang, and was thus separated, even by his familiar appellation, from the whole lineage of Ham. For in the same place where Abraham is first called ivri, or Hebrew, his ally Mamre is styled Emori, or Amorite; so that we may easily perceive that the reason of both surnames was the same, for both alike are patronymics. As, therefore, all the inhabitants of that land, marked with some patronymic name, were distinguished among themselves, so Abraham was called a Hebrew from the most celebrated founder of his stock. Nor do the Targumists take that word in any other sense. He who consults Uzziel [pseudo-Jonathan ben Uzziel] on the place will meet with a fable which I have thought fit to set out here, that the reader may understand what coals in place of treasure they will find who happen to fall in with that Talmudical fabulist; for scarcely anywhere does he show his quality more than in this place. Thus, then, he on Gen. 14:13: "And there came Og, who had escaped from among the men who died in the flood; and he had ridden upon the ark, and there was a covering over its top, and he was fed from the food of Noah; nor was he delivered for his own righteousness, but that the inhabitants of the world might see the power of God and say: Did not the giants who were from the beginning rebel against the Lord of the world, and were destroyed from the earth? And he came to Abraham at the going down of the day of Passover, and found him making unleavened cakes." To these you may add the death of the same Og, the antediluvian, namely, which he relates thus on Num. 21 [22]: "And it came to pass, after the wicked Og... saw the camp of the Israelites, which stretched out six miles, he said within himself, I will draw up a battle line against this people, that they may not do to me as they did [ p. 174 ] to Sihon. He went and tore up a mountain six miles in extent, and set it upon his head, to hurl it upon them. Straightway the word of the Lord prepared a worm, which gnawed the mountain and bored through it, and his head was sunk into the midst of it; he wished to draw his head out of it" (not, as in the London translation, "he wished to draw it off his head") "but he could not, because his teeth and the molars of his mouth had been drawn this way and that. Moses went and took an axe of ten cubits, and leaped ten cubits, and struck him on the ankle of his foot, and falling he died." Thus he; things worthy to be numbered among the fables of Aesop; and yet not worthy, since they are insipid and in every way useless, nay noxious. The Koran of Mohammed does not teem with more shameless lies than this Pentateuch of Ben Uzziel. And he is so much the more to be detested, in that the trifler set himself to befoul the sacred oracles of God. The Targum of Onkelos has ivra'ah, "to the Hebrew"; and at Exod. 2:6 it renders ha-ivriyyim, "of the Hebrews," by "of the Jews" [Aramaic: yehuda'ei]; and at Deut. 15:12, achikha ha-ivri, "thy brother a Hebrew," by "thy brother a son of Israel." To him "Hebrew," "Jew," and "son of Israel" are the same; that is, that name is a patronymic.

IX. Some object that no probable reason can be assigned why the whole family should have been denominated from the name of Eber rather than of others, namely of Shem or of Arphaxad; or why Abraham, rather than the other descendants of Eber, should be called a Hebrew from Eber. But a probable cause and reason for both are not lacking. That Eber was greatly eminent in the new people is probable from this, that, the rest being passed over, Shem is pointedly said to be "the father of all the children of Eber," Gen. 10:21. And no reason can be assigned how he attained that degree of honor except through holiness and virtue toward God. Further, of all the patriarchs born after the flood he proved the longest-lived [Greek: makrobiotatos], and survived his son Peleg. After Peleg's death no one can doubt that he undertook a peculiar care of his grandsons, and was to them in the place of an immediate parent. Thus, although Aram was the youngest son of Shem, yet because he lived longer than all his brothers, the Syriac language, which very many others besides his own progeny used, was called Aramaic, as Elias Levita teaches us in the preface to the Methurgeman. Eber also lived, distinguished for piety, at that time when the tongues were confounded and the earth divided; and in view of that specimen of adorable divine providence he gave his son the name Peleg [division]. Also from the preservation of the primeval tongue in his family, he seems to have been the leader of those who did not consent to the construction of the tower. It was not, therefore, except for the weightiest reasons that his name was transmitted to his descendants. And that they constituted a family or tribe separately from the rest of the posterity of Shem appears most plainly from those words of Moses which we related before.

X. Further: it is evident from the event not only that all the sons of Joktan, who was the younger son of Eber, betook themselves to the apostate crowd, but also that very many sprung from Peleg himself mingled with it, and bade farewell to the paternal family [ p. 175 ] in which the pure worship of God flourished. But Abraham above the rest trod in the footsteps of his godly forefathers, and indeed of Eber himself, although he too was by no means free from the stain of the common defection. At length, however, under the leading of God, by the benefit of new revelations, he brought back the ancestral piety and the worship of God, then an exile from the earth. Should it then seem strange, especially since he became celebrated above all the other mortals of his age, and was constituted the head and author of a most glorious nation, if he claimed for himself and his posterity that celebrated patronymic name (which the rest of that kindred had cast off by their apostasy, or at least had esteemed as nothing)? Nor was the crossing of a river of such moment that from it a name should be imposed on him and on the whole holy nation, to endure for all time. The migration of Abraham from his fatherland and from the land of his kindred, that he might follow God to a place which he knew not, was indeed an illustrious proof of his faith. But he judges ill who thinks this Abrahamic obedience is to be estimated from the crossing of a river, which was common to him with innumerable others. That, obedient to God's word, complying with his will and commands, leaving his ancestral hearth and his own seats, and the superstitious worship in which he had been brought up, enticed by no hope of a richer condition, he followed him he knew not whither: this, among innumerable other things, commends his faith. If the crossing of a river be compared with these things, it will be a thing of nothing. But that that migration so greatly pleased God that on that account he received the promises, namely, that his posterity should possess by hereditary right the land in which he was to sojourn, and that in him all the kindreds of the earth should be blessed, as a certain writer affirms, is false and untheological [Greek: atheologon]. False, because he had received those promises before he had crossed the Tigris, not to say the Euphrates, as may be seen, Gen. 12:1-4. And untheological, because it teaches that this is in any way to be imputed to the obedience of Abraham, which it is most certain proceeded in every way from mere grace. For that God gave those promises to Abraham without any respect to any antecedent obedience is too well acknowledged to need proofs. Concerning this derivation of the name of the Hebrews, there agree with us, from among the Jews, Josephus, Antiquities, book 1, chapter 6; the threefold Targum; Aben Ezra on Exodus 1; Kimchi, under the root avar; Elias Levita, Methurgeman; from the Fathers and ancient Christians, Eusebius, Preparation for the Gospel, book 7, chapter 2; Jerome, Questions on Genesis; Augustine, On the City of God, book 16, chapter 3; Eucherius on Genesis, book 2, chapter 7; Zonaras, Annals, book 1; from the moderns, Calvin on Gen. 10:20; Pererius on Genesis 20; Mercerus on Gen. 14:13, and in Pagninus's Lexicon, root avar; Munster on Gen. 11:16; Drusius on Gen. 14:13; Pareus on Gen. 14:15; Junius, Oration on the Hebrew Tongue; Cornelius a Lapide on Genesis 14; Buxtorf, Dissertation on the Preservation of the Hebrew Tongue; Sigonius, On the Hebrew Republic; Schindler in his Lexicon, root avar; Bochart, book 1, chapter 10; and innumerable others.

XI. Let us return to the renowned Grotius, treating of the primeval language. [ p. 176 ] He concedes that the Hebrew language was the language of Abraham; but what that language was, since according to him it was not that ancient one coeval with the world, he does not show. "Chaldean," says Cappellus, Sacred Chronology, Notes to the Second Table. "That language of Abraham and his posterity," says Grotius, "by their sojourn in Canaan approached nearer and nearer to the Canaanite language." Cappellus asserts that he learned from the Canaanites the Canaanite language, which was that primeval tongue now called Hebrew. We concede that the language of the Canaanites (whence the Punic) came near to the Hebrew language. This has been shown most plainly, after others, by the renowned Bochart, in a work altogether admirable. The argument is from the proper names of cities and men. But that those who without doubt were among the chief builders of the tower of Babel retained the holy primeval tongue pure, while those who religiously abstained from that crime, among whom, if any others, were the progenitors of Abraham, forgot it, surpasses all belief. Joseph Scaliger went before Cappellus in that opinion, in his Letter to Thomson, and in his Animadversions on Eusebius, at number 804. But we are about to show that the Hebrew language was the primeval one. The loss of that language was penal. Whether anyone has such power over his own mind as to bring himself seriously to believe that those were punished with the ignorance or corruption of that language who had never consented to the crime which merited that punishment, I for my part greatly doubt. That some of them, with the progress of time, deviated somewhat from the purity of the dialect, is probable. Elias [Levita], in the preface to the Methurgeman, affirms that a deflection of that sort was the origin of the Chaldean language. But that the accursed Canaanites, devoted to eternal destruction, retained the primeval language pure; that Abraham, moreover, learned their language, whatever in the end it was, and communicated it alone to his posterity, who were most strictly forbidden to have anything in common with those Canaanites, whom, namely, they were to exterminate from the face of the earth: these things seem paradoxes [Greek: paradoxa].

XII. Besides, it is most certain that the Philistines, contrary to what seemed good to the learned man, did not use the Hebrew language. For those who were born of Jewish fathers but of Ashdodite mothers "spake half in the speech of Ashdod, and could not speak in the Jews' language." With whom Nehemiah was most gravely angry, chapter 13:24, 25. And Jerome, on Isaiah, book 7, chapter 19, affirms that "the Canaanite language is midway between the Egyptian and the Hebrew." "Wherefore it is truer," says Grotius, "that the primeval language exists nowhere pure, but that its remains are in all languages: and that the names of Adam, Eve, and the rest were expressed by Moses in the Hebrew speech, for the sake of the Hebrews, with the same signification which they had had in the primeval language. So Curtius says that certain soldiers were called by the Persians Immortals; that the entrance of Cilicia is called by the inhabitants the Gates [Pylae]; that those who commanded each thousand of soldiers were called by the Persians chiliarchs." Thus he dismisses both of the contending parties, the Hebrew, namely, and the Syrian, adjudging the palm of antiquity to neither. And in this opinion he stands almost alone. Goropius [ p. 177 ] Becanus, indeed, endeavored to claim the honor of primitive antiquity for his own vernacular tongue [Dutch], but in vain, for his case is settled along with all the rest. But that God should have willed to consign his oracles in a language which was mixed out of others, and those corrupted, seems hardly credible. But to these things the most learned Mercerus replied long ago, nor do we need other words to convict the vanity of these exceptions; thus, then, he: "I hold it as certain, settled, and undoubted, that that first-born language was the Hebrew, although some, less fair to it, say that the proper names were changed by Moses and translated into his own language, which he favored; as if historians everywhere were not accustomed to retain proper names, even barbarous ones, unimpaired, although they sometimes change them where, with the lapse of time, the names of regions or places have been changed. But they retain the names of men almost unvaried, only accommodated to the inflection and idiom of their own language, somewhat twisted and corrupted; yet so that the origin is easily recognized. Add to this: the etymology of all the proper names before the flood, and after, down to the division of tongues, cannot be ascribed to any other language than the Hebrew; nor indeed the names of others after that time which Moses uses; as Adam from adamah [ground], and Chavvah [Eve], because she was 'the mother of all living.' Cain, Abel, Seth, Mahalaleel, Kenan, Noah, etc., the fountain of all of which, and of the rest, can only be Hebrew." Thus he, learnedly and piously.

XIII. But whether it is probable that all these proper names of men were translated into another language by Moses, when of very many of them no suitable reason for such a translation can be rendered, let the learned judge: Qayin [Cain] is so called from qanithi ["I have gotten"]; Sheth [Seth] is "he set," because God "set another seed," etc. What also will those of the contrary part [Greek: hoi ex enantias] say of the allusions drawn from the Hebrew language? such as that, "God enlarge [yaft] Japheth" [Hebrew: yaft Elohim leYefeth]. But some say these words were also in use in the Chaldean or Syrian language; yet their pretension is plainly met by those words of Adam, "She shall be called woman [ishshah], because she was taken out of man [ish]"; ishshah from ish; whereas among the Chaldeans itta is "woman" and gevar "man," in which words there is no such community as the first-formed man [Greek: ho protoplastos] intimates.

XIV. But this whole argument was not long ago so handled in a special dissertation on the origin and antiquity of the Hebrew language by the most learned Buxtorf the younger, that no room seems left for anyone to doubt hereafter. The primeval language, therefore, the use of which God implanted in man at the very creation, is the Hebrew. After it had been common to the whole earth for 1,757 years, at the division of tongues it remained among those descendants of Shem who were not present at the construction of the insane tower; so Genebrard, Chronicle, book 1: "The Hebrew, which before was common, remained uncorrupted with Eber (from whom both the language and the people have their name), because he had taken part neither in the counsels nor in the work of those who were pleased to raise a tower into heaven." But as many of them gradually fell away from the true worship of God, they seem also to have insensibly deviated from the pure dialect of the language; whence arose the Chaldean dialect, as the renowned Selden holds, Prolegomena 2 of On the Syrian Gods. And so Elias Levita teaches us in [ p. 178 ] his preface to the Sepher Methurgeman: "The Aramaic language is the holy tongue corrupted." Elsewhere he proves from R. Aben Ezra that it is nearer to the holy tongue than all other languages; and he adds besides: "It is evident that in the time of the patriarchs that language was already corrupted. The proof of which is yegar sahadutha [the Aramaic name Laban gave to the heap of witness, Gen. 31:47]; therefore it seems to me it must be said that it was corrupted as soon as Abraham went out of Chaldea. For without doubt he and his fathers spoke the holy tongue, as they received it from Adam down to Noah, one from the mouth of another [Hebrew: ish mippi ish]."

XV. Where the church remained, or at least its express footprints, especially that family from which it was destined to be renewed, there remained also the purity of the ancient language. That was the family of Eber, from whom this Hebrew language was named, which before had been marked by no name; nor did it need a title by which to be distinguished from others, since it was alone; as Augustine rightly says, On the City of God, book 16, chapter 11: "Because," he says, "this language remained in the family of Eber when the other nations were divided by other languages, the language which is not undeservedly believed to have been before common to the human race was thereafter called Hebrew; for then it was necessary for it to be distinguished from other languages by a proper name, as the others also were called by proper names; but when it was one, it was called nothing other than the human language, or human speech, which alone the whole human race spoke." From Eber also Abraham, who almost alone holily embraced the religion of his fathers, was called a Hebrew, as we have proved; and he carried that language into Canaan, the land promised to himself and to his seed. The remaining brothers and kinsmen of Abraham himself, and others of his contemporaries from the family of Eber, as they degenerated from the true religion, so also from the pure use of the holy tongue.

XVI. Now the sacred page affirms that this division of tongues happened either a little before the birth of Peleg, or about those times, Gen. 10:25; 1 Chron. 1:19. The Jews in the Seder Olam, or Order of the World, count 340 years from the flood, says Munster; for so he makes the computation in the margin of his translation. The text has: from the flood to the division, two hundred and seventy-two years; for they relate that that division happened when Abraham was forty-eight years old. But from the flood to the forty-eighth year of Abraham there are 340 years; whence Munster added fifty-two years to their number, that the computation might be consistent with itself. But this is false; and that division falls in the two hundred and forty-first year before the birth of Abraham, about the nativity of Peleg, as was shown above. But this dispute also is most learnedly decided by Buxtorf, in his Dissertation on the Confusion of Tongues; David Pareus also treats of it at length in his Commentary on chapter 12 [11] of Genesis; and most recently Ussher, in his Sacred Chronology, chapter 5, where he vindicates the credit and truth of the computation of the Hebrew text against the exceptions of Morinus.

XVII. Now when almost the whole human race had been driven into this state and condition, [ p. 179 ] and left to the counsel of its own mind, after, namely, a weariness of the true God and of his worship had seized them, it poured itself out by degrees into every idolatry. The earlier ages indeed could not utterly cast away all the knowledge of God which they had either from the instruction of their parents or from wandering tradition. Of them, therefore, we find that very many, as is the manner of men convicted by little sparks of divine light, feared God indeed, but served idols. But with the lapse of time, all revealed theology being step by step rejected, and the memory of the solemn promise and divine covenant utterly abolished, the whole globe of the earth universally polluted itself with abominable rites and the foulest idolatries. It is our pleasure, therefore, as we promised above, to investigate more diligently in this place their origin and progress.
Or, of the origin and progress of idolatry, that is, of self-willed worship [Greek: ethelothreskeia]. Chapter 1.

Chapter argument: The beginnings of idolatry most uncertain; diversity of opinions concerning them; the reasons for a new investigation; Adamic idolatry in paradise; the apostasy and superstition of Cain; the Enoshian reformation of the church; before the flood no idolatry properly so called; the "golden age" after the flood; in it no traces of idolatry; whence men were called meropes [Greek: meropes, "dividers of the voice"]; the time of the division of tongues and the dispersion of the human race; the builders of the tower of Babel, what kind of men they were; what purpose they had in building it; the beginnings of idolatry to be sought among them.

I. That no manifest traces of the origins and occasions of idolatry survive, we have both seen before, and the fruitless efforts of the investigators have placed it beyond controversy. That it had a beginning is evident from the event. The times, manners, causes, authors, and all the beginnings of its rise must be investigated by conjectures. Here, namely: "The best prophet is he who guesses well" (Euripides, uncertain fragment 128). "Count him the best seer who conjectures well": so Cicero renders that maxim [Greek: gnome] from Euripides. Some judge that it had taken possession of the world before the flood. Of that opinion are very many of the Jews, especially Maimonides, at the beginning of his work On the Worship of the Stars; whom Selden follows in the Prolegomena to his Syntagma On the Syrian Gods. Others opine that Satan first sprinkled this poison upon those who came out of the ark. The Targumists fasten this crime by name upon Ham. To others it seems that before the construction of the tower of Babel, which the contumacious and proud sons of Noah undertook for an everlasting memorial, no certain traces or express monuments of arbitrary and profane worship can be found. Here, then, we must fix our foot for a little while, and search out the sources of this Nile, most fertile of monsters, whence, namely, "the monstrous shapes of gods of every kind, and the barker Anubis" [ p. 180 ] came forth (Virgil, Aeneid 8.698). It is a field most rich in the surveying of the monuments of ancient time; and although great minds have cultivated it, yet, as is wont to happen in investigating the most obscure beginnings of things, of which "scarcely a thin breath of report glides down to us" (Virgil, Aeneid 7.646), there remains matter in which others may still exercise themselves much and long. The reader will easily perceive, from the things we are about to set down in this place, that after the most abundant vintages of others there were by no means lacking reasons for bringing forth these gleanings of ours. For if we cannot bring new light to the things themselves, that is, to the beginnings of strange worship, overspread with more than enough darkness, yet we are led into some hope that we shall set forth, lending some little light to a most obscure truth, at least what relation they bore to the worship of God, and by what juggleries that most ancient impostor deceived the human race and led it into fraud, and many other things silently passed over by the learned men who have cultivated this field with very great erudition, things not unwelcome to the reader. But it is my intention only to touch the heads of the matters, that I may satisfy the work proposed and be free to keep to the way I have set out upon.

II. We showed before that God implanted in man, created for his glory, an inborn [Greek: emphytos] law of obedience, embracing all those things which necessarily followed from his nature, condition, and ultimate end. Yet he never permitted that law to be the plenary rule and revelation of all divine worship. To it, in the state of creation, before the entrance of sin, he added the institution concerning not eating the fruit of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil. In that institution, with respect to the external act, the rational creature made the beginning of its defection from God. But it laid the foundation of the defect in moral worship. For by attaching faith to the serpent against the threatening of God, it held him, so far, in the place of God. And this was a certain species of idolatry, indeed the worst, which even now flourishes among all those who, setting aside the authority of the divine promises and threatenings, show themselves obedient to the word of Satan. But this is not, properly, that idolatry whose beginnings we are investigating.

III. After the entrance of sin, God restored natural theology, amplified by new revelations and by the institution of sacrifices, and consecrated to new ends. In instituted worship, unbelief leading the way, sin was straightway committed again. We expounded above the nature and the outcome of that sin. We showed also that Cain, cursed by God, fell away entirely from his solemn worship. It is indeed probable that he, and the citizens of the city of Enoch over whom he presided, Gen. 4, repudiated that worship, namely of sacrifices, from which he had drawn the first stain of evil, and, worshipping after his own will [Greek: ethelothreskon], took up new rites at his own pleasure. But that he procured for himself "new gods" [Hebrew: elohim chadashim], as the Scripture speaks, Judg. 5:8, there are innumerable things which hinder us from believing. For it was not possible for Cain so far to put off the man as to feign other gods for himself after [ p. 181 ] that express and open communication which had passed between him and Jehovah, the creator of all things.

IV. With the progress of time, some even of the Sethites having lapsed into the manners of the Cainites, the godly men of the days of Enosh, with a reformation of divine worship, that it might be the better and the longer preserved pure, instituted separate assemblies for the due calling upon the name of God, and thence were called "the sons of God." Gen. 4, last verse: "Then began men to call upon the name of the LORD." That some falsely suppose the rise of strange worship to be noted in those words, we have also shown above. For the Holy Spirit intimates not a profanation of the divine name, but a reformation of the church.

V. God himself complains that before the flood all flesh had corrupted its way. Nor is there any doubt that the apostates most openly declared war on God and religion. But that they were bound by this crime, namely idolatry, no testimony exists, no foundation for making a probable conjecture. And since we have demonstrated this clearly by arguments above, I do not wish to do here what has been done already. We must therefore pass over the antediluvian ages in this inquiry. For the opinion of Justin Martyr does not please me, who interprets the sons of God, who, seized with love of the daughters of men, joined them to themselves, as angels; and holds that from them were begotten demons, who straightway gave their efforts to the promoting of idolatry: Apology to the Roman Senate.

VI. The ravings of the Targumists and other Jews about the idolatry of Ham and his magical incantations, and the nature of his crime, do not belong to this place. That age which lies between the flood and the far-famed [Greek: diaboetos] Babylonian dispersion [Greek: diaspora] of the nations has left very few traces of itself, or none at all, in the monuments of things that survive. For that space of time, whatever it was, Noah, the great father of the family, held the whole human race sprung from himself under a pious and paternal rule. That was the golden age, about which the ancient fable-makers have very many things.

So Virgil: "Before Jove no tillers subdued the fields: it was not lawful even to mark or divide the plain with a boundary: men sought all things for the common stock; and the earth herself bore everything more freely, with none demanding" (Georgics 1.125-128). And Martial: "Great king of the ancient heaven and of the earlier world, under whom there was lazy quiet, nor any toil, nor the too royal thunderbolt, nor men deserving of the thunderbolt; the earth not cut open down to the shades below, but rich in itself" (Epigrams, book 12 [12.62]). Both refer to Saturn, following Hesiod; for thus he, Works and Days 111: "They lived in the time of Cronos, when he reigned in heaven; and like gods they lived, having hearts free from care, apart from and without toils and misery." ["When Saturn, master of all, held the empire of the heavens, men lived like the gods," etc.] [ p. 182 ] And Tibullus, book 1, elegy 3: "How well men lived of old, when Saturn was king, before the earth was laid open into long roads. No house had doors; no stone was fixed in the fields to govern the lands with fixed boundaries. The very oaks gave honey," etc. Lucian has similar things in his pieces addressed to Cronos [Saturnalia], chapter 7. He brings in Saturn speaking: "and for this reason everywhere there is applause, and song, and play, and equality of honor for all, both slaves and free; for in my time no one was a slave"; all of which reflect the paternal rule of Noah and the undivided earth. That from this flowed the license of slaves at the Saturnalia among the Romans, Plutarch relates in the Life of Numa.

VII. For Noah was Saturn. He was that greatest "herald of righteousness" [Greek: dikaiosynes keryx] of the antediluvians, 2 Pet. 2:5, the contempt of whose admonitions was followed by the horrid universal destruction [Greek: panolethria] of the human race. So long, therefore, as his sons and grandsons either stood in awe of his authority, or showed themselves obedient to his words and discourses, it is not fair that we should judge them to have admitted this crime among themselves.

VIII. Most believe that the confusion of tongues happened in the hundred and first or hundred and second year after the flood. Then men became meropes, and were so called: "the generations of speech-divided men" [Greek: geneai meropon anthropon], in Homer, Iliad 1.250; "the ages of men of divided speech." Meropon, that is, says Didymus, "of those having a divided voice, that is, having an utterance divided into syllables and articulate"; that is, because men have a tongue divided and fitted for pronouncing syllables, or use articulate speech, whereas the voices of the other animals are inarticulate [Greek: anarthroi]. Hesychius agrees with the scholiast: "Men are meropes because they have a divided utterance, that is, voice." And Eustathius recites the same opinion in the first place; to which he adds, by way of correction: "But those of our divine court [the Christians] say that men were surnamed meropes from the division, or partition, of the voice which they suffered after the tower of Chalane [Babel], being divided into all parts of the earth"; that is, "it was the common opinion of the ancient Christians that men were called meropes from that division of tongues which they suffered at the building of the tower of Babel." John Malelas of Antioch has similar things: "The tongues were divided; wherefore men have also been called meropes, because their speech was divided into many tongues and voices" (Chronicle, Second Discourse of the Theogony). Now as men were called meropes on account of the division of tongues, so the place in which that punishment was inflicted on the rebels was called Babel [Hebrew: Bavel]; as if one said bo tevel, "confusion came"; or Babel is said for balbel, that is, "confusion," the middle lamed being cast away to avoid a cacophony. In the hundred and first year after the flood Peleg was born. That name was given him "because in his days the earth was divided" [Hebrew: ki veyamav niflegah ha'aretz], Gen. 10:25. And the verb palag is used elsewhere in that sense, Ps. 55:9: "Divide their tongue" [Hebrew: pallag leshonam]. Most of the Jews affirm that this name was imposed on that son of Eber by a prophetic spirit. [ p. 183 ] And of the same opinion are Cornelius a Lapide and Augustinus Torniellus: the former in his Commentaries on Genesis, the latter in his Annals at the year of the world 1931. So in the imposition of the name of Noah his pious parents seem to have had respect to things which were not to be fulfilled until after six hundred years. The Jews therefore hold that the tower of Babel was built in the two hundredth year of the life of Peleg, led by arguments not altogether inept. But whatever pertains to the understanding or right settling of that chronological dispute, the most renowned Buxtorf the younger has set forth in a special dissertation on the origin of the plurality of languages. Seth Calvisius in his Chronology; Pererius, Pareus, Rivet in their Commentaries and Disputations on Genesis; Torniellus in his Annals of the World; Masius in his Commentary on Joshua; Ussher in his Sacred Chronology; and very many others, both theologians and chronologers, have woven the same web.

IX. Those who assert that the division of tongues happened at the birth of Peleg, and that the name was imposed on him from the event, seem, in the opinion of some, to fix an interval shorter than is fair between it and the flood, within which men could have grown to that multitude which applied itself to the building of the tower: for the birth of Peleg falls, as we have said, in the hundred and first year from the flood. But those who think the name was given to Peleg by a prophetic spirit, and who put off the dispersion of the human race to the end of his life, and therefore to the year of the world 1996, are obliged to place the arrival of Abraham in the land of Canaan in the 88th year after that dispersion: which space seems shorter than is fair for the erecting of those kingdoms which sacred history relates to have been founded in those times. Buxtorf therefore denies that Peleg was the firstborn of Eber, or born in the 74th year of his father, but throws back his birth, and therefore the division of tongues, to another time, long after. But John Temporarius shows, in the second of his Chronological Demonstrations, that from the stock of Noah, in the space of 102 years, 1,554,420 males and females could have been born. Half of that number Ussher admits, Sacred Chronology, chapter 5; where he most learnedly beats back the madness and signal audacity of John Morinus in rejecting the Hebrew chronology. Let us grant, then, that the construction of the tower of Babel and the division of tongues happened in the year of the world 1757, in the 101st or 102nd from the flood. That down to that time any traces of idolatry, any monuments, any testimonies survive, or ever existed, nothing remains to testify; at least no traces of it survive.

X. The Jerusalem Targum teaches that the builders of the tower of Babel had it in mind to set an image on the summit of that impious mass, to be honored with religious worship: "They said," it runs, "Come, let us build us a city and a tower, whose head may reach to the top of the heavens, and let us make us an object of adoration on its summit, and let us put a sword in its hand." In almost the same words Jonathan Ben Uzziel sets down the same fable. It is the common opinion of learned men that that tower endured for a long time after the confusion [ p. 184 ] of tongues. Nor indeed does it seem other than that which Herodotus describes at length under the name of the temple of Belus, book 1, chapter 181. It consisted of eight towers, one set upon another (of which the first at least, if not every one, was a stadium in height). Of the topmost he speaks thus: "On the last tower stands a great shrine; and in the shrine lies a great couch," etc.; "in the last tower is a great chapel, in which is a couch splendidly spread," etc. Not long after the confusion of tongues, therefore, that insane edifice was turned into the temple of an idol, that is, of the sun. But the Jews fetch the origin of their opinion from another quarter; they think those words in Moses, with which the builders exhorted one another to set about the work, intimate that purpose: vena'aseh-lanu shem, "And let us make us a name"; that is, they say, "an idol of the great name." For they hold that shem [name] is thus said absolutely of some god, true or false. Ha-Shem ["the Name"], indeed, in some places emphatically signifies "God," Lev. 24:11; shem absolutely, nowhere, nor does it mean an idol. Assuredly, if this were true, no one could anywhere find a more illustrious record of the origin of the idolatry of the nations. Nor would this crime have to be reckoned to have settled upon the globe of the earth gradually and by various degrees, with various occasions seized as helps; a deliberate defection of the human race from God would have given it birth. But this is a rabbinical delirium; and the words which the builders used look plainly elsewhere. Their purpose was that, being mortals, exposed to various chances, especially to dispersion into remote regions of the earth, they might become eternal in fame and glory. That purpose of mind they signified by those words, "Let us make us a name"; that is, let us leave to posterity an hereditary title of praise and glory. Nor is it credible that almost all the families of the whole world conspired, as it were in the moment of a whirlwind, against the living God, and resolved at one stroke, all reverence and worship of the Creator being cast away, to adore idols.

XI. No reason, therefore, persuades us that they built the tower with the mind and purpose of worshipping new gods; Scripture relates that they had a far different design. It is indeed probable that those builders were a race of men defiled with many crimes, who reckoned the power they had acquired slight and poor unless it included wrong and oppression. We showed before that they rejected the admonitions and the rule of Noah. Hence they were utterly abandoned to the commands of Satan, and stood ready to execute all the things which the innate vanity of the mind might suggest.

XII. The speech of all being confounded and divided, the intercourse of new tongues threw them into various herds. Reduced to that state, God dispersed them, by tribes and families, over the surface of the whole earth. There were indeed among the ancient Christians some to whom it was a heresy to deny that Noah distributed the globe of the earth among his sons and grandsons, and assigned to each family its seats. For after the designation of heresies had begun to be arbitrary, there was almost nothing said or written by anyone, however true, which was not by some referred to their class. [ p. 185 ] But Scripture shows that it was by divine disposition, not by a Noachic assignment, that the sons of men occupied their new seats, or were carried into the diverse coasts of the globe, Gen. 11:8. That any of the builders of the tower retained the primeval language without some signal change cannot be proved by testimonies or arguments. We confess that words from it remained in some use among almost all nations; but they were very few, as Boxhornius rightly affirms, Origins of the Gauls, chapter 7. It is not improbable that, together with the loss of the primeval tongue, forgetfulness of many instructions concerning divine things seized them. Among those men, therefore, namely the builders of the tower of Babel, left to themselves, destitute of new revelations, forgetful of former things, dispersed hither and thither and wandering, the beginnings of idolatry are to be sought.
Or, on the origin and progress of idolatry, part II.

Chapter argument: Colossians 3:11; Epiphanius' distinction of the various religions; the two kinds of idolatry, Sabaism and Hellenism; the peculiar superstition of the Druids; the beginning of idolatry in the worship of the heavenly bodies, Job 31:26-28; the exposition of the ancients noted; the time in which Job lived; the beginning of the error in the seduction of the heart, Deut. 11:16; the manner of the most ancient worship; what adoration is; religious salutation; the ecstasy [ekstasis] of Socrates; Catulus; Pompey; the kiss; religious adoration, Ps. 2:12; the version of the Seventy noted; that of Jerome, Hos. 13:2; objections [enstaseis] concerning adoration by kisses, from Pliny, Apuleius, Lucian, Minucius Felix; the reason for the bending of the body in adoration; the first sacrifices to idols were without blood; the opinion of Porphyry; the reconciliation of the opinions concerning the origin of sacrifices, Gen. 4:4, 8:20; the offering of fruits added to adoration, Hos. 2:8; the origin of other ceremonies; the nature and demerit of idolatry; the Egyptians the first worshippers of the stars; the Chaldeans join them, and the Persians; whether the Sun was the only God of the Persians; the opinion of Herodotus; the number of gods continually increased with the passage of time; the writers of the history of the magi; the custom of the Greeks and Romans in reckoning up the gods of other nations; the gods of the Germans and Gauls; Tacitus noted; the Sun the most ancient god of the Carthaginians, and of the Greeks also, as Plato testifies; the reason for the orientation of sacred places; the first beginnings of idolatry; the follies and blasphemies of Cardano; Nimrod among the builders of the tower of Babel; the trifles of the Jews; the catholicity of the worship of the Sun, and its many names [polyonymia].

I. The apostle Paul, in the third chapter of the Epistle to the Colossians, teaches that in Jesus Christ there is neither Greek, nor Jew, neither Barbarian, nor Scythian. From this Epiphanius, in the first book of his work against the Heresies, draws out various species of religions, namely Barbarism, Scythism, Hellenism, and Judaism. Barbarism, he writes, prevailed from Adam down to Noah; Scythism from Noah to the age of Serug, in which Hellenism was introduced; the beginning of Judaism he derives from the circumcision of Abraham. Since, however, he holds that idolatry entered the world after the deluge, it is plain that by Barbarism he understands that savagery of manners and violence [ p. 186 ] which ruined the antediluvian world. The distinction between Scythism and Hellenism he places in this, that the making of statues [agalmatopoiia] was introduced by the latter, which the former lacked. Whether he has distinguished these things rightly or otherwise, let others judge; Petavius, in his annotations on the passage, denies it. For our part, we hold that there were two kinds of all that idolatrous worship which occupied almost the whole world, of which one may be called Sabaism, the other Hellenism. But since there were many peculiar features in the superstition of the Druids, we shall recount that separately in a few words. Sabaism consisted in the worship and religion of the heaven, the Sun, the Moon, and the stars; Hellenism added to it the veneration of dead men and of demons. The adoration of statues, images, and stars was common to both. But the idolatrous multitude worshipped the heavens before it worshipped its fictitious heaven-dwellers. In Sabaism, therefore, that is, in the worship of heavenly things, idolatry had its beginning. This we shall first establish. Then we shall show in a few words what handles for depraved superstition the human race seized upon, and by what vain reasonings Satan made use of to entice men into deceit and error; and, so far as we can attain by conjecture, we shall mark out the time of the origin of this most pernicious error.

II. That idolatry made its beginning in the world in the worship of the heaven and the sun, all the monuments of past ages prove. For there exist, beyond all the credit of profane history and the most ancient memory of things among the nations, illustrious testimonies of this crime in the Old Testament. To this purpose that most ancient writer, whether he was Moses, which is most probable, or some other divine man nearly of his age, through whom the Holy Spirit entrusted the history of Job to the faith of the church, reports the apologetic words of that most holy man, chapter 31:26-28, "If I beheld the sun when it shone, and the moon walking in brightness; and my heart has been secretly enticed, and my mouth has kissed my hand: this also would have been judged an iniquity, for I should have denied the God who is above." With their leave be it said, the comments which Chrysostom, Nilus, Olympiodorus, and others of the ancients make on this passage are utterly inept. They deny that idolatry is here in question, and hold that Job, when he denies that his mouth kissed his hand, meant to signify that he had never thought so arrogantly of his own virtues and works, which he denotes by the sun, nor of the fame of his name, which he assigns to the moon, that his hand was kissed by his mouth, as though whatever resource or ability he possessed he had accomplished of himself. Inept, I say, are these things, and repugnant both to the scope of the passage and to the very words. The holy man treats most openly of the worship of heavenly bodies. And while he carefully labors to clear himself of this crime, he makes it plain that very many others of that time were engaged in it. Job lived about 350 years after the dispersion of Babel, in the year of the world 2100, or thereabouts. That in that age this superstition had made progress and spread itself far abroad, the words show. But the beginning of the error Job ascribes to the seduction of the heart. [ p. 187 ] Elsewhere also the Holy Spirit uncovers this fountain of idolatry, Deut. 11:16, "Take heed lest your heart deceive you, and turn you aside to strange gods." All other things were naturally fitted to point men to the true God; in the human heart alone lay all the danger. Deceived, therefore, by the appearance of empty reasonings (which would have had no force, had they not first been wholly turned away from the love of God and of spiritual things), the most ancient idolaters worshipped the sun and moon religiously, at first in secret, soon openly. Those shadows of reasons we shall set forth in the next chapter. The mode of worship which Job mentions is simple adoration. Now adoration is a religious salutation, performed by bringing the hand to the mouth. In this the error of the heart first expressed itself: the other ceremonies of the religion were introduced by degrees. Thus Plato relates that Socrates saluted the sun, and several times suffered an ecstasy [ekstasis] in that worship. Though superstition increased beyond all measure, yet that simple adoration endured through all the ages of ancient ignorance. Catulus, in Cicero, On the Nature of the Gods, book i. chapter 28: "I had taken my stand, as it chanced saluting the rising dawn, when suddenly on my left Roscius rises."

For they venerated the rising sun above all. Hence Pompey's disdainful jest against the waning dominion of Sulla: "More men adore the sun rising than setting" (Plutarch, Life of Pompey). And from this, temples were built facing the rising of the sun. Nor did that custom of bowing and adoring toward the east, ancient enough among superstitiously inclined Christians, proceed from any other source, although other reasons are pretended for that superstitious practice; for they are all plainly futile.

III. Further, Job denies that his hand, brought to his mouth, was kissed by it, or pressed with kisses. For the kiss was a pledge and sign of worship, even of religious worship. So the Psalmist, Ps. 2:12, "Kiss the Son" [Hebrew: nashshequ-bar]; that is, adore him with religious worship. The Seventy corrupt the sense of the passage, rendering the words "Lay hold of instruction" [Greek: draxasthe paideias], whom the Vulgate translator follows. Jerome acknowledges that religious worship is intended, and therefore translates the words, "Adore purely." To venerate idols also with kisses was formerly, and is even now, customary: Hos. 13:2, "Let them kiss the calves" [Hebrew: agalim yishshaqun], that is, adore them; as the same Jerome notes against Rufinus, book i. "Men were accustomed," says Cicero in the fourth oration against Verres, "not only to venerate the statues of Hercules, but even to kiss them." "In adoring, we bring the right hand to the kiss, and turn the whole body around," as Pliny says, book xxviii. chapter 2. The reason for this turning or wheeling about of the body Plutarch gives in his Life of Numa. He says it was an institution of Numa "that worshippers should turn themselves about as they adore" [Greek: to proskynein peristrephomenous]. He adds, "The turning about of the worshippers is said to be an imitation of the revolution of the universe," that is, of the circuit of the heavens which the sun performs. But that bending of the body seems to have been introduced after temples facing the east were founded. For since in these those who adored the idols were obliged to bow themselves toward the west, [ p. 188 ] they turned the body around, in order to show that they directed their devotion chiefly to the sun. Lucian, in his work On Dancing, affirms that the Indians added dances to the kisses in their adoration. Apuleius also, in the fourth book of The Golden Ass, speaking of the beautiful maiden whom men admired as a goddess, says, "Many of the citizens, moving their right hand to their lips, with the forefinger resting upon the upright thumb, venerated her with religious adorations as the very goddess Venus herself." After statues had been set up, men indeed kissed the statues themselves; but in the worship of heavenly bodies, namely of the sun and moon, since these were absent, they were accustomed only to move the hand to the mouth; and so likewise with other statues, when in passing by it was not permitted to give leisure to religion. Thus Minucius Felix says of Caecilius that, upon noticing the statue of Serapis, he "moved his hand to his mouth, and pressed a kiss with his lips."

IV. Not yet (that is, in the age of Job) had sacrifice been offered to the sun, to horses, bats, mice, and the other creeping things (of which Maimonides treats in the third part of the Guide for the Perplexed, chapter 29), or of the fruits of the earth, much less with human blood, which was done afterwards. Simple adoration, that is, the worship of the heart, expressing itself only in the actions and bowings of the body, was succeeded by oblations of the fruits of the earth. So far we agree with the opinion of Porphyry in the second book of his treatise On Abstinence from Animal Food. The first sacrifices offered to idols consisted not of animals and blood, but of the fruits of the earth. It is most certain that the first sacrifice acceptable to God was a bloody one, Gen. 4:4; and so also after the deluge, Gen. 8:20. Nor is there any doubt that as long as men pursued the true God alone with religious worship, they venerated him with bloody sacrifices. When they turned to idols, they did not dare at once, nor until long afterward, to offer them sacrifices of animals. Their first sacrifices after adoration were of the fruits of the earth. Thus the prophet complains that the Israelites offered grain, wine, and oil to Baal, that is, to the sun, Hos. 2:8. And in the poet:
"They, turned with their eyes toward the rising sun, offer the salted grain with their hands" (Virgil, Aeneid 12.172-173).

The Pythagoreans, abstaining from the blood of animals, added hymns to adoration. These hymns are also a very well-known part of religious worship, whether true or superstitious. Of this kind were those of Homer and Callimachus, which are extant. Afterwards very many other ceremonies were admitted among the nations, which proceeded partly from the most obscure report of the Mosaic lawgiving [nomothesia], partly from the perverse imitation [kakozelia] of Satan.

V. Next, that most holy man adds the nature and the demerit of this crime. He teaches that its nature lies in the denial of the almighty God, who is above, ruling all things, disposing all things, and who created the sun itself. "I should have denied," he says, "God who is from above [Hebrew: mimma'al]." Quite contrary, perhaps, to what has seemed true to many entangled in that error. Very few mortals consider that all religious worship of creatures is a denial of God the Creator. And he significantly adds "from above" [mimma'al]. For since the chief reason for the worship of the Sun always lay in this, that [ p. 189 ] men saw it borne above all things, he indicates that God alone is exalted above all things. Rightly to this purpose Maimonides, On Idolatry, book ii.: "He who turns aside to idolatry is as one who apostatizes from the whole law." And a little before: "Behold, here you learn that he who takes up idolatry denies the whole law, and all the prophets, and whatever has been commanded to the prophets, from the first man to the end of the world."

VI. He adds that such worship of the sun and moon was judged an iniquity; that is, either most worthy of the severe judgment and punishment of God, or deservedly to be abominated by all the godly. For "sun" the text has or [Hebrew: 'or], that is, "light" by way of antonomasia. Hence the Sun was called Horus.

VII. With this testimony agree several passages of Deuteronomy. Now this worship of the heavenly bodies, especially of the sun, which without doubt invaded the world immediately after the dispersion of Babel, obtained a catholic, universal sway before the idols which Hellenism worshipped were either known or born.

VIII. The first who openly gave themselves to this depraved superstition were the Egyptians. This we shall presently show, from Diodorus, Lactantius, and others. They were followed by the Babylonians and all the Chaldeans. Bel was no other than the Sun. God was therefore angry with Hezekiah because, when the king of Babylon had sent ambassadors to inquire about the miracle of the sun's going back, 2 Chron. 32:31, he did not seize the occasion of declaring the true God, and the sun a creature obedient to his word.

IX. Concerning the ancient Persians [the Massagetae], Herodotus testifies the same in his Clio, chapter 216: "Of the gods," he says, "they worship the sun alone, and to him they sacrifice horses." He also was Mithra, the greatest, if not the only, God among the ancient Persians: "The Persians consider Mithra to be the sun," Gregory Nazianzen, Oration i. against Julian. Similarly Strabo: "They consider the sun alone to be God, and to him they sacrificed horses."

X. All seem to affirm that the one and only God of the ancient Persians was the sun. The distinguished Vossius denies this, in book ii. of his work On Idolatry, and against Herodotus, bringing forward testimonies from Plutarch and Hesychius, teaches that they admitted other gods as well. But it is by no means established that, after the custom of idolaters, they did not before the times of Plutarch enroll among the number of the gods others whom in the days of Herodotus they did not yet worship. For after the human race had presented itself with the right of apotheosis and of manufacturing divinities, the number of gods grew daily. Varro reckoned up thirty thousand. But concerning the earlier ages the Satirist speaks well, Juvenal, Satire xiii. 46:
"Nor was the throng of gods such as it is today; and the stars, content with few divinities, pressed upon wretched Atlas with a lighter weight."

But neither does Herodotus say absolutely that the sun was held the only divinity [ p. 190 ] among the Persians, but rather that of all those gods which Greece venerated, the sun alone was in divine honor among the Persians. For he himself elsewhere mentions other heavenly bodies and elements, which stand in the same relation as the sun, among the objects of Persian worship [sebasmata] (Clio, chapter 131). "They sacrifice," he says, "to the sun and the moon, and to earth, and fire, and water, and the winds." For the worship of these almost everywhere accompanied the worship of the sun. "To these alone," he adds, "they sacrifice from the beginning." In these idolatry had its origin. "But they have learned besides to sacrifice to the Heavenly One [Ourania]," that is, they learned from others to worship the host of heaven with sacrifices.

XI. Hence, in Diogenes Laertius, in the proem of his work, the writers of the history of the Magi accuse Herodotus, as forgetful of himself, of falsehood, because he wrote that Xerxes hurled javelins at the sun, since the sun was held for God by the Magi who accompanied Xerxes on his expedition, the chief priests of the Persian superstition.

XII. Herodotus indeed adds, "They have the custom, having ascended the loftiest mountains, of immolating victims to Jupiter, calling the whole circuit of the heaven Jupiter." But by what name they called the circuit of the heaven in their own tongue is wholly unknown. For it was a most common practice with the Greeks and Romans to designate the gods of other nations by the names of those whose worship had prevailed among themselves. Yet so far Herodotus is correct, that, although he pretends the Persians worshipped Jupiter, he shows that he did not intend that Greek Hellenism which he introduced. Others speak otherwise. Thus they write that the Gauls and Germans worshipped Jupiter and Mercury. But all the memory that survives of the first beginnings of those nations teaches that none of the Celts had so much as heard those names spoken before the coming of the Greeks and Romans into Gaul. Since, however, the Greeks and Romans were utter infants in the knowledge of the origins of peoples, nations, and religions, they did not believe that the mortal race had anywhere been wiser than their own fictions.

XIII. More correctly than most, and nearer to the truth, Caesar writes of the Germans, Gallic War, book vi. chapter 21: "They count among the number of gods those only whom they see, ... the Sun, Vulcan, and the Moon; the rest they have not received even by report." Tacitus says otherwise, who is the authority that they worshipped Hercules, Mars, Mercury, and Isis. "Tacitus, therefore, knew more and surer things about the religion of the Germans than Caesar," says Lipsius. "And whether Caesar inquired into these matters in Gaul or in Germany, either the account given him was not correct, or he himself did not investigate with sufficient diligence," as Montanus comments on that passage of Caesar. But in truth Tacitus either was himself deceived, or deliberately deceives others. He imposes names familiar to the Romans, at his pleasure, upon the gods of the Germans. For Woden was not Mercury, nor Taranis Jupiter, nor Haesus Mars. Those Roman names were as unknown to the Germans as the fables which in Hellenism were the foundation of their worship. But Eostre, or Aestar, was perhaps the same goddess as Astarte of the Syrians. Of her Bede writes: "Eostermoneth, which is now Pascha [Easter month]; this month is interpreted [ p. 191 ] to have had its name formerly from a goddess of theirs who was called Eostre, and to whom they celebrate feasts in it." And indeed the word Eostar comes near to the Syrian name [Hebrew: Eshterah], which is proper to the Syrian goddess; for the fact that it is also said in the plural number, Ashtaroth, denotes a multitude of images, or her worship established in many places. The Christians also, instituting a feast according to the observation of the moon's course, were thought by the gentiles to be worshipping the moon, or Astarte, that is, Eostre; whence that name is retained even to this day.

XIV. Caesar affirms that the Gauls worshipped Mercury, Mars, Jupiter, and Minerva, assigning to them the same attributes as the Greeks and Romans. But they drew that superstition from the Greeks of Massilia; for it does not belong to the institutions of the Druids. Also, in the treaty struck between the Carthaginians and Philip king of Macedon and others against the Romans, recorded in Polybius, Histories, book xvii., the Carthaginians call the Sun and Moon the gods of their armies; whereby they show that among their other false divinities these were the most ancient gods, since nearly all nations have placed their first beginnings in arms and wars.

XV. That the Greeks themselves also originally worshipped no other gods than the sun, moon, and stars, Plato is witness in the Cratylus. "It seems to me," he says (in the person of Socrates), "that the first men who inhabited Greece accounted those only to be gods whom many of the barbarians now worship: the sun, the moon, the earth, the stars, and the heaven; and since they saw all these always moving and running in their course, from this nature of running [thein] they named them gods [theoi]." Nor is that etymology of the name theos which he brings forward inept, as we shall see.

XVI. And hence it was that in ancient times all temples, as being erected to the sun, faced toward the east, yet had also sacred doors to the south. Thus Homer makes his sacred cave of Ithaca double-doored, but the southern door sacred to the gods (Odyssey 13.109-112): "But the doorway is twofold: this, facing the North wind, admits men; but that, looking toward the South, is more divine, wholly impassable to man, and affords a way to the immortals alone."

There was no other reason for this positioning of sacred places than that at midday the sun stands over the southern door, as Porphyry testifies. "They set up," he says, "a symbol also of midday and of the south, at the door, the god being at his meridian." The same custom is still everywhere retained in the arrangement of those places which they call sacred.

XVII. Let it suffice to have said these things concerning many nations here and there [sporaden]. Concerning [ p. 192 ] the orientals we shall hereafter show the same in particular. In this, I say, idolatry made its beginning in the world. Statues or images had not yet been invented; nor pillars or columns erected for religion's sake; nor had dead men yet been enrolled in the order of divinities: in the heaven and the sun the first error was committed. The primary authors of the crime, the precise time, and the places in which it had its rise, it has seemed good to God to bury in dark night.

XVIII. Plainly vain and trifling are the things which Girolamo Cardano discourses on the origin of the worship of idols, in his commentary on Ptolemy's Astrological Judgments, book ii. text 17. "Mercury," he says, "joined with Venus, made the law of idols weak and given over to luxury, in which all things are permitted; and manifold, because of the power of Mercury; and therefore, since Venus is weak in comparison with the superior planets, it became light, a law of many gods, full of superstitions, and divination, and fables, which are also full of adulteries and other shameful deeds, and the loves of boys; for Mercury signifies all such things." And again: "The law of idols, since it had its origin from Venus, took its beginning from the south, namely from Assyria, Babylonia, and the Chaldeans; for Belus, king of the Assyrians, first commanded that he be adored." This, I say, is to rave. But although these things are fatuous and insipid, and altogether worthy of one in whom "something flutters beneath the left breast" [a tag from Persius for a disordered mind], yet it were to be wished that the learned man had set a limit to his madness in these matters, and had not babbled forth those things which, in the same place, he impiously, foolishly, and blasphemously vomited out concerning the nativity of our Lord Jesus Christ and his law.

XIX. All the ancients judge that Nimrod, after he had spent his labor in vain on the building of the tower, not only sank into the worst of morals, but also lapsed into idolatry. The distinguished Bochart, book i. chapter 10, thinks that he was not present at that work, "since he was either not yet born, or still a boy." But the conjecture lacks foundation; for although I believe that Nimrod did not seize his tyranny until after the dispersion of the peoples, yet since he was the son of Cush, the eldest-born son of Ham, he could have been sixty years old at the time the tower was being built. "But he placed the beginning of his empire in that very place from which he would have been dispersed," says that most learned man, "if he had been one of the builders." But it is not necessary for us to say that all men universally were driven from that place; and if they were, it is most certain that they immediately occupied it again. Therefore the opinion of the ancients is not at all improbable: I have nothing to add. Among the dispersed builders of the tower, who had cast off all reverence for the true Deity, this crime grew, at first gradually and moderately, then widely and headlong, and invaded the world.

XX. Indeed, within the space of not many generations, it is evident that almost all nations took up the divine empire of the sun. So the Phoenicians, as Sanchuniathon testifies. "Him," he says, that is, the sun, "they believed to be God, the sole lord of heaven, calling him Belsamen, which among the Phoenicians means lord of heaven."

[ p. 193 ] XXI. His worship therefore obtained catholicity, and he himself a multitude of names [polyonymia]. He was the same as [Hebrew: ha-El Elyon, the Most High God; Baal; Bel; Adonai; Chemosh; Baal-zebub; Baal-peor; Molech; Chiun; Adrammelech], and Saturn, Jupiter, Hammon, Mithra, Horus, Dionysus, Mars, Apollo, Osiris, Pan, Bacchus, Liber, Jupiter Sabazius, Hercules, Janus, Belenus, Abellio, and what not. Hence Plutarch calls the Sun and the Moon "of ten thousand names" [myrionymous]. For by heaping up encomiums and piling together titles they thought the divinity was won over, and thereby honored with the greatest honor; so that at length those which had been only different names of the superstition came, as the error spread, to be held different divinities; as the most learned Rivet rightly observes on Hos. 2:8. In the multitude of names [polyonymia] there was also a certain species of worship. Whence Ovid, Fasti, book vi. 213: "I was asking whether I should assign the Nones to Sancus, or to Fidius, or to you, father Semo: when Sancus said to me, To whichever of them you give it, I shall have the gift; I bear the three names: so willed the people of Cures." And Horace, Secular Hymn, verse 14: "Gentle Ilithyia, protect the mothers, whether you approve being called Lucina, or Genitalis." And Satires, book ii. 6, verse 20: "Father Matutinus, or Janus, if you hear that name more gladly." Catullus also, Poem 34, verses 5, 13, 21: "O daughter of Latona, great offspring of greatest Jupiter; you are called Lucina Juno by women in the pains of childbirth; you, mighty Trivia, and Luna with your borrowed light. Be hallowed by whatever name pleases you." Likewise Callimachus, Hymn to Apollo: "O Apollo, many call you Boedromios, and many Clarios, and everywhere your name is manifold; but I call you Carneios."

From the diverse manner of the sun's worship, from the ceremonies with which he was worshipped, from the good things sought from him, from his benefits, from the places in which he was solemnly adored, acquiring new names and new titles, the sun in the process of time thrust forth new and diverse divinities. Hence, from the event, the Jews, perverting Scripture, assert that God granted the sun and moon to the nations, that they might be adored. This they gather from the words of Moses, Deut. 4:19, "Lest perhaps you lift up your eyes to the heavens, and see the sun, and the moon, and the stars, all the host of the heavens, and be driven to bow down to them and serve them, which the LORD your God has apportioned to all the peoples under the whole heaven" [Hebrew: asher chalaq YHWH Eloheykha otham lekhol ha-ammim tachath kol-hashshamayim]. A most powerful argument for restraining the minds of the peoples from the worship of heavenly bodies. That dominion which they imagine in the sun and moon is in fact servitude, destined for the use of all [ p. 194 ] the nations that are under heaven. But the Jews drag these words elsewhere, in their Jewish fashion. God, they say, calling Israel to his own worship, set forth the sun and moon to the other nations as an object of adoration. So Trypho in Justin's Dialogue. "Declare to us," he says, "another God, taking care not to speak of the sun and moon, which it is written that God has conceded to the nations to adore as gods." The Vulgate translator renders the sense of the passage excellently: "Which the Lord your God created for the service of all the nations"; compare Matt. 5:45.
Or, on the origin and progress of idolatry, part III.

Chapter argument: The occasions of the origin of idolatry; the reputed dominion of the sun and moon; in what sense the sun and moon are the two great luminaries; the portentous fiction of Jonathan the Targumist; dominion the ground of worship, viciously transferred to a delegate; the vain reasonings of idolaters, Rom. 1:21; the influence and operations of the sun and moon upon earthly things, Jer. 44:17, 18, Acts 14:17; God alone the author even of earthly goods; the adornment, course, glory, and allurements of the heavenly bodies; the God who is above; some shut God up in heaven; others assign omnipresence [pantotopia] to the human nature of Christ, Deut. 4:19; the opinion of Lactantius and of Diodorus Siculus concerning the occasions and first beginnings of idolatry; the opinion of the philosopher Plotinus concerning the worship of the stars, Ps. 19:4; the Seventy noted, and the Vulgate translator; the hallucination of Marsilio Ficino; the old Syriac translation; the sum of the vain reasonings [dialogismoi].

I. In the preceding chapter we set forth the first beginnings of idolatry in the worship of the sun and moon. Before we proceed to set forth the progress which, as is plain from the event, this error made, it is fitting first to explain in a few words certain handles and occasions of the worship of the heavenly bodies, which the first idolaters seem to have seized upon, and those vain reasonings by which they seduced themselves.

II. And first, it seems to me that there had settled in the minds of men a tradition concerning the dominion over day and night committed to the sun and moon. For it was necessary that they should believe the objects of their worship to be set in a position of eminence [en hyperoche]. The report had spread abroad of those things which are consecrated to eternal memory in the first chapter of Genesis: "And God made the two great lights: the greater light for the rule of the day, and the lesser light for the rule of the night" [Hebrew: vayya'as Elohim eth-shenei hamme'oroth haggedolim]. Dominion is here attributed to the sun and moon; that is, from the very origin of all things. But all lords must be treated with honor. It has been believed, moreover, that the sun and moon are called the two great luminaries not properly but by a certain condescension [synkatabasis] to the capacity and perception of the common people. Why so? Because, to be sure, certain stars are greater than the moon. But is the moon therefore not to be called a great luminary, or not to preside in a peculiar manner over the government of the night? Nor indeed is it called a great luminary in respect of its substance, but of its light; which, [ p. 195 ] however borrowed, is greater than that of all the stars. To this knot, which in truth is none at all, the Jews apply the wedge of a most portentous fiction. "God made," says the Targum of Jonathan, "the two great luminaries, and they were equal in glory for twenty-one years, less six hundred and seventy-two parts of an hour" (so that we may know how most accurately he has here attained the truth!); "and afterwards the moon brought an accusation against the sun, and was diminished; and he appointed the sun, the greater luminary, to rule over the day, and the moon, which was the lesser luminary, to preside over the night." In this manner that paraphrast befouls the sacred page almost everywhere, as we have observed more than once before; nor does he seem to have been born for any other end, so rank is everything that is peculiarly his own. No one will come away from reading him either more learned or better. But it is probable that the report of this dominion became known to the nations. This Satan used as an enticement to idolatry, even while the knowledge of the true God was not yet wholly abolished. For what should they honor next after God, if not those things to which God had committed the empire over days and nights, and so over the whole life of men? Hence the poet feigned Juno herself a suppliant to Aeolus, who is said to have presided over the winds: "Juno used these words as a suppliant: Aeolus (for to you the father of the gods and king of men has given power both to calm the waves and to raise them with the wind), a race hateful to me," etc. (Virgil, Aeneid 1.64-67).
Now to serve absolute power with sacred rites is natural; to serve delegated power so, is vicious. But this very many who are called Christians do not yet understand.

III. This, then, was the first occasion of idolatry in the worship of the sun and of the whole host of the heavens, for men who had of their own accord cast away the true knowledge of God. The report of the dominion over day and night delegated to the sun and moon, corrupted by depraved traditions, enticed those who had cast off almost all religion of the true God into the fraud of error. Hence the Sun is king, lord [anax], Molech, and Baal, and whatever else denotes rule and dominion. Add that they were eyewitnesses [autoptai] of the things they had heard by report; they saw that the government of days and nights, and so of all things that are measured by time, was regulated according to the motion and course of the sun and moon; so that all doubt seemed to be removed.

IV. By other vain reasonings also they were carried into this impious opinion.
