For the apostle refers the beginning of idolatry to this, that men became vain "in their reasonings" [Greek: en tois dialogismois auton], Rom. 1:21. For since the minds of men were utterly empty of spiritual and heavenly things, and turned away from God, they burned beyond measure with desire for earthly things. They would never have worshipped, had they not first loved "the creature rather than the Creator" [Greek: ktisin para ton ktisanta]. Now how much weight lay in the operations and influence of the sun and moon, that earthly affairs might succeed prosperously and happily, they thought they had thoroughly ascertained. They judged that there was no need of conjectures here; they were eyewitnesses. And they easily persuaded themselves that all that was God which, being above them, could bring them help [ p. 196 ] in those matters for which they had the greatest care and hope. By no other reasoning [dialogismos] were the idolatrous Israelites deceived into the worship of the host of heaven and of the queen, that is, of the moon and the stars, than that from that religion of theirs they experienced prosperity in all earthly things: Jer. 44:17, 18, "When," they say, "we burned incense to the queen of heaven, we were satisfied with food, and were merry, and experienced no evil. But since we ceased burning incense to the queen of heaven, and pouring out drink offerings to her, we have lacked all things." God therefore, intending to bring that people back to a better mind and to his own worship, recalls to their minds that those vanities of the nations could not give rain, Jer. 14:22. And the apostle Paul, severely rebuking the Gentiles themselves for their nefarious idolatries, and charging them with folly, asserts that the only true God, the creator of all, gave men "from heaven rains and fruitful seasons" [Greek: ouranothen hyetous kai kairous karpophorous], Acts 14:17, indicating not obscurely that the nations had been enticed into the worship of idols by the hope and expectation of the goods of this life [biotika]. And this was the second fountain of this most pernicious error.

V. Further, the Psalmist affirms that the adornment of the heavens, the course of the stars, and the order of the celestial bodies declare the glory of God, Ps. 19:1, as does the apostle in Rom. 1:20. All that glory the apostates snatched from the Creator and assigned to the creatures themselves. The adornment, which was from God, they thought to be God. There is also a persuasion innate in the human mind, that the deity which ought to be worshipped is in the heavens and above us. Hence, when men were placed in dangers, where the present help of God was needed, it was always the custom "to stretch both palms toward the stars." Therefore God, in Hesiod, is he "who inhabits the dwellings on high." Our Lord Jesus Christ also taught us to call upon our Father in heaven; where, namely, he makes his glory manifest in an altogether special manner. But Biddle the Socinian, in his Catechism, ineptly on this account shuts God up, circumscribed within the heavens; which plainly overthrows the divine nature: while others, assigning to the human nature of Christ, which the heavens contain until the restitution of all things, a presence in many places [polytopia], and others a presence in all places [pantotopia], abolish that nature no less effectually; such, among very many Christians, is the itch [cacoethes] for disputing. To those, therefore, who were not pleased to be wise above sense, the sun above all seemed to occupy the place of God in the heavens. This origin and allurement of the worship of the stars the Holy Spirit himself indicates, Deut. 4:19, "Lest perhaps," he says, "you lift up your eyes to heaven, and see the sun, and the moon, and the stars, with the whole host of the heavens, and being driven, adore them." Upon the sight of the eyes follows the impulse of the heart to idolatry. Allured by the beauty, glory, order, and motion of the heavenly bodies, and carried away by excessive admiration, since they knew not how to learn from them the glory of the true God which they declare (inasmuch as they had previously cast away the knowledge of him), they were gradually driven into their worship. Lest the same should happen to the Israelites, warning was given here and elsewhere.

VI. This fountain of the error Lactantius long ago uncovered, book ii. chapter 24, On the Origin of Error: "Since," he says, "in Egypt, on account of the delightful constitution of the land, [ p. 197 ] they rarely kept themselves within houses and huts, they spend the whole night sleeping under a serene sky exposed to no malignant impressions; and from the frequent contemplation of the celestial bodies and of the stars proceeding by a constant law, they came by degrees to the opinion that the stars are gods, and the conservators of the things of the universe, whom they venerated with various rites and ceremonies." Diodorus Siculus, Library, book i. chapter 2, near the beginning, relates similarly: "The men, namely, who anciently inhabited Egypt, looking up at the world above them and struck with awe and admiration at the nature of the universe, supposed that the sun and the moon were eternal and first gods, of whom they named the one Osiris, the other Isis." Hence he himself pronounces that the first and eternal gods were two, the sun and the moon.

VII. From these things the reason of the divine caution mentioned above appears most plainly. For since very many nations had been driven into the worship of the heavenly bodies by the very sight, contemplation, and admiration of them, he sternly admonishes his people, lest the same should befall them. And truly not without cause: Maimonides affirms that he learned the reasons of many laws from the idolatrous worship of the Sabians, in the third part of the Guide for the Perplexed, chapter 29; and he above all is to be consulted by the reader studious of these matters. Of how great weight the reasonings drawn from the contemplation of the order and motion of the stars were, among men ignorant of the true God, for assigning a divine nature to them, Plotinus shows after many centuries, Ennead ii. book 9. "The stars," he says, "which are in the lower spheres, and those which shine in the highest, why should they not be gods, seeing they are borne along in order and revolve in beautiful array?" Hence, after the Christian religion had spread its rays far and wide, the followers of the Platonic philosophy, who with most absurd rivalry attempted to oppose to it the authority of their own wisdom, having rejected the other innumerable fictitious gods of the ancient Gentiles, nevertheless contended that the Sun and the other heavenly bodies were to be adored, and accordingly attributed sight and hearing to them. This the same Plotinus teaches us, Ennead iv. book 4: "But now," he says, "since we have laid it down that memories are superfluous in the stars, we have granted them senses, hearing as well as sight, and have conceded that they hear the prayers which we direct to the sun; and certain other men direct prayers to other stars; for they believe that they will obtain many things from them by their prayers." That most famous interpreter of Plotinus appears to patronize this idol-madness (to add that in passing), bringing forward to that end the passage of the Psalmist, "In the sun he has set his tabernacle," [ p. 198 ] Ps. 19:4. For so the Vulgate translator reports the words of the Psalmist, from the so-called Greek translation of the Seventy. But the Hebrew verity has a far different sense: [Hebrew: lashshemesh sam ohel bahem]; that is, as our countrymen render the words, "In them," that is, in those heavens, "hath he placed a tabernacle for the sun." This version greatly vexed the ancients. Question 63 of the Questions to the Orthodox runs thus: "Since some have interpreted variously and obscurely the words, 'In the sun he has set his tabernacle,' teach us its plain meaning." The most learned author of the solutions answers: "The phrase, 'In the sun he has set his tabernacle,' means that he has set the heavens as a tabernacle for the sun; for the rendering of the expression from the tongue of the Syrians has come about thus: 'In them he has set the tabernacle of the sun.'" Yet in this way it is certainly not read in the Syriac version which is now extant; which also, for other reasons besides, does not seem to be that ancient Syriac translation known to many of the ancients.

VIII. And these were the heads of those most vain reasonings [dialogismoi] by which the impious apostates seduced themselves into the worship of the stars. First, the depraved and corrupted tradition of the dominion of the sun and moon over days and nights took possession of their minds. Then they thought they clearly perceived that the produce of all earthly goods, of the things of this life [ta biotika], with the care and pursuit of which alone they were held, depended chiefly upon their influence. Wrapped up and entangled in these vain opinions, when they contemplated the admirable adornment of the heaven, so full of august majesty, the course of all the stars, the splendor and efficacy of the sun, and his station in the heavens, they rushed headlong into this idol-madness.
Or, on the origin and progress of idolatry, part IV.

Chapter argument: Another corruption of natural theology; the pretext of some, of worshipping natural things under the mask of dead men; the allegorical exposition of physical [physike] or mythical [mythike] theology; the method of philosophizing made new; the theology of the Stoics, of Zeno, and of Chrysippus, refuted by Philo of Byblos, and by Eusebius; idolatrous confusion; whether the Sun and Apollo are the same; the tradition of the history of Jacob in the servitude of Apollo; the follies of the philosophers in excusing the ancient idolaters; a comparison of the ancient idolaters with the more recent; the frauds of mountebanks in excusing the sins of men; of the Pharisees, of the Jesuits; the art of exorcising demons common to the Gentiles, the Jews, and the papists.

I. We have shown that, through the depraved superstition of men, the sun and the rest of the host of heaven first occupied the place and empire of the Deity in the world. From this, in process of time, there sprouted another new and most pernicious superstition, which they call physical theology [theologia physike]. Its first beginnings also we shall recount, but in passing, and as it were by the way [en parodo].

[ p. 199 ] II. According to the precepts of natural theology, the works of nature were designed for promoting the true worship of the Creator of all. In the exposition of that theology in the first book we proved this. The first idolaters taught, as we have also shown, that religious worship was due to those very works. The worship of these they called natural theology; because the things worshipped were things of nature, whereas natural theology in truth teaches us to take instruction from natural things for promoting the worship of the author of nature. That in the succession of time another kind of idolatry was introduced, we shall see below. It took its rise from fabulous traditions, and from the impious and imaginary apotheosis of wicked men. But after men began to philosophize somewhat more strictly among certain schools, and more refined opinions concerning the divine nature had obtained among very many, the wise began to be ashamed of those gods whom the iron ages, wholly sunk in ignorance and darkness, had brought forth. They contended, therefore, that all the things commonly celebrated concerning the fictitious gods, namely Jupiter and the whole sacred troupe of Hellenism, had shadowed forth natural things among the ancient mythologists [mythologoi]. This theology they call mythical [mythike], which they say was nothing else than the allegorical teaching of the physical [physike]. Diodorus Siculus, book i., and Plutarch, On Isis and Osiris, weave this web, and very many others in Eusebius, in that most excellent treasury. To them Jupiter was nothing but the Sun or the Air; Juno, the Moon, or the Earth; and the other idols of the earlier ages, whose origins we shall show hereafter, were either the elements or the natural causes of things. The more recent poets also hint at this everywhere. So Juvenal, Satire v. 78:
"When the spring Jupiter roars with cruel hail."

And before him Virgil, Georgics ii. 324-327: "In spring the lands swell and demand the generative seeds. Then the almighty father, Aether, descends with fertilizing showers into the lap of his joyful spouse, and, mighty himself, mingling with her mighty body, nourishes all her offspring." And Lucretius the Epicurean, who preceded him, book ii. verses 654-658: "Here if anyone resolves to call the sea Neptune, and the crops Ceres, and prefers to misuse the name of Bacchus rather than utter the proper appellation of the liquor, let us concede that he may declare the orb of the earth to be the Mother of the Gods, provided that in reality he keep himself from staining his mind with foul religion." And the more ancient Greeks: Orpheus: "Earth, mother of all, Demeter, giver of wealth." For Demeter was Ge Meter, Earth-Mother, as Diodorus testifies. Empedocles also, in the verses which Athenagoras records in his Embassy: "Gleaming Zeus, life-bearing Hera, and Aidoneus, and Nestis, who moistens with her tears the mortal fountain"; that is, "Jupiter glittering with fire, Juno the vital air, Pluto lowest of the gods, and the moist power of Nestis that waters." And another, Ennius, in Cicero, On the Nature of the Gods, book ii. chapter 25: "Behold this shining height above, which all invoke as Jove."

[ p. 200 ] III. Of this same kind also were the tenets of the Stoics. Thus of Zeno, Velleius the Epicurean in Cicero, book i. of On the Nature of the Gods, chapter 14: "When he interprets the Theogony of Hesiod," he says, "he does away entirely with the customary and received notions of the gods; for he reckons neither Jupiter, nor Juno, nor Vesta, nor anyone bearing such a name, in the number of the gods; but teaches that these names have been attributed, by a kind of symbolic signification, to inanimate and mute things." And shortly after, of Chrysippus: "The same man argues that the aether is that which men call Jupiter; and that the air which flows through the seas is Neptune; and that the earth is what is called Ceres." Hence Seneca observes that sacrifices were offered to the clouds, that they might not send down hail and storms, Natural Questions, book iv. chapters 6 and 7.

IV. The same path was trodden by the more recent Platonic-Pythagoreans: Plotinus, Porphyry, Iamblichus, Amelius, Eumenius, and others. Let Porphyry's fable serve as an example, his interpretation [hermeneia] of Saturn's castrating his own father Coelus [Heaven], and of Jupiter's castrating Saturn, and its application to the celestial causes of things. "Kronos," he says, "is bound and castrated, as was Ouranos: whereby the theologian (that is, Musaeus) hints in riddles that divine essences are bound by the bait of pleasure and drawn down into generation, and that, dissolved in pleasure, they sow forth certain powers as seed: whence Kronos castrates Ouranos as he descends to Earth in desire of intercourse: for Kronos, and his sphere, is the first of those that move counter to Ouranos; and certain powers descend both from Heaven and from the wandering stars. But Kronos receives the powers of Heaven, and Zeus in turn those of Kronos." Thus he, in his book on the Cave of the Nymphs in Homer. He contends, that is, that the fable hints that Saturn, who, next to Heaven in order, confronts its motion with his retrograde course, so intercepts the prolific power of Heaven that it cannot descend immediately to the earth; while the power of Jupiter in turn intercepts the influence of Saturn. By this device, I say, they busied themselves to lay a cosmetic gloss over the stupidity of the first idolaters and the insanity of Hellenism.

V. Philo of Byblos, in Eusebius, Preparation for the Gospel, book i. chapter 9, shows at length that the philosophers most ineptly invented these things, the matter being plainly otherwise. "But the most recent of the sacred writers," he says, "rejected the things actually done from the beginning, and having devised allegories and myths, and having fabricated a kinship with the phenomena of the universe, they established mysteries."

VI. Eusebius attacks that vanity at length, Preparation for the Gospel, [ p. 201 ] book iii. chapter 1. For with this the Christians of that time had to deal. For after the light of the gospel had so far struck the orb of the earth with its rays that the shameful insanity of the old superstition came into contempt with the very common people, the keener sophists, as I have said, in order to render that folly amiable by painting it with a new color, clung most pertinaciously to this fiction, against which stands the credit of all history. Indeed, to say this in passing, in the first ages of the church the whole method of philosophizing among the gentiles themselves was made new. For after Ammonius of Alexandria, the leader [Coryphaeus] of the philosophers of his age, whose hearers were Origen, Herennius, and Plotinus (the last the teacher of Porphyry, as Porphyry was of Iamblichus), had sown in the minds of his hearers, through his philosophical discourses, certain seeds of heavenly wisdom; those who, on account of inveterate prejudices and the hatred of the world, refused to embrace the Christian religion, nevertheless did not cease to cultivate those seeds with all their might, drawing rivulets of truth into the gaps of Plato. Add that others, from the reading of our books, had drawn very many things from the most hidden mysteries of the gospel. Of these were Numenius, Amelius, Plotinus, Herennius, Porphyry, Iamblichus, Proclus, Hierocles, Marinus, Damascius, and others, who, although they abandoned neither the curious speculations of the Platonists nor the magical incantations of the Pythagoreans, yet mingled with them many little sparks of heavenly truth.

VII. Among the ancients, however, all things were confused and uncertain. Plutarch disputes at length whether Apollo was other than the sun, in his book On Why the Pythia No Longer Gives Oracles in Verse. But the most ancient of the poets teach in their very hymns that he is the sun, and set forth where he was born and from what parents. So that the Arcadians could feign themselves older not only than the Moon but also than the Sun. They add also fables stitched together out of ancient traditions. Such is what they write of the pastoral exile of Apollo; which indeed Callimachus celebrates in such a way that I could almost believe that the story of Jacob, passing that kind of life with Laban the Syrian, is represented in that fable. He teaches that the cause of the journey and of the labor undertaken was love: "Burning with love for the young Admetus"; just as love was the cause of Jacob's pastoral servitude. Now since Callimachus, in assigning the cause of Apollo's servitude, departs from the rest of the crowd of poets, beyond doubt the report of some obscure tradition gave him the handle for so doing. Then he excellently depicts the increase of the young cattle under his care:

[ p. 202 ] "Easily would the pasture abound more in herds, nor would the she-goats mingled with the sheep lack young, on whom Apollo cast his eye as they grazed; nor would the ewes be without milk or barren, but all would have lambs beneath them; and she that bore one would soon become the mother of twins."

If one cared to examine the poet's words one by one, very many things would be found which agree wonderfully with the history of Jacob; but because a reader of any diligence will perceive them at first glance, and we have other business in hand, it does not please us to dwell on them in this place.

VIII. But that new mystical natural theology was a most rotten fiction of men blinded in ignorance and malice. The greatest part of mortals venerated the sun, the moon, and the other heavenly bodies with divine honors long before the fables of Hellenism had their rise or even their occasion. Greece was as it were the native country of the man-shaped [anthropophyeis] gods, and the workshop of idols. But that the Greeks themselves anciently worshipped no other gods than the sun, moon, and stars, we have shown above from Plato. Yet so it pleased these new masters to undertake the patronage of the ignorance and darkness of primitive times, themselves no whit wiser than those of whom they were most ashamed. Not otherwise do the Jewish rabbis at this day twist all the monstrosities of the Talmud into I know not what allegories; it is a wonder the Jesuits have not done the same with their legends. They would perhaps have acted more prudently and more modestly if, imitating the philosophers and the rabbis, they had devised ingenious lies and thereby won credit for them, than was in fact done when, after they had led so many thousands of men in every nation for so many years through the byways of the foulest errors, they utterly and openly rejected them. But this, you will say, is an injury, indeed an outrage: to wish to compare and set side by side the most holy fathers of the Society of Jesus with philosophers and rabbis? Come then, let us see by one example (or perhaps a second), but one of the greatest moment, whether they tread in their footsteps or not. No one can inflict a more atrocious injury upon the immortal souls of mortals, upon human society, and upon the obedience owed to the supreme Deity, than he who so labors in cloaking and excusing the crimes of sinners that they thence take courage to commit them further and more freely. What the masked hierophants among the gentiles accomplished to this end, the divine philosopher teaches at length in the second book of the Republic. "Begging priests and soothsayers," he says, "going to the doors of the rich, persuade them that there is with them a power procured from the gods, by sacrifices and incantations, to heal with pleasures and feasts any wrong done by a man himself or by his ancestors; and if anyone wishes to harm an enemy, that for a small outlay he can injure just and unjust alike, since by certain charms and binding spells they persuade the gods, as they say, to serve them. And for all these claims they bring forward the poets as witnesses, some granting an easy indulgence to vice: 'Wickedness may be chosen easily and in abundance; smooth is the road, and she dwells very near;' [ p. 203 ] but before virtue the gods have set sweat,' and a certain road both long and steep. Others, for the leading astray of the gods by men, call Homer to witness, since he too said: 'The gods themselves are turned; men, praying, turn them aside with sacrifices and gentle vows, with libation and the savor of burnt offering, whenever anyone has transgressed and sinned.' And they produce a clamorous heap of books of Musaeus and Orpheus, offspring, as they say, of the Moon and of the Muses, according to which they perform their sacrifices, persuading not only private men but cities also that there are indeed absolutions and purifications of wrongdoings, through sacrifices and the pleasures of play, both for the living and likewise for the dead; which rites they call teletai [initiations], which release us from the evils of that other world; while for those who have not sacrificed terrible things await." [Owen subjoins his own Latin rendering of the same passage, to the identical effect; it is translated once above.] This superstition, as one that works the confusion of all duties, and indeed the plague and destruction of the whole of life, the philosopher censures at length.

IX. Let the reader learn from this that there is almost nothing new in the devices of those who, putting on the mask of religion, either serve the various lusts [epithymiai] of the flesh, or gape after riches, or after dominion over the consciences of blinded men. That the Pharisees among the Jews, most like these mountebanks in wickedness, audacity, and superstition, devised lies twin to their inventions, from which they might extract an excuse for sins, our most holy [ p. 204 ] Savior, who is the way, the life, and the truth, shows in Matt. 5-7. And that in the Jesuits there is equal avarice, like depravity, the same impudence, twin audacity, in extenuating, excusing, and expiating the sins and crimes of the most villainous scoundrels, in whetting and inflaming the minds of men to vices, in blunting all the goads of conscience, was made public not long ago by him who in a particular book uncovered their mysteries. Let the reader compare what we have adduced from Plato with what that learned man excerpted from the books of the Jesuits; he will scarcely find egg more like egg than the dogmas of these men are like the tenets of those mountebanks.

X. I will add another instance, which likewise concerns gain and vainglory [kenodoxia]. Let us take it from the exorcisms of demons. Philostratus, book iv. of the Life of Apollonius, chapter 6, narrating the casting out of a demon from a young man by Apollonius, after setting forth the frenzy and debauchery of the possessed, adds: "And most people thought that it was the wantonness of youth that carried him into these things. But the youth was in fact acting under the prompting of the demon, and seemed to be playing the drunkard when it was the demon's drunken violence; and when Apollonius gazed upon him in anger, the phantom uttered cries of fear and rage, such as are heard from men being burned or put to the rack, and swore that it would depart from the lad, and would never enter into any man again. But Apollonius, speaking to it with anger, as a master to a slave that is shifty, knavish, shameless, and the like, commanded it to depart with a visible sign. 'I will throw down yonder statue,' it said, pointing to one of those about the royal portico, near which these things were taking place; and when the statue first stirred, and then fell..." [Owen subjoins his own Latin rendering of the Philostratus passage, to the same effect; it is translated once above.] Whether these things were really so done, I do not know. Eusebius, writing against Hierocles, very often catches Philostratus lying abed with his falsehoods. But I know that things twin to these are daily done, or feigned, among the fathers of the Society. It is wonderful, too, how much these men and that man agree. You would think you were hearing Menghi, Thyraeus, or Sprenger speaking. And as that Apollonius was perspicacious above other mortals, so that he discerned a demon in a drunken youth when all others saw nothing in him except what they judged should be ascribed to debauchery and impudence, so it very often happens that the fathers of the Society descry impure [ p. 205 ] spirits in persons, especially in young men and girls, in whom the rest of mortals, blinder forsooth than moles, can discern nothing at all, save what they judge must be imputed to the ravings of some and the jugglings of others. Again, the idol of Philostratus did not rage at the presence and gaze of the magician Apollonius any more than is customary with the Jesuitical idols at the presence, gaze, or touch of a popish priest. This we are taught, among others, by Edmunds himself, that mass-priest, in his miraculous history of the things done in England about the year 1585 and afterwards. "Scarcely," he says, "had I begun the exorcisms and laid my hands upon his head, when he" (the Jesuitical youth) "immediately began to rage, to be driven on high, to thrash with hands and feet, to push away the priest's hand, to fill the whole place with cries, oaths, and blasphemous curses"; so that that young man of Philostratus was plainly no match for him. But in the matter of the reproaches to be spoken against the demon, it will plainly appear that Apollonius was an infant, if he be compared with these men of ours. He called the spirit shifty, knavish, and shameless [poikilon, panourgon, anaide]. But if anyone will consult Menghi, in the fourth part of his Scourge of Demons on Exorcism, he will easily perceive how trifling these things are in comparison with the terms which he teaches the fathers to use in exorcisms: "Hear," says the priest, "you senseless one, false one, reprobate, master of demons, most wretched creature, tempter of men, deceiver of evil angels, beguiler of souls, leader of heretics, father of lying, fool, bestial one, witless one, drunkard, infernal robber, most iniquitous serpent, most rapacious wolf, you lean, famished, most filthy sow, scabby beast, most savage beast, cruel beast, bloody beast, most bestial beast of all beasts, spirit of Acheron, sooty spirit, spirit of Tartarus." Away with the trifles of Apollonius; the very demon may learn from this how to revile. It must needs be an untamed and utterly stubborn spirit that is not terrified by these reproaches. Without doubt Saint Michael did not dare to use a railing judgment [krisis blasphemias] of this sort, Jude 9. These new masters also imitate the overthrow of the statue at the demon's departure. But here they sink shamefully below Apollonius. For they very rarely proceed beyond the snuffing out of a candle or the breaking of a window. To throw down statues is not for every mass-priest. Now this art of exorcising demons the Jews received from the gentiles, and the papists from both. "Indeed, the exorcists from among you make use of a certain art in their exorcisms, just as the gentiles do, and employ fumigations and bindings," Justin Martyr in the Dialogue with Trypho.

XI. But we must return from the byway into the road. The worship of the heaven and of the sun flourished through a long series of years, while it had not yet come into men's minds to raise their own likes to the throne of God; nor among the Greeks themselves did he escape unpunished for his blasphemy who said that the Sun was "a great mass of fire" [pyros athroisma mega].
Or, on the origin and progress of idolatry, chapter 5.

Chapter argument: The beginnings of idolatry assigned by the ancients to the age of Serug, without reason or authority; Eusebius's Chronicle; Suidas interpolated; Serug of the family of Shem; whether Terah the father of Abraham was a maker of statues; the opinion of Epiphanius on the origin of idolatry, and that of Tertullian; the arts of painting and statuary in the service of idolatry; the rude beginnings of the art of painting; who, according to Pliny and Athenagoras, were its inventors; the distinction between shadow [skia] and image [eikōn], Heb. 10:1; the sun and moon the first idols; the first idolatrous worship simple adoration; its progress; to worship God is natural; all superstition is full of fluctuations; the absence of the new gods; Elijah's mockery of the idolaters on account of the absence of their idols; the Ithyphallic song sung by the Athenians to Demetrius Poliorcetes; the occasion of the making of Aaron's calf; the influences of the heavenly bodies upon things generated; the madness and obduracy of idolaters; the occasions of a new kind of superstition; the origin of pillars, stelae, and images; the testimony of Maimonides, and of Plotinus; the method of fabricating images; the idolatry of the astrologers; the folly of image-making [eikonopoiia], ancient and modern.

I. Nearly all the ancients assign the beginnings of idolatry to the age of Serug. Serug was born in the sixty-third year after the Babylonian dispersion. That span of time they reckon to have been free from this crime. We, for our part, do not doubt that immediately upon the dispersion the greatest part of the apostate multitude bade farewell to the worship of the one true God. Nor is any reason assigned why the age of Serug should be believed to have given rise to this crime in some special way. Very many follow Eusebius in his Chronicle. He asserts that Serug was of the tribe or family of Japheth, whereas Shem was his great-great-great-grandfather. The words of Eusebius are recited twice in Suidas, and in both places Serug is said to have been of the family of Japheth; for in that work a great many things are inserted two or three times over, which are the additions of later hands. But the things they ascribe to Serug pertain to Hellenism; for they pretend that he first entered upon the way of religiously worshipping the dead, a practice which they say Terah, the father of Abraham, advanced by the fabrication of statues. Epiphanius says this concerning Serug: "Not yet had that superstitious worship attached itself to carved images and sculptured stones or woods or statues wrought of silver or gold or fashioned from any other material; only by means of colors and likenesses did the mind of man discover for itself this wickedness." All the apparatus of idolatry then consisted, as Tertullian says, in pictures and colors.

II. That the arts of painting and statuary had their beginnings in the service of idolatry would be easy to prove; but that the former preceded the latter in that employment is not so easily shown. The graphic art [graphikē] remained exceedingly rude for several ages, and was unfit for representing the gods, unless they were first fashioned out of some [ p. 207 ] solid material. Pliny teaches that it had its beginning in shadow-painting [skiagraphia], a rough likeness being formed from the observation of a man's shadow. So Athenagoras, in his Embassy for the Christians: "Saurias invented shadow-painting, having traced the outline of a horse's shadow in the sun." But to paint in shadow the Sun itself, which they held almost alone and above all for a god, was exceedingly difficult. He adds: "Craton first displayed the graphic art" (that, namely, which added colors to lines), "who delineated on a whitened tablet the shadows of a man and a woman." Others added other refinements. Hence that distinction of the apostle in Heb. 10:1, "For the law, having a shadow of good things to come, and not the very image of the things" [skian gar echōn ho nomos tōn mellontōn agathōn, ouk autēn tēn eikona tōn pragmatōn]. Terah was the grandson of Serug through his son Nahor; the Jews invented the tale, and very many of the ancient Christians believed it, that he practiced the potter's art and statuary, and fabricated images.

III. The image-making [eikonopoiia] of Terah therefore followed the Serugian idolatry by about eighty or a hundred years. And this is commonly assigned as the first progress of idolatry. We have entered upon other lines of reasoning in our investigation of the beginnings of superstitious worship; and the road we have begun, we shall pursue further. The "new gods" [Hebrew: elohim chadashim], as the Holy Spirit speaks in Judg. 5:8, which the apostates dispersed from Babylon first procured for themselves, were the sun and the moon, as we have shown, and the other heavenly bodies. These they used as guides, so to speak, of their wanderings. The first worship consisted in simple adoration. Into other germs of error, and the foulest rites of superstition, the human race slipped by degrees. This, as the matter and the occasion allow, we shall set forth in few words.

IV. That some God be acknowledged and worshipped is demanded by that very reason whereby we are men. Hence the goads of conscience harass those who have forgotten the true God, until they adopt false ones. Thus the first apostates betook themselves to the sun, the moon, and the heavenly stars. For a time the novelty of the thing pleased them, and the name of gods. But when they were driven into new straits of affairs, in peril of their dearest possessions, the goads and horrors of conscience afflicted them with new anxiety and trouble. For no one can enjoy true tranquillity of mind where, by depraved superstition, war is declared against the true God. First, then, those divinities did not seem sufficiently present; for unless a god be present, he is not to be held for a divinity. By the same instinct of nature by which we are imbued with the persuasion that God exists, we are persuaded also that he is present. Nor can the usefulness of a divinity, which among men wise only in earthly things is the chief consideration, suffice for warding off dangers, if that divinity is not more present than every danger; and therefore such a one is no God at all. Hence Elijah reproaches the Baalite Israelites with the absence of their idol, 1 Kings 18:27: "Cry with a loud voice, since he is a god; for either he is in conversation, or he is pursuing, or he is on a journey; perhaps he sleeps." With severe irony [eirōneia] he strips the idol of all divine nature, of all attributes befitting God, and so wrests from it all power and removes it from divine dominion.

V. And this consideration always held all idolaters so suspended and anxious that, although they attempted to remedy their fear and wavering by sacred pillars and images, as we shall presently see, yet it could not be but that the uncertainty of mind with which they were agitated should sometimes burst forth and betray itself. Hence it was that the Athenian people, receiving Demetrius Poliorcetes, the liberator of their city, with divine honors, inserted among other things these lines into the Ithyphallic song, that is, the sacred hymn sung to him as he entered the walls:

"O son of Poseidon, mightiest of gods, and of the goddess Aphrodite, hail! For the other gods are either far away, or have no ears, or do not exist, or pay no heed at all to us; but you we see present here, not of wood, nor of stone, but real." (Athenaeus, Deipnosophists, book 6, citing Duris of Samos.)

VI. For this cause the Israelites in the desert drove Aaron to cast the golden calf, Exod. 32:1. "This Moses," they say, "is absent; arise therefore and make us gods, who shall go before us." They do not say other gods, nor gods absolutely, but "gods who shall go before us," and be present with us by their help. For they did not demand another God than Jehovah, but some visible sign of the divine presence, to which they might flee in doubtful and difficult affairs. And signs of that kind were afterwards indulged by God to that carnal people, yet instituted by himself alone. And this was the beginning of transgression fatal to that people. Whence it passed into a proverb: "There befalls you no punishment, O Israel, in which there is not an ounce of the iniquity of the calf" [Hebrew: ein lekha Yisrael pur'anut she-ein bah unkiya me-avon ha-egel]. Frivolous, and most thoroughly Jewish [Ioudaikōtaton], is the attempt of some of the rabbis who, from those words of Aaron, "I cast the gold into the fire, and there came out this calf," as if it had leaped forth unexpectedly and without his thinking of any such thing, try to transfer the sin of forming the calf onto the rabble of Egyptians, among whom there were certain magicians, by whose art it was brought about that, beyond the expectation of all, the calf came forth.

VII. This, then, was the occasion of a new superstition for the first idolaters, after they had concluded that the stars of heaven were to be held in the place of deity. As every lie is full of others and fruitful of them, so the greatest of lies is the most fruitful of all. Nor is there any error which does not either suggest to the erring the enticements of very many more, or bring in their necessity. Those divinities were absent. But for men placed in dangers, in doubtful and arduous affairs, there was need of a present God. Cast into these straits, by the dictate of right reason they ought to have returned to the God who is everywhere present. [ p. 209 ] "But they did not see fit to retain that true God in their knowledge," as the apostle speaks, Rom. 1:28; and therefore God gave them over to a mind destitute of all judgment. Endowed with such a mind, something had to be done by which they might render the gods they had chosen present to themselves.

VIII. Next, since the effects of the operations of the heavenly bodies were common and equal to all, it was exceedingly difficult to understand in what way each individual might make himself a partaker, in some peculiar manner, of those benefits which they believed to flow from them. For he who is so the God of all that he takes no care of individuals, and confers benefit on no one otherwise than when he bestows something on all together, is in truth God to no one. He who is not my God is no God to me. And hence too they might most easily have understood that they had hurled themselves into incredible difficulties. But the die was cast; and where they had begun, they had to go on. Violence therefore had to be done to the common notions [koinai ennoiai], and to nature herself. The defection of the human race from the empire and government of the one God cost no less than this. When the new sensible gods had been set up, what remained of right reason cried out in protest that they were not present, that they took no care of individuals, and that therefore they were neither gods, nor to be worshipped, nor to be feared. To bad knots a bad wedge was applied; and lies were roofed over with lies, lest the rain come through. For in order to meet this double defect arising from the first and greatest sin of idolatry, the idolaters, by the help of Satan, entered upon new ways by which they might exhibit the absent divinities as present, and draw down their beneficent influences, and enclose them in some present object set before each individual. And this was the origin of sacred pillars, stelae, statues, and images; which we shall expound in what follows.

IX. There were two inconveniences, as we have said, by which the idolaters felt themselves pressed in their choice of new gods: first, that these were absent and far distant; second, that they took no care of individuals. These they wished to remedy by the erection of pillars and stelae. For they supposed that, by the relation of these to the sun, moon, and stars, whether induced by diabolical incantations or imposed by the force of dedication, the heavenly bodies themselves could be made to stand present, through a certain ethereal Spirit, and that salutary influences could be drawn out of them by anyone through vows and prayers. So Rambam [Maimonides] teaches us, from the ancient monuments of the Sabians, in the Guide for the Perplexed, part 3, chapter 29: "According to those opinions of the Sabians," he says, "they erected images to the stars: to the sun, indeed, golden images, but to the moon silver ones; and so they divided the metals and the climates of the earth among the stars. For they said of such-and-such a climate, its god is such-and-such a star. Then they built shrines and placed the images in them, supposing that the powers of the stars flow into those images, and that the images possess the faculty of understanding, bestow the gift of prophecy on men, and, in short, point out to them the things that are useful and salutary to them. So also they say of trees which fall under the lot of those stars, that when a certain tree [ p. 210 ] is dedicated to some star, planted in its name, and tended in this or that manner, the spiritual powers of the star are infused into that tree." Plotinus lays open the whole mystery, Enneads 4, book 3, chapter 11. "And it seems to me," he says, "that the wise men of old, whoever desired that the gods be present to them" (those, namely, whom they saw with their own eyes to be situated far away from them), "in fabricating sacred rites and statues, directed the gaze of their mind upon the very nature of the universe; and thence perceived that the nature of the soul is everywhere easy to lead and exceedingly compliant, and that it could most easily of all be captured, if anyone should construct something capable of being affected by it and of receiving, by being so affected, some portion of it." This is what we have taught; namely, that the idolaters, those Plotinian wise men, hoped to capture the force or virtue of the soul of heaven and of the stars, and to enclose it in stelae or statues, so that it might be always present with them. And to teach that this could be done, he adds: "That which has been in any way made in imitation is sympathetically receptive, like a mirror able to seize some form." That is, when a statue [agalma], or image, is erected to a heavenly virtue, that virtue is prone to descend into it, so as to answer to the imagination of its fabricator; and the statue straightway seizes it, no otherwise than a mirror seizes the appearance of a body set before it. "For," he says, "the nature of the universe, which skilfully fashions all things, makes them in imitation of those things whose rational principles it possesses." That is, there is nothing among these lower things which has not been fabricated after the pattern of some virtue which first existed in the mind of the universe; which is the doctrine of the Platonists concerning ideas. There follows in the philosopher: "Since, then, each thing thus came to be a rational principle in matter, formed according to the principle that is prior to matter, it was joined to that God according to whom it came to be, toward whom the soul looked and whom it held while it made." These words complete the whole mystery. There is granted a certain principle of the matter cast into an image, which exactly answers to that heavenly principle which existed before that matter; and hence arises a relation between the image and that very being to which it is consecrated. But why this image, or this portion of matter, should have this god, or this portion of heavenly power, joined to it, rather than any other whatsoever, does not yet appear. He shows that this comes about for a twofold reason. First, because it was fabricated according to that god: "It was joined to that God according to whom it came to be." But how could the maker of the image know according to which god's power [ p. 211 ] that matter, so formed by him, emanated? This he can have perceived from his own soul; for the god toward whom the soul looked while it made the statue is the one according to whom it is fabricated. But whether the iconolaters of our own day can render better reasons why this or that image should represent this or that saint rather than any other, I for my part very much doubt.

X. For the rest, to add this in passing, this idolatrous delirium still openly clings to very many who hawk a nefarious art under the name of judicial astrology. For they make figures and images which they pretend to correspond to the forms of the heavenly bodies; and thence they persuade the foolish that these acquire power and marvelous effects from the heavenly figure. They have also statues and images in which they say the spirits of the stars are enclosed. But whether those spirits are powers of the heavenly bodies, or demons that are attendants of the stars, they do not know. All these things savor of gross idolatry; and yet Albertus Magnus in his "Mirror," Marsilio Ficino in "On Obtaining Life from the Heavens," Girolamo Cardano, and others, were not ashamed to thrust these most absurd monstrosities of heathen men upon the Christian world, without truth or modesty. And in these things consists the whole rationale of magic; from which crime I can by no means clear the consecration, among the papists, of the little images which they call Agnus Dei, of candles, of lustral water, and of other things.
Or, on the origin and progress of idolatry, chapter 6.

Chapter argument: The progress of idolatry; the visible objects of the first superstitious worship; pyramid-shaped columns [kiones pyramoeideis], unwrought stones [lithoi argoi], baetyls [baitylia], the pillar [Hebrew: matstsebah], stelae; what an agalma is; the testimony of Clement, of the Phoronis, of Eumelus; the origin of stelae according to Clement; the truer opinion; the columnar stone in a field at Colchester; unwrought stones; the testimonies of Pausanias, Maximus of Tyre, Suidas; Theusares; Stonehenge; Elagabalus; the Idaean Mother; the Persians destroy and mock statues [agalmata]; the image of Diana of Ephesus; the idols of the Germans; groves empty of images: oaks; the most ancient temples without images; no agalmata in antiquity; rough and four-cornered stones; baetyls; sacred stones; the testimony of Sanchuniathon; he feigns that Uranus devised living stones; most of what Eusebius produces from him is ridiculous; the ancients feigned that stones were ensouled [empsychoi]; Baitylos in Hesychius; whence the proverb "You would swallow a baetyl" arose; the baetyls in Damascius as cited by Photius; what therapeuō means; anointed stones, in Clement and Arnobius; whence baetyls got their name; Scaliger's conjecture; the erection of the stone at Jacob's Bethel; what matstsebah signifies; what kind of deed Jacob's was, and to what end; why the Vulgate interpreter renders matstsebah as "title"; the epiphanies [epiphaneiai] of the gods, Dionysius of Halicarnassus, Cicero; a festival of the Christians; the worship of the Bethel-stone among the Phoenicians; the progress of idolatry; statues in the likeness of the human face dedicated to the sun; and many other things.

I. To what we have said concerning the progress of idolatry in the invention of present objects of religious worship, credit is given by the memory that survives, in the records of [ p. 212 ] things, of the first visible objects of veneration [sebasmata]. These in antiquity were of two kinds: namely, pyramid-shaped columns [kiones pyramoeideis], pointed pillars; and unwrought stones [lithoi argoi], rude and shapeless stones, to which the baetyls [baitylia] may be added. Not until long afterwards, with the passage of time, were images fashioned after the human form, when, that is, the worship of the dead had been introduced through Hellenism.

II. Sacred columns the Greeks call stelae [stēlai]. So the Septuagint renders matstsebah; these are prohibited in Lev. 26:1 and Deut. 16:22. "A matstsebah," says Kimchi, "is a stone which is set up for adoration." It is from yatsab, "he set up." So Apollo Agyieus was a pyramid, as Hesychius witnesses, or a column ending in a point [kiōn eis oxy lēgōn]. Concerning these Clement of Alexandria writes, in book 1: "Before the proportions of statues had been exactly determined, the ancients set up columns and venerated these as seats [aphidrymata] of the deity." An aphidryma of that kind is an image which is, as it were, the seat of a divinity. This is what we have said: while statues [agalmata] were not yet accurately fashioned, they set up columns to receive the virtue of the absent divinities, and so to become, as it were, the seats of the gods. But an agalma is an image artfully fabricated. "Everything in which one takes delight [agalletai]," says Suidas; that is, what so feasts the eyes as to affect the mind with a certain joy.

III. To this point belong the lines which the same Clement reports from the author of the Phoronis, concerning Callithoe, the first priestess of Juno: "Callithoe, key-bearer of the Olympian queen, Argive Hera, who first adorned with fillets and tassels the tall column of the mistress." The poet calls it tall [makron] because the stele was erected to a height, unlike the unwrought stones [lithoi argoi]. So the column [kiōn] in Homer is sometimes called tall [makros], sometimes lofty [hypsēlos]. He brings forward similar lines also from Eumelus, namely: "That we might hang up for the god the tithe and the firstfruit spoils from the sacred doorposts and the lofty column." Although perhaps Eumelus meant nothing else than the firstfruit offerings [akrothinia] and spoils customarily hung from pilasters and columns; which is very well known.

IV. Clement writes that the demons invented these things in imitation of that pillar which went before the Israelites in the desert. Let others judge; for my part I do not doubt that at the very time when the tabernacle was erected, columns and stelae of this kind were in common use among many nations. From this source perhaps came the Anthelian gods, the guardians of doorways; little columns at the doors of temples and of other houses. Hesychius: "Anthelian gods, those set up before the doors." Athenaeus, book 10, says "in the courtyard"; that is, in the first entrance of the house, under the open sky. The making of statues [agalmatopoiia] or of images [eikonopoiia] had not yet come into men's minds when they first set up columns and stelae for the sake of religion. For since they had held only the heavenly luminaries in the place of divinities, there was no reason why they should erect to them images feigned and fabricated in human form. That indeed came to pass as superstition gained strength, when, to wit, the same body was at once the sun and some [ p. 213 ] Apollo or other; but so long as the idolaters had nothing else in mind than to make the absent divinities present by some visible sign, they had no need of the representation of any animal whatever in that sign. I remember seeing a column of this kind in a field at Colchester among the Trinobantes; on which later ages placed the image of a cross, and consecrated it anew to the use of a new superstition.

V. Next, together with those pointed columns, they used rough stones for the same end. So Pausanias in his book on Achaia: "Very near the statue stand four-cornered stones, about thirty in number; these the people of Pharae venerate, calling each by the name of some god; and in the still more ancient times, among all the Greeks, unwrought stones held the honors of gods in the place of statues." An illustrious testimony. "Near the image about thirty stones are set up, of quadrangular shape; the people of Pharae venerate them, calling each by fixed names of gods; and assuredly it was an ancestral custom of the Greeks to worship rude stones in the place of images of the gods." He mentions stones of that kind also in his book on Boeotia. This, I say, was the custom of those most ancient Greeks, who, as we have taught out of Plato, worshipped the sun, the moon, and the other luminaries of heaven, and these alone. And Maximus of Tyre, on the Arabs, Dissertation 38: "The Arabs venerate I know not whom; but the image which I saw was a four-cornered stone." The sign of the unknown god was a quadrangular stone. But that god was called Theusares. "That is," says Suidas, "theos Arēs, the god Mars"; in which exposition of the name he without doubt errs; but he adds, whoever that god was: "The image is a black stone, four-cornered, bearing no graven figure, that is, rude, four feet in height, two in breadth; it rests upon a base of beaten gold. To this they sacrifice and pour out the blood of the victims; and this is their libation."

VI. Of this kind perhaps were the stones here in England, in the county of Salisbury, vast and rough rocks, unwrought stones [lithoi argoi], as Pausanias speaks. Jones, a most skilful architect, in a special treatise proves them to have been sacred. That the ancient Britons worshipped the moon under the name of Andraste, Dio Cassius is our authority, History, book 62. And it was to the sun and moon that stones of this kind were erected.

VII. We have shown above that the worshippers of the stars erected these stones and columns in order to have ready at hand in them the power and energy [energeia] of protecting and benefiting belonging to the divinities they worshipped. This they believed could come about through the descent of an Ethereal Spirit. At length the stones themselves were styled gods or divine ones. Lampridius, in his Life of Heliogabalus: "He wished to carry off the stones which are called Divine from their own temple, and the image of Diana of Laodicea from its inner shrine, in which Orestes had placed it." The historian means nothing other than unwrought stones of this kind consecrated to the sun and moon, which in the course of time were reckoned under the name of gods. Hence the custom of swearing by Jupiter the stone [Jovem lapidem]. And concerning the idol Agabalus, from which that monster of a man, the pseudo-Antoninus, borrowed his name, Herodian writes: [ p. 214 ] "There is a certain very great stone, rounded below, ending in a point." Moreover, agli baal is "the round lord"; by which name they denoted the sun itself; unless rather the sun was called, in Syriac, elah Gabal, that is, the God of the Gabalites, the name being taken from the place of worship, like Baal Peor. The Idaean mother, which, conveyed from Pessinus to Rome with such great religious ceremony, the whole city placed in the temple of Victory, was a stone of this kind, as Livy testifies, book 29, chapter 11.

VIII. In primitive times, therefore, among many nations statues [agalmata] were in no use, nor even known, and by some they were even held in hatred. Of the Persians Herodotus writes in his Clio, chapter 131: "It is not their custom to set up statues and temples and altars; nay, they impute folly to those who do so; because, as it seems to me, they have not believed, as the Greeks do, that the gods are of human form." An illustrious testimony. That the Persians pursued the sun and moon with religious worship we have shown above. Adhering to that superstition received from the first idolaters, they never admitted the divinities which Hellenism brought in, and for that reason they had no statues. Hence those who wrote the history of the Magi, cited by Diogenes Laertius in the preface of his work, relate that Xerxes, in his European expedition, "fittingly destroyed the statues," removed the images according to the institute of his training. Indeed, they not only removed images of that kind, but also mocked them merrily. For when Cambyses had entered the Memphite temple of Vulcan, "he laughed heartily at the statue" (Herodotus, book 3); and in the shrine of the Cabiri, "he burned the statues, after jesting much upon them." He burned them after the manner of his country, because they were images of men; he laughed, because they were ridiculously fabricated. For this cause he gave all the temples of Asia to the fire, as Solinus testifies, with the sole exception of that of Diana of Ephesus, whose image was a pointed trunk of elm or beech. So Callimachus teaches us, in his Hymn to Artemis:

"For you also the Amazons, lovers of war, once set up a wooden image [bretas] on the seashore of Ephesus, beneath the trunk of a beech" (Callimachus, Hymn to Artemis). For the rest, the father of history does not say that the Persians had no pillars at all. Statues [agalmata] they did not have, because they did not believe the gods to be of human form [anthrōpophyeis]. But that they repudiated all columns and stelae he does not say, nor is it true. Tacitus relates the same of the Germans, On the Customs of the Germans, chapter 11 [9]: "For the rest, they think it unworthy of the greatness of the heavenly beings to confine the gods within walls, or to liken them to any appearance of the human countenance: they consecrate groves and woods, and call by the names of gods that hidden presence which they behold only with reverence." Yet a little before he had said, "They believe that God is present with them as they fight, and they carry into battle certain effigies and standards taken down [ p. 215 ] from the groves" (ibid., chapter 7). The groves, therefore, were not empty of every effigy or standard, but only of such as are formed into any appearance of the human countenance.

IX. Perhaps also, in place of the stones and columns which the Orientals erected, the Germans used living oaks, or the ancient trunks of oaks. For that the heavenly luminaries were adored even in trees we have shown from Maimonides. Hence Claudian, in his Praises of Stilicho: "So that far off, through the vast silences of the Hercynian forest, one may hunt in safety, in groves grim with ancient religion, and among oaks held in the place of a barbarian deity." Likewise, "oaks held by the Greeks as oracles" (Virgil, Georgics 2.16). And the Gauls in Lucan, book 3, line 412: "And the gloomy images of the gods lack art, and stand shapeless from hewn trunks. The very site, and the squalor of timber now rotting, strike men with awe." Maximus of Tyre teaches also that the Celts, that is the Gauls, worship Jupiter, whose sign is a most lofty oak. Procopius of Caesarea too, History of the Gothic War, book 4, affirms that the Abasgi, a people stretched from the Pontic sea to the roots of the Caucasus, "down to his own times," that is, those of the Emperor Justinian, "venerated woods and groves with superstition, and in their barbarian simplicity believed trees to be gods." Similarly also the first Huns, the expellers of the Goths from their ancient Scythian seats, whom Jordanes, On the Deeds of the Getae, chapter 24, carried away by a marvelous credulity, reports to have been procreated by unclean spirits from witch-women whom they called Aliorumnae; the remnants of these in Lithuania, the historians relate, worshipped trees down to late times, until King Jagiello cut them down with his own hand. Philostratus also relates that at Gades there was a most ancient temple dedicated to Hercules. In it, he says, there were no statues, but only unmarked altars [bōmoi asēmoi]. He shows, however, that the inhabitants of that place had stelae inscribed with unknown letters; and that these were four-cornered, like anvils. See his Life of Apollonius, book 5, chapter 1. Of the same temple Silius Italicus writes, "But no effigies or known images of the gods." And under the figure of Hercules, Curtius relates, book 4, chapter 1 [2], the Tyrians, from whom the Gaditanes descend, venerated Apollo, that is, the sun. So true is what Themistius says, Oration 15: "Before Daedalus, not only the workmanship of the Herms was four-square, but that of the other statues as well; but Daedalus, since he first parted the feet of the statues, was thought to fashion living and breathing works." Of which hereafter.

X. To these unwrought stones belong the baetyls [baitylia]. The first to mention them is Sanchuniathon, in Eusebius, in his Phoenician Theology. [ p. 216 ] "Moreover," he says, "the god Uranus devised baetyls, contriving by art ensouled stones." A most learned man judges that Sanchuniathon wrote not avanim nefashim, "living stones," but avanim neshafim (the letters pe and shin being transposed), that is, as he says, "anointed stones." For it is ridiculous, he says, to feign that Uranus made "animated stones." And it is truly ridiculous; yet it by no means follows from this that Sanchuniathon did not so write, since, partly from horrendous confusion, partly from a mixture of the most portentous trifles, everything which Eusebius produces from Sanchuniathon in that place is utterly absurd and ridiculous; nor are these things recorded by him for any other end than to make plain how absurd they were, and how abhorrent from all theology, nay, from reason itself. Further, Uranus devised those stones that they might be at hand to help him in the war which he was setting on foot against Saturn for the recovery of his kingdom; for which purpose he had need of living stones rather than anointed ones. It is also very well known that the ancients feigned stones of this kind to be ensouled [empsychoi], no less than they feigned the Daedalian statues to be self-moving [autokinēta]. Hesychius draws the word in another direction: "Baitylos: so was called the stone that was given to Cronus in place of Zeus." A very well-known fable. Whence the proverb, "You would swallow even a baetyl," which Erasmus explains from this place of Hesychius in his Adages. "Baetylus," he says, "is the name of that rock wrapped in swaddling bands which Saturn devoured in place of Jupiter."

XI. It is certain, however, that there were other stones destined for sacred uses and called baetyls, besides that one which was given to Saturn in the place of Jupiter. They are mentioned by the author of the Life of the philosopher Isidorus, in Photius, codex 242, whom Suidas writes to have been Damascius. He says that he himself saw "the baetyl moving through the air, sometimes hidden in garments, and sometimes even carried in the hands of its attendant [therapeuōn]; and the name of him who attended upon the baetyl was Eusebius." Andreas Schottus, a man otherwise learned, interprets those words quite absurdly: "the baetyl was moved in the air, sometimes covered with garments, sometimes also carried in the hands of its physician. The name of the physician who carried the baetyl was Eusebius." For what has a physician to do with a baetyl? Schoolboys know what it is to attend upon the gods [therapeuein tous theous], and what the service rendered to the gods [hē peri tous theous therapeia] means. That Eusebius was an impostor who ministered to the baetyl in its rites; who sometimes carried it in his hands, and sometimes feigned that it moved itself in the air. "The name of him who attended the baetyl was Eusebius": the name of him who ministered to the baetyl in its sacred rites was Eusebius. Julius Pollux, Onomasticon, book 1, on occupations: "The names of those who attend upon the gods: servants [therapeutai] of the gods, priests, temple-keepers." Damascius himself teaches, moreover, how that Eusebius religiously worshipped the baetyl, and drew oracles out of it. And this stone was one of those ensouled [empsychoi] stones; which Isidorus asserted to be moved and as it were animated by a demon, not of the worst sort: "For there is a certain demon that moves it, neither of the harmful kind, nor of those excessively bound to matter." For in the course of time, wherever an idol of this kind [ p. 217 ] was erected, the demons straightway took possession of it, as we shall afterwards show. Damascius also, a little later, records other stones dedicated to the Sun, which he calls synodiatai [companions of the way].

XII. It must be confessed, however, that the ancient idolaters were accustomed to anoint these stones, so that they might be called no less anointed than living. Clement, Stromata 7: "worshipping every stone, the so-called glistening [liparos] stone." And Arnobius, of himself, while he was still a heathen: "If ever I caught sight of a smoothed stone, dressed with the ointment of olive oil, I would fawn upon it, as though a present power dwelt within it."

XIII. The first, so far as I know, was that admirable man Joseph Scaliger, who most acutely conjectured that these stones were called baetyls from the stone which Jacob erected at Bethel, or from Bethel itself, as the place where he erected it was called from that time; whence others judge that the whole practice of erecting rude stones for superstitious uses proceeded from the perverse imitation [kakozēlia] of Satan. Jacob's deed is recorded in Gen. 28:18, 19. He took the stone which he had made his pillow, and set it up as a matstsebah; "for a title," says the Vulgate interpreter; a stele, say the Greeks. From yatsab, "he set up," comes matstsebah, "a pillar," as we said before; the same word which is employed in the prohibition of idolatry, Lev. 26:1; Deut. 7:5, 12:3. A rude stone found by chance in the field, erected in this manner, he anointed with oil; and he, first of mortals, pronounced to God in precise words a solemnly conceived vow. Among other things he promises that this stone shall hereafter be to him the house of God, Gen. 28:22; that is, that he would there solemnly perform the worship of God, when he should have at hand things to offer to God, of all of which he was then destitute. Afterwards, admonished by God, he discharged his pledge, chapter 35, verses 6, 7.

XIV. But whether Jacob did rightly or otherwise in erecting a pillar, the interpreters inquire. Tostatus [Abulensis] gives two answers. First, that this was lawful before the law, and afterwards prohibited. Second, that Jacob erected this monument for the memory of the event, not that he might adore it. In his opinion very many others acquiesce. Without doubt it was lawful for pious men, before the law was given, to erect altars in any place whatever, and to offer sacrifices upon them. And that Jacob erected this stone in memory of the angelic vision granted to him in that place, and did not employ it in religious worship, is evident from this, that a long time afterwards, admonished by God of his vow, he built an altar in the same place, chapter 35, verse 7. The Latin interpreter renders matstsebah by "title" from the accustomed practice of inscribing titles upon sacred stones of this kind, indicating to which god they were dedicated. So Damascius: "He showed us also letters written upon the stone," the baetyl, namely, "colored over with the pigment which they call tingabarine [cinnabar]."

XV. From this deed of Jacob, who was likewise renewing in memory a vision of the true God, there afterwards flowed the superstitious custom of erecting columns to the feigned epiphanies [epiphaneiai] of the gods. Of the epiphanies of the gods very many make mention. Dionysius of Halicarnassus, book 2, chapter 68: "It is well worth [ p. 218 ] relating also the epiphany of the goddess, which she displayed to the virgins unjustly accused"; "that is memorable, how the goddess, appearing, brought help to the imperiled virgins." To which he subjoins a refutation [elenxis] of the Epicureans, "all those who practice the atheistic philosophies, if indeed they must even be called philosophies, ripping to pieces all the epiphanies of the gods," mocking the presences of the gods, as Cicero expounds that word. Athenaeus too, book 12, from Carystius of Pergamum, relates that Demetrius of Phalerum observed with religious worship the epiphany of his brother Himeraeus, who had been put to death by Antipater; which kindled great odium against him among the Athenians. For after he had feigned, as it seems, that his dead brother had appeared to him, he pursued him with religious worship, and so introduced a foreign demon [xenos daimōn], which was unlawful at Athens, Acts 17:18. Of these epiphanies Cicero, in the second book On the Nature of the Gods, chapter 2: "And so both among our own people and among the rest, the worship of the gods and the sanctities of religion grow day by day greater and better. And this comes to pass not at random, nor by chance, but because the gods often declare their own presence." And let us know from what workshop proceeded that festival among Christians which they call Epiphany; concerning which a certain priest was in doubt whether it were male or female, but intrepidly pronounced that it had been a great saint.

XVI. Moreover, the Jews affirm that the Phoenicians afterwards worshipped that Bethel-stone; and it is very probable that those rude rocks which they erected to their gods were called baetyls [Baitylia] from Bethel.

XVII. In this manner, then, the worship of the sun grew: to the simple adoration were added columns and Baetylic stones; to which there came first anointing, then the offering of fruits and flowers, and finally sacrifices of animals.

XVIII. It did indeed come to pass, in the process of time, that the sun himself was worshipped in statues formed after the appearance of the human countenance. Of this kind was the Colossus of Rhodes, seventy cubits high. Macrobius too records, Saturnalia, book 1, chapter 23, that the Egyptians and Assyrians were accustomed to carry in procession an image of the sun, holding in its left hand a thunderbolt and ears of grain, and raising its right hand with a whip after the manner of a charioteer. And Pliny, book 34, chapter 7: "Zenodorus," he says, "made a Colossus intended as an image of the emperor Nero, one hundred and ten feet in length; which, when the crimes of that emperor had been condemned, was dedicated to the veneration of the sun." This was done by Vespasian, as Suetonius testifies, Vespasian, chapter 18: "He rewarded the restorer of the Colossus," he says, "with a notable gratuity and a great fee." When it was restored, a head of the sun bearing seven rays was set upon it in the place of Nero's; but this was afterwards removed by Commodus, who set his own head upon it, as Herodian testifies, chapter 48: "Cutting off the head of the greatest colossal statue, which the Romans venerate, bearing the likeness of the Sun, he set up his own." Scaliger supplies the defective passage with the words "and putting his own in its place." But this was not done until after dead men had been admitted into the number of the gods, and some Apollo or other had been substituted for the sun.

XIX. As, therefore, Jeroboam erected the golden calves, lest the people, destitute of every visible sign of the divine presence, should return to Jerusalem to perform the true worship of God, [ p. 219 ] not perhaps without the utmost damage and ruin of his kingdom; so Satan, providing for his own kingdom, which he had obtained in the world by such various arts and manifold fraud, lest idolaters, placed in dangers and anxious for help, attending to the voice of nature herself crying out to the Lord of nature, should begin "to seek the Lord, if perhaps they might feel after him and find him, though indeed he is not far from every one of us," as the apostle speaks, and so withdraw themselves from his rule, superadded to that other error this new one, by which, raised up to a false hope, they could not be removed from their wicked prejudice.

XX. Clement of Alexandria sets forth this progress of superstition excellently in his Exhortation to the Greeks: "The Scythians," he says, "in antiquity adored a scimitar, the Arabs a stone, the Persians a river; and of other men, those who were still more ancient set up conspicuous timbers and placed columns of stones, which were also called xoana, because they were scraped and polished out of their material. In Icarus, certainly, the image of Diana was an unworked piece of wood, and that of Cithaeronian Juno at Thespiae a hewn trunk; and that of Samian Juno (as Aethlius says) was at first a plank, but afterwards, when Procleus was Archon, it was fashioned into the form of a statue. But after statues began to be formed, they were called bretē, from brotos [mortal], because they were like men. And at Rome, in antiquity, the writer Varro says that the statue of Mars was a spear, since craftsmen had not yet arrived at this showy indeed, but wicked, artifice. But after the art flourished, the error increased. That they made, therefore, out of stones and timbers images like to men, is now plain." Thus he; and he then recounts in order who in each several place stood forth as the first fabricators of images.

XXI. And this was the first success and good fortune [eutychia] of the Satanic war against the human race, destitute of divine revelations and left to itself. This was the origin of the total defection from the foundation of natural theology. Here the prince of darkness laid the foundations of that empire which he exercised far and wide over the greatest part of mankind through an innumerable series of years.
Or, on the origin and progress of idolatry, chapter 7.

Chapter argument: The further progress of idolatry; polytheism [polytheismos] ending in antitheism [antitheismos]; what Hellenism is; its beginnings uncertain and obscure; the opinion of the author of the book called the Wisdom of Solomon; gods and goddesses from flattery; the mother of Demetrius worshipped by Adimantus, and Lamia, the harlot of Poliorcetes, by the Athenians and Thebans; Demetrius's gibe against the Athenians; the opinion of Lactantius, and of Eusebius; the rationale of apotheosis [apotheōsis]; the Jews feign that Terah the father of Abraham was the first statue-maker [agalmatopoios]; the origin [ p. 220 ] of the fable; the consensus of the ancient Christians in it; the testimony of Epiphanius, of Suidas, of John of Antioch; Haran died before his father; not the first since the founding of the world, as Epiphanius would have it; Terah a holy man; Pindar ascribes the invention of automata [automata] to the Rhodians; Isidore to Prometheus; more ascribe it to Daedalus; why he was said to have made self-moving statues [agalmata autokinēta]; the statue of Memnon in Philostratus; and many other things.

I. Satan had not yet carried through to their final issue the things which he was impiously contriving against God and men. From the success expounded above there came upon him a keener and loftier lust of occupying the throne of God. In Hellenism, that is, the worship of dead men, he obtains his wish. Under the pretext of that superstition, using no roundabout way, he set himself forth as the very object of religious worship to wretched mortals. And so polytheism ends in antitheism. But it was not until many years, perhaps even ages, had run their course that this pinnacle of impiety and spiritual wickedness was reached. For the human race did not immediately upon the first defection procure for itself gods of human form [theoi anthrōpophyeis]. In the worship of these Hellenism consisted. Grown weary of the heavenly luminaries, which, being driven by an inviolable law, could not satisfy the vanity and fluctuation of the human mind, they chose gods like themselves, easy, propitious, whose manners they might imitate and whose honors they might share. Although, therefore, by Hellenism may rightly be understood a certain collection [syllogē] of false religions, and a confusion in the worship of heaven, of the sun, and of the other luminaries, of demons, of dead men, of images and fictitious phantasms, in short, the whole idolomania of the ancients; yet we shall here consider it separately from Sabaism, or the superstition consisting in the worship of the heavenly luminaries, insofar as it brought in the religion of dead men, and of images fashioned after the appearance of the human countenance.

II. All confess that the origins of this superstition are doubtful, nay, most uncertain. After we have related the opinions of others, which either the dignity of their authors or their own verisimilitude has placed in repute, we shall add some things which seem still to be wanting for drawing the truth up out of the well.

III. Well known is the opinion of the author of the book called the Wisdom of Solomon, chapter 14, verses 13-20. "For neither were they from the beginning," he says, "neither shall they be for ever. For by the vainglory of men they entered into the world, and therefore a speedy end of them has been devised. For a father, worn with untimely mourning, having made an image of his child quickly taken away, now honored as a god him who was then a dead man, and delivered to those under his authority mysteries and rites. Then in process of time the ungodly custom, having grown strong, was kept as a law, and by the commands of tyrants the graven things were worshipped." To this purpose he adds more. And assuredly the things which afterwards came to pass give credit to this opinion. Flattery made gods and goddesses without number. Let us bring forward an example or two. These are the words of Athenaeus, book 6: "Of the flatterers of king Demetrius, those about Adimantus [ p. 221 ] of Lampsacus built a temple at Thria and set up statues, under the title of Phila Aphrodite, and called the place Philaeum, from Phila the mother of Demetrius; as Dionysius the son of Tryphon says."
