Also the historian Demochares, the cousin of Demosthenes, wrote that the whole Athenian people consecrated temples to Lamia, the harlot of Demetrius Poliorcetes, and pursued her with divine honors, and that the Thebans did likewise; while Demetrius himself detested the foul adulation, and openly said that "no Athenian citizen of great and lofty soul would ever be found among the dead."

IV. There are, therefore, those who judge that it was human affections that first set up images to the dead, lest they should be buried in oblivion along with the common crowd; and that thence, in the course of time, some seized the occasion of superstition in their worship. There are others who hold that the same superstition first enrolled them in the number of the gods, and only afterwards set up images to them. Lactantius refers the beginning of erecting images, and of worshipping dead men with divine honors, to the devotion of foolish peoples toward their dead kings; so that to set up an image to someone, and to enroll him among the gods, appear to be the same thing. Eusebius, in the first book of his Chronicle, narrates things resembling the truth: "Of the tribe of Japheth was born Serug" (an oversight [parorama] which we have noted), "who first began Hellenism and the doctrine of idolatry; for he and those with him honored with statues upon stelae the men of former times, whether warriors or leaders, or doers of anything in their life worthy of remembrance for courage or virtue, as being their own forefathers" (we have taught above that idolatrous stelae were erected long before it came into anyone's mind to pursue dead men with divine honors), "and they worshipped them as gods, and sacrificed to them. But the men who came after, not knowing the intention of their ancestors, that they had honored them with memorials only, as forefathers and discoverers of good things, honored them as heavenly gods." But what he adds in that place concerning the rationale and form of apotheosis is most worthy of consideration by those who know the ecclesiastical Diptychs, and the depraved superstition which has flowed from them. "The manner of the apotheosis," he says, "was as follows. In their priestly books their names were enrolled after death; and at that season they celebrated a festival for them, saying that their souls had gone to the islands of the blessed, and were no longer judged or burned with fire." The names of the dead being inserted in the sacred books, they instituted festival days, anniversaries namely, for their memory, believing that their souls had been received into the seats of the blessed, and were not tormented by any purgatorial fire.

V. The Jews feign that Terah, the father of Abraham, was the first fashioner of images and statues. With this fable they serve the no less fabulous histories of Abraham cast into the fire for not adoring the image of Nimrod. It is marvelous, indeed, by how great a consensus of the ancients this rotten figment is approved. Epiphanius: "Nahor begets Terah; from this point arose the modeling of statues, [ p. 222 ] out of clay-working and the potter's science, through the craft of this Terah." Similarly Suidas: "Terah was a statue-maker [agalmatopoios], working images out of divers materials, and saying that these were gods and ought to be worshipped, as the authors of good things." And John of Antioch, Malalas, book 2: "For Terah was a statue-maker and modeler, making gods out of stones and woods, and selling them; and he introduced to men the error of statues and of idolatry through likenesses of their ancestors, especially of those who had discovered letters and the arts" (of whom in truth not one had then existed anywhere).

VI. Scripture teaches us that Haran, the son of Terah, died before him. Epiphanius notes that this then for the first time since the founding of the world came to pass, as a punishment of the impious father. "No son," he says, "of the earlier men had ever yet died before his father, but the fathers, dying before their children, left their sons as their successors" (Against Heresies, book 1, chapter 1). To the instance of Abel he answers that he was snatched away by a violent death. But the learned man is manifestly deceived; for not only did the antediluvian Lamech die some years before the death of his father, but the same thing also befell Peleg, the great-great-grandfather of Terah himself, who met his fate about two hundred years before the death of his father Eber. But this was the common opinion of the ancients. For my part, I do not doubt that a signal injury is done by those fables to the memory of a pious and holy man, who had left his fatherland with the intention of going to Canaan (without doubt being divinely directed [chrēmatistheis], Gen. 11:31). For the accusation lacks all color; and it is plainly a Jewish figment. Pindar seems to ascribe some peculiar artifice of statue-making [agalmatourgia] to the Rhodians, Olympian Ode 7, lines 91-98.

"And the grey-eyed goddess herself bestowed on them the art to surpass all dwellers upon earth with their excellently laboring hands; and the roads bore works like to living and creeping things; and deep was their glory." The poet intimates that the Rhodians [ p. 223 ] were craftsmen of such a sort that they were wont to express living images of animals and creeping things, so that they seemed, as it were, to move upon the road and in their course. Isidore, Origines, book 8, affirms that Prometheus first fashioned images of men from clay, and that from him arose the art of erecting images and statues; and that for this cause the poets feigned that men were made by him.

VII. More celebrated is the opinion of those who ascribe this artifice to Daedalus. Hence the ancients called all images Daidala. Others write that he invented images self-moving and automatic [autokinēta kai automata]. These are also spoken of in the proverb, "the works of Daedalus" [ta Daidalou poiēmata]. Most of the ancients were of this opinion. As Plato, in the Meno: "Socrates: Because you have not turned your mind to the statues of Daedalus; but perhaps there are none among you. Meno: To what purpose do you say this? Socrates: Because these too, if they are not bound fast, run off and escape." Hence the statue of Hercules among the Tyrians, and that of Bacchus among the Chians, were fixed with chains in their seats, because they were believed to be self-moving. Aristotle also mentions it, Politics 1: "If each instrument," he says, "could accomplish its own work, either at command or perceiving beforehand, as they say the statues of Daedalus did." And this, they say, he effected by pouring quicksilver into the statues; which the same Aristotle shows in the first book On the Soul: a certain Philippus said that "Daedalus made the wooden Aphrodite move by pouring in molten silver," that is, quicksilver.

VIII. The occasion of the fable, without doubt, was that Daedalus first among the Greeks fashioned images after the appearance of the human countenance and figure, with the feet especially separated, and one advanced beyond the line of the other, as if they were preparing themselves to walk. So Tzetzes solves the riddle: "Before the times of Daedalus they used to fashion statues without hands, without feet, without eyes; but Daedalus himself first separated the hands and feet, articulated the fingers, and the eyelids and the rest; whence the tale has arisen that the Daedalian works move." So it is; the first images were unwrought stones [lithoi argoi], without hands, feet, or eyes; but when Daedalus had bestowed upon his figures all the things that pertain to the external form of man, the fable spread abroad that he had made self-moving images. Diodorus Siculus, Palaephatus, and others have similar accounts.

IX. Nay, that the statues fabricated by Daedalus were exceedingly rude Plato sufficiently indicates, Socrates in the Greater Hippias near the beginning; as the beginnings of all things, invented by the imperfect, are imperfect: "If indeed now, O Hippias," he says, "Bias should come to life again, he would incur laughter among you; just as [ p. 224 ] the sculptors of these times relate of Daedalus, that if he were now alive and should produce such works as those from which he obtained his fame and name, he would be ridiculous."

X. But it was a great thing to erect images after the figure of a man, with hands extended and feet separated; since, after the columns, stelae, rude rocks, and baetyls dedicated to the sun, moon, and stars, the most ancient images appear to have been of that sort which are exhibited in the monuments of the Egyptians, with no hands, or with hands glued to the sides, with feet joined, or rather with the figures ending in a base. Hence Philostratus, book 6, chapter 3 [4] of the Life of Apollonius, ascribing something peculiar to the statue of Memnon, says: "It is of black stone; and its two feet are joined together according to the statue-making in the time of Daedalus." Which words the translator renders thus: "The statue is fabricated of black stone, and touching the ground with both feet, according to the statuary art of Daedalus." Of which interpretation Scaliger employs the phrase "nothing sound" [ouden hygies], Animadversions on Eusebius, at number 1737; "having the feet joined," he himself renders. Similarly are interpreted the words of Apollodorus concerning the Palladium, "It was three cubits in size, with its feet joined together"; it was three cubits, "with feet joined," say some; "with feet so composed that it seemed to walk," say others. From the use of the word, at least in this matter, nothing can be gathered with certainty. But if the statue of Memnon had been fabricated with feet joined, that is, in truth, without any shape of feet at all, as the figures they call mummies show to have been commonly done among the Egyptians, there would be nothing peculiar in it, or memorable in that respect. Further, since it was of such a sort as were made in the time of Daedalus [epi Daidalōi], not before Daedalus, Philostratus seems to me to intimate clearly enough that it differed from the other Egyptian images in this, that it was fabricated with feet separated, after the Daedalian manner.

XI. And these are nearly all the things that occur among the ancients concerning the origin of Hellenism and of statue-making [agalmatopoiia]. To these more of the same kind could be added, if it had been resolved to relate everything, and not only what is sufficient. The things which seem to us to come nearest to the truth, or at least to be most like the probable, we shall state in the next place.
Or, on the rise and progress of idolatry, chapter 8.

Chapter argument: The gods sprung from men were all Orientals; the restorers of the human race after the deluge; the leaders of the Babylonian columns; Greece the workshop of the gods; to them the east was the abode of the gods above, the west of those below; eastern quarters sacred to the gods, western to the demons; the placement of idols in temples; Jupiter Capitolinus; the testimony of Porphyry; the fame [ p. 225 ] of things done in the east; the deeds of the gods; nectar a Babylonian wine; the first gods of the idolaters were themselves the first apostates from the true God; some memory of the names of the true God retained after the confusion of tongues; these names ascribed to idols; on the pronunciation of the Tetragrammaton; the fables of Sanchuniathon; Jehovah El Elyon [Hebrew: YHWH El Elyon], Iaō, Ia; Hallelu-Yah, Eleleu; Adoni; hieron, Ie, Yah; adamah, Hades; bohu, tohu, Thoth, Theoth, Theos, Bau; erev, Erebos; erets, Earth, and so on.

I. That all those who were first enrolled in the number of the gods lived in the east can be demonstrated by sure arguments. From that quarter of the globe, immediately upon the confusion of tongues, the leaders of tribes and families led out colonies into all parts of the earth. These, in the course of time, became anshei hashem, "men renowned in fame." And in whatever direction they took their journey, no one can doubt that the new flocks retained some fame, however slight and obscure, even of their great progenitors, namely Noah, Shem, Ham, and others. Recalling in memory the latter as the authors of their race, the former as the fortunate guides [hodēgoi] of their wanderings, since they were now poured out into every superstition, the traditions concerning their condition and deeds being gradually augmented, as is wont to happen, they feigned to themselves that these men had possessed some divine quality, I know not what [theion], above the common lot of the human race. And since they knew that they had passed their life on earth and in the company of all, they felt it reasonable to suppose either that they had descended from heaven to accomplish illustrious exploits among men, or that, by the merits of their deeds, they had after death been in some manner carried up into the heavens.

II. The Greeks were the chief fabricators of gods; so much so that Greece is not unfittingly wont to be called the fatherland and workshop of the gods, and that superstition which consists in their religious worship is the Hellenism of which we treat. To them the east was the abode of the gods above; but the west, and whatever turned toward the northern regions of the world, they called the lower regions [inferi]. Those who crossed over into those parts of the globe they supposed to have sailed to the world below, or in some manner to have passed through it; for no other reason than that they had received all their gods from the east. The river Lethe is in Gallaecia in Spain; Avernus in Italy; the Cimmerians were Scythians by the Maeotic marsh, afterwards called Huns; and there were certain others in Italy itself. Hence in religious worship "the eastern quarters were assigned to the gods, the western to the demons," as Porphyry testifies in his book on the Cave of the Nymphs of Ithaca in Homer. And so although, as we have taught above, the ancient temples dedicated to the sun had their sacred doors toward the south, the sacred places set up for these idols always had eastern entrances, and the images looking toward the east, which the worshippers, entering, venerated while facing the west. The sole exception was Jupiter Capitolinus, who, having been placed from ancient times with his face looking toward the west, was, by the counsel of the Haruspices, turned toward the east in the consulship of Cicero, as he himself testifies in his Oration against Catiline addressed to the Quirites. The same Porphyry teaches us these things: "Since nearly [ p. 226 ] all temples have their statues and their entrances turned toward the east, while those who enter look toward the west, when, standing face to face with the statues, they offer honors and worship to the gods." In order to oppose that superstition, it was the custom of certain of the ancient Christians, entering their churches toward the east, to bow themselves with no less ceremony, and to adore something, I know not what.

III. The first gods of the nations sprung from men, therefore, whom (after the luminaries of heaven had been adored for some ages) they enrolled in the number of the gods, were the leaders of families and tribes, either those who became famous in the east, or those who were guides out of the east to peoples seeking new homes. The fame of these men, mixed with fables, gradually passed into belief, and their memory into worship. And so, whatever they had received, by fame or by any tradition whatever, concerning the life, deeds, manners, and wars of those great men, things true and false, for the most part false mingled with true, were transferred into the exploits and acts of divinities, as though they had fallen down from heaven. Nectar itself, the drink of the gods, was a Babylonian wine. "Chaereas says that there is produced in Babylon the wine called nectar," says Athenaeus, Deipnosophists, book 1; "Chaereas relates that there is a wine in Babylon which the natives call nectar." Hence nectar is the divine drink [poma theion], which Suidas foolishly supposes to be called as it were neoktar, "because it makes young [neoi] those who drink it." And the author of the Etymologicum: "Nectar, the drink of the gods, as it were that which keeps those who drink it in youth." Others report other Greek origins of the word, all of them inept, since it came into Greece from the east together with the gods themselves. Whence Strabo prudently warns that Greek etymologies are not to be sought in the appellations of barbarian nations. That Noah was Saturn, Janus, and Bacchus; Ham, Jupiter Hammon; Japheth, Iapetus and Neptune; Shem, Pluto and Dis, has long since been shown by others. To bring in here the metamorphosis of these and others out of the most common fables is needless. And the history of those who afterwards augmented the number of the gods in Greece itself is too recent and too well known for me to need to dwell upon it.

IV. In obscure traditions, then, and in the slender and corrupted fame of celebrated men, who either had first restored the human race after the deluge, or had led the Babylonian columns across into the various regions of the globe, Hellenism laid its foundations. These men the idolaters exalted to heaven, first by admiration and praises, then by superstitious worship, although the greater part of them had stood forth as the authors and standard-bearers of the most shameful defection from the true God. Hence in all that confusion, which we shall show to have crept gradually into the superstition of the nations, many things are found in which almost all unanimously conspired. For fame handed down to posterity many things concerning those great founders of the various stocks. After, therefore, superstitious men [ p. 227 ] had raised their great progenitors to heaven by vows and a certain vain opinion, partly lest their memory and praises should fall out of men's minds, partly that they might set them present before the senses, they set up to them images fabricated after the form of man (whereas, while they worshipped the heavenly luminaries, they had been content with columns and rude rocks). And this worship of images also became universal, for the reasons brought forward above.

V. Further: after the confusion of tongues, the builders of the tower, dispersed hither and thither, retained in memory, in whatever fashion, some names of the true God, to leave them, as received, to their sons and grandsons. But these, in the course of time, not knowing what or whom those great and venerable names denoted, imposed them all upon those new gods whom they had adopted for themselves. So Sanchuniathon, among the progenitors of the Phoenicians who were afterwards enrolled in the number of the gods, records Elioun, or the Most High [Hypsistos], that is, Elyon; he writes that this one begot heaven and earth: and Ilos, who is nothing else than El. YHWH was the name of Jove, and Jova, and Jupiter; that name Sanchuniathon renders by Ieuō, when he relates that he learned many things "from Hierombalus the priest of the god Ieuō." Diodorus indeed, book 1, says that the God to whom Moses ascribed his laws was called Iaō; with which agrees the oracle in Macrobius, Saturnalia 1: "Declare that Iaō is the highest god of all." By which Satan endeavors to ascribe the incommunicable name to other idols. Eusebius, in the Demonstration of the Gospel, book 4, last chapter: "Joshua is the salvation of Iaō, that is, the saving help of God." Hence it is probable that in that other place Eusebius wrote, from Sanchuniathon, Iaō, not Ieuō. Iaou is found in Clement of Alexandria, Stromata 5, perhaps also erroneously for Iaō; and Iaōia in Origen, but corruptly without doubt. "From the Hebrew scriptures," he says, "they took the name Iaōia, as he is named among the Hebrews, and Sabaoth, and Adonaios, and Eloaios; but the names taken from the scriptures are surnames of one and the same God; which the enemies of God, not understanding, as they themselves also confess, supposed that Iaō was one being, and Sabaoth another" (Against Celsus, book 6). He speaks of the heretics, who had drawn their portentous figments from the sloughs of heathenism. For the rest, whether they learned those names, the epithets of the one true God, from the scriptures or from ancient tradition, the matter is the same. Out of each single name a new divinity is constructed. Moreover, Iaōia is read corruptly for Iaō Ia. Reynolds first suspected the fault, On Idolatry, book 2, chapter 3. Sandford restored the true reading, in the first book of his On the Descent of Christ into Hell; after him Grotius, on Matthew 22, and others.

VI. And hence it appears, a thing to be noted in passing, that the pronunciation of the Tetragrammaton was once known. The Jews today, and for many ages past, are ignorant of it; and so they have utterly forgotten the name of the God who was once theirs. For they are so far cast off by him that they cannot even call upon his name. This was the name which he first made plain to them [ p. 228 ] when he adopted the whole nation as a peculiar people and church for himself, Exod. 3. For although the name YHWH was known to all the faithful from the founding of the world, yet God had used it in no confirmation of his covenant, in no promise, but only the name El Shaddai. After, therefore, that nation ceased to be the people of God and the church, it either voluntarily cast away all memory of that name of God which he graciously revealed in that covenant transaction by which he adopted them as a peculiar people, or has been punished with the forgetting of it.

VII. Next, El Elyon ha-El, the mighty God, the Most High, was converted into Helios. Adoni was Adonis; Evan and Evius took their rise from Eheie [Ehyeh, "I AM"]; Eleleu from Hallelu-Yah. That cry, says Plutarch in his Theseus, "they are accustomed to shout aloud when hastening and exulting, and when singing the paean." Hieron, a word which regards all sacred things, they write is derived from Ie, which signifies nothing at all in Greek; nor is it anything other than Yah. From adamah came Hades, after God had said to Adam, "you shall return el ha-adamah," that is, to the ground, that is, "into Hades." Jupiter Sabasius is from Jehovah Sabaoth. I should weary the reader if I were to recount everything of this kind that occurs.

VIII. Sanchuniathon feigns that the names of the principal things which are recorded in the creation of the world were the appellations of men, of wise men, of kings; and thence he begins his long fables. Hesiod wove his Theogony from no other source. From erets is "Earth," the greatest goddess among the English and other peoples of Germany, as Tacitus is our authority, Customs of the Germans, chapter 11 [40]: "In common," he says, "they worship Aerthus, that is, Mother Earth." Tohu and bohu are Thoth and Theoth (whence also Theos), and Bau. Erev is Erebos; and from that order of words which is found in Gen. 1, "and there was evening and there was morning, one day" [Hebrew: vayehi erev vayehi voker yom echad], many peoples preferred the night to the day. Of the Germans Tacitus says: "They compute not the number of days, but of nights; thus they make appointments, thus they fix engagements; the night seems to lead the day." And in truth it did lead it; for light came out of darkness. The same custom obtains among the English to this day. For a week we say "seven-night," and "fortnight," that is, "fourteen nights," for two weeks. And the ancient Britons also, whom we now call the Welsh, with whom a week is "Withos" [wythnos], that is, eight nights; and two weeks "Pymthec nos," that is, fifteen nights; which is also a monument of Celtic origin. The same custom existed among the ancient Gauls, flowing from the same fountain of traditions, and not, as Caesar would have it, because they supposed themselves sprung from father Dis. Of this kind were the first beginnings of Hellenism, or of that confused superstition which invaded almost the whole globe of the earth.

Chapter argument: British idolatry. The most ancient inhabitants of the island unknown, and whence it was called Britain. The origins of peoples and nations obscure. The causes of that obscurity. How great the ignorance in tracing them out. The ignorance and malice of the Greeks and Latins. The ancients buried the Jewish antiquities under lies; hence slender credit is due to them in other matters. The peculiar causes of our ignorance of British origins: the island situation; the ambition and cunning of their wise men in concealing things past. The islands of Europe occupied by the posterity of Japheth (Gen. 10:5). From the first settlement to Caesar's arrival in this island, all things wrapped in darkness. The testimony of Caesar. The interior parts of the island unknown to the Gauls. He held the inland inhabitants to be sprung from the soil [Greek: autochthonas]; Tacitus, a medley of very many peoples: both ineptly. The attempts of Camden and Boxhorn to prove that the Britons and the Gauls used the same language and were one and the same people. Gomer the eldest son of Japheth; from him the Cimbri. The Britons call themselves Komeroi or Cymry: Gomerites or Cimbri. What gomer [Hebrew: gomer] signifies. The Britons are Cimbri. Another etymon of the name Cymro: Camber the son of Brutus, whence Cambria. Whence Britain was so named; the derivations of various writers rejected: Prytaneia; Brytona a Cretan nymph; a Britannia in Gaul; Bridanium; Prid Cain. Very many judge it so called from Brutus the Trojan; that opinion assailed by Camden. Camden's own conjecture: Brith, Tania. Learned men assent to it; reasons against it. Bochart's conjecture the most probable: this island was known to the Tyrians, and was called barat anac [Hebrew: barat anak], that is, the field of tin. Defense of this conjecture against Hornius. British idolatry. The priests of the British rites very well known. Origen writes that the Druids worshipped one God; that god was the Sun. Tacitus makes them the same as the Gauls. Their custom of pouring out prayers with hands spread toward heaven. Andraste and Andate, Astarte, the Moon; Boadicea's vow pronounced to her. The gods of the Gauls in Caesar were received from the Greeks of Massilia, and unknown to the Britons. The most ancient Gauls were without images. Their gods: Teutates, Hesus, and Taranis. Teutates from Diw Taith, the guide of the way; the Sun, and his worship. Hesus, [Hebrew: ezuz, "strong"], and Haud. Taranis, whence taran, thunder, among the Welsh; Thunder, Donder, Thursday. The images of these gods. Their priests the Druids. The carrying of their superstition into Gaul and Germany; its rise in Britain. Whence the Druids were so called: Druys king of the Celts, whom Bardus succeeded; Drw a magician; Dru and Trou a truth-speaking diviner. The true etymology is from the oak [Greek: apo tou dryos]; Drw in British is an oak. Groves of oaks the seat of the superstition; the same men called Saronidae for the same reason. Who the Bards were; their office; their memory and name among the Cambro-Britons; Strabo censured. The peculiar dress of the Druids: in sacred rites a white garment. Their academy and studies; the flocking of young men to their instruction; its methods, privileges, and authority. Their manner of teaching in their schools; the time of learning; the promotion of learners. The philosophy they taught; magic; what sort of medicine they practiced; their peculiar theology, whose chief dogma was the immortality of souls and the end of the world. They taught also rhetoric, geography, astronomy, natural philosophy, and ethics. The order of the Druids among themselves; their authority in the commonwealth: under the pretext of religion they mixed themselves in all affairs; the excommunication of offenders. Their sacred rites: human sacrifice [Greek: anthropothysia]; two kinds of human victims; the manner of the human sacrifice, out of Diodorus, Caesar, Strabo, Dio, and others. The Romans' attempt to abolish the superstition fruitless. Britain set free by the gospel. The beginnings of the Christian religion in this island: Joseph of Arimathea; Claudia; Pudens. Concerning King Lucius; the epistolary exchange between him and Eleutherius; Bede the author of the story; his error in assigning the time; Henry of Erfordia; Ado. [ p. 230 ] The success of the Eleutherian letter according to Geoffrey and Leland. Whether Lucius took up an apostolate; Camden yields to prejudice. The falsity of the story demonstrated. A compendium of the history of Britain under the Romans: conquered by Claudius; reduced into the form of a province; almost cut off by Suetonius Paulinus; pacified by Agricola. The royal name and authority taken away throughout the whole province; in the time of the Emperor Commodus there was no king in Britain, therefore no Lucius. Baronius' defense of the story examined. No Lucius held a kingdom, either within or beyond Hadrian's wall. Who that Lucius was. The letter of Eleutherius a forgery.

I. That idolatrous superstition was carried into these British islands after the flood, together with their first inhabitants, is as certain as it is uncertain who those first inhabitants were, or whence the islands themselves came to be called Britannia, or the British isles. For the first origins of almost all peoples and nations have been so obscured by the intervals of time, the vicissitudes of human affairs, the barbarism of the most ancient ages, and the want of writers in the ages that immediately followed the first, together with the audacity of empty fable-mongers, that the most excellent minds for the most part sweat in vain in digging them out. There are very many things, accordingly, of which learned men ingenuously confess themselves utterly ignorant; but as for some things which they fancy they understand admirably, I fear they are in truth ignorant of them with greater loss to themselves and to the truth than of those things which they feel they do not know at all; since the approving of false things as true compels us to swerve further from the truth than does mere ignorance of the truth. In most matters, beyond what we learn from the Greeks and Romans, mere conjectures bear rule. But with what fidelity, scrupulousness, and skill in ancient affairs the most celebrated writers of those nations conducted themselves in relating the first beginnings of peoples, we have sufficiently ascertained from those matters concerning which we possess the most certain knowledge from another quarter. No one is ignorant with what lies they strive, as it were with one mouth, to overwhelm the Jewish origins, namely with the most monstrous fables, stitched together out of vain traditions, or such as they spun out of their own brain like spiders' webs. But that I should yield credit, in uncertain matters at whose every turning-post I stand doubtful in mind, to men whom I have found to have lied splendidly, nay prodigiously, in all those things of which certain knowledge has come to me, I am not easily induced. There is therefore no reason to wonder that the origins of the British peoples lie hidden in darkness, since the same murky night presses upon the first beginnings of almost all nations, those alone excepted which the sacred page has placed beyond the hazard of controversy. Yet this is peculiar to the Britons, along with a few others, that to the common calamity a twofold disadvantage has been added, each of them only too well fitted to increase the darkness of the most ancient times. For since they were islanders, shut out from commerce with the rest of the world, they had opportunity neither of communicating the memory of their name to others, nor of procuring for themselves the traditions or the information of others. And then, if it be lawful to believe so great an enormity, after the use of letters became known, those who were accounted the most sharp-sighted and wise among them, in order that they themselves might remain objects of perpetual admiration, and that nothing might be capable of being known by anyone without [ p. 231 ] their assistance (a design which Cicero, in his oration for Murena, shows the ancient jurisconsults also to have entertained), refused to commit anything of antiquity to writing; and therefore, since very many things, if not all, that are necessary for the accurate investigation of the origins of these nations have been utterly wiped out by oblivion, it will be enough for me to press the traces of those things that survive, whether true or most resembling truth.

II. That the inhabitants of these islands drew their origin from the posterity of Japheth, all agree, relying on the words of the Holy Spirit, Gen. 10:5, "By these," that is, the sons of Japheth, "were the isles of the Gentiles divided in their lands, every one after his tongue, after their families, in their nations." From these words down to the times of Caesar (how vast a void of ages!) one may borrow that line of the poet: "Wherever I look, there is nothing but sea and air, the one swollen with waves, the other threatening with clouds" (Ovid, Tristia 1.2.23-24). Whatever traces of the truth remain must be dug out by conjecture. For although it is most certain that these islands, contrary to the opinion of the most learned Camden, were known to the ancient Greeks and to the Tyrians, yet no one has been found who committed anything about them to the memory of the ages. "What mortals first inhabited Britain," says Tacitus, "whether natives or new-comers, is, as is usual among barbarians, little ascertained" (Agricola, ch. 11). And in what obscurity all British affairs lay wrapped, even for himself, a man of the greatest sagacity, furnished with the advantages of all the knowledge of the whole world, and spending several years in neighboring Gaul, Caesar himself testifies, book iv of the Gallic War, ch. 20: "He judged," he says, "that it would be of great use to him, if he could but visit the island, observe the race of men, and become acquainted with the localities, the harbors, and the landing-places; all of which were almost wholly unknown to the Gauls. For no one readily goes there except traders; and even to these nothing is known except the sea-coast and those regions which lie opposite the Gauls." In his fifth book, ch. 12, he makes the inland inhabitants aborigines, or sprung from the soil [Greek: autochthonas]. "The interior part of Britain," he says, "is inhabited by those who, according to received tradition, were born in the island itself"; as though, forsooth, the earth had sprouted men like mushrooms or blites. Tacitus speaks more fully in the Life of Agricola, ch. 11: "For the rest, what mortals originally inhabited Britain, whether natives or immigrants, is, as among barbarians, little ascertained. The physical characteristics are various, and from these inferences are drawn. For the red hair of the inhabitants of Caledonia, and their large limbs, point to a Germanic origin. The swarthy faces of the Silures, their hair generally curled, and Spain lying over against them, make it credible that Iberians of old crossed over and occupied those seats. Those nearest the Gauls are also like them; whether from the enduring force of their origin, or because, where the lands run forward toward each other in opposite directions, the position of the sky gave its character to their bodies. Yet, to one estimating the matter as a whole, it is credible that the Gauls occupied the neighboring soil." It is absurd in him to hint that these islands were inhabited by an offscouring of the neighboring nations; he comes nearer the truth in supposing the inhabitants to have crossed over from Gaul. For it is long since most of the more learned have gone over to the opinion of Camden, who asserts that the Gauls and the Britons [ p. 232 ] sprang from the same stock, and that the latter crossed over hither from Gaul. He contends also that the language of both peoples was the same, although Caesar and Tacitus hardly agree to this, arguing from various words which, while the ancient writers testify them to have been in use among the Gauls, the Welsh, the genuine remnant of the ancient Britons, retain to this day. The same web Boxhorn has very recently undertaken to weave through to the end, in his Gallic Origins, with the help of Davies' Lexicon.

III. We have said that the inhabitants of the European islands drew their origin from the sons and posterity of Japheth. Now his eldest son was called Gomer. From him very many judge the Cimbri to have sprung. And indeed the Welsh, who alone are the genuine descendants of the ancient Britons, call themselves Kymroes, that is, Gomerites, their nation Cumro, and their language Cumraeg. Hence Camden contends that the ancient Britons were Cimbri, and crossed over hither from neighboring Gaul, since the Gauls, the Cimbri, and the Celts are one and the same. Add that Gomer signifies "finishing," or "one who brings to an end," from gamar [Hebrew: gomer], "to finish"; as though there had been something of an omen in the giving of the name [Greek: onomatothesia], and it had been foretold that he would occupy the ends of the earth. And these things indeed are highly probable, and I shall embrace them until someone teaches better. Yet it ought not to be passed over in silence that very many of the Britons hold that they are called Kymroes or Cambri, and that "Cambri" comes from Cambria, the name imposed upon that part of the island which fell by lot to Camber the son of Brutus. And all these things we leave in the same place where we found them, content to have related those points for which nothing more certain is available.

IV. So much for the inhabitants themselves. The name of Britain is most ancient. Many derivations of it are extant, devised by many men; very many of them false, if not all; some ridiculous. Eliot, a noble and learned man, in the dictionary which Cooper enlarged and published, supposes Britain to have been named from the Greek word prytaneia. The prytanis at Athens was the governor of the market, and the prytaneia was that prytanic dignity; sometimes also by prytaneia they understood the public markets or fairs. Since therefore the Greeks perceived that this island abounded in things serviceable for provision, they called it prytaneia, whence Britannia was born. But this conjecture offends in many ways. For first, it makes these islands not only known to the most ancient Greeks, but even quite familiar to them; whereas Herodotus testifies that he did not know whence tin was imported, which they had from no other quarter than from these islands. Next, although I would by no means say with Camden that these regions were utterly unknown to the Greeks before Caesar's coming, yet I have no doubt at all that they were called British long before anyone dares to pretend that Athenians landed here, since their mastery of the sea [Greek: thalattokratia] was a late and recent thing. Bale fetches the British name from Brytona, a Cretan nymph, who, in order to escape the violence of Minos attempting to ravish her, threw herself headlong into the waters. Of fables of that sort lying Greece will furnish six hundred to any inquirer. Bede derives it from a Britannia in Gaul, which, formerly called Armorica, took its name from Britons who crossed over thither from here. But the [ p. 233 ] folly of all is outdone by the delirium of Goropius Becanus, who would have Britannia to be, as it were, "Bri-dania," that is, as he himself interprets it, "free Dania" [free Denmark]; whereas the British name was most celebrated through the whole world before that of Dania or of the Danes was either known or born. Humphrey Llwyd deduces this name from Prid and Cain, two little British words which signify "a fair form." To that conjecture there is no one, so far as I know, who gives his vote, although it is not so absurd as others which have found backers.

V. All these being rejected, two etymologies of this name remain which, still fortified by the suffrages of learned men, hold their ground. The first is that which rests upon the story of the arrival in this island of Brutus, of Trojan blood; for it was formerly believed that from him the island was called Britannia and the inhabitants Britons. The opinion is old, and one which has obtained mention in some public acts of the kings of this island. But that whole story, harried by Camden and Eliot and moved from its place, most of the more recent writers judge to deserve no place at all. Into their opinion I shall go with my whole heart, just as soon as I have anything ascertained concerning another account of the name. The second is that of Camden himself, who asserts that the name flowed from Brith, a British word. Now Brith, as he says, signifies "painted"; and by Davies in his dictionary it is indeed rendered "of divers colors" or "spotted." To this word the Greek termination Tania being added produced "Britannia." Certainly very many have recorded that the Britons were accustomed to paint themselves, and that from this they were called Picti, "the painted," by the Romans. Caesar himself, book v of the Gallic War, ch. 14, relates that "all the Britons stain themselves with woad [vitro]" (a word variously emended by various critics, as nitro, luteo, glauco, glasto, ultro, guasto), "which produces a blue color; and by this they are of a more horrid aspect in battle." Into this opinion of Camden, accordingly, very many have gone. George Hornius, a most learned man, in his preface to Boxhorn's "Gallic Origins," pronounces intrepidly, as though the matter were set beyond controversy, that the inhabitants, because they were painted over their whole body, were called Britons, since in their own tongue, that is, the Cimbric, Prith (he meant to say Brith) signifies "painted." But in truth, if it be lawful to dissent from that supreme steward of British antiquities, I confess I cannot subscribe to this conjecture; and that, as for other reasons, so also because in setting it forth this man, otherwise great and exceedingly learned, contradicted himself. For by whom, pray, would the inhabitants of these islands be called Britten, from Brith, that is, "painted"? By themselves? But Camden himself has shown before, what is most true, that they neither call nor ever called themselves anything but Cymeroes, and their language Cymraeg; and from this the same learned man proves that they sprang from the ancient Cimbri. By the Greeks themselves, then? But he declares that these islands were utterly unknown to the Greeks before the times of Caesar, at least in the days of Polybius of Megalopolis; and he contends that none of the Greeks knew what mortals possessed these shores, whether painted or finely clothed. He would have it, then, that the Greeks, when [ p. 234 ] they observed that the inhabitants of this island had stained their bodies with a blue color, learned from them how "painted" was said in their language, and added Tania to that word, whence flowed Britannia, at a time when they did not yet know what mortals, white or black, had set their seats in it. Nor would the Greeks have lacked words in their own tongue to express painted men and a painted nation, if the origin of the word is to be fetched from them. Since therefore it is certain that the Britons never called themselves Britten, or "painted," from "Brith," "painted," but constantly Cymeroi; and that this island was called Britannia long before the Greeks knew who or what sort of mortals possessed it; and since a composition of the name seems absurd in which the Greek termination far outweighs the Cimbric foundation; to pass over the point that it does not seem to me so very true that all the Britons either were painted or were so called (for although Caesar testifies that they all stained themselves with a blue color, he does not therefore say they were "painted" [tattooed]; and it is well known that there was a nation dwelling in Caledonia which was peculiarly called "Pictish"), I cannot deny that all that hope has been beheaded for me, of which I was for some time full, of gathering anything certain about the origin of the British name from this conjecture of that eminent man.

VI. For disentangling the truth, a new conjecture is brought forward by a man learned on every side, Samuel Bochart, Sacred Geography, part ii, ch. 39. For after he has shown that these islands surrounded by the ocean were known to the Greeks, and were called the Cassiterides from the lead and tin in which they are most fruitful, he teaches that this island was known also to the Tyrians, and for the same reason was called barat anac [Hebrew: barat anak], that is, "the field" or "land of tin"; and from this, among the Greeks and Latins, the name Britannia may appear to have flowed. The matter is too long for me, engaged on another business, to dwell upon it. I will say briefly what I think: whether because (since all other derivations, after long and attentive consideration, either displease altogether or please less) this conjecture of that eminent man commends itself by its very novelty, or because the mind perceives some light of truth shining out of it in the deepest darkness, I confess that it smiles upon me above all the others. I know that this derivation of the British name has been jeered at and repudiated by the most learned George Hornius, in his "Introduction to Ancient Geography" and in his "Preface to the Gallic Origins" of Boxhorn. On what ground? On this, namely, that the Phoenicians obtained the mastery of the sea [Greek: thalattokratia] only very late, and that after other nations, in the seventh or eighth place, among which were some Greek nations, and only then sailed into these seas. And so at one and the same stroke he both rejects the etymology of Britannia, and strives to cast down and overturn the foundation of many other things which Bochart, with heroic endeavor and marvelous success, has seemed to me to have dug out of the darkness. I have no wish to thrust my sickle into another man's harvest; I will say only that it seems wonderful to me that any man, leaning on the good faith and authority of the Greeks, could bring his mind to believe that the Phoenicians, who had carried colonies into Greece itself several ages before the Greeks ever [ p. 235 ] set foot abroad from home, had only late, in comparison with them, explored the maritime parts of the world.

VII. But whatever at last be the true origin of the name of this island, and whoever its most ancient inhabitants were, it is most certain that the Britons were most thoroughly defiled with idolatrous superstition. From remotest antiquity, however, the priests of the British rites, whose discipline, as is well known, spread itself far and wide into other nations, were better known than the very deities whom by their ministry the Britons worshipped. These were the Druidae or Druids, of whom we must treat hereafter. That they "taught that one God was to be worshipped," Origen affirms, on the fourth chapter of Ezekiel. If that be true, that one god was the Sun himself, not the most holy Maker of heaven and earth, from whose knowledge the savage superstition of the Druids was at the farthest possible remove. To him the Moon was added, worshipped by the women. Hence Dio, History, book lxii: "Boadicea, stretching out her hand toward heaven, said, 'I give you thanks, O Andraste'" (perhaps Astarte) "'and I, a woman, call upon you, a woman.'" Now the Moon was commonly the queen of heaven, and among the Britons the bestower of victory. And because they worshipped the heavenly bodies, Tacitus relates, in the Life of Agricola, that they poured out their prayers with hands lifted up to heaven, as Dio also relates of Boadicea. That the superstition of the Druids arose in Britain, Caesar affirms, book vi of the Gallic War, ch. 13: "The discipline," he says, "is thought to have been found out in Britain, and from there carried over into Gaul; and now those who wish to gain a more exact knowledge of that system generally journey thither to learn it." Tacitus also, to prove that the Gauls occupied the neighboring soil, adds, "You may detect their sacred rites, through the persuasion of the same superstitions." That the Gauls worshipped Mercury, Apollo, Mars, Jupiter, and Minerva, Caesar reports. But that they received both the names and the worship of these from the Greeks of Massilia we have shown elsewhere. From them also they learned the use of images and statues, unknown, as Lucan witnesses, to the ancient Gauls.

"And the gloomy images of their gods lack art, and stand shapeless from hewn trunks; the very site, and the squalor of the timber now rotting, strikes men with awe" (Lucan, Pharsalia 3.412-415). The superstition of these gods, therefore, never invaded Britain. The most ancient deities of the Gauls, common to them with the Britons, were those which the same Lucan records, book i, 444: "And those by whom pitiless Teutates is appeased with dreadful blood, and Hesus, horrid on his savage altars, and the altar of Taranis, no gentler than that of Scythian Diana."

Some judge that Teutates flowed from Diw Taith. The words are British, signifying "the guide of the journey." Now we have shown above that almost all the nations dispersed from Babylon worshipped the sun as the guide of their journeys and wanderings. That Moloch, the idol of the Ammonites, was the sun, all the learned agree. His rites [ p. 236 ] we shall set forth in our exposition of Jewish idolatry. To these rites scarcely anything anywhere equal or similar occurs in the superstition of the nations, except what Caesar has recorded concerning the Gauls, who received all their religious worship from the Britons, Gallic War, book vi, ch. 16: "They have images," he says, "of immense size, whose limbs, woven of osiers, they fill with living men; these being set on fire, the men perish, enveloped in the flames." Teutates, then, is the Sun. Who Hesus was is uncertain; and that you may see the uncertainty of conjectures, Gosselin, in Bochart, derives the name from ezuz [Hebrew: izzuz], a Hebrew word which signifies "strong"; Camden from Haud, a British word which denotes a dog.

VIII. Taranis is derived from Taran, that is, "thunder"; for so it is called by the Welsh, as by the English "thonder," by the Dutch "donder"; whence "dondersdag" and "thundersday," or "thursday." They understood by him some deity presiding over the air; unless perhaps we should suppose him so called from Tarian, which is a buckler or shield. But I should prefer the derivation from Taran, because it was the custom to appease him with the harshest of victims.

IX. That they worshipped any gods of human form [Greek: anthropophyeas], no testimony or record exists. Yet in process of time they set up various images to these idols, as Gildas witnesses. "In Britain," he says, "are those diabolical monstrosities, almost surpassing in number those of Egypt, of which we still behold some, with their wonted stiffness and grim faces, mouldering within or without the deserted walls." Nor is more recorded of the idols of the Britons, although without doubt there was among them a vast chaos of superstition. But the superstition of these islands was better known from its ministers than from its gods; of them, therefore, we must treat.

X. Over the British rites presided the Druides or Druidae. Under that name they were celebrated throughout the whole Celtic or Cimbric nation everywhere. "Among the Celts and Galatians are those who are called Druids" [Greek: para te Keltois kai Galatais tous kaloumenous Druidas], Diogenes Laertius, in the Proem of his work. That Britain gave origin to this order of men and to their superstition, we have already shown from Caesar. Concerning the etymology of the word the learned are not agreed. Most uncertain are the things related of Dryis, and of his successor Bardus, kings of the Celts, and the other matters which Bale has collected. Montanus says that Dry signifies a magician, and that thence they were called Druydes by the Greeks, since they were magicians. Vossius judges that the name flowed from Dru or Trou, a Germanic word which signifies truthful and faithful, like the English "true." The more common, and without doubt the truer, derivation of the word is that which Pliny first delivered, book xvi, last chapter: "The Druids," he says, "hold nothing more sacred than the mistletoe, and the tree on which it grows, provided only it be an oak. They choose groves of oaks for their own sake, and perform no sacred rites without that foliage; so that from this they may seem to have been called Druids, even by a Greek interpretation." For drys [Greek] is an oak, and the Druids are named from the oak [Greek: apo tes dryos]. Add that dru in British has the same sound and sense. That the Druids performed their rites in groves of oaks and in oak-woods, all acknowledge. "The groves consecrated to their savage superstitions were cut down," says Tacitus, Annals, book xiv, ch. 30. For the same reason they were also called Saronidae. [ p. 237 ] "They name their theologians Saronidae" [Greek: Saronidas onomazousi], says Diodorus, book v, ch. 31, namely from the oaks. Whence Callimachus, in his Hymn to Jupiter: "Surely many oaks [Greek: Saronidas] did the watery Iaon bear aloft." On which place the scholiast notes that Saronides are oaks, and subjoins the reason of the word. Maximus Tyrius also testifies that the emblem of Jupiter among the Celts was a very lofty oak.

XI. Whether the Bards were of the number of the Druids, appointed to a peculiar work, or another class of men, is uncertain. Lucan celebrates their office, book i, 447: "You also, you Bards, who with your praises send down brave souls cut off in war to a long age of fame, poured forth your many songs in safety." And Marcellinus: "The Bards sang the illustrious deeds of brave men, composed in heroic verses, to the sweet strains of the lyre." And before him Diodorus, as above: "There are also among them," he says, "composers of songs, whom they name Bards." So likewise Posidonius, in Athenaeus, book vi: "Their reciters are those called Bards; and these are poets, uttering praises with song." There are even still among the Cambro-Britons some men occupied in weaving songs and genealogies, whom they call bards.

XII. Strabo, book iv, distributes the ministers of sacred things among the Celts into three classes, namely Bards, Vates, and Druids. He would have spoken more correctly had he named two classes of seers, Bards and Druids; for he assigns to the Vates the things that were proper to the Druids. "The Vates," he says, "are sacrificers and natural philosophers" [Greek: hieropoioi kai physiologoi], that is, they sacrifice, and contemplate or teach the nature of things. But that the Druids led the way in all sacred rites, we have shown from Caesar.

XIII. The Druids made use of a peculiar dress, by which they might draw to themselves the veneration of the common people. Johannes Theophilus relates that he found it expressed in six stone images. "Each of them," he says, "was seven feet high, with bare feet, the head uncovered, with a Greek cloak and hood, a mantle, and a beard hanging down to the groin and forked about the nostrils; in their hands a book and a staff of the Diogenic sort; with a severe brow and a gloomy frown, the head bent forward, fixing their eyes upon the ground." In their sacred rites they used a white garment, as Pliny witnesses, book xvi, last chapter: "The priest, arrayed in a white robe, climbs the tree and cuts the mistletoe with a golden sickle; it is caught in a white cloak." They established academies for the sake of their studies in the groves: "You dwell in the deep forests, in sequestered groves" (Lucan, Pharsalia 1.453-454).

XIV. In these they kept schools crammed full of studious youth. "To them," says Caesar, in the place above cited, ch. 13, "a great number of young men flock together for the sake of instruction." Mela also, book iii, ch. 2: "They teach many things to the noblest of the nation, secretly and for a long time, for twenty years." That they spent so many [ p. 238 ] years in study before they were promoted to the doctorate, Caesar testifies: "Some," he says, "remain twenty years under instruction." Consider the enticements of their studies, the privileges of the students, and the supreme authority of those who had completed the course. "The Druids," says the same Caesar, "are accustomed to keep away from war, nor do they pay tribute along with the rest; they have exemption from military service and immunity from all burdens. Stirred by such great rewards, many gather to their instruction of their own accord, and many are sent by their relatives and parents." Their authority we shall see presently. The same author relates their method of instruction: "They are said to learn by heart a great number of verses; nor do they think it lawful to commit those things" (their sacred lore) "to writing, although in almost all other matters, in their public and private accounts, they use Greek letters. This practice they seem to me," he says, "to have instituted for two reasons: because they neither wish their discipline to be carried abroad among the common people, nor those who learn, relying on letters, to apply themselves less to memory; for it generally happens to most men that, trusting to the help of writing, they relax their diligence in thorough learning, and their memory" (Caesar, as above, ch. 14). They cultivated a varied and almost omnifarious philosophy. Pliny ascribes magic to them, book xxx, ch. 1: "But why should I recount these things," he says, "concerning an art which has even crossed the ocean and pushed forward to the void of nature? Britain at this day celebrates it with such awe, and with such great ceremonies, that she might seem to have given it to the Persians."

XV. They were physicians also, as the same Pliny witnesses, book xxx, ch. 1: "The principate of Tiberius Caesar put down the Druids, and that tribe of seers and physicians." And that the medicine they practiced was mixed with magic, the same author teaches, book xvi, ch. 44.

XVI. In theology they taught many peculiar things, and some excellent. That they had a wisdom of their own, singular and apart from other men, Lucan notes, book i, 450: "And you, O Druids, when arms were laid aside, resumed your barbaric rites and the sinister custom of your sacrifices: to you alone it is given to know the gods and the powers of heaven, or to you alone not to know them." The chief dogma of their theology was that souls do not perish, nor does the world. "They say that souls and the world are imperishable" [Greek: aphthartous de legousi tas psychas kai ton kosmon], says Strabo. And Caesar, in the place above cited, ch. 14: "This above all they seek to persuade men of, that souls do not perish, but pass after death from one to another; and by this above all they think men are stirred to valor, the fear of death being set at naught." Which words of Caesar Lucan brings out admirably, book i, 454: "If you sing of things you know, the shades, by your teaching, do not seek the silent abodes of Erebus and the pallid realms of Dis below; the same spirit governs the limbs in another world; death is the midpoint of a long life. Surely the peoples on whom the Bear looks down are happy in their error, whom that greatest of terrors, the fear of death, does not oppress; hence their men have minds prone to rush upon the sword, souls capable of death, and hold it cowardice to spare a life that will return." [ p. 239 ] They taught moreover that the world would at last be destroyed by fire and water. "Fire and water will one day prevail," as Strabo has it; though it is doubtful whether their meaning was rightly grasped, since perhaps, from ancient tradition, they taught that the world was once destroyed by water, and is to be consumed anew by fire.

XVII. They gave attention also to eloquence and rhetoric. "They have nevertheless," says Mela, book iii, ch. 2, "their own eloquence, and the Druids as masters of wisdom." And the symbolic image of Hercules Ogmius is known to everyone.

XVIII. With theology they likewise mingled geography and astronomy. The same Mela: "These men profess to know the magnitude and form of the earth and the world, and the motions of the heaven and the stars." And Caesar himself, in the place cited, ch. 14: "They dispute much concerning the stars and their motion, concerning the magnitude of the world and of the lands, concerning the nature of things" (natural philosophy), "and hand these things down to the youth." To these they added ethics, as Diogenes witnesses, Proem of his work, sect. 6: "And they say that the Druids philosophize in riddling utterances" (to be learned by heart, that is), "teaching that the gods are to be worshipped, that no evil is to be done, and that manly courage is to be practiced."

XIX. Since they were very numerous, they maintained such an order among themselves that one man presided over all the rest as pope. "On his death," says Caesar, in the place cited, ch. 13, "if any one of the rest excels in dignity, he succeeds; but if there are several equals, one is chosen by the vote of the Druids; sometimes they even contend for the primacy with arms": which, as we know, has also been done at Rome among Christians.

XX. With what favor and authority they prevailed among the peoples, the same author teaches us. "They take part," he says, "in divine affairs, they attend to public and private sacrifices, they interpret matters of religion. Concerning almost all controversies, public and private, they give decision; and if any crime has been committed, if a murder has been done, if there is a dispute about an inheritance or about boundaries, the same men decide it and appoint the penalties." Thus, busying themselves with other men's affairs, they intruded themselves under the pretext of religion into all matters public and private, themselves the while exempt from every duty owed to the commonwealth, exercising their dominion in another's province. But since they did not ordinarily bear the office of magistrate, and so had neither the rods nor the axes, nor the power of life and death, which are absolutely necessary for exacting obedience, it may justly be asked how they compelled the peoples to keep themselves within the bounds of duty and to obey their word in all things. To meet this inconvenience, then, recourse was had to the invention of a politico-religious excommunication; and thus a certain Caesareo-papal authority was made complete. "If anyone," says the same Caesar, "whether private person or magistrate, does not abide by their decree, they interdict him from the sacrifices. This penalty is among them the heaviest. Those who are thus interdicted are reckoned in the number of the impious and the wicked; all men withdraw from them, and shun their approach and conversation, lest they receive some harm from the contagion; [ p. 240 ] nor is justice rendered to them when they seek it, nor is any honor shared with them." Either no man exists, or he is a man of no account, who does not perceive that this whole mystery of iniquity was long ago carried over into Christianity.

XXI. There remain their sacred rites and the worship of their gods. These were various, and almost all of them monstrous. When Caesar, after his first entry into this island, had withdrawn, or rather had been put to flight, "the Britons, performing divers sacrifices, gave themselves up to the slaughter of cattle; they offered there forty thousand oxen and a hundred thousand sheep, and fowls of divers kinds which could not easily be brought under number; besides thirty thousand wild beasts of the forest of every sort gathered together." These are the words of Geoffrey, book i, ch. 24; that is, of a man of the most abandoned audacity and impudence in inventing anything whatever. The chief thing recorded by trustworthy authors concerning the sacrifices of the Druids is human sacrifice [Greek: anthropothysia]. Now there were two kinds of human victims among them. The first was private, when someone devoted either himself or another for the safety of another person. "Those who are afflicted with the more serious diseases, and those who are engaged in battles and dangers, either immolate men as victims or vow that they will immolate them, and they employ the Druids as ministers for these sacrifices; because they think that the divine power of the immortal gods cannot otherwise be appeased, unless for the life of a man the life of a man be rendered," says Caesar, in the place cited, ch. 16. The form of this immolation, as appears from Strabo and others, was this: the victim being brought to the altar, the priest sprinkled wine, or, where this was lacking, some other liquor, upon his forehead; then, seizing the forelocks of his hair with his left hand, he poured libations on the fire, at the same time performing prayers or vows to the gods; presently the victim himself was struck with the sword; as Cluverius observes. The other kind was public, and, as we observed before, not unlike that with which Moloch was worshipped in the East. "They have also," says Caesar, in the place cited, "sacrifices instituted by public authority. Some have images of immense size, whose limbs, woven of osiers, they fill with living men; these being set on fire, the men perish, surrounded by the flames." This he says of the Gauls, who received the Druids themselves, and this whole superstition, from the Britons. Similarly Strabo, book iv: "For they would strike a man, consecrated as an offering, in the back with a sword, and divine from his convulsions; and they sacrificed not without the Druids. Other kinds of human sacrifice are also reported; for they would shoot some down with arrows, and crucify others in their sanctuaries, and, constructing a colossus of hay and throwing wood into it, they would burn whole cattle and all kinds of wild beasts and human beings together." Of public victims, in which men were sacrificed, besides that one recorded by Caesar, he here reports three other kinds: for those to be immolated were either struck with the sword, or pierced with arrows, or even fastened to crosses; and to these he adds that custom of burning men shut up in colossal images. Yet another kind of death employed in these sacrifices is recorded by Diodorus, book v, ch. 32: "Keeping their malefactors for a five-year period, they impale them in honor of their gods, and, preparing enormous pyres, consecrate them together with many other first-fruits." [ p. 241 ] By fire, then, by sword, by arrows, by the cross, by sharpened stakes, as the fancy took them, they dispatched those who were to be immolated. Nor was this crime perpetrated upon malefactors only: "They descend even to the punishment of the innocent," says Caesar, in the place cited. And with what monstrous ferocity the Britons bore themselves in their public sacrifices, Dio testifies in book lxii of his Histories: "Upon the persons captured by them there was no extremity of horror that was not inflicted. And this most dreadful and most bestial thing they did: they hung up naked the noblest and most beautiful women, cut off their breasts and sewed them to their mouths, so that they seemed to be eating them; and after this they impaled the women on sharp stakes driven lengthwise through the whole body; and all this they did while sacrificing and feasting and behaving with wanton insolence, both in their other sacred places and especially in the grove of Andate." And of this sort were we Britons, slaves of Satan.

XXII. On account of these unspeakable sacrifices the Romans attempted to remove the whole superstition of the Druids. Strabo, of the Gauls, book iv, ch. 4: "From these practices the Romans drew them away, and from those rites of sacrifice and divination which were contrary to our usages." And Suetonius, in his Claudius, ch. 25: "The religion of the Druids among the Gauls, a religion of dreadful savagery, which under Augustus had merely been forbidden to citizens, he utterly abolished." That is, he wished to abolish it, in Gaul, but in vain; Britain, the seat and origin of the superstition, remained untouched. Only in the island of Mona were the Druids slain and the groves cut down by Suetonius Paulinus. Tacitus relates the deed, Annals xiv, ch. 30: "On the shore stood the opposing line of battle," he says, "dense with arms and men, with women running between them, ... and round about the Druids, pouring forth dire prayers with hands raised to heaven, so struck the soldiers by the novelty of the sight that, as though their limbs were frozen, they offered their motionless bodies to wounds. Then, at the exhortations of their general, and spurring one another on not to quail before a womanish and fanatical host, they advance the standards, lay low all who meet them, and wrap them in their own fire. A garrison was afterwards imposed upon the conquered, and the groves were cut down, consecrated to their savage superstitions: for they held it lawful to feed their altars with the blood of captives, and to consult their gods by means of human entrails."

XXIII. But this impious superstition, God at last showing mercy, the heavenly truth of the Christian religion gradually took away. For although this island was "divided from well-nigh the whole world," and lay at the farthest remove from the fountain of the waters [ p. 242 ] of the sanctuary which went forth for the healing of the nations, yet, God graciously so providing, almost from the very cradle of the gospel messengers and heralds of divine grace were carried into it. That Peter preached the gospel here is asserted by Simeon Metaphrastes and the Menology; that Paul did, by Theodoret and Sophronius; Simon Zelotes, by Dorotheus; Aristobulus, whom Paul mentions in the Epistle to the Romans, by Nicephorus; Joseph of Arimathea, by almost all, especially the more recent of our own countrymen, Bale, Parker, Foxe, Camden, and others; and concerning Joseph there is scarcely a doubt among any. Hence the most ancient Christian writers, Tertullian and Origen, record that Britain received the Christian faith: the latter in his commentary on Ezekiel, ch. 4; the former in his book Against the Jews, ch. 7. Claudia Rufina also, a British woman, most noble and most accomplished, Martial celebrates, book xi, Epigram 54: "Since Claudia Rufina is sprung from the blue-stained Britons, how comes she to have the heart of the Latin people? What grace of form! The matrons of Italy might believe her a Roman, those of Attica their own." That she was that Claudia whom Paul mentions in 2 Tim. 4:21 learned men hold, and the times agree. Add that he couples her with Pudens, whom the same Martial likewise celebrates, book vii, Epigram 10: "You compel me, Pudens, to correct my little books with my own pen and hand. Oh, how excessively you approve and love me, who wish to possess my trifles in their author's autograph."

XXIV. Very many have also written that King Lucius, whose letter to Pope Eleutherius is reported, together with the rescript of the same Eleutherius, was the first of all kings to embrace the Christian faith; just as no fewer have related that Philip the Arabian was the first Christian emperor. But, to deal freely, and as becomes one to whom nothing is more venerable than truth, I frankly confess that it is now long since the credit, if not of the whole history of this Lucius, yet certainly of that epistolary exchange, and of many other things which are commonly stitched on to the story, became deeply suspect to me.

XXV. The story, so far as I know, was first committed to record by Bede, Ecclesiastical History, book i, ch. 4. He says that Lucius wrote to Eleutherius in the year 156 of the Christian reckoning. Nauclerus follows him; but Baronius shows in his Annals that Eleutherius did not preside over the Roman church before the year of Christ 178. There is therefore an error in the assigning of the time. Henry of Erfordia reports that it was done in the year 196, the nineteenth year of the Emperor Verus (who by no means reigned so many years). Ado, in his Chronicle, disposes the time more correctly, and writes that Eleutherius lived under the Emperor Commodus and ruled the Roman church; which is true, for the end of Eleutherius' third year falls upon the beginning of the Commodian empire. Let this, then, be the time at which, if ever, that epistolary exchange between King Lucius and Eleutherius [ p. 243 ] took place. The success of the undertaking Geoffrey of Monmouth relates at large. "And without delay," he says, "the peoples of the nations, running together from every quarter, follow the king's example, and, cleansed in the same laver, are restored to the heavenly kingdom. The blessed teachers therefore" (namely Fugatius and Damianus, sent from Rome by Eleutherius), "when they had blotted out paganism through almost the whole island, dedicated the temples, which had been founded in honor of a multitude of gods, to the one God and to his saints, and filled them with divers companies of ordained men. There were at that time in Britain twenty-eight flamens and three archflamens, to whose power the other judges in the island were subject. These also, by the precept of the apostolic father, they rescued from idolatry; and where there were flamens they placed bishops, and where there were archflamens, archbishops. Now the seats of the archflamens had been in the three nobler cities, namely London, York, and the City of the Legions, which old walls and buildings testify to have been situated upon the river Usk in Glamorgan. To these three, therefore, the superstition being swept away, the twenty-eight bishops are made subject." So says he, a man who never knew at all what it is to be ashamed. How childishly he invented these things will appear in a moment. The British superstition no more possessed flamens or archflamens than it possessed Geoffreys of Monmouth. Yet I would not deny that he has duly laid open the origin of that gradation among the ministers of Christian worship, the Roman church itself confessing it, Decretum, part 1, distinction 21: "Among priests," they say, "a certain gradation has been observed, so that some are called simply priests, others archpriests, others chorepiscopi, others bishops, others archbishops or metropolitans, others primates, others patriarchs, others supreme pontiffs; and this gradation was chiefly introduced from the heathen, who called their flamens, some simply flamens, others archflamens, others protoflamens." Thus the author of the Distinction, nakedly and openly. More modestly, and within the bounds of decorum, writes the more learned Leland. "It remains," he says, "that I intimate the event which removed so great a superstition out of the way. Provision was made by Roman edict that no practice or repute of the religion of the Druids should be exhibited in the provinces. Yet the barbarous nations clung tooth and nail to the ancestral rites, given and received from the Druids themselves so many ages before; until Christ the Savior, best and greatest, having scattered the darkness of errors and gross ignorance, by the counsel of his own Spirit poured upon the world a light conspicuous to all. Then that great Lucius, the immortal glory of the Britons, despising the discipline of the Druids, gladly willed that the evangelical doctrine should be common to all his people. Eluanus, therefore, and Meduinus, noble British spokesmen, were sent by Lucius to Eleutherius at Rome; and Fugatius, together with Damianus, being sent back hither by the Roman, solidly established, God being propitious and so willing it, a religion that shall never die." Certain Bavarians, as Bale witnesses, Scriptores Britannici, century i, ch. 29, add that this Lucius afterwards took up an apostolate and converted their nation to the faith; and they count it an honor to themselves to have been [ p. 244 ] converted by a great king; but our own countrymen protest, affirming that Lucius never carried his foot out of his own kingdom, but met his end at Gloucester in the twelfth year of his reign. The most learned Camden (in whose footsteps Selden treads in his Anglo-British Analecta) yields to the common prejudice. For after he had most accurately woven together the history of those times out of all the most approved historians, and had set clearly before all men's eyes that British affairs were in such a state and condition from the days of Claudius Caesar, who had reduced the island into the form of a province, that no place was left for such a king as this Lucius is feigned to be, one who should hold this whole island under his sway, he nevertheless judged it prudent to insert the story itself into his work; though the very sequence of events and times which he himself set forth cries out that it should be utterly cast out.

XXVI. To pluck out of the minds of men so great an opinion, so deeply implanted, so ancient, is neither within my power nor my purpose. But since falsehood has in no place much foundation or strength, if I first obtain from my readers that which is most equitable, namely that they bring no prejudice to the question, I have no doubt that I shall persuade the lovers of antiquity [Greek: philarchaiois] that I have causes for my suspicion (which is by no means mine alone) sufficiently just, nay most weighty.

XXVII. Britain, twice attempted by Julius Caesar almost in vain, neglected by Augustus and Tiberius; over whose ocean-shells, cast up on the Gallic shore, Caligula, hedged about with a vast army, celebrated a triumph; worn down by civil wars and the zeal of factions, while the inhabitants obeyed divers kings: Claudius Caesar, assailing it through the praetor Aulus Plautius, reduced it into the form of a province, and handed it over to Plautius to govern. The history of the things done by him in this island is related at length by Dio and Suetonius. So great in those times was the glory of this victory, that the senate decreed not only to Claudius himself the name of Britannicus and a most splendid triumph, but to Plautius an ovation, and to Vespasian, who followed up the remnants of the war, the triumphal ornaments. Nor do the poets triumph less over the addition of this province to the empire. So Seneca the tragedian, in the Octavia, Act i: "He whom the whole world obeyed beyond the ocean, and before whom the Britons turned their backs, unknown to our generals before, and a law unto themselves." To which he presently subjoins: "Lo, he who first imposed the yoke upon the Britons, covered unknown straits with fleets so great, and was safe among barbarous nations and savage seas." The first little verse was commonly read, "Lo, he who first [imposed the yoke] upon the shores of the Tanais," etc. Scaliger, in his notes on Tibullus, restored "Lo, he who upon the Britons"; but without doubt [ p. 245 ] we should read, "Lo, he who first imposed the yoke upon the shore of the Thames"; since Claudius was the first to cross the Thames, a point observed by the most learned Camden. Of the same Claudius Annaeus Seneca had sung before, in his Apocolocyntosis ["Pumpkinification"] of Claudius Caesar: "He bade the Britons beyond the shores of the known sea, and the blue-shielded Brigantes, give their necks to Roman chains, and Ocean himself tremble at the new jurisdiction of the Roman axe" (Seneca, Apocolocyntosis 12).
