Most elegant also are those epigrams which the great Scaliger brought forward in his Catalecta. It is pleasing to subjoin some of them: "The land never violated by Ausonian triumphs fell prostrate, Caesar, struck by your thunderbolt; and Ocean now looks out upon your altars beyond himself: that which is the boundary of the world shall not be the boundary of the empire. Conquered before by none, now at last beheld in triumph, the nation lies untouched until now, to swell your titles. Long deemed a fable, and hidden in the midst of the deep, she has but now given her free neck to the conqueror. Britain, free, that suffered neither enemy nor king, who lies far off, eternally removed from our world, happy in adversity and oppressed by prosperous fortune, shall be common, Caesar, to us and to you. Father Mars, and Quirinus, guardian of our race, and both you Caesars set in the great heaven: you behold the unknown Britons under Latian law: the sun bends his course within the bounds of our empire." But when the Britons again raised tumults, on account of civil dissensions among the petty kings who had taken up the Roman yoke (for that in the days of Caesar, or thereafter, one king ruled the island, is a Galfridian dream, and one which no one but Geoffrey would dream), the same Claudius restrained them through the propraetor Publius Ostorius. That war Tacitus has accurately handed down to memory. Under the rule of Nero, the whole nation, and all the peoples in it, roused by the wrongs and the spirit, both of them very great, of Boadicea, queen of the Iceni, attempted bravely and strenuously, but in vain, to throw the Roman yoke from their necks and to recover their ancestral liberty. The end of the war, under the conduct of Suetonius Paulinus, was for the nation partly destruction, partly slavery. Nero being dead, while Galba, Otho, Vitellius, and Vespasian held almost the whole world torn asunder in civil war, the Silures and Ordovices and other peoples took such courage as to give occasion to that most deadly war by which Julius Agricola the propraetor, father-in-law of Tacitus the historian, reduced the whole island into the power of the Romans, and compelled it to use Roman law and statutes; one petty king only being left, with precarious authority, in a certain part of the island, namely Cogidunus, that here also they might have a king as an instrument of servitude. In that war Galgacus was conquered, the last of the Britons. A certain northern part of the island being left to the barbarians as rough and unfruitful, the rest Agricola reduced "plainly and fully," as Camden speaks, into a province, [ p. 246 ] and, as Tacitus witnesses, delivered it quiet and safe to his successor. The royal name being taken away, together with all that authority of the petty kings which the peoples had formerly venerated, Britain became a praesidial province, a province of the Caesars. Under the reign of Nerva it was quiet; Spartianus relates that Trajan reduced to order certain peoples who revolted. The barbarians, assailing the province from the northern parts of the island with wars and brigandage, Hadrian restrained by a wall raised for eighty miles; which the same Spartianus narrates. Nor was the government of the Antonines, Pius and the Philosopher, free from wars or tumults in this island; yet all these were crushed by the valor of Lollius Urbicus and Calphurnius Agricola. Under the rule of Commodus also, all things in the province itself were for a time full of seditions; and the barbarous nations, crossing the wall, gave very great trouble to the Romans and Britons, and several times wrought the greatest slaughter upon the inhabitants who lived nearest the wall. But upon the arrival of Ulpius Marcellus, as Dio and Lampridius relate, all things were so pacified that down to the death of Commodus nothing appears to have been attempted against the Roman peace. In this state and condition of British affairs, I, to confess frankly (such is my dullness), cannot see where that Lucius could have been, who held this whole island accountable to no one [Greek: anypeuthynos], and as the vicar of God alone, as he is called in the letter of Eleutherius; for that he was not in this Britain of ours is only too probable. It seems plainly to be another island which he distributed among archbishops and bishops with the consent of the British nobles; another London, another York, over which he presided; for these of ours lay under the commands of the Romans.

XXVIII. To these things Cardinal Baronius makes many replies, at the year of Christ 183, where he produces and defends this story of Lucius. First, then, "never," he says, "before these times did the Romans possess the whole of Britain; rather, it is clear that several unsubdued peoples remained in it, who often gave trouble to the Roman armies; and again, it is certain that those same peoples in Britain who had been subdued by the Romans very often fell away and rebelled outright. For, to pass over the more remote times, it is agreed, on the authority of Julius Capitolinus, that under Marcus Aurelius a British war was kindled, but that it was crushed by Calphurnius Agricola, sent thither by Marcus. And again, after the death of Marcus, under the rule of Commodus it was renewed, to such a degree that the Britons (as Lampridius writes) wished to create another emperor against him, refusing altogether to obey Commodus; but the same author affirms that all was settled by the generals. Dio adds moreover that the British war in these times of Commodus was the greatest of all, but was utterly extinguished by Ulpius Marcellus."

XXIX. It is wonderful that Baronius, a most learned and cautious man, did not observe that these statements are both incoherent [Greek: asystata], and, where they contain truth, strike at the very throat of the cause which he undertook to defend. They are incoherent: [ p. 247 ] for by them he hints that Lucius reigned among peoples unsubdued by the Romans, who often revolted from the Roman empire, and who wished to create another emperor against Commodus. But these were plainly different parties. For the peoples who are said to have been unsubdued by the Romans were the barbarians beyond the wall, and they alone. Those who repeatedly rebelled against the Romans were the provincial Britons; and those who wished to create another emperor were the Roman garrison soldiers; so that it is plainly impossible that one and the same man should have ruled over all of these. Moreover, he utterly cuts his own throat with his own sword when he teaches from Dio that the British war was completely extinguished, in the reign of Commodus, by Ulpius Marcellus, and that thus the whole province yielded peaceably to the power of the Romans. For in that state of affairs, as we showed before, it was impossible for Lucius to hold a kingdom within the province; nor will anyone ever believe it who is not ignorant of what a Roman praesidial province is. Further, no writers, whether worthy of credit or unworthy, have handed down anything to memory concerning any such defection of the Britons from the Roman empire by which they set up for themselves a king who should hold the whole island under his sway; such a king as they never had even when they were their own masters and free. That war was several times stirred up against the Romans, now by one people, then by another, or by several with joined forces, we have shown; yet it was always extinguished within a short time. That in those tumults there existed such a king as not only the most mendacious Geoffrey, but the Eleutherian letter also, feigns Lucius to have been, is an opinion only for men blinded by excessive prejudice. Well known is that saying of Tacitus concerning the state of the Britons in these times: "Rarely," he says, "do two or three states assemble to repel a common danger; and so, while they fight singly, all are conquered" (Agricola, ch. 12). "But in the province itself there were always some unsubdued peoples," says Baronius, over whom Lucius could have reigned. But who, pray, were those unsubdued peoples? Wretched little bands of barbarians, driven from their proper seats, without king or law, inhabiting the groves and mountains and the most desolate corners of the island; that any king with supreme command presided over them, no credible history teaches; nor would it teach it if Geoffrey himself were to come back to life. Add that this conjecture contradicts every tradition of the matter, whether of fact or of fiction; for it supposes that rude and unsubdued barbarians received the faith before the provincials; against which figment all the monuments of antiquity protest, and all the historians, who write that in the time of Lucius not certain wild and rustic tribes, but the province itself, embraced the faith. Hence it is that Sabellicus says, "Britain was the first of all the provinces to take upon herself the name of Christ publicly and by ordinance"; and against the cardinal protests the Eleutherian letter itself, which presents Lucius as governing the whole British kingdom by the counsel of his nobles. Since, therefore, these arguments are altogether feeble, the cardinal proceeds in his answers to the objections. "Dio relates," he says, "that the Britons crossed that wall which had been set in the midst between them and the camps of the Romans. By which it is plainly signified that Britain was at that [ p. 248 ] time so divided that the Romans occupied that part which lay on this side of the wall, while the Britons freely possessed the other part, situated beyond the wall, and often, issuing forth from that wall, provoked the Romans to battle. Thus, then, nothing prevents Lucius from having been king of Britain; for however much the Romans held the island in part, he could nevertheless have been king of that further part which the Britons freely possessed." But there is nothing sound in this [Greek: ouden hygies]. For, in the first place, the translator of Dio deceived the most learned man; for Dio did not write that the Britons crossed the wall, as the Latin version has it, but that certain nations of the island, most hostile to the Britons properly so called, crossed the wall. "For," he says, "the nations in the island having crossed over the wall" [Greek: ton gar en te neso ethnon hyperbebekoton to teichos], book lxxii; and the writers always distinguish those peoples from the Britons; although it ought not to be doubted that many of the Britons, that is, of the most ancient inhabitants of the island, passed their lives beyond the wall. The cardinal would have it, then, that this Lucius reigned in that part of the island which lies outside Hadrian's wall. But how few, pray, of those countrymen of ours who maintain the story know in what region that wall was raised and drawn? Whoever does will scarcely believe that the things said of Lucius and of the British kingdom suit some petty barbarian king, I know not whom, beyond the wall. Nor is the question whether some Lucius, somewhere in the world, was converted to the faith of Christ; but whether such a king as presided over the British kingdom under God alone, and whom Eleutherius bishop of Rome was to instruct in the administration of his kingdom, publicly embraced the Christian faith. To say nothing of the archflamens and flamens of Geoffrey, whom this fancy casts out beyond Hadrian's wall, together with London and York and the City of the Legions, or Caerleon. Who, pray, ever heard that Fugatius and Damianus, passing by the Roman province, which embraced almost the whole of the present kingdom of England and of Wales, betook themselves to the barbarians beyond the wall? This also most utterly overthrows the fable, that it is most certain and most notorious that those barbarous nations which held the parts of the island beyond the wall did not submit to the yoke of Christ until long afterwards, and after provincial Britain had for some centuries publicly received the Christian faith; which is sufficiently established from Gildas alone, On the Destruction of Britain. In the third place, therefore, but in passing, and as though he could scarcely give credit to himself, the cardinal adds: "Lucius, moreover, could also have been some petty king, placed in Britain under the Roman dominion, and obeying the Roman empire." Now although this answer might be dispatched in a single word, as being by no means to be compared with those which he had set down above, yet it is most certain that the story lacks not only all credibility and probability, but even all color, unless it be fetched from this quarter. Let us see, therefore, in a few words, whether even this can in any way be reconciled with the things that are said of Lucius, and which the Eleutherian letter proclaims concerning him. First, then, concerning that petty king there is the deepest silence among the approved historians who wrote of British affairs, Dio, Julius Capitolinus, and Lampridius; nor is anything said by them which hints at any such petty king in the [ p. 249 ] whole province wielding royal authority; nay, they so describe the state and condition of those times as to show that there was none. Next, the Letter asserts that Lucius wrote to Eleutherius asking him to see that the Roman laws were sent to him; which, as none of those beyond the wall, bitterest enemies as they were of the Roman name, would have done, so no provincial had any need to send envoys to Rome to bring back those laws, which they saw expounded and administered throughout the whole province by men of the highest rank. Nay, the poet hints that long before these times the Britons were thoroughly versed in the Roman law; for, "Eloquent Gaul has trained the pleaders of Britain" (Juvenal, Sat. 15.111). Nor were those petty kings, whom the Romans anywhere tolerated as instruments of servitude, possessed of such power over their subjects that the choice of what laws they should use lay in their own hands; it is even ridiculous that a petty king under Roman dominion, one who, as the cardinal speaks, "obeyed the Roman empire," should have sought imperial laws from the bishop of Rome. And what has all this to do with the Lucius of Eleutherius, not to say of Geoffrey, who, as the vicar of God and subject to him alone, presided over the whole British kingdom, and administered it by the counsel of his subjects? Then again, that supposititious Eleutherius, whose letter by no means savors of the spirit of those most ancient times, condemns and rejects the imperial laws as unfit for the civil administration of the kingdom; which the true Eleutherius would neither have done, nor have dared to do. For my part, I would by no means deny that a certain Lucius, perhaps sprung of royal blood, and of the greatest authority among the Britons, took up the profession of the Christian religion about those times, and promoted the faith to the utmost of his power. But that the fable-mongers transformed him into a very great king, and (because it was perfectly well known that no one succeeded him in any kingdom) into a childless one [Greek: ateknon]; and that the letter of Eleutherius to him, as it is called, is fictitious: this no one, I think, will deny who turns his mind away from inveterate prejudices to the knowledge and contemplation of the truth.
That is, on the origin and progress of idolatry, chapter x [of that series].

Chapter argument: The knowledge of the true God; he who alone can be known was called "unknown"; the notorious superstition [Greek: deisidaimonia] of the city of Athens; the division of the city according to the names and worship of idols; a twin superstition among the Jews; what "pagos" signifies; very many acknowledge one natural God; Antisthenes, the Brahmans; Sophocles; Menander; the books of Numa found and burned; Valerius Soranus; Orpheus; Homer; Prudentius; the seven-lettered name [Greek: heptagrammaton].

I. In the midst of these shadows, and in this confusion of all things and religions, it is clear that very many retained some knowledge and memory of the one true God, infinitely distant from all those fictitious gods whom they superstitiously worshipped. The writings of nearly all the historians and philosophers teach this. For how few there are among the ancient [ p. 250 ] sages who do not celebrate some divine nature existing above all things, ruling all, preserving all, and disposing all at its own pleasure? Now although this true God alone exhibits in all things that which may be known of him [Greek: to gnoston], so much so that he alone can truly be known, because he alone exists, and cannot be utterly unknown, because he is God, nevertheless, because it did not please them to retain this only true God in their knowledge, he alone was called "the unknown" [Greek: agnostos]. Hence, when mention was to be made of the God of the Jews, it was customary to add, "whoever at last he may be" [Greek: hostis pote houtos estin], as it stands in Dio's History, book xxxvi. And in Lucian's Philopatris we find, "By the Unknown God at Athens." So Philostratus in the Life of Apollonius, book vi, chapter ii: "And this at Athens, where altars are set up to unknown daemons." The inscription had "gods" [theon]; Philostratus substituted "daemons" [daimonon], after the manner of the Pythagoreans. The origin of this religion is attributed to Socrates. He, as Justin testifies in his First Apology, "exhorted the Athenians to the knowledge of that unknown God through the inquiry of reason."

II. But whatever Socrates may have done, it is most certain that Paul, from the inscription of the Epimenidean altar, which among others was dedicated "to the unknown God" [Greek: theo agnosto], took occasion to preach the true God. And these things were at Athens, where there was scarcely anything that was not sacred; since the city itself was "all one altar, all one sacrifice and offering to the gods," as Xenophon testifies in his treatise On the Athenian Republic. For they distributed the whole city into districts marked with the names of gods. They constituted the city thus: 1. the Rock of Cronus [Kronou Pagos]; 2. the Rock of Pan [Panos Pagos]; 3. the Rock of Poseidon [Poseidonos Pagos]; 4. the Rock of Ares [Areios Pagos]; 5. the Rock of Hermes [Hermou Pagos]. Now "pagos" among the Greeks signifies not the Latin "pagus" [a village], but a rock. The quarters of the city were so named because upon each was placed some rock (that is, a pillar or stele) consecrated to this or that idol. From this the use of the word was first transferred to signify some part of the city. The same thing that was done at Athens was done also at Jerusalem: Jer. 11:13, "For according to the number of your cities are your gods, O Judah! and according to the number of the streets of Jerusalem you have set up altars to the shameful thing, altars to burn incense to Baal."

III. But it was not at Athens only that altars were erected to the unknown God: "Near it there is an altar of unknown gods," says Pausanias in his Eliaca. That this unknown God was one only, they sometimes confessed. "The popular gods are many, the natural God is one," said Antisthenes, as quoted by Cicero in On the Nature of the Gods, book i, chapter xiii. "That great living being, the soul of the world," said the Pythagoreans. To him all of them assigned at least the supreme place in the heavens. So the Brahmans instruct Apollonius in Philostratus, book iii, chapter xi: "The first and most perfect seat must be assigned to God, the begetter of this living creature [the universe]; the second to the gods who govern its parts."

IV. Indeed, some advanced so far under the auspices and guidance of reason, [ p. 251 ] that for the sake of this one God, the maker of all, they judged that all idolatrous worship was to be cast away. Sophocles speaks admirably (see the Fragments of Sophocles): "There is in truth one, one only God, who alone framed the fabric of heaven, the dark blue back of the sea, and the unbridled winds. But we wretched mortals, wandering in heart, have fashioned for ourselves a solace for our calamities: forms of gods in stone or wood, or wrought of molten gold or ivory; and while we sacrifice to these, while on the appointed day we pay them solemn honors, we fancy ourselves pious." And Justin in his Apology to the Emperor: "In saying that the works of men's hands ought not to be worshipped, we say the same things as Menander the comic poet and those who have affirmed these things."

V. It is also probable that those books of Numa Pompilius, which were found beneath the Janiculum in the year 556 from the founding of the city, and which the senate ordered to be burned in the public assembly, because Quintus Petilius, the city praetor, having read them, declared upon oath that they tended to the dissolution of the established religions, taught one God, and that he was to be worshipped without images.

VI. Lactantius shows that the gentiles of his time contended that this one God was to be called the supreme Jupiter. Hence Valerius Soranus: "Almighty Jupiter, progenitor of kings, of things, and of gods, and mother of the gods, God one and all." Concerning him there is that famous golden song of Orpheus to Musaeus: "Behold him alone, the king of the world: he is One, self-begotten; all things are made the offspring of the One; he himself moves throughout them all; no mortal beholds him, but he himself beholds all mortals." And again: "Zeus is one, Hades one, the sun one, Dionysus one; one God in all things." This is he of whom Virgil, following Homer, so often repeats the solemn phrase, "father of gods and king of men." They wished him also to be Serapis, as in the epigram: "Seven vowels praise me, the great imperishable God, the unwearied Father of all things; I am the imperishable lyre of all, who have attuned the lyric melodies of the heavenly whirl." For Serapis is a seven-lettered name [Greek: heptagrammaton], and was so called according to the testimony of Hesychius; unless one should rather judge that the name Jehovah is indicated by the seven vowels. [ p. 252 ] Rightly therefore Tertullian, Apology chapter xvii, teaches that "even the worshippers of idols, when they are in straits, lift their eyes to heaven, and forgetting their gods, naturally invoke the one God." Similar things are found in Justin, On the Monarchy of God and in his Apologies; Athenagoras in his Embassy; Clement, Stromata iii; Arnobius, book ii Against the Gentiles; Lactantius; [Eusebius,] Preparation for the Gospel, book xi, chapter ix; Theodoret, On the Cure of the Affections of the Greeks, book ii. Prudentius puts it excellently in the Apotheosis, lines 268-277: "Consult the ravings of bearded Plato, consult also the goatish Cynics, and those sinews which Aristotle dreams up and weaves with twisted vertigo; all these, though a doubtful labyrinth and winding error drive them about, though they themselves are accustomed to promise a hen, or a cock, that God as a physician may deign to show himself favorable to the dying, yet when they come to the rule of reason and of art, their muddied perceptions and their litigious arguments in their jarring fashions conclude in one Deity." But that the wisest of the gentiles held all these notions of the one God shut up and imprisoned, and did not glorify him as God with worship and obedience, the apostle clearly shows in the Epistle to the Romans, chapter 1.
That is, on the origin and progress of idolatry, chapter xi [of that series].

Chapter argument: A new and pestilent corruption of the notions of the one supreme God; the Homeric Jupiter occupies his place; contempt of the true God; Cicero's judgment on the religion of the Jews; the Cretan Jupiter, where born and where he died; the opinion of Papinius [Statius] and of Callimachus; the sepulcher of Jupiter in Crete; the scholiast of Callimachus; the shades [manes] of Jupiter; Summanus; Dis pater; the manes, who and what they were; images fashioned after the likeness of bodies; souls in the heavens and shades in the underworld; remnants of Sabaism; the notions of the true God confounded with the superstition of Jupiter and the Sun; the twelve gods; the origin of the superstition; the worship of daemons [Greek: daimonia], ancient and recent; the banquet of the twelve gods [Greek: dodekatheon]; the unnamed crowd of gods.

I. Abusing that obscure notion of the one supreme God, the ancient enemy of the human race assailed the idolaters with a new and astonishing mockery. From among the fictitious gods he substituted one in His place, and feigned that all the others either sat beside this Most High or served him as attendants. This was the Homeric Jupiter, who boasts most absurdly of his power over the inferior deities, Iliad VIII, lines 5-27: "Hear me, all you gods and all you goddesses, that I may speak what the heart in my breast commands me. Let no female god, nor any male, attempt to cut short my word; but all of you together assent to it, that I may most speedily accomplish these works. Whomever of the gods I perceive willingly going apart [ p. 253 ] to bring aid either to the Trojans or to the Danaans, he shall come back to Olympus shamefully smitten; or I will seize him and hurl him into murky Tartarus, far away, where the deepest pit lies beneath the earth, where the gates are iron and the threshold bronze, as far beneath Hades as heaven is from earth; then he shall know by how much I am the mightiest of all the gods. Come now, try it, you gods, that you may all know: hang a golden chain from heaven, and lay hold of it, all you gods and all you goddesses; yet you could not drag down from heaven to the plain Zeus the supreme counselor, not even if you toiled exceedingly. But whenever I should be minded to pull in earnest, I would draw it up with the earth itself and the sea itself; and then I would bind the chain about a peak of Olympus, and all those things would hang aloft in mid air: by so much am I above gods, and above men."

So Homer; all of which Eustathius vainly endeavors to accommodate to natural phenomena out of the doctrine of the Stoics, of Plato, and of others. But he often drags Homer's sayings to a point to which Homer himself never suspected they tended; just as Donatus does in his explanation of the plot-constructions of Terence. Plato says admirably in the Phaedrus: "If anyone, having no faith in the poetic fables, should wish to reduce them to some suitable sense, relying as it were on a certain rustic wisdom, he will assuredly stand in great need of leisure." Now this Jupiter, to whom they assigned the prerogative of supreme dominion, and the things they judged proper to the supreme God, was no other than that most notorious Cretan scoundrel, or rather the devil himself, masked under the tradition of that Cretan Jove. The greatest of orators, in his speech to the pontiffs On His House, chapter lvii: "You, Capitoline Jupiter, whom for your benefits the Roman people has named the Best, and for your power the Greatest; and you, Queen Juno," etc. The God who is Best and Greatest, the Capitoline image, the husband of Juno: Statius, with the most shameful confusion, feigns these to be one and the same, Thebaid book iv, line 782: "Like the Berecynthian mother, while she bids the trembling Curetes leap about the infant Thunderer."

II. And so, although they professed one supreme deity, they utterly despised the one only God as he had revealed himself. Hence the contempt of the religion of the Jews, who alone possessed the knowledge of the one true God. Cicero, in his Oration for Lucius Flaccus, is a most ample witness. "Every state, Laelius, has its own religion," he says in chapter xxviii, "and we have ours. While Jerusalem was yet standing" (that is, while Judea was its own master, not yet subject to the Romans, for the city was by no means overthrown at that time) "and the Jews were at peace, even then the religion of their sacred rites was abhorrent to the splendor of this empire, the dignity of our name, and the institutions of our ancestors: and now all the more, because that nation has shown by arms what it thought of our empire; how dear it was to the immortal gods, it has taught us by the fact that it has been conquered, farmed out for tribute, and enslaved."

[ p. 254 ] III. Hence concerning this Jupiter of theirs, whom they knew to be a man sprung from men, they could never resolve the perplexity of their minds. The most learned poet, who acknowledges that his Thunderer was once an infant, yet denies that he died, Thebaid i, 277: "Yet here the honor of a temple is pleasing to you; guilty Ida finds favor, and Crete that lies about your shade." These things he had drawn from Callimachus, in his Hymn to Zeus: "O Zeus, some say that you were born on the mountains of Ida; others, O Zeus, in Arcadia: which of them, Father, have lied? The Cretans are always liars; for the Cretans have even built a tomb for you, O King; but you did not die, for you exist forever."

But in order to convict the Cretans of falsehood concerning the sepulcher of Jupiter, they devised a not unclever fiction, which the old scholiast on Callimachus mentions. "In Crete," he says, "upon the tomb of Minos was inscribed, 'The tomb of Minos the son of Zeus'; but in time the name of Minos was effaced, so that there remained only 'the tomb of Zeus'; and from this the Cretans speak of the tomb of Zeus."

IV. On this account Athenagoras in his Embassy mocks Callimachus, because, while he believed his Jupiter to have been begotten of men, he would not admit the sepulcher of a mortal man. He was also foolish in asking which of the two were lying, the Arcadians or the Cretans, each claiming for themselves the birth of Jupiter, since Cicero teaches in the third book of On the Nature of the Gods that there were three Joves, of whom the first two, begotten of Aether and Caelus, were born in Arcadia, and the third, son of Saturn, in Crete. Philostratus also records, in the Lives of the Sophists, book ii, that Antiochus the sophist pleaded the cause of the Cretans concerning the sepulcher of Jupiter; and Lactantius proves, from Euhemerus, that he was buried there.

V. Pluto himself, moreover, was nothing other than the shade [manes] of the dead Jupiter; and hence he was called Summanus, that is, the chief of the manes. Whence the beginning of the formula of devotion: "Father Dis, or shade of Jupiter, or by whatever other name it is lawful to name you." For Salmasius observes most correctly, in his Diatribe on Loans, that those whose souls were received into heaven had their manes placed in the underworld. For these manes were, as it were, likenesses of bodies destitute of soul, which the Greeks called shades [skiai] and phantoms of the dead [eidola kamonton]; concerning which the most learned Reynolds discourses excellently at length in his Praelections against Bellarmine, who contends that an eidolon is only the likeness of a false thing, or of one that never existed. This shade is, for Lucretius, "air void of light," book iv, 369. Thus the phantom of Hercules, [ p. 255 ] although he had long since been enrolled among the gods, is placed in the underworld by Homer. And that phantom of Achilles, which was raised up from the dead by Apollonius the magician, demanded that sacred rites be instituted to it by the Thessalians as to some god. The soul, therefore, was not the shade, for the soul they placed on high; nor was it the body, which they saw decay in the earth; but a likeness so fashioned after the figure of the body that it could be seen and recognized by those to whom the man whose shade it was had been known, but could not be touched. So Aeneas in Virgil, Aeneid ii, 771: "As I searched, the unhappy phantom and shade of Creusa herself appeared before my eyes, an image larger than the one I knew." The shade of Deiphobus, too, was mutilated, after the fashion of his body, which Menelaus had cruelly mangled, Aeneid vi, 494: "And here he saw the son of Priam, Deiphobus, torn in his whole body, his face cruelly mangled."

This Jupiter, then, whom all confessed to have been born, whom very many confessed to have been buried, and whose shades they placed in the underworld, they endeavored to raise to the throne of the supreme God, and to exhaust in him all knowledge of the One true God. Hence also in the worship of the gentiles, and in all their sacred rites, there was always a marvelous frustration, no less than that, and no less ridiculous, which Plautus feigned in the double Amphitruo and the double Sosia.

VI. Nor does the heap of confusion end here. Among almost all idolaters everywhere, Sabaism, that is, the worship of the heavenly bodies, firmly maintained some station at least, if not that which it had held from the beginning. In this worship the Sun was alone, supreme, most high, almost the only God. He too occupied the place of the Supreme God. We have shown this above at greater length. Hence a new delusion, a new frustration arose, as if it were a pleasure to the old serpent to make sport of wretched mortals enslaved to blindness and crimes. The notions of the true God, the Cretan Jupiter (or rather Satan masked under that idol), and the Sun, a heavenly luminary, the best things and the worst, were confounded into one idol.

VII. But this miserable confusion of idolatrous superstition advanced still further. The angels, those most noble creatures, serve the true God in the heavens, awaiting his nod in all things. Of this also the gentiles retained some notions, as appears in Orpheus: "Beside your fiery throne stand the much-laboring angels, whose care it is that all things be accomplished for mortals." But even in this ray of divine light, that word of Jude holds good, verse 10: "Whatever they know naturally, as brute beasts, in these things they corrupt themselves." These angels they changed into I know not what lesser gods, fabricators of the world, and inferior governors, to be adored and worshipped with the highest religion and divine honors. For these, in Hellenism, they substituted the gods (as they call them) of the greater nations [dii majorum gentium], who always stood beside Jupiter, the chief of deified men; embracing, in the place of God and the angels, the shades of Jupiter and of the gods of the greater nations.

[ p. 256 ] VIII. They feigned also that lesser deities, godlings [deastri], served the Sun. Especially were the twelve signs of the zodiac thus feigned; hence in Hellenism also they admitted twelve gods of the second order, or eleven together with Jupiter himself. Ennius enclosed them all in this distich: "Juno, Vesta, Minerva, Ceres, Diana, Venus, Mars, Mercury, Jove, Neptune, Vulcan, Apollo." Others say that there were twelve besides Jupiter himself, assigned to him as counselors and assessors. So Seneca: "Jupiter indeed sends the second [bolt], but by the judgment of his council; for he calls in the twelve gods." Hence they were called the Consenting gods [dii consentes], because in every sentence to be passed in the heavens their consent was asked. Whence that saying of Augustine: "Who can endure it, that felicity has been given a place neither among the consenting gods, whom they say are admitted into the council of Jupiter, nor among the gods whom they call select?" Others reckon eleven besides Jupiter himself: "If Jupiter should bring with him eleven gods besides himself" (Plautus, Epidicus v. 1, 4); and again, "The twelve gods, more than there are of immortal gods in heaven, are now my helpers and allies" (ibid. v. 2, 10), by which he intimates that there are just so many in heaven. And Demochares: "O Theophrastus, it was Athenian men who were the judges, not the twelve gods." To all these together altars were erected everywhere; in the territory of Ilium: "At Dekton is shown an altar of the twelve gods; they call it a foundation of Agamemnon" (Strabo, Geography, book xiii, chapter i). And again: "Then the harbor of the Achaeans, where are the altars of the twelve gods." And at Athens, in the forum itself, one was erected by the tyrant Pisistratus, as Thucydides testifies, History, book vi. These gods, according to Apollodorus, were employed as arbiters between Neptune and Minerva when they contended over the name of Athens; and before them Mars, accused of the murder of Halirrhothius, pleaded for his life on the Areopagus, that is, at the rock afterwards so named from that judgment. Hence the "banquet of the twelve gods" [Greek: dodekatheos] of Augustus in Suetonius, at which there were present just so many guests adorned in the dress of the gods.

IX. The origin of the superstition was from Egypt, according to Herodotus, book ii, chapter iv: "They say," he writes, "that the Egyptians were the first to employ the appellations of the twelve gods, and that the Greeks received them from them." But the twelve gods among the Egyptians were nothing other than the twelve signs of the zodiac.

X. Among these, as we have said, they preferred one infinitely before all the rest, and feigned that the others composed themselves according to his pleasure. Whence that saying of Plautus in the Casina, Act ii, scene v, line 23: "Provided only this one Jupiter is propitious to you, you need not care a straw for those petty gods." And Ulysses, speaking of Hector to Achilles in Homer: "He rages terribly, trusting in Zeus, and regards neither men nor gods." [ p. 257 ] They therefore supposed that these gods discharged a kind of mediatorial office with that supreme God on behalf of mortals. No scene occurs more frequently in the poets than the prayers of the gods of the second order to the supreme God on behalf of those with whom they had contracted some bond of relationship. So Venus pleads for the imperiled Trojans, in Virgil, Aeneid x, 18-20: "O Father! O eternal power of men and gods! Do you see how the Rutulians exult, and how Turnus is borne conspicuous on his horses through their midst?" Of the same kind is that of Pindar, Pythian Ode vi, 23: "Above all to reverence the son of Cronus, deep-voiced lord of lightnings and thunderbolts, chief of the gods." But these mediators, themselves mortals, they pursued with prayers and supplications; just as even today among the papists the worship of daemons [Greek: daimonia] is carried on; concerning which the Diatribe of the most learned Mede, On the Apostasy of the Latter Times, should be consulted. And thus, God and the angels, the sun and the signs of the zodiac, the Cretan Jupiter and other dead men, being confounded together by the horrid madness of superstition, all the notions concerning the One God were rendered corrupt, vain, and utterly useless.

XI. There is no need to say anything of the remaining crowd of gods, whose origin is most uncertain. Plautus divides them into the gods above, the gods below, and the middle gods [medioxumi]; elsewhere into the great, the least, and the dish-gods [patellarii]. Ovid calls them the commons of the gods, In Ibin, 79: "You also, plebs of the gods above, Fauns, Satyrs, and Lares, Rivers and Nymphs, and the race of demigods." In patching together the fables of these gods, the ancients scarcely anywhere agreed; accordingly Pausanias in his Boeotica, after he had reviewed the opinion of the Thebans about certain gods, adds: "And the Megarians do not agree with it; and as for the rest, the Greeks for the most part tell them differently from one another." They distributed them all into fixed classes, assigning some to heaven, who were called supercelestial, in-celestial, and upon-celestial [hyperouranioi, enouranioi, epouranioi]; some to the air, who were called aetherial and aerial [enaitherioi, enaerioi]; others to the earth and sea, who were commonly called terrestrial [epigeioi], and more particularly earth-dwelling, marine, of the sea, in the sea [epichthonioi, enalioi, thalattioi, enthalattioi]; others to the underworld, who were called subterranean, chthonic, under-earthly, beneath-the-earth [hypogeioi, chthonioi, hypochthonioi, katachthonioi]. They were named also from their peculiar offices and charges: guardians of the hearth, guardians of the city, ancestral, hospitable, paternal, gods of the marketplace, givers of fruits, warlike, trophy-keeping, gods of suppliants, gods of victory, averters of evil, releasers, purifiers, saviors, securers, avengers of blood, gods of those who turn to them for vengeance, gods of birth, gods of marriage, and so forth. And there is scarcely anyone who does not know that Papism has carried this whole stage-furniture of the heathen theater into the Christian religion.
That is, on the origin and progress of idolatry, chapter xii [of that series].

Chapter argument: The progress of idolatry into every kind of confusion; the peculiar gods of the nations adopted by themselves; the self-deification [Greek: autotheismos] of very many; the deification [Greek: ektheosis] of Alexander among the Lacedaemonians; the fable of Tiberius wishing to enroll Christ among the gods; in the fashioning of images and the painting of gods, contempt of all religion; the madness of the Egyptians; penalties established by law against foreign gods, inflicted only upon the worshippers of the one true God; the truly golden words of Justin Martyr.

I. We have spoken of those whom the crowd of idolaters venerated religiously, commonly and generally, with consenting suffrage, through almost the whole world. As polytheism grew strong, every nation, every city, all peoples adopted for themselves whatever gods they pleased. In earnest and in jest, publicly and privately, from ancient tradition, from present authority, from vain affections, a most abundant crop of deities sprang up everywhere. Pausanias relates these origins of new gods in his Attica, book i: "The Oropians," he says, "were the first to establish the custom of regarding Amphiaraus as a god; afterwards all the Greeks came to esteem him so. I can enumerate others also who, having formerly been men, have received divine honors from the Greeks, and to whom cities have even been dedicated." For at length the custom everywhere prevailed that, when anyone had pleased men, he became a god; for every people had the right of enrolling among the gods whomever they wished. And from this a not trifling controversy arose between the Roman tax-farmers and the Greeks. For since it was provided by the censorial law that the lands of the immortal gods should pay no tribute, the tax-farmers stoutly denied that any were immortal who had once been men, as Marcus Tullius testifies in book iii of On the Nature of the Gods.

II. Even Alexander, Caligula, Claudius, Domitian, and other monsters of wickedness, wished to be reckoned gods while alive, secure perhaps in the thought that when dead they would be in the underworld. Callisthenes, in Curtius, elegantly lashes that madness: "Am I, then, and you, Cleo, to make gods? Is the king to receive the warrant of his divinity from us? I should like to put your power to the test: make someone a king, if you can make a god; it is easier to bestow an empire than heaven" (Quintus Curtius, book viii, chapter v). Force was threatened against the Lacedaemonians for denying the right of deity, until he was admitted by that formula which Cicero (who so sharply attacks the Clodian dedication of his house) would have denied to be legitimate: "Since Alexander wishes to be a god," they said, "let him be a god." Athenaeus, book v, testifies that the Athenians also at that time fined the orator Demades ten talents, because he endeavored to persuade them to acknowledge Alexander [ p. 259 ] as a god; although these same Athenians afterwards bestowed divine honors, while he was alive and present, upon Demetrius Poliorcetes, how trifling a part of an Alexander! The self-deification of Claudius [read rather Caligula] vanished at the sound of thunder; for fleeing into his tent he cried, "This is a God, not Claudius."

III. From this custom among the gentiles a fable was born: that Tiberius attempted to have Christ enrolled among the gods of the Romans, but, the senate opposing, desisted from his purpose. Would that it had never entered the minds of good men to spread abroad fables of this sort; and God forbid that anyone hereafter should give them credit.

IV. Kings also, and the Roman emperors, enrolled in the census of the gods whomever they pleased; and they themselves, all after Julius, were judged by the senate either gods or enemies: whence the jest of the dying Vespasian, "I think I am becoming a god." But for one to be held a god, it was generally necessary that he die wearing the purple; whence Eutropius says of Diocletian, "There befell him what has befallen no man since men were born, that, though he died a private citizen, he was nevertheless enrolled among the deified." Yet this is not everywhere true. Alexander ordered divine honors to be paid to his friend Hephaestion, and sacrifice to be offered to him (Curtius, book x). And Antoninus Caracalla himself, that monster of a man, was enrolled among the gods by Macrinus, who had killed him, as Aelius Spartianus testifies. Concerning Hadrian, who first sacrificed his favorite Antinous, that he might use his entrails for divinations and sorceries [Greek: manteias and manganeias], and then enrolled him among the gods and set up images to him in every part of the world, there is a horrible history in Dio, History, book lxix; which impiety Clement, in his Protrepticus, and Tertullian, in his Apology, castigate as it deserves. To private persons also it was granted to worship privately whomever they pleased. Witness the saying of Cornelia to her son Caius Gracchus: "When I am dead, you will offer the parental sacrifices to me, and invoke the divine shades [dii manes] of your parent; at that time let it not shame you to seek the prayers of those as gods whom, while they were alive and present, you held abandoned and deserted."

V. But this contempt of all religion, and of all notions of Deity, advanced so far that it became at last a sport to mingle the foulest crimes with their shameful superstitions. What outrages they practiced in fashioning and painting the images of the gods, the reader may see in Pliny, book xxxv, chapters ix and x. Nothing was more usual with the painters (of whom Cicero says, "We know all the gods by that countenance which the painters and sculptors have chosen to give them") than to paint the goddesses from the portraits of harlots whom they fancied; which was the practice of Arellius, who was never not burning with love for some woman. And the image which Clodius consecrated in the house of Cicero under the name of the goddess Liberty, Cicero himself proves, in his Oration to the Pontiffs On His House, to have been a harlot of Tanagra. Thus nowhere more than in religion itself do they appear to have cast off all sense of religion. The old serpent, then, having conquered the human race, also triumphed over it; nor was he content to hold them bound to himself, unless he should moreover hold his captives in derision, and foully set them against one another [ p. 260 ] with arms and war, for the sake of the most senseless superstitions. Such is the remarkable proof of human blindness and misery, laboring under the yoke of Satan, which the Satirist exhibits in the neighboring peoples of Egypt, as he speaks, Satire xv, lines 33-38. The crocodile was the torch of war: "Between neighboring towns there burns an old and ancient grudge, an immortal hatred, a wound that can never be healed; Ombi and Tentyra still blaze. The height of fury on both sides springs from this, that each place hates the deities of its neighbors, since each believes that those gods alone are to be held gods which it itself worships." And he writes that both peoples continued the fight, undertaken for this cause, until the victors devoured the vanquished, ibid., lines 77-83: "Here a certain man, hurrying his course in excessive terror, slips and is taken: he is cut into many pieces and morsels, that one dead man might suffice for many, and the victorious mob eats him whole, gnawing his very bones; nor did they boil him in a glowing cauldron or on spits: they thought it too long and too slow to wait for hearth-fires, content with the raw corpse." That the poet related this incredible savagery of the Egyptians with almost historical fidelity, I am persuaded both by the inbred and blind fury of very many, even now, over dissensions about matters of almost no moment in religion, and by certain deeds of nearly the same kind which men who were Christians in name only have perpetrated against innocent professors of the Christian religion.

VI. But however the idolaters carried on quarrels and enmities among themselves, in this they all seem to have conspired, to spurn the only true God and his worship. Although, therefore, it was provided by law at Athens that no one should venerate foreign divinities [Greek: xena daimonia], and at Rome by the Twelve Tables, "Let no one have gods separately for himself, nor worship privately new or foreign gods, unless publicly adopted," yet no one (with the single exception of Socrates, whose indictment ran, "Socrates does wrong in not acknowledging the gods whom the city acknowledges, and in introducing other new divinities") can be found to have been prosecuted for violated religion on account of worshipping another god besides those publicly adopted, except such as venerated the one true God. And with the rise of Christianity this madness of men and envy of Satan broke forth most of all. Justin, that most ancient Christian writer, most elegantly reproaches the philosophic emperors with this blindness and rage; and with his truly golden words it pleases me to put an end to this digression. "We alone," he says, "are hated [ p. 261 ] for the name of Christ; and though we commit nothing against the laws, we are dragged to death as malefactors; while others, in other places, worship trees and rivers, mice and cats and crocodiles, and many of the irrational animals. Nor indeed are the same objects honored by all, but different ones in different places, so that all are impious in one another's eyes, because they do not worship the same things. And this is the one thing you have to charge against us, that we do not worship the same gods as you, nor offer to the dead libations and the savor of fat, nor crowns for their images and sacrifices; for you know perfectly well that the same animals are with some accounted gods, with others wild beasts, with others sacrificial victims."

books.title: Of the Abrahamic and the Mosaic Theology

blocks.text: Of Abrahamic Theology

Chapter argument: The state and condition of pious theologians after the Babylonian apostasy. Abraham entangled in superstitions before his calling, Josh. 24:2. Jewish fables concerning Ur of the Chaldees, and Abraham cast into the fire. The calling of Abraham, how it was accomplished, and when. The enlargement of Noachic theology. The renewal of the divine covenant. The institution of circumcision. The new separation of the church from the world completed.

I. In the preceding discourse we set forth the origin, progress, and ruinous outcome of the Babylonian or Nimrodic apostasy, investigating the first beginnings of idolatry; and thus, so far as our present design requires, we have dispatched natural theology. That the apostate church was very nearly catholic, or universal, is plain from the event. Noah indeed was still among the living when the tower of Babel was being built, and he survived the dispersion of his rebellious descendants. For this proud defection occurred in the seven hundred and second year of Noah's life, after which he survived two hundred and forty-eight years, or thereabouts; and Shem, accordingly, considerably longer. That these men ever fell away from the true worship of the true God it would be impious to suspect. While Noah therefore was yet alive, many without doubt, especially among those with whom the knowledge and use of the primeval language remained, cleaved to God, the Creator of all things, in the promised Redeemer, and persevered in his sacred worship. As is the manner of witnesses to the truth in every catholic apostasy, scattered here and there, held in hatred, exposed to the persecutions of the ungodly, they seem to have led a toilsome life upon the earth. [ p. 262 ] Things divine and human being therefore reduced to this state, while idolatry daily grew and multiplied, and while the impiety and wickedness of men prevailed more and more, every hope of ecclesiastical reformation, or of recalling the human race to a sound condition, being cast away, God determined to erect a new church, furnished with a new theology. But this could not be done except by the visible separation of certain persons from the rest. It was effected, accordingly, in the calling of Abraham (which is recorded in the eleventh and twelfth chapters of Genesis), by which God accomplished that purpose of his.

II. Now from the renewal of the church in the family of Noah after the flood, through the enlargement of postlapsarian Adamic theology in the compact of the express covenant, down to the apostasy and cursing of Ham, there intervened 40 years, or thereabouts. So long did the church endure pure, newly restored after the flood. From that reformation which was made in the ejection of Ham and his family from the assemblies and communion of the godly, to the Babylonian defection, there were 62 years. In so brief a space of time did that reformed church fall away, in horrible fashion, from the worship of the true God. Between that defection, moreover, and the separation of Abraham, and the institution of the new church in his calling, there intervened 324 years. The whole space of time, therefore, during which Noachic theology was the rule and norm of divine worship and of the obedience owed to God, was 426 years.

III. That Abraham himself, before his solemn calling, was to some degree contaminated by the contagion of the Babylonian defection and crime, Scripture relates, Josh. 24:2: "Your fathers of old dwelt beyond the river" (Joshua is addressing the Israelites), "Terah the father of Abraham, and the father of Nahor: and they served strange gods." That Abraham himself is here intended all the Jews deny, a proud and obstinate race of men, eager to ascribe everything to themselves and to their fathers according to the flesh, and nothing to divine grace and mercy. That whole people traced its paternal line from Abraham, its maternal from Nahor. But God here charges the authors of the whole Israelite stock with that crime for no other end than to show them that whatever they obtained above other mortals, they obtained by no other benefit than that of his mere grace. For he foreknew with what pride that stiff-necked people, puffed up with the glorious name of the fathers and the title of sons of Abraham, would despise his righteousness in the promised seed, and his mercy. Nor is it here only that the Holy Spirit endeavors to draw them back from the vain persuasion of their own righteousness. To this very day, dispersed through the whole world, boasting of nothing but the merits and obedience of the fathers and their own righteousness, they clamor against evangelical grace, miserably perishing.

IV. But in order to give some color to this conceit of empty self-glory, they have invented a most rotten fable about Ur of the Chaldees, out of which, as the sacred page testifies, God called Abraham. The Targum ascribed to Jonathan Ben Uzziel, on Gen. 11:28, narrates the matter thus: [ p. 263 ] "Now it came to pass, when Nimrod cast Abram into the furnace of fire because he would not worship his idol, that the fire had no power to burn him; and therefore a doubt arose, Haran saying, If Nimrod prevail, I will stand on his side; but if Abram prevail, I will be on his side. And when all the peoples who were there saw that the fire had no dominion over Abram, they said among themselves, Is not Haran the brother of Abram full of divinations and auguries? Surely he has enchanted the fire so that it should not burn his brother. Immediately fire fell from the high heavens and consumed him, and Haran died before the face of his brother, when he was burned in the land of his nativity, in the furnace of fire which the Chaldeans had made for his brother." So he writes, after his manner, a most absurd patcher-together of fables. He interprets Ur as fire, which is indeed the signification of the word, though it is plainly the name of a place; and "al-pene" [Hebrew: al-pene], that is, "before the face of his father," he takes to mean "while his father was yet living and surviving, in the sight of his father." While others, as was said above, trifle that Haran died before his father as a punishment, because he was a maker of images. That the lying Jews seized the occasion of the fable from the history of Nebuchadnezzar casting the three holy men into the furnace of fire because they would not adore the idol he had set up, which stands in Dan. 3, no one fails to see.

V. That Abraham, for refusing the worship of idols, was punished by the king of the Chaldeans not with fire but with exile, Maimonides relates out of the traditions of the Sabians, More Nebuchim, part iii. chap. xxix. The masters of the Jews put the story together a little differently in Bereshith Rabba, chap. xxxviii. They allege, namely, that Abraham broke the images of his father, and on that account was denounced by his own father to king Nimrod; by whom, after various disputations, he was cast into the fire, and was delivered by an angel. But that the greatest injury has been done by these fable-mongers to the memory of Terah, a man, as it appears, pious and holy, I do not at all doubt. Maimonides himself, in the first book of his treatise On the Worship of the Stars, relates at length that the king of the Chaldeans wished to kill him on account of his disputations against idols and the violence he had offered to the images. And the fable is sufficiently ancient; for Jerome mentions it, Hebrew Questions on Genesis. And long before him Josephus, Antiquities, book i. chap. vii. He says, "When the Chaldeans and the other peoples of Mesopotamia rose up against him, he resolved to remove; and relying on the will and favor of God, he took possession of the land of Canaan." Nor indeed is it unlike the truth that impious idolaters, after he had been instructed in the true knowledge of God, rose up against him. And of the contention of Abraham with his father Terah concerning the making and worship of images, scarcely any even of the ancient Christians failed to be persuaded. So John of Antioch, surnamed Malalas, an unpublished trifler [anekdotos] in the Bodleian Library, book iii.: [ p. 264 ] "But Abraham, when he had now come to the knowledge of God, and had considered with himself that the images fashioned by his father Terah were of dead men, and that therefore they ought not, being dust and ashes, to be worshipped as celestial gods, dared to rebuke him in such words as these: Why (said he) do you lead mortal men into error for the sake of base gain? For there is one God in heaven, who formed all these things that we see. And no sooner said than done, he broke all the images in pieces, and forsaking his father, took his journey toward Mesopotamia."

VI. But in that they bring in Terah not only as a maker but as a seller of images, the fable betrays itself. "For the ancient image-making did not do this, nor did they go about the cities selling the gods," said Apollonius to a statuary who was selling images of the gods, in Philostratus, book v. chap. vii.: "The ancient statuaries did not make merchandise of the gods, nor did they go about the cities as if to sell them; for in that manner one ought to treat Hyrcanian and Scythian slaves, not gods." But Ur [Hebrew: ur], as we have said, was the name of a place, whatever the word itself may signify.

VII. Rambam indeed confesses that Abraham was ensnared in the worship of idols up to the forty-eighth year of his age, but that, in that year of his age, he cast off all communion with them. He was indeed called by God in the seventy-fifth year of his age, Gen. 12:4, or perhaps one year earlier, when he left Ur and came into Haran. Between his renunciation of idolatrous worship and his calling, therefore, there intervened at least twenty-six years. And to what end is all this? Why, that we may know that God did not elect or call Abraham out of his mere grace, but on account of his righteousness and obedience, and love of God, and the merits of his former life. So constantly, seizing every occasion, do these poor creatures of men undertake the patronage of their own righteousness against the grace of God.

VIII. I confess indeed that very many of the ancients, deceived by the Jews, believed that Abraham arrived at the knowledge of the true God through the contemplation of the stars, before he was called. Suidas narrates the fable at length under the word Abraam; all these things are the dreams of the vainglorious Jews, which the Arabs imitate, as may be seen in Abraham Ecchellensis, Arabic History, p. 125.

IX. With a not dissimilar design Simon Episcopius forges a figment of nearly the same kind, Theological Institutes, book ii. chap. iv. He writes that it is credible that Abraham before his calling, though a man of upright life and a serious lover of probity, was addicted to the worship of idols. And why, pray, should it not be credible? What does he think follows from it? "Dispositions of this kind," he says, "are not only capable but also compliant, as soon as some great light shines upon them, by which they are irradiated and affected." [ p. 265 ] There was therefore no need of the irresistible Calvinian grace to engender faith or obedience in Abraham. But what, I ask, is "serving a hypothesis" [Greek: to douleuein hypothesei], if this is not? Of the probity and integrity of Abraham before his calling we read in sacred Scripture not so much as a syllable [Greek: gry, "a grunt"]. He is only said to have served strange gods. But the purpose of the Holy Spirit and of this man appear contrary the one to the other. The Spirit everywhere proclaims the grace of God; this man, the probity and integrity of men destitute of divine grace. The Spirit sets forth those things which make for exalting the glory of divine mercy; this man carves out those things which make for the praise of capable and compliant dispositions. But such is the manner of some men: they think they have accomplished nothing unless they have said much to the reproach of the grace of God.

X. For the rest, nothing forbids our believing that Abraham, even in the midst of the worship of idols, retained at least some measure of the knowledge of the true God. For he alone is not said to worship strange gods who, having cast off all reverence for the true Deity, adopts other gods for himself as the object of his worship; but those also who would join other gods with him in whatever order, or who have thought themselves to worship him in images and idols.

XI. Now that solemn calling of Abraham, since it was effected by an oracle, or a certain external word, proceeded from the efficacious and heart-turning operation of the Holy Spirit; in that he who had lain in such darkness, exposed to such temptations as could arise from the close bond of kinsmen and intimates, and from the fear of a distant pilgrimage, destitute of all human aid, presented himself at once obedient to the word with so even and willing a mind, and submitted himself to the command of God. Moreover, that calling had respect both to holiness of heart and life, internal, in the renovation of the whole man, and to an external separation to the worship of the true God; for it is not counted to Abraham's praise only that he worshipped God with a renewed rite and solemnly called upon him, but also that he was faithful, the friend of God, that he received the promises, that he was justified by faith, and proved a most constant lover of God. God therefore, being about to erect a new church by a visible separation from the world and from all mixtures and corruptions of false religion, laid its foundations in the true faith, holiness, and obedience of the called, upon the promised Messiah himself.

XII. Abraham being in this manner called according to the will of God, God delivered and committed to him, as the norm of worship and of living, the Noachic theology explained and enlarged by new revelations. And so great and so illustrious an exposition of the divine will and of the mysteries of grace was published in that explanation of postlapsarian theology, that the economy of revelation scarcely obtained a richer harvest from any single revelation, until he should come for whom all things were reserved. For, first, there was given to him a clearer and fuller explication of the ancient promise, and that repeated at various turns, Gen. 12:3, 7; 13:15, 16; 15:1, 5, 6; 17:1, 2, 9; 22:17, 18.

XIII. Christ now began "to look forth at the windows, and to show himself through the lattice" [ p. 266 ] (Song 2:9); nay, "to make haste, and to be like a roe or a young hart upon the mountains of spices" (Song 8:14); that is, to manifest himself openly and sweetly to the faith, love, and desire of the elect. For at least seven times the promise of the conquering and beatifying Seed is renewed. Then, secondly, to this explanation of the promise was added a richer declaration of the covenant of grace, consisting in promise and stipulation. For God not only throws in express mention of the covenant where he deals with Abraham concerning obedience, as was done before with Noah, but also expounds the express nature of the federation, joining the promise with the stipulation of faith and of universal and sincere obedience, Gen. 17:1: "I am God Almighty; walk before my face, and be perfect" [Hebrew: ani El Shaddai, hithhallekh lephanai]. And this solemn intervention he calls a "covenant," ver. 7, and also expounds what was the sense of those words, I am God Almighty; namely, that he would be such to him, in himself and in his omnipotence, undertaking his care, and that it should be eternally well with him through the promised Seed: "I will establish my covenant between me and you," he says, "for an everlasting covenant, to be a God unto you." The promise of the Seed, the foundation of all grace, being premised, God graciously promises that he will be to him, in that Seed, a God pardoning, sanctifying, justifying, and saving, freely and immutably, requiring in turn from him faith and new obedience. Further, thirdly, for the comfort of Abraham himself there is given a certain appropriation of the covenant to his family, so that no one, except from that family, or by virtue of the blessing annexed to that family, should attain blessedness, Gen. 12:3. Thus he is constituted the father of the faithful. Fourthly, circumcision is instituted as the sign of the covenant, Gen. 17:10, 11. And fifthly, the communication of the privileges of the covenant and of the church is granted to the infant seed (which, after it had begun in a peculiar manner to be the object of God's care, the church never absolutely lacked), Gen. 17:7. Nor, lastly, was it the least part of Abraham's theology that God received him into friendship, and by revelations repeated from time to time rendered him so familiar with himself, that he came forth upright, just, pious, and instructed beyond what can be expressed in all divine wisdom and in the mysteries of the faith. But of these things, since they are particulars, and so do not immediately pertain to our design, this is not the place to speak.

XIV. Now from the year of the world 2083 this illustrious exposition of theology took its beginning; and it endured, by the apostle's testimony, 430 years, Gal. 3:17. For Abraham received the promise when he was 76 years old, or at the beginning of that year, when, having now gone out from Haran, he had arrived in the land of Canaan. From the giving of this promise, "It came to pass at the end of the four hundred and thirty years, even the selfsame day, that all the hosts of the LORD went out from the land of Egypt," Exod. 12:41. But the law on mount Sinai, which gave beginning to Mosaic theology, was given in the third month after that departure from Egypt, chap. 19:1. [ p. 267 ] That economy of theology therefore lasted exactly four hundred and thirty years and three months; concerning which space of time, and the right settling of its epochs, let the reader consult Ussher, Sacred Chronology, chaps. viii., ix.

XV. Being therefore called, separated, and instructed in the will of God, Abraham, by the benefit and virtue of the renewed and enlarged theology, constituted a new church in his family, and observed the solemn worship of God separately from all who had been contaminated with the superstitions of the apostate church. For he built an altar to the LORD between Bethel and Ai, "and called upon the name of the LORD," Gen. 12:8. Those words, "and he called upon the name of Jehovah" [Hebrew: vayyiqra beshem YHWH], are the same as those employed in the narrative of the reformation of the church of Enosh's day and the restitution of divine worship, Gen. 4:26, of which we treated at large above. And all confess that the whole external worship of God, even the sacrifices themselves, is comprised under "calling upon the name of God."

XVI. But neither did this state of the church endure without notable blemish, whence it had need of reformation long before the end of its economy appointed by God was at hand. For besides that some of the descendants of Abraham fell away entirely from the true church, namely Ishmael and Esau with the sons of Keturah, superstition also crept, through the Syrian wives, into the very house of Jacob, where the solemn worship of God was wont to be performed. For Rachel would not have stolen her father's teraphim (of which hereafter) unless she had been to some degree held by perverse superstition. For the things the Rabbins here feign are absurd, namely that she carried off her father's household gods for this reason, lest by consulting them he should be informed of Jacob's flight; for we shall see that the teraphim were employed in auguries and divinations. How far, however, superstition crept in and spread itself in the house of the most holy patriarch is uncertain. But that it did cleave to it appears from the solemn reformation which he instituted. It is recorded in Gen. 35:1-4: "And God said to Jacob, Arise, go up to Bethel, and dwell there: and make there an altar to that mighty God who appeared to you when you fled from the fear of Esau your brother. Wherefore Jacob said to his household, and to all that were with him, Put away the gods of the strange people that are among you.... And they brought and gave to Jacob all the gods of the strange people which were in their hand, and the earrings which were in their ears; and Jacob hid them under that oak which is by Shechem." The most holy man understood himself called by an oracle to the reformation of the church, and to the restoring of the solemn worship of God (too much neglected in the midst of tumults, dangers, and migrations). Obedient therefore to the word of God, he undertakes the reformation, and piously admonishes the whole family, both wives and children and the servants joined to them, concerning the work committed to him by God. And it appears both that not a few had been defiled with idolatrous worship, and that idols of more than one kind had place in his family. For it is said of many, "They brought the fictitious gods and the earrings"; that is, such as had been consecrated to the name of some false god. And all these he buried in the earth, where without doubt perished the Labanic household gods. [ p. 268 ] And this was a peculiar and hitherto unused method of instituting reformation; namely, directed not against persons, but against the things themselves destined to false worship; and God here approved it for the use of all ages. Now this reformation of the church in the house of Jacob occurred 190 years after its institution in the family of Abraham, namely in the 105th year of Jacob's life, or thereabouts, 4 or 5 years after he had departed from Mesopotamia.
Of Mosaic Theology

Chapter argument: The church founded and fixed in the calling of Abraham. What the visibility of the church is, and whence it arises. The state of the church after the calling of Abraham down to the giving of the law [Greek: nomothesia]. The primeval language preserved. The descendants of Abraham by no means free from the stain of superstition [Greek: deisidaimonia] in Egypt. The Sinaitic legislation, or the beginning of Mosaic theology. The first state of the church under arbitrary law. Theology, delivered piecemeal, reduced into one body; committed to letters; the doctrine of the church completed. The certainty, authority, and evidence of the divine word committed to letters. The twofold consideration of Mosaic theology. In what sense it is evangelical, in what sense legal. The fable of the Jews concerning the oral law exploded. The threefold foundation of present-day Judaism.

I. After God had preserved his church in the midst of dangers, often even on the very border of destruction, through so many ages (irradiating with the light of heavenly truth, here and there, wherever among the nations, whomever or whatever persons he would), it pleased him at length, in the solemn calling of Abraham set forth above, to fix and establish her, who had been as it were wandering and unstable; and by a total separation from the world, which was now plainly and openly set in the power of Satan, and by the institution of new rites, to render her more visible and conspicuous. For the visibility of the church, as men speak, is not to be estimated from the number of professors. For since the assembly of the people of God on earth, which is the church, is wont to be called visible from that piety which is the distinction between the just and the unjust, it can happen, nay it very often does happen, that in the greatest crowd of professors, because that piety from which alone the church is conspicuous is neglected, the church almost lies hidden, and cannot be clearly discerned. For the church is visible from no other source than that from which an assembly is a church. Although, therefore, in the calling of Abraham the church was reduced to almost a single family, yet, since she was increased and amplified with spiritual light and with new institutions of divine worship, which made her separation from the rest of the world remarkable, she shone forth from that time more illustrious, more visible, and more glorious than ever before.

II. Now that revelation, which, made to Abraham, was the foundation of the new church, we have briefly narrated above, according to the plan of our design. We have also shown that the theology resting upon it stood for four hundred and thirty years as the rule or canon of obedience acceptable to God, of the faith and worship of nearly all the godly. But what befell, by divine appointment, this new race of theologians, or the Abrahamic church, through several ages, [ p. 269 ] is most plainly set forth in the books of Genesis and Exodus. They were from time to time incited and spurred by new revelations to render due obedience to God, to celebrate his worship assiduously, and to trust his promises. And in the various afflictions and persecutions to which by the determinate counsel of God they were exposed (by which they were exercised rather than broken or oppressed), they obtained in all straits and dangers unspeakable testimonies of the divine favor and care. It is recorded in the Scriptures that Abraham, that great father of the church and of the family, communicated to his posterity the promises he had received, the knowledge of the will of God with which he was furnished, and all the rites of worship, both those handed down from the fathers [Greek: patroparadotous] and those newly prescribed to him, Gen. 18:18, 19. And all these things, according to the measure of each, were observed by all those who descended in the natural order from that son of his to whom alone the promises pertained, or who, by the will and counsel of God, joined themselves to them, until that appointed time should come in which all things were to be renewed and brought into a new form of external worship.

III. We have shown that the language used by Abraham was that of the first men; it was at length called Hebrew from Eber. This his posterity, wandering through many parts of the East where other dialects, especially the Syrian, were in use, preserved pure and inviolate. This appears from the distinction which Scripture notes between the speech of Laban the Syrian and that of Jacob, Gen. 31:47: "Laban called" (the heap of stones) "Jegar-sahadutha: and Jacob called it Galeed." The Vulgate interpreter adds, "Each according to the propriety of his own tongue." Yet it seems probable that Laban, since he was of the posterity of Eber, had not entirely unlearned his language, from the Hebrew names which his daughters imposed on the sons of Jacob; but he used the Syrian as now more familiar to him. In Egypt also, where they served a hard servitude for two hundred years more or less, the native purity of the language remained. Of the worship of the true God the same can by no means be said. For the Holy Spirit testifies that the whole church in Egypt fell away to some degree from the principles and norm of this Abrahamic theology. The prophet Ezekiel teaches that both women, Aholah and Aholibah, that is, Israel and Judah, in other words the whole race of the descendants of Abraham, committed adulteries in Egypt, chap. 23:2, 3, and brought their adulteries from there, ver. 8; whence God complains that this people did not forsake the idols of Egypt, in the twentieth chapter of the same prophet, ver. 8. Likewise Joshua expressly affirms that the fathers of the wilderness people served strange gods not only beyond the river, but in Egypt also, chap. 24:14. And hence it was, some judge, that when Satan boasted of having gone through the whole earth and of its being subject to him, God had only Job to set against him, Job 1:8; 2:3. But how far this defection of the Abrahamic line advanced, with what idols or with what foreign worship they polluted themselves, is plainly uncertain. Whether they admitted the superstition of Apis we shall see hereafter. After that time, [ p. 270 ] not only did they make the calf [Greek: emoschopoiesan] in the desert, a stupendous example of the vanity and blindness of the human mind, but down to the Babylonian captivity, by which God determined utterly to purge that plague from the church, they are reported to have been ever more and more addicted to idolatry. But these things belong to the defection of the Mosaic church.

IV. When therefore the time was now at hand in which God willed to set, as it were, the finishing hand to theology as it was to be taught and administered by mere men, Moses acting as mediator and messenger, he called this elect people out into the most desolate solitudes of Arabia, separated from all other mortals; and there he taught them most wise and most holy laws, innumerable sacred rites, and his whole will, not without an immense enlargement of theological light. Now no state of the church arising from arbitrary law, or pure institution, had preceded these times.
