Up to this point, from the founding of the world, each family, either separately or joined with others, as right reason judged it should be done according to the circumstances of things, had given itself to the instituted worship of God. Nor was it done otherwise by the Israelites themselves while they remained in Egypt. All indeed worshipped the same God according to the dictates of the same theology; but that they should do this united in the bond of some ecclesiastical assembly, this the law of nature, which alone they followed in all things not revealed in a special manner, did not prescribe. But since it was necessary that a type or conspicuous exemplar should stand forth of the spiritual glory one day to be brought to light through the promised Seed, God compacted that whole people into one visible assembly, which should be the more solemn seat of his worship. And these things were transacted in that most celebrated Sinaitic legislation [Greek: nomothesia]. To pursue the heads of the particular revelations, or to recount the institutions of worship, would be foreign to our purpose. For one investigating the progress of theology in general, it may suffice to note some things by which it was now first increased.

V. First, then, that heavenly doctrine, or knowledge of God, which from the laying of the foundations of the world had been revealed in many portions [Greek: polymeros] and on various occasions, was compacted, as it were, into one general and stable rule of worship and obedience, and delivered in a single uniform manner [Greek: monoeidos] to the whole church. Next, since this doctrine had hitherto been preserved only by oral tradition, whence it was partly lost altogether, partly rendered useless by the admixture of various superstitions and false opinions, it was now, by the special command of God and his unspeakable care and mercy toward the church, committed to the faithful monuments of letters. Thus theology was snatched out of the keeping of mortals, and no longer exposed to their audacity, blindness, or negligence. Whatever therefore became of the theologians themselves, theology from that day onward remained pure, without the least blemish or corruption. Further: to the body of the former revelations and institutions, collected and digested into laws, new revelations were besides added, which rendered the rule of worshipping God and of living to him perfect in such a manner that the church had need of no new institution or new doctrine [ p. 271 ] until he should come "in whom all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge were to dwell."

VI. Now those revelations of the divine mind committed to letters declare their author, and persuade men that they are from God, with no less evidence than each revelation immediately made to anyone rendered him certain that he had received it from God. When it pleased God to inspire any mortals immediately with his Spirit, or to make them partakers of divine revelations, in the manner in which we have shown he dealt with Adam, Noah, and Abraham, it is agreed that they were all most certain of the will of God, and that it was God who addressed them. But that divine certainty and persuasion was peculiar which those obtained to whom these inspired men communicated those breathings or revelations at God's command. For he who would have any certainty concerning a divine and supernatural revelation not granted to himself immediately by God, must rest upon a double foundation: namely, the ministry of him with whom God communicated it immediately, and the affinity of the thing revealed itself to the divine nature, which should manifestly show that something divine was in it. But in a far different manner does the divine persuasion of truth revealed from heaven insinuate itself into the human mind, where one hears God speaking immediately. Most of those who feared God, before his word was committed to letters, had no other assurance [Greek: asphaleia] of doctrines divinely revealed than what the things themselves, by the benefit of the ministry of a few, implanted in their minds. But the mind of God being now consigned to letters, every mortal to whom the Scripture has come has God addressing him no less immediately than if he heard God speaking with a living voice, even as Adam, when he said he had heard the voice of the LORD. For as no voice can strike the ears of men except by means of the air in which it is formed, and yet it cannot on that account be denied that that voice is immediately from God, provided it be truly the voice of God; so, although the voice of God is delivered in writings, and God uses that medium by which to address men, there is no reason on that account why he should be said to address them any less immediately. But this is said to be done immediately with respect to such ministry of men as we mentioned before. There remains in the written divine word that divine quality [Greek: to theion] which, accompanying immediate revelations, gave a most firm and utterly infallible persuasion of heavenly truth to those to whom they were made. What credit the oracles of God immediately granted once procured for themselves, Abraham himself can serve as an example. There was nothing of right reason in Abraham, this one principle excepted, that God is to be obeyed absolutely in all things, to which the command he had received in that most celebrated divine declaration [Greek: theopropion] concerning the sacrificing of his son was not opposed. Yet the oracle had made it plain to him so certainly, indubitably, and infallibly that God himself had spoken, that, all doubt and disputation being excluded, he immediately girded himself to execute the divine command. Nor does the matter stand otherwise in the written word. God, its author, so clearly reveals himself and his authority in it, [ p. 272 ] that the human mind, resting on no other testimony, is bound to render it religious subjection in all things; which we have demonstrated more at large in a separate little book.

VII. But it appears that this theology, revealed and confirmed in that celebrated legislation and committed to books, can be considered in more ways than one. For if we attend to the doctrine itself, its scope and primary faith, it savors of nothing at all but a manifest elucidation of the first evangelical promise. The greatest part of the institutions, nearly all the rites of the sacrifices, refer to, point out, and proclaim the promised Mediator, his whole mediatorial work, with the eternal redemption one day to be purchased by his blood. The mode of teaching is called legal; for since all the institutions of worship which it establishes and exhibits were carnal, instituted out of the sole and most free good pleasure of God only for the time appointed for reformation [Greek: diorthosis], and imposed on the Israelite people, they could in no wise by their own force effect those divine and sacred accomplishments [Greek: apotelesmata] which they presignified and showed in type. Those therefore who were instructed in this theology, although they were bound by the will of the legislator to bear the yoke of external rites, nevertheless both understood and apprehended by faith the promises of eternal life through the Messiah to be exhibited in the flesh, and of all spiritual good things lying hidden under the shadows of the legal ceremonies. For Christ himself affirms that Moses and the prophets, his interpreters, bore testimony to him and to his mediatorial work. The apostles also, proclaiming the death and resurrection of Christ himself, and eternal life flowing from them, asserted that they taught nothing other than what was written in Moses and the prophets. Of the corruption of this theology, and the catholic apostasy of its theologians, we must treat more fully hereafter; for the present it will suffice to have noted that the present apostates are held in the knot of a threefold most pernicious error. For, first, they feign that beyond and besides that theology committed to writing, of which we have briefly treated, God delivered a certain other oral, cryptic, mystical theology, containing the exposition of the written one, and very many other things, I know not what, superadded to it. And this fable, against which the Scripture itself everywhere protests, is the foundation of present-day Judaism. Now the tradition of this oral law, the manner, namely, in which it was made, they narrate in the Seder Olam, whose authors show at the beginning of the work that they lived about the year of Christ one thousand one hundred and twenty-eight, since they teach that from the devastation of the second temple to that present time in which they labored at the work, one thousand and fifty-three years had elapsed. "Joshua the son of Nun," it says, "peace be upon him, received from him" (namely Moses) "both the written law and the oral law [Hebrew: torah shebikhtav... torah shebeal peh]. It is well known that Moses our teacher sat judging all Israel from morning until evening, and that law which was written contained not one of all the cases which newly arise. Afterwards, however, he set over them tribunes, centurions, captains of fifty, [ p. 273 ] and captains of ten, and said to them, Hear your brethren, and judge them justly; he said also of himself, And I commanded you at that time. Certainly this command was nothing else than that law which he had in his mouth. Concerning the office of slaughtering also, and its ceremonies, it is written, You shall offer and eat what I have commanded you; teaching, namely, that certain precepts had been laid down concerning slaughtering which were not written in the law. Further, Joshua delivered this law to the elders when he departed to the life of the world to come: the elders who prolonged their days after Joshua delivered it to the prophets, and one prophet to another through the generations, down to Haggai, Zechariah, and Malachi: the prophets delivered it to the men of the great synagogue, who are Zerubbabel, the son of Shealtiel the son of Jeconiah king of Judah, and those who came with Zerubbabel, Joshua, Nehemiah, Seraiah, Reelaiah, Mordecai, Bilshan, Mispar, Bigvai, Rehum, and Baanah. These are the heads of the great synagogue." But that Rabbi Judah, who lived two hundred years after the destruction of the second temple, afterwards committed this oral law to writing, Rabbi Moses Ben Maimon narrates at length in the preface to Seder Zeraim, which the famous Pococke published in Arabic and Latin, together with other small works of the same Maimonides. That law thus grew up first into the Mishnaioth, then into both Talmuds, with the answers and explanations of the wise men from that time added. The same old song they sing in Pirke Aboth, chapter one, and indeed everywhere. In expounding these things Galatinus is wholly occupied in the first book of his Secrets of Catholic Truth, who is found to have plowed with the heifer of Raymundus Martini. But the consideration of those things which the Talmudic Jews had up to that point fabricated concerning this law through the preservation of traditions will meet us in another place. Next, the Jews maintain that that legal mode of teaching, and all the rites of carnal worship, ought to be eternal and altogether immutable; to which error the author of the Epistle to the Hebrews opposes himself, whom we shall elsewhere show to have been Paul. And they divine, moreover, that through the observation of those rites and constitutions, together with that obedience which they can render to the moral law by their own strength, they shall be justified and accepted before God. But of these things, when we come to the narration of the Jewish apostasy [Greek: apostasia], we must, as we have said, treat more fully.
Digression I. Of the Origin of Letters

Chapter argument: The reasons for investigating the origin of letters. The authors who maintain an antediluvian use of letters. The arguments for that opinion. Whether it is probable that Adam invented letters. The Jews and Mohammedans trifle that letters were created by God. The authority of Suidas and Pliny. The prophecy of Enoch. The pillars of Seth. The opinion of Kircher. A response to the arguments for antediluvian letters. Augustine [ p. 274 ] calls that opinion a conjecture. Whether the prophecy of Enoch was written under the Old Testament. Arguments against the use of antediluvian letters. The word of God not committed to writing before the flood. No report of letters among the nations after the flood. Testimonies concerning the origin of letters among the nations. The Phoenicians commonly held the first inventors of letters. No certain monument or memory of letters before Moses. The constant report that Cadmus first invented letters among the nations. Whence the Phoenicians (of whom Cadmus was one) learned the use of letters. The arguments of Kircher for ante-Sinaitic letters examined. Mercurius Trismegistus. Of the book called Asclepius. The Book of the Wars of the Lord, Num. 21:14. Of the astronomical observations of the Chaldeans.

I. Since theology, or the doctrine of the truth which is according to godliness, was now first committed to letters, it does not seem foreign to the work in hand briefly to investigate the origin of the use of letters, or of writing. We approach a topic, I confess, of no great moment, exposed to the conjectures of all men, and long enough vexed by the writings of very many; but we shall be brief, and, as I hope, without tedium to the reader; especially since there is no lack of cause for inquiring yet further into the sources of matters of this kind. For since we have demonstrated above that God gave very many promises and commands to men before that most celebrated legislation which we have expounded, and some of them were such as pertained to the whole human race, while all were given to the whole church by the mediation of this or that minister; if we should determine that the use of letters was known before this time, it would be worth the labor to inquire why God willed that none of them should be committed to writing, when this could have been done with the highest benefit to all, as is plain from the event. Nay, one might not unfitly conjecture from this that letters had no origin before the law was written, and that those which God inscribed with his own finger on tables of stone were the first of all properly so called; and since I am myself of that opinion, it has seemed good briefly to subject to examination the things which learned men bring forward to the contrary.

II. There are, then, those who maintain an antediluvian use of letters and of writing. In order to put a semblance of truth upon that opinion, they employ proofs, or rather conjectures, which they judge at least to resemble the probable. For, first, they hold it fair that we should suppose that Adam, the parent of the world, most wise by long experience and use of things, watching diligently over the good of his posterity, also invented letters, so great a benefit to mankind. So Theodore Bibliander determines, On the Origin of Letters, and Angelo Rocca in his description of the Vatican Library: "On a half-column of brick," he says, "Adam is depicted prettily enough by the painter's art. Above his head are characters or letters of the more ancient sort, now called Hebrew; but at the feet of the same Adam a Latin inscription is read in this form of words: 'Adam, divinely taught, the first inventor of the sciences and of letters.'" And he feels that reason itself persuades us this was true. But he chose to spare himself the tedium of producing that reason. On his authority leans Hermann Hugo, On the Origin of Writing, chap. vii. [ p. 275 ] To these may be added the Bishop of Auxerre, Annius of Viterbo, and Camerarius. The Jews even affirm that Adam wrote certain books on the creation of the world and on repentance. "Which miracles of books," says Giraldus, On the Poets, Dialogue i., "if not rather monstrosities, the Hebrews have been accustomed to relate, mocking certain unskilled Christians of ours." But why should they not boldly say that Adam used books, when they affirm that the letters themselves were created by God on the evening of the sabbath, as Munster testifies, Annotations on Gen. 1:1. The Mohammedans likewise trifle that two little verses of the Alcoran were created two thousand years before the world was founded, and written by hand in the paradise of God. Suidas, under the word Adam, says, "his are the arts and letters" [Greek: toutou technai kai grammata]. But what sort of letters he means he does not show, since before, under the word Abraham, he had said, "this man invented sacred letters" [Greek: houtos heure hiera grammata]; by which words he seems to have meant Hebrew letters. Next, they use the authority or testimony of Pliny, Natural History, book vii. chap. lvi. "I have always judged," he says, "that letters were Assyrian." And a little after: "From which appears the eternal use of letters"; that is, coeval with the world. Further, mention is made of the prophecy of Enoch in the epistle of Jude. How that could have been retained in the memory of men unless it had been committed to writing, it is difficult to conjecture. They say besides that Seth the son of Adam erected two pillars, one of stone, the other of brick, on which Josephus affirms that he inscribed the courses of the sun and stars, and other observations of the nature of things, Antiquities, book i. chap. ii. To Josephus's opinion subscribe Polydore Vergil, book i. chap. vi.; Ludovicus Vives on book viii. of Augustine's City of God, chap. xxxix.; and others. Lastly, if I mistake not, we observed before that Maimonides affirmed that "the antediluvian idolaters wrote many books in defense of their most pernicious error."

III. But there is no one who has ever, so far as I know, undertaken the patronage of this opinion so spiritedly as Athanasius Kircher, Obeliscus Pamphilius, book i. chap. i. pp. 2, 3. He leans, however, on Rabbinical testimonies, all of them late, most of them utterly absurd; nay, lest anything should be wanting to his cause, he seems to give credit to that most monstrous figment of the creation of letters in paradise; at least he would have it placed beyond controversy that God wrote a book and gave it to Adam. He also adduces the testimony of Josephus, but in words which nowhere stand in him, nor without doubt ever did, p. 7. That Ham invented impious arts, and, because Noah would not permit him to bring them into the ark, inscribed them on rocks and plates of iron, he nothing doubts. But so it is with that learned man: he scarcely ever seems to have considered what he ought to write, but what he could; being ready meanwhile to believe whatever "lying Greece dares in history" (Juvenal, Satire 10.174).

IV. And these are the arguments which learned men use to render that opinion concerning antediluvian letters probable. Whether by their help they have obtained what they wished, it remains for us briefly to see. [ p. 276 ] That Adam invented letters is only a suspicion and conjecture, to which no reason compels us to assent. Whence Augustine, Questions on Exodus, book ii. qu. lxix.: "It seems to some that letters began from the first men, and were carried down to Noah, and thence to the parents of Abraham, and thence to the people of Israel; but whence this can be proved, I know not." Under which ignorance of that most learned man we all still labor. After the remission of his sin, "he spoke a song with God," the Chaldee Targum on the Song of Solomon relates, chap. 1:1. Of writing the author adds nothing, though elsewhere full of trifles. Angelo Rocca leans on images, I know not what, and newfangled pictures in the Vatican Library; but how inept an author he is, and of how little judgment, we shall see hereafter. But it is certain that Adam did not invent all the arts and sciences; nay, perhaps he did not even devise many, since he doubtless led a most toilsome life, and great was his misery as long as he lived. The authority of Pliny can be nothing in this cause, seeing he knew nothing of the origins of things beyond the fables and conjectures of the Greeks, and was therefore a mere infant in all true antiquity. And those who deny that the world was eternal cannot, and ought not, to use his testimony; since he feigned the eternity of letters for no other cause than because he supposed the world to have lacked a beginning.

V. Nor does the commemoration of Enoch's prophecy seem to bring credit or authority to this conjecture. That the memory of sayings of this kind should be preserved through many ages, and why not through all, ought not to seem impossible, especially to those whose stomach and palate are satisfied by ecclesiastical traditions. Among the Druids, Caesar relates, it was "not lawful to commit sacred things to letters," book vi. of the Gallic War, chap. xiv. That all the Pythagoreans and the theologians of the Egyptians observed the same discipline is well known; and we showed it before from the second book of Herodotus. Nay, the Lacedaemonians committed no laws to writing, which is observed by Justinian, Inst. 2, 20. Plutarch relates in the life of Lycurgus, "He laid down no written laws" [Greek: nomous de gegrammenous ouk etheken]. Nor yet was hope wanting of retaining sayings concisely and laconically uttered, fixed in the memory. But there is no need to claim credit for traditions in this cause. For there is no reason why we should believe that this prophecy of Enoch existed either unwritten [Greek: agraphos] or written [Greek: engraphos] through all those ages from when it was first uttered and became known, down to the times of Jude, or that he learned it either from tradition or from written books. He who first committed it to letters, since he was "carried along by the Holy Spirit" [Greek: apo Pneumatos hagiou pheromenos], it is not credible that he drew it from any other source than from the breathing of that same Spirit. And there was a most weighty cause why it should at that time be rescued from so long an oblivion. The time was near at hand in which that prophecy was to be most fully fulfilled in the utter destruction [Greek: panolethria] and horrible ruin of the Jewish church, of which the type and exemplar went before in the flood; as Peter teaches, weaving the same web with Jude, 2 Epistle, chap. iii. The Holy Spirit himself, its author, therefore repeats and recalls to memory that most ancient prediction of the judgments of God which were even then to be executed, for the confirming of the faith [ p. 277 ] of the saints. And he, for the repeating of his own words, has need neither of traditions nor of books.

VI. Further: it is indeed true that Josephus affirms that the posterity of Seth (not Seth himself) erected two pillars, to which narrative some are pleased to give credit. But although I am not greatly moved by his bare authority in a matter by no means clearly known to him, let it suffice in this cause to deny that he makes any mention of letters; although Hornius confidently pronounces that in that whole matter he showed "incredible audacity, or futile credulity," Introduction to Ancient Geography. And that this could have been done in other ways, without the use of letters properly so called, is plain from the Egyptian hieroglyphics. But all these things are uncertain, in which there is neither monument nor weight of reason, nor so much as a shadow, for establishing this conjecture. Nor ought the testimony of Maimonides to avail against those most valid arguments by which we have proved that there were no antediluvian idolaters.

VII. The suspicion, therefore, of certain men concerning Adamic or antediluvian letters is in no way probable. Nor are reasons wanting by which (beyond the weakness of these proofs) it can be shown to be destitute of all semblance of truth, nay, even of any color of probability; it will suffice to produce one or two. That which we related at the beginning of this discussion is valid enough to settle this dispute. From the beginning of things God, for the use of those who feared him, had given to men many promises and commands, which had their foundations nowhere except in his most free revelation. Without the knowledge of those promises and commands, all that natural knowledge of God which they could obtain from the implanted law of nature, or from the contemplation of created things, was in vain. And that God, who is supremely good, willed that the human race should use all the means granted by himself necessary for obtaining eternal life, needs no proof. Since, then, it is incontrovertibly [Greek: anantirrhetos] agreed that those means could best have been preserved by writings, and so have come to the knowledge of men, and since that mode of preserving the divine promises and precepts did not displease God, nay, pleased him exceedingly, what fitting reason can be given why he was unwilling that this should be done before the flood, or did not command it to be done, if only the use of letters was then known? Since, then, no traces exist of any sacred writing drawn up by God's command before the flood, do not merely suspect that the use of letters was not then known, since the thing is certain. Hence Theophylact: "Those divine men before the law," he says, "were not taught by letters and books," on Matthew.

VIII. Next, if letters had been in common use before the flood, and consequently known to all those who survived that diluvian destruction, how shall we suppose it could come to pass that forgetfulness of a thing so excellent and so useful in human life should immediately seize all the nations? But that any memory of letters, or of their use, was preserved among [ p. 278 ] the nations dispersed on every side from the Babylonian catastrophe, or that any report (however slight) of them survived, the accounts everywhere celebrated of their first invention, here and there, do not permit us to believe. And since those origins of letters which can be investigated with certainty deny them not only to the antediluvian world, but to that whole series of time which intervened between the flood and the giving of the law, we may briefly look into them. Whatever can be scraped together from the ancients, namely Herodotus, Diodorus Siculus, Pliny, Tacitus, Lucan, and others, concerning the origin of letters, Lilius Giraldus has related in his first Dialogue on the poets, mixing many absurd and false things with the true. Among other things he feigns, absurdly enough, that certain Jews, I know not who, invented the Hebrew points and called them Mesoroth; that the Hebrew pointing is not the Massora, nor is so called, even those know who are still bathing for a penny [i.e., mere novices]; his compilation Hermann Hugo transcribed into his own book, On the Origin and Antiquity of Writing. The rise of the Phoenician letters, and their first arrival in Greece, that excellent man Samuel Bochart has diligently and learnedly set forth from the monuments of the ancients in the second part of his Sacred Geography, book i. chap. xx.; to whose consummate erudition and diligence scarcely anything can be added. The same service was rendered by that admirable man Joseph Scaliger, Animadversions on the Chronicle of Eusebius, at the year 1617. Angelo Rocca, in his description of the Vatican Library, reviews the inventors of many letters in divers languages. But what Athanasius Kircher accomplished, when he endeavored to obscure the diligence and erudition of them all in his great work to which he gave the title "Oedipus Aegyptiacus" (whether any literary work since men were born ever came forth into the light with greater fantasy [Greek: phantasia] and ostentation, I for my part very much doubt), the learned will judge. We, that we may arrive where we are tending, shall dispatch the whole matter in few words.

IX. Concerning the antiquity of letters, and the glory of their first invention, the Assyrians, Egyptians, Phoenicians, and even the Greeks themselves contend; to say nothing of the fables of the Chinese. That Pliny thought the Assyrian letters had always existed, and so were eternal, we showed before. That the Phoenicians received letters from the Syrians, or Assyrians, Diodorus Siculus relates, book v. That Mercury taught the Egyptians the use of letters, Manetho handed down; Cicero reports it, On the Nature of the Gods, book iii., and Plutarch, Symposiacs ix., Question iii.: "Hermes," he says, "is said to have been the first of the gods in Egypt to invent letters." As for the Greek letters, some write that Cecrops the Athenian invented them; so Tacitus, Annals xi. chap. xiv.: "Some record that Cecrops the Athenian, or Linus the Theban, and in Trojan times Palamedes the Argive, devised the forms of sixteen letters; then that others, and chiefly Simonides, found out the rest." And they say that Cecrops sprang from Egypt, and brought letters thence into Greece. Of letters among the Phoenicians before Cadmus and his brother Phoenix no memory exists. And these are nearly all the things related concerning the origin of letters with any semblance of truth. Let us see first whether their antiquity above the times of Moses can be elicited from these accounts, intending to speak of the rest afterwards. Cecrops, who is called both "of double nature" [Greek: diphyes] and "earth-born" [Greek: autochthon], [ p. 279 ] Eusebius in his Chronicle judges to have been contemporary with Moses himself, and more ancient than Cadmus. But he makes Moses 200 years younger than all the ancient Christians, and even Porphyry, determine; on which account he gets a thorough drubbing from George Syncellus.

X. But that Cecrops invented letters there is no report worthy of credit: what remains to be said will perhaps refute the fable. The things related of Hermes, or the Egyptian Mercury, very many judge to be all fabulous. Tully affirms that he was the fifth Mercury, in the third book On the Nature of the Gods: and that he was called Thoyth by the Egyptians, to whom he delivered laws and letters. But that he lived a long time after Moses, Augustine proves, City of God, book xviii. chap. xxxix. Yet whenever he lived, he seems to have taught the Egyptians no other letters than hieroglyphics; whence Plutarch says of his letters, Symposiacs ix., Question iii.: "Wherefore also the Egyptians write the ibis as the first of the letters"; that is, in the place of the first letter they paint that serpent-eating bird. And Kircher, vol. iii. of the Oedipus Aegyptiacus, Doubt ii. p. 47, proves that the ibis was the first letter among the Egyptians. This Mercury is Taautus, whose books Clement of Alexandria accurately reviews in the sixth book of the Stromata. That Sanchuniathon also diligently searched into them, Philo relates: "With great care he searched out the works of Taautus, knowing that of all who have lived under the sun, Taautus was the first who devised the invention of letters." And this Taautus, he says, was the son of Misor, the brother of Sydyc, who begot the Dioscuri; who, it is known, lived a little before the Trojan war. That Cadmus brought letters into Greece is a very old and constant opinion. Especially illustrious is the testimony of Herodotus in Terpsichore, chap. lviii.: "Those Phoenicians who came with Cadmus, of whom were the Gephyraeans, dwelling in this country, introduced many other branches of learning among the Greeks, and in particular letters, which, as it seems to me, the Greeks did not have before." And a little after, continuing to speak of the same letters, he says it was just that, "since the Phoenicians introduced them into Greece, they should be called Phoenician." And so they were called; or "the Phoenician signs of Cadmus" [Greek: Phoinikeia semata Kadmou]. The words are those of Timon in Sextus the Pyrrhonist. And Plutarch, Symposiacs, book ix. qu. 3: "They were named Phoenician because of Cadmus"; namely those 16 letters which the Greeks first used. And in his Comparison of Water and Fire, "letters from Phoenicia." Pliny also, Natural History, book vii. chap. lvi., affirms "that Cadmus brought letters into Greece from Phoenicia, sixteen in number." And Athenaeus, from Critias: "The Phoenicians invented letters, the helper of speech" [Greek: grammata alexiloga], Deipnosophists, book i. But why letters are called alexiloga, Eustathius gives various reasons, on [ p. 280 ] Odyssey xiv.: "Either as caring for, that is, aiding speech," or, "as that by which the saying of more is accomplished in brief," that is, "by whose help speech is abridged." Casaubon, on Athenaeus, book i. chap. xxii., thinks letters may not unfitly be called alexilogoi "on account of the power of discourse in healing the perturbations of the mind." According to that saying: "Words are the physicians of the ailing soul." Tacitus also, Annals xi. chap. xiv.: "The report is that Cadmus, borne by a Phoenician fleet, was the author of that art" (namely of writing) "to the still rude peoples of the Greeks." Among the epigrams written on Zeno, which Diogenes records in his life, sec. xxx., is that of Zenodotus the Stoic, which ends thus: "But if his fatherland was Phoenicia, what reproach is that? Cadmus also was of that country, from whom Greece has the written page" (Diogenes Laertius, Life of Zeno, sec. 30). Clement assents to the report, Stromata, book i.: "Cadmus was a Phoenician, and the inventor of letters among the Greeks, as Ephorus says" (Ephorus by the correction of Lipsius and Bochart, for Euphorus); "whence Herodotus writes that the letters were called Phoenician." So Suidas also under Kadmos, where he recites Zenodotus's epigram from Diogenes; and "Phoenician letters" in Hesychius. "They say," he writes, "that they were named from Phoenice the daughter of Actaeon." But that they were so called from Cadmus himself, since he was a Phoenician, is more probable; although Actaeon was Cadmus's grandson by his daughter Autonoe. And Lucan, book iii. 220: "The Phoenicians first, if report is believed, dared to mark the voice for permanence in rude figures." In which words he reflects that saying of Critias in Athenaeus, book i.: "The Phoenicians invented letters, the helper of speech." But elsewhere the same Lucan teaches that the hieroglyphic mode of writing was in use among the Egyptians before the use of letters was invented, in the place above cited, line 223: "Memphis had not yet learned to weave the river's papyrus: and only birds and beasts and animals carved in stone preserved the magic tongues." Curtius also, book iv. chap. iv.: "This nation" (namely of the Phoenicians) "first either taught or learned letters." And Irenaeus, book i. chap. xii.: "The Greeks confess that they received sixteen letters from Cadmus, and afterwards, with the progress of time, invented now the aspirated, now the double letters; and last of all they relate that Palamedes added the long ones." Concerning no origins of things in all antiquity is there scarcely a more constant report. Kircher also assents to this opinion: but in vain would he have Cadmus to be an Egyptian; which he contends, with almost no reasons but with many words, Oedipus Aegyptiacus, vol. iii., diatribe iii., prelude pp. 56, 57. I confess that Eusebius so thought, book ii. of the Chronicle, n. 562: "Phoenix," [ p. 281 ] he says, "and Cadmus, setting out from Thebes of the Egyptians into Syria, reigned at Tyre and Sidon." The contrary is proved by the manners, religion, and Phoenician names, utterly abhorrent from the Egyptian.

XI. There is no less agreement also as to the number of the letters which Cadmus brought into Greece. They were sixteen. So Tacitus, Pliny, Plutarch, and Eusebius, Chronicle, book ii. nos. 16, 17. Pliny affirms that in the Trojan war Palamedes added four to them, namely Theta, Xi, Phi, Chi; and that after him Simonides the lyric poet added as many, namely Zeta, Upsilon, Psi, Omega. It appears therefore that the Cadmean letters were Alpha, Beta, Gamma, Delta, Epsilon, Eta, Iota, Kappa, Lambda, Mu, Nu, Omicron, Pi, Rho, Sigma, Tau. The words of Plutarch are to the same sense: "The first letters, called Phoenician because of Cadmus, the tetrad taken four times supplied; and of those afterwards invented, Palamedes first added four, and Simonides again as many more," Symposiacs, book ix., problem iii. And Irenaeus, Against Marcus the Heretic: "The Greeks confess that they first received sixteen from Cadmus." But if it be true that Cadmus brought only sixteen letters into Greece, it does not appear that they were those which were then in use among the Hebrews. For before the times of Cadmus the law was written by Moses; in which twenty-two letters occur throughout; nay, in the decalogue all are found, the one Teth excepted. If therefore Cadmus had used those letters, why should we not suppose he made the remaining six known to the Greeks? It is probable, therefore, that the Phoenicians, after they had observed the use of letters among the Hebrews, devised after their example certain letters, sixteen namely. These Cadmus brought into Greece. Whether they were the same as those which are called Samaritan is uncertain. The Samaritan alphabet indeed, as we shall see, received the number of twenty-two letters only late. It is agreed also that they changed the figures or characters of the letters. Of any memory of letters before the age of Cadmus, then, among the nations there is plainly nothing. That the Phoenicians, of whom Cadmus was one, learned the use of letters from the Syrians, Diodorus Siculus is authority, book v. chap. lxxiv. "The Syrians," he says, "are the inventors of letters, and from them the Phoenicians learned them," etc. That the Hebrews were called Syrians no one is ignorant. But that the Phoenicians received from the Syrians the use of letters, not the figures, not the number, is plain from what we have brought forward concerning Cadmus. Some report indeed of the mode of writing customary among the Syrians reached Greece; not, as appears, its use likewise. They call it Taepocon; and it is done from right to left. So Giraldus notes from Sextus Pompeius that that kind of writing was called. Some have attempted to trace out the etymology of the word; unless I am mistaken, in vain. Some memory of it seems to have been retained in that kind which they called boustrophedon ["as the ox plows"]. Of which Pausanias treats in the Eliacs; Isidore, Origins, book vi. chap. xiii.; Suidas under "the law from below" [Greek: ho katothen nomos]; Meursius, Attic Readings, book i.; Vossius, Grammar, book i.; Bochart, Sacred Geography, part 2, book i. chap. xx.; and others. But what were the figures of those letters which Cadmus first brought into Greece, or of those which the Ionians first used, is not in every respect agreed. Herodotus relates, Terpsichore, chaps. lix. and following, that he himself, among the Thebans, in the temple of Ismenian [ p. 282 ] Apollo, saw Cadmean letters engraved on certain tripods, for the most part like the Ionic. The first of the epigrams was: "Amphitryon dedicated me from the spoils of the Teleboans."
Another: "Scaeus the boxer, having conquered, dedicated me to far-darting Apollo, a most beautiful offering for you."
And the third: "Laodamas, reigning alone, dedicated this tripod itself to keen-eyed Apollo, a most beautiful offering for you."

But the learned Vossius, On Grammar, book i. chap. x., labors in vain to prove, against Josephus, who affirms that the Greeks at that period were ignorant of the use of letters, that Laodamas dedicated this tripod before the Trojan war; since his reign fell almost exactly upon that very time. For he was the son of Eteocles, and the son of Polynices took part in that war. These inscriptions, in the old Ionic letters, which Herodotus affirms were like the Cadmean, Joseph Scaliger has set out, Animadversions on the Chronicle of Eusebius, at Eusebius's number 1617, according to the types which he copied from the columns in the Farnese gardens at Rome; of which, for the first verse, this is the specimen: AMPHITRVON m. anetheken. eon. APO. teleboaon.

XII. But that these were the most ancient figures of letters among the Greeks I should scarcely indeed have believed, had not Pliny taught that the ancient Ionic letters differed little from the old Latin, book vii. chap. lviii. And Tacitus, Annals xi.: "The forms of the Latin letters are those of the most ancient Greek."

XIII. For they do not so depart from those which were in continuous use that the knowledge and account of them should escape the most learned of the ancients; which nevertheless we read concerning the Ionic. For concerning the Eretrians, who in the time of king Darius were led captive into a certain region of the Medes not far from Babylonia, Philostratus relates this in the Life of Apollonius: "They write letters in the Greek manner," that is, from left to right, not from right to left, like the orientals; "and the letters are indeed Greek, but such, they say, as they had never seen." The same author relates that the Greeks who inhabited Gades inscribed certain things on the pillars of Hercules "in letters neither Egyptian nor Indian, nor known to anyone," book v. chap. v. And yet the Greeks migrated there long after the Ionians had learned the Cadmean letters. I judge they were Phoenician, from which those set down above differ very widely.

XIV. Since, therefore, it is plain that the use of letters became known to the postdiluvian world very late, gradually, by various degrees, and from various occasions and endeavors, however the invention of antediluvian letters may please certain men, to me at least, assuredly, it is not probable.

XV. Let us proceed to those ages which immediately followed the flood, [ p. 283 ] that we may track out, if it can be done, the footprints of the rise of letters before the Mosaic law was divinely given and written. Athanasius Kircher labors of set purpose to prove that the Egyptians had letters before the times of Moses; but he uses this sole argument, that they were taught the use of letters by Mercurius Trismegistus, whom he determines to have been more ancient than Moses and contemporary with Abraham. Further, he contends that he wrote many books, and that some of them survive even now. And so, the doubtful suit being at last decided, he seriously and with many words celebrates a triumph. But that that learned man (whether I ought more to marvel at his leisure or at his ingenuity I greatly doubt) has heaped together nothing but raw conjectures, mostly absurd, interlarded with the most prodigious fables, appears in many places of his dense work. For the testimonies which he uses are mostly of that kind which no learned man could bring himself to review, except for the sake of displaying much knowledge [Greek: polygnosia]. Nay, they are the most rotten figments of unlearned men, of which none of the ancients except some trifler makes mention. And in those which seem of somewhat better note, of the age or writing of Hermes there is not a syllable. What of the fact that Athanasius himself elsewhere concedes that this Mercury used only hieroglyphic letters? For when he had fallen upon mention of the many thousands of books ascribed to this Mercury, he immediately adds: "Yet I would not have anyone think in this place that Trismegistus wrote thirty-six thousand entire volumes, but by books of this kind are properly understood certain hieroglyphic systems, by which he pointed out various arts and sciences." But he also ascribes and claims for the same Mercury the book which still survives, called Asclepius, and denies that it was composed by the ancient heretics, as some think; and he devotes a special digression to that argument. Plainly, when I read it, I pitied the man. For no mortal could ever bestow labor and study more uselessly. He who can bring himself to believe that those patchworks, most evidently stitched together out of the sacred Scriptures and the writings of the Platonic philosophers, were written by that Mercurius Trismegistus more ancient than Moses, is indeed, as it seems to me, not only prepared to yield a very easy assent to the most rotten and ridiculous lullabies, but also holds this fixed and settled with himself, never, namely, to open his eyes in the sunlight, lest the light should shine on him against his will. But Kircher dotes upon the most portentous figments of that rhapsodist; even upon that horrible blasphemy by which, on account of the generation of the Son, he calls God male-female [Greek: arsenothelys]. And, to speak freely, the things he babbles about a threefold deity and other matters in his exposition of the obelisks and hieroglyphics are plainly horrible and full of scandal. Yet the same man confesses "that he can by no means deny that many spurious books were formerly in circulation." For it is very well known that some of the ancients devoted themselves to contriving that fraud. We have demonstrated it above from Plato. Concerning that book falsely ascribed to Mercury, let the reader consult Casaubon, Exercitationes on the Annals, i. sec. 1, at num. 18, [ p. 284 ] and learn how absurdly that most impudent impostor feigned very many things. That many men celebrated for prudence were of old famous under the name of Mercury, Cicero, Plutarch, and others record. That one of them, whom they called Thoyth or Thouth or Touth, taught the Egyptians letters we have shown before. That he lived before the times of Moses, and invented letters properly so called, no testimony worthy of credit exists. And beyond all doubt, if they had known the use of letters from the first antiquity, they would not have been so foolish as to choose to use perpetually that perplexed, obscure, and useless mode of indicating the thoughts of the mind by hieroglyphics. Some relate also that Zoroaster was more ancient than Moses, and wrote I know not what books. But it is well that Porphyry, in the Life of Plotinus, affirms that he showed by many arguments that the book inscribed with the name of Zoroaster is spurious and recent.

XVI. These trifles, therefore, being dismissed, let us see what is wont to be said seriously in this cause. There are two things which the patrons of the antiquity of letters bring forward with some appearance of probability. One is that Moses himself mentions "the book of the Wars of the Lord," written, it is supposed, before those times, Num. 21:14. The words are: "Wherefore it is said in the book of the Wars of the LORD" [Hebrew: al-ken ye'amar besepher milchamoth YHWH]; by which, they say, he shows that a book had been written before. But sepher signifies not only "a book," but also any kind of "narration." Saphar is properly "to recount" or "to number"; "to write" only by accident. So Gen. 5:1, "zeh sepher," "This is the narration," namely "of the generations of Adam"; nor is anything asserted to have been written in a book, but to have been told in a certain narration. So the ancient name of the city Debir is said to have been Kiriath-sepher, Judg. 1:11. The Vulgate adds, "That is, the city of letters"; from the Greek version of the LXX, namely, which has "city of letters" [Greek: polis grammaton]. And the same city was called Kiriath-sannah, Josh. 15:49; that is, the city of doctrine. By sepher, therefore, doctrine or history is understood; for history teaches, especially that which is of the deeds of the Lord. Then ye'amar denotes not what has been said, but something that shall be said. The Hebrews think the book of Judges is intended; I think this very book of Numbers is meant. "It shall be said," says Moses; that is, "out of the book of the Wars of the Lord," it shall be celebrated, and in the mouths of men.

XVII. The other argument in this cause is one which the famous Vossius also uses, On the Art of Grammar, chap. ix. book i. That Simplicius, Commentary xlvi. on Aristotle's first book On the Heavens, asserts "that the astronomical observations which Callisthenes had sent from Babylon at Aristotle's command were of one thousand nine hundred and three years, which Porphyry reports," as a certain writer adds, "to have been preserved down to the times of Alexander of Macedon"; which indeed was not very difficult, if Callisthenes sent them from Babylon. But Simplicius lived a thousand years, or thereabouts, after that Callisthenian sending.

[ p. 285 ] XVIII. Now how easy it is to slip in the reckoning of years, especially when no faithful monuments of the transaction exist, we all know. Nor does Simplicius set forth his own opinion, but reports what Porphyry wrote somewhere; "there were sent," he says, "astronomical observations, which Porphyry relates to have been of one thousand nine hundred and three years, and preserved down to the times of Alexander of Macedon." In what work Porphyry related these things is uncertain, since those words are nowhere found in his extant writings [Greek: tois sozomenois]. Berosus affirms that those observations were of only 480 years, that is, up to the 13th year of Antiochus Soter, to whom he presented his historical works. Epigonus taught that they were of 720, as Pliny testifies, book vii. chap. lvi. But observations of 470 thousand years among the Babylonians and the inhabitants of the Caucasus are mentioned by Marcus Tullius, book ii. On Divination, chap. xlvi.; it is certain that nothing certain can be elicited from these rumors. Next, the memory of astronomical observations can be continued by other arts, without letters; after the use of letters was discovered, those things which before were preserved from perishing by the memory of men, by traditions, and by hieroglyphics, were committed to them. Epigenes, or Epigonus, in Pliny, teaches that the observations of the Babylonians were inscribed on baked bricks. How that was done an old anonymous author narrates, On the Divine Wisdom according to the Egyptians. "The wise men of Babylon and Egypt," he says, "penetrating by acuteness of mind to those hidden things of the supreme world, described the conceptions of their minds (as we ourselves are eyewitnesses) on stones by means of signs. And they did the same in all arts and sciences. Then they placed the stones on which those things were described in their temples, and exhibited them as pages, so to speak, to be read through; and such things served them in the place of books." The famous Vossius adds besides, and says, unless "there had been letters before the law was given, to what purpose would the law have been written on tables of bronze by the hands of God himself?" Bronze, for stone, is a slip of memory [Greek: sphalma mnemonikon]. But the answer also is easy; for this was done by God that he might provide both for the perpetual preservation of his law, and for the instruction of Moses, and so of the whole people, in the use of letters. Although, therefore, in a matter of no greater moment I would not draw the saw of contention with anyone who thinks otherwise, yet I do not hesitate to affirm that no one has hitherto demonstrated, either by reasons or by testimonies worthy of credit, a use of letters properly so called more ancient than the Mosaic legislation; so that I plainly acquiesce in the opinion of Eupolemus, a most ancient historiographer among the Greeks, whose words Clement of Alexandria reports, Stromata, book i.: "They say that Moses was the first wise man, and that he first delivered letters [Greek: grammatike] to the Jews, and that the Phoenicians received them from the Jews, and the Greeks from the Phoenicians."
[ p. 286 ] Of the Ancient Letters of the Hebrews
Of the ancient letters or characters of the Hebrews, in which the law was written by God himself, and according to the norm of that writing all the books of the Old Testament.

I. There still exists, and from the remotest Christian antiquity there existed of old, a Mosaic Pentateuch written in those letters which are commonly called Samaritan; but the language is Hebrew. Origen and Jerome make mention of it. Whether either of them ever saw the book itself, or any copy of it, is uncertain. Than that compilation [Greek: syllage] of the Hebrew verity and the Greek translations which Origen, with great labors and great expense, produced and published, no literary monument in the whole ancient church was more celebrated. If he had ever seen that Samaritan Pentateuch, or (had he seen it) had judged it worthy of any place or value, he would without doubt have assigned it some notable place, at least some place, in that much-talked-of [Greek: polythrylletos] work; which nevertheless was not done by him.

II. Jerome affirms that the Samaritan text is written with just as many letters as the Hebrew; but since this is most evidently false, that most learned man seems to have pronounced upon it only from rumors and the information of others. Each of them also writes that the letter Tau among the Samaritans is like a cross. Jerome on Ezekiel, chap. 9: "To come to our own concerns: in the ancient letters of the Hebrews, which the Samaritans use even to this day, the last letter, Thau, has the likeness of a cross." Of that opinion also were Tertullian and Augustine. But there is no mention at all of the letter Tau in Ezekiel; and certain men trifle most absurdly, and not without asinine stupidity, about the sign of the cross to be fixed on the foreheads of men, out of this passage. The words are "vehithvitha tav" [Hebrew], which our countrymen rightly render, "And set a mark," "You shall mark with a mark." For that intransitive verbs delight in a cognate noun among the Hebrews, schoolboys know. But it is false that the last letter in the Samaritan alphabet is cross-shaped [Greek: stauroeides]. And that character of this letter which appears in the alphabet which Schickard produced from the Vatican Library in his Bechinath Happerushim is without doubt fictitious. I confess indeed that on certain coins a figure not unlike a cross appears in place of Tau; but of coins there is nothing in Jerome; nor was that bad merchandise as yet in any esteem. It is not probable, therefore, that those most learned men ever saw that Samaritan Pentateuch, at least the one which still survives. From their testimony, however, it is sufficiently clear that the Samaritans of that time had the Mosaic Pentateuch, written indeed in the Hebrew language, but in a character different from that which was in use among the Jews. The Samaritans were that people whom Shalmaneser king of Assyria, in the days of Hezekiah king of Judah, one hundred and thirty years [ p. 287 ] or thereabouts before the Babylonian captivity, transplanted into Samaria and the other cities formerly inhabited by the ten tribes whom he had led captive. Sacred Scripture shows that they sprang from Babylon, Cuthah, Avva, Hamath, and Sepharvaim, 2 Kings 17:24: from Cuthah they were afterwards called Cuthaeans. They were therefore Chaldeans and Syrians, as is clear from the names of the places, cities, and regions from which they derived their origin. That at their first coming into the land of Canaan they used, as their vernacular language, that is the Syro-Chaldean, so also the Chaldaic letters, can, I think, be doubtful to no one. That the use of letters was known to the Chaldeans from such antiquity that some determined the Assyrian letters to have been eternal, has been shown before from Pliny. The art of writing, therefore, and that in Chaldee, was known and familiar to those nations transplanted into Samaria. Thus Babylonian and Syrian colonists, using their own language and their ancestral customs, occupied the Israelite seats. Concerning the idols which they worshipped, first in the place of God, then together with God, namely Succoth Benoth, Nergal, Ashima, Adrammelech, and Anammelech, let the reader consult Selden's most erudite treatise On the Syrian Gods.

III. This most abject people, therefore, a sweeping-together of various nations, and of almost no account, inhabiting desolate places, without law or king, subject to the Jews, remained ignoble and obscure down to the Babylonian captivity. But being harassed by lions, divinely sent against them because the holy land had been polluted with idols contrary to the Noachic precepts, or natural right, since the neighboring Jews, among whom alone the worship of the true God flourished, would have nothing in common with them, they procured to be sent to them by king Shalmaneser a Jeroboamite priest, who should teach them the manner of worshipping God observed, namely, by the idolatrous Israelites. The Rabbins write that his name was Dustai. For thus they speak: "Sennacherib sent to the Cuthaeans Rabbi Dusthai the son of Jannai, to teach them the law." Most absurdly, after their manner; feigning some Rabbi or other, the Rabbinic name being born many ages later, and that he was sent by Sennacherib, to whom this business in no way belongs. Quite portentous, and very like the ravings of dreamers, are the words which Epiphanius reports on this matter, written with such carelessness, to say nothing more severe, that he scarcely seems to have deigned to consult the sacred history. For first he narrates that "the Assyrian nations were sent into Judea by Nebuchadnezzar, at the request of the elders who were carried away into captivity in the days of Jeconiah"; almost as many monstrosities as words. Then he says that "Ezra was sent from Babylon to instruct the Samaritans in the law of the Lord." "With the law," he says, "they send a certain priest Esdras from Babylon, a teacher of the law, to instruct the Assyrians settled in Samaria"; nor in narrating the whole history is he even once awake. Then he would have them called Samaritans as if guardians: "Samaritans," he says, "is interpreted guardians, because they were set in the rank of guardians in that land; or because they were guardians of the ordinance according to the law of Moses." [ p. 288 ] Which things, though they are most absurd, yet Dionysius Petavius, Animadversions on Epiphanius, affirms that almost all the Greeks and Latins embrace that idle interpretation of the word: and indeed Eusebius expressly went before him in this etymology in the Chronicle; unless the words themselves, "which in the Latin tongue is expressed guardians," were added by Jerome, as Scaliger and Petavius suspect. But that priest, whoever at last he was, in no way solicitous for the worship of the true God, which ought to have been solemnly performed nowhere but at Jerusalem, taught the new nations that impious manner of worshipping God which had prevailed among the Israelites before the captivity. The Samaritans, therefore, were admitted into the Israelite religion in the same manner in which the Americans were admitted into Christianity by the Jesuits and the other Romanist emissaries; for, in order to make short work of the business of conversion, they granted them the liberty of worshipping and adoring the idols which they had before, especially their penates or household gods, which they called Zemes, decked out with the titles and names of saints. But why should I speak of the audacious Jesuits, corrupters of the whole religion of Jesus Christ, who study only the enlargement of the pontifical kingdom, when that whole crop of ceremonies which sprouts everywhere among Christians has grown up from nothing but Gentilism and heathen superstition.

IV. Whether that priest communicated the written law of God to these Samaritans is uncertain. No testimonies of the matter exist, no monuments. But the affairs of the Jews being trodden down and verging toward ruin, it is credible that these Cuthaeans little by little lifted up their heads; and afterwards, when the Jews were carried away captive, occupied their empty seats. Whether they made any progress at all in the Jewish religion and the knowledge of God, or cast it off entirely, is uncertain. It is indeed true that, when, allured by a carnal hope of partaking in the privileges which the kings of Persia had granted to the returning Jewish people, they wished to join themselves to them, they affirm that they had sacrificed to God from the days of Esarhaddon, who had transplanted them into the Samaritan region; or that they had "not sacrificed to another," Ezra 4:2. For the written text [kethib] has lo ["not"], the keri has lo ["to him"]. But the people of God gave no credit to lying hypocrites catching at the opportunities of the times to promote their carnal advantages. Being therefore rejected by those first reformers of the Jewish church, and by the suffrages of the whole returned people, catching at the occasion of a dissension arisen between the high priests, by the help and persuasion of a vain man who had been put to flight for his crimes, they built for themselves a temple on mount Gerizim after the likeness of that which they had seen the Jews laboring to build at Jerusalem. But Jerome affirms, and Epiphanius also, that they feigned the very mountain, and falsely called it Gerizim, when in truth it was not so. Both contend that Gerizim is situated near Jericho, far enough from Neapolis: Mercator is of the same opinion; against whom, however, Masius disputes, Commentary on Joshua viii. But with what sense of religion they were possessed from that time, the histories of those [ p. 289 ] times show. For grant that the common people of the Jews lied when they affirm that the Samaritans placed on the summit of the temple they were founding the image of a dove, which they worshipped; yet that those things are most true which Josephus produces from monuments worthy of credit, there is no cause to doubt. For when Antiochus was vexing the church with various afflictions, the Samaritans, suspecting lest some evil might arise to them also from that quarter, openly, by letters addressed to the king, renounced all worship of the true God; as that most credible author proves, Antiquities [Greek: Archaiologia], book xii. chap. vii.

V. Whether they had received the use of the Pentateuch by that time, I for my part very much doubt. For since they knew that that impious tyrant held nothing in such hatred as the sacred letters, which he strove with all diligence to wrest out of the hands of men and consume in the flames, and since they were of such a mind that they freely offered their temple to be dedicated to the Grecian Jove, why should they not have delivered up the law itself to be burned, whereby assuredly they could have clearly demonstrated that they stood far from all participation in the Jewish crime? But the contrary seems to be persuaded by the corruptions which still stand in their Pentateuch, which it is probable were made while the temple they had built on mount Gerizim was yet standing, that is, before the reign of Hyrcanus the Jewish high priest, who overthrew that temple and leveled it with the ground. And these show clearly with what fidelity they conducted themselves in that religion which they had unwillingly taken up. Since men were born, nothing was ever attempted or perpetrated by the most wicked of mortals, in any religion, true or false, which they had determined to observe, with such wickedness and impious audacity as those most filthy scoundrels employed in corrupting the sacred books. Let us pass over those lesser crimes, of thrusting the name of Shechem into the text, and of substituting Gerizim in the place of Ebal, that they might patronize their own error. Only consider this, that to the ten commandments of God they added another, namely their own crime. The decalogue is set out in two places, Exod. 20, when the law was first given, and Deut. 5, when it is solemnly repeated; in both places they have stitched that precept of theirs onto the commandments of God; nor does any copy of their Pentateuch survive in which the most impious fraud does not stand. The words, as they are exhibited in the Paris and London Bibles, run thus: "And when the Lord your God shall have brought you into the land of the Canaanites which you are going to possess, you shall set up for yourself two great stones, and plaster them with lime, and you shall write upon those stones all the words of this law. For after you have crossed the Jordan you shall set up those stones, which I command you this day, on mount Gerizim, and you shall build there an altar to the Lord your God, an altar of stone; you shall lift up no iron upon them. Of unhewn stones you shall build that altar to the Lord your God, and you shall sacrifice peace offerings, and shall eat there, and shall rejoice before the Lord your God on that mountain, beyond the Jordan, toward the going down of the sun, in the land of the Canaanite who dwells in the plain, over against Gilgal, beside the oak of Moreh, toward Shechem." These words, by which they strive to confirm their whole crime, [ p. 290 ] the audacious impostors feign that God himself pronounced on mount Horeb together with the law itself. Ussher judges that Dositheus, the most notorious corrupter of the Jewish religion, first delivered to them the Pentateuch as interpolated by himself, Sacred Chronology, chap. vii. And he there gives a specimen of the imposture of that audacious man in corrupting the text. Hence, I say, it sufficiently appears with what mind they were occupied in sacred things, and what is to be judged of their Pentateuch, wherever it shall be found to depart from the Hebrew text.

VI. But about the time of the church's return from Babylon, and of the restoration of the temple and of divine worship, certain very learned men affirm that something marvelous and altogether unprecedented occurred, the like or equal of which no record of earlier times, no monuments of history, relate. For they suppose that both peoples, namely the Jewish and the Cuthaean, as though by a concerted conspiracy, cast away their ancient letters, long known and familiar to their ancestors and to themselves, and that each took up the other's: the Jews, that is, taking the Chaldean letters, and the Cuthaeans or Chaldeans the Jewish. What this matter really amounts to we shall see in few words. They teach that those letters which are commonly called Samaritan were the old and primeval letters of the Hebrews; in these the law was inscribed by the finger of God, in these, according to their opinion, the whole Old Testament was written. These alone, they likewise affirm, were in use throughout the whole nation down to the Babylonian captivity, and besides them none. That the Samaritans were Assyrians, and were skilled in the Assyrian script, we have shown before. No one can doubt that they had the almost daily use of it under the kings of Assyria and Babylonia, under whose empire and dominion they lived. Yet these letters, they say, the Samaritans renounced, and took up the Hebrew in their place. Their language indeed was Syro-Chaldean, mixed according to the circumstances of the time; that they retained it is not in doubt. Only their own letters, native, beautiful, easy, thoroughly known to them, they are alleged to have abandoned, and to have adopted those deformed, gaping, perplexed letters of the Hebrews which they now use, letters exhibiting no words except such as they themselves scarcely, or not even scarcely, understood. And the Jews, as the story goes, did the same. For the same authors relate that the two tribes, after they had endured some years in captivity (some say more, others fewer), had forgotten their own language. For this reason the returning people, although they took no care to have the Scripture translated into that language which they are said to have unlearned, are nevertheless said to have resolved to cast away the letters in which it had been written from the beginning, and which had been native [Greek: oikeiai] to the whole nation from its very cradle, and its only letters, and either to devise new ones or to adopt the Assyrian. To what end or advantage this should have come into their mind does not at all appear; nor what profit the church could have expected from it. Since the language expressed by the letters remained entirely the same, it seems impossible that any good should arise from this exchange of characters. For the knowledge of the characters in which a language is written contributes little to the attaining of skill in that language.

VII. Since, therefore, I think very differently from the authors of this opinion, I have judged it not foreign to the matter proposed if I briefly examine the reasons and authorities on which it rests, and also set forth those considerations which make me hold the contrary; and this I have determined to do nonetheless, although on this account, among others, I have lately been assailed with the revilings of a most petulant tongue.

VIII. We have set forth what the opinion of these learned men in this matter really is: they lay down beforehand certain assertions in its support, which please me no better. These therefore must be considered in the first place. First, then, they affirm that "the Jews during the captivity forgot their own language and letters"; and from this they would have us believe that some necessity was introduced for changing the ancient characters. But to me this seems not even probable; much less a foundation firm enough on which to build a doubtful and most uncertain opinion. From the destruction of the temple and the city to the beginning of the reign of Cyrus, in which the greatest part of the people returned, there elapsed fifty years or thereabouts. That very many survived that space of time is both probable in itself and attested by the sacred page. For it teaches that the same men saw the burning of the first temple and the foundations of the second. But that so great a people, or very many of them, nay the very same men, within the space of a single generation forgot their own vernacular language, which moreover they esteemed holy above all others, is not very credible.

IX. Next: during that captivity, most of the captives dwelt together in the same cities. This came to pass by the gracious disposition of God, that they might be the readier for the return, and meanwhile a comfort to one another. That a great multitude of the common people, and very many elders of the people, dwelt in the place where the prophet Ezekiel exercised his ministry, appears from the whole course of his prophecy. Nor did they ever mingle themselves with those nations among whom they lived dispersed; in sacred things they had nothing in common with them, in civil things very little. All causes of corruption or change of language were far away. In the "bowels" of other nations "that people dwelt alone, and was not reckoned among them." They contracted no marriages with the Chaldeans, no affinities; moreover they had without doubt carried with them very many copies of the sacred books; the prophet Daniel used them (Dan. 9:2). That all these were written in the ancient and accustomed character of the Hebrews, no one denies. That they saw, possessed, or read any other books, in another language, or drawn in other shapes of letters, does not appear and cannot be proved. To learn the Chaldean arts was unlawful for them. It is also credible that they had correspondence by letters with the Jews in other parts of the world, as the epistle of the prophet Jeremiah to them indicates. Unless in these they had used their vernacular language and the ancient letters, they would have been unintelligible to one another. Further, among the captives there were many priests and Levites. These gave their labor to instructing the people in the knowledge of God out of the holy Scripture. This could not be done except by the reading and exposition of the law. With these teachers [ p. 292 ] they applied themselves to all the sacred duties which it was lawful for them to perform outside the borders of the holy land. And this was a most effectual means of retaining the vernacular language. Moreover, by the command and promises of God, they always cherished a steadfast hope of return, and continually devoured the end of the captivity with eager expectation. For this reason, unless we suppose that they studiously retained their native language, letters, and customs, we shall scarcely think them to have been men at all. To what purpose, then, should they learn the language or letters of those whom they knew to be devoted to destruction from abroad, and from whose company they daily expected deliverance?

X. Further: God graciously granted them prophets through that whole space of time in which the captives were detained. Daniel survived to the return. These prophets both discharged their ministry in the Hebrew language and used Hebrew letters. With respect to language, there is a peculiar reason for some parts of the prophecy of Daniel. Yet that even these were written in Hebrew letters, no one doubts. But while the autographs of these prophets were not only extant, but almost still wet from the hands of the writers, to suppose that Ezra, casting them aside, copied out those prophecies in another character, seems to me little short of madness.

XI. It is alleged, moreover, that the Jews lived dispersed through all Assyria, and accordingly so learned the language of the Assyrians or Chaldeans that they forgot their own; and hence it came about, they say, that when the law was publicly recited to the mixed common people after the return, the Levites found it necessary to interpret the words that were read into Chaldee, that the people might grasp their sense; as appears, they claim, from Nehemiah 8:8. From this, some write, flowed also the custom of reading and writing the law in alternating Hebrew and Chaldee verses, so that when a small verse of the text had been recited or written, its Targum was immediately joined to it. Copies of the law so written are still extant in some number. To the point: we have shown before, what is also most fully established from the history of the returning Jews, that those to whom God granted the heart and ability to return to their fatherland lived not at all scattered, but together in very numerous colonies. To anyone weighing the history of the return contained in chapters 2 and 8 of Ezra, this will be manifest. The Holy Spirit narrates the exposition of the law by the Levites in these words (Neh. 8:7, 8): "The Levites taught the people the law, the people remaining in their place; for they read in the book, the law of God, distinctly, and by expounding the sense they gave understanding through the Scripture itself." That anyone should wish to understand by this exposition of the sense of the words of the law a translation into the Chaldean language, which the Rabbins call a Targum, there is beyond all doubt no one, unless he would rather play the fool with the Rabbins than embrace the open truth. The holy man relates that the Levites diligently performed their office in teaching the people. God had set them over the duty of instructing the people in the law by its assiduous exposition, the declaration of its sense, and preaching. What they had been accustomed to do before the captivity in the discharge of their appointed office, that they also did then at that solemn [ p. 293 ] reformation of the church, as the sacred history testifies: namely, they publicly read the law, declared its sense, and instructed the people in the understanding of it; which is a most manifest description of the solemn preaching of the word. Finally, the Scripture plainly teaches that the returned people spoke the pure Jewish language; and therefore Nehemiah was vehemently angry with those who from impious mixture with the neighboring peoples had contracted a corruption even of language (Neh. 13:23-25): "In those days also," he says, "I saw Jews who had joined to themselves wives of Ashdod: and their children spoke partly in the speech of Ashdod, and could not speak in the Jews' language, but according to the tongue of each people. And I contended with them, and cursed them, and smote them," etc. Why the holy man should grow so hot because they could not speak pure Jewish but used a mixed and indeed barbarous speech, if the whole people labored under the same fault of unskilfulness in speaking, there was assuredly no reason at all. A copy of that Hebrew-Chaldee Pentateuch is held in the Bodleian Library among the books of the distinguished Selden; the very copy, if I mistake not, which is drawn in for the confirmation of this opinion about the corruption of the language of the returned Jews. But it would be utterly absurd to refer that arrangement of the Targum to the alleged custom of first reading the text and then the Chaldean paraphrase. For that codex was written, and whether traces of an older one of its kind exist anywhere I have not yet heard, at least a thousand years after the Syro-Chaldean language had ceased to be in any use among the Jewish people.

XII. Further: it is very well known that colonies can preserve their language uncorrupted for a very long time, provided only that a great multitude of men of the same lip pass their lives in the same place, and do not mingle themselves with other peoples by marriages or commerce. Let the Anglo-Saxons in Ireland serve as an example. Almost four hundred years have flowed down to our own age since a by no means large band of Englishmen, carried over into Ireland, settled at Wexford, and there has retained its vernacular language so exactly that Camden does not hesitate to affirm that an Englishman, if he wishes rightly to learn and understand his own native tongue, ought to betake himself to Wexford. Vain, therefore, is what is alleged in the first place in this cause, namely that the Jews forgot their own language; and yet for that forgetfulness they would foolishly and to no purpose have sought a remedy in a change of letters, the language itself being retained.

XIII. Next, the defenders of the transposition [Greek: metathesis] of the Jewish letters suppose that those letters which are called square were the ancient Assyrian or Chaldean letters; but by what argument, I ask, are they led, on what testimonies do they rely, in affirming this; what reason persuades us to believe it? No writings of the Chaldeans or Assyrians are extant, none have ever existed within the memory of man; there is no one of the ancients who affirms that any were read or even seen by him; whence then can we learn what shapes of letters they used? The Targums indeed are written in that language, but in the letters which were in common use among the Jews from the days [ p. 294 ] of Ezra. Nor do the ancient promoters of this opinion assert this. "Ezra changed the Hebrew letters," writes Eusebius; that he used Assyrian ones, he does not teach. "He found others," says Jerome; of Chaldean letters he is silent. "He devised new ones," so Bede. The Jews who incline to the opinion of a change of letters trifle that the pattern of these letters was taken from the angelic writing on the wall which is recorded in Daniel 5. Johann Potken of Cologne, who was the first of Europeans to publish anything in Ethiopic, supposed that language and its letters to be Chaldean, as appears from the epistle prefixed to his edition of the Psalms.

XIV. Utterly futile, moreover, is what they say, that it came to pass not without the providence of God that the holy Scriptures were written in Chaldean letters, since the empire of the Chaldeans had spread itself far and wide through the whole East, and thereby the truth could be more largely made known. Futile, I say, this is, and that on more than one account.
