For what fruit or advantage, I ask, could accrue from this to men ignorant of the Hebrew language, that the sacred books were written in Chaldean letters? Can anything be gained toward learning a language from the fact that it is written in a known character, and not in another, which can be most perfectly grasped and retained in the space of a single little hour? Since the language which alone embraced the heavenly truth remained the same, beyond all doubt that change of letters brought no advantage whatever toward promoting the knowledge of that truth. Moreover, the empire of the Chaldeans had been utterly overthrown, and the Chaldeans cut down to extermination, when this prodigious transformation [Greek: metamorphosis] is feigned to have taken place. It does not appear that the Medes or Persians, in whose hands the supreme power in the East then lay, used either the same characters or the same language as the Chaldeans. What then could bare Chaldean letters do toward promoting the truth on account of the empire of the Chaldeans, at the very time when that empire had wholly ceased to be?

XV. But since we are moving among most uncertain conjectures, I would dare to wager any stake, if an umpire could be appointed, that the Samaritan letters were the ancient Chaldean ones. Nay, it is not even improbable that they were in some use among the Israelites before the captivity of the ten tribes. For that some of the Jews understood the Syro-Chaldean or Assyrian language, they themselves testified. "Speak," say the servants of Hezekiah, "to your servants in the Syrian language, for we understand it" (2 Kings 18:26); and the Assyrians likewise understood the Jews' language, as appears from the same place. But as the language of each people was known to the other, or at least to some among either people, why should we not also judge that the letters of the Assyrians were in some use even among the Jews, those letters which afterward the Samaritans alone used, since they were native to them?

XVI. Some affirm that the Jews made this change of letters out of hatred of the Samaritans, that they might have nothing in common with them in the worship of God; and why then did they not for the same reason also cast away the Mosaic [ p. 295 ] Pentateuch, which the Samaritans had embraced? It is indeed true that the Jews pursued the Cuthaeans with an immortal hatred, and were in turn held by them in the place of capital enemies: the reasons of this everlasting quarrel must be related when we come to treat of the reformation of the Jewish church. Nor would I deny that such is the nature of that depraved disposition, that those who are thoroughly imbued with it care nothing what loss falls upon themselves, provided they may cast down and crush those with whom they wage their enmities. But that the Jews were driven by their hatred to such madness that, with no loss or prejudice to their enemies, they dismissed their own ancient letters, given by God himself and consecrated by all the divine writers, taking up in their place some other letters or other, whether newly invented and devised, or borrowed from the most superstitious nation of all, is altogether incredible. It is well known that the Samaritans had a suit joined with the Jews concerning sacred and ecclesiastical privileges; is it reasonable that we should suppose the Jews so far indulged that hatred of theirs as to concede to the Samaritans so great a matter of boasting as seems to lie in this, that they alone retained the letters written by the hand of God himself and of Moses, while the Jews used some profane upstart letters or other? Not so, assuredly, does that most stubborn nation even now deal with those against whom it is incensed.

XVII. And these are the general foundations which the learned men lay down beforehand for their opinion concerning the Ezraitic change of letters; all of which we have shown to be uncertain, if not openly false. For neither reason, nor occasion, nor any probable cause of the much-vaunted transposition [Greek: metatheseos diaboetou] can be assigned.

XVIII. But the learned men will prove that it was done, although there were no reasons why it should be done. The arguments they use are testimonies, and other monuments of fact; the chief of these I shall briefly weigh, that we may see whether they are equal to the task of establishing credit for this paradoxical opinion.

XIX. Of the testimonies they use, some are taken from the Jews, others from the writings of certain Christians. The chief of the Jewish ones is from the Talmud, tractate Sanhedrin, chapter 2, section 3: "Mar Zutra said, and some say Mar Ukba: At first the law was given to Israel in the Hebrew script and the holy tongue; afterwards it was given to them in the days of Ezra in the Assyrian script and the Aramaic tongue. Israel chose for themselves the Assyrian script and the holy tongue, and left to the common folk [Hebrew: hedyotot] the Hebrew script and the Aramaic tongue. Who are these common folk? Rab Chisda said, the Cuthaeans," that is, the Samaritans. He who is openly false and lying in better-known matters ought to be held of only slender credit in things doubtful and uncertain. [ p. 296 ] Now most of the things here recorded from Zutra and Ukba are openly most false. For what, was the holy Scripture given in the days of Ezra in the Syro-Chaldean language? Most false; since only certain minute parts of one or two books were written in that dialect, which they retained in that language. Those prophets who were contemporaries of Ezra, Zechariah, Haggai, Malachi, did not utter even one word in Chaldee in their writings. And no Targum was written until long afterwards. Most false, again, that the holy Scripture was left to the Samaritans in Hebrew letters but in the Assyrian language. The Pentateuch, which is said to have been delivered to them, is Hebrew, not Chaldean, as everyone knows.

XX. Since, therefore, all the rest that these Amoraic Rabbins assert is most false, why should not what they babble about the change of letters also be judged false? The Talmudic Jews answer that those words of the Rabbins in the Talmud, and accordingly all those places where mention is made of Assyrian letters, are to be interpreted far otherwise than they are commonly taken. For they affirm that this Assyrian script was the very one in which the tables of the law were written, and consequently the whole Old Testament. So Ben Israel on chapter 1 of the Talmudic tractate Megillah: "This script," he says, "in which we today write the books of the law and every sacred thing, and which is called the script of Ashur, is the very same that was on the tables of the testimony and in the book of the law which was laid up at the side of the ark of the Lord." Then they give a reason why it is called Ashuri; Ribbi in the Talmud, tractate Sanhedrin, chapter 2: "Why is its name called Ashurit? Because it is blessed [Hebrew: me'ushshereth] in its writing." Others give other reasons for this word; but concerning their sword-fights I am unwilling to pronounce anything, since they are most lying triflers, especially the more ancient of them, every single one. Let the reader consult Joseph de Voisin, Preface to the Pugio Fidei, pages 86, 87; Hottinger, On the Hebrew Coins, pages 122, 123; Buxtorf, Dissertation on the Antiquity of the Hebrew Letters.

XXI. There are extant in the Talmudic work itself very many testimonies, ascribed to masters more ancient than those which are brought forward for the contrary opinion, which openly, directly, and constantly affirm that the square letters which the Jews and Christians use today were the ancient letters of the Hebrews, in which the law and the whole holy Scripture was written. There is no need for me to transcribe them, or any of them, here, since in my judgment what is said by them contributes little or nothing at all to the settling of this dispute. But the most distinguished Buxtorf has collected them in such number (for they are a number, and nothing besides) that he does not fear to conclude his thesis with these words: "This we think we have shown more clearly than the noonday light, that the greater and better part of the Hebrews, both ancient and modern, contrary to what others have hitherto wished to persuade us, stand and fight for the antiquity of the Hebrew letters."

XXII. But of greater moment seem the things which occur in certain of the ancient Christians; and therefore these too must be briefly run through. Eusebius, Jerome, and Bede are thought to give sentence in this cause. And from Eusebius these words are produced (Olympiad 180, year 2): "Esdras was a most learned scribe of the divine law and an illustrious teacher of all the Jews: and it is affirmed that he composed the divine Scriptures from memory, and that, so that they might not be mingled with the Samaritans, he changed the Jewish letters." But this Eusebian statement offends in many ways, and is by no means equal to establishing the aforesaid opinion. For first, it rests on rumors of I know not what kind: "it is affirmed," he says; but by whom, or when it was affirmed, he does not teach; nor whether he himself puts any faith in this rumor. Next, he cites an author, namely rumor, guilty of the most open lying. For this rumor affirmed that Ezra restored the lost Scriptures from memory; than which blasphemous figment nothing more absurd or more abhorrent to the truth could ever be devised. But when any witness is caught lying, it is just, by what is fair and good, that credit be withdrawn from him altogether. Furthermore, not even those persons, whoever they were, affirmed that Ezra introduced the use of Chaldean letters; but only that he changed the ancient letters, which perhaps may be referred to the invention of the points. And it is evident that the Jews, or the Samaritans themselves, were the authors of this report; and how much deference is to be paid to them in matters of history is very well known.

XXIII. The words cited from Jerome in the preface to the books of Kings run thus. "That there are twenty-two letters among the Hebrews," he says, "the language of the Syrians and Chaldeans also testifies, which is in great part akin to the Hebrew; for they too have twenty-two elements, with the same sound but different characters. The Samaritans likewise write the Pentateuch of Moses in the same number of letters, differing only in shapes and points [apices]. And it is certain that Ezra the scribe and doctor of the law, after the capture of Jerusalem and the restoration of the temple under Zerubbabel, found other letters, which we now use; whereas up to that time the characters of the Samaritans and of the Hebrews had been the same." So he. Do you say indeed, Jerome, that it is certain that Ezra abrogated the letters given by God, and substituted others in their place? Certain that he laid hands on the writings of all the prophets, even some recently published? But whence, I pray, is this certain? Or by what evidences may we know it to be probable? Next, what, I pray, are those other letters which you say it is certain he found? Chaldean ones? You deny that, surely, when you say the Chaldeans have the same number, and that these are akin to them. Or would you have it that he devised new ones? On what author's word, or by what authority, that may be suspected, does not yet appear. Do you say also that the Samaritans had a Pentateuch written with the same number of letters as the Hebrew? But that indeed is openly false, if at that time they had the one of which several copies have come down to us. But whatever is certain in this whole little tale, it is most certain [ p. 298 ] that lying Jews more than once imposed upon the credulous Jerome. Next, I should like to know what those apices are by which, besides and beyond the shapes, he says the letters of the Samaritans differ from the Hebrew. How letters that differ in their entire shapes can be said to differ also in their apices, I do not understand. That Ezra found out apices of the letters I do not deny; but I hold it most true that by those apices the vowel points are intended. Jerome, therefore, seems here to have in mind a threefold alphabet, consisting of twenty-two letters: the Syro-Chaldean; the Jewish with the apices or points added by Ezra; and another Jewish one which the Samaritans retained, which he says it is certain was in common use among the Jews before Ezra. And thus the Eusebian "it is affirmed," within a brief space of time, grew into that Hieronymian "it is certain"; which in Bede issued in something most certain, for he expressly affirms that Ezra devised new letters. And so far did that at first uncertain rumor grow by going on, as is wont to happen, that among some learned men he is scarcely thought worthy of the name of a man who is not prepared to give it credit: just as Tacitus would perhaps have thought them asses who denied that the Jews worshiped the image of an ass, after the report of that worship had become so widespread that there was hardly one of the most approved historians who had not committed it to record. But why they should equally be reckoned half-men who denied that Ezra restored anew the Scriptures utterly lost, no reason can be feigned. For far more of the ancients bear testimony to the restoration of the lost Scriptures than to the exchange of letters. Irenaeus, in Eusebius, book 5, chapter 8: "When the Scriptures had utterly perished while the people of Israel were held captive under Nebuchadnezzar, God stirred up Esdras the priest, of the tribe of Levi, by the divine breath of his Spirit, both to write out afresh the books of all the prophets who had gone before, and to restore to the people anew the law promulgated by Moses." So Eusebius: "It is affirmed that he composed the divine Scriptures from memory." Tertullian, On the Apparel of Women, chapter 3: "When Jerusalem had been destroyed by the Babylonian assault, it is agreed that every instrument of Jewish literature was restored through Esdras." And Basil, in the Epistle to Chilo: "This is the field in which, having withdrawn there, Esdras poured forth all the divine books at the command of God." Theodoret has similar things in his Preface to the Psalms. Even before these, Clement of Alexandria, Stromata, book 1: "Esdras the Levite, who had been made priest, prophesied, renewing again all the ancient Scriptures." And there is scarcely one of the ancients who is not of the same opinion; although this most rotten delirium is contrary to the express testimony of Scripture (Ezra 8), and is plainly a blasphemous figment.

XXIV. There is, therefore, no reason why those testimonies should be thought to have any moment or weight toward settling this dispute. The most ancient of these witnesses, Eusebius, lived eight hundred years after the death of Ezra, to whom this deed is attributed. Some of those intervening centuries were made notorious by signal calamities, which brought in the greatest ignorance of past affairs. And the Jews, after they began to publish [ p. 299 ] some things about their own history, following their long misery, filled the whole world with the most shameless fables. From their sloughs these trifles were drawn. Moreover, these witnesses by no means agree among themselves. They all affirm things that are most false; they produce or urge no history of the times, no catholic tradition, no consenting report; but they bring forward the rumor of certain shady fellows, to which it is not the part of a sensible man to attribute authority. The weight of the cause, then, rests on the Rabbins; and those neither the most ancient nor the wisest of that herd: more, and more learned, cry out against it; and those who are of the opinion that Ezra introduced the use of new letters feign, in rabbinic fashion, that is, audaciously and most mendaciously, that they were divinely devised and so consecrated to sacred use. Whoever wants more by way of answer, let him go to Pico della Mirandola, Epistle to an Unknown Friend; Buxtorf, Thesis on the Antiquity of the Hebrew Letters; Joseph de Voisin, Preface to the Pugio Fidei; Kircher, Oedipus Aegyptiacus, volume 2, chapter 2.

XXV. But we have by no means yet dispatched this business; those who have undertaken the patronage of the Ezraitic change of letters place the strongest bulwark of their cause in the ancient coins of the Hebrews. They affirm that very many of these have been dug up in places around Jerusalem, and where that city once stood. And almost all of these have Samaritan letters inscribed on them. There are indeed a very few in which square letters appear; but these, they say, are late, struck after the return of the people from captivity; the others ancient. Here the patrons of the Ezraitic transposition [Greek: metathesis] triumph in earnest, and rage against all those to whom the contrary opinion commends itself, as if they were stupid, obstinate, endowed with a stubborn temper, men who know not how to yield to the truth. But to be frightened by threats, or driven by revilings from an opinion which one has embraced only after a serious prior examination of everything that bears on its explanation, is not the part of a man who loves the truth. I hope, therefore, that I may without offense to anyone call this testimony again under examination; especially since it has long been clear from the investigation of others that it is deservedly held suspect of the crime of forgery.
Here the learned men suppose several things:

1. That the coins which are produced by some are true and genuine coins of the ancient kings of the Jews, struck or cast while the first temple still stood; and that no fraud lurks or lies hidden here.

2. That the coins on which Samaritan letters are engraved were struck under the first temple, while those to which the square letters cling were all struck under the second; for it is certain, namely from these very coins, that the Samaritan letters alone were in use before the people's return from the captivity.

3. That there was only a single kind of letters among the Israelites before the Babylonian captivity, destined for every use, both sacred and profane; so that the character they used in striking coins, that and no other they used in all writing.
But indeed all these things are doubtful and uncertain; some openly false, [ p. 300 ] destitute of all testimony and probability, as will appear from their examination.

XXVI. That coins cast in silver and bronze, with Samaritan characters, or at least with characters not unlike those which are commonly so called, are in the possession of some persons, is well known. Learned men judge these to have been from the ancient coins of the kings of Judah; and from them they have figured several in their writings. Kircher exhibits nine, from Villalpando and others; Hottinger several. Some are also held in the Bodleian Library, which had belonged to the distinguished Selden. Selden himself indeed, when he published his most learned book on "the law of nations among the Hebrews," exhibited two or three figures borrowed from the books of others, not having yet become himself the possessor of any coins; some of which he afterwards obtained, being a curious investigator of ancient things, letters, and monuments. I had thought to exhibit impressions [Greek: ektypa] of them here for you, reader, but there are things that prevent it. That coins of this sort exist, then, adorned with the letters or characters which they call Samaritan, we gladly confess. How much authority they ought to have remains for us to consider in the next place. That many men have practiced impostures in striking and hawking coins, the counterfeits which turn up everywhere allow no one who is not out of his mind to doubt. There is perhaps no greater deception anywhere in the whole field of antiquities, in which nevertheless it is a pleasure to very many to make sport of guileless [Greek: akakous] men. Some for the sake of gain, some tickled by a wicked itch for deceiving credulous men, have given their labor to working this imposture. Innumerable counterfeit pieces [Greek: paracharagmata], the figments of impostors, are still extant. On coins of Ptolemy Euergetes this title is read: "Of Ptolemy Physcon Euergetes" [Greek: Ptolemaiou Physkonos Euergetou]. Likewise on other coins one is called Auletes ["the Flute-player"], another Lathyrus ["the Chickpea"]; but Physcon ["Pot-belly"], Auletes, and Lathyrus were ridiculous taunts, imposed on unwilling men. It is not the part of a sane man to believe that most proud kings consecrated their own reproaches to eternity on their coins. It would be a wonder if there were not somewhere extant a coin of Tiberius with the inscription "Caldius Biberius Mero" [a tavern pun on Claudius Tiberius Nero: "hot wine, the drinker, the unmixed cup"].

XXVII. Rightly, therefore, Cardinal Baronius, at the year of Christ 324, number 113: "There have come forth," he says, "in this our age, shall I say impostors, who get hold of a bronze coin covered over with thick rust, and with cunning industry fashion on it what forms and letters they please, for the sake of base gain; not without great prejudice to the study of antiquity." Moreover, it is an artifice of deception old enough and very well known, to feign underground discoveries. Romulus used that artifice when he wished to bring the Sabine virgins into his power. Plutarch says in his Life: "A report was first spread abroad by him, that he had discovered the altar of a certain god hidden under the earth; they called the god Consus." Hence it passed into a proverb among the Romans, "to venerate a stone from a tomb as a god," which Cicero uses in his speech for Plancius, and elsewhere. So Martinus Polonus, in his Chronicle at the year of Christ 104, says that at Rome the body [ p. 301 ] of a certain giant was dug up, whose height exceeded the height of the walls; and he affirms that it was the body of Pallas, whom Turnus slew; for this appeared from the epitaph inscribed upon it: "Pallas, son of Evander, whom the lance of the soldier Turnus slew, lies here after his fashion." Which, for all I know, may have been composed by the mother of Evander himself, as Favorinus speaks in Gellius.

XXVIII. Among the innumerable, not traces merely, but express testimonies of this fraud which exist everywhere, I judge that most elaborate fable of Prosper of Fiesole should be placed, which Curzio Inghirami published under the title of the Antiquities of Etruria. From this one case let the reader learn how much labor, study, and expense those are prepared to lay out who have it in mind to spread snares of deceit and drive others into fraud. The little balls indeed which he records, and in them the histories and figures of things which he published, I would not deny that the noble gentleman found. For it is not fair that we should suspect any man to have been so desperately wicked and worthless as first to invent and weave out a long fable for the sake of deceiving others, and then to swear that he had not invented it. But that the whole work was the figment and sport of a man lacking neither leisure, nor wit, nor learning, who buried those little balls in the earth, I have no wish merely to suspect, since the thing is manifest. The marks that betray the imposture are innumerable; for that in so long a fable he should be so consistent with himself, so mindful of circumstances, as everywhere to preserve verisimilitude [Greek: to prepon], was nearly impossible. He divined that he would be dealing with Phaeacians when he feigned that the man whom the Catilinarian war consumed, so many years before the birth of Christ, made express mention of his crucifixion. For on the first little ball, which like the rest he calls a "scarith," these words were inscribed: "You have found the treasure; mark the place and depart; in the year 1624 from the announcement of the King of the Jews, 1691 from his crucifixion." Trifles! That any King of the Jews would be crucified was granted to no angels, nor to the devil, nor to man to foreknow. Let the reader learn from this how much emptiness there is in these matters, and how far the ingenious but utterly worthless vanity of certain men has advanced in feigning false images of ancient things: how many signs and impressions of seals and subscriptions are there related to have been dug out of the rubble of walls, the roots of trees, the depths of caves? All the Hebrew coins known to exist anywhere do not equal the number of the devices which a single page of that book exhibits; and yet all those appear to have been fabricated by one man.

XXIX. Of this stamp was that deed of Alexander which Plutarch records in his Life; for when he had come to the Ganges and could not obtain the consent of his soldiers to cross the river, doing many things for his glory and fame among posterity, he fabricated arms of larger size, and mangers for horses, and bits of greater weight than were ever in use, [ p. 302 ] and hid them; namely, so that posterity, finding them, might suppose that the soldiers of Alexander, whose arms those were, and the horses, whose mangers and bits those were, had far exceeded the common measure of men and horses.

XXX. But after the Orientals began to scent how high a price was set among Europeans on the monuments of ancient things, they left no stone unmoved to mock them with these juggleries and to put a false gloss upon them. For so long as the East was held by Christians, it does not appear that any Hebrew coins were dug up there. Unless the thing were very well known, scarcely anyone would believe that there are still to be found men professing the Christian name who undertake long and most dangerous pilgrimages to view and venerate things the sight and touch of which the Mohammedans, who invented them the day before yesterday, sell at a great price. A most distinguished man, an eyewitness [Greek: autoptes], a witness above all exception, related to me not long ago that in the eastern parts he had seen the miter and pastoral staff of Saint Andrew, or of James (for which of the two it was I do not well remember), which were visited with great devotion by very many Christians, who for so great a benefit were obliged to pay thanks and a fee to a certain Mohammedan mountebank in whose keeping they were. But when this gentleman, brought there by I know not what occasion, where this treasure was kept, inspected the miter a little more carefully, he at once perceived woven into it the Arabic words in which the Mussulmans, as they love to be called, express their solemn profession concerning the one true God and his prophet Mohammed; and he straightway departed, not without detestation of the impious fraud, and pity for credulous men. Things of this kind in great number are related by Abraham Ecchellensis in his Arabic History, chapters 7 and 8, and also by Kircher, Obeliscus Pamphilius, book 1, chapter 2, pages 10, 11, who nevertheless is eager to snatch from all comers the palm of the most absurd credulity. So, from the time when the opinion became widespread that some of the Americans were descended from the Israelites, immediately pillars and sepulchral stones were found in many places in those parts of the world, carved with Hebrew letters, and even with Samaritan ones (because the opinion about their antiquity had by then been published abroad). And so ingeniously villainous are the eastern Jews in this, as in all other evil arts from which any hope of gain seems to gleam forth, they being the sole dealers in this counterfeit merchandise, that a learned man who traveled through those parts has testified that a Jew received from him an ancient coin, as it seemed, and the next day brought back, together with the one he had received, another so like it that he plainly did not know which was his own. If, moreover, the things which Kircher relates (Oedipus, class 2, volume 1, chapter 2, section 2) are true, they will without doubt settle this dispute; but if they can be caught lying in the crime of forgery, they will serve to bring into the light this fraud wrapped in darkness. He relates them on the credit of Thomas Obicini of Novara, a brother of the Franciscan family, whom he adorns with excessive praises; this Thomas, then, among other rare monuments of antiquity brought from the East, carried with him into Europe a copy of a certain epigraph from Mount Horeb itself, in Hebrew [ p. 303 ] letters, cut on a stone placed at the roots of the mountain. And a certain Checaud, page 119, himself also long and much conversant in those parts, applauding the Kircherian interpretation of the inscription, asserts that this stone was the very altar which Moses built on Mount Horeb. And Kircher does not doubt that the inscription itself was carved on the rock at the time when the Israelites, returning out of Egypt, sojourned in the Arabian deserts. Of it this is the facsimile [Greek: ektypoma]. [The figure is here reproduced in the printed text.]

XXXI. And these, it seems, were the ancient Hebrew letters which Moses and the people of the wilderness used. But if these things are true, no one, I suppose, will deny that all labor of further inquiry in deciding this dispute is in vain. For without doubt these letters, if letters they are to be called, are neither Hebrew nor Samaritan. Yet out of them Kircher elicits these words: "God will cause a virgin to conceive" [Hebrew: YYY almetha yibbattez]; a phrase by no means agreeing with that age, as Hottinger shows in the Preface to his Hebrew Inscriptions. For out of those misshapen little lines he had the power of fashioning anything whatever with equal probability. But that that whole inscription, with those others of the same kind which Kircher mentions in that place, was a figment of that Thomas Obicini, I have no doubt; or at least that crafty Mohammedans, when they saw the man scraping together from every quarter things to transport with him into Europe, imposed upon the credulous friar. Nor is it improbable that the solemn Mohammedan symbol is enigmatically set forth in those figures. And from these things it appears how much faith is to be placed even in the coins that are brought from those places.

XXXII. Those who first made mention of the Ezraitic change, and asserted the antiquity of the Samaritan letters, are wholly silent about these coins; none of them were found until the report had everywhere prevailed that those Samaritan letters were most ancient. Thus lying came to the aid of fraud. That the coins might be believed ancient, the letters which were held most ancient were first stamped upon them; [ p. 304 ] then it is proved from this that those letters are most ancient, because they are engraved on most ancient coins. Just so, after it had been believed, through a slip of the Vulgate interpreter, that the face of Moses descending from Mount Sinai was horned, coins were straightway struck with a horned effigy of Moses.

XXXIII. There are also some coins inscribed with Samaritan letters in which the Tau exhibits the figure of a cross. But there are many things to persuade us that a figure of that letter resembling a cross was never in use among the Samaritans. The occasion for fabricating them was given, without doubt, by the hallucination of Origen and Jerome.

XXXIV. For there are extant coins adorned with the Cabbalistic names of God, expressed in Samaritan characters. But that these were forged only a long time after the destruction of the second temple, everyone knows. There are also some on which the name of Jesus, who is also called Savior, is inscribed in Samaritan letters. That fraud underlies all these, no one doubts. And who, I ask, will give us assurance that whatever remains of these coins did not come forth from the same workshop of impostures?

XXXV. But it may even be conceded that that character which is called Samaritan was known to the Jews before the overthrow of the first temple, and was in some use. That the Jews of old used a twofold kind of letters is the constant opinion of them all. This Buxtorf shows at large, with very many testimonies adduced. Among the ancient Christians, Irenaeus was of the same opinion (book 7, chapter 41). For he makes mention of priestly letters differing from those which were in common use. Whence the author of the Targum on the book of Esther affirms that the Jews in the days of Purim read the roll of the law "according to the writing of the character of the Hebrews" [Hebrew: kemikhtav roshem ivra'i] (Esth. 9:27); plainly indicating that in his judgment the law had been written also in another character besides that ancient sacred one of the Hebrews, namely in that which they call the Libonaean.

XXXVI. That other nations also adopted the same custom cannot be denied. That the Egyptians indeed used a threefold kind of writing, Clement of Alexandria is witness (Stromata 5). "Now those," he says, "who are educated among the Egyptians first of all learn thoroughly that method of Egyptian letters which is called epistolographic; second, the hieratic, which the sacred scribes use; and last and final, the hieroglyphic, of which one kind speaks plainly by means of the primary elements, the other is symbolic." The same Heliodorus relates of the Ethiopians (book 4): "I read through the band, which was marked with Ethiopic letters, not the popular but the royal, which resemble those of the Egyptians called hieratic." So Diogenes Laertius, in the Life of Democritus, section 49, after reviewing from Thrasyllus the catalogue of the books which he had written, adds: "Some also set down separately from his notebooks these: On the Sacred Letters in Babylon, and On the Sacred Letters in Meroe." [ p. 305 ] A twofold kind of letters was therefore in use among the Babylonians and the people of Meroe. Of the Greeks likewise Theodoret, on Genesis, question 61: "In the Greek temples there were certain peculiar characters of letters, which they called hieratic." There was, then, no nation to which letters were known that was content with one kind of characters for all uses. But some affirm that the Jews kept all their sacred things open to view, and therefore had no need of another character than that which was in common use. For, say they, the sole reason why other nations invented certain cryptic letters was precisely this, that the sacred things written in them might not leak out to the common crowd. But that this was the sole reason for that custom no one can prove. There could have been other causes. Moreover, although the Jews were bound to instruct all their countrymen in the knowledge of all sacred things, yet it was a matter of religion with them that the things held sacred among them should not flow out to the profane nations. Hence so great an ignorance among all nations of those Jewish matters which concern religion. Nay, those who suppose that Ezra devised a new kind of writing affirm that he did so in order that the Jews might have nothing in common in sacred things with their Samaritan neighbors. It cannot therefore seem unlike the truth that the Jews used one kind of characters, namely that one, in their commerce with the nations, distinct from that which was consecrated to the copying of the sacred books.

XXXVII. It may also be that they used that profane kind of letters in sculptures, incisions, embossed work, engravings, gold-lettering, and other things of that kind. For such uses the Samaritan letters seem suitable, since in comparison with all others they are misshapen and unnatural. But when I had said this elsewhere, I know not what tumults were stirred up by a certain learned man about natural and unnatural letters, and trifles of that sort, which, poured out from black bile, I am ashamed to repeat. I meant that they were unfit and unsuited for writing, and accordingly an arbitrary invention [Greek: heurema] peculiarly destined for the use we have mentioned. So Schickard, in Bechinath Happeruschim, page 82: "It is gaping," he says, "deformed, rough, laborious, and difficult to write, and to that degree artificial." Fuller has similar things, Miscellanea Sacra, book 4, chapter 4.

XXXVIII. Very many judge this Samaritan character to be the very one which the Jews call Libonaah [Hebrew: libona'ah]. Concerning the sense and etymology of that word there is disagreement among learned men, as also for what cause the Samaritan character should be so called. Morinus derives it from laban; thence libben; he says this means a graver, a chisel, or a burin, and takes "Libonaean" for a thing incised or sculptured. For that use, then, it appears to have been devised. There is also mention among the Rabbins of large letters, which, as they say, the ancients used in amulets. Rabbi Solomon affirms that these were the Libonaean: "The letters," he says, [ p. 306 ] "are large, such as those with which they write in amulets and on the posts of door-frames [mezuzoth]." The words are reported by Rabbi Azariah, Meor Enayim, page 171. Fuller expounds the whole matter lucidly in the place cited above, whose words I have therefore thought fit to set down here. "Granting," he says, "that the written letters of the Samaritans are altogether the same as those enclosed on the shekels, it is nevertheless not necessary that these alone, and no others besides, were in use among the ancients. As if indeed one nation must always have had only one kind of letters in use; a thing which experience itself easily refutes among all the more civilized nations, I believe. Certainly among our own countrymen we see that the characters proper to the archives differ much from the common ones. Nay, the skilled know that a manifold variety of letters is familiar to the Arabs, differing not a little among themselves. But these things are perhaps rather obscure, and not very well known to students. But who is ignorant of the most illustrious and most ancient commonwealth of the Egyptians? What? Shall we suppose it was sufficiently furnished with only one kind of characters? I hold it more certain than certainty itself that their hieroglyphic characters had an extreme and almost marvelous dissimilarity from the common and ordinary ones. And who will fiercely contend that the wisest nation of the Hebrews should be adjudged, as if bound over into perpetual servitude, to only one form of letters? We do not doubt, therefore, that for those two chief diverse kinds of affairs and business, namely sacred and civil, at least a twofold figure of letters was customary among the ancient Hebrews. And this, to my mind, the thing itself can at once teach; for the one kind is lucidly demonstrated by the written monuments of the Jews extant in abundance to this day, the other the books and coins of the Samaritans rescue from destruction." So he.

XXXIX. There are also extant coins marked with square letters. But these, they say, "are not ancient." Why so? Because, forsooth, "they are not inscribed with the ancient letters." But whence does it appear that those other letters are ancient? From this, they say, that "we see them carved on the ancient coins of the Hebrews." Utterly ridiculous: the coins prove that the Samaritan letters are ancient, and in turn those letters testify that the coins are ancient. But these coins of the latter kind, they say, were struck under the second temple; how, I ask, or whence is that established? Why, because they are marked with Assyrian letters, which were not in use before that time. And here too it is plain that the point at issue [Greek: to en archei, begging the question] is being assumed. Nor are reasons lacking which convict these conjectures of vanity. For if that kind of coins on which square letters are inscribed was struck under the second temple, whence, I pray, should we suppose it comes about that coins of that kind are so rarely produced, while of the other kind, which was struck or stamped only while the first temple stood, enough are found, and frequently, so that a learned man does not fear to affirm that they are dug up daily? The people which lived at the time of the destruction of the second temple was much richer than that which perished with the first, worn down and almost [ p. 307 ] consumed by very many calamities through several preceding ages, and had more money in its possession. The historian also relates that men of every sort, when the Roman devastation was at hand, hid the whole mass of money they had in underground places. Moreover, at the restoration of the temple and the city, it is credible that the ruins and rubble of all the walls and buildings were thoroughly grubbed through by the new inhabitants. Then too, for four hundred years before the decisive total destruction [Greek: panolethria], the city was inhabited by a most avaricious race, which for the sake of seeking money would not hesitate to penetrate into the very bowels of the earth. It may therefore justly seem a wonder to anyone that so great a number of those coins which were cast or struck before the Babylonian captivity is found, while those others, which are said to have been stamped while the second temple stood, are so infrequent.

XL. Further, Schickard testifies that he saw an ancient coin dug up at Jerusalem, struck while the kingdom of the Hebrews was still intact, marked with the common square letters, exhibiting on one side the effigy of Solomon, and on the other a figure [Greek: typos] of the temple. Villalpando denies that this coin is genuine; Kircher affirms it with Schickard. Others find it necessary to follow Villalpando; for if that coin were granted to be genuine, the whole opinion for whose defense they fight so spiritedly would at once collapse. Yet they would not wish to seem to exercise this judgment [Greek: krisis] without reason. For they say it was unlawful for the Jews, without special command, to fashion or paint the effigy or image of any animal; and accordingly a coin which exhibits the face of a man is not to be acknowledged as genuine, since it was unlawful for them to strike effigies of that sort. But why, I ask, do they say "of an animal"? The precept extends itself to every likeness of all things that are "in heaven or earth, or in the waters," whether they are animals or not. And yet all the Hebrew coins which exist anywhere exhibit effigies of things of that sort. The pot and the rod of Aaron were things existing on the earth, no less than Solomon himself. And when they fell into idolatry they changed the glory of the incorruptible God not only into the likeness of an image of corruptible man, but of inanimate things as well [Rom. 1:23]. If therefore there were any force in this reasoning, it would equally sweep away all the coinage along with that one figured with the face of Solomon. Nor indeed is it altogether improbable that the ancient Hebrews made no shekels adorned or marked with figures or inscriptions of letters. For we contend only for this, that all the coins, this argument notwithstanding, be held in the same standing and value. Nevertheless, that it was absolutely unlawful for the Jews to fashion any effigy whatever, provided it had no respect to religious worship, is not yet proved. For though they shrank with the utmost horror from the images prohibited by the law, they nevertheless used Roman coins struck with the image of Caesar in relief; and even now throughout the whole world they all but adore the images of kings figured on coins. When indeed Pilate brought the busts [Greek: protomas] of Caesar into the temple, the people, as Josephus testifies, began to riot. But we know that the same people embraced busts [ p. 308 ] and images of that kind on coins. Those busts the Arabs still call methal or methalias; whence arose the name of "medallions," now commonly received; and perhaps our word "medal." And the author of the Targum on Esther affirms that "Mordecai was clothed in a purple garment having depicted on it birds of every kind and the fowls of heaven."

XLI. And so likewise of old they did not reject the little images of the Philistines, offered to them at the return of the ark. Unless, therefore, those who have undertaken the patronage of the change of the Hebrew letters possess such sovereign command over the ancient coins that they may pass sentence of forgery on whichever they please at their own discretion, and admit others to give testimony, this numismatic argument is plainly done for. But whoever, on account of these frigid little conjectures, can bring his mind to believe that the prodigious change recorded above took place, let him do it without me for a rival; for I would impose on no one the necessity of believing what he would not, or of not believing what he would, provided it be allowed me to use my own judgment in these matters.

XLII. It remains that we briefly review the reasons which fight for the antiquity of the Hebrew letters. Passing over very many on which learned men are accustomed to insist, and which may be sought in their writings, it will suffice to have noted the heads of those which weigh most with me.

XLIII. First, then, possession itself, from which they cannot be ejected by the testimonies and conjectures brought forward above, is an argument for the antiquity of these letters. If we are ready to give ground before such feeble assaults of any adversaries whatsoever, we shall nowhere remain safe, stable, and firm. The square letters, and they alone, were in use in the church for writing out the holy Scriptures for almost a thousand years, by the consent of all, before any mortal, Jew or Christian, summoned them into court to plead their cause. Shall it be thought fair that the bare affirmations of a few ancients, and the raw conjectures of moderns, should prejudice a prescription of so many years, nay, of centuries? Who, I ask, said, wrote, or handed down to memory, until at least six hundred years after the death of Ezra, that he cast away the letters given by God and employed by all the prophets, repudiated the ancient copies written in those sacred letters, packed them off to the Samaritans, devised new ones, or introduced into sacred use the letters of an idolatrous, accursed nation, only just then laid waste and cut off for its oppression of the church? If indeed anyone is pleased to suspect that he so handled the word of God, I cannot prevent him, since suspicion lies in every man's own power; to me they seem to be telling Milesian fables when they bring out those suspicions.

XLIV. Secondly, we have shown above that there was in truth no just, lawful, necessary cause, nay, not even a trifling occasion, of this prodigious change. For the Jews did not forget their own language in the captivity; nor, if they had forgotten it, would the inconvenience under which they labored have been in any way met by that change. But it was only after the death of Ezra and of all the prophets that that people began to fall away from the purity of the primeval [ p. 309 ] language; and that not from the forgetfulness which is said to have seized them during the captivity, but from the barbarism and Syriacism which their commerce with the neighboring nations rubbed into them. For when, a little before the times of the Maccabees, they were oppressed by the dominion and power of the Syrian empire, scattered hither and thither, retaining nothing sacred or whole in commonwealth or religion, they admitted also a corruption of their language; but this has nothing to do with the times of Ezra, who is said to have removed the ancient letters from their possession.

XLV. Nor, thirdly, will a change of this kind, if we would rather speak true things than marvelous, be judged even possible. Many thousands of Jews, very many of them priests and nobles, remained in the eastern parts, and never returned to Jerusalem. To deny that they kept copies of the holy Scripture among them is the part of men ignorant alike of the custom of that nation and of all ancient history. That all those copies were written in that character which had been in ancient use, even those who feign that a change was made by Ezra must necessarily concede. Now that the returned people sent off the ancient letters to the Samaritans, having devised new ones, or adopted the Assyrian into their place and use, at the very time when the greater part of the whole people, clinging to their eastern seats, employed and retained those ancient letters, no one, I think, unless possessed by prejudices, will readily believe. And that innumerable thousands of Jews remained in the East, that is, in Babylon and the adjacent Assyrian regions, Josephus is a most ample witness: within some years they grew into so great a multitude that they stirred up huge wars, which, after many battles in which they carried off the victory, were at last scarcely quieted and suppressed by the whole strength of the Parthian empire. By this argument the author of the Sepher Ikkarim (book 3, chapter 22) most powerfully proves that the sacred codices were never corrupted: "When," he says, "Ezra went up from Babylonia, there went up with him only a few; but the great men of Israel, the wise men and the nobles, all remained in Babylonia. This being so, since all the great men and those skilled in the law remained there, Ezra could not change anything in the law; otherwise his law would not have agreed with the law of all the others who remained in Babylonia, and who dwelt in the cities of Samaria and in the land of Assyria and in other places, who would not go up with Ezra." And this reasoning, which is indeed most powerful, holds equally against the change of the letters as against the corruption of the books. What, moreover, would have been done with those innumerable codices, written in the ancient letters, which were dispersed through almost the whole world, no one has yet shown. Indeed, after Ezra had devised the vowel points and inserted them in the text, there nevertheless remained innumerable copies throughout the whole East in which they were not inscribed; and that for other reasons, but also for this, namely, that it was not the work of a short time, nay, not of one generation or century, to communicate that divine invention [Greek: heurema] to all the Jews everywhere among the nations. Very many, therefore, using the ancient unpointed copies, transmitted others copied from [ p. 310 ] them to their posterity in the same manner in which they had received their own from their predecessors. And thus through all ages very many codices written without points always survived. But on the concession of this opinion of the learned men, this rejection of all the ancient letters having been made, and the substitution of the new completed within perhaps a few days, we must suppose that immediately, throughout the whole world, whatever Jews there were anywhere so consented to it that no vestige, report, monument, or rumor survived of those copies which alone they had possessed before, and which in some parts of Scripture they had received fresh from the very amanuenses of the Holy Spirit. From the time when this change of letters is feigned to have been made, down to the completion of the Babylonian Talmud about the year of Christ 500 or 600, there were very many colonies, synagogues, and schools of the Jews, continued in never-interrupted succession, to whom the Samaritans, for whose sake this innovation is pretended to have been made, were scarcely known; much less did they have any fellowship with them, or fear their commerce lest they be infected with the contagion of their superstition. It would be the very likeness of a prodigy that no one out of so many thousands took care to have copied the ancient exemplars of the Scriptures, whose characters had been consecrated to sacred uses first by God himself at the founding of their church, and then by the prophets in continuous succession down to the times in which some of them had lived. At whatever time, finally, the points were devised, it is plain that in every age very many copies were preserved and transcribed in which they were not inserted. But as the opinion of our adversaries has it, the new letters once adopted into use, the old were at once utterly banished from the borders of the church: "let Apella the Jew believe it!" (Horace, Satires 1.5.100).

XLVI. Fourthly: there are extant innumerable testimonies of the ancient Jews, by which they give sentence against the figment of the Ezraitic change of letters. Enough of them, if any testimonies can be thought enough in this cause, the distinguished Buxtorf long ago produced, to whom we remit the reader.

XLVII. Lastly: most of the things which are said about these Samaritans, and which are laid as foundations for the opinion of the change of letters, are altogether most uncertain. When they first received the Mosaic law is uncertain; that they corrupted it is most certain. Whether they ever seriously admitted the worship of God is uncertain; that they openly renounced it is most certain. Whether any of that off-scouring of nations which they were still survive is uncertain; or whether they have survived through many centuries; for those who now embrace their ravings are most of them, if not all, Jews by origin and blood, called Samaritans on account of their depraved superstition, as appears from the Itinerary of Benjamin. Our first commerce with those who are commonly so called we owe to the most noble Scaliger. He first wrote to them, and was the one who prompted Peiresc to send into the East to obtain a copy of their Pentateuch, as Gassendi relates in the Life of Peiresc, page 157.

XLVIII. What was accomplished by that mission the same author shows, [ p. 311 ] page 327. He likewise relates that Scaliger met his death before any copy of the Pentateuch was brought to his hands. He had indeed previously received an alphabet, and certain fragments concerning the computation of the year and seasons. From that time, all were pronounced half-men who would not believe that their letters, the figures of which he almost alone in all Europe possessed, were the most ancient Hebrew ones. For that supreme man had so framed himself that he could not endure anyone to dissent from him, even in the merest trifles, without revilings. Benjamin of Tudela in his Itinerary affirms that those Samaritans who lived at the time when he traveled through the East lacked three letters, namely He, Cheth, and Ayin [Hebrew: he, cheth, ayin]; which everyone knows could in no way have been the case if they used the ancient letters of the Hebrews; since of the He at least special mention is made [in Scripture, in the name Abraham]. Many indeed affirm that Benjamin lied. I confess he was a Jew, and therefore justly suspect in narrating the affairs and conditions of his own nation. But to say that he was a liar, and lied in this very narrative, is not sufficient, unless some weighty ground for casting that reproach is at hand. They say, then, that all the copies of the Pentateuch which have been brought into Europe prove that he lied audaciously and ignorantly. But in fact all those copies are much more recent than the age of Benjamin. What and how many they are Morinus teaches in his Samaritan Opuscula, and Hottinger, Smegma Orientale, book 3, part 2, chapter 4, and others. There is none of them which existed three hundred years ago. Since, then, we have shown the nefarious audacity of those men in corrupting the sacred text, who will give us assurance that they did not, at their pleasure, make whatever innovations they wished in their letters? Yet these things are not said by me as though I had resolved to put faith in Benjamin; although I know that he ought to be of no less authority among the learned than any of those who among the Jews bore testimony to the Ezraitic change of letters of which we have treated.

XLIX. But I fear I have dwelt longer than is fitting on these minutiae; which assuredly I would by no means have done, did I not perceive that this opinion about the change of the ancient Hebrew letters holds a primary place among those pernicious opinions which certain learned men cherish to the prejudice of the Hebrew Verity, and defend of set purpose.
Of the Mosaic Theology

Chapter argument: The fame of the Sinaitic legislation; before it laws nowhere publicly enacted, much less written; the Gentiles acknowledge Moses as the most ancient lawgiver; the origins of laws ascribed to God; Zaleucus the first among the nations to commit laws to writing; his age; the foundations of the Mosaic theology; First, divine revelation [ p. 312 ] the only rule of religious worship; the worship of God never left to the discretion of men; explication and confirmation of this first foundation; no doctrine to be accounted infallibly true except what, and so far as, it is immediately revealed by God; all the institutions of God to be observed with the utmost submission of mind; nothing uninstituted is pleasing to God in his worship; this foundation established by the punishment of transgressors; Second, justification by mere grace; its parts; acknowledgment of natural misery; all privileges granted freely to unworthy sinners; destitution of spiritual strength; reconciliation through the sacrificial expiation of sins; the uselessness [Greek: anopheleia] of the sacrifices of brute animals to that end; the true high priest to bear all sins in his own person; Third, that the ceremonial worship would not be absolutely eternal; a term appointed for it beforehand; Christ set forth to all; the glory, holiness, and faith of the Jewish church; the prophetic ministry.

I. That the fame of the most celebrated Mosaic legislation [Greek: nomothesia], which we have briefly narrated, passed early into almost the whole world, all the records of antiquity and the monuments of the origins of laws teach. There are indeed those who affirm that the use of letters was known to the peoples before that time. The things that hinder me from thinking the same with them have been set forth elsewhere. But there is no one, so far as I know, who has taught that written laws existed among the human race earlier than these laws of God. Nay, they are mere conjectures which are brought forward to prove that any writing preceded that which God himself engraved on the tables of stone. Nor, to proceed on our appointed way, is it confirmed by any adequate testimony that the peoples, before the Mosaic age, had established any standing laws for their own government, even unwritten [Greek: agraphous], beyond the dictates of right reason. In those beginnings of things, of peoples, and of nations, the wills of those in whose hands the rule lay were the law of public society. That they in turn should religiously observe that common equity and goodness which is congruent to human nature, with the ancient customs and the principles of reason and utility, relying on which a people coalesces into one body, was expected of them. But the Holy Spirit had foretold that the fame of this most holy legislation would spread itself most widely (Deut. 4:5, 6): "See, I have taught you statutes and judgments, even as Jehovah my God commanded me. You shall therefore observe and do them; for this is your wisdom and your understanding in the sight of the peoples, who, hearing all these statutes, shall say, Surely this great people is a wise and understanding people." That is, having heard of this legislation and perceived the equity of these laws, they would convict themselves of folly and rashness for having lived until then wandering and unstable, almost in the manner of wild beasts, loosed from laws. Those, therefore, who watched over the good of the peoples earnestly endeavored to express and imitate among their own this benefit of God conferred on that nation: for the wisest of the Greeks acknowledge that Moses was the first lawgiver of all. So Diodorus Siculus, Library, book 1, chapter 94: "After the ancient [ p. 313 ] settlement of life in Egypt, which is fabulously reported to have been under the gods and heroes, they say that Moses first persuaded the multitude to use written laws and to live by them, a man recorded as both great in soul and most capable in his manner of life."

II. The wise have always clearly perceived that all right and power, of one or of many over others, draws its origin from God himself. For since all power resides in God by way of eminence [Greek: kath' hyperochen], they have most rightly judged that no one can duly become a partaker of it except through him; and accordingly they thought it fitting that laws should be imposed on the society of peoples by his divine authority. And this truth they had either seen or heard sealed up in this Mosaic legislation. Hence the most celebrated lawgivers among the nations, Zaleucus, Lycurgus, Minos, Numa, steadily feigned that they had received the laws with which they had it in mind to bind their peoples from gods, I know not which: this one, namely, from Egeria in the grove of Aricia; that one from Jupiter in the Cretan cave; another from Apollo at Delphi; the first from Minerva. For those figments owe their origin to nothing else than the fame of this most celebrated Israelite legislation.

III. He who first among the nations wrote laws was Zaleucus the Locrian, who lived either a little before, or about, the Babylonian captivity. Strabo says of the Locrians: "They are believed to have been the first to use written laws." And Marcian of Heraclea: "And nearby lie the Locrians called Epizephyrian; these, they say, were the first to use written laws, which Zaleucus is thought to have laid down" (Marcian of Heraclea). For indeed it was among the laws of Lycurgus that they should not be written.

IV. I return to my purpose. The Mosaic theology, whose revelation we have set forth, had certain general heads, or most holy principles, on which, as on unmoved foundations, the heavenly doctrine and all the institutions of divine worship rested. These must be briefly run through.
V. "That divine revelation is the only foundation, the sole norm, the one rule of all religious worship that is pleasing or acceptable to God, occupies the first place in this theology."

VI. This truth, if any other in religion most necessary to be known, is now at last consecrated to eternal memory, handed down in the faithful monuments of the divine writings. God indeed never, from the laying of the world's foundations, allowed the will of men to give mode or measure to his worship. But this eminent foundation of true theology was, until this Mosaic legislation, hedged about with much darkness. Many things, moreover, by the connivance of God, were passed over without public rebuke which were by no means pleasing to him. But now at length the religious observance of this clearly revealed principle became a celebrated part [ p. 314 ] of divine worship. In it there are these three things, of which there is frequent mention in the books of the Old Testament.

VII. For first, that theological principle teaches that no doctrines concerning God or his worship, save those alone which he himself had revealed, are to be accounted of infallible or undoubted truth in religion (Deut. 4:2): "You shall not add to the word which I command you"; and chapter 12:32. That truth alone is saving and life-giving which is written. It ought to be taught, it ought to be expounded; to be augmented by anyone, by no means.

VIII. Further: the same truth teaches that all and each of the things instituted by God in his worship, however discordant with reason, difficult, or useless they may seem, are, on account of the supreme authority of him who instituted and sanctioned them, to be observed and practiced with the utmost religion and submission of mind. For very many of the Jewish institutions were arbitrary, flowing from the sole good pleasure of the lawgiver, and enacted by the right of his absolute sovereignty [Greek: autokratoria]; and Peter testifies that the whole body of them was a yoke hard to bear [Greek: zygon dysbastakton] (Acts 15:10), to which is opposed the rational service [Greek: latreia logike] of Christians. On account of the preceptive will of God alone, all those things were to be religiously observed. That nothing at all is to be added to these in the worship of God is what remains of that first foundation. And all these things are sanctioned by the second precept of the Decalogue.

IX. But since proud little men are prone to bring into religious worship the figments of their own heart, smoothed over with specious pretexts, especially of a wisdom [Greek: sophia] placed in will-worship and humility [Greek: en ethelothreskeia kai tapeinophrosyne, Col. 2:23], as the experience of all ages testifies, God, in order to render this part of the new theology utterly inviolable and sacred for the future, confirmed and established it by the horrendous punishment of those who first revolted from its rule. For after he himself had sent down fire from heaven upon the altar, the type and emblem of the Holy Spirit, and the sons of the high priest, not attending to his mind, had used strange fire in the sacred services, immediately, because they had not duly sanctified the name of God, they were themselves consumed by fire. And this is the first foundation of divine truth in this Mosaic enlargement of theology, which is solemnly consecrated to eternity.

X. "That righteousness, acceptance before God, deliverance from sins, and finally eternal salvation, are to be expected only of mere grace, through the promised seed, this theology indicated in the next place." For there is promised a mediator of peace between God and men, who through the sacrifice of himself was to obtain eternal redemption, and to reconcile sinners to God. It taught that all favor with God is to be ascribed wholly and entirely to him. That saving foundation, therefore, of supernatural theology, or of the reconciling of sinners to God, which by the giving of the first promise, and then by the renewal of the covenant with Noah, had been published somewhat more obscurely, but was more clearly set forth to Abraham, is now openly and plainly unfolded. And in it are contained these eminent dogmas of the new theology.

XI. 1. "That those theologians who were now endowed with the light of this heavenly truth were in themselves most miserable sinners, lost, accursed, children of wrath no less than others, and no better than those whom it pleased God to pass by in this manifestation of his grace." All this spiritual misery, whatever it amounts to, God solemnly compelled these new theologians to acknowledge and confess, that the sharper and firmer sense of it might penetrate into their hearts and consciences (Deut. 26:5): "You shall protest, saying before Jehovah your God, A wretched Syrian was my father."

XII. 2. "That the spiritual privileges, whatever either they themselves or their forefathers obtained, and moreover the singular benefits of which they were made partakers, all befell them out of the mere grace of God, without any regard to their works or services." Distinguished above others to this end is the passage found in Deuteronomy 9:4-6; to which may be added chapter 7:6-9, and 10:15.

XIII. 3. "That although God had bestowed upon them the unspeakable benefit of his word, they were nevertheless so far destitute of spiritual strength that, unless he himself deigned besides to come to their aid with his effectual and heart-turning grace, they would use it in vain, so far at least as concerns pleasing God and obtaining eternal salvation" (Deut. 29:4; 30:6).

XIV. 4. "That reconciliation, the expiation of sins, and remission must be procured through a bloody sacrifice." There was no institution that did not teach this; especially that solemn sacrifice to be offered yearly on the day of expiation (Lev. 16); whence that word of the apostle (Heb. 9:22): "Almost all things are by the law purged with blood, and without shedding of blood there is no remission."

XV. 5. "That no sacrifices of brute animals were of themselves suitable or equal to the taking away of sin, so that he who wished by them to draw near to God and expect his favor might come away perfectly free from its guilt." And therefore they were all instituted to this end, that they might prefigure another more excellent sacrifice. Hence it came about that when these theologians lost the true knowledge of the use of these sacrifices, and had so squandered their faith in the Messiah to come, nowhere secure or free from the goads of conscience and the sense of the curse of the law, they fell headlong into horrendous idolatry (Mic. 6:6-8).
XVI. 6. "That the high priest in his own person would bear all the sins of the whole people" (Exod. 28:38). And these were the things contained in that second foundation.

XVII. In the place of a third foundation this theology taught: "That all these institutions of religious worship, however excellent and full of glory, the partakers of whose benefit attained an eminent honor beyond the lot of others, were to endure only until an appointed time, and had a fixed period prescribed, beyond which it would be lawful for no one to cleave to them." Namely, that he would at last come for whom all things were laid up, furnished with the authority of a lawgiver, who would most perfectly announce the will of God, [ p. 316 ] to whose word all would be bound to be obedient at his bidding, under penalty of extermination from the people of God (Deut. 18:16-19).

XVIII. By the light and help of these principles, that great foundation and corner-stone, Jesus Christ, the promised seed of Adam and of Abraham, occupied his place in that Israelite church. And from this flowed the glory and dignity of that church, God moreover rendering it enriched and heaped up with new benefits, that it might become a more illustrious type, of more excellent glory, of the kingdom of Christ to be administered more spiritually (2 Cor. 3). It cannot indeed be denied that many of these theologians, through excessive love and admiration of that carnal glory which accompanied the ceremonial worship, were hindered from looking forward to the end of the things to be abolished. But it is equally certain that all the faithful burned from then on with desire to enjoy the things which were prefigured through that whole most holy apparatus [choragium: the furnishing of a chorus, the entire equipage]. But that singular thing which rendered that church, imbued and instructed with the doctrine we have briefly set forth, glorious and dear to God, was that holiness, or saving communion with God himself, which very many of its members cultivated. The faith and obedience of many indeed are consecrated to eternal memory by the Holy Spirit, that they might be for our instruction and consolation. And there were very many others, whose names, unknown to us, are written in the book of life, who, relying on the light and guidance of this theology, walked continually before God in all holy obedience, most full of inward peace and of the consolations of the Holy Spirit.

XIX. And after God, in the very founding of this church, had entrusted to its faith, through the revelations made to the lawgiver, those theological principles which we have reviewed, in order that they might be the better rescued from oblivion and the more effectually inculcated upon a rebellious people, in almost every age, beyond the ordinary ministry of the priests and Levites, he raised up prophets out of the ordinary course, who partly by the living voice, partly by God-breathed [Greek: theopneustois] writings, should elucidate those principles, and exhort the people to order their religious worship and life according to their rule. For in preserving the honor of the word of God, and thereby the authority of God himself, whole and entire; in preaching righteousness and the remission of sins through the Christ to come; and in sustaining the church with the hope and expectation of the spiritual glory to be brought in by the Messiah, the prophets bestowed all their labor, as is plain from their writings.

books.title: On the Corruption and Solemn Restoration of the Mosaic Theology; and on Its Abolition

blocks.text: Chapter argument: The duration of the church before and after reformation. The state of the reformed churches. The twofold defection of the Jewish church. The first, partial. Its nature. The denial of the first foundation of the Mosaic theology. The superstitious worship flowing from it. This twofold, with respect to the object of worship, and the means.

I. This Mosaic worship, although it was not to be eternal, was nevertheless more permanent and more stable than the condition, under it, of those who drew near [Greek: ton proserchomenon] to God. For just as we have found that all others, from the laying of the foundations of the world, in the course of time fell away from the primitive divine institution and from the principles of theology in which they had been instructed, so we shall see this new nation of theologians entangle itself in the fellowship of the same crime with them. Now the defection of the Jewish church, which used this theology, was twofold: a partial defection, to be repaired by the grace of God through a restoration; and a final one, which was to receive its last punishment in the utter destruction [Greek: panolethria] of the nation. And since that church had of old been divided into two parts, the part which embraced the ten tribes rushed into both defections, and so into perpetual ruin, more quickly than the other part, to which fell the seat of the solemn worship of God prescribed by God himself.

II. The apostasy of the Jewish church took its beginning from a certain degree of defection from the divine truth exhibited in those three theological foundations which we set forth above. And there was added, as commonly happens, a corruption of morals. For wherever men bind themselves with the crime of contempt for divine truth, they straightway show themselves accomplices in all other crimes as well. For it is inevitable that the morals of men go to ruin where the whole rule and foundation of true obedience lies despised.

III. Under the name of partial apostasy I reckon all the errors, superstitions, idolatries, and other crimes of which the Jewish people was guilty at various times from the first institution down to the Babylonian captivity. Now from the institution at Sinai to that captivity this church had completed a thousand and seven years; but once restored by the people on their return, it was blotted out by total destruction within six hundred years. So it generally is: since no ecclesiastical reformation has ever perfectly expressed its archetype [Greek: prototypon], so no church will ever be found to have equalled, in its reformed state, the duration of its original constitution. I fear the reformed churches in Europe may serve as an example. That they never attained the perfection of the apostolic churches we all confess and lament; for, hampered by very many earthly affairs, they have hitherto scarcely been able to extricate themselves happily from their snares. [ p. 318 ] There are therefore many things which strike fear into me that they will not continue, adorned with that holiness and purity which they graciously obtained, for so great a span of time as those first churches measured out, which nevertheless, as we know, fell away. The causes and reasons of this fear we shall perhaps explain elsewhere. What we have said stands firm: mingled with the affairs and political conditions of peoples and nations, they have never been able to banish far enough from themselves the manners, corruptions, and filth of the age. Hence already, in certain places not so long ago illumined with divine truth, matters have come to such confusion that scarcely any face of an evangelical church remains; while to those whose lot it has been, by God's gracious disposing, to pass their age under the discipline of the cross, the magnates have long been almost their destruction, the degenerate sons and grandsons of noble and heroic parents, who, gaping most greedily after the honors and riches of the age, by their most shameful defection from the profession of divine truth have dragged very many along with them to perish eternally. I fear I may speak only too truly: "Our parents' age, worse than our grandfathers', has borne us more worthless still, soon to produce an offspring yet more corrupt" (Horace, Odes 3.6.46).

IV. But to return from this digression. This partial defection had respect to those three foundations of the Mosaic theology mentioned above. The first taught the entire fullness and perfection of the sacred Scriptures. This they at times either consigned to oblivion or counted as nothing. From that crime flowed all that infamous superstitious and arbitrary worship, so often and so gravely rebuked under that name by the Holy Spirit. For hence they judged it right and lawful for themselves to devise whatever they, in utter vanity, supposed would serve to promote the worship of God; as if, forsooth, the glory of God were to be fashioned according to their whim and pleasure. With the same madness the Christian world still rages. To root out this most pernicious error God employed the extraordinary ministry of the prophets. And that the authority of this foundation of his worship, which they were wickedly tearing up, might stand unshaken, although there was no lack, as perhaps it may seem to us, of graver charges he might bring against an idolatrous people, yet he presses upon them chiefly this aggravation of their impiety, that they observed in religious worship things "which he himself had not commanded, neither had they come into his heart" (Jer. 7:31; 19:4, 5).

V. Now the apostates fell away from this theological principle in two ways: first, with respect to the object of worship; then, with respect to the means. For, to speak of the first point first, they took to themselves strange gods besides the true God, to be worshipped with him, without him, after him. But all worship of idols, or of strange gods, carries with it either a tacit or an express denial of the true God. For however the idolaters may hold out his name as a pretext, he does not choose to be of the number of the gods. And since the principal part of the initial defection of these theologians consisted in the crime of this foreign worship, it may not seem [ p. 319 ] foreign to our design if I briefly recount the idols themselves which this people, prone to superstitions even to a marvel, worshipped in the place of the living God; after I have first said a few things which must necessarily be premised.

Chapter argument: The idols of all nations worshipped by the Israelites. A brief summary of ancient Israelite geography. Which nations were known to them. Chaldea. Mesopotamia. Egypt. Arabia. Phoenicia. Syria. Canaan. The idols of those nations. The modes and means of superstitious worship.

I. Very often God complains through the prophets that his people, setting him aside, wickedly worshipped the gods of all nations everywhere. But it is evident that this must be understood of the nations known to them; and the Holy Spirit himself thus expounds his own meaning in 2 Kings 17:15: "They followed," he says, "the nations that were round about them." Which these were, and how far the most ancient sacred geography extended itself, it is fitting to set out summarily beforehand. Abraham sprang from Ur of the Chaldeans.
