Chaldea has on the north Mesopotamia, touching the Euphrates; on the east Susiana, toward the Tigris; on the south the Persian Gulf; and on the west Arabia Deserta. There was no region they knew better, especially since it was the head of the Assyrian empire.

II. From Chaldea, having crossed the river at God's command, the same Abraham removed into Aram Naharaim, or Mesopotamia: a region most famous for its situation between most celebrated rivers, and for many reasons familiar to the Israelites. From there Jacob had of old taken his wives; and accordingly that nation was the second stock of the people, to whose borders also the bounds of the Davidic empire once extended themselves. Egypt they called Mizraim, from the son of Ham, who occupied that land immediately after the dispersion of Babel. Their long servitude there, while the nation was being formed, did not allow them to be ignorant of it; nor afterwards did the nearness of that empire, or their commerce with it.

III. Arabia in general they called Ereb; Arabia Deserta, Kedar; Arabia Felix, Sheba; Arabia Petraea, Aram Zobah. In it the Ammonites, Moabites, Midianites, and Edomites were thoroughly known to them, from the common origin of their stocks; and the rest of the inhabitants from their nearness, and from the wars which they waged against them. The Mediterranean was to them the great sea; the western sea, and whatever lands lay beyond it, they called Tarshish, although the Septuagint renders that word variously, and notably at Isaiah 23:1 as Carthage [Greek: Karchedon].

IV. In the land of Canaan they had a most familiar knowledge of all the nations, both those which were subdued or destroyed by them in war, and those which, surviving that destruction, lived under their own law. That all Phoenicia thence, together with that part of Syria which borders on Arabia and is called Coele-Syria, which they scarcely distinguished from Arabia Petraea, was known to them, is evident from the sacred [ p. 320 ] histories. The Persians, or Elamites, they came to know only late, and that very obscurely. But since commerce with the nations was forbidden to them, and they made no use of navigation, except very sparingly and for one or two generations, it is no wonder that the remaining regions of the world were unknown to them, just as they themselves were unknown to most. The East Indies, perhaps, they designated by the name Ophir; all Europe, Kittim; Greece, Phobel [Tubal]; Ionia, Javan; Media, Madai; Lydia, Lud; Africa, Put; Ethiopia, Lud; Spain, Sepharad; Gaul, Zarephath. But of all these regions only a thin and obscure report reached them, so that it wholly escaped them by what manners their peoples lived, and what gods they worshipped. Being ignorant, therefore, of Hellenism, that is, of European superstition, they chose for their own adoration and worship the gods or idols of the eastern nations, as they knew them. For the Chaldeans were the authors to them of the worship of the Sun; the Teraphim came down from Mesopotamia; to Egypt they owed the Calves. Into the crime of Baal-peor they were led by the counsels of the Moabites and Midianites. Molech was the idol of the Ammonites. The Tyrians taught them the multiform worship of Baal; with the Philistines they pursued Beelzebub with religious honor; the rites of Astarte, or the queen of heaven, they learned when ensnared by the wiles of the Syrians; to the unnamed gods of Damascus king Ahaz sacrificed; and with the Persians or others they began to mourn for Tammuz. And these things bring to light the true sense of very many passages in the prophets, especially Jeremiah, Hosea, Amos, and Ezekiel, in which God complains that his people defiled itself with spiritual adultery with all the nations.

V. Further: all the superstitious rites which those nations used in the worship of their idols, however ridiculous, shameful, filthy, abominable beyond what can be said or imagined, they adopted in serving those very idols. They sacrificed horses to the Sun with the Chaldeans and Persians; the Calf of the wilderness they worshipped with dances and revellings, exactly as the Egyptians did Apis. They imitated the Ammonites and Tyrians in the immolation of their sons and daughters. The Syrian libations made to Astarte they reproduced in the wine and cakes which they offered to the queen of heaven. In a word: nothing so silly or ridiculous, nothing so unspeakable or wicked, nothing so obscene or dung-like had ever been devised by the most foolish and accursed idolaters, or rather suggested to them by the prince of darkness, but that they preferred it to the most holy and most chaste worship of the living God.

Chapter argument: How false gods are called appellatively in the Scriptures: "no gods" [Hebrew: lo elohim]; "vanities" [Hebrew: elilim]; in the Septuagint, things made with hands, abominations, dumb idols, gods, images, demons, auguries, vain things. Whence elil is derived. With respect to efficacy: dead things [Hebrew: methim], lies [Hebrew: sheqarim], iniquities [Hebrew: onim]. With respect to their effects on the consciences of the superstitious: vapors [Hebrew: havalim], emptiness [Hebrew: tohu], pangs [Hebrew: tsirim], griefs [Hebrew: atsabbim]. With respect to matter: gods of gold, gods of silver, gods of stone, of wood. With respect to external form: semel, tselamim (images). With respect to matter and [ p. 321 ] visible form: molten gods [Hebrew: elohei massekhah], graven things [Hebrew: pesilim]. With respect to the true God and the covenant people: other gods [Hebrew: elohim aherim], gods of the stranger [Hebrew: elohei ha-nekar]. With respect to their worshippers: gods of the peoples, gods beyond the river. From peculiar reasons: new gods, teraphim, sun-images [Hebrew: hammanim]. From the guilt of idolatry: detestable things [Hebrew: shiqqutsim]. By way of contempt: dung-gods [Hebrew: gillulim]. The reasons of all these are indicated.

I. The Old Testament sometimes expresses idols, or false gods, in common or appellatively; there are also places where it distinguishes them singly by their proper names. In common, however, they are called by various names for various reasons. It is worth while to subjoin a specimen of this variety.

II. First, according to the truth of the matter they are called lo elohim, "gods which are no gods": 2 Chron. 13:9, "Whoever comes to consecrate himself shall be a priest to lo elohim," "to no gods"; that is, to false gods, who are anything but God. And again: Jer. 2:11, "Has a nation changed its gods, and they themselves no gods [Hebrew: elohim ve-hemmah lo elohim]," that is, gods which are no gods. The same words are repeated in chapter 16:20, "Shall a man make for himself gods, and they themselves no gods?" These are those "so-called gods" [Greek: legomenoi theoi] of whom the apostle speaks, 1 Cor. 8:5: those, namely, who are wickedly called gods by many, but are not; that is, they are not, as he speaks elsewhere (Gal. 4:8), gods by nature [Greek: physei theoi], but only in opinion [Greek: kata doxan] and putatively.

III. Next, with respect to their efficacy and effect they are called by various names; as elilim: Lev. 26:1, "You shall not make for yourselves elilim." This word occurs about twenty times applied to idols. The Septuagint renders it variously. In the place just cited, by cheiropoieta, things made by hand: "gods wrought by the hands of men" (Isa. 2:8); by bdelygmata, abominations, or things to be abominated; and in the same way at Ezek. 30:13. At Lev. 19:4, eidola; and at Hab. 2:18, eidola kopha, "idols" and "dumb idols." Also at Ps. 97:7, glypta, "graven things"; at Isa. 19:3 they have theoi and agalmata, "gods and images"; at Ps. 96:5, daimonia, "demons"; at Jer. 14:14, oionismata, "auguries"; at Zech. 11:17, mataia, "vain things." In this last place only do they render the sense of the word. Now elil may be regarded as if formed from al el, "not God": whatever it may be, God it is not. But it is more correctly derived from alal, vain, "a thing of nothing"; which an idol truly is. Hence the apostle, 1 Cor. 8:4, "We know that an idol is nothing in the world"; that is, in power or efficacy it can do nothing at all. Hence false gods are called elilim, that is, vain, things of nothing. And the apostle calls all the gods of the nations "vain things" [Greek: mataia], that is, elilim (Acts 14:15). Our translators almost everywhere render this word "idols."

IV. In the same respect they are called onim: Isa. 66:3, "as one that blesses aven [iniquity]." Our translators: "As he that blesseth an idol." The Septuagint: "as a blasphemer" [Greek: hos blasphemos]; for he who blesses an idol curses God. The same word occurs at Hos. 6:8; Amos 1:5. Jerome renders it "idol." The word itself properly signifies a lie, and labor with weariness; iniquity also, and vanity; all of which as it were conspire together in the worship of idols. For they are themselves lies, that is, they speak false things, since they mock their worshippers, or rather the worshippers mock themselves, with vain hope; and those who weary themselves in any superstitious worship will carry away nothing whatever besides vanity and misery.

V. For the same reasons, namely with respect to efficacy and outcome, they are called sheqarim, "liars" or "lies": Isa. 44:20, "Is there not a lie [Hebrew: sheqer]" (Septuagint: pseudos) "in my right hand?" That is, an idol, which will be to me as a lie, performing nothing of what it seems to promise. Hos. 7:1, "They have wrought sheqer," a lie; Jerome, "an idol."

VI. And hence also they are called methim, "the dead": Ps. 106:28, "They joined themselves to Baal-peor, and ate the sacrifices of the dead [Hebrew: methim]." "Sacrifices of the dead" is a genitive of object: sacrifices which were offered to the dead; that is, to gods who can furnish help to no one, but are altogether like a dead thing, vain and useless, from which to expect any help or comfort is nothing short of the utmost madness. And therefore, in opposition to all the gods of the nations, Paul says pointedly that he had hoped "in the living God" [Greek: epi Theo zonti] (1 Tim. 4:10).

VII. Thirdly, with respect to the effects which worship not instituted by the true God has upon the consciences and minds of the superstitious, and the final outcome of all idolatry, they are likewise called by various names.

VIII. In this respect they are called atsabbim, "sorrows" and "troubles"; for being occupied with the utmost solicitude in arbitrary worship, the superstitious [Greek: deisidaimones] attain nothing besides the greatest sorrows and anxieties: 2 Sam. 5:21, "And the Philistines left there their sorrows [Hebrew: atsabbehem]." Septuagint, "their gods" [Greek: tous theous]; our translators, "their images." 1 Sam. 31:9, "They published it in the house of their atsabbim," "of their idols"; Septuagint, "to their idols." And in several other places they render it by eidola, sometimes by glypta, graven things, as at Isa. 46:1, Ps. 106:36. For atsav also signifies "to form" or "to smooth"; as Job 10:8, "Your hands have fashioned [Hebrew: itsvuni]," or formed, "me." And thus images might seem to be called atsabbim from this smoothing and forming, and on that account to be rendered by glypta; for so the rabbis call the sculptured images of the saints among Christians. But the origin of the word is otsev, indeed it is the very word that is used, that is, "pain" and "sadness": for images bring nothing to their worshippers besides labor and pain, that is, the utmost anxiety of conscience together with a sense of divine wrath.

IX. For the same reason they are called tsirim, that is, "pangs": Isa. 45:16, "The craftsmen of pangs [Hebrew: tsirim] have gone away in shame." Junius, "the makers of images"; our translators, "the makers of idols"; the Vulgate, "the fabricators of errors." The Septuagint: "Be renewed unto me, O islands" [Greek: enkainizesthe pros me nesoi], wide of the mark altogether [literally, "quite beyond the olive trees," a proverb for straying off course]. The Arabic follows them. They omit what is in the text, and add things that are in no way indicated. The word properly signifies "the pangs of women in travail." That superstition afflicts and torments the consciences of men with the like of these, the prophet testifies (Mic. 6:7).

X. In the same respect also they are called tohu; that is, "something undigested and confused," also desolation, devastation, solitude. For all religious worship instituted outside the divine ordinance devastates and confounds the consciences of men, which it ought to cultivate and confirm. [ p. 323 ] 1 Sam. 12:21, "And turn not aside after ha-tohu, which will not profit nor deliver, for they are tohu." Septuagint: "they are nothing" [Greek: outhen eisi]. Some think they are also called amal, "toil" (Num. 23:21).

XI. By reason of the same effect, and with respect to the outcome, they are also named havalim, "vanities": Jer. 14:22; 8:19; 10:8, "Are there any among the vanities of the nations [Hebrew: be-havlei ha-goyim] that cause rain?" Septuagint: "Is there among the idols of the nations one that gives rain?" See Deut. 32:21.

XII. With respect to the matter of the immediate objects of worship they are also designated in various ways. Hence, and by way of contempt, they are named ets, "wood": Hos. 4:12, "My people asks counsel of its wood [Hebrew: be-etso]"; and Jer. 10:8. Whatever the idolaters may falsely imagine for themselves, their gods are wood, and nothing besides. Also elohei even, "gods of stone," or stone gods: Deut. 4:28, "You shall serve gods of wood and stone." Also elohei keseph, "gods of silver," or silver gods; and elohei zahav, "gods of gold," or golden gods: that is, composed and cast of silver or gold (Exod. 20:23). And the one calf of Aaron is called elohei zahav, "gods of gold" (Exod. 32:31).

XIII. From their external form also they are variously named. Most frequently tselamim, "images, likenesses": Num. 33:52, "You shall destroy the images of their molten gods [Hebrew: tsalmei massekhotam]." Septuagint, "molten idols" [Greek: eidola choneuta]. And similarly 2 Chron. 23:17. Our translators everywhere, "images." To form also is referred semel; it frequently occurs with pesel, a word which properly signifies a graven thing: Deut. 4:16, "Lest perhaps you corrupt yourselves, and make for yourselves a pesel, a graven thing, the likeness of any figure [Hebrew: temunat kol samel]," or idol. Septuagint: "a graven likeness, any image."

XIV. With respect to matter and form together they are called pesilim, "graven things"; for a pesel is an image or likeness hewn out of stone, wood, or any other material whatsoever (Exod. 20:4; Lev. 26:1; Judg. 18:17; Isa. 42:17). The Septuagint renders this word variously: glypta (Lev. 26:1; Deut. 4:16), that is, carved things; in the same sense also glymmata, "sculptures" (Isa. 45:20); eidola (Exod. 20:4; 2 Chron. 33:22); also agalmata, or "statues" (Isa. 21:9); and eikones, "images" (Isa. 40:19, 20); and perizomioi, "the enclosures of altars" (2 Chron. 34:3). Our translators constantly, "graven images." By this name the rabbis commonly call the images which certain Christians make in order to adore them. Hence pasal in Chaldee is to profane, or render profane; for nothing is more profane than the making of statues [Greek: agalmatopoiia].

XV. Hence also they are styled elohei massekhah, "gods of fusion," or molten, "molten gods": Exod. 34:17, "You shall not make for yourself molten gods." And there is a description which comprehends all these, Deut. 4:28: "gods, the work of the hands of man" [Hebrew: elohim maaseh yedei adam].

XVI. With respect to the true God and his covenant people they are styled elohim aherim, "other gods" (Exod. 20:3); who, because they are not that true God, are in reality no gods at all; such as that covenant people ought never to have admitted. And in the same sense elohei ha-nekar, "strange gods," or foreign; and zarim, "strangers" (Deut. 32:16), in several places.

XVII. Taking their denomination from their worshippers they are called elohei ha-ammim (Deut. 13:7 [8]), "the gods of the peoples," peoples rejected by the true God and from his covenant and worship. For which reason also, Josh. 24:14, they are called elohei be-ever ha-nahar, "the gods beyond the river," whom, namely, the Chaldeans venerated.
XVIII. From peculiar reasons, or from a peculiar imaginary use, some idols have obtained particular names.

XIX. Of this kind is hammanim: "The groves and the hammanim shall not stand" (Isa. 27:9). Septuagint, eidola; our translators, "images." And Lev. 26:30, "I will destroy your high places, and cut down your hammanim." Septuagint: "your wooden things made with hands." And Isa. 17:8, "He shall not look to the hammanim." Septuagint: "the abominations" [Greek: ta bdelygmata]. They seem to be so called from hammah, the sun, which is called by that name from hom, heat. Whence our translators, whenever this word occurs, add in the margin "sun images," although another derivation of the word can also be given; and sometimes it signifies simply "images."

XX. Of the same kind is teraphim. The etymology of the word is unknown. They are mistaken who derive it from raphah. It seems to be a gentile, that is, a foreign word. We shall say more about it under the proper names of the idols. The Septuagint very often retains the word itself, as at Judg. 17:5; 18:14, 17; 1 Sam. 15:23. Sometimes they render it by eidola, as Gen. 31:19, 34, 35; and kenotaphia, that is, "empty images," as 1 Sam. 19:13. Also glypta, "graven things" (Ezek. 21:21); and apophthengomenoi, "speakers" (Zech. 10:2), for reasons soon to be explained. Our translators either retain "teraphim," as at Hos. 3:4, or render it by "images," with the word "teraphim" everywhere noted in the margin.
XXI. Also elohim hadashim, "new gods": Judg. 5:8, "The people chose new gods," mushrooms of the earth, which the day before yesterday had no existence at all. See chapter 10:14.

XXII. Next, from the nature and desert of idolatry, idols are frequently called shiqqutsim, "abominations": 2 Kings 23:24, "Josiah purged out all the idols and all the abominations [Hebrew: ve-et kol ha-shiqqutsim]." Septuagint, prosochthismata, "detestations"; things detestable, execrable. In 1 Kings 11:5, 7, Milcom is called the shiqquts of the Ammonites; and Chemosh, the shiqquts of the Moabites; and Molech, the shiqquts of the children of Ammon. The Septuagint everywhere, eidolon. See Ezek. 20:7, 8; Zech. 9:7; Jer. 4:1; 32:34; Isa. 66:3.

XXIII. Finally, by way of utmost contempt they are called gillulim; that is, "filth, dung, wallowing-places": Ezek. 22:3, "She has made gillulim against herself, to defile herself," dung-gods. Lev. 26:30; Deut. 29:16. Galal is "dung": Job 20:7; Ezek. 4:12, 15. Hence this name was fastened upon the gods of the nations, since they deserve no more honorable one.

XXIV. These things being premised, let us now proceed to review all those idols, in the order in which they are mentioned in the sacred writings, which before the Babylonian captivity were worshipped either by the whole people, or at least by such a part of the people that its defection might most justly be imputed to the whole.

Chapter argument: The first foreign god worshipped by the Israelites, Baal-peor or Baal-phegor. He was a Moabite idol. In his worship a snare was laid for the Israelites by the Midianites, with Balaam as instructor. Whence the name Peor [Hebrew: pe'or]. Whether Baal-peor was Priapus. The obscenities commonly said to have been employed in the worship of this idol. Sacrifices of the dead. A Moabite mountain called Peor. Baal thence called Peorian.

I. The first foreign god, with whose worship the people returned out of Egypt defiled itself, was Baal-peor. He is first mentioned in Num. 25:3, "Israel joined himself to Baal-peor." Likewise Ps. 106:28, "They joined themselves also to Baal-peor, and ate the sacrifices of the dead." And Hos. 9:10, "They came to Baal-peor, and separated themselves to that shameful thing." He is also called simply Peor: Num. 25:18, "They have plotted against you in the matter of Peor"; and Josh. 22:17, "We have not yet cleansed ourselves from the iniquity of Peor."

II. Now Baal-peor was a Moabite idol. Nevertheless the Midianites, together with the Moabites, performed sacred rites to him. For they used their snares against the Israelites who were sacrificing to him; and they did so with Balaam as their instructor (Num. 31:15, 16); and in the New Testament these enticements to fornication are called "the doctrine of Balaam" (Rev. 2:14). With what allurements and arguments the Midianite women enticed the Israelites into the deceit and crime of both kinds of adultery, Josephus relates (Antiquities, book 4, chapter 6).

III. What kind of idol it was is greatly disputed. Perhaps this name was given by the Holy Spirit, in contempt, to the abomination of the Midianites or Moabites, while it was called otherwise by its worshippers. For very many say that Peor signifies something obscene and filthy; the word indeed is of most uncertain origin. Pa'ar signifies "to open"; also, in Chaldee, "to void flesh that has been eaten"; also "to lay bare" and "to uncover." From this Bucer derives the origin of the word in his comments on Ps. 106; Calvin mentions this etymology on the same passage, but does not venture to embrace it. The rabbis chiefly use this word when they make mention of this idol: "uncovering himself before Baal-peor" (Tractate Sanhedrin, fol. 60).

IV. Most of the ancients believed Baal-peor to have been Priapus: so Jerome, on Hosea 9, and Against Jovinian, book 1, chapter 32; for no other reason, I think, than that the Midianites, having enticed the Israelites to fornication, polluted them at the same time with the rites of this idol. But Priapus was not yet born; namely the man of Lampsacus on the Pontus, who, driven from his country for his outrages, was afterwards enrolled among the gods, for reasons it is shameful to relate. Moreover, those fornications did not belong to the rites of the idol; for that some Israelites committed that crime in the camp itself is proved by the well-known intervention [Greek: enstasis] in the case of Zimri and Cozbi.

V. From the signification of the word pe'or, and the fiction of Priapus attached to it, [ p. 326 ] some take occasion, in explaining the mysteries of this idol, to bring forward obscenities of such a kind as cannot be pleasing to modest minds and ears, and ought not to be thrust upon others. Let him who pleases consult the distinguished Vossius, On the Origin of Idolatry, book 2, chapter 1. In a word, the Hebrews say: "It is called Peor because they uncovered before it the orifice of the bowel and discharged excrement." But other reasons also might be given why the idol obtained its name from pa'ar, to open. Perhaps it was so called from the immense gaping of the mouth with which it was carved; or, as Isidore says, because some gazed upon the image with open and gaping mouths. But it is only too probable that, as the Israelite fornications gave Christians occasion to think this idol was Priapus, so the Jews seized occasion from the signification of the word to refer everything filthy and obscene to its worship.

VI. To the adoration of this idol is joined the eating of the sacrifices of the dead (Ps. 106). Some understand rites celebrated to the gods of the shades [the di manes]. But that superstition had not yet grown up; indeed it was scarcely born. We showed above that all idols, with respect to efficacy and power, are dead, and are so called. Of these Baal-peor was one; eating of the sacrifices offered to him, they forsook the living God and ate the sacrifices of the dead.

VII. It is therefore not yet settled what god this Baal-peor was. Of Baal we shall see hereafter; the whole difficulty is created by the addition "Peor." From it have sprung the various conjectures we have related. But Peor was a Moabite mountain: Num. 23:28, "Balak led Balaam to the top of Peor." On that mountain a sacred house had been built, which was called Beth-peor. In Deut. 34:6 Moses is said to have been buried "in a valley in the land of the Moabites, over against Beth-peor"; that is, the mountain on which the sacred house was built. The idol itself, therefore, was called simply Baal; and so it is called absolutely in Num. 22:41, "Balak brought Balaam to the high places of Baal." Baal, then, from the place where he was worshipped among the Moabites, was called Baal-peor: just as Jupiter was called Capitoline and Olympian, and the Virgin called "of Halle" or "of Loreto." And this account of the surname Suidas gives under Belphegor: "Beel," he says, "is Kronos; and Phegor the place in which he was honored." So too Theodoret on Ps. 106. And he who from the place of his worship was called absolutely Peor, like Capitolinus or Olympius, without the mark of "Baal," is also called Baal without the addition of Peor. Baal-peor, therefore, is Baal worshipped on Mount Peor. Farewell, then, to the learned filth which scholars heap up on this point. Baal alone remains, concerning whom we shall hazard some conjectures in the next chapter.

Chapter argument: The worship of Baal, Judg. 2:11. The progress of his worship under the reign of Ahab. Whether a proclamation abjuring Jehovah was affixed to the gates of Samaria. Baalim [Hebrew: be'alim] in the plural number. Whether Baal was a name common to several idols. Every image of Baal called Baal. "The Baal" with the feminine article [Greek: te Baal]. Baal-zebub. Whence Baal is so called. What sort of proper [ p. 327 ] name the idol had among the idolaters. "Lord of the fly," the reason of the name. Apollo Smintheus, Jupiter Myagros. The temple at Jerusalem in no way infested by flies. Beelzebul the prince of demons. Why he is so called. Zevel, "dung." The Tyrian Beelsamen. Baal-shamayim, "lord of heaven." Baal-berith, "lord of the covenant." Berytus a Phoenician city. All are one Baal.

I. The next place in the Israelite defection is occupied by Baal himself, so called absolutely; an idol most celebrated throughout the whole East. The Holy Spirit first mentions him in Judg. 2:11, "They forsook Jehovah, and served Baal." With the superstition of this idol the people polluted itself at intervals down to the Babylonian captivity. Under the reign of Ahab, who had married a Phoenician wife mad upon that idolatry, it advanced so far that among the ten tribes the stinking idol was openly preferred to the God of Israel. Indeed there scarcely remained anyone, known to the most sharp-sighted Elijah, who was free from that atrocious outrage. For that remnant of seven thousand which God had reserved for himself was invisible to him, lying hidden because of the fury of Jezebel's persecution. The rabbis relate that Ahab affixed to the gates of the city of Samaria a proclamation abjuring Jehovah. But that is Jewish; that is to say, trifling and fabulous: for even Jezebel herself, the keenest champion of the Baalite worship, when she contrived that supreme crime, fatal to the family of Ahab, made away with Naboth using the pretext [Greek: prophasei] of the law against blaspheming the name of Jehovah.

II. In Judg. 2:11 he is called Baalim, in the plural number, and often elsewhere. Hence some think that Baal is not the proper name of any one idol, but an appellative, denoting all the gods of Syria. Indeed the Hebrews assert that all the images of the nations, since they are lords and masters of those who serve them (which is the signification of the name), are called Baalim: "for they are as a lord to their worshippers." On this view the prophets are to be regarded as attacking under the name of Baal not the impious veneration of a single idol, but all superstition and arbitrary worship by which anyone grants to others besides the one true God a dominion over his own conscience.

III. But in Judg. 10:6 the Baalim are recounted by name, and distinctly from the gods of Syria, Sidon, the Moabites, the Ammonites, and the Philistines. Also in the thirteenth verse of that chapter [rather, of chapter 2], where the first mention of this idol is made, the name is pronounced in the singular, designating some particular idol.

IV. More correctly, therefore, they are called Baalim from the multitude of images erected to that idol. For every image of Baal was a Baal. Hence there were Baalim, although he to whom they all had respect was one; and Baalim and Baal are used indiscriminately. Indeed, so far did the Holy Spirit always have regard to the very statues or images in the Baalite worship, that Paul uses the name of Baal in the feminine gender, namely to denote his image [Greek: ten eikona] (Rom. 11:4).

V. The same also was Baal-zebub, the god of Ekron, a Phoenician city on [ p. 328 ] the sea coast near Ashdod. When the Israelites were destitute of all divine oracles, king Ahaziah sent to him to inquire whether he would recover from his disease (2 Kings 1:2). Concerning the reason of the addition "zebub," as of "Peor" before, there are various conjectures of the learned. It denotes a fly. Perhaps the epithet [Greek: epitheton] was added in reproach of the idolaters, that he should be a god who looked after flies; or who could not even drive away flies. Scaliger thinks the idol was called by another name among the Philistines, namely Baal-zevahim, which is "lord of sacrifices"; whence by a slight change it was contemptuously made Baal-zebub. It is commonly supposed to be so named because of the multitude of flies which, attracted by the blood of the sacrifices, flocked into the idol's temple; some, however, think it was because he was supposed to ward off the most destructive and deadly flies which infested the inhabitants of the Ekronite territory on the sea coast. Muenster denies that he could find among the Hebrews the reason of the name. But epithets of this kind are not infrequent among the nations. Thus Apollo among the Phrygians was called Smintheus, that is, "of the mouse." And among others Hercules was called Kornopion [locust-driver], and Jupiter Myagros [fly-catcher]. The Jews agree, nor is the thing improbable, that the temple at Jerusalem was by a special divine kindness kept free from flies, although so great a number of sacrifices was slain there daily. It is therefore not unlikely that the infestation of flies was cast in the teeth of the idols and idolaters.

VI. This name, changed into Beelzebul, we read in the New Testament attributed to "the prince of demons." For since the gods of the nations were demons, who under various pretexts offered themselves to be adored in idols consecrated for another end, the reason is plain why the name of that idol which was everywhere held in chief veneration should be assigned to their prince. But zebub was changed into zevel for the greater disgrace of the idolaters. For zevel is "dung"; by which name we showed above, under gillulim, that the gods of the nations were called. That word therefore means "Baal of dung," "dungy Baal"; for no reason can be given why it should be derived from zebul, "habitation."

VII. Among the Tyrians, from whom all this Baalite worship proceeded, this idol was called Beelsamen, as Sanchuniathon testifies; that is, "lord of heaven." So Augustine, Questions, book 7, question 16: "Baal," he says, "the Carthaginians seem to use for 'lord'; whence Baal-samen is understood as if they said 'lord of heaven.' For samen among them is the name for the heavens." That is, Baal-shamayim, "lord of the heavens," or the heavenly Zeus [Greek: Zeus ouranios]. In all these names, therefore, we have no other idol than the one Baal.

VIII. In Judg. 8:33; 9:4, the deity of the Shechemites is called Baal-berith; that is, "lord of the covenant." The Septuagint translators supposed that the Israelites, deserting God, entered into a covenant with Baal, that he should be a god to them. So they render the words: "They made for themselves a covenant with Baal, that he should be their god." And Jerome: "He had struck a covenant with Baal, that he should be to them for a god." Similarly the Chaldee interpreter has [ p. 329 ] "Baal of the covenant" [Aramaic: ba'al qeyam]. But the fourth verse of the ninth chapter proves that the idol itself was called Baal-berith; and there the Seventy elders retain that word. Others therefore conjecture that this god belonged to the Berytians, the inhabitants of the neighboring Phoenician city called Berytus, from whom the Israelites learned his worship.
IX. But all these gods, as has been said, were only the one Baal, to whose name various epithets were added from various occasions.

Chapter argument: The Baalite worship. Religious worship twofold: moral, and instituted. Among the superstitious, arbitrary worship occupies the place of the latter. Baal worshipped with both. Faith and hope placed in him. The solemn invocation of his name. Swearing by the name of Baal. Genuflection; adoration; kisses. The house of Baal. The occasion of its building. Altars. Images. Groves, natural and artificial. The occasion of making them. Priests; who were the worshippers of Baal. Servants, priests, prophets. The Chemarim; whence so called. Priestly vestments. A high priest. Sacrifices: oil, new wine, grain, incense. Bullocks fattened for that end. Human sacrifice [Greek: anthropothysia]. Who Baal was. Not an appellative name. The Sun. The only god among the Phoenicians. Sacrifices to Baal, to the Sun. The many names [Greek: polyonymia] of the Sun. Whether the Israelites understood the sun by Baal.

I. It remains for us to show briefly how the apostate Israelites venerated this Baal in the holy land; and then who he himself was, for whose sake they declared war upon the God of their fathers. All divine worship is either moral or natural, or else instituted by God himself according to his good pleasure; among apostates, arbitrary worship takes the place of the latter. The Holy Spirit teaches us this in the example of Jeroboam (1 Kings 12:31-33), who in performing sacred rites acted "according as he had devised in his own heart." See 2 Chron. 13:9. Both kinds of worship the deserters snatched from God and bestowed upon Baal.

II. Moral worship is comprised in faith, hope, love, and in all things a religious subjection of the soul. These affections of the soul are expressed in invocations, that is, prayers, vows, and sacred and solemn attestations of the deity as if present, all-seeing, and ruling all things. They also display themselves simply and almost naturally in genuflections, prostrations of the body, adorations, and other similar external tokens of the mind's disposition. With all these the idolaters paid religious honor to Baal.

III. That they placed their faith and hope in him they made manifest by the solemn invocation of his name: 1 Kings 18:26, "They called on the name of Baal [Hebrew: vayyiqre'u be-shem ha-Baal]," saying, namely, "O Baal, hear us." And that they had chosen him for their God Elijah shows by that option which he allowed the people, whether they would follow Jehovah or Baal (v. 21). Next, they swore by his name, whereby they set him up as a God most present, a knower of hearts [Greek: kardiognostes], and supreme governor of all actions: Jer. 12:16, "They taught my people to swear by Baal," [ p. 330 ] or by the name of Baal. To these they added genuflection, or prostration of the body, with kisses: 1 Kings 16:31, "He served Baal, and bowed himself down to him [Hebrew: vayyishtahu lo]." Hence God distinguishes the remnant of those fearing him from the deserters, in that "their knees have not bowed, nor have they given kisses to Baal" (1 Kings 19:18).

IV. In the arbitrary worship which they offered to the idol they seem to have traced out a certain image of the whole worship divinely instituted, and besides added very many things, either devised by themselves or borrowed from the neighboring Baalites. They built him temples or sacred houses: 1 Kings 16:32, "Ahab built a house for Baal in Samaria." Mention of these houses occurs everywhere; they were at last torn down by Jehu, with all their superstitious apparatus and furniture, and turned into latrines (2 Kings 10:27). Now the first who built him a temple was this Ahab. The older idolaters were content with groves and altars, as appears from the history of Gideon's reformation (Judg. 6:28). But after Solomon, at God's command, had prepared a temple for him, the ancient rival and ape of God, seizing the next occasion, took care that a sacred house should be set up for himself also.

V. In that house they dedicated altars to him for the offering of sacrifices: 1 Kings 16:32, "He set up an altar for Baal in that house of Baal." That there were several in the same house is shown by 2 Chron. 23:17; and with the altars, images (2 Kings 3:2; 2 Chron. 23:17). But into what form they fashioned these images is altogether uncertain.

VI. To the apparatus of idolatry belonged also the groves. Of these there were two kinds. Some were natural, of planted trees, which idolaters far and wide through the whole world used in their sacred rites; such was that grove which Gideon cut down (Judg. 6:28), and such as it was the custom to plant on the high places. There were also artificial groves, that is, fashioned images and likenesses of natural ones, which they brought into the sacred houses and used in some manner in religious worship. Such was that grove which king Josiah brought out of the house of God and burned (2 Kings 23:6). What is here called simply ha-asherah, "the grove," is called in chapter 21:7 pesel ha-asherah, the graven thing, or "image of the grove." It seems likely to me that, just as when the temple of Jerusalem was built they carried into it the tabernacle which they had previously used in celebrating divine worship (1 Kings 8:4), so likewise the idolaters, after they had caused a house to be built for Baal, since they could not carry into it the groves themselves in which they had long been accustomed to venerate him, carried in their images, or fashioned and painted representations of them.

VII. For the carrying on of the Baalite worship they procured a great crowd of priests. Eight hundred and fifty are mentioned (1 Kings 18:19). Of these, four hundred and fifty performed the rites in the house of Baal at Samaria; the rest, town by town and village by village, in the groves. These were commonly called ovdei ha-Baal, "those serving Baal," that is, servants or worshippers of Baal (2 Kings 10:21). They were distributed into three classes: [ p. 331 ] for they were either ovdim, properly so called, who handled the servile tasks, ordering themselves at the nod and pleasure of their superiors; or kohanim, "priests," to whom was committed the charge of performing the sacrifices; or nevi'im, "prophets," whose office it was to deceive the people with doctrines, and the princes with predictions. See 2 Kings 10:19.

VIII. All these were called by the peculiar name Chemarim: Zeph. 1:4, "I will cut off the name of the Chemarim [Hebrew: et-shem ha-kemarim]"; that is, the Chemarim themselves, the priests of Baal, of whom he had made mention immediately before. The same word is used in 2 Kings 23:5 and Hos. 10:5 in the same sense. And the Chaldee kumar is rendered by Buxtorf, "a petty sacrificer, a gentile priest, an idolatrous hierophant, a sacrificer and minister of idols." Nor is it anywhere used in the sacred text in another sense; and wherever kohen is attributed to priests of idols, the Chaldee renders it by kumra, as Judg. 17:5. By the Syriac paraphrast, however, the name is two or three times taken in a good sense for priests of God; and in the Epistle to the Hebrews 2:17, Christ himself is called by him rav kumre [great high priest]. Very many think the priests of Baal were so called from the black or dark dress in which they used to be clothed; but kamar also signifies "to grow hot" and "to burn." Why then should we not conclude that they were so called from that most savage office which they discharged in the burning of infants, a derivation which also embraces the other signification of the word, in that in that work they contracted a blackness of face. That in their rites they used a peculiar vestment is intimated by the history of Jehu (2 Kings 10:22).
Over them all one man presided, as it were a high priest. His name was Mattan at the time when Jehoiada was procuring the reformation of divine worship (2 Kings 11:18).

To this idol they offered sacrifices of every kind. That they gave oil, new wine, and grain to Baal, God complains through the prophet (Hos. 2:8). We have shown elsewhere that these were the first oblations of idols. That they made incense-offerings to him Jeremiah testifies several times (ch. 7:9; 11:13, 17); and that they offered bullocks (1 Kings 18:26). That some of these were fattened for several years to that end, that the victims might fall the more splendid, the Hebrews teach on Judg. 6:25, 26. To these they added human sacrifice [Greek: anthropothysia] (Jer. 19:5; 32:35), of which crime we shall say more in the following chapter.

IX. Let us now see in few words who he was whom they venerated with so great an apparatus. Some judge Baal to be an appellative name, denoting any idol whatever to which superstitious worship was paid. But that fancy is most clearly refuted by these words of Elijah, 1 Kings 18:21: "If Jehovah is God, follow him; but if Baal, follow him." For just as Jehovah was the proper name of the God of Israel, so Baal was the proper name of that false god whom the idolaters worshipped. I will say in one word who he was: namely, the Sun. We have shown that the Phoenicians taught them this Baalite worship. Now among them the Sun was the only god of heaven [Greek: monos ouranou theos], and Beelsamen, "lord of heaven." For the Sun was called Baal, or "lord," from that dominion which he was seen to hold over the light, and so over them [ p. 332 ] all. He, first of creatures, was taken for God. Nor is it any objection that in 2 Kings 23:5 they are said to have offered sacrifices "to Baal, to the Sun, and to the Moon," as if Baal were other than the Sun; for since there is no mark of distinction in the Hebrew between Baal and the Sun, the name of the Sun can be placed exegetically [Greek: exegetikos], as if he had said, "to Baal, that is, to the Sun" [Hebrew: la-Baal la-shemesh]; nor does the multiplication of names always indicate a difference of things. For the same idols were adored under various names. The many names of the Sun we have shown above. And since that foolish and stubborn people had taken to itself the idols of all nations to worship, it could not but be that they venerated the same idols under various names and with various rites. I will not affirm, however, that the Israelites always expressly understood the sun by Baal, since they had it in mind only to worship the gods of those nations which they saw enjoying prosperity, with affairs flowing according to their wish. For this reason that impious Ahaz sacrificed to the gods of Damascus, whom he suspected of having brought help to his enemies against him.

Chapter argument: Molech, the abomination of the children of Ammon. Molech, Moloch, Milcom the same. The Septuagint: Archon, "the ruler." The worship and the place appointed for him. The worship: the passing through fire. Its two kinds: burning alive, and lustration. The opinion of Vossius and of Maimonides concerning purgation rejected. The burning alive of infants customary among the Phoenicians. The manner and rite of the unspeakable sacrifice. The custom variously reported. The image of Molech and of Saturn. On lustration between two fires. In the course of time the milder kind of torment prevailed. Idolatrous initiation through various degrees of torment. The origin of this most savage superstition. The place appointed for the worship, the valley of the son of Hinnom. A most pleasant place; whence called the valley of Hinnom. Gehenna [Greek: Geenna; Hebrew: gei Hinnom]. For what reasons that name is used to designate the place of the damned. Whence Tophet is so called. On the words of Job, chapter 17:6. The word denotes the place and punishments of the damned. Molech the same as Baal-peor, that is, the Sun. Saturn the Sun. Adrammelech and Anammelech. The worship of the sun. The dedication and adoration of horses.

I. Next to Baal in the sacred history Molech holds his place. He is called "the abomination of the children of Ammon" (1 Kings 11:7). Him whom the Tyrians called Baal, that is, lord, the Ammonites called Molech, that is, king. Nor does it matter at all whether you call someone a king or a lord. He is also called Moloch (Amos 5:26); and Milcom, the ending and sense of the word slightly altered (1 Kings 11:33); and also Malcham (Zeph. 1:5). The Septuagint at Leviticus 18 renders Archon, "ruler," a word midway between a king and any lord whatever. The first mention of this idol, most notorious for its hideous worship, occurs in Lev. 18:21. Concerning him two things are chiefly celebrated: his worship, and the place appointed to him. The worship was "the making of one's seed to pass through the fire" (Lev. 18:21; Deut. 18:10; 2 Kings 16:3; 23:10). Of this there were two kinds: burning alive, and lustral purification. Vossius contends that only the latter is to be understood in the performance of this idol's rites. Of the same opinion is Rambam [Maimonides], [ p. 333 ] On Idolatry, chapter 6, sections 4 and 5: "Of your seed," he says, "you shall not give to make pass over to Molech. How was this done? A great fire being kindled, they took a part of their seed" (that is, some son or daughter) "and delivered it to the priests who served the fire. But the priests, when they had received the son, restored him to the father, that he himself might, according to his intent, make him pass through the fire. The father therefore, with the permission of the priests, drove the son through the fire, and bade him pass through on foot until he had traversed the whole extent of the flame. For they did not burn him to Molech; but that worship consisted only in the passage." But the plainest testimonies of Scripture cry out against this. The place appointed for the worship of Molech, as we are about to relate, was "the valley of the son of Hinnom." But in it king Ahaz "burnt his sons with fire" (2 Chron. 28:3). That others did the same Jeremiah testifies (ch. 7:31, 32), who accordingly calls that valley "the valley of slaughter." And the psalmist thus interprets the Molechian burning, Ps. 106:37, 38: "They sacrificed their sons and their daughters to demons, and shed innocent blood, the blood of their sons and of their daughters, whom they sacrificed." This plainly indicates burning alive. Hence the same word which signifies "to make pass through" signifies also "to immolate." And of this opinion among the Jews are also Moses Gerundensis [Nahmanides] and Isaac Abarbanel.

II. That the Israelites received this Molechian superstition from the Phoenicians no one doubts. We have shown elsewhere that they sacrificed their sons to Saturn. So Ennius of the Carthaginians: "The Phoenicians were wont to sacrifice their little boys." Which little verse of Ennius Silius Italicus expands thus: "It was the custom among the peoples whom the stranger Dido founded to seek the gods' favor by slaughter, and on blazing altars, unspeakable to tell, to lay their little sons" (Silius Italicus, Punica 4.765). And Sophocles in the Andromeda: "For it is a custom among the barbarians from of old to sacrifice the race of mortals to Kronos." And Plato in the Minos: "With us it is not lawful to sacrifice men, but unholy; the Carthaginians, however, sacrifice them as a thing holy and lawful for them, and some of them even offer their own sons to Kronos, as perhaps you too have heard." Likewise there is scarcely one of the ancients who does not record the Phoenician human sacrifice. By their example the Israelites gave themselves to that crime.

III. The rite and manner of this unspeakable sacrifice the rabbis relate. "Molech," they say, "was a hollow image having seven chambers. One they opened for an offering of fine flour; another for turtledoves; a third for a sheep; a fourth for a ram; a fifth for a calf; a sixth for an ox. But for him who wished to offer his son, the seventh chamber was opened. And the face of this idol was like the face of a calf, its hands spread out ready to receive from the bystanders; and meanwhile they danced, while the boy was burned in the idol by the kindled fire, beating drums lest the wailing of the boy should be heard." From them also Lyra: "Moloch," he says, "as the Hebrews say, was an idol [ p. 334 ] of copper made in the form of a man, hollow within. And in that hollow a fire was made until the idol was as it were red-hot, and then the boy was placed between the hands of the idol. And the priest, beating drums, made so great a noise that the voice of the dying boy could not be heard, lest the parents should be moved with compassion, but rather believe that the soul was received by the gods in peace and without pain." Others relate it otherwise: "The Chemarim," they say, "kindled a great fire, and one of them received the portion of seed from the father and delivered it to the other Chemarim; and they in turn gave the son back to his father after he had been delivered to them, that he might make him pass through the fire by their authority. And the father of the boy was himself the one who made his son or his daughter pass through the kindled fire by the authority of the Chemarim, and made him cross from one side to the other through the midst of the flame, until the boy was burned up." That delivering of the boy to the Chemarim they think is meant by those words of the precept, "You shall not give," or, "You shall not hand over, of your seed"; and the "making to pass" they expound by that crossing; yet they do not deny that he was at last killed and burned. Diodorus relates that at Carthage there was a bronze statue dedicated to Saturn, beneath which was a fire for burning men alive; and that a similar custom obtained among the Druids we have taught above.

IV. What is likewise said of lustrations or purification by fires may also have place here. For perhaps in the course of time the idolaters descended to that milder kind of torments, just as elsewhere, grown weary of horrid slaughters, they offered effigies of men instead of living men. A purification [Greek: katharismos] of this sort they also set before idolatrous initiation. For no one could be duly consecrated in the rites of certain idols unless he passed through many degrees of tortures; one of these was the passage through fire, by which the candidates for initiation were burned and wounded not lightly. So Gregory Nazianzen, First Oration against Julian: "No one could be initiated into the rites of Mithras unless, having passed through certain degrees of torments, he showed himself holy and impassible." And Nonnus: "First they are subjected to lighter torments, then to more atrocious ones; and so, after running through all the torments, they are admitted to his rites; for they are tortured with fire and water and kinds of torments of this sort." Of these same rites also Virgil (Aeneid 11.785): "Apollo, guardian of holy Soracte, whom we worship before all, for whom the blaze of pine is fed in a heap, while we his worshippers, in confidence of our piety, press our footsteps through the midst of the fire upon many a glowing coal" (Virgil, Aeneid 11.785). On these things Pliny should be consulted, book 7, chapter 2. And it is not improbable that the Israelites imitated these things also.

V. The causes of so great a crime the prophet sets forth, Mic. 6:6, 7: "With what shall I come before the Lord, and bow myself before the high God? Shall I come [ p. 335 ] before him with burnt offerings, with calves a year old? Will the Lord accept thousands of rams, myriads of torrents of oil? Shall I give my firstborn for my transgression, the fruit of my womb for the sin of my soul?" The deadly gnawings of conscience drove the wretched hypocrites, ignorant of the true sacrifice which alone takes away the consciousness of sin, into that madness.

VI. The place appointed for the worship of this idol was the valley of the son of Hinnom. "It was a place," says Adrichomius, "in the suburbs of Jerusalem (toward the east, beneath the mount of ascension near the Fuller's Pool), most pleasant like Tempe, watered by the springs of Siloam and the flow of the brook Kidron; planted with pleasure-grounds and gardens, all wooded and full of delights." So it is; for whereas all the other idols had their groves and shrines in the city itself, the houses dedicated to Moloch were built outside the walls of the city. It was called by that name before the Israelites entered the land of Canaan; for in the first division of the land it is expressly named "the valley of the son of Hinnom," and "the valley of Hinnom" (Josh. 15:8); nor is the origin of the word to be sought elsewhere. For there are those who derive it from a root meaning "he roared, he wailed," as if it were so called from the roaring which the sons of the idolaters, immolated there, sent forth; but, as we have said, the place was so called from its possessor long before it was defiled with that crime. From gei Hinnom, "the valley of Hinnom," was formed in one word Geenna [Gehenna]; we also have it in a single word as Gehinnom (Josh. 18:16). For many causes the place of the damned afterwards began to be so called; Jerome denies that this was done by the ancients. But evidently the Chaldee paraphrast was unknown to Jerome, who very frequently uses that word for the place of the damned, as at Isa. 33:14 and elsewhere. It is certain that our Lord used the word Gehenna in that sense (Matt. 5:29, 30; 10:28). For since that place was rendered horrible and abominable, first by the burning alive of wretched infants, then by the utter destruction [Greek: panolethria] of the Assyrian army under Sennacherib, and by its extreme pollution through Josiah's casting in of dead men's bones, heaped up with so many crimes and horrid punishments, it seemed fit to recall to the memory of the wicked those horrible and everlasting penalties which, at the appointed time, God will exact of them all. By carrying in corpses Josiah so polluted the whole valley (2 Kings 23:10) that it does not appear ever afterwards to have been set apart for superstitious use.

VII. In that valley there was a peculiar place, Tophet; perhaps the word denotes all that space which lay within the enclosure of the rites: 2 Kings 23:10, "He defiled Tophet in the valley of the children of Hinnom." And Jeremiah 7:31, "the high places of Tophet in the valley of the son of Hinnom." Most hold that Tophet is so called from toph, "a drum"; namely, the drum was beaten while the boy was alive in the fiery chamber of the idol, lest the wailings he uttered amid the flames should stir the impious bowels of his parents. Forerius, on Isa. 30:33, says that tophet signifies a prodigy, and that the place was so called because the spectacle was horrible. And in [ p. 336 ] confirmation of his opinion he adduces the words of Job, chapter 17:6, "He has made me a byword of the peoples, and aforetime I was as a tophet [Hebrew: ve-tophet le-phanim ehyeh]." These words the interpreters indeed render variously. Arias Montanus: "And I shall be beforehand the fire of Gehenna." Junius: "I shall be as before matter for drum-beaters." The Vulgate: "I am an example before them." The Septuagint: "I became a laughingstock to them." The Chaldee: "And I shall be from the interior of Gehenna." Our translators: "And aforetime I was as a tabret"; or rather, "afore them," as they note in the margin. Without doubt the holy man complains that he has been made a reproach, a mockery, and a byword to worthless men, upon whom, that is, they played as upon a drum, or whom they beat like a drum. But since that word tophteh in the prophet Isaiah, chapter 30:33, is taken for hell, or is employed to figure eternal punishments, interpreters have supposed it to hold the same signification in this place. For the rest, nothing hinders it from denoting a drum here, rather than a prodigy or portent, as the learned man would have it. But that the place and punishments of the damned are described in Isaiah can be doubted by no one who compares somewhat attentively that fire, of old prepared for the king, with that which our Lord teaches was prepared for the devil and his angels.

VIII. Who it was whom they venerated with this nefarious worship is not so certain a matter. That he was the same as Baal the sameness of the worship seems to prove; for they sacrificed their sons to Baal also, and that in the valley of Hinnom (Jer. 7:31). Further, Amos affirms that the people worshipped Moloch in the wilderness (ch. 5:26); but in the history of those times no idol besides Baal-peor is recorded as worshipped by them there. Moloch therefore is the same as Baal-peor and Baal, that is, the Sun. Some think the sacrifices point to Saturn; but that the Tyrian Saturn himself was the Sun, the distinguished Vossius shows at length.

IX. Nor were Adrammelech and Anammelech, to whom the inhabitants of Sepharvaim burned their sons with fire (2 Kings 17:31), other than this Moloch. Adrammelech is addir melekh, "the magnificent king"; and Anammelech anath melekh, "the answer of the king," from whom, namely, they sought oracular responses.

X. In these, then, however covertly, they venerated the Sun. But at length, every mask laid aside, they pursued him openly and professedly with divine honors. That the kings of Judah set up horses and chariots to the Sun the sacred history relates (2 Kings 23:11). Throughout the whole East horses were sacred to the Sun. Xenophon teaches this in the Cyropaedia, book 8, and the Anabasis, book 4; Pausanias also in his book on Laconia. That four-horse chariots were added to them Heliodorus shows in the tenth book of the Aethiopica. These horses seem to have been like post-horses, used by mounted couriers who daily saluted the sun at the king's command; or perhaps even the king himself and the princes rode upon them when about to adore the sun. Whether the chariots were real, or images of chariots, is uncertain. But it is certain that, beyond this dedication of horses, they adored the sun directly and immediately: Ezek. 8:16, [ p. 337 ] "They were worshipping the sun toward the east." That this solar worship was most ancient we have taught elsewhere.

Chapter argument: Ashtaroth. The goddess of the Sidonians. Called by the Hebrews a god. Worshipped by the Philistines. A town of that name. Images of sheep. The groves of Ashtaroth. Asheroth and Ashtoreth. Astarte. Atergatis. Derceto. Of Queen Gatis, and "without Gatis" [Greek: ater Gatidos]. Abstinence from fish; the rise of the superstition. What the image of Astarte was like. The queen of heaven. The Moon. The heavenly goddess, Urania. The Sun lord of heaven; the Moon queen. Gad and Meni (Isa. 65:11). Who Gad was. The opinion of Jerome. The host of heaven. The gods of Damascus. The horror-idol of Maachah. Other matters.

I. It remains for us to treat of Ashtaroth, and of a few other unnamed gods who are left. Ashtaroth is called elohei Tsidonim, "the god of the Sidonians" (1 Kings 11:5, 33), god being put for goddess, since the Hebrews have no word which expresses deity in the feminine gender. She is first mentioned in Judg. 2:13, and again in chapter 10:6; 1 Sam. 7:3. That this idol was adored not only by the Sidonians but also by the Philistines, and indeed by all the dwellers on the sea coast, is shown by 1 Sam. 31:10.

II. There was also a town of this name (Deut. 1:4; Josh. 9:10), which no one can doubt took its surname from the worship of this idol; conversely, names were very often imposed on idols from the places in which superstitious worship was paid to them.

III. The Jews commonly hold that the Ashtaroth were images of idols formed in the likeness of sheep; for they say that ashtoreth denotes "a ewe lamb." Others would have it that the idol obtained its name from the multitude of sacrifices of sheep and cattle (1 Sam. 7:4). The Septuagint renders ashtarot by "the groves of Astaroth" [Greek: alse Astaroth], joining with it another word of almost the same sound; for asherot signified "groves." Hence some think this idol was adored in groves.

IV. This idol was also called by other names: Astarte, the most renowned Syrian goddess, and Atergatis, and Derceto; all of which, it is evident, flowed from that other name. For the things which Athenaeus produces from Antipater the Stoic about this goddess (Deipnosophists, book 8), and why she was called Atergatis, are vain. He feigns a certain queen Gatis, who published an edict that no one should eat fish "without Gatis" [Greek: ater Gatidos]. Hence, he says, among the ignorant common people came forth the name Atergatis: absurdly, and after the manner of the Greeks in investigating the origins of the names of the gods; for everyone sees that Atergatis is a word corrupted from ashtoreth.

V. From that source indeed came a most absurd conjecture, because part of her worship was abstinence from fish; the cause of which among the Syrians Diogenes of Erythrae, in Hyginus's Poetic Astronomy, assigns no less absurdly. "He writes," says Hyginus, "that at a certain time Venus with [ p. 338 ] her son Cupid came into Syria to the river Euphrates, and that in that place the giant Typhon suddenly appeared; that Venus with her son threw herself into the river, and there changed her shape into the form of fishes, by which deed they were freed from danger. And that therefore afterwards the Syrians, who are nearest to these places, ceased to eat fish, fearing to catch them, lest by a like cause they should seem either to assail the protections of the gods, or to entrap the gods themselves." Nothing is more foolish than these tales. Very many say that the image of the idol was fashioned from the thighs down to the ends of the feet in the figure of a fish, while the remaining part of the body was a woman. The origin of the figure is uncertain; but that it gave occasion to the religious veneration of fish is almost certain. Nicetas, on Gregory, says it was a statue of Venus. For my part I have no doubt that she was the same as the "queen of heaven" so often mentioned by Jeremiah. She was the moon, who to others was both Venus and Diana, and Juno Lucina; called ashtarot on account of her many names [Greek: polyonymia] and the multitude of her sacrifices. Lucian affirms that he considers the Syrian goddess, that is, Astarte, to be the moon. Jerome, on Jeremiah chapter 7, agrees that the moon, ruling among the stars, is denoted by that name. Thus the poet describes her: "The Julian star shines among them all, as the moon among the lesser fires" (Horace, Odes 1.12.46). And again: "It was night, and the moon was shining in a clear sky among the lesser stars" (Horace, Epodes 15.1). That is, the queen of heaven. Tertullian, Apology, chapter 24, joins with the Astarte of Syria the Caelestis of Africa. By Caelestis, or Urania, Venus was commonly understood, as is evident from Lucian. But that Venus was nothing but the moon, as has been shown elsewhere. There are some, however, who think the sun was the queen of heaven. For examples prove that shemesh [the sun] is sometimes of the feminine gender; whereas yareah, the word that denotes the moon, as the other does the sun, never is. Whence among the Chaldeans the deity was called the god Lunus [the masculine moon]. But I can scarcely believe that this grammatical consideration prevailed; rather, as they called the sun Baal-shamayim, "lord of heaven," and also king, so by the name "queen of heaven" they meant to denote the moon.

VI. Philastrius, Heresies 15, affirms that the Jews worshipped Fortune, who in Africa is styled the queen of heaven. He has respect, without doubt, to that passage of Isaiah, chapter 65:11, "who set in order a table for Gad, and who fill a mixed drink for Meni" [Hebrew: ha-orkhim la-Gad shulhan ve-ha-memal'im la-Meni mimsakh]. These words the interpreters render variously. The Septuagint renders Gad as "demon" [Greek: daimonion], and Meni as "fortune" [Greek: tyche]: "preparing a table for the demon, and filling a mixed cup for fortune." And in the Latin version of the Greek text which is extant in Jerome, "fortune" answers to Gad, and "demon" to Meni. The Vulgate renders not Meni but Gad as fortune, and takes Meni only as a particle: "You who set a table for fortune, and pour libations upon it." And so also Symmachus, as Jerome testifies, renders it "without me." Junius gives the force of the words, for he translates thus: "You who furnish a table for that troop, and who fill a libation for those numbers." For gad signifies a troop; and meni seems to be derived from manah, [ p. 339 ] to number. Our translators follow him: "That prepare a table for that troop, and that furnish the drink-offering unto that number"; but they relegate Gad and Meni to the margin. Arias Montanus: "Preparing a table for Jupiter, and filling a libation for Meni." Amid such variety it is not easy to say which pleases most. That the queen of heaven is not understood here is certain; learned men heap up on this passage many things about the star of Jupiter, about Mercury, about the constellation of the stars, about good fortune, about the horoscope, the ascending planet propitious and happily placed, about the idols of the Edomites, and other things, all uncertain. And though they nowhere give full satisfaction, I confess that what the most learned Jerome once noted on the passage answers best to my mind. "There is," he says, "in all cities, and especially in Egypt and in Alexandria, an old custom of idolatry, that on the last day of the year, and of the month which is the last, they set out a table filled with dishes of various kinds, and a cup mixed with honey-wine, auguring the fertility either of the past year or of the coming one. This also the Israelites did, venerating the portents of all the images."

VII. With the sun and the moon they adored also the whole host of heaven, that is, all the stars. Jerome, on Isaiah 15, writes that Chemosh (1 Kings 11:33) was the same as Baal-peor. More truly he was the same as Molech, as may be seen from Judg. 11:24.

VIII. There remain the unnamed gods. Among them occur the gods of Damascus, to whom king Ahaz sacrificed (2 Chron. 28:23), and built an altar in the very house of God (2 Kings 16:11-13). Maachah, the grandmother of king Asa, who is called his mother, set up a peculiar idol to be worshipped in a grove (2 Chron. 15:16): "she made a horror [Hebrew: miphletset] for the grove [Hebrew: la-asherah]." The Vulgate, "an image of Priapus"; others, "a horrible statue"; the Septuagint, "Astarte." The word signifies "a thing of terror," or terror itself. Hence Castalio renders it "Pan," who in groves was wont to strike terror into men. I rather think the Holy Spirit intimates an empty bugbear.

IX. What is said of Ob and Yiddeoni and the other kinds of ventriloquism [Greek: engastrimythia], and of chiromancy, augury, geomancy, sortilege, Pythonesses, and conjurers, does not pertain to our design. Let it suffice to have touched briefly upon the chief, if not all, of those idols which the apostates, by nefarious crime, forsaking the true God, superstitiously worshipped.

Chapter argument: The Aaronic calf. The occasion of its making. The people demanded not the supreme God but an intermediary. They did not believe the calf to be God. So called metonymically. Whether it was made after the likeness of the Egyptian Apis. The golden images of the lost Apis. The figure of a male calf. Apis a female ox. Why the calves of Jeroboam are called heifers. No other God besides Jehovah worshipped in the calf. The superstition hateful to God. The lies of the gentiles about the adoration of an ass's head [ p. 340 ] at Jerusalem. The occasion and coloring of the fiction. A passage of Tacitus noted. Moses ground the calf to powder and scattered it on the water. Whether in likeness of the drowning of Apis. Aaron foolishly cleared by the Jews; falsely by Moncaeus. The true crime of Aaron.

I. There follow those things in which they offended in the mode and means of religious worship, substituting arbitrary worship in the place of the instituted. And here first occurs the Aaronic calf (Exod. 32:1-5). While Moses was absent for some days on the mountain, the carnal people, greedy for the visible sign of the divine presence promised to them, and not yet instructed in the whole worship for the common assembly, drove Aaron to cast a golden calf for those ends. "Up," they say, "make us gods to go before us; for as for Moses, the man who brought us up out of the land of Egypt, we know not what has become of him." They asked, that is, for gods to fill the place not of Jehovah but of Moses, through whom they might consult God. For it was scarcely possible that they should believe that what they were today committing to Aaron to fashion would by tomorrow turn into a true deity. Moreover, to attribute the name of God to images metonymically was customary even with those who in no way believed the images themselves to be gods. They set about, therefore, to fashion and fabricate for themselves a visible sign of the divine presence.

II. Some think that Aaron fashioned this calf in the likeness of the Egyptian Apis; or rather of the golden image which the priests were accustomed to consecrate in memory of a lost or deceased Apis. He may also have had in mind the animal itself, always accounted most excellent on account of the labors from which we receive the fruits of the earth. Indeed Scripture seems to intimate that this calf was the image of a male calf; for in Ps. 106:20 there is joined to the noun by which it is signified a participle of the masculine gender: "an ox that eats [Hebrew: shor okhel] grass." But Herodotus, in the Euterpe, teaches that the Egyptians consecrated female oxen to Isis and sacrificed the males. Nor is it an objection that the prophet Hosea calls the calves of Jeroboam, fashioned after the pattern of this calf of the wilderness, eglot (ch. 10:5), a word which, being of the feminine gender, the Septuagint renders by damaleis, that is, heifers; since the prophet so calls them in contempt, as Jerome most rightly notes on the passage. Scripture also intimates that the image was large, when it teaches that the Israelites "changed their glory into the form of an ox that eats grass" (Ps. 106:20). And it is something that Stephen affirms that by the erection of this calf "they turned back in their hearts into Egypt" (Acts 7:39), where, namely, they had seen images of that kind.

III. That the people had no mind to worship in the calf any other God besides Jehovah is plain from the history of the affair. We showed above that they demanded not a supreme God in the place of Jehovah, but an intermediary in the place of Moses; and when the calf was seen they said: "These are your gods, O Israel, who brought you up out of the land of Egypt. Which when Aaron saw, he built an altar before it; and made proclamation, saying, Tomorrow is a feast to Jehovah." "Your gods," that is, your God; for the calf was a single one, and consecrated to one. So Neh. 9:18, "When they had made for themselves a molten calf, they said, [ p. 341 ] This is your God who brought you up out of Egypt"; that is, the image of your God, or the visible sign of his presence in the midst of you. Senseless indeed and rebellious the people may have been; but that they were so far out of their wits as to believe that the brand-new calf, which they had made the day before yesterday in the desert, had brought them up out of Egypt, no one can suppose, unless he holds not only that they cast one calf, but that they were all turned into calves themselves. Then, the people being summoned to consecrate the calf, Aaron proclaimed "a feast to Jehovah." "Tomorrow," he says, "let there be a feast to Jehovah"; that is, dancing before this image we will celebrate Jehovah. To him the feast was appointed; not to the calf, not to Apis, not to Osiris or Isis. It is certain, therefore, that the Israelites of the wilderness resolved to worship no other God besides Jehovah. This argument was most learnedly pursued of old, in his books on idolatry and his lectures on the book of Revelation, by the ornament of his age, John Rainolds.

IV. With how great a crime the people bound itself by the devising of this arbitrary worship, and how hateful to God that superstition was, appeared most speedily from the horrible outcome. This is not the place to relate these things at large; but from those words, Exod. 32:34, "In the day when I visit, I will surely visit this sin upon them," it passed into a proverb among the Jews, "No punishment is inflicted upon Israel in which there is not an ounce of the calf."

V. By occasion of this crime, also, the whole people was bespattered among the other nations with a most shameful and enduring stain of infamy. For from no other source than this calf-idolatry did some seize the handle for coining that most celebrated fiction about the golden head of an ass adored in the temple at Jerusalem. For however putrid the lie was, yet this folly, or rather this madness that surpassed all folly, does not allow us to suppose that the fable-mongers were destitute of all coloring. The words of Tacitus, Histories, book 5, chapter 4, are: "They consecrated in the inner shrine an effigy of the animal by whose guidance they had dispelled their wandering and their thirst." He had said before that the animal was an ass. And the eminent man seems by his concise and compressed manner of speaking to have achieved this here, that no man since men were born ever uttered more lies in fewer words. Yet it cannot be denied that a certain shadow of true history, and of the actual event, may be discerned, though most obscurely, through the windings of these lies. The sacred history teaches that the people was more than once pressed and burdened by thirst, and by losing the way in a most vast wilderness. We know that they importunately demanded a guide for the way, and water; that they fell in with a herd of asses going to water, and used them as guides, is an old wives' fable. But that they cast that golden calf with this intent, that in that sign of God's visible presence they might use it as an oracle in their straits, and proceed on their journey under its auspices, is plain from the narrative of the event. Even long afterwards, settled in their Canaanite abodes, led by no other example than that of the wilderness, they consecrated the same effigy in certain shrines, namely at Dan and Bethel; [ p. 342 ] and by a marvelous confusion of everything, the gentiles transformed the golden calf into a living ass, the miracle of bringing water out of the rock into a spring or brook found by the help of asses, and Dan and Bethel into Jerusalem. And lest he should offer any handle for continuing these lies of the Gentiles, Josephus, otherwise a most diligent narrator of the antiquities and exploits of his nation, buried this history of the golden Aaronic calf in deep silence. But the Targum of Uzziel says of this crime: "Their evil report went out among the peoples of the earth, and they acquired for themselves an evil name throughout their generations."

VI. This calf, ground into powder, "Moses scattered upon the face of the waters, and made the people drink" (Exod. 32:20). From this deed of Moses, Selden and Grotius conjecture that he alluded to the drowning of the Egyptian Apis; for at the appointed time they solemnly drowned Apis in the Nile. But that drowning of Apis was a part of the idolatrous worship, and by far the most superstitious part of it. That Moses imitated it, especially in that act by which he inflicted punishment with the utmost ignominy upon the idolaters, is both in itself incredible and plainly contrary to the sacred history.

VII. The masters of the Jews, in order to clear Aaron of the crime, feign from those words of his (Exod. 32:24), "I cast the gold into the fire, and there came out this calf," that he threw the mass of gold into the fire with the intention of despoiling the Israelites of those ornaments which they were foolishly about to lavish on idolatry; but that the calf came forth by the work of magicians, some of whom belonged to that Egyptian rabble which went up with the people into the wilderness. Ben Uzziel says that Satan, slipping into the midst of the gold, produced that form; things which assuredly ill agree with what the sacred history records of Aaron's deed (v. 4), namely that "having received the gold from the hand of the people, he shaped it with a graving tool, and made of it a molten calf." Franciscus Moncaeus also endeavors to remove from him all suspicion of crime, in that book to which he gave the name "Aaron Purged." But in those dissertations, to say nothing harsher, got up for the display of his wit, he boldly and rashly invents many things which I marvel could ever have come into the mind of any mortal with a sound head. He feigns that Aaron was at that time high priest; he feigns that he had seen the likeness of a calf on the mountain, although God expressly said that no one saw any likeness; he feigns that he formed the calf after the pattern of the cherubim which Moses afterwards erected at God's command; he forgets that God severely forbade them to form any image; he contradicts the Holy Spirit to his face in very many things, and brings forward much else which it is wearisome to relate. But although Aaron did not propose to the people any other god to be worshipped in the calf besides Jehovah, yet in this he manifestly bound both himself and the people with crime, that without God's command he devised a way and mode of arbitrary worship in a visible sign.

Chapter argument: The calves of Jeroboam. The occasion of their making. The impelling reasons; their use. The teraphim, what kind of images, and how made. Necromancy [Greek: nekyomanteia]. The teraphim of Micah. The ephod of Gideon. The brazen serpent. The captivity of the ten tribes.

I. After the pattern of the Aaronic calf, a long time afterwards, two other golden calves were erected by Jeroboam. For to this source I refer the origin and occasion of the Jeroboamic superstition. The accounts which some give concerning Joseph are too forced. They say that a calf was of old dedicated in Egypt to his memory; which is utterly uncertain. Then, that Jeroboam the Ephraimite, of the stock of Joseph, in order to keep the people in veneration of his family, chose this figure to dedicate in his rites. But these things, as we have said, seem too harsh and forced. I am better pleased to hold that the memory of the Aaronic calf furnished the occasion for the making of these others. For in how great veneration the ancient deeds of their fathers, whether rightly done or otherwise, were held among that people, is attested by what is recorded of the brazen serpent, likewise of the wilderness, and of Gideon's ephod, and other things. The history of these calves the Holy Spirit relates, 1 Kings 12:28-32. The reasons for Jeroboam's taking this counsel are set forth more at large, 2 Chron. 13:6-9.
