The whole tribe of Levi, and very many of the pious Israelites, adhering to God's institutions, left their ancestral seats and withdrew into the territories of the tribes of Judah and Benjamin; for without doubt it was not open to them to go into the Judean dominion and return as often as they wished. For after the manner of those who hold power, especially where the newness of a kingdom and the lust of reigning meet, men who twist every pretext of religion to a bad sense, this Jeroboam judged that those who went to Jerusalem for the sake of worshipping God might there safely enter into a plan of revolting from his yoke; and accordingly he took from them all that liberty. Moreover, three times every year all the males were bound by the law to present themselves before the Lord at the temple; and if they were obedient to that precept, it must needs be that just so often the king would be left destitute of all his subjects, all of whom would be in the power of his most hostile enemy. He concluded that no kingdom in which that custom prevailed could last. What then should he do? Should he openly revolt from Jehovah and his worship, and drag the people into the same crime? It is indeed probable that the nefarious man, who postponed all things divine and human to his ambition, would have attempted it, had there been hope of driving the people, over whom he had obtained a recent and precarious rule, to that pitch of impiety. He therefore calls to mind that Aaronic calf, and persuades the people that Jehovah could be worshipped in the manner which Aaron had marked out: 1 Kings 12:28, "The king, having taken counsel, made two calves of gold, and said to the Israelites, It is enough for you; go not up to Jerusalem: behold your gods, O Israel, who brought you up out of the land of Egypt." He uses the same words which Aaron used in the dedication of his calf. Now it is plain that Jeroboam and [ p. 344 ] the people worshipped Jehovah in those calves, which they set in opposition not to God himself but to the worship at Jerusalem. And so, though the Baalite, that is, the solar worship was afterwards abolished by Jehu, yet to this worship, of adoring God in the calves, the people of the ten tribes pertinaciously adhered down to their last and everlasting captivity.

II. Of this kind were the teraphim. They are first mentioned among the Jews in Judg. 17:5. We said something of them in the exposition of the Abrahamic theology, on Gen. 31:19. What they were is not yet settled. "Images made under a certain position of the stars, with figures," says Grotius. But that the little images were made in human shape is indicated by the history of Michal, the daughter of Saul, who, to deceive the messengers of her father sent to kill David, placed teraphim in his bed; deceived by their appearance and figure, they supposed they had seen David himself lying sick (1 Sam. 19:13). Rabbi David Kimchi, in his Book of Roots, writes that the images were made in the appearance of a human face. The manner of making them Elias relates in his Tishbi: "They slew," he says, "a firstborn man, whose head they wrenched and cut off. The severed head they seasoned with salt; and they wrote upon a plate of gold the name of an unclean spirit, and so, lighting candles before it, they adored it." Divination by the dead was familiar to the ancients. That he might consult the gods by his entrails, the Emperor Hadrian killed his catamite Antinous, and then enrolled him among the gods. The custom of lighting candles before images still endures among some. That the teraphim were also in use for eliciting prophecies the sacred history teaches: Zech. 10:2, "The teraphim have spoken vanity"; Ezek. 21:21, "He consulted the teraphim." But that they were consecrated among the Jews in the manner Elias describes is a Jewish figment; for in using them they had no intention of worshipping any god besides Jehovah. They were merely a kind of small images, easy to carry, after the fashion of household lares and penates. Accordingly the mother of Micah affirms that she had "consecrated to Jehovah" the silver from which those idols of Micah were cast, "to make teraphim" (Judg. 17:3). It also greatly delighted Micah's mind that he had obtained a Levite as priest (v. 10); whereas it would have been all one to him from what family his priest sprang, had he not resolved to venerate Jehovah (see v. 13). An ephod accompanied those teraphim; that is, "a linen priestly garment." The author of the book of Judges relates that they lasted until the captivity (Judg. 18:30). Hosea foretold that after the captivity the Israelites would live without them (Hos. 3:4); for I do not agree with those who think that mention is there made by the prophet of the true and the false worship side by side. The ephod, they say, for the Lord; the teraphim for idols. But both were the furniture of superstition; for the use of the ephod was no more lawful to the Israelites than that of the teraphim.

III. To this head belongs also the ephod of Gideon (Judg. 8:27). Of the form of the garment there is no need to speak, since it is described at length in the book of Exodus. It was a gilded garment: first a memorial [Greek: mnemosynon] of victory, afterwards changed to a superstitious use. Augustine thinks "ephod" is taken synecdochically [Greek: synekdochikos], [ p. 345 ] and that Gideon made all the pontifical vestments, in order to draw the worship of God to his own city of Ophrah. With a good zeal perhaps, but with the worst outcome. For he instituted these things without God's command; yet the Gideonites did not utterly desert God himself.

IV. The facts concerning the brazen serpent of the wilderness are known to all. First erected for an evangelical end, then transferred to a superstitious use, the pious king Hezekiah broke it and cast it away with the utmost contempt.

V. And these were, if not all, at least the principal idols, and the means and instruments of false worship, with which that people, utterly abandoned to idolatry, initiated in this apostasy, polluted and contaminated itself, rejecting the first foundation of the Mosaic theology. The church of the ten tribes, wallowing in the foul sink of these idolatries and incurable (2 Chron. 36:16), God caused to be carried away into everlasting captivity, without hope or promise of return. Concerning the Judean church a few things still remain to be said.
Chapter argument: The progress and outcome of the defection of the Jewish church.

I. We have shown at length that the apostates repudiated the first foundation of the Mosaic theology by the introduction of arbitrary and idolatrous worship; it remains for me to show briefly that they also spurned those other principles of the same truth which, as has likewise been shown, occupied an equal place with that first one in that theology. That theology taught also free justification, and eternal salvation to be obtained by the merits and mediation of the Messiah. In the course of time there grew strong a most pernicious error concerning righteousness through the works of the law and the observance of ceremonial rites. Puffed up with this opinion, the stubborn people, full of itself, carried itself loftily in all things and everywhere, as if it alone were worthy of God's care. At last, moral obedience also being despised and they themselves poured out into vices, they placed all their hope in sacrifices alone and external rites. These most pernicious errors God met by the ministry of the prophets. But not even so were they beaten down from the depraved conceit of their own righteousness, with whose leaven those most often swell who are furthest removed from all righteousness; and this at length formed a great part of this initial defection.

II. The promise of the Messiah, who together with righteousness and eternal peace would institute new rites of divine worship, those Mosaic ones being abolished which had been imposed by direction for an appointed time, constituted the third foundation of the Jewish theology. Beginning to dream of an absolute eternity of the ceremonies, they cast off all care of this principle also.

III. This was that partial defection of the Jewish church which it pleased God, using the utmost gentleness and mercy, to correct by the captivity of seventy years. For after the people would not suffer itself to be torn away from those wicked manners and errors by the prophetic ministry, and was moreover heaped up with every injustice, lust, rapine, defilement, murder, and oppression, the church was reduced to such a condition that, partly on account of its national constitution, partly because by far the greatest part of the people had consented to the defection, a reformation of the church by the separation of the good became altogether impossible. It therefore pleased God to bring in a universal deportation [Greek: metoikesia], so that the people, returning to their native land, might begin anew, as it were from the very foundations, all things that pertain to the observance of the Mosaic theology.

IV. Such, then, was the horrid end which that partial apostasy of the Jewish church found: the city itself, and the temple, the sole seat of all solemn worship, being laid waste with sword and fire and levelled to the ground, the remnant of the people was driven away into captivity, a total interruption being made of the celebration of worship.

V. But God had not yet exhausted all his love toward that people, nor had he yet cast away all care and hope of that church; he willed that it should undergo another trial, such as the rabbis dream of concerning individual souls.

Chapter argument: The necessity of the Babylonian captivity for the reformation of the church; the heads of the Ezraitic reformation; the great synagogue restored; its origin and work; the feast of tabernacles restored, pleasing to God; the single rule of ecclesiastical reformation; the repudiation of polygamy [Greek: polygamia]; the accuracy of the biblical text; the origin of the Hebrew points.

I. That God did not cast off all care of the church when it was carried away into captivity was both evident from the event itself, and he had himself given the godly assurance of it by his promises. Nay rather, to that most merciful Father that brief deportation [Greek: metoikēsia] was punishment enough for the greatest of crimes. Far different was the condition of that people while the captivity lasted from the condition of the same people now, cut off from God in the most absolute separation. For at that time they were destitute neither of the written word, nor of the Holy Spirit, nor of the prophetic ministry, nor of a most certain hope of return. But, as we have said, it was impossible for that church to be recovered to any good fruit without a total interruption of its solemn observances. For since the temple was holy, the city holy, and the whole land holy; since the priests all sprang from one fixed family, and the sacred administrations were bound to a single place; and since the greatest part of the priests and of the people was corrupt and clung obstinately to its apostate defection, a general removal from the seats of the church had of necessity to be set in train as the preparation for reformation. And when the appointed time of the captivity had run its course, the returning people, under the leadership of godly princes, [ p. 347 ] priests, and prophets, undertook a most complete reformation of the church according to the rule of the Mosaic theology, as the historical books of Ezra and Nehemiah and the prophetic books of Haggai, Zechariah, and Malachi bear witness.

II. Of that reformation, which from its principal author we shall call the Ezraitic, there were six more solemn parts, namely: 1. The restoration of the temple; 2. The institution of the great synagogue; 3. A most diligent examination of the copies of the sacred books; 4. The sedulous preaching of the word of God; 5. The solemn excommunication of the Cuthaeans; 6. Separation from the mixed people.

III. What pertains to the restoration of the temple, since it has been more than sufficiently belabored by others on account of certain chronological difficulties, and does not properly belong to our design, we have thought fit to pass over.

IV. As the second part of the Ezraitic reformation we set down the institution of the great synagogue, which is remembered with celebrated fame among the Jews. "The men of the great synagogue" [Hebrew: anshei keneset ha-gedolah] is the most customary periphrasis for those reformers. Whoever wishes to know more of it may consult, besides the Jews, Martinus Raymundus, Pugio Fidei, part ii. cap. iv.; Petrus Galatinus, Arcana, lib. iv. cap. vi.; Bodin, De Republica, lib. vi. cap. viii.; Baronius, Annales, tom. i. A.C. xxxi. 10, 11, xxxiii. 19; Lorinus on Numbers xi. 16; Casaubon, Exercitationes, xiii. 5; Cappellus, Vindiciae pro Casaubono, lib. iii. cap. ii.; Cunaeus, De Republica Hebraeorum; Constantine l'Empereur on the tractate Middoth, cap. v. sec. 3.

V. A sanhedrim of seventy-one men had been instituted by Moses in the wilderness, which, in comparison with the lesser assemblies of judges, is commonly called the great. Some affirm that it served only for the age of the wilderness wandering, and did not continue when judges or kings were established in the land of Canaan; but that these men are egregiously mistaken is shown by Selden, that keenest vindicator of these sanhedrims, who nevertheless does not deny that they were many times intermitted. This sanhedrim the returning people restored in the first place. There are, I confess, those whose stomachs cannot bear this most eminent part of the Ezraitic reformation, and who hold what is said about it for Jewish figments; while they themselves stand ready to believe whatever the lying Talmud dares to invent in its traditions, provided only it turns to the advantage of the opinions they cherish. But, as we have said, the origin of this synod of seventy-one was sacred, proceeding from the express command of God. And those who dream that those great men and prophets returned from the captivity, men most learned in the law of God and burning with zeal for a reformation of every kind, did not restore that assembly, without which they could preserve neither order nor authority, will not have me for a rival. For they are not found to have been so wanting to their duty in other matters that we should suspect them of negligence in this institution of the highest utility, and of necessity for the government of the people. VI. Further: that assembly of reformers was called the "great synagogue" preeminently [Greek: kat' exochēn], not from the number of its members, which was precisely determined, but on account of the dignity of the persons who composed it. [ p. 348 ] For very many of its members were men divinely inspired [Greek: theopneustoi], prophets, and amanuenses of the Holy Spirit. An assembly of that kind the church had never seen since the days of Moses; and therefore it ought to seem strange to no one if it brought back into sacred practice things which, though instituted by God, had lain despised and neglected almost from the very beginnings of the church; and if, relying on divine authority, it made certain determinations concerning the sacred Scripture itself, for the use and benefit of all the faithful to the end of the world. The reformation of a church comes nearest to its first institution. It was therefore necessary that all those prophets and divine men should be, as it were, one Moses; for a great and arduous work lay upon them, by which the ruins of many generations were to be repaired. And it pleases me to add, by one or two examples, a specimen of their supreme diligence in restoring all things exactly to the rule of the Mosaic theology.

VII. Let the first be the restoration of the feast of tabernacles, Neh. 8:14, 16-18: "When they found written in the law that Jehovah had commanded by Moses that the Israelites should dwell in booths in their feast of the seventh month... The people went out... and made themselves booths, every one upon his roof, or in their courts, and in the courts of the house of God, and in the street of the water gate. And when all that congregation of those returned from the captivity had thus made booths, and sat in those booths (for the children of Israel had not done so from the days of Joshua the son of Nun until that day), there was very great gladness." That is, although they had observed the feast of the seventh month, they had not sat in booths since the days of Joshua. But what need was there, you will say, to recall into use an antiquated institution, which Samuel the prophet, David the king, and Josiah, the greatest of the reformers before the captivity, had omitted? Was it not mere and excessive curiosity, in a reformation, to look back beyond the times of David, in which the religious observance of divine worship most especially flourished? But not so did it seem to God. For to testify that this duty of theirs was grateful and acceptable to him, he forthwith by the prophet not only extols that feast with great praises, but moreover celebrates under its name the spiritual worship of the gospel that was to be introduced, Zech. 14:16-19; namely, that the godly reformers might learn from this of how great price it was with God to attend strictly and accurately, all other considerations laid aside, to the rule of the Mosaic theology in their work. VIII. That this synagogue also banished from the borders of the church the unlawful mingling [Greek: athemiton mixin] with the Gentiles, which from the days of Solomon had scarcely been piously avoided, we shall presently show; but they ascended higher still. For these men, furnished with the spirit of prophecy, set themselves against a most ancient custom, never approved by the true God, of taking several wives, and of dismissing for the most trifling causes those whom they had joined to themselves by lawful covenants, a custom which had prevailed among the whole nation from ancient times [Greek: ap' archaiōn], Mal. 2:14-16: "Jehovah has been witness between you and the wife of your youth, against whom you deal treacherously, though she is your companion and the wife of your covenant. For did he not [ p. 349 ] make one, though the residue of the spirit was with him? And why one? That he might seek a seed of God... He hates putting away, says Jehovah of Israel." An illustrious testimony against polygamy, such as exists nowhere else in the whole Old Testament before the captivity. I confess the former words, ver. 15, are ambiguous, "and did not one make" [Hebrew: ve-lo echad asah], which interpreters render variously: "Did not one make?" "Did he not make one?" The Greek has "And did not another make?" [Greek: kai ouk allos epoiēse]. For since echad is of the masculine gender, it seems unable to denote a woman. But that word has no feminine form derived from it. For achat ["one," feminine] is not from echad, which would have produced achedet, but from ach, as Rabbi David Kimchi teaches in his Michlol. It is no wonder, then, if echad is sometimes employed in the feminine gender, inasmuch as by the force of its acceptation it signifies neutrally, Zech. 11:7; and the following words, moreover, admit no other sense. The Targum of Jonathan trifles with some nonsense about the one Abraham. But the words must manifestly be interpreted either of the single woman created at the beginning, as their author himself and the best interpreter of Scripture, our Lord Jesus Christ, seems to render their sense; or of the counsel of the one God, joining one male and one female in lawful matrimony.

IX. The words also in which the prophet treats of divorces are not free from all ambiguity, ver. 16: "for he hates putting away, says Jehovah" [Hebrew: ki sane shallach amar Yehovah]. Jerome: "When you hate her, put her away, says the Lord." The old English version: "If he hate her, let him put her away;" which reading the royal translation retains in the margin, though it interprets the words in a plainly different sense, "The Lord saith that he hateth putting away," according to the mind of the Holy Spirit. For since the words are set down elliptically, that sense is to be embraced which they most nearly yield without arbitrary supplements. The word shallach ["putting away"] is therefore put absolutely, and God testifies that he holds that putting away in hatred. X. In this manner were the first foundations laid of the reformation of the Jewish church returned from captivity. Searching out with the utmost fidelity and diligence the very original institutions of God, however antiquated through so many ages and consigned to oblivion, they adhered to them most religiously. XI. The accuracy bestowed upon the sacred Scripture we mentioned in the next place. That is the greatest treasure of the church, to which, under the care of God and the administration of the Spirit, it owes its existence on earth. This was the single law of the whole reformation, and therefore it was to be attended to with accuracy in the first place. That Ezra and his prophetic companions had with them ancient copies preserved with the highest fidelity, no one can justly doubt. They devoted their supreme labor to transcribing these most correctly, and to removing from others the faults which it is not impossible could have crept in during the dispersion. Some ascribe to the collation of all the copies the beginning of the Keri and Kethib [Hebrew: qere u-khethib, the "read" and "written" marginal variants]. And in the whole work they enjoyed the most immediate inspiration and guidance of God. But that Ezra neither wished, nor ought, nor was able, nor had any need [ p. 350 ] to change the ancient letters in which the amanuenses of the Holy Spirit, the prophets, had written the sacred word after the pattern of those which God himself first engraved, we have proved before. There is also another figment which, otherwise than was fitting, has gained credit with some great men: namely, that the whole sacred Scripture was lost, and was restored by Ezra from memory and committed to writing. A most absurd lie, and a blasphemy at once! God forbid that anyone hereafter should cast this reproach upon his care, providence, and love toward the church! XII. Very learned men are of the opinion that the invention of the Hebrew punctation [the vowel points] belongs to the Ezraitic accuracy bestowed upon the biblical text. I accede to their judgment so far as to count it most highly probable. For after the prophetic ministry ceased in the Jewish church, it is a matter of religious scruple to suppose that any others whatsoever undertook that work. Whether, therefore, we say that the vowels were coeval with the letters themselves, or devised by Ezra, it comes to the same thing, since either opinion ascribes to them a divine origin. But there are those who determine the whole Hebrew punctation to be a rabbinical, post-Talmudic invention [Greek: heurēma]. What I think in this cause, since the plan of my undertaking requires it, I hope I may bring forward without offense to anyone, here or elsewhere; although another attempt of mine in this quarter did not succeed so prosperously, by which, all unwitting, I frightened off a hen clucking over her new-hatched chick. [A wry allusion to the controversy stirred by Owen's earlier treatise against Brian Walton's Polyglot prolegomena on this question.]

chapters.heading: A Digression on the Origin of the Hebrew Punctation

blocks.text: I. It is the opinion of great men that Ezra and his colleagues, the assessors of the great synagogue, while they were bestowing their care upon the sacred Scriptures, invented the Hebrew punctation, since before that time the Jews used in writing only the twenty-two consonant letters. For my part, I have no wish to gainsay the opinion of those who maintain that the vowels were coeval with the letters, nor yet to persist obstinately in it; since even those who hold that the origin of the points is to be ascribed to Ezra assert that it was plainly divine. That the Hebrew punctation, then, which by the goodness of God we now use, is to be assigned to no one after the times of Ezra, is the position whose defense we undertake in this digression; or, what comes to the same thing, that it had a divine and infallible origin. For there are some who suppose that certain post-Talmudic rabbis devised it; nor do they merely suppose it, but they think those who judge otherwise deserve to be received with insults. But the monstrous confidence which they employ does not at all deter me from subjecting their opinion to examination. For whoever has not learned to despise, or at least [ p. 351 ] to disregard, the angry passions and the foot-and-a-half-long words which some men, as though pleading the cause of truth, put forward from minds puffed up with disdain and excessive self-opinion, or swollen with the leaven of party zeal, will find it necessary in these festering times to cast away all hope and care of defending the truth. II. When learned men first parted ways into this division of opinions, it seemed truly a matter of very little moment however the dispute might be settled. Elias Levita is almost the only one among the Jews who inclines to the opinion of the novelty of the punctation. But he, since he held that it had always been a part of the oral law, that shadow or cloud for which that most superstitious nation pines away, and was only committed to writing by the rabbis, is untroubled about its divine authority. Perhaps, indeed, it was all one to him whether it be reckoned to have proceeded from God himself or from the masters, who all but occupy the place of God. The Christians who first embraced that opinion could not foresee in their thoughts what evils would take their rise from it. But now that the audacity of certain men in profaning the sacred Scriptures has ceased to be tolerable, and advances daily, lest at last nothing unshaken [Greek: akinēton], nothing sacred, be left in sacred things, it seems to concern all who are held by a due reverence for the divine word to guard most religiously every tittle of the Hebrew verity. For from this one error concerning the rabbinical and arbitrary invention of the Hebrew points, we see that very many evils have poured themselves out upon the church of God, threatening destruction to most holy truth. Hence some affirm that the single words of sacred Scripture, apart from all connection with others, ought to be considered and weighed as so many hieroglyphic signs, if you please; others, that the sacred Scriptures have been corrupted by the Jews in that disposition or fixing of the points which we now use; among these is Bellarmine, De Verbo Dei, lib. ii. cap. ii. Johannes Morinus contends that the sense of the Hebrew text cannot be perceived by us without the help of the points; and that therefore, since the points owe their origin to the labor of uncertain men, fallible in all things and in some openly false, that Hebrew text cannot be the rule and norm of faith, Exercitationes, lib. i. cap. ii. Gregory de Valentia accordingly wishes it to be corrected to the exemplar of the Vulgate Latin version, tom. i. disput. v. quaest. 3. Another, whom for honor's sake and for other reasons I do not name, holds that this rabbinical invention [Greek: heurēma] is not so perfect but that it could be made better. Another judges that the punctation may be changed by anyone at his pleasure, provided a more convenient sense of the words be elicited by that change. Cappellus adds that very many differences between the Hebrew text and the ancient versions, especially the Greek of the Seventy, took their rise from this, that that version was made before the invention of the points, and accordingly followed that reading which was then in use, from which the Masoretes in their punctation many times departed. And I fear that, this door once opened, still greater monstrosities of opinion may be expected from the curious and itching wits of this age, especially if diligence be added to audacity. How this dispute was agitated not long since among learned men, especially between Buxtorf and Cappellus, is hidden from no one [ p. 352 ] who cares to know these things. What has lately been said by certain others is almost all borrowed from them. Nay, I am well enough assured that some would by no means have marched forth so thickly hedged about with testimonies of the rabbis for the novelty of the points, had they not plowed so far with the heifer of the most distinguished Buxtorf as to convert to their own use what he had brought forward by way of objection against himself. III. It is now two years and somewhat more since I wrote and published in our vernacular tongue certain things on this question of the origin of the points. These a certain person received in such a manner as becomes a man who feels it to belong to his honor to display the greatest contempt for others. It would be easy to strip that little crow of the borrowed colors, relying on which he arrogates everything to himself, if that were the business now in hand. But our present design does not suffer it. Nor would the work before us, swelling to an unexpected bulk, permit me to pursue in this place everything that bears on the unraveling of this question and the deciding of the dispute, as I purposed at the outset. Briefly touching, therefore, the weight of the reasons on which I rely in still persevering in the opinion of the divine origin of the Hebrew punctation, and subjecting to examination the chief objections by which some strive to assail it, I shall press on to what remains to be said of the Ezraitic reformation. IV. First, then, we argue from universal use and possession. The possession of so great a treasure is not lightly to be abandoned. The whole church of God holds this punctation of the Scriptures of the Old Testament as her own peculiar property. The adversaries of our opinion will not deny that a great benefit is lodged in it. Nowhere among the nations does there exist a monument of any copy older than the punctation. No legitimate prescription, therefore, can be brought against the possession. Whatever is said on the adverse side is conjecture. It is indeed true that there are very many copies, and that there formerly existed, from all antiquity backwards, copies written without points. But that none exists coeval with the time at which the patrons of the novelty of the points pretend they were invented, they themselves, I think, will confess. The possession, therefore, is legitimate, and the prescription duly established by antiquity. Among the books of Selden which are now preserved in the Bodleian Library there is a most ancient Pentateuch, most beautifully written out in two volumes, with verses alternately in Hebrew and Chaldee, so that the Targum everywhere adheres to the text. At the first glance at the book I immediately perceived that the vowel points had been added and inserted long after the writing of the letters. The manner of the writing and the color of the ink bear witness to it. But that this codex was written only some centuries after the pretended rabbinical invention of the punctation can be shown by very many indications. Now although this argument is not of itself strong enough to settle this whole dispute, yet if the weightiest considerations of reason are not produced on the contrary side, it will be found to have strength enough and to spare for repelling objections. Whoever will concede that grammatical trifles, the silence of one or two ancient writers about the points, sophistical quibbles, most uncertain conjectures, and other things [ p. 353 ] of that kind, suffice to eject the church of God from this possession, is, if I am any judge, too easy in a cause not his own. The arguments, therefore, which are wont to be brought forward to overthrow the divine origin of the points are not to be weighed in an equal balance, inclining neither this way nor that, against those which fight for it; since on this side there hangs also the weight of this possession, which must first be moved from its place before the adverse reasons can be reckoned to advance on an equal footing. In other affairs there are few who labor under such sloth and negligence as to suffer themselves to be so easily circumvented and expelled from their hereditary goods. Let every man do as he will; for my part, assuredly, I shall not yield up this possession except unwillingly, and overborne by reasons. V. 2. The very nature of the thing clearly shows that it is from heaven, not from men. It breathes everywhere something divine [Greek: theion ti]. From the beginning of the holy books to the end, like itself, consistent with itself, everywhere sincerely and infallibly determining the sense of the letters, this punctation points out and declares the mind of the Holy Spirit. The men of the opposing party [Greek: hoi ex enantias] will never to all eternity produce one objection [Greek: enstasis] to the contrary that is of any weight. This shines forth most of all in the most obscure prophecies, those that conceal the profoundest mysteries, in the understanding of which all the Jews are blinder than moles, and always have been since the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ. Grave and learned men, I hope, would not wish to sell the light which the punctation brings to innumerable places of sacred Scripture at the price of most uncertain conjectures, were there not present a disproportion in the counter-pull [Greek: ametria antholkēs]. As for the claims that certain accents are superfluous and useless, that the silent sheva is often added in vain and multiplied without cause, and quibbling subtleties [Greek: leptologiai] of that sort, which Cappellus hints at in his reformation of the grammar in support of his hypothesis, they are so frivolous and inept that I marvel that learned men could insist on them or ever bring their minds to entertain them. Learned men will not say, I think, that they rightly grasp the reason of every single point in the sacred Scriptures: certainly I know who they are that have told me, in their writings, that is, and published books, that certain marks in the Hebrew punctation are utterly useless, and the accents superfluous; but who has told them the same, I know not; nor in so great a cause am I minded to attach faith and authority to grammatical trifles and to the audacious conjectures of puffed-up men. Others say that the Hebrew punctation is not so perfect a work but that it can be corrected, amended, and rendered more perfect. There is more than one reason why I should judge these men too much elated with self-opinion, or knowingly and willingly carrying themselves more magnificently than their merits warrant, the more fastidiously to despise others. But come: if they are men, and so please, let them try their strength. We shall see at once how far "brass coins differ from lupine counters" (Horace, Epistles 1.7.23). Him, and the wisdom of him, who put forth the punctation, they will never equal, though they burst themselves in the attempt. Let them proceed by particular instances, or it will profit nothing to pour out empty words, of which "there is a wide range, this way and that" (Homer, Iliad 20.249). Whoever shall seriously weigh this work will find it perfect, divine, most absolute, like the other works of God, to which nothing can be added and nothing taken away. To the man who would amend or [ p. 354 ] correct it I will say, "Remember the battle, and do so no more" [Hebrew: zekhor milchamah al-tosaph] (Job 41:8). Nor indeed can I sufficiently wonder what came into the minds of learned men, that they should ascribe a work of this kind, to the amending of which in any particular the whole nation of the learned has always been, and even now is, unequal, to certain unlearned worthless fellows, destitute of all aids, both spiritual and of secular science. VI. 3. In this question, which is one of fact, the testimonies of the Jews themselves are of no small weight. Here they all think the same: namely, that the origin of the punctation was divine. Even Elias Levita, who almost alone among his own people dared to ascribe the designation of the open sounds by little marks to the post-Talmudic Masoretes of Tiberias, nevertheless everywhere contends that it was a part of the oral law delivered by God himself on Mount Sinai, and preserved no less religiously and inviolately than if it had been committed to writing from its first origin. But very many of them, and the most learned, constantly assign that writing to Moses or to Ezra. Whoever wishes to see this established by testimonies, let him go to the most learned dissertation of the renowned Buxtorf on the origin of the Hebrew punctation, and to Joseph de Voysin in his most erudite preface to the Pugio Fidei of Martinus Raymundus. But when I had said elsewhere that the testimonies of the rabbis avail much in this cause, it was retorted that they ought therefore to be reckoned competent to invent the punctation itself. But the fatuity of that most unfair comparison betrays itself. For some men can testify that a thing was done divinely, or at least that neither they nor their fathers did it, who are in truth altogether unequal and unfit for the doing of the thing itself. But that they may mock the testimonies of the Hebrews, they say that the contention is not about the sounds of the vowels, but only about the signs. For they acknowledge that the Hebrews always had the same vowel sounds which they still use, and thus that the language was never destitute of vowels. Not long ago a certain person judged the observation of this distinction to be of such moment for the settling of this whole dispute that he fell upon me with unbridled temper, because, in recounting this controversy, I had thought no account need be taken of it. But I could not suspect that the question at issue was whether all the Hebrews were utterly dumb down to the time of the Tiberian Masoretes. For if they were destitute of vowel sounds, they were also dumb, and could never pronounce one complete word. For that nothing can be uttered by the letters themselves without the open sounds of the vowels is known even to those "who are not yet washed in the bath for a penny" [that is, to mere children]. Whoever, therefore, shall strive to prove by reasons and arguments that in this sense the Hebrew language had vowels, not only before it came into anyone's mind to express them by marks or figures, but even before a single consonant letter was written or engraved, has without doubt got himself a cause in which "it is easy to be eloquent." Meanwhile it is the origin of the points, not of the sounds, that we are investigating. But since the patrons of the novelty of the points judge it to concern their cause that this distinction between the sounds and the figures of the vowels be diligently weighed in the agitation of this [ p. 355 ] question, come, let us concede that the Hebrews before the invention of the points were not dumb, but spoke after the manner of other men. What they can carve out of that for the support of their cause, however, we have still to wait and see. VII. 4. The supreme utility, if not the absolute necessity, of the points and accents for the right understanding of the sacred text, since I have set it forth elsewhere, I do not wish to relate here at greater length. I leave these matters to the judgment of all who have ever seriously employed the Hebrew language in the study of the sacred letters. Rabbi Bechai brings forward to this end an excellent testimony from the book Bahir, on the section Beshallach, Exod. 13:17: "The points stand to the letters of the law of Moses as the soul of life stands in the body of a man." But if that heavenly light, which accrues to the truth from the fixing and restricting of the sense of the words which the Holy Spirit uses, be reckoned the product of human wit and discretion, how great evils will at once arise from thence, and what prejudices to the faith, no one can easily conjecture. The memory of the attempts of certain men, which that supposition has spawned, is exceedingly unwelcome. Hence we have seen some men, without horror and without the most just indignation, parading the uncertainty and obscurity of the Hebrew text, the institution of its correction by means of the versions, and even by conjectures, if only a man will use them modestly, in the judgment, forsooth, of those very men who, that they may show themselves skilled craftsmen in the trimming up of learned conjectures, stand ready to cast off all reverence for God and men, while they lean on conjectures that are doubtful, uncertain, nay, utterly inept. And the itching and ambitious wits of this age compel us to dread things worse still. Let the vowel points be removed, and all the distinguishing accents; let the bare letters remain, or rather letters thus stripped bare, with no distinction applied throughout any book, each word divided from the rest by some point, as among the Samaritans, or at least by an imaginary one, and it would at once be evident what an Iliad of evils, what scandals and perils, would inundate the church. And yet, if the whole punctation be the invention [Greek: heurēma] of uncertain men, of Jewish rabbis, no reason of any weight can be brought forward why all these things might not be so removed and cast aside, nay, perhaps, why they ought not to be. But they say that those letters aleph, vav, yod, which they call mothers of reading [matres lectionis], together with he, were before the invention of the points either vowels or employed in the place of vowels, by whose help readers could easily remedy all those inconveniences which seem to attend the absence of vowels; and that the points were not affixed by the Tiberian Masoretes arbitrarily or at their pleasure, and that therefore the punctation does not rest upon their authority. But I, in the first place, would gladly know whether those letters which they call mothers of reading were employed to that end only as they now appear in the sacred text, or whether they were also added and inserted in all the other places in which the sound of the word demands their presence. If they shall say that only those were employed of old which still adhere to the text, and in those places and words in which they persist, [ p. 356 ] it is clearer than the noonday light that they could not supply the office of vowels nor make good their lack; since there are innumerable words in which they nowhere appear, and sometimes, where they are present, they do not rightly serve the pronunciation, if any faith at all is to be placed in the present punctation; for it would be necessary that the single letter aleph should by itself render almost all the open sounds, and everyone sees how ridiculous it would be to imagine that. But if they shall say that these vowel letters were of old multiplied in the text, and written everywhere according to the rule of the sound of the words, so that, for example, davar ["word"] was written d-aleph-b-aleph-r, I should like to know who they were who dared to pluck out and cast away so many thousands of letters written in the sacred Scriptures by God himself and the prophets, or when we are to suppose that this was done. The religion, or the superstition, of the Jews alike forbids us to ascribe this audacious crime to them. It is not the part of the same men to keep a scrupulously exact account of how often every single letter is employed in the whole Bible, and to throw away so many thousands of their own accord. This figment, therefore, will not heal the wound which the newfangled opinion of the novelty of the points inflicts upon the light of truth and the perspicuity of the Scriptures. And as for the further pretense that Origen expressed the Hebrew text in Greek characters by the help of these vowel letters, it is established by no reasons or testimonies. It is not fair that any place should be assigned in so great a cause to bare affirmations, suspicions, and conjectures. He could learn the pronunciation of the Hebrew language from the usage of the Jews, or from the points themselves; so that for representing the Hebrew text in Greek characters he had no need of that fictitious and most imperfect aid. Next, as to the other part of the answer set down above, that the Tiberian Masoretes did not point the Scriptures at their own discretion: the Jews, relying on their futile tradition of the oral law, can put this forward with some modesty and show of probability; not so others, those, namely, who acknowledge that oral law to be a figment of superstitious men. Yet there is one who, in his defense of the novelty of the points, asserts twenty times over, and not without insults against those of the contrary mind, that those Masoretes pointed the Scripture according to the received reading, which duly exhibited the mind of the Holy Spirit, and not at their own discretion. Let us see, then, what sound reason lies beneath this, to persuade anyone that this pretended punctation of the Masoretes is to be held of any value. "The Masoretes," they say, "affixed the points to the Bible according to the reading then received and in use." But among whom, I ask, was that reading received which is set up as the norm of the affixing of the points? Among those very Masoretes, of course. For whether at the time when they say this deed was done there were any Christians who understood the Hebrew text is uncertain. The Masoretes, therefore, pointed the Bible according to the reading received among themselves. That is the hope we are given. Although we have shown elsewhere that they were the worst of worthless fellows, if such men there were, perpetually employing the audacity of feigning whatever they pleased, yet be it so, that in this undertaking it suited them to be honest. [ p. 357 ] What then follows? "That accepted reading, to which they attended in affixing the points, exhibited the genuine sense of the Holy Spirit." But of that sense, pray, who were the judges and arbiters? The Masoretes themselves, without doubt. And so the Masoretes affixed the points not at their own discretion, but according to the reading received among them and the sense which they judged best and true. If I were to deny this, how, pray, would my adversary prove it? Will he show that they acted in good faith in other matters? He never will. Will he produce witnesses? He has none; and Seneca teaches us that no one ever exacted an oath from a historian. Let us deal, then, by what is fair and good, and concede what cannot be proved. But in truth these things are not mutually repugnant: they could use their own judgment in discerning the true reading and in investigating the sense of the Holy Spirit, while nevertheless they had no form or norm of punctation, and were thus arbitrary judges of the work they undertook; unless no one is to be reckoned to act at his own discretion except he act perversely. Marcus Tullius, indeed, in his oration for Roscius, affirms that strict judgment is one thing and arbitration another. But the laws would never have appointed arbitrators if it were necessary that whoever determines anything at his discretion should determine it without judgment. We ourselves, surely, when we commit anything to the arbitration of others, do not intend that in pronouncing sentence upon us and our affairs they should indulge their own inclinations with no care for what is fair and right. When, therefore, I said that the Masoretes affixed the points to the Bible at their discretion, I understood nothing else, and could understand nothing else, than that they completed that work using and trusting their own judgments. The knowledge, then, the skill, the judgment, and the integrity of the Tiberian rabbis, all of which I would not purchase at the price of a rotten nut, are, according to this opinion, the sole foundation of the present reading of all Hebrew codices. For there is no mortal who has learned the use of the copies which even now are written without points, except by the help of the points. But, you say, relying on tradition, not on their own judgment, they exhibited in that punctation of theirs the reading received from the fathers. But how faithful guardians of traditions they were, how expert patchers-together and vendors of the most inept fables, nay, of horrendous blasphemies, under the name of most ancient traditions, as though they had counted it their glory to have utterly overturned every vestige of the truth, I do not wish to say at greater length here, since the matter is manifest and will be spoken of hereafter. From that tradition, then, since they themselves were constituted by their own authority, if not the dispensers, yet the guardians and conservators of it, we are not yet delivered from having to lean upon the good faith of one of those worthless fellows for the reading of the whole Hebrew text. But they say further, that the true reading of the text was preserved, after that language ceased to be vernacular, not so much by oral tradition as by the very writing of the text, which always remained entire. But this too seems strange to me; for it does not admit of any easy explanation how the true reading can be preserved for the writing by that written thing which cannot be read. For I would gladly know, since letters cannot be uttered without [ p. 358 ] the use of the open sounds or vowels, and may signify anything according to the pleasure of him who joins those sounds to them, how the distinction of the vowels and their legitimate use can depend upon them. But it does not please me to dwell longer on these things, lest I fall in again with disputants of that sort, who would hold that the pernicious consequences which the rabbinical invention of the points, whose patronage they have undertaken, would bring in, are to be imputed to those who are assured of their divine origin. VIII. 5. Furthermore: strength and authority accrue to the opinion of the ancient and sacred origin of the points from the uncertainty and wavering of the authors of the contrary opinion in assigning their rise. They will not have it that Ezra invented the points; who then was the author of the work? When, and where in the world, did he live? To what end did he undertake the work? At whose command, persuasion, or authority? What taskmaster or taskmasters [Greek: ergodiōktai] of the work did he have, what overseers, what judges? What did those whose interest it chiefly was that the work be carried through exactly think or determine concerning the work undertaken and finished? All these things they either openly profess themselves ignorant of, or it is easy to prove it. It is said to have been the common work of the Tiberian Masoretes. So, to be sure, said Elias Levita. Some determine that it was completed in the fifth century after the birth of Christ, others the sixth, Morinus the seventh or eighth. But what if I should say that those Tiberian Masoretes never existed at all, and that the authors of this opinion, in order to feign that the points were devised by those Masoretes, first feigned the Masoretes themselves? Would they convict me of falsehood out of any approved history, out of the monuments of past events? I know well enough they cannot. I confess indeed that about the year 350, when Jerome was in Palestine, the Jews had an obscure and inglorious school in the city of Tiberias. Whether they continued in that place down to the time to which the contrivers and builders of this imaginary work are obliged to assign it is uncertain, and even improbable. For such was the condition of the nation at that time, that nowhere within the bounds of the Roman empire, which they had so often shaken with slaughters, dissensions, and wars, could they long subsist in safety in any great number. In that very place, moreover, where they are said to have completed this work, they had not long before suffered a great defeat at the hands of Gallus, the legate of the emperor Constantius. From that time forward it is utterly uncertain whether any vestige of a Masoretic school remained at Tiberias. Furthermore: everything in which the Jews ever had any learning or science was at that time cultivated more diligently in the east, around Babylon, where they had occupied more peaceful seats, than in Palestine. When the western rabbis had now for a century or two been almost as mute as fishes, there came forth from the east that great and most consummate work of fables and trifles, the Talmud which is called Babylonian. That a few obscure, ignoble manikins, of no name or worth, should have undertaken and perfected so great a work, without the knowledge and without the counsel of the celebrated men of the same profession, is for those to believe who hold their faith easy and ready at hand for everything that makes for the advantage of their cause. Furthermore: [ p. 359 ] whoever has saluted any Jewish writings even from the threshold knows that all Jews are, and ever since their final rejection have been, the most insolent trumpeters of the deeds done, as they imagine, gloriously by themselves and their people. So far have they always been from silently passing over true things in this arena, that they bring forth everywhere falsehoods, fictions, things unheard of, lying most impudently. Yet now, concerning this most divine invention, to which that nation never produced anything like or equal, there is the deepest silence among them for several centuries. After a thousand years, at length, came forth one Elias, who suspects that certain Tiberian Masoretes devised this work. Was there no one who could have committed to writing, for posterity, the origin of that divine artifice among the inventors themselves, especially when its glory and utility were so great that nowhere in the world has there been found Christian or Jew, learned or unlearned, who either wished or dared to gainsay its use or its perfection, in any part, or in the whole, or in the smallest point? Is it credible that the fame of so great an endeavor, exulting in prosperous success, utterly perished within the space of a brief age among a nation most retentive of all traditions, even about futile things and things of naught? I confess I do not have so much credulity in my store as to lend faith to monstrous conjectures of this kind. IX. That the punctation of the Bible was a most excellent invention, no one, I think, will deny. It must plainly be said that nothing like it would ever arise elsewhere, and nothing like it ever has arisen. In its use the church has received an immortal benefit. There are learned men who determine that it is absolutely and indispensably necessary to the true understanding of the sacred letters. "Without the points," says Marcus Marinus in his preface to his Hebrew Lexicon, "no certain doctrine concerning this language can be delivered; since everything can be read in different ways, so that the confusion of this one language would be greater than that of Babel." And Rudolphus Cevallerius [Antoine Rodolphe Chevallier], De Rudimentis Linguae Hebraicae, cap. 4: "As for what remains concerning the antiquity of the vowels and accents, I subscribe to the opinion of those who affirm that the Hebrew language, as the most absolute archetype [Greek: archetypon] of all others, was plainly written so from the beginning; since those who think otherwise not only render the authority of sacred Scripture doubtful, but in my judgment tear it up by the roots, because without vowels and the marks of distinction it would have nothing certain and firm." Of the same opinion is Franciscus Junius, Animadversions on Bellarmine, De Verbo Dei, lib. ii. cap. ii., where, out of Johannes Isaac, a Christian Jew, "he compares the man who undertakes to read the Scriptures without points to an unbridled horse [Greek: achalinōtos]." I will not repeat what Jerome said of the writings of Jovinian, after another had said it of those of Plautus, "no one will read these but the Sibyl;" but I may borrow that saying of the poet, "opinion is spread over all things" [Greek: dokos d' epi pasi tetyktai] (Xenophanes, fr. 34); so on every side do difficulties present themselves in the bare letters. And worthy of note is what Engelbertus Engles has in his preface to the lexicon of Valentinus Schindler. "There are," he says, "many most learned and distinguished men who, because certain rabbis of the Jews assert that the points were invented by men long after the twenty-two [ p. 360 ] letters of the alphabet, suppose that the vowels of the Hebrews did not exist at one and the same time with the consonants; but if the places of Scripture, a few out of many just now enumerated, be considered more attentively, it will become evident either that the points arose together with the twenty-two letters, or that the Scripture was formerly flexible like wax, now to this side, now to that. And if the points were invented by men, it follows necessarily that the Scripture in this our time has an authority not divine but human." Hence that saying of Aben Ezra, in his Balance of the Holy Tongue: "Whatever exposition is not according to the rule of the accents, do not consent to it, nor listen to it." He has similar things in other places; I shall add only what he has in his commentary on Exod. 20:1: "Know that the words are as it were bodies, and the accents as it were souls; and the body is to the soul as a garment." X. The far greater part of the theologians who have been exercised in this arena think the same. Some of the papists, indeed, freely concede that the Hebrew text, the punctation removed, either cannot be understood at all, or only with difficulty and very imperfectly. Then, snatching most audaciously to their own purpose the concessions of certain great men, who in their writings, in passing and while engaged on other business, have pronounced an over-hasty judgment in this cause without weighing all that bears upon it, it is marvelous with what audacity they triumph over the sacred Hebrew verity, as useless and scarcely, or not even scarcely, intelligible. Nay, there are not wanting those who bring a grave accusation against the men who most firmly believe the divine origin of the punctation, because these assert that, on the opinion of those who hold its origin rabbinical, it could, and perhaps even ought, to be done away with; as if they were doing injury to the sacred Scriptures, and contriving that by which much of the perspicuity of the text would be lost. By the same reasoning some Socinian might say that I deny that Christ is to be adored with religious worship, because I deny that he should be so adored unless he were that supreme and only God, while the Socinian himself is fully persuaded that he is not that supreme God. In this, then, we have all learned men voting with us: namely, that the use of the vowel points and accents is of such moment for perceiving the true and genuine sense of sacred Scripture, that were they removed, almost everything would be thrown into marvelous confusion and rendered uncertain. It is indeed true that those who have attained the true reading of the text by the help of the punctation, and retain the knowledge of that sense which the pointed words and the passages distinguished by accents pour forth, can make much progress in the study of the Scriptures by the help of other means. But let us suppose that those points had never been devised; let us utterly cast away all the sense and understanding which by their benefit we have in any way drawn from the sacred Scriptures, whether immediately or through the mediating studies and labors of others; let us approach the bare letters empty of preconceived opinions: [ p. 361 ] we shall see at once into what difficulties, uncertainties, and darkness we are thrown. For when the discretion of every individual has been set up as the norm of the pronunciation of words, of the reading of sentences, and of all pauses and distinctions, who will prevent the whole nation of the learned from falling at once into inextricable and pernicious quarrels? That word of God, thrown open as a prize of contest, will be the one apple of strife [Greek: mēlon eridos]. Whether proud and luxuriant wits, swollen with the opinion of their own knowledge, or anxious for a reputation for learning, would leave anything in it unshaken [Greek: akinēton] and undoubted, is plainly uncertain; nay rather, that they would leave nothing of the kind is almost most certain. On this punctation depend all those means which we use in learning that language, and likewise very many helps to progress in the solid knowledge of it. All these being cast aside (not this or that conjugation, as pleases Cappellus, since the reasoning is the same for all), without whose help and benefit not even the Jews themselves, who are wholly given to the study of this language, would have anything certain to lean upon, there will most certainly be opened to eternal quarrels not so much a wide window as the main camp gate. For is there almost any man of somewhat more learning than common who would not be ashamed, and weary of his own sloth, if he could devise nothing new which, himself being judge and witness, would conduce to amending the true and genuine reading of sacred Scripture, and to rightly fixing and settling the distinctions of the sentences? And shall we suppose that no one would straightway be found who, to show himself a more excellent craftsman than the other, or more skilled in trimming up learned conjectures, would throw down the other's structure? He must be a stranger in all literary matters, especially critical ones, who thinks otherwise. But there is no need, they say, that the present reading or punctation be changed in anything. But, say I, for any work to be held so sacred that no one has the right to change it in anything, or to innovate anything in it, this must accrue to it either from the excellence of the work itself, or, beyond that, from the supreme and inviolable authority of its author. Where the excellence and contrivance of the work alone is pleaded, it is fair that that excellence and contrivance be accurately and diligently weighed, that it may appear they are not pleaded in vain. And the examination once instituted, it is wholly open and free to the examiner to reject what seems less congruent with right reason. And there is nothing to forbid its being done so in this cause. They say this device of the punctation is indeed most excellent, and therefore not lightly to be rejected in anything. Very well; but it is not exempted from free examination on account of its excellence; nothing less; nay, where by a change of it a reading truer in the judgment of the examiners, or a more convenient sense, can be elicited, since it holds its place only by merit, subject to examination, that change will be not only lawful but even necessary. And it is plain that this must be said of all sentences, nay, of every single word. Come then, let the critics try their strength, let them exhaust here their whole faculty of opining [Greek: doxastikē], let them cart hither their wagonloads of annotations, let them found new readings and new senses; and that most faithful guardian of sacred truth, namely reverence for the received [ p. 362 ] reading, "is gone, is lost, has taken ship" (a tag from Roman comedy). Many things, I confess, are brought forward by the patrons of the novelty of the points in answer to the argument drawn from the impossibility of learning that language, and of attaining the genuine sense of Scripture in many places, the benefit of the punctation being removed. Johannes Morinus confesses that the matter truly stands so, and that an accurate knowledge of this language is plainly impossible; and he laughs at those who suppose that the Masoretes in their punctation observed the correct pronunciation of the language. It is true that the Hebrew language, compared with other languages, is contained in very few words. But it is not thereby rendered easy or expeditious to learn. For although the words or terms are few, yet many are of the same signification; and there is scarcely one which does not admit several senses, and very many admit a multitude. The same letters exhibit various, often plainly diverse, significations. The case of dabar ["word," "thing," "pestilence," etc.] is well known. The letters of shelomoh yield "Solomon," and "perfect," and "recompense," and "garment." Innumerable examples of this kind may be sought from any vocabulary of this language, and from the concordances. While indeed it was vernacular, and was daily uttered by an innumerable multitude of men, it was not very difficult to perceive rightly the force of each word and to retain it by use. But when it ceased to be in common use, and there was no man to whom it was native and vernacular, and the whole of it was preserved in one book, it is plainly impossible that its correct pronunciation should be continued except from a consideration of the text of the book itself. Some urge that the Greek language formerly lacked accents, and yet was sufficiently open to the understanding. But whoever decides that the Greek accents are to be compared with the Hebrew points and accents seems not sufficiently to perceive the use or office of either in either language. The Greek language has such amplitude that almost everything abounds in it. Then, so great in it is the regular variation of words, and their mutual dependence in construction, that the accents conduce little or nothing to the understanding of a discourse. Very few are the words which, consisting of the same letters in certain tenses, are distinguished in sense by the accents. In Hebrew everything stands otherwise: the variation of the words is the least possible, the dependence in the series of a discourse rare and abrupt, the ellipses most frequent, the ambiguity of the words almost infinite. And yet even in Greek itself, where a word is truly of doubtful signification through the lack of an accent, which does not often happen, it is not hard to slip. Let an oversight [Greek: parorama] of the great Jerome serve as an example. Eusebius wrote in his Chronicle, at the year 1426: "Epimenides purifies Athens" [Greek: Epimenidēs tas Athēnas kathairei]. Jerome translated it, "Epimenides overthrew Athens," instead of "purified;" since it is doubtful whether kathairei should be read as "he destroys" [kathairei from kathaireō] or "he purifies" [kathairei from kathairō]; but the matter is known from history, for Epimenides did not overthrow the Attic land, but purified it with sacrifices. Again, as some think, the true sense of a proverbial saying escaped Aristotle himself, which he mentions in the third book of the Nicomachean Ethics, cap. v.: "No one is wicked willingly, nor blessed against his will" [Greek: Oudeis hekōn ponēros, oud' akōn makar]. He understands by ponēros, with the accent on the last syllable, a wicked man; whereas without doubt the old saying intends ponēros with the accent on the first, that is, a man full of toil and hardship. For "toilworn" [poneros] and "blessed" [makar] are rightly opposed; "wicked" [ponēros] and "blessed" [ p. 363 ] not at all. The same is the case, and the same too the signification, of the word mochthēros, which means a wicked man, and mochtheros, which means one who leads a life of hardship; both words yield the superlative mochthērotatos; whence that question founded on the ambiguity, proposed to Stratonicus in the eighth book of Athenaeus' Deipnosophists: "Who are the mochthērotatoi [most wretched, or most wicked] of the people in Pamphylia?" since the life of the Pamphylians was both miserable and infamous for brigandage. Nay, that the greatest men have sometimes been deceived as to the sense of words where an ambiguous word occurs with no distinguishing accent, although an attentive consideration of the whole context [Greek: periochē] seems precisely to demand one particular sense, let Marcus Tullius himself be the example, the most learned man in Greek and Latin since men were born. He, in the first book of the Tusculans, cap. xlii., reports thus the words of Leonidas to the Spartans who were about to fight against Xerxes in such a manner that they believed they would without doubt die in the battle: "Go on with brave spirit; perhaps we shall sup among the dead below." Now ariston is "breakfast"; it also signifies the best or bravest in war, derived from Ares. But Diodorus Siculus, lib. xi. cap. ix., says that Leonidas ordered them quickly to take their breakfast; he said, "Breakfast! or, Make your breakfast quickly!" [Greek: Aristate! Aristopoieite tacheōs!], that is, "Eat your morning meal in haste." This sense the logic of the antithesis demands; namely, "since we are to sup among the dead." But Cicero, not attending to the opposition, and taking aristan as if it were said [like aristeuein, "to be bravest"] from aristos, which signifies a brave man, translated it, "Go on with brave spirit." Other instances [Greek: enstaseis] are at hand, but the ill-founded comparison will not permit me to dwell longer on them. You will say, perhaps, that the punctation also rests upon authority to this extent, that it is utterly impious to cast it away. But it has all its authority from its authors, and from nowhere else. That we may come, therefore, to the point at which we are aiming, we shall consider in few words who, according to the opinion of those who assail the divine origin of the punctation, were its authors. XI. Those who deny that the Bible was pointed by Ezra, as we said before, assign that work with one voice to certain Masoretes or Tiberian Jewish rabbis. In designating the time they hold contrary opinions. They conjecture that certain Masoretes lived at Tiberias after the completion of both Talmudic works, which they make prior to this invention [Greek: heurēma] of the points; conjecture, I say, for they cannot prove it by any adequate argument or testimony. That the Jews had a school in that place some centuries earlier, we grant; but we say it was deserted and desolate by the time the Babylonian Talmud was compiled. But let us grant, since learned men are so very eager for it, that some Jewish rabbis, more or fewer, lived at that time at Tiberias; let us look into who and of what sort they were, and what is to be ascribed to their learning, judgment, and integrity, detracting nothing injuriously from their reputation. XII. I do not wish here to repeat what I have written elsewhere on the state, condition, and horrible hardening of the Jews after the second temple and the final rejection of the nation by God. Let us satisfy the present design by subjoining things which, being known to all, can be denied by none. That the greatest privilege of the Jews of old was "that the oracles of God were entrusted to them" [Greek: hoti episteuthēsan ta logia tou Theou], [ p. 364 ] the apostle relates, Rom. 3:2. But from that privilege they had long since fallen; for the Word is no longer in any degree committed to their care or trust. For the word had been entrusted to their fathers by divine covenant, Isa. 59:21; and when they had fallen from that covenant through unbelief, and had ceased to be the people and church of God, they began to be possessors of the word itself only in bad faith. With the legitimate right to the use of the word, they also utterly lost the Holy Spirit, promised, namely, in the same covenant. What ought to be expected in or about the word of God from men to whom it is in no way entrusted, and who are destitute of the Spirit, it would be easy to show, were they not themselves a horrendous example of it. Approaching the most holy word of God with unwashed hands, impure in conscience and in mind, themselves contaminated by everyone, they foully contaminate all sacred things; for "to the defiled and unbelieving nothing is pure, but even their mind and conscience are defiled" [Greek: memiasmenois kai apistois ouden katharon, alla memiantai autōn kai ho nous kai hē syneidēsis], as the apostle speaks of these very men, Titus 1:15. Then, so far were they from perceiving the true sense of the Holy Spirit in the word, that they set themselves obstinately, stubbornly, and most pertinaciously against the whole truth which is solely contained in those books which they daily wore out with their hands. Whatever in the whole Scripture pertains to the glory of God, whatever to their own salvation, all this was to them an object of the greatest hatred and abomination. Nay, from the time of Rabbi Judah the Prince, who composed the Mishnah, and onwards, they most superstitiously cultivated a certain new religion, plainly alien from that prescribed to the fathers, that is, from the Mosaic theology, and diametrically opposed to the Christian religion, which that older one served as handmaid. For, pressed continually by the horrible curse of God for having repudiated and slain his most holy Son, the Savior of the world, they have striven in vain to extricate themselves from its bond and penalty by new crimes; stupid meanwhile, and ignorant beyond all belief of all the good arts and sciences, of all history, and of all things useful in human life. That their greatest masters were a race of men inept, futile, raving, magical, idolatrous, superstitious, unlearned, and depraved, both Talmuds teach; and so occupied in devising portentous fables, that they seem to have descended together into the arena to outdo one another in a contest of lying. Whoever can persuade himself that the biblical punctation proceeded from manikins of this sort, most shameless, vain, foolish, lying, accursed, and hateful to God, and that it was forthwith received and employed by all Jews and Christians, without any previous examination or any hesitation whatever, as the norm of all reading, both private and public, and even of all expositions and interpretations, shall keep his credulity to himself without me for a rival. XIII. I know indeed that very learned men have used other and more numerous arguments in this cause; let it suffice me to have briefly indicated those which with me are of such weight that I cannot, and would not, depart from that opinion which is fortified by their authority. The learned men also [ p. 365 ] who have undertaken the patronage of the rabbinical origin of the punctation have reasons which they put forward for their opinion, and these remain to be considered. XIV. In the first place they recount the names of certain great men with whom the opinion that asserts the novelty of the points has the preference. Yet a hundred years ago, or thereabouts, it would have been impossible for anyone to use this argument. It is scarcely a century since this audacious conjecture became widespread; nor has it so much stability but that there is hope it may vanish in a lesser space of time. And although I am a man than whom none rises more willingly at the names of learned men, yet there is more than one reason why I should not sweat much in this place over an argument taken from the testimony of certain persons. For since there is no lack of names not less great, of celebrated men of no slighter fame in the republic of letters, whom it would be the easiest thing to set against these, the prudent reader will judge that no decision of this dispute can follow from a conflict of this kind, not so much of testimonies as of men. Moreover, I find that learned men do not always use that diligence which is fitting in citing authors. Let those serve as an example who cite Valentinus Schindler with approval, as if he supported the opinion of the rabbinical invention of the points; whereas under the word shabal he shows that he judged the points to be more ancient even than the age of Ezra. Some are in doubt; others settle this dispute in passing and while engaged on other business; those who have undertaken the defense of that opinion of set purpose are exceedingly few. And as for some of these, who by their merit were of great name in theology and in the church of God while they lived, and still are among a not ungrateful posterity, I judge that if they could have foreseen to what end certain men would use their words, they would have pronounced nothing in this cause except after an accurate weighing of the moments of the reasons. XV. Secondly, they say: "The ancient Hebrew letters were the Samaritan; but the Samaritans never had vowel points; and therefore neither did the ancient Hebrews." I answer: If learned men were not at a loss for arguments to confirm their opinion, they would doubtless not lean upon most uncertain conjectures of this sort. That the ancient Hebrew letters were those very ones which are still in use among the Jews, we have proved above. It is also plainly uncertain whether the Samaritans of old used vowel points in writing. Postellus affirms that they did. Nor is there any reason why anyone should wonder that that stupid people lost the use and skill of so excellent an artifice. Furthermore: suppose that Ezra repudiated the ancient letters for new ones, either devised by himself or borrowed from elsewhere; by what reasoning, pray, would it seem to follow from this that he who introduced those new letters did not also invent the points? But since this argument has at last been cast aside as feeble and useless by the very men who formerly used it, there is no cause why I should pursue it further. XVI. Thirdly, they say: "The Jews keep in their synagogues a book of the law written without [ p. 366 ] points, which they all but adore, and which they affirm represents the autograph of Moses; that Israelites are found even in China, in whose most ancient books no points appear, although some of them were written six hundred years ago; from which, they say, it is evident that not Moses or Ezra, but the post-Talmudic rabbis devised the artifice of the Hebrew punctation." I answer: How light this argument is, nay, of how little weight in this cause, we have shown elsewhere. Here we shall only run through the heads of the answers. We say therefore, first, that this practice of the Jews is proved by no adequate testimony to be universal, that is, common to the whole nation in all synagogues everywhere; and it matters nothing to us what a few western Jews do in their synagogues, since the practice of the greatest number is wholly unknown to us. Secondly, no one has yet shown that it is even ancient; most of those synagogue customs are superstitious and idolatrous, very many are newfangled, and all post-Talmudic. Thirdly, the Jews themselves, at least the most numerous and most learned of them, constantly deny that they intend by that practice to teach that the ancient Bibles were unpointed, since they themselves adhere to the opinion of the divine origin of the whole punctation. Fourthly, that synagogue book, although it has no points, nevertheless has the Keri and Kethib, which those who use this argument will concede to be no more from Moses than the points themselves. Either, then, the codex, or rather the custom of certain Jews, does not prove that the points are not coeval with the letters, or it proves that the Keri and Kethib are so coeval with the text.
