Fifthly, those who most audaciously seize this synagogue practice of the Jews for their own purpose say that the rabbis devised the points for a twofold reason: first, namely, that the reading of the sacred text might be rendered more expeditious, and as easy for others as for themselves; then, that some who were less learned might by their help read the biblical text in the synagogues; they themselves therefore concede that pointed codices were formerly accustomed to be read in the synagogues. Nay, it is most certain that in antiquity the Hellenizing Jews used the Greek version of the law in their synagogues; so that nothing certain can be gathered from this modern custom of some. Sixthly, the Jews themselves render other reasons for this custom; some of which we have elsewhere recounted from Azariah's Imre Binah, cap. lix. And whoever weighs those twenty-one rules which the masters lay down as necessarily to be observed in copying out a codex that is to be employed in the sacred services of the synagogue will easily perceive that it is not without cause that they employ an unpointed codex, since the accurate observation of all those rules in the writing of the points is plainly impossible. But the chief, if not the only, reason of this custom seems to consist in this: namely, that no one should presume to read the law publicly in the synagogues until he be so far skilled that he can exactly observe all the points, both vowel and accentual, in the reading of codices in which they are not written. For those who recite that book not only distinguish all the vowel points most diligently in pronouncing, but also chant every single word to the modulation of the accents. Another reason also for this custom, [ p. 367 ] perhaps the truest of all, which we too have indicated elsewhere, can be gathered from what Johannes Morinus most truly pronounces, Grammatica Samaritana, cap. xxv. "Writing of this kind," he says, "is liable to far more senses than that which writes out the words with their vowels, and is therefore far more fertile in mystical interpretations, which can be learned only from tradition handed down from the fathers [Greek: patroparadotos]." Finally: suppose they say that by this book they mean to represent the autograph [Greek: autographon] of Moses, which some of them feign to have been written like one single word, without any distinction of words; yet that Ezra did not point the Bible in no way follows from this; and therefore this practice of the Jews contributes nothing at all to the settling of this dispute. XVII. Fourthly, they adduce the testimony of Elias Levita, who lived in Germany about the year 1520. He not only openly affirms that the post-Talmudic Tiberian Masoretes invented the points, but labors to prove it by several arguments. And what then, pray? "Therefore that opinion is most true." How so? For what cause ought the one Elias to be of greater authority with us than very many others, far earlier in age, no less celebrated in learning? It would be easy to overwhelm his testimony not only by the number but by the weight of the others. But, they say, there is no lack of reason why the authority of the one Elias ought to be preferred to that of all the other masters of the same profession who think the contrary. And what reason is that? The Jews, they say, always itch to ascribe to themselves and their own everything that in any degree redounds to the honor of the nation. To that end they lie most impudently everywhere. No faith, therefore, is to be placed in them when speaking in their own cause. But since Elias embraces an opinion which detracts much from the antiquity, and so from the honor, of the points, he must be supposed to have gone over to it only under compulsion of the force of truth. But the matter stands far otherwise. Since the rabbinical name was born, nothing has ever been done, either by these men or by the whole nation, that can be compared with this illustrious invention. What, pray, could be feigned more magnificent for the honor of the nation than that some of them were furnished with such wisdom and skill in the sense of the Scriptures that they could, and with such sanctity and fidelity that they would, invent and bring forth the biblical punctation, by the consent of all the most consummate and most useful work that has ever come to light since men were born? I would dare to say that all the adversaries of the divine origin of the points would most willingly concede that the whole Jewish nation, from the day on which they were rejected by God, has never undertaken or perfected anything that is to be set beside the invention of the points. I would not deny that some of the masters, after they applied themselves to the Peripatetic philosophy recovered by the Saracens into the light and use of men, ceased to play the fool, and wrote things which may be read with some profit. But that all the more ancient ones, Mishnic and Talmudic alike, patched together nothing but ravings, dreams, impostures, and lies, all the learned agree, and the thing itself bears witness. That the punctation [ p. 368 ] was affixed to the sacred letters with the highest judgment, conscience, love of truth, and knowledge, there is no one, that I know of, who denies. There is nothing, therefore, that would redound equally to the honor of the whole nation and of the Tiberian masters, nothing that would equally consign all Christians into bondage to the rabbis, as that Elian figment of the rabbinical invention of the punctation. And indeed it is very probable that Elias was carried into that opinion for no other cause than that he well understood that, this once admitted and established, all students of the sacred letters must necessarily acknowledge themselves bound by an immortal benefit to those Tiberian rabbis. XVIII. They add, moreover, the testimony of Aben Ezra, whose antiquity and authority are greater. He, in his commentary on Exod. 25 [31], has these words: "I have seen in the books which the wise men of Tiberias examined, concerning which some of their elders would swear that they three times diligently considered every word, every punctation, and every term, full or defective; and behold, a yod was written in the word te'aseh ['it shall be made']; but I did not find it so in the books of Spain and France, nor in those beyond the sea." Readers will easily perceive that they labor under a great penury of testimonies who produce ones of this kind, which are so far from bringing succor to the opinion they would have asserted that they plainly confirm the contrary. They say, I confess, "that the Tiberians examined those codices in order to know whether the punctation answered exactly to the true pronunciation." But nothing of the kind occurs in the author himself. He affirms only that they examined those codices in order to know, namely, whether they were exactly written. Nor was the question he agitates about any point, but about the letter yod, which he teaches ought to be in the word te'aseh, as appears from the accurate examination of those codices. He says, therefore, that some of the writers of those codices (that the authority of those which he himself had seen, in which the yod is found written in that word, might be the more clearly established) swore that they had diligently examined every term, every word, and the whole punctation; according, namely, to the norm of those ancient exemplars which they had in their keeping. Nor does it any more follow from this that they devised the points than the letters themselves, since they subjected to examination every term and every word, that is, all the letters, no less than the punctation. Rabbi Aben Ezra, therefore, relates nothing else of those Tiberian elders than that they swore they had most diligently examined the writing of those codices (one of which he himself had seen), consisting of letters and points. XIX. Fifthly, some urge the silence of both Talmuds about the points; not so the renowned Vossius, who contends that no mention of them is made in the Mishnah, but confesses that mention is made in both Talmuds. Two things, however, are presupposed in this argument: first, that both Talmuds are utterly silent about the points, for if they are mentioned in any one place anywhere, the whole force of this testimony will instantly vanish; and then, that from the silence of the Talmuds it necessarily follows that the use of the points was at that time unknown.

XX. First, then, learned men deny that any mention of the points is made in the whole Talmudic work. But since it is all too probable that some of those who use this argument have by no means read through those huge volumes so diligently that they can safely pronounce that the whole of them is utterly silent about the points, they point out certain places in which, if the points had then been known or in use, it could not have happened, themselves being judges, that they should fail to be mentioned. I answer: For my part I would by no means concede that the points had not yet been devised, even if it were established beyond contradiction [Greek: anantirrhētōs] that no mention of them is made in the Talmudic work, since it is most certain that there are very many things pertaining to the doctrine of the Hebrew language about which the deepest silence reigns in it; so that I could pass over this whole argument without the least loss or prejudice to the truth. Nevertheless, since there is no lack of testimonies drawn from both Talmuds which prove that the punctation was known at the time when those works were published, it is clearer than light itself that this argument is exceedingly feeble and plainly of no strength. There is no need for me to transfer hither the places which the most erudite Buxtorf produced in his Tiberias, and which his most learned son solidly vindicated from the exceptions of Cappellus. Let the reader consult what Rabbi Azariah has collected in Imre Binah, 59, from the Jerusalem Talmud, Megillah, cap. iv., and the Babylonian Talmud, Megillah i., fol. 3, and Nedarim, fol. 37. XXI. But they urge further that no mention is made of the points precisely where it would evidently have been necessary, if only the artifice of pointing had then been devised; for when the Talmudic doctors wished to distinguish the various senses of the words dabar and also banayikh ["your sons" or "your builders"], which signify plainly different things according to the diversity of the vowels which may be affixed to the letters or pronounced with them, they say nothing at all about vowel points, but by repeating the words themselves over again they show that they signified by pronunciation a distinction which they could not mark in writing. For how easy would it have been for them to indicate that this word, written or pronounced with kamets, or with tsere, or with holem, or hirik, signifies this or that, if only those points had been known to them? I answer: We concede that the vowel points were not of old distinguished by technical names. These were imposed upon them at the discretion of the grammarians long afterwards; so it has been done in other languages also. The Masoretes very often call tsere simply kamets, and sometimes kamets katon, those names being not yet distinguished. Segol likewise is called by them patach katon, and kibbuts, shurek. The open sounds which they denote were their first names. Although, therefore, the Talmudic doctors could perhaps not designate the vowels by those names by which the later age of the grammarians distinguished them from one another, yet nothing hinders us from reckoning that they knew how to write their figures. Next, how can it be proved that the Talmud was written without points? The Jews constantly assert that the Mishnah, more ancient than either Talmud, was anciently pointed, although for many centuries those codices have nowhere appeared; [ p. 370 ] until very recently it was again carefully edited at Amsterdam. Now the whole force of this argument hangs on the assumption that they did not use points in writing at that time; which is the very point at issue [Greek: en archē, the thing assumed at the start]. Furthermore, the renowned Buxtorf showed long ago that in those places where the various sense of those little words is treated, the train of the discourse is such that there is no need of the addition of points to assign the distinction. XXII. Sixthly, Cappellus draws an argument against the antiquity of the punctation from the number of the vowels and accents, which is said to be laborious and useless. The vowels are fourteen; many of them, or at least some, are said to be useless, since there are not so many distinct sounds in human speech. Then they say that no account can be given of several of the accents, since they signify nothing at all, distinguish nothing, exhibit no meaning, and are of no help or assistance in learning the language or in reading. The points of both kinds, therefore, vowel and accentual alike, seem to have been a rabbinical invention [Greek: heurēma], and one not even well fitted to the very purpose it serves. But there are not wanting others who think they can reach the same end from a consideration of the number and distinction of the vowels by a means plainly contrary. For, they say, those points exhibit all the open sounds or vowels so exactly and accurately that they appear an invention too artfully devised to be ascribed to the first inventors of letters, whose care was to express only what was necessary. Thus in the judgment of some they are superfluous and useless; of others, accurately fitted to the vowel sounds; of neither, for reasons plainly contrary, divine. But the solution of both these petty objections is easy. Those who acknowledge a most accurate perfection in this punctation have without doubt no just cause for removing it from the divine order. The more perfect anything is in its kind, the more divine it ought to be reckoned. And the most learned men judge, not without the weightiest reasons, that the Hebrew language, the most ancient and most perfect of all languages, was put forth as the norm of the others by God himself and by divine men furnished with the prophetic spirit. Nor would I deny that there is force in this reasoning, did we not assign the origin of the points to a most perfect wisdom, comprehending all things at once. But the discourse of those who plead that several of the vowels and accents are useless is plainly futile, and proud. How, pray, will those petty doctors ever make us believe that everything in the sacred letters whose reason they do not grasp is useless? Is this to deal seriously? Is this to argue? "We do not understand the reason, use, and utility of many of the accents; therefore they are superfluous, and were devised by the rabbis at their discretion." It would indeed be easy to settle any dispute whatever in that fashion. But ought we to make determinations about the word of God from our own ignorance and want of knowledge? Will that, in the end, be reckoned fair? For my part I would rather give my faith to the man who professed to know nothing than to him who professed to know everything. What if some one man, utterly unknown to us, has been helped by some accent in perceiving the sense of even the most minute particle [ p. 371 ] in the sacred letters, or directed in reading in that manner which God approved while the carnal institutions of the Jewish church flourished; shall we manikins, to whom, among innumerable other things, that use is unknown, judge it useless? Beyond doubt, had not learned men determined beforehand with themselves, for other reasons, that the punctation was not of divine origin, they would not have endured to use that audacious cavil in this cause. "The Jews indeed think that no one exists today to whom the complete rationale of the accents is known," writes Sebastian Munster, in the epistle prefixed to the commentary of Rabbi David Kimchi on Amos. Elias Levita, indeed, in a Hebrew epistle to the same Munster, testifies that he wrote a book on the rationale of the accents, which perished at Rome. If that writing had been published, perhaps learned and modest men would possess something of whose knowledge they would by no means repent. XXIII. Seventhly, they contend that Jerome also knew nothing of the points, since, as they say, he nowhere made any mention of them. They suppose, it seems, that that most learned man committed to writing everything he knew, whether he had any occasion of doing so or not; in which opinion I cannot agree with them. Next, I would have them show what reason Jerome had for relating that the Hebrews in writing their language used not only letters but vowels also, when all the other nations known to him did the same. There is no ancient historiographer who has handed down to memory more, and more certain, things about the affairs of the Carthaginians than Polybius of Megalopolis. He shows everywhere not only that he understood the Punic language, but also that he had read their records. For when in the third book of his history he had accurately taught with what forces Hannibal entered Italy, he affirms that he gathered all that he related from bronze tablets inscribed by Hannibal himself. And yet this Polybius nowhere in his histories mentions the unusual manner of writing from right to left which the Carthaginians used, of which nevertheless he himself could not have been ignorant. Little help, therefore, is to be expected for this opinion from the silence of Jerome. For it is certain that he could have known the points and not mentioned them in his writings; it is certain that they could have existed and yet escaped him, since many copies were always written out without them; it is certain also that Jerome obtained the use of very few copies, and employed but one or two masters; so that it ought to seem by no means strange if he had never seen a pointed codex; and there were no grammars, nor had anyone at that time cultivated the technical art of that language. To lean on his silence, therefore, is to contend against the truth not with arguments or judgments, but with most uncertain conjectures. But neither are testimonies wanting, drawn from the works of Jerome, which openly indicate that he had both seen and known the points of both kinds. Elsewhere I have cited that place in the epistle to Evagrius: "The Hebrews very rarely use the vowel letters in the middle." The same words are cited in the same sense by Rabbi Azariah in Imre Binah, cap. lix., [ p. 372 ] by Simeon de Muis, Defensio Veritatis Hebraicae, and by Joseph de Voysin, preface to the Pugio Fidei. They answer that Jerome called the letters aleph, vav, yod vowel letters, since in other languages they perform that office. True, yod and vav are vowels, namely when they have points adjoined; although very often they take the place of consonants, otherwise than happens in the Greek language. That aleph was ever a vowel is not yet proved; it is also frequently pronounced with a sound different from that which it would pour forth if it were a vowel. Those bare letters, therefore, are not the vowels Jerome indicates. Then, in his exposition of the third chapter of Jonah he has these words: "I wonder much why it was so translated, since in the Hebrew there is no community either of letters, or of syllables, or of accents, or of the word." I know not whether anyone could make more open mention of the accents; and no one will say that these were in use before the vowel points. So on Gen. 19:33, at the word be-qumah ["when she arose"], "They point it above," he says, "as though it were incredible that anyone should lie with a woman without knowing it," observing the irregular punctation [the extraordinary point over the word]. And on Ezek. 27: "Hebrew nouns are frequently interpreted variously according to the diversity of the accents and the change of the letters and vowels, which among them most especially have their own proprieties." And in the epistle to Paula and Eustochium, or the preface to Isaiah, he affirms that that prophecy "is written among the Hebrews in cola and commata." In the Hebrew Questions on Gen. 47:31: "And in this place," he says, "some feign to no purpose that Jacob adored the top of Joseph's scepter, namely that in honoring his son he adored his power; whereas in the Hebrew it reads far otherwise: And Israel adored toward the head of the bed." The word about which the question arises is mittah ["bed"]. Jerome knew that the apostle had rendered that word by "staff" [Greek: rhabdos] (Heb. 11:21). If it had not been so pointed that it ought really to be rendered "bed," how, pray, would he have dared to say "in the Hebrew it reads otherwise," when without points it does not read otherwise? Ought Jerome to have hearkened to his Nicodemus [his Jewish instructor] against the translation employed by the apostle himself, or the sense of the place rendered by him, and boldly pronounce "in the Hebrew it reads otherwise"? which, unless we concede that he knew the points, is most false. It is therefore sufficiently established that Jerome knew the vowel points and the accents, and made more open mention of them than was to be expected from a man who had no other occasions of recording them. But some say that Jerome could have learned from common usage that the word mittah was to be pronounced in that place in such a way as to signify "bed." But in Jerome there is nothing about the pronunciation of the word; he shows how it was read in the Hebrew, that is, by every reader inspecting the text; and what, pray, was that common usage which he is feigned to have followed? He employed, as we said, one or two masters. But that he preferred the whispering of an obscure master to the authority of the apostle is not credible; especially since the train of the discourse admits both senses; for it is most certain that Jacob adored God leaning on the top of his staff. XXIV. In the last place, some plead the kinship of the Arabic and Syriac languages with the Hebrew; [ p. 373 ] and these, they say, admitted punctation only very recently, and the condition of the Hebrew language in antiquity seems to have been the same as theirs. I answer: At what time the Arabs and Syrians first obtained the use of letters, and at what time the use of points, is plainly uncertain. That the ancient Arabic character fell out of use centuries ago is shown by the renowned Golius, preface to Surah 31 of the Koran. What other changes the vicissitudes of times and of all things have introduced into that language we plainly do not know. Perhaps the points also passed into desuetude, and were recalled into common use by the Saracens. In truth, as to when the vowel points first began to be in use among the Arabs and Syrians, there exists no testimony worthy of credit. Suppose, too, that all those oriental languages lacked vowel points from the beginning; what hinders Ezra from being reckoned to have added them in the use of the sacred language? XXV. And these are the chief heads of the arguments by which learned men think they have proved that the Hebrew punctation was an invention [Greek: heurēma] of the post-Talmudic rabbis; and those who suffer themselves to be cast out of the possession of so great a treasure by such arguments shall by no means have me for a companion. XXVI. Many also judge that from that care which Ezra applied to the sacred Scriptures proceeded that well-known variation in the sacred Bible which goes under the name of Keri and Kethib. It does not please me to repeat here what we have written elsewhere on the nature and number of those variant readings. There are those who ascribe this variety to the first amanuenses of the Holy Spirit; of these, some think a mystery lies beneath it; others, that the ambiguity of the bath kol [Hebrew: bat qol, the "daughter of a voice"] was its cause. There are those who assert that the Kerioth are Masoretic notes, partly various readings collected out of divers codices, partly critical corrections of the text; this is the opinion of men who care little what is thought of the purity of the Hebrew codex, which they call Masoretic, as if there were need here of a distinguishing label, or as if any other biblical codex had ever been in the use of men. Having read through the opinions and arguments of many learned men, I confess that here my stream runs aground; yet I am glad to add what Isaac Abarbanel has on this matter, in his preface to the prophet Jeremiah. Having rejected the less convenient opinion of others, he adds: "But the matter seems to me truly to stand thus: Ezra, with the men of the great synagogue, found the books perfect and entire, just as they had been written; but before he instituted the punctation and the accents and the distinction of the verses, he attended to the reading; and if anything occurred to him alien from the nature or propriety of the language, and from the rules of grammar, he reckoned with himself that it had come about for one or the other of two causes. First, that the scribe in these irregular forms had regard to some one of the mysteries of the law, according to the dignity of his prophecy and the profundity of his wisdom, and therefore he did not dare to attempt to delete anything from the divine books; for he understood that those books had been written with singular wisdom, and that the defective and redundant letters [ p. 374 ] and outlandish expressions of that kind had not been written without cause; wherefore he left them in the text just as they had really been written, but in the margin he placed the Keri, or the exposition of this exotic writing according to the propriety of the language and the simple sense... It may also be that Ezra thought that there existed in the sacred Scripture letters and expressions irregularly written for no other cause than that he who uttered them had not expressed them correctly, as was proper, whether through want of skill in the Hebrew language or through want of skill in orthography; ... and therefore it was needful that the true writing and reading of the expression according to the rules of grammar should be set forth. This is the rationale of the Keri, which the holy scribe placed in the margin, fearing to lay hands on the words of authors speaking by the inspiration of the Holy Spirit."

Chapter argument: The remaining parts of the Ezraitic reformation of the church; the restoration of the solemn preaching of the divine word, Neh. 8:1, 8; its neglect before the captivity; Jehoshaphat's endeavor for its restoration, 2 Chron. 17:7-9; princes joined to the priests and Levites in that work; the diligence and success of the first reformers of the Christian churches in the same; the trifles of the Jews, and of others; the excommunication of the Cuthaeans, Ezra 4:2, 3; the separation of the people from impure mixtures. I. We proceed in the reformation of the church returned from captivity. In the third place we assigned to these reformers their supreme diligence in the preaching of the divine word, by which the returning people, who had long lain in the darkness of Babylon, might be instructed in the knowledge of God and in the right performance of their duty. There stands the history of this truly divine undertaking, Neh. 8:1, 8: "All the people gathered themselves together;... for they read in that book, in the law of God, distinctly, and by expounding gave the sense, and caused them to understand through that scripture." That duty of instructing the people by sacred discourses and solemn expositions of the word of God had been despised and neglected during the former defection, so far, namely, as it pertained to the ordinary care of the priests and Levites. The extraordinary preaching of the word by the prophets was so much a matter of divine administration that on that account the church was neither to be praised nor reproached. God had ordained that "the lips of the priests should preserve knowledge," Mal. 2:7. From them it was the duty of the people to seek the law of God, that is, the sense of the mind of God set forth in the law. The priests, accordingly, God severely rebukes for the neglect of this work, Hos. 4:6. On the Levites also lay the charge of instructing the people, Deut. 33:10. To Jehoshaphat, therefore, when he undertook to reform the church according to the will of God, nothing was more important than the care of the preaching of that word through the whole church; and since the priests and Levites were unequal to the charge, whether through fewness of number, or because very many of them were themselves entangled in the crime of a foul ignorance of the divine law, by common right he joined to them other men, princes and magnates, who had the reformation of the church at heart, [ p. 375 ] 2 Chron. 17:7-9. For the neglect of the administration of this charge is a most certain proof [Greek: tekmērion] of ecclesiastical defection; and exactness in it, not the least part of reformation. By the godly reformers, therefore, account was taken of this duty with all possible diligence. To that work also did those men apply themselves, not without admirable success, whom it pleased God to use in recovering his churches from the long servitude of mystical Babylon. II. The more recent Jews think that the Chaldee paraphrase is indicated by those words which we have brought forward out of Nehemiah. Some Christians also either accede to that opinion or show themselves akin to that delusion, having first determined to be enslaved to a hypothesis [Greek: douleuein hypothesei] in another opinion no less absurd. But we have shown above that the use and skill of the Hebrew language had not yet perished from the people, as they would have it. Furthermore, it is expressly said that these Ezraitic preachers first read the book of the law "distinctly," and then by "exposition of the sense gave understanding." To what end, pray, would that distinct reading have served, if the common people had not at all understood the very sound of the words? What is recorded, therefore, was a solemn restitution of the divine institution of the assiduous preaching of the word. There follows the excommunication of the Cuthaeans, or rather their rejection. The history stands at Ezra 4:2, 3: "They came to Zerubbabel, and to the chief of the people, and said to them, Let us build with you: for like you we will seek your God; for we have not sacrificed to another since the days of Esarhaddon king of Assyria, who brought us up hither. But Zerubbabel said to them, and Jeshua, with the rest of the chiefs of the paternal families of Israel, It is not for you to build with us a house to our God; but we who are together will build to Jehovah the God of Israel." It was not participation in divine worship, but the favor of the Persian kings, which the Jews then enjoyed, that these hypocrites courted. For if they had sought with a sincere mind to join themselves to the God of Israel, they were not to be rejected. But it was plainly another matter, as appeared from the event; nor, while pretending religion, had they anything else in their minds than what, so many ages before, the Shechemites had in a like case, Gen. 34:23. III. To the rejection of the Samaritans was added the separation from impure mixtures with profane peoples. That is related at large in the ninth and tenth chapters of Ezra, and in the last chapter of Nehemiah. And thus at length was completed that celebrated [Greek: diaboētos] reformation of the church, and the restitution of the Mosaic theology to its pristine place, honor, use, and dignity.

Chapter argument: The final apostasy of the Jewish church; its general occasions; the people mingled with the Gentiles; the entrance of philosophy; the various evils sprung from it; disputations, quarrels; sects; the forgetting of the holy tongue; the oppression and ignorance of the common people; the ambition and blindness of the doctors; the corruption of morals; versions of the Scriptures.

I. The grace of God in the restoration of the church was followed, in process of time, by the defection of ungrateful men, and that by a horrible fall and ruin. [ p. 376 ] The plan of our undertaking requires that we also set this forth in few words. The new church, founded by the prophetic ministry, retained while that ministry lasted, though not free from all fault, a purity pleasing to the merciful God. When that was taken away, the whole fabric of the church gradually inclined toward ruin. We shall briefly point out the rough places that drove it into straits from which it could never emerge, and then the nature of its total defection. II. From the time the reformation was completed, the people, crushed by the tyranny and oppressions of the kings of Syria and Egypt, dispersed hither and thither on every side by various calamities, and compelled even to bear foreigners in the holy land, was straightway infected with the contagion of very many evils. In those ages the Greeks brought forth into the light, far and wide through the whole east, that eristic philosophy packed with empty wranglings, which they had not long before been laboring to bring to birth, the arms of the Macedonians playing the midwife. This the doctors of the Jews also drank in. While the temple of Solomon stood, and they worshiped God in it chastely, with sincere and due worship, that philosophy was utterly unknown to all the godly. For before the Babylonian captivity no mention occurs among that nation of polite letters or of exotic sciences, with the single exception of the mathematics, which, received from the Chaldeans, they seem to have perverted to idolatrous uses. For all human wisdom is prone to pride and superstition [Greek: deisidaimonia], especially where it sits in a mind not subdued to the obedience of the truth. But that Greekish philosophy, gradually introduced into the church, very swiftly issued not only in a great evil, but in the destruction of the purer theology. For hence those whose religion consisted in faith alone and the observance of the commandments of God began to erect schools wholly unlike those over which the ancient prophets presided, to institute endless disputations, to tie knots, and to fly apart into sects, not otherwise than we know happened among the Greeks by reason of that philosophy. The names of Plato, of Aristotle, of Zeno were not more the badges of disputatious mobs among the Greeks than those of Shammai, Hillel, and others among the Jews. The Pharisees, Sadducees, and Essenes rivaled the Academics, the Peripatetics, and the Stoics; but the itch for disputing in the former proved more pernicious than in the latter, because they entered into quarrels not about goat's wool, that is, about figments which like spiders they wove out of their own brains, as those Greeks did, but about religion, about the worship of God, about the sense of the Scriptures, all of them things sacred and to be adored. III. Next, the people had in great part forgotten the holy tongue. This was the second fruit of the mixture with the Gentiles. Those who lived in the dispersions spoke Greek; those in their ancestral seats, Syriac. Thus the common people was little by little brought into the deepest ignorance of the law of God, since it scarcely understood the language in which alone it was written. Relying on that ignorance, some petty doctors did not hesitate, casting away all zeal for the fear of God, to raise their crests, to arrogate everything to themselves as if they alone were wise, to despise the people and lord it over their faith, to assume magisterial titles, and to contend with one another. [ p. 377 ] It would be far too long to pursue in this place the origins, distinctions, opinions, crimes, and pretexts of the sects. Since for the plan of the work I must move among things well known and already occupied by others, it pleases me only to touch briefly their heads. Let it suffice, then, to have summarily numbered among the causes of the general defection the ignorance of the common people and the pride, vanity, and blindness of the doctors, since the former were destitute of the very letter, the latter of the Spirit. IV. Add to this that, after the Hasmoneans had for some time gained the mastery of affairs, the theocracy [Greek: theokratia] divinely instituted being cast aside, they openly defected in political government to Greekish customs and rites. Greek were the names, Hyrcanus, Alexander, Aristobulus, Antigonus, of those who ruled the people after the Macedonian fashion. And from that source also arose no small evils. Since, therefore, the prophetic ministry had ceased in the church, while the people, exposed to profane mixtures with the Gentiles, had almost forgotten its own tongue, and the doctors, flying apart into various sects after the manner of the philosophers, consulted only their own honor, and there was no one to cut down the crops of enormities growing up daily, everything was openly hastening to final ruin. But before I recount the heads of this fatal defection, it is necessary that we say something of the versions of sacred Scripture, which very many judge to have had their origin about these times, when the people had lost its skill in the holy tongue; and there is hope that these matters can be dispatched in the briefest of digressions.

chapters.heading: Digression II. On the Greek Version of the Sacred Scriptures by the Seventy Elders

blocks.text: I. Concerning the Greek version of the Scriptures by the Seventy, which is commonly assigned to this interval of time, while the Jewish church was on the slope toward its general defection, I find it necessary to say what Protagoras of old, as Laertius reports, lib. ix. 51, said of the gods of the nations. "Concerning the gods," said he, "I am not able to know either that they are or that they are not; for many things hinder knowing: the obscurity of the matter, and the shortness of man's life" [Greek: Peri men theōn ouk echō eidenai, eith' hōs eisin, eith' hōs ouk eisin...]. For everything that is commonly said about that version is so uncertain, and so many and so great are the sword-fights of opinions about it, that the span of a life would scarcely suffice for the accurate examination of them all; and so we have seen what he once said come true: many men, themselves uncertain, contending over this matter. II. Very many agree that some version of the Scriptures was made in these times by seventy-two men; yet there are not wanting those who repudiate the whole history of it as damaged goods and a merely Jewish fable. Irenaeus and Clement of Alexandria assign that work to the time of Ptolemy the son of Lagus; Aristeas, the well-known father of the history, as found in Josephus, and Josephus himself, to that of Philadelphus; some, to that of Philometor. Justin, in his second Apology, is authority for the statement that Philadelphus sent to Herod, king of the Jews, asking him to send those interpreters to him. Such a lapse in history [Greek: anistorēsia], or chronological oversight [Greek: parorama chronikon], in so eminent a man [ p. 378 ] proves that he lent too easy a faith to the rumors which were commonly tossed about concerning that version. Most write that Demetrius Phalereus was the author of the counsel by which Philadelphus obtained the books of the Jewish law; but others judge that Philadelphus never used the services of that Demetrius in furnishing his library; for Laertius writes that he was held in great hatred by that king, and accordingly died of the bite of an asp near Diospolis at the beginning of his reign. Nor could the renowned Vossius meet this testimony otherwise than by asserting that Philadelphus founded his library while his father was still reigning, against the common belief of all the ancients; and even so the wedge does not suffice for the knot; for Philadelphus pursued Demetrius with capital hatred long before his father's death. Furthermore, very many almost dare to swear that six men out of every tribe were sent by the high priest of the Jews to Philadelphus to complete that work; yet there are those who will not believe, even on oath, that so great a number of learned men out of every tribe was then living at Jerusalem. Some will have it that they were not so much interpreters as prophets; among whom at one time was Augustine, De Civitate Dei, lib. xviii. cap. xliii., of which no one could ever have persuaded Jerome. Justin affirms that they were distributed into seventy-two cells, and that each one finished the whole work separately, in such manner that they disagreed in the rendering of not a single word; Jerome pleasantly laughs at the figment; Serarius and others lend faith to the story after Justin; more, with Jerome, deny it. Some write that the whole version was finished in seventy-two days; others repudiate that frightful haste. There are those who write that they translated the Pentateuch only, as Aristeas, Josephus, Philo, and almost all the Talmudic doctors; Espieres, Morinus, and very many of the moderns, that they translated the whole Scripture of the Old Testament. Some determine that the Jews rejoiced greatly in that work; others, that on account of the undertaking they solemnly instituted a fast, and execrated the work itself. Philo asserts that the Seventy made their version not from the Hebrew verity but from the Chaldee Targum, and some of the rabbis assent to him; Cappellus, and those who owe everything to him in the controversy about the points, that it was from a Hebrew codex written without points. Certain men say that the high priest sent to Philadelphus a codex, if not written, at least adorned with golden letters for honor's sake; most of the rabbis cry out that a codex of that kind is profane, and fit for no use. Some affirm that the interpreters were furnished with the highest skill in the languages, and used the highest fidelity in executing the work; others, that they deliberately corrupted the sense in many places for various reasons; Munster and Ximenes, that they lacked either fidelity or understanding. Truly marvelous are the things Jerome writes of this version to Augustine. And to Paula and Eustochium, in the preface to Isaiah, he expressly affirms that the interpreters were unwilling to betray the sacraments of their faith perspicuously to the heathen. Very many conjecture that the Greek version which still exists under their name is the very one [ p. 379 ] which they elaborated of old; Drusius contends, and not alone, that the ancient one perished long ago. If it survives, very many concede that it has been most vilely corrupted in numerous places since Jerome, among whom is Bellarmine; Morinus asseverates that it has been preserved pure and inviolate. Some reckon the whole tradition of that version a Jewish fable, as we said before. Heinsius, in his Aristarchus, cap. x., determines that the fable-mongers snatched the handle for patching it together from Exod. 24:7, 11. "It is said," says he, "that Moses, Aaron, and the seventy elders went up to God, and that upon the princes of the children of Israel he laid not his hand; for which last words the Hellenists have, 'And of the chosen ones of Israel not even one was discordant' [Greek: Kai tōn epilektōn tou Israēl diephōnēsen oude heis]. Hence, no doubt, the history of Ptolemy and those seventy elders... hence those famous stage-scenes which the blessed Jerome hisses off, hence that invention that no one in so great a number disagreed in expressing the sacred things." The conjecture of that most learned man would perhaps have pleased those who are not ignorant how skilled the Jews are as craftsmen in fabricating the most portentous lies out of the slightest occasions, had not a more recent Aristarchus pronounced him, after he had ceased to be among the living, a grammarian and a vain man, deserving ridicule for his arrogance. To subject these divers opinions, and the other sword-fights about this version, to examination in this place would be foreign to the work in hand. But since it is commonly referred to that time of which we are treating, nothing, so far as I know, hinders me from saying briefly what I think of it. III. That a version of the law or Mosaic Pentateuch was made into the Greek language about the times of Ptolemy Philadelphus, the testimonies of very many and a celebrated tradition establish. It is probable that the work was undertaken by the Alexandrian Jews for the sake of those who in that dispersion used the Greek language and were not learned in Hebrew. Whether some Targum, as some will have it, existed at that time is plainly uncertain. The greatest part of the people, certainly, used at that time the Syro-Chaldee dialect. There were therefore, perhaps, among them men who went before the Hellenists in this work of translating the Scriptures into the vernacular tongue. But what is said of this is mere conjecture, since no vestiges of any such labor survive. To the Pentateuch, in the course of time, was added what remains of the Scriptures of the Old Testament. That the whole work did not proceed from the same authors very many confess, and there exist certain indications of it. That the Targumic work was completed after the same fashion we shall presently see. But from whomsoever it proceeded, this is plain, that the work was undertaken and completed when the Jewish church was already rushing headlong to destruction; and from this it may be conjectured how much that version would be worth, if it still existed entire and uncorrupted. But whatever remains of that first Alexandrian version, Jerome himself everywhere shows that in his days it had been variously corrupted and interpolated. It would be well if from that time onward [ p. 380 ] the common Greek version which then existed had suffered no harm. However it came about, it cannot be denied that it now dissents from the Hebrew verity in innumerable places, and quite often gives words under which lies no tolerable sense; in expounding which, how vainly the interpreters labor there is no need to say; the thing itself is evident. Yet there are those who affirm that our Savior used that version, and so commended it to the church; just as a certain mass-priest wrote that he was accustomed to sing Masses. Of the writers of the New Testament this might seem capable of being affirmed with a greater show of probability. For many places of the Old Testament are recited in their writings in the same words which still stand in the Greek version, although very often they seem to differ in some things from the Hebrew. Very many have shown that this discrepancy is of words only, not of sense; some also, that the apostles really followed the Hebrew text where they commonly seem to have adhered to the Greek version. Most pleasing to me is the opinion of those who affirm that the Holy Spirit, the author of the whole Scripture, expounding his own mind at his own pleasure, carried over testimonies from the Old Testament into the New using the words that pleased him; and that the Christian copyists of the Greek version gradually inserted those very words into it. IV. There is no lack of most powerful arguments on which the authors of this opinion rest. It will suffice me to point out one in this place, which I know not whether anyone has hitherto observed. It is well known that some testimonies are quoted in the New Testament somewhat obscurely, so that it is not easy for everyone to discern at once from what place of the Old they are taken. It is certain that some persons inserted several of these into the Greek text in places where formerly there was no trace of them, and from which they neither were taken nor could be taken. Let those words serve as an example which the apostle cites in the Epistle to the Hebrews, cap. 1:6, "And let all the angels of God worship him" [Greek: Kai proskynēsatōsan autō pantes angeloi Theou]. Since certain men did not know whence the apostle had borrowed these words, they thought fit to insert them in Deuteronomy cap. 32:43; and so after these words, "Rejoice, you heavens, together with him" [Greek: Euphranthēte ouranoi hama autō], they added, "and let all the angels of God worship him;" of which words there is in the Hebrew text neither palm nor footprint, nothing that could offer any handle for inserting them; and that we may understand the place to be interpolated, immediately after the violent intrusion of those words they repeat that "rejoice" [euphranthēte], so that the sense of the Mosaic discourse might thus proceed in due order. But that the apostle did not bring forward those words from that place is made manifest, besides the fact that the words of the Holy Spirit, in whose name he brings them forward, are not there, by the preface which he sets before the testimony. "When," he says, "he brings the firstborn into the world, he says" [Greek: Hotan eisagagē ton prōtotokon eis tēn oikoumenēn, legei]; but in that Mosaic context [Greek: periochē] there are no words about the bringing of the firstborn into the world, the unfolding of which is the direct scope of the Holy Spirit in Psalm 97, in which that testimony was really spoken.

V. I shall add a single place, and that a most familiar one, from those words of Moses, Gen. 47:31, "And Israel bowed himself upon the head of the bed" [Hebrew: va-yishtachu Yisrael al-rosh ha-mittah]. The apostle shows that Jacob adored God leaning upon the top of his rod or staff; strong in faith, that is, though almost at the point of death. Those apostolic words crept into the Greek text, "And he worshiped upon the top of his staff" [Greek: Kai prosekynēsen epi to akron tēs rhabdou autou], as though, forsooth, the apostle had so translated those words of Moses; for that the Greek interpreters did not originally render mittah in that place by "staff" [rhabdos] is evident enough from this, that they constantly render that little word, repeated several times in the same history concerning the same person and matter, by klinē, that is, a bed, as may be seen, cap. 48:2, etc., cap. 49:33. But of these things, God granting, elsewhere.

chapters.heading: Digression III. On the Origin of the Targums, or Chaldee Paraphrases

blocks.text: I. To this interval of time, when the Jewish church was already rushing to destruction, very many assign the origin of the Chaldee paraphrases, or Targums [Hebrew: targumim], at least of some parts of them. Whether there were formerly more versions of the sacred Scripture into that language than survive has been made uncertain by what some of the ancients said about the Greek version of the Seventy being made from the Chaldee paraphrase. All the learned, Jews and Christians, confess that there never existed an entire paraphrase upon the whole Scripture, elaborated by one man or by several in the same time or age. There still survives upon the law or Pentateuch of Moses a threefold Targum. One of these is distinguished by the name of Onkelos; another by that of Jonathan Ben Uzziel; the third, for what cause I know not, is called the Jerusalem Targum. That which exists upon the historical books and the prophets bears the name of the same, or of another, Jonathan. That upon the books of Psalms, Job, and Proverbs is attributed to a certain Joseph the one-eyed, or the blind, whoever he was. Of plainly uncertain authorship are the fabulous and old-womanish commentaries which exist under the name of Targums upon Esther, the Song of Solomon, Ruth, Lamentations, and Ecclesiastes. The books of Ezra and Daniel never had a Targum, since from the beginning they were written partly in Chaldee. Elias Levita does indeed mention a Targum of Daniel in his preface to the Methurgeman; but the train of his discourse proves that by Targum he there understands the Chaldee language itself, not an ancient paraphrase; and accordingly he desires that a Chaldee grammar be drawn up according to the norm of the Targum of Daniel. That a Targum upon the books of Chronicles survives, Ravius indicated not long ago; and a copy of it having at last been obtained by the worthy Samuel Clarke, Architypographus of the University of Oxford (a man most worthy of a better lot for his manifold erudition), he is shortly to publish it; and thus the chain of paraphrases upon all the books of the Old Testament will be complete.

II. All that is said among authors about the time at which these Targums were elaborated is most uncertain. Josephus, Origen, and Jerome, those most skillful searchers-out of literary monuments of this kind, are silent. Sixtus Senensis was of opinion that some Targum was known to the ancients under the name of the Syriac codex. That codex, when at last published (if indeed it has been published), shows itself to be a Christian work, sufficiently different from those Chaldee paraphrases of the Jews. There are those who suspect that this Jonathan the son of Uzziel was, on account of the equivalence of the names [Greek: isodynamia], the same as Theodotion, the well-known interpreter of the Scriptures, whom all the ancients mention. But there is no one who records that that proselyte of Pontus interpreted the Scriptures in Chaldee, whereas his Greek translation was very well known. The ancient Christians, therefore, seem to have been ignorant of all these paraphrases. Some accordingly judge that they were all written not only after the destruction of the city and temple, but even after the age of Jerome. For it is known that he spent his life in those very places where it seems impossible that the names and deeds of these sons of Pelops should have wholly escaped him. The hatred which subsisted between Christians and Jews, by reason of which neither people deigned to celebrate the achievements of the other, is pleaded by some as the cause of this silence. That the common crowd on both sides was so minded is credible. But what is this to Josephus, the most learned of the Jews, who expounded most diligently all the achievements of his nation, keeping far off the causes of wrath and partisanship against the Christians, and who weaves a sufficiently long and honorable narrative even of the Greek version? What is it to Jerome, the most curious searcher not only of all books but of all Hebrew traditions? Why these men made no mention of these paraphrases must have some other cause. But I do not wish to cling tooth and nail to this argument. It is certain that Jerome did not see all the books and all the literary monuments which existed in his age; for neither the weakness of human wit nor the brevity of this life suffices for so great a thing. Nor is it credible that he committed to writing everything he saw or knew. We have also shown above that the argument drawn from the silence of certain ancient writers is not fit to deprive the antiquity of books or letters of their credit. Let the reader consult Petrus Galatinus, Arcana Catholicae Veritatis, lib. i. cap. iii. pp. 7-9; Martinus Raymundus, Pugio Fidei, lib. i.; Wilhelm Schickard, Bechinat Happeruschim, p. 37; Buxtorf, De Origine Punctorum, p. 127; Joseph de Voysin, preface to the Pugio Fidei; Cocceius on Sanhedrin, p. 226; Buxtorf, Abbreviaturae, p. 120; Morinus, preface to the Greek Bible; Hoornbeek, preface to his Controversiae Judaicae; Hottinger, Philologia Sacra, lib. i. cap. iii., p. 259; Rivet, Isagoge ad Sacram Scripturam, p. 119. III. Of them all I shall treat severally in few words. Among all the Targumists, if you regard the dignity of the work, the first place by the consent of all the learned is due to Onkelos. I know not whether the Jews are of the same opinion, who take such great delight in figments and lies, of which there is a smaller stock in him [ p. 383 ] than in the rest. But in what age he lived, and who he was, is not yet settled. Some judge that he was a proselyte, and lived in the days of Hillel and Shammai, that is, at the least a hundred years before the destruction of the city. To this conjecture subscribes Rabbi Azariah in Imre Binah, and the author of the book Zohar, col. 131: "Shammai and Hillel," it says, "did not teach Onkelos a word of the law until he was circumcised." Others make him a proselyte also, but the sister's son [or brother-in-law] of the emperor Titus, and so to have lived at the very time of the destruction. So the author of the Juchasin, p. 52. Which opinion Azariah expressly rejects in Imre Binah, relying on no other argument than that of the age in which, on the tradition of certain masters, he had affirmed him to have lived. Some suspect, from the similarity of the name, that this Onkelos was that Aquila who, as Jerome testifies, in a second endeavor translated the sacred Scriptures into the Greek language with strict exactness [Greek: kat' akribeian]. Others relate also that a certain Aquila, celebrated among the Jews, lived about those times, whom they think to have been this Onkelos. Since, therefore, nothing certain remains about the time in which he lived, anyone is free to opine whatever he pleases at his own discretion. But his Targum, whoever he was, is almost the only one among the extant Chaldee paraphrases worth reading. For it adheres to the text, very often rendering the words, and generally the sense, of the Holy Spirit, nor does it wander of set purpose into old wives' fables [Greek: mythous graōdeis], which is the solemn custom of all the rest. Led by this consideration alone, I conjecture that this paraphrast [Greek: paraphrastēs] lived before the destruction of the city; for from that time onward, every man of them, being utterly destitute of the Holy Spirit, trifled childishly and ridiculously. In what place or price the work stood among the more ancient Jews is wholly unknown; that some of the later ones made much of it is attested by the stitching together of that codex in which the Hebrew text itself and this paraphrase of Onkelos are written out in alternate verses; but that it was ever read publicly in the synagogues the Jews deny; nor is there any reason at hand why that should have been done, since from the last dispersion the Jewish common people was most utterly ignorant of the Chaldee language. But to judge that this translation, of uncertain author and uncertain origin, is of any price or authority where it differs from the Hebrew verity, is the part of none but madmen.

IV. He who turned the prophets (Daniel excepted) and the historical books into the Chaldee language is said to be Jonathan ben Uzziel. There is also extant, as we noted above, a Targum of the whole law ascribed to the same name. Concerning the time in which he lived, all that is brought forward is no less uncertain than what we have related of Onkelos. Very many of the Jews affirm that he was a disciple of Hillel; for to him and to Shammai, as the most famous masters of the sects, they delight to ascribe everything whose origin they do not know. They feign also that miracles were wrought from heaven about the beginning and the completion of this work. For they write that the beginning of the work was received by an earthquake, and by a bath qol [Hebrew: bat qol, "daughter of a voice"] thundering horribly against the author for profaning the Scriptures; its progress, by an approval given through fire falling from heaven [ p. 384 ] to consume the flies which by chance fell wandering on the parchment on which he wrote; and its completion, by the highest praises, not unlike those with which the image of Christ honored Thomas [Aquinas], crying, after he had finished the first part of the second, "You have written well of me, Thomas"; whether they believe it themselves is uncertain. Galatinus contends (bk. 1, ch. 3) that the same Jonathan who wrote the Targum on the prophets also translated the law paraphrastically [Greek: paraphrastikos]. He relates also that he himself had at that time seen a copy of that version, which was not made public until a full century afterward. And, what is a most certain proof that he reported in good faith what he had seen, he recites the opening words of that paraphrase just as they now stand in the printed copies. For copies of the Targum of Jonathan were of old very rare, nor could more than one or two be found among the Jews themselves in any province, as Elias Levita testifies in the preface to the Methurgeman. Rabbi Azariah also, in Imre Binah, ch. 9, affirms that the same Jonathan composed the translation of the Pentateuch and of the prophets. Those who are of another opinion rest chiefly on this argument, that it is established by most certain indications that that paraphrast of the law lived not only after the destruction of the city, but even after the completion of the Talmudic work, of which he makes express mention on Exod. 26:2; whereas that other, the prophetic one, lived before the coming of Christ, nay, as some will have it, a little after the return of the people from the Babylonian captivity. But by what weight of reasons they prove that that prophetic Jonathan lived in the times which lay between the return from Babylon and the destruction of the city, on which the whole force of the argument hangs, is not yet established. The testimony of the Jews, unaccompanied by any evidence of fact from circumstances, does not suffice for proof. There are also rabbis besides Azariah who, with Galatinus, assert that both works are the offspring of the same author. I do not wish to make this quarrel my own. The only thing that, with any show of reason, persuades that these Jonathans were different men is that the prophetic Targumist does not trifle quite so far as that most foul contaminator of the Pentateuch. What is to be thought of the whole work I will show in few words. In rendering the historical books he is modest; he rarely gives the text word for word, yet neither does he wander at will everywhere. Yet even in these there are places where he trifles so childishly and Talmudically that I can scarcely believe that a manikin of such audacity and ignorance ever set his hand to expounding the Scriptures before the apostasy of that church was complete. It wearies me to bring his most trifling ravings in here; let the reader make trial in the fifth chapter of Judges, and he will find that we have invented nothing. In just the same manner he carries himself everywhere in translating the prophecies of Isaiah, Jeremiah, and Ezekiel. Wherever the sense of a passage seems a little more abstruse and recondite, especially if it exhibits anything of the person, grace, or office of the Messiah (although he gladly retains the name of Messiah, and even sometimes inserts it where it does not occur in the text), he twists it with depraved invented allegories to carnal and [ p. 385 ] legal senses, boldly and desperately, that is, Jewishly. Let the fifty-third chapter of Isaiah serve as an example; from it the reader may understand how the blind Jews twist themselves and the words of the Holy Spirit, that they may extricate themselves from that rabbinic torture-house of unbelief. Into the rest of the prophets, as if he had settled with himself nothing else than perpetually to stray from the true sense of the words, he everywhere mingles the most perverse allegories with unbridled license. Expositions of genuine truth "appear, swimming here and there in the vast deep" (Virgil, Aeneid 1.118).

V. There remains the exposition of the law, which you may truly call a wagon-load of lies. For I do not fear to affirm that more monsters of fiction are not to be found in the Mohammedan Koran itself than that impure artificer of lies has heaped together into that paraphrase. There is scarcely anything of the most putrid old songs among the most foolish Talmudists which that impostor of signal audacity has not carried into his patchwork. That dunghill book is plainly unworthy to occupy any place among the expositions, commentaries, or Targums of the law. And all this I would have understood as said also of that other one, commonly called the Jerusalem [Targum].

VI. That Joseph also, who is said to have written the paraphrase on the Hagiographa, was truly blind. If anyone desires a specimen of Jewish audacity, blindness, impudence, ignorance, and lying speech, let him go to this author. The whole book of Esther, from head to heel, is befouled with the most impudent lies and ridiculous fables. The Song of Solomon swarms everywhere with Talmudic follies, and the Talmud itself is commended by name at ch. 5:10; nor is anything done more reverently in Ecclesiastes. The paraphrase on Job is without doubt a patchwork, blown together out of the attempts of several, and it does not lack rabbinic ravings. The book of Psalms and the Proverbs of Solomon have felt the contaminations of the impure man less. Yet I would not say that there is plainly no use in these Targums, since they express cautiously the things that are said of God in the Scriptures after a human manner [Greek: anthropopathos], and do not twist to others the places that pertain to the Messiah, as is the manner of the more recent Jews (for the name of Messiah occurs more than fifty times among the Targumists, as Elias observes in the Methurgeman), although they miserably corrupt their sense; and he who wishes to become acquainted with the Jewish fables may learn them from these.

Chapter argument: Other occasions of the final apostasy of the Jewish church; The cessation of prophecy; A summary of the occasions; The degrees of the apostasy; The first foundation of Mosaic theology; The defection of the apostates from it; The origin of traditions; And of the sects; The foundation of the oral law; The rise and nature of the oral law; Born after the destruction of the city; Jewish observances not continued in the institution of the evangelical church.

I. When the fullness of time was now hastening on, in which that great prophet, promised to the church from the laying of the foundations of the world, was to be exhibited [ p. 386 ] in the flesh, and when God had strictly commanded the people to attend to the law of Moses until he should come, and by that word had set, as it were, the final hand to the revelations of the Old Testament (Mal. 4:4-6), that prophetic and extraordinary ministry, which God had variously employed in the reformation of that church from its very beginnings, ceased entirely for several ages. From the death of Malachi to the desert preaching of John (who was not sent for this purpose, that he might recover that church from the ruin of the apostasy into which, by God's will, it was then hastening, but that he might prepare the way for the great builder of the new church), through four hundred years, no one was commissioned by God in an extraordinary manner to recall to a sound mind a people prone to every wickedness. Since, therefore, the church was destitute of prophecy, its Mosaic theology corrupted by the philosophy of the Gentiles, the common people mingled with the nations, forgetful of the holy tongue, leaning upon teachers who repudiated the Holy Spirit, poured out into the manners and vices of the age, gradually and universally falling away from all the principles and foundations of Mosaic theology, it collapsed into final ruin. The traces of its apostasy must therefore in the next place be briefly surveyed.

II. The first foundation of Mosaic theology was placed in the self-sufficiency [Greek: autarkeia] of the sacred Scriptures. This we have shown at length above. For after God had consigned his word to writing, he willed that it alone should be the norm and rule of all faith, obedience, and religious worship; and lest anything should be added to it under any pretext whatsoever, he strictly forbade it to the church, that is, to all its theologians. And this theological principle is the basis and fence of all the others. This the standard-bearers of the apostates had first of all made void. There was nothing in the doctrine or manners of the impious Pharisees which our Lord Jesus Christ censured more severely; and he teaches that they had brought innumerable figments into the worship of God and the observance of religion under the name and pretext of "the traditions of the elders," that is, of the masters who first dared to depart from the pure word of God. It is probable that immediately after the departure of the prophets, certain men who seemed wise in their own eyes, putting forward expositions of the legal customs and Mosaic institutions, obtruded upon the people new ceremonies of worship and new theological precepts. Once that window was opened, and a most ample field given to the vain wits of petty teachers in which to exercise themselves, others pressing in the footsteps of others, the arbitrary store of institutions and rites gradually grew into so great a multitude that their observance occupied almost the whole of religion. For when once a man has determined that the limits of the divine command may be leaped over by him, there is nothing to hinder him from wandering without measure. Now what the masters of one age invented, those of the next called traditions. These, after they had begun to be in favor and authority with the people, a people most ready to prefer the figments of men to the institutions of God, whatever had been newly devised by anyone was imposed under that name upon the observance of a credulous populace ignorant of the divine law. But when very many men in various places had bent themselves to this work, [ p. 387 ] there at length arose among the precepts and inventions [Greek: heurēmata] of the masters, which they called traditions, not only discrepancy and diversity, but (nor could it have happened otherwise) even repugnance and contrariety. So great also was the multitude of new fables that it was not easy for anyone so to perceive and comprehend them as to be fit to instruct the people in their religion and observance. Hence schools were erected, not rivals of those ancient ones in which the sons of the prophets devoted themselves to the worship of God and the study of the sacred letters, but rather of those which the Greeks had instituted for the perpetuation of philosophical disputations and wranglings. These, however, were nothing else than a confusion of sword-fights over the most absurd old traditions, and workshops of new ones. And as each master set over a school became celebrated (the most ancient of whom on record were Shammai and Hillel), his opinions, sayings, and dogmas were forthwith religiously cherished by his disciples and by the people as though they were divinely inspired [Greek: theopneusta]. It was also from the diversity of traditions that the crowd of teachers split apart into various sects. The most ancient of these were those three best known, namely of the Pharisees, the Sadducees, and the Essenes; to which Judas the Galilean, in the days of the census [Greek: apographē], added a fourth, as Josephus testifies, and the headless Herodians a fifth. The atheism of the Sadducees is said to have flowed from the saying of a single master wrongly understood. For when he, under the pretext of the greatest love toward God himself, had pronounced that no one ought to render obedience with a view to eternal reward, they relate that his stupid disciples, ready to swear by the words of their master, supposed that he had denied the resurrection and eternal life. So the author of Tzemach David, in his Chronicle under the second temple at the year 4460: "Tzadok and Baithos," he says, "were two disciples of Antigonus, and they erred concerning the sayings of their master, in so far as he had said, 'Be not as servants who serve their lord in hope of receiving a reward.' One said to his companion, 'Behold, the master in his exposition has taught that man has neither recompense nor punishment'; and taking occasion from this, they relate, they fell away from the faith." Now all those traditions tended to this, that either all authority should be plainly and directly abrogated from the precepts of God, or at least that they should be so interpreted that no one should be held by their command to render that obedience which God exacted, which comes to the same thing; while the arbitrary commands of the masters themselves were to be observed with the utmost exactness under penalty of eternal death. And wherever that Pharisaism flourishes, by which, through devised ravings and depraved interpretations, the virtue, efficacy, and perfection of the commands of God are enervated, while the arbitrary prescriptions of men, under whatever pretext, are severely enjoined, there, far from all true theology, apostasy flourishes and reigns. Nor is there need that I should show at length that the Jewish church before the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ was tinged [ p. 388 ] and corrupted with this poison, since the evangelical history sets it everywhere before the eyes of all. In this most pernicious error that horrendous and final apostasy of that church took its beginning. And this was the foundation of that oral law, which in the religion of the apostates has for many ages past filled both sides of the page. For after the medley of traditions had grown beyond all measure, and it alone was the delight of the blind apostates, destitute of the Spirit of God, and when their origin, as happens in a long series of things and years, was unknown, new masters arose who credited them all to Moses himself. I shall abridge the long fable as best I can. They recite it summarily in the tractate Pirke Aboth, chapter 1: "Moses received the law from Sinai and delivered it to Joshua, and Joshua to the elders, and the elders to the prophets, and the prophets delivered it to the men of the great synagogue." By this they understand the law upon the mouth [Hebrew: torah she-be'al peh], or the oral law. Rabbi Moses Maimonides narrates the whole fable more fully in the preface to the Yad Chazakah, that is: "All the precepts which were given to Moses at Sinai were given with their exposition, according to that saying, 'I will give you the tables of stone, and the law and the commandment.' The law, namely, is the written law; and the commandment, that is, its exposition. And God enjoined upon us to observe the law according to the commandment; and the commandment is what is called the oral law." He adds, moreover: "Moses our master wrote out the whole law with his own hand before he died, and committed a book to each of the tribes. And one he laid up in the ark for a testimony, according to that saying, 'Take the book of this law and put it by the side of the ark of the covenant of the LORD our God, and let it be there for a witness against you.' But the commandment, or exposition, Moses did not write, but commanded it to the elders and to Joshua and to all the other Israelites, according to that saying, 'Every word which I command you, that shall you observe to do.' Therefore it is called the oral law. And that oral law, although it was not written, Moses our master taught in its entirety in his consistory to the seventy elders. Eleazar, Phinehas, and Joshua, those three, received it from Moses; and Joshua accordingly delivered it to his disciple." And so he goes on to recount in a long order the names of those who, from the days of Joshua down to Rabbi Judah the prince, delivered to others this oral law received from their predecessors; and at last he adds, "Our holy Rabbi" (that is, Rabbi Judah) "composed the Mishnaioth." This, of course, is what he would have: that those futile, inept, absurd traditions, which that Rabbi Judah compacted into the Mishnaioth, were that oral law, expository of the written law, which they fable that Moses received from God on Mount Sinai.

III. That this elaborate fable is a rabbinical fiction has long since been shown [ p. 389 ] by others. Yet it is the foundation of the whole of present-day Judaism. For with very few exceptions, whom they call Karaites and execrate, the religion of the whole nation has for many ages rested upon this figment. But since no mention of this oral law is made anywhere in the sacred letters; since nowhere are the students of the law of God referred to it, but everywhere to the written word; since Moses was commanded to write, and did write, whatever he heard from God (Exod. 24:3, 4); and since this oral law serves no other end than to supply the defects of the written law, which God everywhere pronounces most perfect and absolute, it is evident that present-day Judaism is something plainly different from that religion which the Old Testament instituted and requires.

IV. But although that doctrine of traditions which we have censured, and which was the beginning of the total apostasy of the Jewish church, did at length produce this most monstrous figment, yet that it was devised before the destruction of the temple and the city, and indeed before the repudiation of the whole church and people by God, has been proved by no one with any weight of argument or with testimonies worthy of credit. Philo, Josephus, and the son of Sirach make no mention of this oral law, though very much of traditions. If it had been devised at that time, much less hawked about as the foundation of theology and of the worship of God, beyond all doubt they would not have passed it over in silence. Moreover our Savior, who severely rebuked the hypocrites for their many crimes and superstitions, and especially for their audacity in bringing traditions into the norm of God's worship and of obedience, would never have passed over this most pernicious figment in silence. That serpent, therefore, which now flies winged, a fiery flying serpent, had not yet been hatched from the egg. We do not therefore number this law among the degrees of this apostasy, since it was rather its end and terminus.

V. That the leaders and standard-bearers of the defection from God, the masters of the sects, brought into the church innumerable most superstitious figments and depraved customs marked with the name of traditions, and with them corrupted the whole of divine worship, both the evangelical history clearly teaches and we have proved before. There are, however, learned men who think that our most holy Lord, in the institution of his church, adopted certain Jewish customs and practices [Greek: praxeis] concerning the worship of God and the sacred things of religion. There are some who do not fear to assert that the Lord's Prayer itself, which he prescribed as the norm and exemplar of all our prayers, was taken from formulas most commonly received among the Jews. They confess, indeed, that Christ put an end to the Mosaic institutions. But they teach that he committed to the evangelical church, fortified by his own authority, certain observances which the doctors of the Jews had of their own accord brought into use, to be religiously cherished. Since, however, this opinion seems to me sufficiently dreadful, and contumelious toward our most holy Lord himself, before we proceed in the exposition of the final defection of this church, it is fitting to subject it to examination.
Whether our Lord Jesus Christ instituted any Jewish customs, prayers, or observances to be retained in the evangelical churches.

I. After the second temple was built, and the ministry of the prophets had been taken away, together with that of the other divinely inspired men to whom had been entrusted the office of restoring the church according to the norm of Mosaic theology, the teachers of the following ages, who wished to be reckoned "teachers of Israel" (John 3:10), held the most diverse opinions concerning the faith itself and the observance and ceremonies of divine worship; I suppose there is no man to whom this is unknown.
