Above all, turning deaf ears to that most holy precept of God, to which they were strictly admonished, in the very sealing of the canon of the Old Testament, to direct their minds exclusively until he should come who would expound all truth, namely, that they should give themselves over to the discipline of the law of Moses, they were wholly occupied in devising new rites and in interpolating unwritten [Greek: agraphois] traditions. So with prophecy ceasing, philosophy entering, and the aid of the Holy Spirit held in last regard, the whole church, corrupted by depraved superstitions, tended gradually toward ruin. Of the things which the teachers perversely established in religious worship and in the interpretation of the Scriptures, and of the pretext they used in imposing them upon the people, the evangelical history relates a number. Now after certain celebrated masters, whose names were sacred and venerable among a populace surrendering itself wholly to the faith of its teachers, had indicated some rite to be observed in religion, or that this or that was the sense of some divine command or institution, their successors, who had it in mind to enjoy the same authority among posterity, taught that these novel institutions were to be cherished with the utmost observance, as though they were from heaven [Greek: ex ouranou] and not from men [Greek: ex anthrōpōn]. Hence, as we noted above, the medley of traditions in a short time grew beyond measure. Thus at length almost nothing sound or uncorrupted was left in the whole of religion. This evil also was added to the heap by the passage of time, that what was of divine institution, what of ancient observance, what of recent invention, it was plainly impossible for a people wrapped in darkness to know or distinguish. While all sacred things among the Jews were in this state and condition, certain learned men suppose that our Lord Jesus Christ, when instituting the evangelical church and, according to his infinite wisdom, ordering the worship of God in it, transferred to his own use (so they speak) certain rites handed down from the fathers [Greek: patroparadotous], which those men at that time religiously observed in the worship of God, indeed almost all the rites he instituted, and that, confirmed by his authority, he delivered them to his disciples to be cherished until the consummation of the age. They add, moreover, that that most holy prayer, the norm and most absolute exemplar of all our prayers (for Christ did not command [ p. 391 ] the words to be recited, which we do not read that the apostles did either, says Grotius, Annotations on Matt. 6:9), which he taught his disciples, he composed out of formulas in most common use among the Jews. And their proof that these things are so is that among the masters of the Jews, especially in both Talmuds, those rites are recorded as observed of old which we Christians even now observe on the authority of our Lord Jesus Christ; and the petitions of the Lord's Prayer, very many at least, if not all, are extant there expressly [Greek: rhētōs]. For these things are not presented as though they were only then born or received among the Jews when the Talmudic work was completed; rather their antiquity and observance is asserted on the authority of masters and doctors who lived long before the destruction of the city; and, what is the head of the argument, they say that they were part of that oral law which, handed down from hand to hand, Rabbi Judah the Holy set forth in the Mishnaioth. Now since this opinion of learned men by no means suits my stomach or palate, nay, since I cannot deny that I dissent from it vehemently, chiefly for this reason, that since it is at best doubtful, nay false, the honor of our Lord Jesus Christ seems scarcely, or not even scarcely, to be sufficiently consulted in it; although my own affairs and public ones now compel me not only to hasten but even to rush to the conclusion of this work, I am not willing to pass it by in silence. First, therefore, I shall treat of the whole position generally; then I shall subject to examination, one by one, the objections [Greek: enstaseis] which some adduce for the confirmation of that position.

II. Let us see, then, first, what may be conceded to these learned men in this cause; and then what those things are which, at least until they are confirmed by other weights of argument than those they have hitherto used in this matter, or by testimonies worthy of credit, I cannot admit. I concede, therefore, first, that some sayings, indeed many, which are contained in the evangelical writings, may be found among the Talmudic rabbis, or at least sayings very similar to them. The same can be said in particular of several petitions of the Lord's Prayer. But learned men have noted, in their annotations on the Scriptures of both covenants, that twins even of these are found in the writings of the heathen sages.

III. Further, it is conceded that the same rabbis teach that certain rites were in use among the Jews not unlike those which the Lord Jesus prescribed to his disciples to be observed in the worship of God. These we shall consider afterwards.

IV. We also concede, in the third place, that the Mishnic doctors and the Gemarists do not produce those sayings, nor make mention of those rituals, as though they had only then become known and published; nor, resting on their own authority alone, do they affirm that these things were in use before the destruction of the city; rather they introduce the doctors themselves, who lived long before those times, each expounding the sense of his own age, or even of the oral law.

V. It is further also conceded that certain proverbial sayings, which were in common use among the nation at that time, were employed by our Lord in that [ p. 392 ] converse which he had with the common people, and accordingly were recorded in the evangelical writings. Of this kind were: "Physician, heal yourself"; "To him who has it shall be given"; "The first shall be last and the last first"; "He who has an ear to hear, let him hear"; and others of that sort. And as every nation is quite tenacious of its proverbial sayings, so we acknowledge that the Jews kept these and the like in memory through many ages, and entered them in their writings.

VI. These things, I say, we willingly concede; it remains therefore to be considered whether the proofs of these learned men accomplish anything beyond what we have conceded. And yet it is certain that this suit cannot be decided from these. For it is not yet established that those rites in question were really in use in the Jewish church before the destruction of the city, or known to that people in the days of Jesus Christ's converse on earth. In order, therefore, that the truth may be laid open, we shall premise some things which will render the explication of the whole controversy easy.

VII. First, then, we say that that oral law, which the Talmudic doctors commonly make the fount and origin of all traditions and unwritten [Greek: agraphōn] rites, is a horrendous figment, a sheer lie, wickedly devised to the total ruin of Mosaic theology and the eternal destruction of the Jewish people. For of that law no more mention is made in the whole of sacred Scripture than of the Muhammadan Alcoran. And those who should dare to stitch onto the word of God additions of that kind, of which that law in great part consisted, are severely execrated and devoted to extermination. Indeed that whole unbelieving nation, of stubborn temper, surviving and dispersed through the whole world by the most just judgment of God, owes its hardening, and its horrendous obstinacy in unbelief, to that satanic figment. For drawn away from the word of God under its pretext, and bewitched by the inveterate prejudices of carnal traditions, they neither understand, nor wish to understand, anything beyond the most inept fables. And this, I hope, will be admitted.

VIII. Secondly, of the rites and ceremonies which are recorded among the Talmudic doctors, and which are not expressly instituted in the law, there are two kinds. For either they pertain only to the manner of celebrating the instituted worship, with certain additions applied, drawn in whatever way from arbitrary expositions of the words of Scripture; or they are themselves new rites, plainly different from all divine institutions. That any memory, any monument, any palm's breadth or footprint of either kind exists, or ever existed, before the return of the people from the Babylonian captivity, or while that church flourished under the leadership or government of the prophets and the first reformers, no one has proved hitherto, and no one will prove hereafter. They all owe their origin to that interval of time in which that church tended headlong toward its final and fatal apostasy and ruin, or to that in which it ceased to be a church.

IX. Thirdly, we deny that our Lord fortified with his authority any institution that was purely Mosaic. That he abrogated them all, all confess. [ p. 393 ] Of those things which are founded in the nature of things, the reckoning is different. For since all the Mosaic rites were instituted until the time of reformation [Greek: diorthosis] (Heb. 9:10), and had only a shadow of the good things to come, not the very image of the things [Greek: mellonton agathon, ouk auten ten eikona ton pragmaton] (Heb. 10:1), they could neither maintain their place when that had been introduced whose foreknowledge they served, nor were they to be recovered again from that death to which they had of old been destined.

X. Fourthly, it is most plainly evident from the whole evangelical history that our Lord Jesus Christ, so long as he lived subject to the Mosaic institutions as a minister of the circumcision, most constantly rejected, without exception, all the unwritten [Greek: agraphous] traditions which the masters, whether older or more recent, had brought in, and condemned them with the utmost detestation. Whether they consisted in depraved interpretations of Scripture or in curious observances and rites, he repudiated them all without distinction, not without severe rebukes of those who were the authors to the people of observing them. It seems therefore quite incredible that out of those very observances he should have seized rites for his own use, as some love to speak.

XI. Fifthly, all confess that there are certain dictates of right reason, proceeding therefore from the best and wisest author of nature himself, which are by their very birth fitted to serve divine worship in every state of the church. These obtain some place among all men who in any degree submit themselves to the government of reason. These, then, our Lord did not "snatch up for his own purposes," but, lest their observance should thereafter depend upon the failing and obscure light of nature, he confirmed them by his authority and entered them among his own most holy institutions. And if any of these were in use among the Jews, it should not on that account be at once said that Christ received them from the Jews, since they pertain no more to the Jews than to the whole human race.

XII. Sixthly, it is certain that very many of the Jews, if not nearly all, who believed in Christ before the overthrow of the temple and the final destruction of the city, and who saw in it the final end imposed upon the Mosaic institutions, religiously observed them, most of them at least, and cultivated them according to the institutes of the law. For the apostles themselves, although they knew from Christ's forewarning that the temple itself, with that whole scheme of worship with which it was furnished, was shortly to be abolished forever, awaiting that day of the Lord's patience, did not shrink from Mosaic worship. Since, therefore, the Jewish church endured in the forbearance of God until the destruction of the city and temple, those who believed in Christ, unless driven out or put to flight by force, nowhere on earth withdrew from the synagogues of the Jews or from synagogue worship. Indeed, in those places where the unbelieving Jews began openly to rage against the faithful on account of their profession of the faith and the religious worship rendered to Christ, the latter, denouncing to them that horrendous judgment of God which he was very soon to execute upon the enemies of the gospel (see 2 Pet. 3:3-11), established separate assemblies. But that the Jewish church endured while the temple still stood, and was not utterly [ p. 394 ] rejected by God, the apostle Paul teaches in his whole epistle to the Hebrews. For he introduces the high priesthood, and indeed all Mosaic worship, as indeed to be abolished forthwith, but not yet abolished. Hence the state of the church, such as it would be when all typical worship was wholly taken out of the way, is called by him the world to come. He also affirms that there is yet a little while until those Mosaic rudiments, shaken by the death of Christ, should be removed, so that the unshakeable things [Greek: ta mē saleuomena] of Christ's kingdom might be established. Nor does it stand in the way that the apostles and elders and the whole church of Jerusalem had long before determined, from the mind of the Holy Spirit, that disciples from among the Gentiles were not bound by the law of Moses (Acts 16); for they themselves show, a long time afterwards, that all the believing Jews adhered to its observance (Acts 21:21, 22). In many places, too, believing Jews and those who had not yet given their names to Christ acted without distinction. Thus James the Just, who lived very many years at Jerusalem and was very well known to the whole people for his eminent and wholly unfeigned sanctity, was by no means suspected by the chief men of the city of being a Christian, because he diligently frequented the worship of God in the temple, as Eusebius relates (Ecclesiastical History, book 2, chapter 23). Nay, Grotius writes that this commingling lasted in some places down to the time of Hadrian.

XIII. Seventhly, when that church had now arrived at the final period of the divine patience, and the Jews, having at last everywhere become tenacious and obstinate enemies of the gospel, were stirring up persecutions against the disciples of Christ, and were greeting with mockery his coming, solemnly foretold and announced, to take vengeance for their unbelief and malice, very many of that nation who had previously given their name to Christ meditated defection to Judaism, as appears from Paul's epistle to the Hebrews. But when the temple was destroyed and the nation cut down, a great part of those who up to that time, along with the profession of the name of Christ, had observed the Mosaic rites and ceremonies, stumbling miserably between religions which no one could any longer practice together, partly betook themselves again to the camp of the Jews, and partly devised a new religion compounded out of both, which in truth not only corrupted both but utterly overthrew them. Of these were the Ebionites and, if Jerome is to be believed, the Chiliasts; all of whom, in the course of time, casting off the title which for a while they had borne in vain, passed over into the Jewish roll. And as all know that they attempted to bring Jewish rites into that Christianity which they had embraced, so it is more than credible that, defecting to Judaism, they carried back with them certain Christian things. But the darkness in which the affairs of the Jews were at that time wrapped prevents us from having a clear view of what was transacted in their cloisters and hiding places.

XIV. Eighthly, the remaining Jews (that is, whatever of the nation survived the Flavian slaughter), driven out and put to flight from every solemn Mosaic assembly, wandered straggling hither and thither, utterly uncertain what they should do, by what rite they should worship God, where they should plant their foot. That they set up a Sanhedrin, or rather a school, [ p. 395 ] or a den of the most absurd sword-fights, here and there, others have shown. But that whole history rests upon conjectures, and upon the testimonies of the rabbis, which are more uncertain than any conjectures. While Hadrian held the empire of the Romans, deceived by the work and cunning of a notorious scoundrel whom they call Bar-Cocheba, and driven by the goads of their crimes, or rather by the divine curse, flying together from every quarter into Judea and Galilee, they stirred up a war against the Romans sufficiently deadly to both sides. But conquered at last, and visited with a slaughter less only than that by which they had been mown down in the destruction of the city and temple, and not yet understanding the most just indignation with which God pursued the whole nation in every land, yet broken in strength and spirit, partly put to flight, partly, under sentence of death proclaimed unless they fled, they were one and all ordered into exile from the whole of Judea. What they did in their hiding places from that time and through the following century is buried in murky night. At length there burst forth that most famous Rabbi Judah, Hakkadosh namely, the author and prince of that superstition by whose crime they are even now held everywhere in the world burdensome to God and men. He set his hand to the fashioning of that new Jewish religion which, augmented by innumerable superstitions, obtains among the whole nation. For being set over a school, when he had observed with how great uncertainty everything pertaining to religious worship and observance fluctuated, out of ancient traditions, new figments, and observances which at that time were in use among many, torn off and scraped together from every quarter, he composed a certain body or system of religious and of civil or forensic constitutions, which they call the Mishnaioth. Upon that book the expositions, disputations, quarrels, and fables of the rabbis make up the two Talmuds. For after Rabbi Judah had raised the standard, there was at once a rush to it from every side. Whatever impure and unlearned manikins could anywhere scrape together from old wives' traditions, from sayings of sacred Scripture miserably and blasphemously distorted, from the rites and customs of Gentiles and of Christians not expressly contrary to the Mosaic law, whatever could in any way be forced to serve their purpose, they carried here as into a Lerna of fables, lies, and every superstition. For as Muhammad patched together his Alcoran out of Jewish traditions, the evangelical history most vilely interpolated, heathen rites, and figments of his own, so also those rabbis put together their Talmudic work. These things being thus premised, all of which will be found unassailable [Greek: adiablēta], since they are drawn from histories and from monuments of past events most worthy of credit, I shall state in a few words what I think in the cause proposed. Our opinion, then, is this: that those evangelical institutions of which any mention is made either in the Mishnah or in the Talmuds or in other masters of the Jews, and indeed all the purely evangelical passages which are extant in any of their writings, the Jews drew partly from the gospel itself, partly from the practice of the apostate Jews and from the traditions which flowed out of the Judaeo-Christian assemblies, partly from the mouth of the common people [ p. 396 ] among whom they lived, and from no other source. This opinion, I say, seems more probable than that other, which very dangerously asserts that our Lord Jesus Christ borrowed his most holy institutions from the cesspools of Judaism; nay, I judge it to be most true, and exposed to no exceptions which carry any weight or moment. For since the blind and stubborn apostates, having lost their ancestral religion, the worship of God, and their fatherland (for whoever thinks that the Talmudic religion, or present-day Judaism, is that which is set forth in the law and the prophets is a stranger in these matters), most utterly destitute of the Spirit of God, bent all their strength to this, that out of traditions and most uncertain rumors they might devise and erect a new method of worshipping God and new rites of worship, there is surely no reason why it should seem strange to anyone that they snatched up for their own purposes certain things which were in use among Judaizing Christians, or at least things very similar to them. All wonder will be still further removed if we consider that some of the patchers-together of the new superstition were Christian apostates, which is surely neither incredible nor improbable. For it is from this cause that so many traces of the evangelical history survive in the Alcoran, namely that its author was assisted by a certain Sergius, a Nestorian monk, who, falling away from Christianity, was taken into a share of the work.

XV. But let us weigh the force of the opposite opinion. On whose authority, I ask, may we certainly know that those formulas of prayers which are now extant among the Jews, or those rites which are not expressly commanded in the law, were in use before the destruction of the city? The Jews, they say, constantly affirm that they received those things from Moses himself by means of the oral law. But that that oral law is a most impudent figment, of which, before the final apostasy of the people and the destruction of the nation, neither footprint nor any memory exists, we have proved above. And things which owe their origin to that oral law could not be more ancient than that law. But they say the Jews had stated times of prayer; I grant it: in Acts 3:1 Luke records "the hour of prayer, the ninth hour." That these daily times were three, Maimonides teaches in the Mishneh, book 2, tractate on Prayer and the Blessing of the Priests, chapter 1; but he denies that they were instituted by the law. "Moreover," he says, "neither the number of the prayers, nor the obligation to this or that prayer, nor any fixed or definite time of prayer, is from the law." Thus they had three prayers daily, of which the first was called shacharith, or the morning prayer; the second minchah; the third arbith, the evening prayer. That certain ancient Christians observed those three times, "without prejudice, plainly, to the complete liberty of praying always, and everywhere, and at every time," as he himself speaks, Tertullian teaches us in his book On Fasting. But what then? Did they therefore also have prescribed formulas of prayers? And whence will that be established? Maimonides indeed relates that Gamaliel, whose age and time of life are known from sacred history, composed a prayer for the extirpation of the minim, or heretics, that is, of the Christians; how well that squares with the counsel which Luke ascribes to him in Acts 5:38 [ p. 397 ] cannot easily be understood. That the Jews, therefore, had prescribed prayers of human devising before the destruction of the city, there is neither semblance nor color of proof. But the Samaritans, they say, affirm that a book of prayers written by Moses himself, transmitted to them, was preserved in the hands of the high priests of Gerizim down to the pontificate of a certain Adrianus. Let Apella the Jew believe it; but no one is willing; for all the Jews with one mouth detest this most impudent lie. These are the men who invented an eleventh commandment, well worthy to invent Mosaic formulas of prayers. To believe that, if a book of that kind had ever existed, the memory of it survived only among the Samaritans, an Assyrian colony, impious, hateful to God and to the church, "not even mad Orestes would swear it the part of a sane man." I grant indeed that most of the Jews hold that after the times of Ezra, when, namely, prophecy ceasing, that church declined into its final apostasy through contempt of the Spirit and of the divine word, the people used certain formulas of prayers in synagogue worship and even in that of the temple; but that the Mishnic or Talmudic doctors, or any masters of the following ages, knew with certainty what those formulas were, or retained anything of their memory, cannot be proved. For to suspect that that church employed in the worship of God those liturgies which are now in use among the Jews is foolish and impious. The same must be said of the rites. Before that impostor Rabbi Judah there exists no Jewish writer in the Hebrew tongue; nor in Greek, except Philo, Josephus, and Jesus the son of Sirach. Concerning these matters there is among them the deepest silence. And who will assure me that that Rabbi Judah or his followers did not lie when they intimate that these or those rites were in use before the destruction of the city, when I know that in matters and histories of much greater moment they lie most impudently? We are taught, with truth itself as witness, that even their fathers, better men than they, if ever there existed descendants of Adam than whom they could be better, invented many things foolishly and superstitiously. Among these men, however, there is no end, aim, or limit of lying; so that everywhere they seem of set purpose to strive with one another to snatch the palm in devising horrendous lies. There is no need of examples; those know it who are not yet washed clean in brass. But learned men say that in their own affairs they are to be believed. Why, I ask, when nowhere are they caught out as triflers and liars more than in their own affairs? But let us hear a most learned man pleading the cause of the rabbis in his own words, On the Law of Nature and Nations according to the Hebrews, book 2, chapter 2 [Selden]. "If to anyone," he says, "a doubt should perhaps here occur, whether credit should be given to the Talmudic corpus and writers in matters of this kind, in so far as they are historical, that is, in so far as things are delivered in them as law of whatever sort recognized and practiced among the ancient Hebrews, on the ground, namely, that that corpus, as it is now possessed in its woven form, and those other writers, belong to ages more recent than the destruction of the temple and city, such a one will perhaps also doubt the credit of Justinian [ p. 398 ] or of Tribonian, when in the Digests or elsewhere he sets forth or records the opinions and legal determinations of Modestinus, Papinian, Florentinus, Alfenus, Proculus, Celsus, and others of that kind, who are older than Justinian by three hundred years or thereabouts, not to say when he records the laws of the twelve tables, which precede even these by very many centuries." And subjoining things similar to these, he at last concludes: "To admit a reason for doubting, whether in law, or in theology, or in history, or anywhere else, on this ground alone (the other attendant circumstances in no way contradicting their credit), is nothing else than to struggle, too perversely and peevishly, against almost all antiquity and against the historical truth of things itself."

XVI. There is no one, so far as I know, who has brought forward more arguments, or more specious ones, to conciliate credit to those Talmudic rumors of which we are treating. The most noble Joseph Scaliger also has similar things. Let us see, then, in a few words, whether that distinguished man has attained what he intends. If indeed the matters which he proposes concerning the rabbis and the ancient jurisconsults proceeded on a like footing, the argumentation would be legitimate. But the case is plainly otherwise. It is most certain that the books of those authors, whose sayings, opinions, and responses in the civil law are recited by later writers, once existed, and indeed existed at the very time when those celebrated judgments [Greek: gnōmai] were copied out of them; or else they were taken from authentic memoranda, the registers of the courts, public commentaries and accounts. But the Mishnic and Talmudic doctors do not even pretend that their predecessors ever wrote anything of the kind which they everywhere ascribe to them. When several centuries had run their course since the death of those men, resting on uncertain rumors of I know not what kind, and on vain traditions, they bring whatever masters they please onto the stage at their pleasure, and these they do not so much report as openly feign to say or to have said this or that. If any pre-Mishnic books were extant, or perhaps had ever existed; if they rested upon any records of events which had ever occupied a place in the nature of things; if anything could be adduced beyond their own credit and authority which would be equal or fit to ground a conjecture that the things they record really were so; if the state or condition of Jewish affairs through several centuries left any room for the supposition that they had preserved in faithful memory the sayings and deeds of their predecessors before the destruction of the city, the comparison instituted would seem to have some force of just reasoning. But all things here stand far otherwise, as we have said. Then, how immense the distance between the credibility of the witnesses! Puppies smell one way, swine quite another. From those whose veracity has been evident to me in many things, I am unwilling to withdraw credit on account of suspicions or mere conjectures. But to surrender oneself in bondage to the belief of those whom one knows to be, in many things, nay in almost all, openly the most lying impostors, is scarcely the part of a man, not to say of one who loves the truth. Those ancient jurisconsults whom Selden mentions were grave men, learned, upright, and most celebrated through the whole world; they said nothing, wrote nothing, assigned nothing to others, [ p. 399 ] except openly, publicly, and in the light of men, where, if they had written anything otherwise than became the most holy observers of justice and truth, they would have been accused of falsehood by almost the whole world. Our Talmudic masters were blind, wicked, unlearned, given over by God to a reprobate mind; men who, as Joseph Scaliger rightly observes (On the Emendation of Times, book 7), seem deliberately "to profess ignorance of all good things"; Halophantae, Aretologi [professional swindlers and tale-spinners], babbling out mere idle trash in darkness and in hiding places; and no reason can be given why we should believe them in anything great or small. But let it suffice to have said these things in general; whatever is of any moment will come to be weighed in the consideration of particulars.

XVII. Let us look at the particular instances by which learned men contend to make good their opinion. There are some, whom for honor's sake I do not name, who assert not only that our Lord Jesus Christ, after the manner most received among the masters of the Jews, prescribed a formula of prayer to his disciples, but also that he took those very words and petitions, out of which he composed his most holy prayer, from formulas in most common use among them. Let us see, then, with what weights of argument, or with what testimonies worthy of credit, they labor to prove this; for to me, truly, the assertion seems scarcely decent enough. The disciples, they say, asked Christ to teach them to pray, as John had taught his disciples. And John, they add, followed the custom in use among the Jews, by which teachers delivered to their disciples formulas of prayers to be learned by heart. But that all these things are most uncertain, and propped not merely on a slender support but on none at all, almost a blind man may perceive. I grant that God prescribed certain solemn formulas of blessings to the Aaronic priests; and that after their pattern the Talmudic rabbis devised others; whether at first eighteen, according to the number of the bones in a man's back, as they trifle, I do not know; if their first inventors had regard to that, I know that they played the fool only after their usual manner. They at length grew to a hundred and eighteen, for trifling little reasons which it irks me to recount. That every celebrated master taught his disciples formulas of prayers is both incredible and without testimony. That John followed the example of those masters whom he openly and constantly convicted of ignorance, hypocrisy, and superstition, is asserted with too much audacity. To force John, much more the Son of God himself, into the same rank with the blind doctors of the Jews, is to me a matter of religious scruple. That the name of God be religiously invoked is the voice of nature, and an eminent part of natural worship. He, therefore, who undertakes to instruct others in the knowledge and worship of God must of necessity teach them to pray, that is, to pour out prayers to him. This John performed among his disciples; with what aids and helps he did so is uncertain; that he taught them to pray is most certain, and likewise for what things they ought at that time chiefly to pray. Our Lord Jesus Christ, who at the appointed time, according to the promise of the new covenant, was to pour out the Holy Spirit richly [Greek: plousiōs] upon his disciples, [ p. 400 ] who would relieve their infirmities in pouring forth prayers, set out beforehand, as it were in advance, a most holy summary of all the things they would ever, by his help, ask. For as the Spirit of grace works nothing in us except according to the norm of the word, so, lest any who rely on the Spirit deceive themselves, in all the duties for whose performance they are made fit by his help and assistance they must attend to that same word. It is worth subjoining the annotation of Grotius on Luke 11:1, "Teach us to pray" [Greek: didaxon hēmas proseuchesthai]: "Teach us a compendium of the things to be prayed for. For at that time they were not bound to syllables. So also it is credible that by John a summary of prayers pertaining to repentance was set forth, just as this prayer of Christ contains the heavenly kingdom. Hence it is called by Tertullian the breviary of the whole gospel, by Cyprian the compendium of heavenly doctrine." Thus Grotius. Cornelius a Lapide is of the same opinion on Matt. 6:9. "Christ here delivers to Christians," he says, "a manner of praying; yet he does not command that we should pray in precisely these words, but only teaches the things which are to be asked of God, and in what order, and with what brevity of words they are to be asked." And on Luke 11:2 he notes from Jansenius that Christ in this prayer set forth a synopsis of the whole Christian doctrine; and that therefore Matthew, by prolepsis or anticipation, joined it to the Lord's sermon on the mount, which he delivered at the beginning of his preaching. For he is vehemently deceived who supposes that Christ twice taught his disciples the same formula of prayer. But that our Lord accommodated to his own purpose, with slight alteration, formulas in use among the Jews, as some love to speak, is too audacious an assertion. Vain things and trifles are the ravings and dreams of the Talmudic rabbis, which some use to build up credit for this figment. A certain Longus of our nation cites in this cause "Josephus Albo Ikkar," as though Ikkar were the name of the man who wrote the book to which he gave the title Sefer Ikkarim [Book of the Roots]. But in these matters error has great precedents. Baronius himself, at the year 34, number 134, by a similar error with the contrary outcome, supposed Alphes to be the name of a certain rabbinical book, whereas it was the name of the writer himself, who is called Rav Alphes. And to him he adds "Jacobus Turim," whereas that Rabbi Jacob wrote a book, Arba Turim, that is, "of the four orders"; which is the same, says Casaubon, as if one should cite among authors "Tullius de Natura," because Marcus Tullius wrote books on the nature of the gods.

XVIII. Without doubt that most absolute exemplar of all prayers, and most exact compendium of the cultivation of our communion with God, flowed into the knowledge of the Christianizing Jews [Greek: christianizontōn], who, at last falling utterly away from the profession of the faith, nevertheless retained the use of certain things which they had learned among the Christians, things not running counter to the Mosaic rites. From them, and from no other source, came that very slender rumor of the petitions of the Lord's Prayer which the Talmudic rabbis seem to have possessed.

XIX. Further, the most distinguished Selden, in On the Sanhedrins of the Ancient Hebrews, book 1, endeavors with many arguments to prove that Christ borrowed the rite of baptism from that which was at that time in use among the Jews. Others attempt the same. Among the Jews there were two kinds of proselytes. The first, gerei toshav, or "proselytes of the dwelling"; and the second, gerei tzedek, or "proselytes of righteousness." The proselytes of righteousness were partakers of all ecclesiastical privileges, and came fully into the roll of the people of God. Of these was Ebed-melech (Jer. 39:16-18). Learned men teach that all these, at their admission into that state, were not only circumcised but also baptized; and they confidently affirm that no one ever became a proselyte of righteousness, however much he was circumcised, unless he was besides washed with baptism. But that circumcision alone was needed in order that anyone might become a partaker of all the privileges of that church, the express testimonies of sacred Scripture teach. For thus stands the law (Exod. 12:48): "If a stranger sojourn with you who would keep the passover to Jehovah, let every male of his be circumcised, and then let him come near to keep it, and he shall be as one born in the land; and let no uncircumcised person eat of it." Of rabbinical baptism, not so much as a grunt [Greek: oude gry]. But they suppose that this baptism of proselytes drew its origin from another legal institution. The same law, they say, applied to proselytes and to the native-born (Exod. 12:49; Num. 15:15); that is, they were equally bound by the same law to the performance of the same duties. But all the Jews, they say, were baptized. Whence, I ask, does that appear? From Exodus 19:10. Before the giving of the law Jehovah declared to Moses and said, "Go to that people, that you may sanctify them today and tomorrow, and let them wash their garments." Now the whole people having been solemnly baptized at this divine command, there was no need that this rite should be repeated among them. The proselytes, however, in order to be rendered conformable to the people, had of necessity to undergo that rite. But that was a washing of garments. By garments, they say, the whole body is understood. Whence is that established? The masters so teach. But there is no reason why we should believe them against the express testimonies of Scripture. Let us hear Selden himself, On the Sanhedrins of the Ancient Hebrews, book 1, chapter 7, page 66. "No one a little accustomed to the Talmudists," he says, "is ignorant that it is a most frequent practice among them to seek the grounds of their superinduced customs, received by human authority as a hedge or fortification of the sacred law, as they say, from some context and words of the sacred text, not as from an institution, but from an example or some analogy. And this is often done in such a way that, much more audaciously than is fitting, the words themselves are twisted, for the confirmation of customs of this kind, into a sense far other than the genuine sense of the sacred passage." That this has been done by them in the distortion of this passage I have no wish merely to suspect, since the thing itself is evident. Moreover, this washing of garments served that single occasion, namely the showing of reverence to the peculiar divine presence at the giving of the law, and did not pertain to the stated worship of God. That the necessity of the baptism of bodies, as a stated and solemn rite for all ages, should flow from a single washing of garments, and that resting upon a cause [ p. 402 ] never to recur to all eternity, of which observance, moreover, there exists in the whole Old Testament no memory, no footprint, and which is fortified by no divine command, institution, or direction, seems altogether improbable.

XX. That washings were also customary among the Gentiles for those being initiated into sacred rites, I concede. From this took its rise the ceremony among the papists of sprinkling with blessed water those entering churches. So Virgil (Aeneid 2.717):

"You, father, take in your hand the sacred things and the ancestral household gods; for me, come away from so great a war and fresh slaughter, it is unlawful to touch them until I have washed myself in a living stream" (Virgil, Aeneid 2.717-720). And again: "Likewise he carried pure water three times around his companions, sprinkling them with light dew and with a branch of the fruitful olive" (Aeneid 6.229-230). Now "carried around" is the same as "purged"; for the instruments of purification [februa] were carried around; the distinction between aspersion and ablution is taught by Macrobius, Saturnalia, book 3, chapter 1. Lucian mentions this rite in the Menippus, or Necromancy, 7. "About midnight," he says, "leading me to the river Tigris, he purged me and wiped me clean, and purified me round about with a torch and a sea-onion and many other things besides." And see what Pliny has, book 36, chapter 4, where Timarchides tells of Asterius, who touched the altar of Jupiter with impure hands, and was for that cause struck with a thunderbolt: "Making libation with unwashed hands he touched the altar of Jove; wherefore the father burned him with the fire of the blazing bolt. Only what is perfectly pure may rightly touch sacred things." A threefold purgation was also wont to be performed, namely by water, by sulphur, and by fire. Whence Ovid: "Three times purify the old man with flame, three times with water, three times with sulphur."

XXI. But Justin Martyr in his second Apology affirms that all the washings which the heathen employed in their sacred rites drew their origin from our Scriptures through the perverse imitation [Greek: kakozēlia] of the demons; and likewise the custom of putting off the shoes on entering shrines or temples, from what God said to Moses when he drew near to the burning bush; which indeed seems not unlike the truth.

XXII. But these things are far from the sacred rite of baptism. A female proselyte could not be circumcised. In her admission, therefore, if anywhere, account would have to be taken of baptism; yet where the ceremonies of her admission and ingrafting into the people of God are recounted, there is no mention of baptism (Deut. 21:10-13). But Paul, it is said, expressly affirms that the whole "people were baptized unto Moses in the cloud and in the sea." I grant it; in the passage of the sea God solemnly dedicated the people to the Mosaic instruction. But that the Jews ever imitated, or were bound to represent, that miraculous sprinkling [Greek: rhantismos], is false. The institution, therefore, of a rite of baptism is nowhere recorded in the Old Testament; no example of it exists; [ p. 403 ] nor while the Jewish church endured was it ever employed in the admission of proselytes; in Philo, Josephus, and Jesus the son of Sirach no mention of it occurs; nor in the evangelical history. That rabbinical opinion, therefore, concerning the baptizing of proselytes owes its origin to the Tannaim, or pre-Mishnic doctors, after the destruction of the city. The most learned Schickard, On the Law of the King among the Hebrews, chapter 5, page 127, thinks that by it they wished to distinguish themselves from the Samaritans. "For a point of difference from the Samaritans," he says, "they added a certain baptism"; which he proves from Rav Alphes and from the Talmud itself. I rather judge that the Tannaite doctors borrowed this rite, unknown before that time, from the baptism of John, which was celebrated through the whole nation, and into which a great part of the people had been initiated. Justin indeed, in the Dialogue with Trypho, mentions a heresy among the Jews which he calls that of the Baptists; but he denies that those who used that rite were Jews properly so called. And the author of the Questions and Answers to the Orthodox proposes to himself this question, or repeats it as proposed by others: "If the baptism of John was not according to the law, as indeed it was not, how was it not contrary to the law, and how was it received by those under the law? And how were those who were under the law, and received a baptism beside the law, not transgressors of the law?" To which he answers: "The baptism of John was the prelude of the gospel of grace, and therefore it was also above the law." That opinion, therefore, of certain learned men, concerning a transfer of the Jewish baptismal rite, which in truth at that time did not exist, by the Lord Jesus to the use of his disciples, is destitute of all probability.

XXIII. Of the eucharist plainly the same things must be said. They suppose that that most holy institution of our Lord, with all the circumstances pertaining to its solemn celebration, was taken from Jewish rites of which no mention anywhere occurs in sacred Scripture. But that those rites were anywhere in use before the final destruction of the nation, we have seen to be not yet proved by any adequate testimony. And since others have discharged this task happily enough, it does not please me, in the great haste by which we are continually pressed, to do here what has been done already.

XXIV. The case of ecclesiastical excommunication is peculiar. We have shown before that some of the evangelical institutions have their foundations in the very nature of things, or in the determinations of right reason. These pertain to men, as men, before they pertain to Christians. Now of those things which are agreeable to the dictate of reason there was always a varied use among the nations, according as men submitted themselves more or less to the discipline of the natural light. Of these is excommunication. For right reason demands the exclusion from the privileges of the community of one who offends against the duties, rights, and rules of the community of which he is a part. Nothing occurs more frequently in good authors than the practice of this right among the ancients. That the Jews observed this custom, they themselves teach. Whether they did so by virtue of any divine institution, or by common right, we do not now dispute. That a custom of this kind prevailed among the heathen, [ p. 404 ] Caius Caesar, among very many others, is witness, book 6 of the Gallic War, chapter 13. "If anyone," he says, "whether private person or magistrate, has not stood by their" (that is, the Druids') "decree, they interdict him from the sacrifices. This penalty among them is the heaviest. Those who are thus interdicted are reckoned in the number of the impious and the criminal; all withdraw from them, and shun their approach and conversation, lest they receive some harm from the contagion; nor is justice rendered to them when they seek it, nor any honor shared with them." Hence some do not fear to affirm that this custom flowed to the Christians from the Gentiles. Whether anything more to the reproach of the ancient Christians could have been invented, I greatly doubt. That it came to the Christians from the Jews is the opinion of very many. Whether ecclesiastical excommunication was divinely instituted in the Jewish church, or whether any examples of it exist while the city and temple were yet standing, I do not wish to make my own quarrel. The Mishnic rabbis and the Gemarists frequently mention it. That our Lord received the use of this rite from the Jews or the Gentiles is false. Reason commands that men, living in concord and ordering their life according to some norm common to all, should dwell in communities. Whatever end or object they have proposed to themselves, sacred or civil, the law is the same. It is likewise equitable that all those who would use, enjoy, and delight in the rights and privileges of any society should hold the bond from which the society arises, and submit themselves to its rules. Otherwise the community cannot be kept in good repair, nor perpetuated. For him who refuses to obey, exclusion from the privileges and fellowship of the society stands ready: this too right reason commands. All these things pertain to the nature by which we are men. To suppose that the human race learned these dictates of reason, which it had cultivated for some thousands of years before the rabbinical name was born, from the rabbis themselves, is the part of madmen. But however much those axioms are founded in the very reason of things, yet since our Lord Jesus Christ willed that nothing at all should be religiously observed in his church except by his own authority, since he is its head, king, and sole legislator, he fortified by his own institution their appropriation and application to the use of the evangelical assemblies; and accordingly, although those principles or sovereign maxims [Greek: kyriai doxai] remain natural, since it is utterly impossible that what has once suited rational nature should not always agree with it, yet their application to those sacred things which depend on the pure institution of Christ is merely evangelical. Thus, after the apostle had proved from the very law of nature, and from the equity both of the nations and of the Mosaic institutions, that a reward is owed to the dispensers of the gospel for their work, he immediately adds, "so also our Lord has ordained" (1 Cor. 9:14).

Chapter argument: The second foundation of Mosaic theology; The free justification of the sinner before God; The defection of the Jewish church from it; The vain boasting about Abraham as father; The acknowledgment of natural misery cast aside; The impious deathbed vow of the Jews; The third foundation; Concerning the coming and authority of the Messiah; The most evil defection from him; A digression on the change and abolition of Mosaic worship; Heb. 7:12-15; All Mosaic worship rested on the Aaronic priesthood; Its change foretold; Upon it follows the abolition [Greek: aphanismos] of the worship; The Messiah the High Priest; Ps. 110:4; What kohen means; The Mosaic laws subject to change; The final defection and destruction of the Jewish church.

I. We proceed to the second foundation of Mosaic theology. The gracious justification of sinful man before God constitutes it. Its principal parts we have expounded above. That the Jewish church fell utterly away from it, the gospel itself clearly shows. For whereas they ought first, from an inward and deep sense of their natural misery and of their most abject condition in this world, always to have professed openly, "A Syrian ready to perish was their father," and very many other things to that effect, they, lifted up with excessive self-conceit, with proud mouth, on whatever occasion they found, thundered out that solemn formula, "Abraham is our father." With that empty boast they fortified themselves against all conviction of natural misery. Hence, at the beginning of his preparatory ministry, John the Baptist found it necessary to counter that prejudice (Matt. 3:9). "Do not think," he says, "to say within yourselves, 'We have Abraham as our father.'" But stubborn pride stood in the way of amendment. For puffed up with the vain imagination of that privilege, they everywhere boldly and obstinately opposed themselves to the truth itself, that is, to the Lord Jesus calling them to repentance. Of the "Syrian ready to perish," no mention; they never ceased to boast themselves "the seed of Abraham, the sons of Abraham," and therefore holy, righteous, and pleasing to God. In the bond of this same most pernicious error very many are held in every age, who, destitute of the power [Greek: dynamis] and efficacy [Greek: energeia] of religion, busy themselves to fortify themselves against the light of truth and the bites of conscience with the conceit of external privileges and the empty pomp of ceremonies.

II. Secondly, that foundation of Mosaic theology taught the free election of God, and the elect's own inability [Greek: adynamia] duly to render obedience to God. But to no particle of divine truth did the crowd of apostates oppose itself with greater spirit. There was scarcely anything which they believed God had freely bestowed upon them. They constantly gloried that they were free, that they had never come under the yoke of bondage, but were naturally furnished with powers fit for rendering obedience to God. And so they cried out that they were treated with the gravest injury whenever they suspected that they were being reckoned among the blind, the sick, and the guilty, who had need of a physician or of pardon. Now that error has another as its inseparable companion. For those who suppose themselves naturally furnished with powers for duly rendering obedience to God determine that it is discharged by external observances [ p. 406 ] and works. And those who think that the greatest or principal part of obedience and divine worship consists in external works and rites have no doubt that they are equal and fit for performing it. By the bond of both errors the apostates were held fast against the doctrine of this foundation.

III. Thirdly, they so obstinately repudiated reconciliation, the remission of sins, peace with God, and everlasting righteousness to be obtained through the Messiah, that they openly professed that no one could be justified except by the works of the law; and so they obstinately pursued the righteousness of the law, which they had never been able to attain. The spiritual redemption and deliverance from the world, death, hell, and sin they neither cared for nor expected. For concerning these things there is, among the true and genuine Jewish writers, the deepest silence. The whole expiation of sins they assigned partly to the legal sacrifices, partly to their own merits and sufferings; as even now they cry when dying, "May my death be an expiation for all my transgressions." All these things are so well known from the evangelical history that there is no need to dwell on proving them. Thus did that church openly fall away from the second foundation of Mosaic theology, concerning the justification of sinful man before God.

IV. The third foundation remains; it set forth the coming and office of the Messiah. That theology taught that the Messiah, the Son of God, the Lord of the church, the greatest prophet, was to be furnished by God the Father with such authority and command that it would be his right and his prerogative to institute new forms of worship and new ceremonies, the old being abolished. To him all Mosaic theologians were bound to be obedient at his word in all things, under penalty of eternal extermination. But after they had longed for his coming through so many centuries of years, being now heavy with defection, they showed themselves so hostile to that truth that, for no greater cause than that they suspected he would impose that end upon the ancient rites, they put the Messiah himself to death by a most cruel death. From that time down to this very day, the doctrine that the ceremonial law would be absolutely eternal and subject to no change has become the primary foundation of the apostate theology. And the nearer those carnal statutes approached their appointed end, and the more the time of reformation [Greek: kairos diorthōseōs] hastened on, with so much the greater madness and obstinacy did they fight for their unchangeable fixity [Greek: atrepsia ametablētos]. This most pernicious error the apostle sets before the eyes of all in the Epistle to the Hebrews, with many arguments deduced from their own principles. And since we are engaged with the primary head of present-day Judaism, it will not be foreign to the work in hand to weigh one of those arguments here. I mean that which the apostle pursues in the seventh chapter, verses 12-15, and elsewhere. "The priesthood being changed," he says, "there is made of necessity a change also of the law. For he of whom these things are spoken belongs to another tribe, of which no man has attended at the altar. For it is evident that our Lord sprang out of Judah, of which tribe Moses spoke nothing concerning priesthood. And it is yet far more evident, if after the likeness of Melchizedek there arises another priest." [ p. 407 ] Now the sum of this apostolic reasoning is this. The whole ceremonial law, all the administrations of the solemn worship and of the Mosaic institutions, rested upon the high priesthood. On this one hinge the whole law always turned. For this cause, in the midst of public calamities, destructions, and ruins, God so far preserved the Aaronic or priestly family that there was never lacking from it one who might discharge the priesthood according to the prescription of the law. For he himself had severely forbidden that anyone who was not of the natural seed of Aaron should dare to undertake that office, forever (Num. 16:40). Moreover, he procured authority for that law by making horrendous examples of those who dared to thrust themselves into the priestly administrations; as in the Levites (Num. 18:7), and king Uzziah (2 Chron. 26:18-21). Such, therefore, was the fellowship of the legal worship with the priesthood that the same fate hung over both; so that, the priesthood being changed, it could not be but that the legal worship should also suffer change. Furthermore, God designated a peculiar place for the performance of his solemn worship. Outside it no sacred services common to the whole church, and expressing its unity, could be performed; no oblations or sacrifices could be offered. Mount Moriah, in the city of Jerusalem, was that place. But these things being thus instituted, God himself foretells that a time would come when he would raise up another priest, from another family and tribe, and so of another order. For he signified beforehand that the Messiah, a king from the tribe of Judah, would also be pontiff or high priest. But if God constituted a pontiff neither from the seed nor from the order of Aaron, it necessarily follows that all those Mosaic institutions, whose administration rested solely on that Aaronic priesthood, should forthwith dissolve and vanish. All these things, says the apostle, cannot but be very well known to Hebrews everywhere. You all believe that the Messiah is to arise from the tribe of Judah. This, he says, is manifest. And you also confess that God never said anything about raising up or fixing in the tribe of Judah a priesthood, that is, one which should have charge of the Mosaic worship. It remains, therefore, only that I prove that the Messiah was to be a high priest. That is established beyond contradiction from the words of the Psalmist (Ps. 110:4): "Jehovah has sworn and will not repent: You are a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek." That this psalm is prophetic and looks to the Messiah, no Jews at that time doubted. The Targumists assent; nor does any of the ancient masters deny it. And when our Lord Jesus Christ had dragged into the sunlight, before all the people, the foul ignorance of the scribes and Pharisees by a question taken from this psalm concerning the person of the Messiah, although covered with shame, they yet did not dare to deny that the Messiah was he whom David in that place called Lord. But he is not only said to be a future priest; rather, that the ineffable prerogative of that priest might be made known, God solemnly [ p. 408 ] swears that he shall discharge the priesthood immutably forever. But here the apostates of today resort to a most wretched subterfuge. Kohen, they say, signifies not only a priest but also a prince; and they most gladly admit that the Messiah will be a prince. And this invention [Greek: heurēma] is indeed most worthy of the rabbis, because it is plainly frivolous and trifling. Melchizedek is called king of Salem, and kohen to God Most High: is this "prince," or "priest of God Most High"? Why, I ask, should the text call him a prince, when it had already called him a king, since kohen never denotes a prince except one who is less than and inferior to a king? Again, it belonged to a priest, not to a prince, to bless others in the name of God; which this kohen is recorded to have done (Gen. 14:18). Nor was Melchizedek a prince of a peculiar order, but a priest. Nor, as we have said, does Cohenim, when that word is transferred to the civil state, ever signify kings, or those endowed with supreme power, but only those who stand in the second or third rank; and such a princeling the Jews will not admit their Messiah to be.

V. Further, the Messiah was constituted heir not of the Melchizedekian but of the Davidic kingdom; and in David was the principal type of his royal office, since David reigned in the church (Ps. 2), the other [Melchizedek] among the nations. It was necessary, therefore, that the coming of the Messiah should be followed by the transposition [Greek: metathesis] and abolition [Greek: aphanismos] of the Mosaic constitutions. From what has been said before there also accrues to the argument no light weight. The city of Jerusalem was by God's command constituted the seat of all solemn worship. This the apostates do not deny. But the last of the prophets foretold that when the Messiah came, an offering [Hebrew: minchah] would be presented to God in every place throughout the whole world (Mal. 1:11). And lest anything should be lacking for the establishing of this truth, which brings most immediate destruction upon Judaism, God foretells that in the days of the Messiah he will take to himself priests and Levites from among the very Gentiles (Isa. 66:21). And yet this can no more stand with Judaism than light with darkness. The more clear-sighted of the Jews, compelled by the force of the truth, at last yield. The author of the Sefer Ikkarim not only expressly affirms that the law is changeable, but also replies to the arguments by which Maimonides, in his book on the foundations of the law, endeavors to prove that it is eternal. For after he had recited the reasons of R. M. B. M. [Rabbi Moses ben Maimon], he adds, book 3, chapter 13: "If anyone," he says, "considers reasons of this kind more attentively, he will find that they do not prove that the divine law is unchangeable"; and he replies to them one by one. And in chapter 20 he expressly teaches that the Messiah will be a prophet greater than Moses, and one who will have the right to change his institutions. R. Moses Nachmanides teaches similar things on Deuteronomy, section Nitsavim. But these things by the way, and as in passing [Greek: hōs en parodō].

VI. This, then, was the fatal apostasy of the Jewish church, openly falling away from all the principles and foundations of Mosaic theology. The defection was accompanied by the utmost corruption of manners. The efficacy of heavenly truth being rejected, which alone is able to set bound and measure to the crimes of men, almost the whole people, abandoned to every wickedness [ p. 409 ] and lost in outrages, hastened its own final ruin. And when the Son of God had been repudiated and wickedly taken away, he who was the soul and life of Mosaic theology, without whose saving and life-giving influence its body was useless, or rather a corpse, not unlike that one (perhaps that very one) over which the dispute arose between Michael and the devil, since it can no longer lead any of its theologians to God, it no longer seems worthy even of the name of theology. For Jewish theology is, as Antonius Hulsius rightly defines it in his preface to his book on the Messiah, "a doctrine delivered by men in the Talmud, comprehending a series of forensic and ecclesiastical constitutions formerly observed in the Jewish polity, and to be observed in it when restored anew by the Messiah, that the Jews may live happily in the world"; and how far this is from the nature and end of all true theology, anyone may easily judge.

VII. Now it was for a twofold cause that this apostate world of theologians was not abolished by the fire to which it had long been destined until a delay of some years had intervened after the repudiation of the promised Messiah. For, first, the apostates themselves had to be rendered without excuse [Greek: anapologētoi]. It was necessary, therefore, as Paul everywhere testifies, that the gospel should be announced to them, not only in all Judea, but throughout all the dispersions [Greek: diasporas] among the nations, before the nation should be destroyed by inescapable ruin. Secondly, there still lay hidden in the people that remnant according to the election of grace, which was to be called out before the wrath to the uttermost [Greek: hē orgē eis telos] should come upon it. For these reasons, I say, that church was continued in the tolerance or longsuffering of God for some years after the apostates had set the final hand to their fatal defection. Concerning this destruction let the reader consult that most renowned historian and eyewitness [Greek: autoptēs], in the Preface to his book on the Jewish War, and book 6, chapter 9, sections 12, 26; book 7, chapter 9, sections 11, 12, 15, 16, 24, 28. He describes not men but monsters; he records not a slaughter or the destruction of a nation, but an inexhaustible abyss of all the miseries which the human race has ever endured. And this end had the apostate Jewish church, after God had borne with it in great longsuffering for 527 years from the Ezraitic reformation.

books.title: Of Evangelical Theology Properly So Called

blocks.text: Chapter argument: The weight and difficulty of the work undertaken. The self-conceit [authadeia] of certain men noted. The author of evangelical theology: the only-begotten Son of God. The time of his coming. What the fullness of time is. The high point [akme] of idolatrous worship and of philosophy, and also of human dominion. The expectation of the restoration of all things. The "fullness of time" appointed by God. What the "last days" are: those of the Jewish church.

I. We are hastening toward the end of our work, though we have not yet attained what we proposed. We undertook, expressly and of set purpose, to give an account first [ p. 410 ] and almost exclusively of Christian theology. Nor was it the evangelical doctrine itself, but that habit of our mind by which we embrace it, that stood chiefly before our eyes when the plan of the work was conceived. For this reason we deferred to this place those things which concern the proper subject of theology, though they pertain equally to every theology of sinners whatsoever. Now in this study of heavenly wisdom I seem to myself to have attained no higher summit than this: that when I discourse or speak of these matters, I feel myself stammering miserably. For we are occupied with things which we perceive only "in part" [Greek: ek merous], and the whole knowledge of which no one can draw out, except him to whom it is granted to enjoy God immediately [Greek: amesos] (1 Cor. 13:12). Nor is there any reason why we should deny being moved by a modest sense of our slenderness, or even of our ignorance, since the apostle of that same epistle declares that he who thinks he knows anything here (perfectly) knows nothing at all (1 Cor. 8:2). Such indeed is the self-conceit [Greek: authadeia] of some, especially of young men who profess to have given their name to theology, that although they are scarcely moderately furnished with knowledge of evangelical doctrine out of three or four books, they nevertheless carry themselves with such superciliousness as if either they alone were wise, or they judged that they deserved no obscure name among the wisest of men. Indeed it would be well if, like so many Suffenuses, they pleased only themselves, and did not also, to their own great harm, despise others who are truly imbued with that wisdom which these men most ineptly boast of having attained. [Suffenus: the poetaster in Catullus 22, charmed by his own verses.] Truly was it said of old by Seneca: "I think many could have arrived at wisdom, had they not thought they had already arrived." But "the young think they know everything and assert it confidently," as Aristotle testifies; and there is nothing that renders anyone more unfit for the study of theology than this disposition of mind. It was said of old, and must still be said, "there come forth new orators, fools, mere striplings"; concerning whom Jude speaks (Jude 10): "Whatever things they do not know, they blaspheme; but whatever they understand naturally, like irrational animals, in these things they corrupt themselves." If they know anything philosophically about the nature of things, swelling up at once with arrogance, which is the greatest corruption of the soul, they despise the mysteries of the gospel which they do not know. May God grant that students may at last understand that an entirely different business is transacted here than in the schools of the philosophers; that there is need here of another affection of mind, another disposition of intellect and heart, than those with which they are accustomed to attack the encyclopedia of human sciences. And if I contribute any counsel to this end, which it may graciously please God to turn to good for the benefit of the godly, then, having obtained my wish and my purpose in this whole work, I shall have this also, among very many other things, to impute to divine grace. But now let us return to our purpose.

II. The Jewish church having been utterly overthrown, the purely Mosaic theology on which it rested lost its force to such a degree that it could no longer lead anyone to God. God, therefore, was now about to set the final hand to his revelation. The church, brought up under various degrees of spiritual economy [Greek: oikonomia], now grown to maturity, was to be translated into a state perfect in its kind and immutable. For the accomplishing of this most holy and most wise work, the only-begotten Son of God, destined from eternity, [ p. 411 ] promised from the laying of the foundations of the world, was sent in the fullness of time. The apostle teaches us this expressly in Hebrews 1:1, 2: "God," he says, "who in many portions and in many ways spoke of old to the fathers in the prophets, has in these last days spoken to us in the Son"; who is therefore called "the apostle and high priest of our confession" (Heb. 3:1), and also "the author and finisher of faith" (Heb. 12:2). But the things that concern the person of Jesus Christ the God-man [Greek: Theanthropos], the greatest ambassador of God, do not belong to this work, and we have set them forth more fully elsewhere. The immediate author, then, of evangelical theology is Jesus Christ himself, the only-begotten Son of God. We granted above, indeed, that every theology of sinners can in some sense be called evangelical. Even of that theology, however administered, the primary author was the Son of God himself. The holy men of God, "borne along" [Greek: pheromenoi] by the Spirit of Christ, expounded it (1 Pet. 1:11; 2 Pet. 1:21). It was he who several times exhibited himself present to the fathers under human appearance; and the same was the angel who was with the church in the wilderness (Acts 7:38). But with respect to its peculiar administration, that theology alone is called evangelical which the Son of God himself, coming forth from the bosom of the Father, expounded in his own person. He, therefore, is the singular author of this theology; whence it has this property, that as it is the most perfect, so it is the last revelation of God. Rightly Tertullian: "We have no need of curiosity after Jesus Christ, nor of inquiry after the gospel: when we believe, we desire to believe nothing further; for this we believe first, that there is nothing which we ought to believe beyond it" (Prescription against Heretics).

III. The time of the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ, and therefore of the revelation of evangelical theology, comes next to be considered. I have no wish, however, to stir up chronological difficulties, no wish to call to account the reckonings of years, days, and hours; since, to speak freely, I do not yet know what advantage has hitherto accrued to anyone from the most difficult and intricate quarrels of the chronologers. For my part I am pleased with that saying of Plutarch in his Life of Solon, who, to show that he would not withdraw credit from the conversation of Solon with Croesus merely because some denied that he lived in the age of Croesus, says: "I do not think I shall abandon this story on account of certain so-called chronological canons, which countless men, correcting them down to this very day, have not been able to bring to any agreed settlement of their contradictions." There are, however, things said concerning that time which do pertain to theology. For Christ is said to have come in the "fullness of time" (Gal. 4:4); he is said also to have come in the "last days" (Heb. 1:1); we must therefore see in what respect these two designations of time denote precisely the same time.

IV. The first passage runs thus: "When the fullness of time came, God sent forth his Son." Now the fullness of time is spoken of in a twofold respect: namely, with regard to those things which are measured by time, the ages [Greek: aiones] of this [ p. 412 ] world, and with regard to the divine determination. In both respects we shall see that Christ came in the fullness of time.

V. First, the world itself, and in it whatever things in any way concerned the economy [Greek: oikonomia] of divine patience, obtained their fullness [Greek: pleroma]. These may be referred to four heads. The worship of the gods held first place. What there was in superstition [Greek: deisidaimonia], or the impious worship of false gods, the whole earth had now at last fully experienced. Idolatry had arrived at the summit of glory, splendor, efficacy, and universality. How it crept in gradually through an unspeakable series of times and years, we have set forth above. From depraved, foolish, and futile beginnings it had now grown into such glory that the world had never seen, nor ever will see, anything destined to perish that was more glorious, more sublime, or more excellent. What consolation, help, or reward the human race was to carry away from that superstition to whose service it had willingly bound itself, it now had sufficiently perceived; which assuredly it would not have had, if God, sending Christ sooner, had sooner called men to repentance. From that day on which he laid the world under obligation by the ineffable benefit of the mission of Christ, nothing at all was added to the glory, the adornment, the ceremonies, that is, to the fullness [Greek: pleroma] of idolatry. This part of the fullness of time Paul sets forth in Acts 14:15-16 and 17:30.

VI. Secondly, that endeavor which we described at length before, of restoring natural theology by means of philosophy, had reached the limits fixed for it by the nature of things and by human ability. At that time human wisdom attained its perfection, so far as a thing most imperfect and most uncertain can be called perfect. Philosophy never possessed anything that penetrated deeper into the nature of the universe, nothing more sublime, nothing more useful, nor was it ever more elegantly expounded, than at that period. The combined geniuses of the Greeks and the Latins claim that honor for themselves. And that the fullness of time had respect to this progress and high point [Greek: akme] of philosophy, the apostle teaches us in 1 Corinthians 1:21: "For since," he says, "in the wisdom of God the world by wisdom did not know God, it pleased God by the foolishness of preaching to save those who believe"; that is, when in the most wise disposition and patience of God it had been sufficiently discerned what that philosophic wisdom could do, whither it tended, and what was to be expected from it, it pleased God to announce the gospel through his Son for the salvation of believers.

VII. Thirdly, the glory of secular power, or of human empire, had raised itself to its supreme height. What very many from the age of Nimrod had striven after in vain, the Romans had brought to accomplishment. All earthly glory, whatever the inner chambers and recesses of perishing things can supply, whatever ambition, luxury, avarice, curiosity, and finally the wisdom of men could wring out of human nature and the nature of almost all things, enslaved and reduced to bondage, was compacted and heaped together, as it were, into one city, and set forth before the eyes and consideration of all. What empire and dominion were, what human force, valor, [ p. 413 ] strength, and power, what riches and all the vainglory of the age, what luxury of life, how high buildings could rise, how deep the tunnels of mines could descend, this epitome [Greek: epitome] of the world exhibited. From that time, by a continuous series of events and outcomes, that glory has been diminished, and something, I know not what, has daily fallen away from the sum and splendor of things. By that fullness of time those who were not utterly stupid, and not wholly entangled in vices to the bottom of their hearts, could easily perceive what was to be expected from all created things, since the very lords of the world burned no less with avarice, envy, ambition, and lust than if they had labored under the utmost poverty of all things.

VIII. Fourthly, a wonderful and unusual expectation had seized the minds of men, of the coming of a greatest king, of the beginning of happy times and eternal peace, of the entrance and first beginnings of the restoration of all things. The Jews daily desired and at the same time expected the appearing [Greek: epiphaneia] of the king Messiah; the Gentiles, the return of the golden age. And that a report had spread abroad that the East (as some said), or the nature of things (as others), would presently bring forth a greatest king, is the constant and agreeing testimony of the most celebrated historians. When, therefore, the human race had exercised itself to the last limit of its powers, in superstition and wisdom, in dominion and luxury, in vices and virtues, that is, in the fullness of time, God graciously sent his Son, announcing peace to those who were near and to those who were far off.

IX. Secondly, the fullness of time formerly appointed by God was at hand. Peter testifies that Christ was "foreknown before the foundation of the world" [Greek: proegnosmenos pro kataboles kosmou] (1 Pet. 1:20). That he would come, according to that foreknowledge [Greek: prognosis] or eternal destination, God had promised through the prophets from the laying of the foundations of the world (Luke 1:70). Hence he was called "the Coming One" [Greek: ho erchomenos]. This is the sense of John's question (Matt. 11:3), "Are you the Coming One?"; and he was longed for from Adam onward: "the desire of all nations" [Hebrew: chemdat kol-haggoyim] (Hag. 2:7). At the appointed time, therefore, that is, when the time destined for fulfilling the promises was at hand, the Son of God was made manifest in the flesh. That the fullness of time at which the Messiah was to be sent had arrived, the Jews themselves do not dare to deny; but that he has been sent, they obstinately deny. Why so? Because, they say, God, angered by their sins, has hitherto deferred his coming.
