We for our part confess that their crimes were very great and most grievous; but that the unbelief which cast them out of the roll of the people of God could have made, or did make, the very faithfulness of God of no effect, we utterly deny.

X. It is certain, therefore, that Christ came in the fullness of time. It remains, then, that we see how he also came at the end of days. For the fullness of time and the end of days scarcely seem to denote the same time and the same days. Now although so many ages have flowed past since his coming, and it is most uncertain how long this world is yet to endure in the patience of God, or what the delay of the age is, as Tertullian speaks, it is nevertheless most certain that Christ came in the last days. The apostle testifies this in Hebrews 1:1-3: "in the last of these days" [Greek: ep' eschaton ton hemeron]. Now a given time can be called "the last days" either absolutely, or with some particular respect. Why so great a series of years, nay of ages, as has [ p. 414 ] flowed past from the incarnation of Christ down to the present, and as still remains to be completed before the consummation of all things, should be called the last days absolutely, no reason can be given. Some judge that those days are called last with respect to the dispensation of the divine will, of which, namely, the final revelation has now been made. On that view, the whole time of gospel preaching, from the incarnation or resurrection of Christ to the consummation of the age, is to be called the last days. But that opinion rests on no testimony and, so far as I know, on no probable reason. That time is the new world, "the world to come," not "the last days" or "the last hour." The time of Christ's coming, therefore, is called the last days with respect to the Jewish church. She was now near [Greek: engys] to her abolition [Greek: aphanismos]. And the exit of the Jewish church fell within the fullness of time. Therefore the apostle affirms, in the place cited, that God in the Son addressed the Jewish people "in the last of these days," namely those days which were then in passing, not yet finished, but immediately to be finished. For throughout that whole Epistle to the Hebrews the apostle supposes that the Jewish church then still existed, and that its solemn worship, in the forbearance of God, was thus far pleasing to God, to the extent that no one became worse by its observance, or less pleasing or acceptable to God. The Gentiles indeed were pronounced free from the yoke of the Mosaic ceremonies (Acts 15). But that believing Jews for a long time afterward religiously observed the whole solemn Mosaic worship, we showed earlier from Acts 21:20-22, 26. Some ancient manuscripts also read "at the extremity of these days" [Greek: ep' eschatou ton hemeron touton]; by which the state of the Jewish church, which now stood at its last gasp, placed as it were between the altar and the knife [literally "between the sacred and the stone," a proverb for desperate straits], is plainly enough indicated.

XI. Further, in that place where Christ is said to have been sent in the last days, the subject is his ministerial office, or apostolic charge, which he discharged while he was on earth. And that pertained solely "to the lost sheep of the house of Israel" (Matt. 15:24). Whence also he was called "a minister of the circumcision" (Rom. 15:8). The Son and heir, namely, was to be sent into the same vineyard from which the servants had suffered rejection (Matt. 21). And that he came to that church in its last days, when it was all but lost, everyone knows.

XII. Those words also, "in the last of the days," recall those prophecies of the Old Testament which expressly denote the last times of the Jewish church. Jacob, about to expound what would befall his descendants down to the coming of the Messiah, begins his prophecy thus (Gen. 49:1): "I will show you what will happen in the latter days [Hebrew: be'acharit hayyamim]"; which words the Seventy elders render "in the last of the days" [Greek: ep' eschaton ton hemeron]. Similarly Numbers 24:14, where the Holy Spirit uses the same words concerning the same time, "at the last of the days" [Greek: ep' eschatou ton hemeron], just as many manuscripts exhibit the apostolic passage. Christ, therefore, the author of evangelical theology, came in the "fullness of time" and in the "last days"; because the last days of the Jewish church fell within the fullness of the days.

Chapter argument: Christ the author of evangelical theology, furnished most perfectly with the knowledge of the divine will. The foundation of the most perfect knowledge of Christ the mediator, John 1:18. Its mode, fullness, most absolute perfection. The revelation of the divine will conformed to that knowledge. Evangelical theology. Its principles.

I. "Holy brethren," says the apostle, "partakers of the heavenly calling, consider the apostle and high priest of our confession, Christ Jesus" (Heb. 3:1). This we treated briefly in the preceding chapter. It remains that we accurately weigh that very confession [Greek: homologia] of ours, that is, the evangelical doctrine. That Christ, sent from the bosom of the Father, was most perfectly furnished with the knowledge of the will of God, so that nothing new at all was left to be drawn from the fountain of truth by those who should come after the king (Eccles. 2:12), almost all Christians agree among themselves. He himself indeed, the Word [Greek: ho Logos], the eternal Word of God, has an infinite knowledge of all things, a most absolute understanding of the whole divine essence and will, connatural to himself, that is, communicated to him by the Father from eternity together with the divine subsistence [Greek: hypostasis]. But insofar as he stood forth as Head of the church, Mediator, and apostle of our confession delegated by the Father, he knew only those things (in that nature, namely, in which he himself performed the whole mediatorial office) which were given and revealed to him by the Father. Hence he affirmed that there were some things he did not know (Mark 13:32), and that he knew nothing beyond what the Father had revealed to him (John 5:30; 7:16, 17). The same economy continues even after his glorious exaltation at the right hand of the Father. Whence he testifies that he received from the Father that solemn Revelation [Greek: Apokalypsis] which he published not by the inbreathing of the Spirit only, but with a living voice from heaven (Rev. 1:1). The Socinians feign that at the time of his fast in the wilderness he was caught up into heaven and instructed in the counsel of God concerning the due discharge of the office delegated to him: a prodigious figment drawn from the sloughs of the Muhammadan Koran, which we exploded in our Vindiciae Evangelicae. The foundation of that knowledge of the divine will which was in Christ Jesus the Baptist sets forth in John 1:18, if indeed those words are the Baptist's, which Chrysostom denies: "The saying is not the forerunner's," he says, "but God's." Whosesoever they were, they are these: "No one," he says, "has ever seen God," not even Moses himself, through whom the law was given (ver. 17); "the only-begotten Son, who is in the bosom of the Father, he has declared him." For since he is the only-begotten Son of God, and therefore of the same essence with the Father, he has an infinitely most perfect knowledge and comprehension of the divine essence itself, and therefore of all the truth lying hidden in it. He alone, therefore, is fit to expound God perfectly, since he alone has seen God. Further, since he was in the bosom of the Father, he was privy to all his counsels and secrets; and for the due completion of the declaration of the divine will [ p. 416 ] committed to him, he was endowed with the Holy Spirit "not by measure" (John 3:34); for God measured out the Spirit to him most abundantly, beyond all measure, and with the Spirit a certain immense sea of spiritual gifts, especially of wisdom and understanding in the fear of the Lord (Isa. 11:2, 3); so that in him were "all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge hidden" (Col. 2:3). Whatever lay hidden in the divine mind from eternity that pertained to the displaying of the glory of God, the erecting of the kingdom, the instituting of worship, the gathering of the church, the calling, instructing, and consoling of the elect, whatever, in short, pertains to the obedience men owe to God, with the knowledge of all this he was most perfectly furnished by God the Father, and stood forth as the messenger of his will to men. In this judgment, therefore, all acquiesce: namely, that Christ, as the apostle of our confession, had most perfectly explored the whole counsel of God concerning his worship and our obedience; whereas all other expositors of the divine will, even the most celebrated lawgiver himself, were made partakers only of some part of it, serving, namely, that dispensation which they served. Nothing lay so deeply hidden in the eternal abysses of the divine mind that he did not perfectly search it out; nothing so minute, ever pleasing or acceptable to God in his worship, that escaped or fled from that most wise breast of his.

II. Further, sacred Scripture testifies, and Christians agree, that this most holy author of our theology communicated that whole counsel of God, the knowledge of which he most perfectly attained, to his disciples, who were to propagate his doctrine among all nations, partly by living voice and partly by the Spirit, whom he received as a gift from the Father after his ascension. For since he was faithful in the whole house of God to the Father who appointed him (Heb. 3:2), and that not only as his faithful servant in the mediatorial work (Isa. 42:1), but also as the only-begotten Son (Heb. 3:6), who was heir of all things, whose was the very house, which he singularly loved (Eph. 5:25, 26) with a love to which nothing was ever equal or comparable (Rom. 5:6-8), it was plainly impossible that he should not perfectly expound, for its benefit and salvation, what he had received from the Father for that end. Now this revelation of the divine will, given by the Father to Christ, and communicated by him through the Holy Spirit to his apostles and others for the use of the whole church, is that evangelical theology, insofar as, taken in the abstract, it denotes the divine doctrine which we are about to set forth.
III. These things premised, we shall briefly review the principles of this theology which are proper [Greek: oikeia] to it, or its governing causes [Greek: aitia kyria].

1. The first of these is this: "No one, relying on his own powers or his own native ability, whatever external means he may employ as helps, can perceive or rightly understand this theology, with respect to the saving ends toward which it directs the human mind." And in this, first of all, its nature is set apart from all other sciences. For as [ p. 417 ] the apostle expressly declares, "The natural man is not capable of the things that are of the Spirit of God, nor can he understand them, because they are spiritually discerned" (1 Cor. 2:14). So the Lord Jesus himself teaches, "No one can come to me, unless the Father draws him" (John 6:44). Everywhere, too, this theology shows that men outside the knowledge of Christ are dead in sins, blind, foolish, stupid, so that they cannot grasp or savingly understand this heavenly doctrine; which things we have set forth more fully elsewhere.

2. Secondly, "It teaches that he must be born again, or spiritually regenerated, who would savingly or usefully know that doctrine which it exhibits, that is, to that end for which it is destined by its most holy author." And here it stands at an immense distance from all human wisdom. For let men be most highly cultivated in every kind of letters, to the highest reach of human ability; let them strain all the sinews and points of the mind; let them leave nothing unattempted: yet unless they are born again, it is impossible that they be savingly imbued with this doctrine. "For unless one is born again," says our Lord, "he cannot see the kingdom of God" (John 3:3). And it is the kingdom of God that this theology expounds. But of this principle more must be said hereafter.

3. Then, thirdly, "No one is born again except by the power of the Holy Spirit, by whose efficacious operation all who are born of God are brought over from spiritual death to life" (John 3:5, 6; 1:13; Tit. 3:5).

4. And accordingly, fourthly, "That Holy Spirit alone can introduce anyone into the saving perception of this theology, or bestow on anyone the understanding by which the evangelical doctrine is savingly understood (John 14:13; 1 John 2:20; 1 Cor. 2:10-16; 2 Cor. 4:6); and therefore all who give their labor to this theology must, distrusting their own powers, seek his help and aid with assiduous prayers" (Luke 11:13; James 1:5; Eph. 1:17, 18).

5. Fifthly, "The worship of God instituted by virtue of this theology is spiritual, and its glory by no means strikes the eyes of men, nor is it exposed to anyone's carnal understanding" (2 Cor. 3:6-10; John 4:21-24).

6. Finally, "All worshipers of God in Christ, according to the rule of this theology, are by its power separated from the world, and accordingly have always been hated by it, and always will be, both for that cause and because they have received the Holy Spirit, of whom the world is not capable" (2 Cor. 6:14-18; John 15:18, 19; 14:16, 17; Gal. 1:4, etc.). But we shall afterward see this theology itself urging these governing maxims [Greek: kyriai doxai] at greater length.

Chapter argument: What evangelical theology is. The various degrees and modes of its knowledge. The first mode. The relation of the merely natural intellect to supernatural doctrine. It is not theology properly so called. In what sense and how far spiritual truths can be perceived and understood without the special aid of the Holy Spirit. Propositions of heavenly truth digested into methods produce a Christian philosophy. The use of the knowledge of languages and sciences in learning divine truth. What kind of knowledge of divine things that is which is acquired without the special aid of the Holy Spirit. It is not evangelical theology. The proof of those who are imbued with it.

I. We have shown that evangelical theology is the doctrine revealed in the gospel through Christ. The knowledge of that doctrine is theology, insofar as it occupies a subject. Now it has this in common with the other sciences, that according to the difference of the subjects in which it inheres, and the variety of the means through which it is communicated, it admits various degrees. But beyond this it also has various modes of perceiving the doctrine set before it, by force of the principles expounded in the preceding chapter, which is peculiar to it. The first of these consists in that relation which the merely natural intellect, or the intellect of a man in the state of nature, can have to spiritual and supernatural doctrine; another is that which an intellect aroused and elevated by the special aid and power of the Holy Spirit, that is, the intellect of a regenerate man, obtains. And each of these admits various degrees. We shall first briefly dispatch that mode of perceiving or understanding evangelical doctrine, which is the rule for obtaining all communion with God, which the human mind can attain without the special aid or grace of the Holy Spirit, and which we deny to constitute theology properly so called.

II. The gospel is the doctrine concerning God the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit, and his worship, and our obedience owed to him. That doctrine, by its own nature, stirs the consciences of men to render that obedience (Tit. 1:1; 2:11, 12). Moreover, it rouses souls to the expectation of the enjoyment of God. Now concerning all these things, the true judgment is contained in propositions whose sense is open, indeed obvious, to human reason. For this heavenly doctrine yields to none of the human sciences in the clarity of its propositions; nay, in this respect it stands out far above all other sciences. The doctrine is written "plainly," so that "he who reads it may run" (Hab. 2:2). God has given his word "precept upon precept, precept upon precept, line upon line, line upon line, a little here, a little there" (Isa. 28:10; Heb. 5:13, 14). Those propositions, therefore, which contain matters of the greatest moment, or the principal heads of this theology, partly from the supreme clarity of the very words which it pleased the Holy Spirit to use, and their singular aptitude for setting forth the things of which it treats, partly from the most frequent inculcation of the things themselves, are so clear and so exposed to the eyes of all, that their genuine sense is hidden from almost [ p. 419 ] no one, except a man hindered by the greatest prejudices. We concede, therefore, that every man endowed with reason and understanding, and in possession of their use, can, according to the measure of his ability, the degree of his industry, and the efficacy of the external means he employs in learning the truth, without the saving aid of the Holy Spirit, draw out the true sense of those propositions, and perceive and hold their meaning. Most ineptly, then, do the sophists prattle who cry out that our judgment concerning the spiritual nature of the things revealed in the gospel, and the native blindness of the human mind, on account of which no one can spiritually perceive them except by relying on the saving power of the Holy Spirit, is contrary to this other judgment concerning the perspicuity and clarity of the propositions in which the true and saving doctrine is contained in the sacred Scriptures.

III. Further, we concede that the principal heads of this theology, or of evangelical doctrine, namely those which most pertain to promoting the worship of God and our obedience, can be digested and gathered into order, series, and method, according to the rules of the philosophical arts and sciences. For since among the very things delivered in the gospel there is a certain real and mutual dependence, there is nothing to prevent their relation to one another from being expounded in a certain order, and as it were technically [Greek: technikos]. Creeds, confessions of faith, catechisms, commonplaces, and other methodical arrangements of evangelical truths of that kind serve this work. And doctrine digested and compacted in that manner has nothing at all that exceeds the merely rational grasp or understanding of men. I speak not of the things themselves, but of the propositions, which are the signs of the things. Now the merely natural knowledge or comprehension of evangelical doctrine, delivered and expounded in that manner, is a certain Christian philosophy; which outstrips all Greek, or merely human, wisdom by many parasangs. For since all knowledge is a certain intellectual perfection, that knowledge which is most certain, and is occupied with the greatest and supreme object, surpasses all others. And of that kind is this Christian philosophy, compared with all the wisdom or science of heathen men. For it both proposes objects infinitely distant from all the objects of natural philosophy, and rests upon an utterly infallible rule discriminating the true from the false, while that other wisdom tosses about in the wretched uncertainty of common conceptions.

IV. This whole matter can be set under one view in a brief summary. All divine truth is revealed in the Scriptures through our Lord Jesus Christ. That doctrine, considered in the abstract, is evangelical theology. It is contained in propositions whose sense is open to human reason. Those propositions, with the conclusions legitimately drawn from them, that is, their sense mingled with other words and philosophical terms, can be, and commonly are, compacted into a disciplinary system. The languages in which all truth was delivered by the Holy Spirit are Hebrew and Greek. Hence the knowledge of those languages [ p. 420 ] is useful to all, and to some, under the providence of God, necessary. Also, for rightly understanding those things which the sacred Scriptures have in common with other books, it is useful that he who approaches their study be furnished with those sciences which serve the exposition of rational meanings through fitting words. But these things are few, and not of great moment, nor do they contribute much to true spiritual wisdom. All modes of true reasoning belong to logic, as does the detection of sophistry. Now the Holy Spirit uses the highest reasoning in sacred Scripture. Where it cannot be reduced to dialectical rules, this happens either through a defect of the art itself as it is commonly taught, or because human ability fails in investigating the ways and modes of true reasoning. He speaks also according to the customary forms of speech in those languages, which learned men have compacted into the art of grammar. The same account holds of rhetoric. These things thus disposed, we say that any man in possession of reason, without any special or saving aid or assistance of the Holy Spirit, relying on natural acuteness of ability and on such endowments of mind as God of old bestowed on the heathen, using the ordinary means, among which the knowledge of the languages and the common arts is eminent, can acquire for himself a theological science, that is, an intellectual habit by which he may perceive the sense of theological doctrine set forth in propositions, discourse subtly about it, expound it copiously, and explain, confirm, and defend what they call the articles of faith; and that he can be affected toward divine truth in exactly the same way in which a man learned in them is affected toward the mathematical sciences.

V. But that this science is Christian theology properly so called, we deny; and those who are furnished with it alone, though they ascend to the highest point and summit of subtlety, we reckon Christian philosophers, not evangelical theologians. That we have not pronounced these things rashly, we shall show more fully hereafter in the description of true theology. Let it suffice here, as it were in passing, to indicate some heads of the reasons by which we prove that the science we have described is not theology, and the arguments by which those who are furnished with it alone, but destitute of the sanctifying Spirit, show that they are not theologians.

VI. True theology is that knowledge of the divine mind and will which God himself requires. See Psalm 119:27; Jeremiah 22:15, 16; John 17:3; 1 John 2:3, 4, and 4:7. Now that knowledge of himself and of his will which God requires is pleasing to God (1 Chron. 28:9; Hos. 6:6). He also who is furnished with it is dear and acceptable to him (Jer. 24:7). But such is not the science we have described, where it is alone. For neither is it itself pleasing to God, nor does it render anyone pleasing to God. "For those who are in the flesh cannot please God" (Rom. 8:8). See 1 Corinthians 13:2. Further, that knowledge of himself which God demands, he himself promises (Jer. 31:33, 34; John 6:45). But that disciplinary science of heavenly truth is by no means promised in the covenant of grace, nor is it communicated to men by its power. For true knowledge [ p. 421 ] proceeds from the saving operation of the Holy Spirit (1 John 2:20, 27; Eph. 1:17, 18). Secondly, he who is imbued with true theology "is light in the Lord" (Eph. 5:8), being called, namely, "out of darkness into marvelous light" (1 Pet. 2:9; Eph. 4:21-24). But notwithstanding that other science, the man in whom it dwells is blind (2 Pet. 1:9), and "darkened in his understanding" [Greek: eskotismenos te dianoia] (Eph. 4:18), and true divine wisdom is foolishness to him (1 Cor. 2:14, 15). Moreover, that science attains no end of true evangelical theology. For it has rendered no one conformed to God in Christ, no one fit and able to obey God; it procures for no one communion with God the Father or the Son; it will never lead anyone to the enjoyment of God. But that a science which by no means dispels the vicious natural darkness from the understanding, which renders no one light in the Lord, pleasing to God, or conformed to Christ, which directs or leads no one into the communion and friendship of God, which neither is eternal life nor has eternal life joined to itself by an indissoluble bond, is that knowledge of God which he himself requires in the gospel, no one, I think, who professes himself a Christian will dare to affirm.

VII. And what of the fact that very many furnished with this science betray, by their own marks, that they are not evangelical theologians? For, to say nothing of internal and spiritual evangelical obedience, the "holiness of truth" [Greek: hosiotes tes aletheias], with which they have absolutely nothing in common, it is very rare for them to attend diligently even to the external works of virtue and piety. Where indeed legal conviction, impressed on the heart by the force and efficacy of the Spirit, is added to that science with which they are very often puffed up, then, reduced to a servile state of mind and stricken with the dread of divine terror, they attempt to perform very many duties of religion with some affection of soul. But that conviction does not belong to that science, nor does it flow from it; since very many are held by its bond and power who are only moderately furnished with the knowledge of divine things, and many who are most highly cultivated in that science live a life plainly free from its efficacy. But setting aside the consideration of those things which arise alongside that science and do not necessarily follow from it, the morals of very many furnished with it, and a manner of life most unworthy of the Christian name, allow no one to doubt that it contributes not the least bit to holiness of life. Further, since they are natural men [Greek: psychikoi] and do not have the Spirit, that very Spirit himself and his truly evangelical gifts are to them objects of contempt and derision; indeed they carry on perpetual enmities against the most holy Spirit of Christ in all those things in which, according to the promise of the new covenant, God has graciously bestowed him. For since it is some part of that philosophy with which they are furnished, that all evangelical goods are to be credited to the Holy Spirit alone, they are rather often ashamed to despise him openly, directly, and in plain words; but it is their settled custom to lash all his saving operations, and those heavenly privileges of which believers are made partakers through him, with petulant witticisms; indeed they are never [ p. 422 ] more Suffenuses to themselves, never more the darlings of certain profane scoffers, than when they think they are cleverly and jocosely baiting the Spirit of God who dwells in the godly. Then, since they have received no gifts of the kingdom of Christ, whatever place they finally occupy in the churches, they contribute either nothing at all, or certainly little, to the edification of the body in love. For although in those things which Christian theology has in common with other sciences, that is, philosophy mingled with evangelical doctrines, they turn out the keenest and most fiery disputants and subtle investigators, and are very often the greatest devourers of books, yet when they come to instruct the people in the mysteries of the gospel, they are altogether cold; and beyond bedizened little orations, thrust upon the Christian common people in place of evangelical instructions and exhortations, there is nothing that they know or do, discharging the ministerial office in appearance only. Excellent are the words of Jerome to Paulinus (ch. 5): "I am silent," he says, "about men like myself, who, if perhaps they come to the holy Scriptures after secular literature, and have soothed the ears of the people with polished discourse, think that whatever they say is the law of God, and do not deign to know what the prophets, what the apostles meant, but fit incongruous testimonies to their own sense; as if it were not the most childish thing, like the tricks of mountebanks, to teach what you do not know, nay, to speak with some indignation, not even to know that you do not know."

Chapter argument: The proper subject of evangelical theology. The distribution of all men into two kinds, the regenerate and the unregenerate. Only the regenerate are Christian theologians. In what sense the philosophers keep young men from hearing moral philosophy. It is proved that only the regenerate are properly called evangelical theologians. The authority of the apostle, 1 Cor. 2:14; of Christ himself, John 3:3; 8:47; 1:13; 5:37. The spiritual incapacity [Greek: adynamia] of the unregenerate. Of spiritual blindness and death. The Holy Spirit the sole author of evangelical theology; he alone teaches it. All the unregenerate, being destitute of him, cannot learn it. The first end of theology: conformity to God. No unregenerate man attains that end. Communion with God, the second end of true theology. The enjoyment of God, the third. None of these does the theology of the unregenerate attain.

I. We here treat of evangelical theology in the concrete, that is, as it occupies a subject, or the minds of men. Who, then, and of what kind they are who are furnished with it, we shall say in the first place.

II. All men who live here before God, the judge, ruler, and rewarder of all, since this very thing, namely that they live before God, has regard to their present duty and their last end, are either regenerate or unregenerate. The state of spiritual new birth is mentioned many times by sacred Scripture. Of every man, therefore, it can be affirmed or denied that he is regenerate. Those who neither absolutely belong nor absolutely do not belong to that state are nonexistent. Some, indeed, seem with respect to it [ p. 423 ] to lean or incline this way and that. But speaking absolutely, as they say, all who know and love the gospel confess that every man either is regenerate or is not. On this rest all the other distributions of the human race found among the sacred writers: into godly and ungodly, wise and foolish, believing and unbelieving, holy and impure, just and unjust. For all the regenerate, however liable they are to sins which do not exceed the compass of the grace of the new covenant, and although at times some of them sin most grievously with respect to this or that particular deed, are nevertheless, as to the spiritual state in which they walk before God and live to him, godly, wise, believing, holy, just, and are so called by the Holy Spirit; whereas the ungodly are foolish, unbelieving, impure, unjust, even though they apply themselves to some divine and human duties, being unregenerate to a man; for "those who are in the flesh cannot please God." And the denomination follows the state, not this or that deed. Now we assert that only the regenerate are capable and partakers of this evangelical theology; all others who profess to give their labor to it being dismissed to the philosophico-theological class. What we said before supports this truth: the progress of our discourse will set the whole matter before the eyes of all. Let it suffice here to confirm the thesis itself in a few words.

III. And indeed, if Aristotle was permitted to remove and eject the young, liable to and enslaved by depraved affections, so far from the ends of his moral philosophy that he judged them not even fit hearers of it, how much more will a Christian man be permitted to pronounce those whom he knows to be not only liable to vices and sins but even dead in them, strangers to true evangelical theology. For I myself have known a great many young men, drunken, raging, intemperate, languid and rotted with lust, who knew the moral doctrine of Aristotle as well as their own fingernails, and were accustomed daily to dispute subtly about its more abstruse points. Such scoundrels, soaked in vices, have no more of the philosopher than of the good man, that is, not even a crumb of either; and not only Aristotle, but the whole chorus of the ancient sages would, with unanimous consent, pronounce this verdict. Let us Christians at least use the same right; and let us not allow those whom we believe to be no whit better than these before God to be reckoned among Christian theologians and genuine disciples of the Lord Jesus on account of a disciplinary knowledge of some evangelical doctrines. Indeed, some of the ancients are so far from holding such men for theologians, that they pronounce them plainly unworthy of the Christian name. So Justin, in his Apology to the Emperor: "But those who are found not living as he taught (namely, Christ), let them be known as not being Christians, even though they profess with the tongue the teachings of Christ; for he said that not those who merely speak, but those who also do the works, shall be saved; and we ask that those who do not live conformably to his teachings, but are called Christians only, be punished even by you." [ p. 424 ]

IV. Nor does it happen here as in other controversies, where the truth is with difficulty drawn out of two or three passages; almost the whole gospel is witness in this cause; nor does the contrary opinion owe its origin to anything but that detestable confusion which ignorance of this truth has brought into the church. I should be prolix, and a weariness to the reader, if I heaped up here all, or even most, of the weighty testimonies extant in sacred Scripture that support this truth. It will be enough to point out the fountains of the arguments. Thus the apostle says expressly [Greek: rhetos] (1 Cor. 2:14): "The natural man does not receive the things of the Spirit of God." The natural man [Greek: psychikos anthropos] is he who is not born again; "the things of the Spirit," which he "does not receive," are all those things which pertain to evangelical wisdom, especially the doctrine of the cross, that is, the theology of which we are treating. The sense of "does not receive" the apostle explains in the words immediately following. "He cannot," he says, "know them"; and no one has the knowledge of things which he cannot know. He subjoins the reason of that incapacity [Greek: adynamia]: "they are foolishness to him," he says, and "they are spiritually discerned," but such a man discerns nothing spiritually. He does not perceive the thing itself, because it is foolishness to him; nor does he attain the manner in which alone these things can be rightly perceived. But that the man who neither understands spiritual things nor can understand them, to whom they are foolishness, and of which he is not capable, is not an evangelical theologian, seems clear enough to me. If there are any who think they judge otherwise, our page shall not be contentious; for in truth they judge otherwise than they think they judge, or they do not believe the gospel.

V. In bearing this testimony the very Master and Lord of us all, Jesus Christ, went before his disciple and apostle: "Unless one is born from above," he says (John 3:3), "he cannot see the kingdom of God." That "to see" is the same as "to know" or "to understand," no one will deny: that use of "words of sense" is most frequent in sacred Scripture. That by the kingdom of God he intends the mysteries of the kingdom of heaven, that is, the doctrine of the gospel, the Lord Jesus shows several times, as in Matthew 13:11, Mark 4:11, Luke 8:10. By his judgment, therefore, he who is not born again can by no means understand evangelical doctrine. Again, rebuking the unbelieving Pharisees (John 8:47): "He who is of God hears the words of God; for this reason you do not hear, because you are not of God." To be of God is "to be born of God" (John 1:13). He who is not of God in that manner "cannot hear the words of God." He is not a fit hearer of them, since he neither understands their spiritual wisdom nor is obedient to their command. In the same way he affirms that those same Pharisees, puffed up with the opinion of their supreme skill in hearing and expounding the word of God, had "neither heard the voice of God" nor ever seen his form [Greek: eidos] [ p. 425 ] (John 5:37); for "hearing they did not hear," nor "seeing did they see," since they rightly understood nothing of the things they had heard and seen. And those who consider men of that sort to be scribes instructed for the kingdom of heaven, taught of God, or genuine disciples of Christ (such as all evangelical theologians are), will bear, I hope, with those who, in agreement with Christ, judge otherwise. To heap up other testimonies after the express words of our Lord is unnecessary.

VI. Further, such things are said of all the unregenerate on the one hand, and of the communication of this theology on the other, as bar them far from all communion with it; they cry out, as it were, "Away, away, whoever is defiled!" [a ritual cry excluding the profane from the mysteries].

They are "blind" (2 Pet. 1:9; Isa. 42:7; Luke 4:18), and "darkness" (John 1:5; 1 Pet. 2:9; Acts 26:18; Eph. 5:8); they "have darkened minds" (Eph. 4:18); they "walk in darkness" (1 John 1:6) and "love darkness" (John 3:19); they are "dead" (Eph. 2:5), enemies to God (Rom. 8:7), "who neither are subject to the law of God, nor can be." But this evangelical doctrine is "light" (Matt. 4:16; 1 Pet. 2:9; Hos. 6:5; John 11:9; Ps. 43:3), and gives light to all who receive it (Ps. 19:8, 9; John 1:5; Ps. 36:9; 2 Pet. 1:19; John 12:35, 36). It is also "life" (John 6:33; Phil. 2:16), and gives life to those who understand it (John 17:3, 25). And this doctrine is communicated through "the opening of the eyes" (Isa. 42:7; Acts 26:18), "the renewing of the mind" (Rom. 12:2; Eph. 4:23), "translation out of darkness into light" (1 Pet. 2:9; Eph. 5:8), "resurrection from the dead" (Eph. 5:14), and a most powerful "irradiation of spiritual light," by which God removes the darkness with no less exertion of his power than that which he once used in the very creation of all things, when he commanded light to shine out of darkness (2 Cor. 4:6). To prove that the blind see, that darkness shines, that the dead live, requires no common sophistry. And to assert that God has opened the eyes of such men, has translated them into his marvelous light, has raised them from the dead, has shined into their minds to give the knowledge of his glory, when he himself openly testifies that he has not so dealt with them, belongs only to angelic doctors and seraphic sophists. But perhaps they will say that all these things are spoken metaphorically; for really and properly speaking the unregenerate are not blind or dead, nor is doctrine light, but only metaphorically. Yet ever since foolish little men have judged that they can speak more properly about divine things than God himself, almost nothing sound or whole has been left in the Christian religion; but the pure, heavenly, spiritual, life-giving doctrine of the gospel, turned into a secular and disciplinary science, has all but ruined all truth, piety, and faith. And of that opinion were of old the most learned Pharisees, who confessed that the poor little man deprived of his natural eyes had been blind, but refused to admit that they themselves were blind, though in truth they were [ p. 426 ] blinder than any moles. But grant that some of these words are transferred from natural things to spiritual uses; yet truly, if there were not the same relation between spiritual blindness and light, death and life, as obtains between natural blindness and light, natural death and life, then by the use of those words the Holy Spirit would not teach us but would mislead and deceive us; whereas he no more deceives us than he can be deceived by us. But no place remains here for disputations among those with whom the gospel has obtained credit, who, I fear, are exceedingly few. "For what fellowship has righteousness with lawlessness? And what communion has light with darkness? And what concord has Christ with Belial? Or what part has a believer with an unbeliever?" (2 Cor. 6:14-16). Moreover, evangelical theology is taught through the Holy Spirit and through him alone: "The Holy Spirit, he will teach you all things" (John 14:26). Whence all the children of the church are taught of God [Greek: theodidaktoi] (Isa. 54:13; John 6:45). He is the anointing that teaches all things: "You have an anointing from the Holy One, and you know all things" (1 John 2:20). "The anointing teaches you concerning all things" (ver. 27). Nor can he be disturbed in his office by anyone. This also the apostle teaches most plainly in 1 Corinthians 2:6-13. Those words of the apostle we briefly expounded when we treated of the general nature of theology at the beginning of this work; let the reader consult the place. He teaches that evangelical doctrine is "the wisdom of God in a mystery," differing in its whole kind from all human science or wisdom; and he denies that it can be perceived without the special aid of the Holy Spirit. And he affirms that the Spirit himself enlightens the minds of believers with that wisdom. But those who are not born again do not have the Spirit: "natural men [Greek: psychikoi], not having the Spirit" (Jude 19). They are the world, which does not receive the Spirit and cannot receive him: "the Spirit of truth, whom the world cannot receive, because it neither sees him nor knows him" (John 14:17). Nor are they partakers of the anointing. It is a wonder, then, how they can be imbued with this wisdom, since they neither have the Spirit, who alone bestows it, nor are willing to have anything in common with him. Excellently Justin, in the Dialogue with Trypho: "If, therefore, anyone should not receive, together with the great grace that is from God, the understanding of the things spoken and done by the prophets, it will profit him nothing to seem to recite their sayings or their deeds."

VII. Finally, the same truth shines forth from the consideration of the end of all true theology. We have demonstrated abundantly enough that by the original natural theology, or implanted knowledge of God [Greek: theognosia emphytos], man before the entrance of sin was made like or conformed to God, and fit and suitable for rendering him due obedience. Now the end of true theology can be learned from that end which God himself gave it at the beginning, and united to it inseparably. Evangelical theology has been brought up as reinforcement to natural theology. It too, unless it is to be said to perform its office badly, renders its subject conformed to God [ p. 427 ] and ready to do his will. That no defect attaches to it on this side, we shall show later. We know, then, that very many unregenerate men excel greatly in that knowledge of spiritual things which we described before; but that they are made conformed to God by its power, we utterly deny; and therefore the science with which they are furnished is not theology. For without faith it is impossible for anyone to please God (Heb. 11:6), and all that they render him in the worship of God is plainly nothing (John 15:5). That their works also are "splendid sins" is a celebrated saying of the ancients, drawn from many places of sacred Scripture. The knowledge they have of God and of his will does indeed goad the consciences of the unregenerate to obey God. It also kindles and increases the natural light of conscience, adds to it little flames of legal fervor, sharpens the sense of sin, and stirs up and nourishes in the depths of their breasts very many forebodings of the judgment to come; but since it does not renew the mind, does not take away the hard heart, does not dispel the natural darkness, nor force the shadows of death to vanish, it does not render the soul itself conformed to God, nor does it bestow spiritual strength for rightly rendering obedience to God; and therefore it does not attain the name of true theology. Epictetus would deny that he is a philosopher who is not free, not master of himself, who has not placed all good in those things which lie within our power. Far be it, then, that we Christians should acknowledge him to be a theologian who is not a disciple of Christ, or a disciple of Christ who neither does nor can do any of the things which Christ commanded. They are enemies of Christ to a man, and are held by him in the place of enemies. And very many of them, often the more openly the more learned they are, hold the Spirit of Christ in mockery and contempt. Excellent evangelical theologians indeed! Let them turn over the books of the ancients, devour them, dispute most keenly, turn the bloodless schoolmen into blood and sap, swallow all the critics, scribble, and preach; yet so far are they from being held theologians, that they do not even properly and directly belong to the church of Christ.

VIII. Next, the cultivation of most holy and most sweet communion with God, which is the initial blessedness of man, is another end of theology. This the first man enjoyed in the state of integrity, by the benefit of the original theology. That sinners might again attain communion with the most holy God, this new theology was instituted by him. That we proved at length before. Unless, therefore, a man has obtained spiritual fellowship [Greek: koinonia] with God through Christ, no man is furnished with that theology. And whether any unregenerate persons have spiritual communion with God, there is, so far as I know, no controversy among the regenerate.

IX. Finally, the last end of true theology is the praise of the glory of the grace of God, to be celebrated in the eternal salvation of theologians; that is, the very enjoyment of God to the praise of grace through Jesus Christ. If that light which the unregenerate have as their own can savingly direct them to this end, so that at last by its benefit they actually attain it, no one will deny that they are [ p. 428 ] theologians; but if not, they are to be dismissed to any other order of learned men whatsoever. The perception of political precepts is not civil prudence, nor is he straightway a good citizen who accurately unrolls Plato's books on the Republic and the Laws, or Cicero's; nor is he a Christian theologian who has a superficial notion and an acquaintance with terms, but nothing besides of the disciple of Christ or the true theologian; nay, such little men shamefully run against themselves, and against that saying of the philosopher: "For if the speech does not agree with the character, we shall seem to speak with an alien tongue, like the flute" (Philostratus, Life of the Critic).

Chapter argument: Who the regenerate are. In what sense the baptized are regenerate. For what reasons they were so called by the ancients. The errors of the papists noted. The truly regenerate never finally fall away. Not all the baptized are truly regenerate. Not every reformation of life is regeneration. What the common work of the Holy Spirit in the unregenerate is like. The office of the ministers of the word. A description of the regenerate. All men dead in sins. The pride of certain men noted. The most miserable condition of the unregenerate. The general nature and names of regeneration. Its author God himself, through the Spirit. Its degrees. The acknowledgment of one's most miserable state. Dreams divinely sent for the instruction of men, Job 33:15, 16. The works of providence, the preached word, means to the same end. From this first effect of the word very many recoil. The work of the law in the conversion of a sinner; and of the gospel. The nature and order of regeneration. The varied procedure of the Holy Spirit in regenerating sinners.

I. In the preceding discussion we set a hedge around the privilege and possession of the regenerate, lest there should be any encroachment upon it by an outsider. But who these regenerate are, all are not of the same opinion. We shall hasten out of the thorn thicket. For some, all who are dipped in baptismal water are regenerate. Evangelical baptism is indeed the symbol of spiritual regeneration [Greek: palingenesia]. In some sense, therefore, all the baptized may seem capable of being called regenerate; that many are born again is evident. From the ambiguous use of the word in the writings of very many of the ancients no small confusion has arisen. They almost everywhere pronounce all the baptized regenerate. For since it was both their duty to be born again, and through the public confession [Greek: exomologesis] of renewal they had obtained its symbol, there was no reason why they should not commonly be so called. For the state of these things was then, in the continual progress and daily extension of the gospel, far different from what it is nowadays. The greatest part of those whose enrollment [Greek: apographe] was made in the church were admitted to baptism only after a public profession of true faith, a confession of sincere repentance, and a promise of regeneration. So Justin Martyr, in his second Apology, among others, sets forth the admission of believers to baptism. "Whoever," he says, "are persuaded and believe that the things taught and said by us are true, and undertake that they are able so to live, are taught to pray and, fasting, to ask of God the forgiveness of their former sins, while we pray and fast together with them; then they are led by us to a place where there is water, and are regenerated in the same manner of regeneration in which we ourselves were regenerated." [ p. 429 ]

II. But now, almost everywhere in the world, we are all baptized as infants. Accordingly, the case of all partakers of baptism is by no means the same. On account of that solemn profession of regeneration made in the reception of baptism, which a manner of life proved among good men for some time, sometimes for the space of many years, consisting in the accurate observance of the evangelical precepts, showed to have been sincere and truly evangelical, and which was confirmed by that symbol according to the mind of Christ, most of the baptized were admitted into the roll of the regenerate. Hence they were called believers, saints, the enlightened. The pernicious notion that a man is necessarily born from above [Greek: anothen] by virtue of the baptismal administration, or, as they say, from the work performed [ex opere operato], prevailed later. And this very dangerous error, though ancient, the more recent apostates have covered over with one patch after another, lest the rain come through. By their help, from these beginnings men have arrived at the absolute necessity of external baptism, at midwives as its ministers, and at the Limbo of infants. But these men, while they contend that the Spirit is bound to one thing, have lost him altogether. Not so the ancients; in whose writings, nevertheless, that confusion very often occurs to which the assigning of what belongs in truth [Greek: kat' aletheian] only to the regenerate, to those who were regenerate only in repute [Greek: kata doxan], owes its origin. In more than one place Augustine removes the equivocation, and teaches that very many are called sons of God by us on account of the sacrament of regeneration, who in reality are not born of God. But from this source also arose no less an error concerning the apostasy of saints or of the regenerate. Without any circumlocution they admit it almost everywhere. And how indeed could it have been otherwise with those whose settled custom it was to call all admitted to the baptismal water regenerate, saints, and believers? But that the same writers teach that the true sons of God, united to Christ, partakers of the Spirit of promise, can never finally fall away, or pass into the possession of Satan, we showed in the preface to our book on the Perseverance of the Saints.

III. Whether, therefore, the baptized, that is, those who have been made partakers of the sacrament of initiation duly and according to the mind of Christ, can by the benefit of that alone be called regenerate in some sense, namely as far as the sacrament goes, I do not wish to make my quarrel. But that all the baptized, simply because they are baptized, unless there is added besides that efficacious operation of the Holy Spirit upon their hearts which we are presently to expound, are actually and savingly regenerate, we utterly deny. Nay, that anyone is born again by the force of the external administration, and so all who are made partakers of it, or that God is peremptorily bound [ p. 430 ] to the communication of life-giving grace through the dispensation of the sacrament, seems to be the opinion of those from whom the gospel is hidden. Nor indeed could the father of lies himself easily devise a more pernicious dogma, or one that would administer a more immediate poison to the souls of sinners. For while wretched men, dead in sins, flatter themselves that they were born again in baptism, and sleep at ease on both ears, holding the absolute and indispensable necessity of the spiritual renewal of the whole man in utter contempt, they neglect to acknowledge their most miserable state and to flee to the life-giving grace of Christ, and so, lulled by a most destructive security, they perish forever. Here, as everywhere, the papists flounder wretchedly. They judge that all the baptized are regenerate from the work performed [ex opere operato], as they say. But are they also saints? They will scarcely concede it; for what then would become of that canonization by which the pope enrolls whomever he pleases in the number of the divine? Yet in the evangelical sense all the regenerate are saints. By no means, they say. Why? They do not produce miracles. But that the working of miracles is the certain proof [Greek: tekmerion] of regeneration or of evangelical holiness, no one will ever affirm, except one who is utterly ignorant of both, and indeed of the whole gospel.

IV. The mere reception of the external rite, then, makes no one regenerate. If there is added to it the institution of a sober life, a change of morals for the better, the abjuration of those vices which produce scandal, the profession of the true faith or orthodox doctrine, the observance of the institutions of Christ among the parties which a man follows, then at least he in whom these things are present will be held regenerate by the suffrage of many. Where these things are wanting, indeed, there is no regeneration; for "he who is born of God does the works of God." But that all these things can exist where regeneration is not, he will concede who has been taught of God what it is "to be born again." For as faith which is alone, without good works, will never justify anyone before God, so good works which are done without faith will never prove anyone justified. Such reformation of a past life, and reclamation to good conduct, was very frequent in the schools of the ancient philosophers. So Pythagoras, so Socrates, so Plato, Aristotle, Zeno, Cleanthes, Epictetus, Apollonius brought back very many men, filthy with the squalor of vices, to the path of virtue. And among those who use the word of God in whatever manner, such corrections of morals are daily occurrences, where there is no creation of a new heart or saving renewal of the mind. Of these changes also, with respect to the vices which sinners renounce and the obedience which they embrace, there are various degrees, and various causes of them. To review them all here would be too long. For there is an efficacious operation of the Spirit of Christ through the word upon the hearts of unregenerate men which is not regeneration. First of all he irradiates their minds with evangelical light. Hence they are said to have been "enlightened" [Greek: photisthenai] (Heb. 6:4). That is, by some special gift he sharpens their understanding and gives it a peculiar keenness for understanding the will [ p. 431 ] of God revealed in the Scriptures. By the power of this light he provokes and goads their consciences to perform diligently their natural duty; and he affects them with a sense of sin, and with grief and a certain penitence for sins committed; he pricks their hearts, scourges them, does not allow them to sleep securely in their vices; moreover he allures them with the sweetness of the word, and with some gentle, evanescent taste and presentiment of heavenly things and joys to come, so that in many things they become obedient to the word of God: he also reduces them to bondage, striking into them the fear of death and hell; whence they are distressed, tormented, filled with horror; they bind themselves with vows to render obedience, follow the righteousness of the law, perform the prescribed duties, sometimes give themselves over to depraved superstitions, and in every way anxiously seek deliverance. These things, and others of this kind, the faithful ministers of God among us expound at length to the people, establish with testimonies and examples, and diligently warn the Christian common people not to allow themselves to be deceived by a shadow or mask of regeneration. But to pursue these matters further in this place does not belong to the plan of the work undertaken.

V. Let us say, then, briefly, in the next place, according to the measure of the gift [Greek: charisma] we have received, and that light, however small, which God will graciously deign to bestow on us out of his own light, who those regenerate are, whom alone we assert to be capable of this divine wisdom. The Holy Spirit openly testifies that all men are born in a state of sin, that is, both destitute of natural righteousness and infected with the corruption of a depraved nature, and so liable to the punishment of sin, which is the everlasting wrath of God. Against his most evident testimonies some struggle, relying on the varnish of the most frigid little distinctions; but ungratefully and ineptly. Those, therefore, whom a blind self-love holds so fast, arising from the deepest ignorance of the spiritual state of their own heart toward God, of the nature of the law of God, and of evangelical grace, that they deny that they are either polluted with the stain of natural corruption, or held by the snares of spiritual impotence [Greek: adynamia], nay of depravity, to such a degree that they can understand, will, or perceive nothing good, we have judged should here simply be passed over. But I fear lest, "because they say they see, their sin should remain," and on account of sin the most just wrath of God be revealed at the appointed time (John 3:36; 9:41). On this hypothesis concerning the most miserable natural state of all men, all evangelical theology rests; nor without the acknowledgment of it, and a most weighty sense of it, can that theology be either understood or observed. For what? Do the healthy need a physician, the righteous repentance, the pure sanctification, the living a life-giving Spirit, the innocent satisfaction, those who see an eye-salve? Do those fit of themselves to render due obedience need the saving, heart-turning grace and efficacious power of God, the friends of God reconciliation, or the blessed deliverance from the curse? I have very recently read disputers of this sort, whom I heartily pitied. Suffer me not, Lord Jesus, by your infinite mercy, me, the first [Greek: protos] and greatest of sinners, to spend even a moment's effort in proving that I am bound to you under fewer titles [ p. 432 ] than I really am, or than it is more desirable to me than life and soul that I should be! Now what all men are like in the state of nature can be understood from those reasons by which we proved above that the unregenerate are incapable of this theology. They are dead, blind, stupid, haters of God and obstinate enemies, void of all saving grace and light, slaves of Satan, prone to every vice, and utterly estranged from every spiritual good. And in this state, however much very many of them are highly pleased with themselves and live in delights, yet they will not, they cannot please God. This is the kingdom of Satan, this the power of darkness, from which all who by the grace of God are to be regenerated must be translated into the glorious kingdom of Christ and his marvelous light. That translation is regeneration; which is also designated new creation, or the new creature, renewal, resurrection from the dead, the opening of the eyes, the circumcision of the heart, vivification: variously, namely, as it has respect either to God the author of the work, or to man its subject. The author of the work is God himself. Hence to be born again and to be begotten of God are the same. And this work of his God accomplishes through the Spirit and the word: "Born again, not of corruptible seed, but of incorruptible, through the living word of God" (1 Pet. 1:23); that is, "through the washing of regeneration and the renewing of the Holy Spirit" (Tit. 3:5); and so sinners are regenerated "not of blood, nor of the will of the flesh, nor of the will of man, but of God" (John 1:13).

VI. Now there are, as it were, two parts of that most miserable condition and state in which we are all born. For there is in it misery on account of sin committed, and therefore on account of all communion with God lost; and there is a total impotence [Greek: adynamia] toward every spiritual good, born of the loss of the practical principle fit for working well and spiritually the things of God, which an habitual propensity to every evil accompanies with an indissoluble bond. Hence the whole man is misery and sin, and nothing besides. God, destining a twofold remedy for this twofold evil, produces from a twofold efficacious operation a twofold effect, opposed to each of those evils. How this comes about we shall show. Not to know one's natural misery is part of natural misery. From that ignorance arise pride and self-love [Greek: philautia]. Every sinner, dreaming, naturally persuades himself that he either is already, or can be, or will be whenever he wishes, in the best state and condition, or at least not in the worst; or else he counts all these things as nothing. Here if a man perchance, as rather often happens even in those places where liberty has been granted to evangelical preaching (and it is of these alone that the present discussion proceeds), has lighted upon a teacher like himself, who, puffed up with some disciplinary science of theological matters, hawks himself about as a minister of the new testament, while really destitute of the Spirit of Christ and so of all saving acquaintance [Greek: epignosis] with the will of God and the mystery of the gospel, that teacher will so soothe the man's mind, and entice him into deception with the hope of obtaining eternal salvation in the state in which he sticks fast, that, casting away all care of saving renewal, he will not only sleep secure on both ears, [ p. 433 ] but will not hesitate to rage against others who, suggesting more wholesome counsels from the word of God, would wish to bring him back to a better course. Hence with some teachers and their disciples, in this very light of the gospel, regeneration is almost a laughingstock. And yet it is here that God first sets the sinner who is to be regenerated. Through his word he summons the sinful soul to a serious sense of itself, and of the state in which it lives before God, the most just Ruler and Judge of all. Hence the first knowledge of God settles in the practical judgment, not without horror of death and fear of eternal punishment. And it is no small undertaking to awaken a man dead in sins, full of himself, enslaved to vices, secure about eternity, to the point of instituting a serious examination of himself in order to the last judgment. In the modes and means which God uses in producing this effect, his most free will and infinite wisdom show themselves. Before the word was set down in writing, it was his custom to fix in men testimonies of his justice and terror through dreams divinely sent. The holy man in Job teaches us this (Job 33:15-17): "In a dream, in a vision of the night, when deep sleep falls upon men, in slumberings upon the bed; then he will uncover the ear of men, and seal it with his bond, to call man back from his work, and that he may hide pride from man, that he may keep back his soul from the pit." Hence that line of Homer: "for the dream too is from Zeus" (Iliad 1.63).

And there was a peculiar kind of dreams, which the ancients called dreams sent from Zeus [Greek: oneiroi diopemptoi]. "Such dreams," says Eustathius on Iliad 1, "properly possessed the name of dream [oneiros], as speaking and declaring what is [to on], that is, the truth; wherefore such a dream was also called a divine voice [omphe], because it makes the truth appear in the dream."

VII. The external works of providence, by which God executes either his kindness and mercy or his judgments and wrath, produce the same effect. So the holy man in the place cited (Job 33:19-23): "He is chastened with pain upon his bed, and the multitude of his bones with pain, and his life abhors bread, and his soul desirable food. His flesh is consumed so that it cannot be seen; and his bones, which were not seen, are laid bare; and his soul draws near to the pit, and his life to the destroyers. Then," etc. The Psalmist also (Ps. 78:33, 34): "He consumed their days in vanity, and their years in terror. When he slew them, they sought him." The prophet has similar things (Isa. 26:10, 11). For dispensations of divine providence of this kind often so affect the minds of sinful men that, though "they have bronze and triple iron about the heart" [adapting Horace, Odes 1.3.9], they cannot restrain themselves from trembling seriously before God and shuddering at the judgment to come. But for the most part this is the work and effect of the preached word of God. So the apostle teaches us (1 Cor. 14:24, 25), and the prophet (Hos. 6:5). That the same force or energy accompanies the preaching of the word we shall see daily. [ p. 434 ]

VIII. In the sinner who turns these cares over in his breast, among the inmost reasonings of his heart, there arises a sharp suspicion whether he has God for an enemy or not. He now at last begins to understand that "it is an evil and bitter thing that he has sinned against God" (Jer. 2:19). Renewing, therefore, the memory of very many sins, perhaps buried in long oblivion, he is entangled in the secret bites of conscience and in troublesome thoughts. "What have I done," he says, "what am I doing, or what am I about to do again? I know not where to turn. What if I should find God angry, and so fall into eternal punishments? What if those hopes with which I have hitherto soothed my sick soul should fail, and be found deceitful? Without doubt I must enter on another way of life, unless I prefer to perish." These things, I say, and the like, sinners, as if thunderstruck by the power of the divine word, turn over in the depths of their breasts. But of those who are thus variously touched with the sense of their own condition, many, overcome by the force of inborn blindness, which still prevents spiritual things from being seen in their own proper light and splendor, of carnal concupiscence, of the desire of earthly things, of satanic temptations, and by the fellowship of evil men, spring back again and fall into their former insensibility [Greek: anaisthesia]. Hence their recovery to a good course is difficult, and the end is very often worse than the beginning (Jer. 8:9).

IX. This, however, is as it were the first skirmish of the Spirit of God against the power of darkness in the hearts of sinners; the first attempt, as it were, in destroying the works of Satan, when the stronger one girds himself to bind the strong man armed. Upon these follow stronger and more efficacious goings forth of spiritual power. We have thus far shown only the wandering and unstable reasonings by which the thoughtful soul rouses itself to carry through some sort of examination of itself. Such are wont to be the fruits of the first admonitions, like the uncertain movements of men just waking. There are other "sharp arrows" of Christ (Ps. 45:5, 6), which he so fixes in the hearts of sinners that, however much they may wish, they cannot shake them out. Using these, he pricks, wounds, and terrifies the consciences and minds of men with the sense of sin, the fear of death, and a horrible foreboding of the judgment to come. See Acts 24:25. For laying bare the nature, guilt, and foulness of sin in the inmost recesses of the soul, and moreover recalling to the sinner's mind the multitude of sins of which he is himself conscious, and the innumerable circumstances of his sins that heap up guilt, he denounces against him the penalty of death, cuts off all hope of escape, and forces him to feel himself on every side most miserable and on the point of perishing. And now whatever the human nature can conceive of horrid, dreadful, and mournful, that hovers continually before the sinner's eyes: "through fear of death they were all their lifetime subject to bondage" (Heb. 2:15). Now this is the special effect of the word or doctrine of the law: "I had not known sin, except through the law" (Rom. 7:7); for the knowledge of sin is through the law; that kind of knowledge, namely, from which conviction, or the practical sense of sin, arises; by which God, casting his net over sinners, [ p. 435 ] forces them to come down like the birds of the heavens (Hos. 7:12). For after he has, as it were, compelled the sinful soul, by the powerful and efficacious force of his word, seriously to weigh the nature and guilt of its sin, and has given it understanding, that in the mirror of the law it may contemplate sin's depravity and desert, which the apostle ascribes to "the coming of the law" (Rom. 7:9), and has awakened the conscience to do its duty diligently; then with legal sorrow, fear, sadness, anxiety, and the other affections that afflict human nature, often greater than the names available for expressing them, he so affects the very inner chambers of the heart that it is never free from the utmost distress: "they were pricked in their heart" [Greek: katenygesan te kardia] (Acts 2:37). "What shall I do now," says the sinner, "caught unexpectedly by my own self in a most miserable state? I am tortured in mind; no counsel holds firm in my breast. God, whom I dreamed to be easy and propitious to me, I find a most hostile enemy; the law too, of which I formerly took no account, I find not only holy and pure, but fiery and mighty, all whose transgressions can be expiated only by death, and that eternal; horror, darkness, the pains of hell surround me, torture me, tear me in pieces." So the apostle in Romans 7:9: "I was alive once without the law; but when the commandment came, sin revived, and I died." I counted myself alive, sound, righteous, pleasing to God; but at the coming of the law that hope perished and was cut off. Hence arises a desire of deliverance, and an anxious expectation of the outcome, nearer to despair than to hope: "What shall we do?" (Acts 2:37); "What must I do to be saved?" (Acts 16:30). Here again come prayers from the bottom of the breast, tears and sighs, divine and human duties diligently performed; for they resolve to obtain righteousness before God in any way whatsoever (Rom. 9:31). And yet, when matters have come thus far, the work of conversion to God is not always carried through to completion. For many, ensnared again by the allurements of pleasures, shaking out of their breasts this whole beginning of a divine work, whatever it may be, return again to the wallowing place of vices, to perish forever (2 Pet. 2:20, 21). Some are pleased with a righteousness "as if by the works of the law" (Rom. 9:32), and while they busy themselves to establish this (a crime and folly of corrupted nature which among very many has passed into an ecclesiastical dogma), they do not submit themselves to the righteousness of God (Rom. 10:3). And so, in the very midst of their efforts to reach heaven, efforts often most strenuous, sometimes horrible and cruel (Mic. 6:6, 7), they hasten to hell. For though they run, driven by the bites and piercing goads of conscience, those who run outside the ways of the Sun of righteousness must needs perish. The case of those whom God wills efficaciously and graciously to lead to the saving knowledge of Christ is different. For first, by his Spirit working powerfully in their hearts, he brings it about that they acknowledge and confess the sentence of the law pronounced against them to be most just and most equitable, not only with respect to actual sins, but with respect to their whole natural condition and state (Rom. 7:12, 13); then also that they justify God himself, the author of the law (Rom. 3:20), and stop their own mouths with the sense of guilt (ver. 19). [ p. 436 ] Taught, therefore, that all things are to be expected from the most free grace and good pleasure of God, they endeavor to depend wholly on his will, and not to seek the remedies of their evils, for which they pant, in a disordered or impatient way (Hab. 2:2-4). That consternation of mind, that dejection of spirit, that fear and trembling of heart, that confusion of face, that shame of countenance, that despair of themselves, by all of which they are all but overwhelmed, permit nothing but a humble and submissive awaiting of the divine sentence concerning them (Luke 18:13; Ps. 130). This only they seem to hold fixed and settled: that if it should please God to slay them, they will nevertheless by no means desert him. And now day by day the soul, laboring under the burden of sins, groaning, growing faint, pants more and more for deliverance, for free mercy, for righteousness, for acceptance before God. The Holy Spirit, therefore, was promised by Christ, to recall to mind and suggest to sinners the things that pertain to spiritual consolation (John 14:26). He begins again and again secretly and efficaciously to insinuate into the heart of the fainting sinner some evangelical word; such as that of Psalm 130:4, "With you is forgiveness itself, O Lord, that you may be feared." The sinner's conscience, indeed, immediately cries out against it, and the law itself as well; both forbid him to hope anything concerning forgiveness or pardon; both loudly and continually proclaim that no sinner will find any such thing with God. It cannot be, they say, that so great a sinner as you should obtain pardon from the most holy and most just God; die, therefore, as the law commands: "For the wages of sin is death" (Rom. 6:23). But here, when the sinner seems again driven to his last move, that very Spirit, who up to this point brought reinforcements to the law and to the man's own conscience against him, and rendered them efficacious and powerful for crushing his hard and obstinate heart and shaking out every opinion of his own righteousness, now, "lest he be swallowed up" [Greek: mepos katapothe], stands on the other side; and against the accusations and bites of conscience, the sentence of the law, and the temptations of Satan, he strengthens, sustains, and establishes the man, now almost sinking, nay often conquered and surrendering, by various props of hope and patience supplied from beneath. For since sinners, laden with the sense of sins and of divine wrath, do not, as soon as they attempt to betake themselves to the divine mercy, feel themselves embraced with favor and grace, but rather feel themselves sent far away by God as unworthy; lest by the craft of Satan, who most greedily seizes for his own purpose that repulse which they fear, but without cause, they should be hurled headlong into the pit of despair, the Holy Spirit gradually and imperceptibly, through various means, administers general supports. Here the evangelical proclamations have their place (Isa. 55:1; John 7:37); declarations (John 3:16; Ps. 130:4); exhortations (Acts 2:38); invitations (Matt. 11:28, 29; Rev. 22:17); and promises. All of these the Holy Spirit uses in the sinner's translation "out of the power of darkness into the marvelous light of Christ." At last, moreover, the same Spirit causes Christ himself, perhaps long before preached, but on account of blindness of mind, hardness of heart, love of sin or of one's own righteousness [ p. 437 ] neglected, at least not prized with that submission of soul which is fitting, to be observed by these sinners, set before their eyes in the promises of the gospel. And when, miserable, lost, weary, accursed, they behold him lifted up on high, as it were "from the ends of the earth" (Isa. 45:22), and see that a propitiation has been set forth in his blood, to declare the righteousness of God in the remission of sins (Rom. 3:24, 25); and that by it he was made a curse, that the blessing of faithful Abraham might come upon believers (Gal. 3:13, 14); and when they see and acknowledge God appeased in him, reconciling the world to himself, they long for him alone, and by him they flee to God, properly speaking, as those guilty of death once fled to the cities of refuge. They believe that he has abundantly satisfied the justice of God for their sins, the greatest of them, all of them, and that in his own blood he has obtained eternal redemption (Heb. 9:12); and that thus he has been made by God righteousness and redemption to all who believe in him (1 Cor. 1:30). Hence arise shame, love, displeasure with self, humiliation, the renunciation of one's own righteousness, repentance; and having received Christ by faith alone, they receive him also to be loved and worshiped; they trust him, and to him they wholly hand themselves over, to be taught, ruled, sanctified, corrected, and saved.

X. But in the meantime, while these things are being transacted between God and the sinful soul through the word, God himself, according to his infinite mercy in Jesus Christ, when and how he wills, in a manner plainly ineffable but powerful and efficacious, through his most holy Spirit, endows the soul, formerly dead in sins, with a new spiritual life and a new heart, opens its blind eyes, and shines wonderfully into the whole of it, to give the light of his glory in the face of Jesus Christ. Hence the whole man is made a new creature, fit and suitable to obey God rightly according to the rule of the new covenant (Jer. 32:39; Ezek. 11:19; 36:26; Acts 26:18; Rom. 8:2, 11; 2 Cor. 4:6; Eph. 2:2-5; 4:23, 24; 2 Cor. 5:17). And the sinner, in this manner brought back to life and born again through the grace of God, furnished with spiritual strength or an infused habit of grace, according to the will of him who works all things in all, now united to Christ through the indwelling Spirit, gives and consecrates himself to evangelical obedience and good works, for the diligent performance of which he was created in Christ (John 14:16, 17; Rom. 6:2, 6; Eph. 2:10; Tit. 2:11, 12; 1 John 3:3). "And so he who is born of the Spirit is spirit" (John 3:6). But these things do not proceed in the same way in all who are to be regenerated. There are also many things in this wonderful work whose manner we do not know; very many things which we feel but cannot utter: "The wind blows wherever it pleases; so also is everyone who is born of the Spirit." One man the same Spirit delivers sooner, another later, from the straits of the new and spiritual birth. One he beats, pricks, and presses with sharp sorrow; another he handles more gently, as seems good to him. But enough of these things.
