Chapter argument: Theology an interweaving [Greek: symploke] of spiritual gifts. Gifts extraordinary or ordinary. The ordinary either peculiar to the ministry or common. Christ the bestower of all gifts, Ps. 68:19; Acts 2:33; Eph. 4:8. The Hebrew laqach signifies both to receive and to give. Christ the author of spiritual gifts through the Spirit, John 7:39; Acts 19:2; John 16:14; 1 Cor. 12:11. That theology is a spiritual gift is proved, Matt. 13:11; James 1:5, 17; 1 John 5:20; 1 Cor. 12:8; 1 Cor. 1:5; Rom. 6:17; Eph. 1:17-20. The nature and increase of spiritual gifts [Greek: charismata]. The foundation of evangelical theology, saving light. Its progress through superadded gifts. The special nature of theology. We must teach the things which the Holy Spirit teaches. The many names [Greek: polyonymia] of theology; its opposites. A definition. It is wisdom, Deut. 4:6; 1 Cor. 2:7; 2 Tim. 3:15; Dan. 12:3; Ps. 19:8, 9; Deut. 4:6; Ps. 119:98, 99; Prov. 8, 9; Col. 1:9; 3:16. Its modes of communication. Its immediate object. Its primary effect. It renders the mind conformed to Christ, another effect. The knowledge of the will of God revealed in the sacred Scriptures. What kind of knowledge that is. A new understanding. The practice of piety, Job 28:12-23, with John 7:17; 8:31, 32; Col. 1:9, 10; 2 Pet. 1:8, 9. A recapitulation [Greek: anakephalaiosis] of the whole dissertation. A crowning conclusion.

I. We define the subject of evangelical theology to be the mind of man born again through the Holy Spirit. Let us now approach theology itself. It is a spiritual gift [Greek: charisma pneumatikon], or rather an interweaving [Greek: symploke] of spiritual gifts. And since those spiritual gifts are either extraordinary or ordinary, we have to treat only of the latter. These are certain spiritual powers [Greek: dynameis] of the mind, implanted in it by the Holy Spirit, by which it is rendered fit and suitable for the perception and comprehension of spiritual things, and for their application to spiritual uses and ends. Of those gifts which are mentioned in the sacred Scriptures there are, according to various respects and various uses, various distributions. That matter, since it does not directly pertain to our purpose, let us here pass by. Let it suffice to have noted only this: that some of them regard in a special manner the office of the evangelical ministry (of which they are the foundation, Eph. 4:8, 11; 1 Cor. 12:7), while others are common to all believers, to each according to his degree and measure. The power of conferring these gifts is the fruit and companion of the exaltation of our Lord Jesus Christ at the right hand of the Father, the most eminent part of his royal inheritance. So the Psalmist (Ps. 68:19 [English 68:18]): "You ascended on high, you led captivity captive: laqachta mattanot ba'adam, you received gifts in man." Which place Peter interprets (Acts 2:33): "Being therefore exalted by the right hand of God, and having received from the Father the promise of the Holy Spirit, he has poured out this which you now see and hear." "This" was those gifts of tongues and prophecy, by the sight and hearing of which the Jews were astonished. He does not, indeed, expressly mention the gifts themselves received from Christ, but the promised Spirit, who works them all immediately in everyone. Paul repeats the words (Eph. 4:8): "Ascending on high he led captivity captive, and gave gifts to men." He departs, however, from the most usual signification of the word [ p. 439 ] laqach, in order to set forth the matter itself more plainly. I wonder that this passage in the ancient Greek version which is ascribed to the Seventy elders escaped those who thought it their business to insert the testimonies cited from the Old Testament in the New into that version in the very words used by the inspired [Greek: theopneustoi] writers; for it still reads "he received gifts" [Greek: domata elabe], not "he gave gifts" [Greek: edoke domata], as in the apostle. But laqach sometimes signifies to give, at least to receive with the mind and purpose of giving: Hosea 14:3 [English 14:2], veqach-tov, "receive good," that is, "give us grace"; or as our countrymen render the sense, "Receive us graciously," that is, "bestow grace and favor upon us." Exodus 25:2: "Speak to the children of Israel, veyiqchu-li terumah, that they take for me an offering," that is, "that they give one." Hence "the verb of receiving signifies giving when they receive from elsewhere in order to give," say the masters of the Hebrews. Paul therefore reported the sense of the words accurately, and so translated the words themselves. Christ received all spiritual gifts from the Father, that he might give them to men; Christ, therefore, is the author of all spiritual gifts. And with the authority of this conferring of the Spirit he was conspicuously furnished after his ascension. Hence that saying of the evangelist: "The Holy Spirit was not yet, because Jesus was not yet glorified" (John 7:39); that is, not yet given and poured out in the manner of which he speaks. And this was the ignorance of the disciples of John, recorded in Acts 19:2: "But," they say, "we have not even heard whether there is a Holy Spirit." Do not suspect that men brought up in the bosom of the Jewish church, initiated by the baptism of John, were ignorant whether the Holy Spirit really existed or not; they knew the Spirit, only they had heard nothing at all of his extraordinary gifts. Now these ordinary gifts the Holy Spirit, in the name and place of Christ (John 16:14), to whom he renders, as Tertullian speaks, a vicarious service, distributes most freely to whomever he wills [Greek: kathos bouletai] (1 Cor. 12:11). "But to each one is given the manifestation of the Spirit for profit" (ver. 7).

II. Theology, as we said, is a certain interweaving of these gifts, in which, as the virtues are contained in justice, all are present: "To you it is given to know the mysteries of the kingdom of heaven" (Matt. 13:11); that is, to you it is given to be evangelical theologians. "If any of you lacks wisdom, let him ask of God who gives" (James 1:5). That this wisdom is theology we have shown, and shall further prove. Concerning which again (ver. 17): "Every good giving and every perfect gift is from above, coming down from the Father of lights." 1 John 5:20: "We know that the Son of God has come, and has given us an understanding, that we may know him who is true." Now "to know him who is true" is theology, or I confess that I am wholly ignorant of what theology is. 1 Corinthians 12:8: "To one is given through the Spirit the word of wisdom"; 1 Corinthians 1:5; Romans 6:17. The apostle sets the whole matter before our eyes in Ephesians 1:17-20. "I pray God," he says, [ p. 440 ] "that he may give you the Spirit of wisdom and revelation in the knowledge of him; the eyes of your understanding being enlightened, that you may know what is the hope of his calling, and what the riches of his inheritance in the saints, and what the surpassing greatness of his power toward us who believe." Would that some men would, or could, learn from these words of the apostle in what darkness they dwell.

III. This theology of ours, then, is a spiritual gift [Greek: charisma pneumatikon] (1 Cor. 2:7-9). And since that almighty Spirit of God works all things in all, in order that we may fix the distinction between this gift and those very many gifts which he distributes to merely natural [Greek: psychikoi] men for various uses, we have shown that it proceeds and is given from Christ the mediator. This anointing is from that Holy One (1 John 2:20).

IV. Now the nature of these gifts is such that they are created immediately in the minds of men by the Holy Spirit himself, and therefore can be obtained by no one by his own effort, however strong he may be in the use of reason; as a learned man once said shrewdly of the rational soul, "They are created by infusing, and infused by creating": yet they are capable of degrees and increases. With respect to these, the assiduous use of those means which God has instituted for increasing them is not only eminently useful, but absolutely necessary. Let the theology of which we are treating serve as an example. Let its principle, foundation, and root, in which all its nature and prolific power is contained, be that saving light by which the mind of the sinner is rendered apt and fit to understand spiritually [Greek: pneumatikos] the things that are of the Spirit of God. This light no mortal can acquire for himself by his own powers, or by the benefit of any common assistance whatsoever. God, who in the first creation of all things commanded light to come forth out of darkness, irradiates our minds with this most holy light by no less an exercise and efficacious going forth of his infinite power (2 Cor. 4:6). But after the mind of man has been furnished with saving light, then, according to the measure and nature of the other gifts either superadded to it or communicated together with it, and the efficacy of the other means, the light itself also is daily increased, and the mind perceives and discerns spiritual objects more and more clearly. And from this let us learn what is the true use of diligence, studies, and sciences in learning theology. So far as they serve to promote spiritual light, just so far, and not a whit further, do they have their use here.

V. But let us now at last look more closely at the special nature of theology. Here it is our settled resolve to follow, as far as may be, the guidance of the Holy Spirit, and to use those words which "the wisdom from above" [Greek: he anothen sophia] teaches. For since I hold it as a thing most fully ascertained that the sacred Scripture expounds this spiritual habit of the regenerate mind in a manner entirely different from all the rules of human wisdom [Greek: anthropine sophia], or of commonly received philosophy; and that the sense of that exposition is far more open and perspicuous to the understanding of believers than are the exact, thorny, and artificial definitions of the philosophizers; and that no one will ever properly, rightly, and savingly attain the mind of the Holy Spirit who drags his sayings to be hewn to the Lesbian rule [the pliable leaden rule of Lesbian masons, proverbial for a standard bent to fit the object] of philosophical notions and testimonies, [ p. 441 ] I have determined to tread in the footsteps of Scripture, to use its words, and to be unconcerned about their philosophical sense.

VI. First, therefore, its many names [Greek: polyonymia] come to be considered. For theology is designated by many names by the Holy Spirit. It is called "wisdom," and "prudence," and "knowledge," and "a habit for the discerning of good and evil," and "doctrine," and "light," and "understanding," and "the indwelling word," and "sense," and "the acknowledgment of the truth," and "the fear of God." That very many of these signify different things among the philosophers no one doubts; it is evident also that some of them cannot be said of any science properly so called. To these, moreover, the Holy Spirit opposes not only ignorance of the knowable object, but also "blindness," "folly," "vanity," and "darkness of mind," nay even "depravity of heart," "love of sin," and "the friendship of this world." You will say, perhaps, that although all these terms can be employed in a general description of theology, yet if we wish to speak accurately and properly, we shall find it comprehended under some one habit. I for my part would not deny that some single denomination, which, being more general than the rest, offers the understanding a greater light upon the thing itself, may be employed in setting forth its nature; provided only that this is by no means done with the rejection of the rest, or so that theology itself is twisted to philosophical senses. For beyond all doubt, if it is science, and that either speculative or practical properly so called, it is not wisdom according to the dogmas of the philosophers. And if it is wisdom, it is not prudence; it is not a habit [Greek: hexis] for discerning good and evil; it is not understanding; and yet we know that it is all of these; for we shall labor in vain if we think that we can philosophize in these matters more accurately than we have been taught by the Holy Spirit.

VII. Evangelical theology, therefore, is "a spiritual gift implanted by the Holy Spirit in the name of Christ in the minds of believing men, that is, of those born again through the grace of God, by which they are made wise, prudent, and understanding in the acknowledgment of the mystery of godliness, that is, of God and of his will revealed in Christ through the gospel; rendering the whole soul conformed to the truth acknowledged, and wise unto eternal life, and directing them to render the obedience and worship owed to God in Christ, according to the sole rule of the word, through the Holy Spirit, that at last they may enjoy God forever."

VIII. That the mind of the regenerate man is the proper subject of this theology, we proved above. We have also shown that it is the gift of the Holy Spirit, who acts among men in the stead of Christ. It is peculiar to that gift that by it he renders believers wise in the mystery of the gospel, that is, of Christ, in whom all the treasures of knowledge and wisdom are hidden. We say, therefore, in the first place, that theology is spiritual wisdom; so the Scripture calls it. Deuteronomy 4:6: "This is your wisdom and your understanding" (or prudence) [Hebrew: hi chokhmatkhem uvinatkhem]. 1 Corinthians 2:7: "We speak wisdom [ p. 442 ] in a mystery." And he who is furnished with it is called wise: 2 Timothy 3:15, "You have learned the sacred writings, which are able to make you wise" [Greek: hiera grammata, ta dynamena se sophisai]; and understanding, whence "the understanding ones" [Hebrew: maskilim] (Dan. 12:3). And the faithful word of God is called "making wise the simple" [Hebrew: machkimat peti] and "enlightening the eyes" [Hebrew: me'irat enayim] (Ps. 19:8, 9 [English 19:7, 8]). For this reason the people of God are called "a wise and prudent people" [Hebrew: am chakham venavon] (Deut. 4:6). Psalm 119:98: "You will make me wise by your precepts" [Hebrew: techakkemeni mitsvotekha], says David. Verse 99: "I have more understanding than all my teachers" [Hebrew: mikkol-melammedai hiskalti]. For this reason, among others, Christ himself is called wisdom, because, namely, he alone instructs men in this wisdom (Prov. 8, 9). And this is what the apostle intends in that solemn prayer which he pronounces for the saints at Colossae (Col. 1:9): "We do not cease to pray for you, and to ask that you may be filled with the knowledge of his will in all wisdom and spiritual understanding." And again (Col. 3:16): "Let the word of Christ dwell in you richly, in all wisdom."

IX. Now the mode of communication peculiar to this wisdom proves that it stands at the greatest distance from all secular wisdom and from systematic or disciplinary science. "We have the mind of Christ," says the apostle (1 Cor. 2:16); that is, we know, we understand. And how so? Because, he says (ver. 12), "We have not received the spirit of the world," namely the spirit that is the author of worldly wisdom, "but the Spirit who is from God, that we may know the things freely given to us by God." Only through the Holy Spirit graciously granted to us do we attain this wisdom (John 16:13). And he produces this effect in us in a manner altogether wonderful. So the same apostle teaches us (2 Cor. 4:6): "For God, who commanded light to shine out of darkness, is he who has shined in our hearts, to give the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ," etc. The manner of the production of this saving light is no other than that of light out of the midst of darkness, of old, in the first founding of all things. And the knowledge which is effected is "of the glory of God, in the face of Jesus Christ." For it is the fruit of an efficacious divine irradiation illuminating our minds. And again the same apostle (Eph. 1:17, 18): "May God give you the Spirit of wisdom and revelation in the knowledge of him (that is, of Christ), the eyes of your understanding being enlightened." For this gift is from the Father through Christ. And there is given the Spirit of wisdom and revelation; who, namely, through the revelation of the mystery of the gospel renders us wise, not with bare knowledge [Greek: gnosis], but also with full acknowledgment [Greek: epignosis], that is, the acknowledgment of Christ. There is nothing human here, nothing that savors in any way of human wisdom [Greek: anthropine sophia].
X. In this manner the regenerate are made wise, prudent, and understanding, through the communication of the Spirit of wisdom and revelation, whom the world cannot receive.

XI. Let us also briefly and separately dispatch the immediate object of this wisdom, although it has been plainly enough indicated in what precedes. The sacred Scripture calls it generally "all things" [Greek: panta]: 1 John 2:20, [ p. 443 ] "You have an anointing from the Holy One, and you know all things"; and ver. 27, "The anointing teaches you concerning all things"; that is, the things which are necessary for the worship of God, our obedience, consolation, and salvation. Also "all the truth" (John 16:13); namely, all that truth which pertains to the spiritual kingdom of Christ.

XII. Thus believers are initiated into the sacred things of Christ. For "it is given" to them "to know the mysteries of the kingdom of heaven" (Mark 4:11). Mysteries not revealed in ancient ages (Rom. 16:25). "We speak," says the apostle, "wisdom in a mystery" (1 Cor. 2:7; Eph. 3:9). And Colossians 2:2, 3: "That you may be established in love, and unto all the riches of the full assurance of understanding, unto the acknowledgment of the mystery of God and the Father and of Christ; in whom are all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge hidden." The foundation is understanding [Greek: synesis], or spiritual intelligence. This, through the grace of God, has joined to it full assurance [Greek: plerophoria], and this brings forth acknowledgment [Greek: epignosis], or the practical recognition of the truth; which truth indeed is the mystery of God, and of the Father, and of the Son, that is, of God revealing himself to us as Father in the Son: for to this we are led through the knowledge of Christ, in whom all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge are hidden; as we showed at length in our book on the Communion of the Saints with God the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit. This mystery-laden [Greek: mysteriodes] doctrine, I say, is the most proper [Greek: idiotaton] object of evangelical theology, namely those "depths of God" [Greek: ta bathe tou Theou] which the Spirit searches, that he may reveal them to us (1 Cor. 2:10).

XIII. Now through this wisdom, by which the regenerate mind is illuminated with the knowledge of the evangelical mystery, the whole man is rendered conformed to heavenly truth. This is the first and most proper effect of this theology. Through it the image of God, formerly lost through sin, is renewed again. We showed in the first book that natural or implanted [Greek: emphytos] theology, considered materially, as they say, was that image of God after which the first man was created. The knowledge of God, together with the faculty of living readily according to the rule of that knowledge, completed it. The obliteration of that image of God through sin we pursued at length above. That we cannot be renewed into the image of Christ except through Christ, all confess. He is the image of God (2 Cor. 4:4); "the image of God" [Greek: eikon tou Theou] (Col. 1:15); "the brightness of his glory and the express character of his subsistence" [Greek: apaugasma tes doxes kai charakter tes hypostaseos autou] (Heb. 1:3). And the gospel is the "image" of Christ, or his "face," in which "the glory of the Father shines forth" (2 Cor. 4:6); or a mirror, in which Christ himself (and therefore the glory of the Father) is exhibited to the renewed mind for contemplation (2 Cor. 3:18). And through the beholding of this image, which we have through this wisdom, the same image is brought into our minds: "We are transformed into the same image, from glory to glory." And the new man is renewed "unto acknowledgment, according to the image of him who created him" (Col. 3:10). That is, that image of God which consisted in natural theology, and has been lost, is renewed through this acknowledgment [Greek: epignosis], which renders our minds conformed to Christ, who is the image of God. [ p. 444 ] The holiness also of which we are made partakers in this renewal is called "the holiness of truth" [Greek: hosiotes tes aletheias] (Eph. 4:24), because it proceeds from that truth which, exercising its power in our minds, brings into them the image of Christ. For since it has pleased God to express his image in Christ by the word of the gospel, that word in our hearts, as if quickened, becomes living and efficacious grace, and the whole soul is delivered over into its mold [Greek: typos] (Rom. 6:17). Hence arise acknowledgment [Greek: epignosis], or the recognition of the implanted truth; and certainty [Greek: asphaleia], from the real and most present efficacy of the truth; also perception [Greek: aisthesis], or a spiritual sense and taste of the evangelical things themselves. This, I say, is the primary effect of this supernatural theology; and if it were unequal to producing it, it would not have been rightly substituted in the place of that natural theology which we lost.

XIV. Further, by the help of this wisdom we savingly understand the truth set before us, that is, the mind of God expounded in the sacred Scriptures. Hence it is called knowledge [Greek: gnosis], science, acquaintance, cognition, madda and da'at [Hebrew terms for knowledge]. For since believers have an understanding elevated and disposed by spiritual light to the saving perception of the evangelical mystery, it must be that they rightly understand the very doctrine in which that mystery is contained. This is that "knowledge of God" so often celebrated in sacred Scripture. But whoever should contend, from the use of this word, that this theology of ours is science according to the philosophical use of that term, and should thus force it, as it were, into the rank of the other sciences, would assuredly err grossly enough. Another matter is in hand here, as we have abundantly proved. Also, what from the act of the mind in perceiving its object is called knowledge, is, from the principle of acting, and the manner in which it is borne toward its object, called light. God has called us out of darkness "into his marvelous light" (1 Pet. 2:9). For as that darkness out of which we have been called was not only objective, that is, the mere ignorance of divine truth not yet revealed, but most of all interior, that is, the native blindness of our mind; so that marvelous light [Greek: phos thaumaston] into which we are called signifies not only the evangelical doctrine, but also that spiritual light by which we are made fit for rightly perceiving the glory of God. There enlightens us "the illumination of the gospel of the glory of Christ" (2 Cor. 4:4). There is in it an illumination [Greek: photismos], and it shines upon [Greek: augazei] our minds. The Holy Spirit sent Paul "to open the eyes" of the Gentiles, "to turn them from darkness to light" (Acts 26:18). The evangelical truth itself is light; and light is also communicated to those who are to believe, namely to open their eyes, that they may see that light, or "light in the light of God" (Ps. 36:9). And this spiritual light so affects the understanding itself, that it is called another and a new understanding (Ps. 119:18, 130). And "the Son of God has come, and has given us an understanding, that we may know him who is true" (1 John 5:20), as if, namely, we were not endowed with one before. At least he changes it so far that it goes forth able to perform its office rightly: "He opened their mind, to understand the Scriptures" (Luke 24:45); and "the Lord give you understanding in all things," Paul to Timothy (2 Tim. 2:7); and the Psalmist, "Make me understand [Hebrew: havineni], and I shall keep your law" [ p. 445 ] (Ps. 119:34 and 104). On account, therefore, of this light perfecting the understanding in its act, that knowledge by which believers perceive spiritual things spiritually stands at an immense distance from all human science, and not only by reason of the truth that is its object.

XV. Further, this wisdom draws with it, by an indissoluble bond, spiritual affections of the heart conformed to the truth acknowledged, because it effects them; and it makes and directs prudent theologians, with the whole soul engaged in cultivating communion with God through Christ, and in diligently rendering obedience according to the covenant of grace. For he at last is wise in the mystery of the gospel who so understands the counsel, love, and grace of God, in and through Christ, according to the revelation of them made in the word, that, affected with a spiritual and ineffable taste of them, he gives himself wholly to God in the obedience of faith. And in this practice of piety, or covenantal obedience, without which whatever is celebrated under that name is a bare name, or rather a certain shadow and mask of wisdom, consists all the vital exercise of this theology. Hence it is called not only "the acknowledgment of the truth which is according to godliness" (Tit. 1:1), but also piety, worship, and the fear of God; in which all its vital acts consist. Remarkable is that passage of Job, and worthy to be held in perpetual memory (Job 28:12-23): "Whence shall wisdom be found? and where is the place of understanding? Mortal man does not know its valuation; nor is it found in the land of the living. The deep says, It is not in me; and the sea says, It is not with me. Stored gold cannot be given for it, nor silver weighed out as its price. It cannot be valued with the fine gold of Ophir, with the precious onyx, or the sapphire. No one can estimate it in gold or the most brilliant gem; nor is its exchange vessels of the most refined gold. Let no mention be made of coral or of pearl; for the acquisition of wisdom is beyond carbuncles. No one can value it with the topaz of Ethiopia; it cannot be assessed with fine pure gold. Whence then would that wisdom come? and where would be the place of understanding? Since it is hidden from the eyes of every living thing, concealed even from the bird of the heavens. Destruction and death say, We have heard its fame with our ears. God alone understands its way, and he knows its place." Verse 28: "And he said to man, Behold, the fear of the Lord, that is wisdom; and to depart from evil is understanding." The holy man institutes a sufficiently anxious inquiry into the place, nature, origin, and operations of wisdom. All the works of God confess with one mouth that it is precious and altogether beyond estimation; but when questioned, they openly testify that it is not seated in themselves. For though a man should turn out the most skilled and knowing in their nature and operations, he would not thereby attain that true wisdom. But lest wretched man should toss perpetually among the rough places in the inquiry after this wisdom, and, after proving himself "a wild donkey's colt" (Job 11:12), should perish forever, God himself, graciously pitying him, showed him the place and nature of that true wisdom, "and said to man, The fear of the Lord is wisdom, and to depart [ p. 446 ] from evil is understanding"; that is, this wisdom consists in the very fear of God and in true holiness; this is its proper work, this its life and soul. It is prudence, I say, or intelligence directing the whole man in the fear of God and the practice of piety: "The beginning of wisdom is the fear of the Lord" (Ps. 111:10). In this also it places its increases: Psalm 25:14, "The secret of the Lord is with those who fear him, and he will make known to them his covenant." And John 7:17: "If anyone wills to do his will, he shall know concerning the doctrine, whether it is from God." These things, then, cohere with one another by so indissoluble a bond, that that wisdom first directs us to do the will of God, that is, to obey him in spirit and in truth, and then by that evangelical practice of piety it is itself daily promoted and increased. Hence if anyone has not received that spiritual light of which we have spoken, or, having received it, has not most diligently stirred it up by the assiduous use of the means which God has instituted, or, having stirred it up, has not exercised it in the daily practice of all evangelical duties, and in departing from evil in all holiness, he will beyond all doubt be disappointed in his expectation of progress in the study of this theology, whether useful to himself or pleasing to God. This is the force of the evangelical promise: John 8:31, 32, "If you abide in my word, you are truly my disciples; and you shall know the truth, and the truth shall set you free." And this connection between wisdom and the practice of piety in all its forms, and the power of theology directing our souls into all evangelical obedience, which is our spiritual prudence, the apostle Paul wonderfully shows in the Epistle to the Colossians (1:9, 10): "We do not cease," he says, "praying for you, and asking that you may be filled with the acknowledgment of his will, in all wisdom and spiritual understanding; that you may walk worthily of the Lord unto all pleasing, bearing fruit in every good work and increasing in the acknowledgment of God." The apostle prays that the holy Colossians may be filled with the acknowledgment of the will of God, that is, with evangelical theology. That is our wisdom or spiritual understanding. And it puts itself forth in obediential practice of every kind. Accordingly the apostle asks that believers may be filled with it, not for the sake of the knowledge itself, nor that they may delight themselves with the cognition of spiritual things, but that they may walk worthily before God, unto all good pleasure, bearing fruit in every good work. For such is the nature, such the genius of this wisdom, that it efficaciously works all those things in those who are made partakers of it. And what then will follow? They will be increased and advanced in that very acknowledgment of God by whose power and benefit they prepared themselves for the exercise of piety. Thus wisdom and piety mutually cherish, promote, increase, and strengthen one another. The blessed Peter, therefore, after he had reviewed in a long order the evangelical virtues in whose exercise the spiritual life puts forth its powers, adds: "These things being in you and abounding, they render you neither idle nor unfruitful in the acknowledgment of our Lord Jesus Christ"; namely, because these are the genuine fruits of the knowledge of our Lord Jesus Christ, so much so that where they are lacking, whatever is pretended, there is really no knowledge of Christ [ p. 447 ] at all. For so the apostle: "For he who lacks these things is blind, seeing only what is near" (2 Pet. 1:8, 9).

XVI. Let a brief summary of the whole bring the disputation to a close. God takes care, and brings it to pass, that the regenerate perceive and understand the truth revealed in sacred Scripture, to his glory and their own salvation. To that end he pours saving light into their minds through the Holy Spirit. By the benefit of that light they become able and fit to understand spiritual things spiritually [Greek: pneumatika pneumatikos]. Without it, all labor and all study spent in acquiring evangelical theology will be in vain. Those furnished with this spiritual light he further heaps with the gift of wisdom, that is, he renders them wise through the Spirit of revelation in the mystery of God the Father and of Christ, and in the knowledge of all saving truth. And by the embrace of the truth their hearts are spiritually enlarged, and a new amplitude comes to their minds in their acts, in which not the least of the schoolmen say that the liberty of the will consists. And through various means and various degrees, the objective truth, or evangelical doctrine, is laid open to everyone furnished with this wisdom, so far as is needful for promoting his obedience and consolation. That perception of the truth is the knowledge of God and of his will proclaimed in sacred Scripture. But that knowledge by no means completes the whole of theology, as some would have it; it is rather the operation of a mind furnished with evangelical wisdom upon its proper object. Hence the whole soul is conformed to the truth known; so that the very thing which is truth in the word is grace in the heart. And this wisdom perpetually spurs to evangelical obedience, and directs the mind in the exercise of the same; which is its proximate end. So far as it does this, it is our prudence and intelligence. Of the ultimate end there is no need that we treat; the things that pertain to it are known from elsewhere. This wisdom, moreover, is capable of degrees. These have respect partly to the spiritual light itself, which the Holy Spirit bestows variously according to his own pleasure; partly to the revealed object, or evangelical doctrine. For since divine truth is delivered variously in the sacred Scriptures, as it has pleased the infinite wisdom of God, the knowledge of it, according to each one's spiritual capacity and the diligent use of the means apt for promoting the investigation of the various modes of revealing evangelical doctrine, cannot but be various with respect to degrees; yet so that every harvest in it, of whatever kind, must be ascribed to him who grants us to be increased in grace and in the knowledge of our Lord Jesus Christ.

Chapter argument: The founding of the new church according to the norm of evangelical theology; The first rule of its founding: its members holy; The holiness of the Jewish church typical, that of the Christian church true; The preaching of John, demanding regeneration; So also that of Christ himself; And of the apostles; The holiness of the evangelical church proved; Second, it is constituted Catholic [ p. 448 ]; What its Catholicism is; Third, the institution of particular churches for celebrating instituted worship; their nature; Fourth, the worship of the new church spiritual; Fifth, the written word alone the rule of faith, worship, and obedience; Sixth, the presence of Christ through the Spirit.

I. In recounting the various degrees and modes of supernatural revelation, we have shown that theology divinely revealed has been, in every age, the norm and root of the church to be instituted for the solemn celebration of the worship of God. We have also seen how, "in these last days" [Greek: ep' eschaton ton hemeron], through his only-begotten Son, who is ever in the bosom of the Father, he has set forth all the mysteries of his counsel concerning the display of his glory in the salvation of sinners and the celebration of his worship. We have further indicated the general nature of that most perfect revelation, insofar as it is communicated to men through the Holy Spirit. According to its norm, our Lord Jesus Christ instituted and erected a new church, destined to endure to the consummation of the age. The plan of our undertaking requires that we briefly recount its state and condition, insofar as they appear to depend upon the things expounded above. I shall speak, however, more briefly than so great a matter deserves to be treated: partly, because our present business is not to weigh directly and of set purpose the nature of the evangelical church; partly, because from what has already been said sufficient light will plainly be lent to the things that remain to be said; and partly, because, God so graciously disposing, we are called away to other cares.

II. First, then, this theology requires that the evangelical church consist of the regenerate alone. Jesus Christ himself, its foundation, the "living stone," admits none but "living stones" to be laid upon himself and built up into a spiritual house (1 Pet. 2:4). No mortal will ever become his disciple on any other terms. And we have proved at length that the theology which he himself has taught us demands the same thing. Let us yet add further weighty considerations. The Jewish people, called into an ecclesiastical state, God himself pronounced wholly holy and pure: "You are a holy people to Jehovah your God" [Hebrew: am qadosh attah la-YHWH eloheka] (Deut. 7:6, 14:2, 26:19). But just as all the institutions proper [Greek: oikeia] to that church were typical, and a shadow of the spiritual "good things to come" [Greek: mellonton agathon], so that holiness which God ascribes to the whole people was nothing other than a certain prefiguration of that "holiness of truth" [Greek: hosiotes tes aletheias] which the Lord Jesus would bestow upon his whole church through the Holy Spirit. We have proved before that all their "ordinances of the flesh" [Greek: dikaiomata sarkos] were instituted only "until the time of reformation" [Greek: mechri kairou diorthoseos]. Looking forward, therefore, to the end when these were to be abolished, the Holy Spirit, in almost every place of the Old Testament, describes the perpetual state of the future church. And in all the predictions concerning that state, nothing occurs more frequently, or is more solemnly set forth, than this true and saving holiness of the members of that church. See Ps. 2:6, 24:3, 4, 45:13, 68:13; Isa. 11:8-10, 35:8, 54:11-14, 60:21; Ezek. 47:9; Zech. 14:20, 21; Mal. 4:1, 2.

III. But when the kingdom of heaven had now drawn near, John, who was sent to prepare the way of the Lord, expressly declared that true repentance, that is, regeneration or true holiness of life, is altogether necessary for obtaining entrance into the kingdom of God, that is, into the evangelical state of the church (Matt. 3:2). Further: when very many were puffing themselves up over the external privileges which they enjoyed in the Jewish church by virtue of carnal descent from the stock of Abraham, he declares most plainly that these would profit them nothing toward obtaining any part in the kingdom of Christ unless they seriously repented (vv. 9, 10). Hence the prophets of old announced that the coming of the Messiah, so greatly longed for by the Jewish church, would be exceedingly terrible; namely, because he would utterly exterminate from the bounds of the church all who had not attained true repentance, holiness, and piety (Mal. 3:1-5, 4:1-4). John teaches the same (Matt. 3:10). Christ, indeed, when by his ministry he laid the foundations of the new church, invited all sinners to himself. But just as he taught that no one could come to him unless drawn by his Father (John 6:44), so he receives no one into his fold who does not profess true repentance. Indeed, he not only denies that anyone belongs to his church whose righteousness does not exceed the righteousness of the Pharisees and scribes (Matt. 5:20), that is, the most rigid profession of the true religion and the most exact "form" [Greek: morphosis] of piety; or that anyone can even be his disciple who has not denied himself (Matt. 10:38, 16:24; Luke 9:23) and done very many other things of that kind; but at the very beginning of his preaching he also openly proclaims, as the foundation and firmest norm of his whole church, that no one, unless he has been born again, can either enter or see that kingdom of God which he was even then erecting (John 3:3, 5).

IV. The preparatory ministry being completed, the work of visibly gathering the church and separating it from the world was committed by Christ himself to the apostles, evangelists, and other disciples, to each according to his ability. Whom and what sort of persons they gathered into the fold of Christ, they themselves everywhere teach. They were regenerate, called with a holy and effectual calling, godly, faithful, holy, washed, justified, separated from the world and from all evils, sons of God, instructed and anointed by the Holy Spirit, conformed to Christ, living stones, devoted to good works, and renewed after the image of God (Acts 2:41, 42, 47, 4:32, 8:37, 10:47, 13:48, 16:14, 15, 19:18, 19; Rom. 1:7, 12:1, 2; 1 Cor. 1:2, 9, 30, 16:1; 2 Cor. 1:1, 8:5; Eph. 1:1, 2, 13, 2:5, 9, 10; Phil. 1:1, 6, 7; Col. 1:2-5; 1 Thess. 1:1-10; 2 Tim. 3:5; Heb. 3:6; 1 Pet. 1:2-5, 2:4, 5; 2 Pet. 1:1-4; 1 John 2:20, 27; Jude 1). But the profane, unbelievers, the disobedient, those ignorant of the evangelical mystery, hypocrites, those conformed to the world, haters of good men, and all others of that kind, they everywhere bar far from the bounds of the church. I confess, indeed, that these things were transacted not in heaven, and not according to the judgment of God the "knower of hearts" [Greek: kardiognostes], but among men. Although, therefore, the utmost care was employed that only the sheep of Christ, and such as were obedient to his word, should be admitted into his holy fold, [ p. 450 ] yet many hypocrites crept in, who afterwards either disturbed the state of the church by foul apostasy, or polluted it by their vicious continuance in it.

V. Secondly, this theology requires that the church, as to its external condition, be catholic. The church is no longer to be confined to one region, land, race, family, or nation; but through all the quarters of the world, through all tongues, tribes, families, nations, and peoples, through all conditions of men, its bounds were to be enlarged by the power of this theology. No longer is any city, any mountain, any place or seat, any temple, to enjoy a privilege above any others of divine worship, presence, or blessing. The veil of the temple being rent, the middle wall of partition broken down, the boundaries of the holy land removed and stretched from the rising of the sun to its setting, all places and all nations are held by God without any distinction. Jerusalem pleases him no more, nor Rome, nor Constantinople, nor London, nor Oxford, than any most despised village in the remotest barbarism, where evangelical worship is duly celebrated and men depart from iniquity through the Spirit of Christ. There is in the whole Old Testament no shadow of a prediction, nor in the New even the slightest hint, of the preference of any seat, place, or city whatsoever above others. Testimonies to the contrary stand everywhere. When of old, indeed, it pleased God to prefer one place above all others, and to constitute a peculiar seat of his worship, he not only everywhere inculcated honorable mention of it, but also commanded all his worshippers, under penalty of extermination from the census of his people, to celebrate his solemn worship there. After the revelation of the gospel, concerning one or more places of that kind there is deep silence. Concerning Rome, nothing, except that in certain books her most foul defection from Christ is wonderfully foretold. "Wherever two or three are now gathered together in the name of Christ, he himself is in the midst of them." No one can any longer cast the faithful out of the inheritance of Christ, since all the ends of the earth have passed to him for a possession. Let them be driven by the force, fraud, and tyranny of the world and of Satan into desert places, into the vastest solitudes, to the ends of the earth; let them live in exile beyond the paths of the year and of the sun: wherever they may be, the dust in which they sleep is equally holy, and their holy assemblies equally so, as they would be at Rome, London, or Oxford. Sheepfolds, the barns of peasants, the dens of wild beasts, the tops of crags, the caves and caverns of the earth, in which two or three or more faithful servants of Christ find it necessary to assemble for celebrating the worship prescribed to them by their Lord, are no less pleasing to God than those edifices to which some, the whole genius of evangelical theology most absurdly resisting, would wish to bind the solemn worship of God.

VI. Thirdly, in order that evangelical worship might be performed visibly, solemnly, everywhere on earth, to the glory of God, the edification of the faithful in faith and mutual love, and their consolation, the Lord Jesus in this theology gave it in command to all his disciples and the subjects of his kingdom that, separated from the world but united among themselves by the bond of brotherly love, they should gather themselves [ p. 451 ] into particular assemblies (for which they should designate and elect, in his name, those who should preside). For he willed that his people should be in every way willing in the day of his power. Therefore, just as he constituted the bounds and limits of his catholic church to be the wills of the elect people subjected to his rule through the efficacy of grace, so that, namely, it should extend just so far as a willing people should be ready to submit itself to him; so for these particular assemblies, in which alone the evangelical worship instituted by him can be duly performed, he constituted no other bounds than the wills of the faithful gathering themselves into them.

VII. That one family, one city, one town or one village, one race, one people, one kingdom, should be one church, Christ never anywhere taught. Rather, the fountain of the constitution of a particular evangelical church is the voluntary obedience of the faithful disciples of Christ; relying on his authority alone, they come together into the society constituted by him. For he himself, as we have said, instituted that all the faithful everywhere on earth should gather themselves into assemblies of this kind; in which they should devote themselves to evangelical worship according to his prescription; should exercise the discipline instituted by him; should diligently procure the preservation and increase of the whole assembly and the multiplication of the faithful; should be subject, according to the ecclesiastical canons published in the gospel, to those who by his prescription preside over the assemblies; should help one another mutually by counsels, prayers, alms, and spiritual communion; should openly proclaim the truth; should spread abroad the sweet savor of the gospel; and finally should convict and judge the world by holiness of life, by zeal for good works, and by the highest observance of peace and love among themselves. Obedient to his word, the faithful of old constituted assemblies of this kind everywhere on earth, and will go on constituting them until the consummation of the age.

VIII. Fourthly, this theology teaches that the religious worship which the Lord Jesus willed to be celebrated in those assemblies is plainly spiritual. All external splendor, all carnal adornment, however resplendent with visible glory, Christ dismissed, very few ceremonies, and those simple ones, being newly adopted into his worship. See John 4:23; 2 Cor. 3:6-11; Heb. 9:1-12. But for so long as it pleased God to use in his worship those things which were glorious in visible and carnal adornment, he himself expressly prescribed everything, at length and accurately, down to the smallest point. The Lord Jesus, therefore, could not more expressly have cast out all worldly adornment, and all ceremonies besides those he himself expressly instituted, from occupying any place in his worship, than he did by his silence throughout this whole theology. To the worship he also added a discipline, the preserver of evangelical obedience, spiritual and to be administered spiritually; of which we have spoken elsewhere.

IX. Fifthly, in order that all things in his churches might be duly performed according to the will of God, he willed that this evangelical theology, committed to writing at his command, [ p. 452 ] that is, his word, should be the sole norm and rule of faith, of obedience, and of all worship; and thus that no more himself than his word should be unique in the churches. Whatever is done in them beyond its prescription is done without his command (Matt. 28:18-20).

X. Sixthly, to these assemblies gathered in his name he graciously promised his presence through the Holy Spirit until the consummation of the age (Matt. 28:20; John 14:15, 16, 16:7). Him alone, therefore, whom he commanded the faithful to ask of the Father with all their prayers (Luke 11:13), he willed to be all things in the whole of evangelical worship. See Luke 12:10; John 3:5, 6, 8, 4:24; Rom. 7:6, 8:1, 2, 9, 13, 26, 15:30; 1 Cor. 2:4, 12:13; 2 Cor. 3:8; Gal. 5:16, 25; Eph. 2:18, 4:3, 5:18, 6:17, 18; Phil. 1:19, 2:1; 1 Thess. 5:19. And so the Lord Jesus sent out into the world his disciples, instructed in this theology, to glorify himself and his Father through the Spirit.

Chapter argument: The apostasy of the Christian church; The causes of the perpetuity of the evangelical church; The defection of particular churches; The predictions of it; The objection [Greek: enstasis] of the Roman church; The defection from the first theological principle of founding the church.

I. That our Lord Jesus Christ instituted his church most wisely, as became him who is the way, the life, and the truth, is both confessed by all and has been briefly proved by us. That that church will endure to the end of the age, he himself promised, and the nature of the kingdom with which he has been endowed by the Father requires it. For since nothing was ever to be added to the faith once delivered to the saints through the Son of God, and since the whole state of the church depends upon revealed theology, it is plainly impossible that any innovation should be made in it like those we have recounted above; or, since it is itself the visible kingdom of Christ, that it should utterly vanish away. But since the evangelical church is catholic, bound to no place, seat, family, or nation, it is not necessary that this or that assembly, gathered and called in this or that place, should be perpetuated. For it abundantly suffices for the preservation of the catholic church on earth that somewhere there have always existed worshippers of the true God in Jesus Christ through the Holy Spirit. But that every assembly which, from some particular place where it gathers, is called a particular church, can fail with the progress of time, Scripture teaches; that such have failed, the event shows. For the Holy Spirit testifies that some churches, within a few years after their first calling, had so far fallen away from faith, love, and purity, that Christ declared that, unless they repented most speedily, he would utterly forsake them (Rev. 3:1-4, 16-20). The same Spirit also foretold a certain general defection of those professing the name of Christ from his yoke and from the norm of evangelical theology, a defection which had already struck some roots while some of the apostles were still living (2 Thess. 2:3, 7, 8). The origin, progress, [ p. 453 ] most hideous effects, and outcome of that defection John expounds at length in the Apocalypse. And although very many other churches, if not all everywhere throughout the whole world, were implicated in a share of this crime, yet that greatest crowd of hypocrites and idolaters which, diffused through various nations, loves to be called the Roman church, has so far snatched the palm of wickedness from all others, and has transferred all the predictions to itself alone, that it alone, in the end, is worthy to be adduced as the example of total defection from the rule of evangelical theology. But since very many learned men have applied themselves to the work of investigating the origin, progress, and various degrees of that defection, whose fault and guilt the Roman church has taken upon itself, and since to their fidelity and diligence there scarcely remains anything that could be added, it will suffice me to uncover in a few words the principles and fountains of the whole execrable apostasy, from which all the particular monsters of errors and superstitions of which it consists gradually flowed forth. And this I shall do the more willingly, because I seem to see that very many churches, by the grace of God delivered from the guilt and stain of that defection in which they were entangled for several centuries, are as yet scarcely cautious and watchful enough not to fall anew into the causes, occasions, and fountains of the same most foul apostasy. The same road, therefore, which we traversed before must here be traveled again. We have shown that the whole apostasy of the churches of all ages lay in the neglect and desertion of those theological principles which were peculiar or proper to that enlargement of theology upon which their institution rested. We have also expounded, in the preceding chapter, what those heads of evangelical theology were which our Lord Jesus Christ appointed beforehand for the building of his church. The forgetting, neglect, and contempt of these gave origin and progress to the defection of the churches of old; would that they did not still do so. This we shall teach in order.

II. The constitution of the church, namely that it ought to consist of the regenerate, and of those blamelessly professing "righteousness and the holiness of truth" [Greek: dikaiosynen kai hosioteta tes aletheias], occupies the place of the first foundation in this our theology. From the neglect of this principle, "the first stain of evil," at least the one which drew with it the impulse toward defection, fastened upon the churches. For since, on the one hand, it seemed to be in the interest of those who presided over the churches in the name of Christ that as many as possible should be admitted into them, and since, on the other hand, there was no lack of worldly enticements to allure many, touched by almost no sense of spiritual and heavenly things, to take up the profession of the Christian name, this solemn institution of Christ being despised, profane, superstitious, carnal, proud, avaricious, and luxurious men rushed into them in droves. Let Augustine be witness, On Catechizing the Uninstructed, ch. 2: "There are those," he says, "who wish to be Christians either that they may win the favor of men from whom they expect temporal advantages, or because they are unwilling to offend those whom they fear; the church bears with them for a time."

III. Hence, the doctrine of the church, especially concerning faith and the new birth, was gradually corrupted; the splendor and glory of holiness was lost; separation [ p. 454 ] from the world was neglected; most superstitious observances were introduced into the use of the Christian common people; and discipline was insensibly depraved into the image of a kind of secular jurisdiction. For after the crowd of hypocrites, and of others who were not regenerate, had as it were overwhelmed the church with their multitude, it either pleased or amused the teachers of the church to change the spiritual doctrine of the gospel into a kind of philosophical wisdom, so that it might be accommodated to the state and condition of those of whom the church now consisted. Thence faith became a correct opinion or persuasion concerning God; the new birth became the external rite of baptism; while the glory of holiness could be preserved by no arts, by no painted pretexts, in those assemblies the greatest part of which either bitterly opposed it or at least was of a mind quite estranged from it. And when almost the whole world, induced by various considerations, had taken up the profession of the Christian religion, and had foully contaminated almost all the institutions of Christ, how could separation from it be instituted without suspicion of schism from the church itself? Further: we have shown that the discipline instituted by Christ was plainly spiritual. In order that it might obtain its force and efficacy upon men, it is required that those very persons toward whom it is to be exercised be likewise spiritual. When, therefore, hypocrites and carnal men destitute of the Spirit had occupied the churches, among whom that most gentle and spiritual discipline of Jesus Christ could by no means attain the end proposed to it, that discipline being despised and neglected, only its shadow retained, there crept into the churches a secular government of such a sort as might to some degree restrain those hypocrites, or depraved and criminal men. Moreover, of that impure crowd which, through contempt of this principle, oppressed the churches, very many were most deeply imbued with Jewish and heathen superstitions, through whom at length almost the whole of Hellenism, patched over with certain Jewish observances, was introduced into the church. For thence the worship of angels, of dead men, and of images, purgatory, virgin nuns, sacrificing priests, the very sacrifice of the mass, the consecration of temples, the observation of festivals (to say nothing of the little images offered to the saints, which were once offered to Dis; the Saturnalian kindling of lights upon altars; the torches carried in February, formerly in the rites of the greater gods, afterwards at the feast of the Virgin; the Hilaria celebrated for the Mother of the Gods at the beginning of spring; and innumerable other things), the seedlings of the ancient superstition, sprouted up in the Christian religion. After recounting some of these, Baronius adds (Notes on the Roman Martyrology, Feb. 2): "So likewise it has happened in many other institutions of the Gentiles, that the usage of their superstition, expiated by sacred rites and rendered sacrosanct, has been laudably introduced into the church of God." That most learned man therefore openly confesses that heathen superstition was admitted into the church; although the Belgian censors, in their expurgatory index, order to be deleted those words of Polydore Vergil from book 5 of On the Inventors of Things, ch. 1: "In this matter someone modestly scrupulous will perhaps say that he plainly does not know whether they are imitating the religion of the ancients, or rather their superstition"; in which words he determines the same thing as Baronius. [ p. 455 ] But in the process of time, not without foul defilement of the sacred institutions of Christ, the prelates of the churches admitted into them the whole offscouring of the nations. Let the admission of the English to the baptismal water by Augustine the monk serve as an example, which Camden relates in his Britannia from an ancient fragment of that age: "Augustine, on one day of the Lord's nativity, on which the universal glory of the English is perpetually celebrated, regenerated with life-giving baptism more than ten thousand men, besides an innumerable multitude of women and little children. But what supply of priests, sacred or of other orders, could have sufficed for washing so great a people? The river called in English the Swale being therefore blessed" (it is a river in the county of York), "the chief bishop commands, through heralds and teachers, that they should confidently enter in two by two, and baptize one another in turn in the threefold name of the Deity. And so all were born again, by no less a miracle than that by which the people of Israel had once crossed the divided sea, and likewise the Jordan turned backwards, to the other bank." Bede refers these things to Paulinus.

IV. Whoever it was, no one can doubt that he furnished an outstanding specimen of defection from the principles of evangelical theology. From this impious mixture, which was the fount and origin of the first ecclesiastical defection after the entrance of sin (Gen. 6:1, 2, 4), and from which also, after God had in many places most gravely admonished the people of Israel to beware of it for themselves (Exod. 23:32, 34:12-14; Deut. 7:2-5), the beginning of their apostasy likewise arose (Judg. 2:1-3), the Christian church contracted almost its first stain and fault.

V. Secondly, the second peculiar principle of evangelical theology that respects the institution of the new church establishes its catholicism. In defection from the norm of this principle the fatal apostasy took its second step. For when very many of the ancient Christians either wrongly understood this principle or foully neglected it, I know not what monsters of opinions were born from it. The fiction of apostolic sees inflicted the first wound upon it. For after it became the interest of certain men that this or that church, gathered in some celebrated city, should be preferred before other churches, petty arguments were forthwith devised, about which there is the deepest silence in the sacred Scriptures, assigning I know not what secular privileges to this or that place. But all those reasonings fought directly against the catholicism of the evangelical church. Although, therefore, all the apostles had received it in command from Christ that, as far as in them lay, they should announce the gospel to all nations, which indeed they performed to the utmost of their power, diligently halting nowhere for long, so that Paul alone, his course not yet completed, "from Jerusalem and the surrounding regions as far as Illyricum preached the gospel" (Rom. 15:19), yet almost immediately upon their death the rumor spread abroad that they had fixed their seats here or there. So here Peter was said to have had his seat, there John or James [ p. 456 ] his throne. From that most false rumor those places began to be held in esteem, and to be reckoned as furnished with I know not what privileges above all others. And after various quarrels resting upon these shameful lies, and contentions among the prelates of the churches, wickedly canvassing for rule [Greek: arche] and primacy among brethren against the express command of the Lord Jesus, secular vanity and Satanic ambition having been admitted into the church, there at length lifted itself up that which plainly destroyed the catholicism of the church, the Roman Papacy. For from that time one city, nay one man, arrogated to himself both the name and the whole dignity of the church; and, what is the height of madness, the flatterers of that one man have palmed off that particular church, over which the pope presides in a manner wholly secular, in the place of the catholic church. Which assertion denies and overturns the nature of the catholic church. And this was the second cause of the monstrous apostasy with whose guilt almost the whole Christian world has bound itself.

VI. Thirdly, Christ constituted particular assemblies of the faithful, freely gathering themselves into them, to be the seats of his solemn worship to be visibly exercised. But together with the nature of the catholic church, the apostates overturned the nature of particular churches as well. For while the catholic church was confined within limits and barriers in no way divinely prescribed to it, they so enlarged particular churches that they became by no means fit for performing those offices for whose discharge they were destined by Christ. For when the profession of the Christian name had, very often with too much haste, taken possession of whole cities, regions, and nations, the church of that place or nation succeeded into the form of that political union and constitution which each of them possessed. One empire, one kingdom, one commonwealth everywhere passed over into one church. Hence also there necessarily followed an arrangement of the church of the same kind as had previously obtained in the political state. Let the Roman church serve as an example, which, though it was particular, yet wished to be catholic; exceeding the measure and nature of a particular church, but not attaining that of the catholic, it lost the whole nature of a church, so that it is neither particular nor catholic. That church, I say, after it had leaped over the limits appointed to it by Christ, and had determined that all subjects of the Roman empire everywhere who had embraced the gospel belonged to it and to its care and government, since it, forsooth, was the Roman church, was gradually transformed into the image of the empire itself, and had nothing further in common with the institution of Christ. That the same happened in particular nations is well known.

VII. Fourthly, evangelical theology teaches, in the next place, that the glory of the religious worship to be celebrated in the churches is internal and spiritual. That there is something divine [Greek: theion ti] and a glory in divine worship is the common presumption of all men. No observance of religious worship, therefore, can please anyone in which he cannot perceive glory or splendor. But the spiritual glory of spiritual worship no one perceives unless he himself [ p. 457 ] is likewise spiritual. After, therefore, the crowd of the unregenerate had everywhere crept into the church, which is the seat of evangelical worship, a crowd which could not perceive its spiritual glory nor be bathed in its sweetness, it resolved to make it its business to devise splendid ceremonies of such a kind as might render the worship to which it applied itself shining and glorious.

VIII. For although the evangelical institutions have an unutterable beauty, a surpassing glory, and a splendor far exceeding all external splendor, in the eyes of those who in their observance obtain most sweet communion with God the Father and the Son through the Holy Spirit; yet to all those who, being carnal, savor only external things, they seem to have no glory or splendor at all. Hence hypocrites, pouring themselves out into every superstition, in order, that is, to supply the defect in the worship, a defect which has its place only in their own minds, busied themselves, under the specious pretext of beauty, adornment, order, and glory, in devising new rites and new ceremonies without end or measure; until not only was the whole worship of the church turned into a vain, superstitious, theatrical hodgepodge of carnal rites, but it ended in nefarious idolatry. To the same fault evangelical discipline owed its ruin. For since hypocrites neither felt its spiritual power, nor cared for it, nor could perceive how it might exert any efficacy upon men like themselves, they determined to introduce in its place a plainly secular power, exercised by carnal means. Hence exhortations, admonitions, brotherly corrections, private and by the whole church, applied with the greatest gentleness, love, and peace in the Holy Spirit, turned into I know not what jurisdictions, courts, citations, fines, lawsuits, tumults, and uproars, all crammed full of tyranny and ambition, until nothing sincere, holy, spiritual, brotherly, or evangelical was left in the whole of ecclesiastical discipline.

IX. Fifthly, the defection from the fullness of the Scriptures, to which fault the ancient churches bound themselves, there is hardly need to recount, since the matter is well enough known from the writings of many. I shall deal with it in a few words. When certain men had placed much weight and importance upon those things of which no mention is made in the sacred Scriptures, and of which, for that very reason, they might have been persuaded that they ought to occupy no place in the worship of God or in the churches of Christ, they straightway betook themselves to traditions, that is, to those things which they had heard, through most uncertain rumors, that their predecessors had observed. And as the reverence for those things which were not instituted by Christ in the sacred Scriptures grew from day to day, since they served exceedingly well the ambition of certain men, and secular advantages, as well as the external splendor of religious worship, Scripture began daily to grow cheap, while traditions, and I know not what authority of the church, began to be held more and more in esteem. A more potent poison the old serpent never administered to the church. For the stable, fixed, infallible [ p. 458 ] authority and rule of the word of God being cast aside, the professors of the Christian name had nowhere to betake themselves in safety, until they threw themselves headlong into the abyss of all errors, heresies, and idolatry.

X. Sixthly, to all these things was added a monstrous contempt of the Most Holy Spirit, whom Christ promised to his churches. Christ took upon himself the care of perpetuating his church even to the consummation of the age. To that end he uses the written word, and the presence and grace of the Holy Spirit. Whatever pertains to the faith, obedience, and whole worship of the church, the word clearly reveals and sets forth. That the faithful may rightly use that word for the calling, edification, and consolation of the church, and finally for the whole work of the ministry, the Spirit continually bestows and distributes his gifts [Greek: charismata]. To that end, as we have shown, he was promised by Christ. But when the professors of the Christian name, weary of the word and of the institutions of Christ, had set their minds upon embracing and following in the worship of God either their own inventions [Greek: heuremata] or things handed down from their fathers [Greek: patroparadota], they gradually came to count as nothing the Spirit himself also, and the promise of Christ concerning his mission. For other means having been devised for perpetuating a church using unwritten [Greek: agraphos] worship, namely through a successive flow of ministerial power, and through sciences and secular learning, all authority of the Holy Spirit, and all expectation of him, forthwith grew cheap. And the word of God being despised, the Spirit of Christ neglected, an unwritten worship introduced, for the performance of which there was no need of the gifts or grace of the Spirit, the apostasy had nowhere further to go.

XI. To all these things may be added the mixture of evangelical doctrine with secular philosophy, which, because I fear we are still sticking fast in its thorns and briar patch, I have thought must be shaken out in a separate digression.
Digression
### On the mixture of philosophy with theology

I. The apostle Paul, in the second chapter of the Epistle to the Colossians, most gravely admonishes the faithful to beware "lest anyone despoil them through philosophy." Almost all interpreters hold that in that place the apostle is censuring not the use but the abuse of philosophy. But whether the mixture of philosophy with theology pertains to its use, or rather to its abuse, is not a point on which all are of the same mind. For it is most certain that in every age there have not been wanting pious and learned men who, led by reverence for this apostolic instruction, have absolutely rejected all use of it in divine things. The stirring up and promotion of the common natural notions which distinguish between good and evil, the honorable and the base, together with the skillful, prudent, and assiduous contemplation and investigation of the works of God, of creation and of providence, so that by these the minds of men may be kindled to render holily the obedience owed to God, [ p. 459 ] neither Paul nor anyone else treading in his footsteps ever condemned. But since those notions are imperfect, and for the most part corrupted, and since the human mind stumbles miserably in the consideration of the works of God, it is necessary that whatever knowledge seems to arise from them be tested against the Lydian touchstone of sacred Scripture.

II. We here consider only the primary end of all knowledge, namely, living to God; troubling ourselves little about bare speculations in which the innate curiosity of the human mind exercises itself, and which neither direct nor perfect the rational intellect in order to God. Moreover, we have shown that unless those common notions [Greek: koinai ennoiai] of good and evil, and the contemplation of the works of nature and of providence, occupy another place in the knowledge of God than that which was assigned to them in the first state of mankind, all study placed upon advancing them will be altogether in vain. But these are not the things with which we are here dealing. The philosophy which now everywhere obtains, as it has been cultivated for many centuries by men most profoundly ignorant of the true God and of his will, is plainly another matter. It is its use in theology that is called into question, that is, the mixing of the principles, notions, and hypotheses which it teaches with the doctrine of the gospel; whereby the means, methods, and terms which philosophy, playing the handmaid to the human intellect occupied with natural objects, devised, or rather which certain men, sharp in their own opinion and speculative [Greek: theoretikoi], have used at their own pleasure in polishing it, are applied in the narration, declaration, exposition, and preaching of that doctrine. What judgment learned men of old held on that question, and what some still think, can be shown by the testimonies of a few. "There are those," says Clement of Alexandria (Stromata, book 1), "who think that philosophy came into life with the greatest evil and for the destruction of men, as having proceeded from some malignant inventor." Tertullian, in his Prescription against Heretics, says: "Heresies are equipped by philosophy. Thence come the Aeons, the forms, and I know not what Trinity of man in Valentinus: he had been a Platonist. Thence Marcion's better God, better for his tranquillity: he had come from the Stoics. And the notion that the soul perishes is taken from the Epicureans; and the denial of the restoration of the flesh is taken from the one common school of all philosophers. What then have Athens and Jerusalem to do with each other? What the Academy and the church? What heretics and Christians? Our instruction is from the porch of Solomon. We have no need of curiosity after Jesus Christ, nor of inquiry after the gospel. When we believe, we desire to believe nothing further; for this we believe first, that there is nothing which we ought to believe beyond." And Justin Martyr, in his Address to the Greeks: "Since from what has been said before, the affairs of your philosophers have been sufficiently refuted, having been shown to be full of all ignorance and deceit." The same Tertullian calls the philosophers "the patriarchs of the heretics," and almost the same is said in [Irenaeus?] book 2, ch. 37.

III. But it is not part of our plan to heap up testimonies. It will be enough to have indicated that there have not been wanting great and holy men who have cast their vote against the opinion and practice of this and of some past centuries. [ p. 460 ] What I myself think in this cause I shall say briefly; more briefly, perhaps, than so great a matter ought to be spoken of, since for many reasons our discourse, panting toward the end of the work, rushes headlong.

IV. In the doctrine of the Scriptures two things come to be considered: first, the divine truth itself; then, the mode of the narration or exposition of that truth. What the nature of the truth itself is, and how it has nothing in common with the common philosophy, we have shown before. The mode in which that truth is delivered and expounded in them out of the sanctuary of the divine mind remains to be weighed. That it is various and "manifold" [Greek: polypoikilos], all know who care to know the will of God revealed in his word. It must be confessed that those rules and methods which all the canons of logic command to be employed in teaching any doctrine or science systematically are almost nowhere observed in them. As, therefore, the doctrine itself, which respects the faith and obedience of sinners, flowed forth from pure, unmixed revelation, having come out, that is, from the bosom of the Father through the only Jesus Christ, so the mode of narrating or expounding it through the Holy Spirit, rejoicing in a certain singular divine sublimity [Greek: hypsos], is of a kind plainly different from, and sufficiently dissonant to, that which is perhaps apt and suitable for teaching merely human matters and sciences; or which at least seems so to most. This the apostle teaches us (1 Cor. 2:4-7): "And my speech and my preaching were not in persuasive words of human wisdom, but in demonstration of the Spirit and of power; that your faith should not stand in the wisdom of men, but in the power of God. Yet we speak wisdom among the perfect: but wisdom not of this age, nor of the rulers of this age, who are coming to nothing; but we speak the wisdom of God in a mystery, the hidden wisdom." The reader will find this place expounded above. Let him consult also Eph. 1:8, 9, 17-19; Col. 1:27, 28, 2:2, 8, 9.

V. Since such, then, was the manner of evangelical truth and of its exposition in the sacred Scriptures, there appear to be two things, among others, sufficiently injurious to the truth itself, which have crept into it from the common philosophy. For from this source there first came forth that disposition or compacting into systematic order, and into a technically [Greek: technikos] methodical series, of spiritual truth revealed variously and in manifold fashion "in demonstration of the Spirit," which now obtains everywhere in the schools and churches. To the partnership, I say, of the common philosophy with theology, entered upon under ill omens, almost all theological systems, commonplaces, and other hodgepodges of credible propositions of that kind owe their origin. Then, under the pretext of more accurate doctrine, the articles of divine truth, or credible propositions collected and excerpted from the Scriptures and digested into those formulas, are expounded mixed with philosophical terms and notions. From these two things there arises a certain philosophical theology (which in the writings of some approaches nearer to theology, in those of others to philosophy), by which the evangelical doctrine is cast down from its spiritual sublimity and loses its heavenly amplitude. Of this kind is the theology which very many learned men in every sect of Christians teach and profess. [ p. 461 ] Hence it comes to pass that there is no article of evangelical truth, however plain in itself, obvious, and exposed to the understanding of the faithful, whose spiritual force and efficacy all the saints who cultivate communion with God in Christ also experience, but that, if some philosophically learned man undertakes to expound it accurately and worthily, it is forthwith so encumbered with those terms, notions, and weighty arguments, or rather minutiae, that the apostle Paul himself would strive in vain to understand or grasp it, unless he were Peripatetically educated.

VI. This subtlety of disputation among the ancient philosophers, whence it came about that no one could perceive the truth, Lucian once deservedly laughed at in his Menippus [the Necromancy], section 4; after he had wittily lashed their most uncertain opinions, he adds: "And what was the most absurd of all these strange things, each of them, in speaking about the most contrary positions, furnished exceedingly victorious and persuasive arguments, so that I could not contradict the one who said the same thing was hot, nor the one who said it was cold, and that though I knew clearly that nothing could ever be hot and cold at the same time." It is fitting, therefore, to trace the origin and progress of this evil somewhat further back.

VII.
