When the gospel was first preached in the world, its professors judged that this was the business given them: to announce, in faith and love, the virtues of him who had called them out of darkness and translated them into his marvelous light. The celebration of the riches of divine grace, by which they were justified through the blood of Christ and delivered from the wrath to come; the admiration and contemplation of the ineffable love of Jesus the Mediator in the work of redemption; the assiduous imitation of his example in all holy obedience and holiness of life, by which, through the help of the Holy Spirit richly [Greek: plousios] poured out, they might daily be rendered more conformed to him; mutual love among themselves; zeal for good works useful among men; the observance of the worship of God in spirit and in truth according to the norm of the gospel and the simplicity of the institutions of Jesus Christ: these made up the sum of their religion, and of their zeal, labor, and method in cultivating religion. Led by the Spirit of God and relying on the word, to do and to suffer great things according to the will of Christ was their wisdom, their life, and their religion. Nor were they ever destitute of spiritual and ineffable joys and consolations, necessary to the state and condition in which they were. But not much time had flowed by from the beginning of the preaching of the gospel when impious men, "corrupted in mind" [Greek: diephtharmenoi ton noun], at the impulse of him who rules in the minds of men besieged by spiritual darkness, began variously to twist and pervert the heavenly truth. For since they were themselves carnal, and therefore it could not but be that spiritual things were foolishness to them, they attempted to pervert the doctrine of the gospel [ p. 462 ] into a kind of secular wisdom. Hence some dreamed that Christ himself was a shadow, a phantasm, an idea, a mere man, a spiritual man deified, even the sun; and that faith was a bare acquaintance with or knowledge of spiritual things, or even a vain conceit of fanatical perfection. And these things indeed they invented, "knowing neither the Father nor the Son." For with men of that sort, since they cannot understand spiritual things spiritually [Greek: pneumatikos], it is customary to indulge their own minds by fastening philosophical senses upon the sacred letters. That this poison began gradually to creep in while the apostles, or at least some of them, were still alive, the sacred page itself testifies, and what survives of the histories of those times supports it. But unequal to their authority, Satan straightway succumbed in that first assault against the truth. When, however, the sacred choir of apostles and evangelists had been removed, he again set about the work of corrupting evangelical truth, not without success. What he could never accomplish in attacking the truth, he gradually obtained in its defense. For after the generation of men infallibly "borne along by the Holy Spirit" [Greek: hypo Pneumatos hagiou pheromenoi] had ceased to be on earth, the patronage of the truth came into the hands of men most thoroughly furnished with all secular wisdom. Of these were Clement, Origen, Tertullian, and countless others. These men, since it did not satisfy them to use against the enemies of the truth those weapons which are not carnal, namely the word and the Spirit of Christ, were pleased to attack them also with arguments drawn from secular learning. And when they saw that the heretics, as they called them (though they were plainly philosophers feigning the Christian name), were dragging the express words of sacred Scripture elsewhere than they ought, they devised new terms in the exposition of the truth, and attempted by these to drag them out of the lurking places of their deceits and fallacies.

VIII. But it came to pass, with the progress of time, that those philosophical arguments which learned men had employed in the defense of the truth, and the terms and words by which they had enunciated this or that article of divine truth, were held to be necessary parts of the religion of Jesus Christ. As yet, however, no methodical confession of faith had crept into the church, no compendium of things to be believed beyond some portion of that which is commonly called the Apostles' Creed. No system or body of theology, no catechism methodically arranged and philosophically adorned, had the faithful as yet admitted. Sacred Scripture alone was the norm for settling controversies and teaching the people; only by a vain conceit of traditions had they been too much drawn away from the expectation of that instruction of the Spirit, who was promised to believers for that end through Jesus Christ. But day by day there grew among learned men the itch for mixing philosophical matters, or secular wisdom, with theological. They rushed headlong into that ruin especially after, being destitute of spiritual gifts, or not trusting them enough, they had nothing besides secular learning by which they might shine among friends or crush adversaries. There has been no age that has not given some notable increase to this evil, in truth a calamity to the ecclesiastical estate. While, therefore, [ p. 463 ] some devised new words and phrases of speaking, others applied quibbles and philosophical modes of arguing, and there were those who invented dialectical knots and meshes with which to bind the adversaries of the truth, and very many twisted the words which the Holy Spirit uses in expounding supernatural truth into an exotic [Greek: exotikos] and philosophical sense, the most holy and spiritual doctrine of Jesus Christ was variously corrupted by secular wisdom and almost lost.

IX. Not otherwise did that most learned man Apollinaris fall into schism, and into the church's severer censure, than by indulging too much, being a most keen disputant, in philosophical subtleties in the defense of the truth; as Acacius, bishop of Beroea, relates in his Epistle to Cyril of Alexandria, which is inserted in the acts of the Council of Ephesus. "For what profit," he says, "did Apollinaris of Laodicea bring, who was formerly one of the great champions, who fought mightily against the enemies on behalf of the right faith (here was the first stain of the evil), having grown confident in his own wisdom? Wishing to introduce certain passages out of the impassable into the pure and guileless faith of Christ, did he not bring it about that he himself was reckoned among the schismatics by the catholic church?" Or, as the Latin rendering has it: "For what advantage did that distinguished disputant Apollinaris of Laodicea bring to the church, while he fought, as one of the foremost, for the right faith against the enemies of the faith? Did he not, while he trusted too much in his own wisdom, and while he strove to lead the uncontaminated and simple faith of Christ through certain passages and as it were roads out of trackless and wholly impenetrable places, attain only this by his endeavor, that he himself was held by the universal catholic church among the schismatics?" If the same religious caution [Greek: eulabeia] regarding heavenly truth which possessed the church in the days of Apollinaris, that keenest disputant for the true faith, had flourished in the following centuries, it would never have admitted, in the place of evangelical theology, a doctrine so deformed, gaping, mixed, and impure as that which it afterwards embraced. But I would dare to say that that defection in the profession of the Christian religion, which all the faithful groan over and lament, owes its origin and progress to the mixture of the philosophy of the Gentiles with theology. For after it had occupied a place in the Christian schools, the good knowledge of God in Christ, as it is taught by the Holy Spirit in the mystery of the gospel, in great part ceased among very many. And this fatal evil will be found to have infected all sacred things with its poison especially after the Peripatetic philosophy, which had lain neglected for some centuries, began to please the students of good letters. Stirred up and cultivated by the Muhammadan Arabs, it invaded the Christian world quicker than a word could be spoken. For that philosophy, especially as it was cultivated by the Arabs, is exceedingly apt and fitted for engendering subtle disputations, and for perpetuating quarrels, brawls, and controversies about any matter whatever. Relying upon it, the schoolmen, almost openly abrogating all faith in evangelical theology, introduced in its place a philosophical and barbarous art or science. "For truly, who of sound judgment can doubt that most of the genuinely scholastic notions are [ p. 464 ] nothing but pure abstractions, and refined distillations of the Aristotelian metaphysics, at least when supernatural things are in question?" says Cornelius Jansen of Ypres, in his Augustinus, vol. 2, col. 19. "These men," therefore, as Verulam [Francis Bacon] rightly says in the Novum Organum, "besides reducing theology into order (well enough, according to their power) and fashioning it into the form of an art, further brought it about that the pugnacious and thorny philosophy of Aristotle was mingled with the body of religion more than was fitting." And in all things the name of Aristotle is held out as a pretext, whereas in fact they rather follow depraved interpreters and commentators. For, to say nothing of the fact that his works, which first came by inheritance to a certain Neleus, a skeptic and a man ignorant of letters, and then passed down to Apellicon of Teos, who sold the worm-eaten codices to Sulla the dictator, have come down to us in a most corrupted state; the Arabs, from whom these men drew their subtleties, utterly ignorant of all Greek and Latin, used translations from the Latin into the Arabic tongue, unlearned and rude, which, from his compressed and tight manner of speaking, in many places conveyed no sound sense. Concerning all these things Erasmus says: "I could wish that those frigid quibbles were altogether cut away, and that the simple and pure Christ were restored, and thoroughly implanted in human minds."

X. It is indeed true that those men used the books of the sacred Scriptures, and confessed that all the things contained in them are true. Nevertheless, thoroughly imbued with that Peripatetic philosophy, and either carried away by the itch of disputing or struck blind by its dizziness, they put together a body of theology, as they call it, of such a kind as in no way exhibits the most holy religion of Jesus Christ. The whole difference between those men and the ancient philosophers lies in this, that among the books which they used in polishing their science they admitted also the sacred Scriptures, of which the old Peripatetics were ignorant. Whence many judge that the schoolmen should rather be called philosophers than theologians, says Beatus Rhenanus in his Preface to Tertullian. "Since," as the same Jansen rightly says (vol. 2, col. 184), "beyond certain entanglements or ineptitudes of the Aristotelian philosophy, mixed into theology out of the dialectical classrooms, they brought forth nothing new at all." Erasmus complains gravely of these men in his Preface to Hilary. "The creed of faith," he says, "began to exist in writings rather than in souls, and there were almost as many faiths as men. Articles increased, but sincerity decreased; contention boiled up, charity grew cold. The doctrine of Christ, which before knew no strife about words [Greek: logomachia], began to depend on the supports of philosophy. This was the first step of the church sliding down to worse things. At length the matter was brought down to sophistical contentions; myriads of articles burst forth." And Trithemius confesses that from the time when the disputations, or rather the doubtings, of the schoolmen began to reign, sacred theology began to be defiled and contaminated by secular philosophy.

XI. But when it pleased God, at the beginning of the reformation of several European churches attempted in the last century, to send forth his light and his truth [ p. 465 ] in the preaching of evangelical simplicity, there was almost nothing in the whole apostate church which was equally an object of hatred and abomination to pious and good men as that theological science which at that time ruled in the schools and academies. Whether or not, in the very schools of the reformed churches, and among learned men of every kind, that same philosophical theology has recovered its place anew, let the judgment rest with others. I shall add what that most learned man Johannes Drusius has on 2 Pet. 2:22, on those words, "The dog returned to his own vomit." "The dog returns to his vomit," he says, "who returns to that which the first reformers vomited up. That is the scholastic theology, and those who pursue it neglect the true theology, that is, the word of God; whence all Christian truth, even that very truth which they call scholastic, is mixed with human leaven, so that it is not so pure and sincere as it ought to be. When at last will these things be reformed? For before that there will be no peace in the church. Would that those who are being nourished for divine things would order their studies otherwise, and, despising these entanglements, would seriously give themselves to the study of the principal languages and to the reading of the sacred text. If that were done, we should have theologians such as we ought rather to wish for than to hope for; so utterly does the word of God grow vile in the eyes of the crowd. Many do not understand what they expound to the people from the pulpit: they are wise only out of a commentary, than which nothing is more wretched or more to be shunned by him who seeks the true food of the soul." And on "the washed sow": "It can be said of him who, having been washed (for the reformation is as it were a kind of washing), wallows again in the sophistical mire, to the great harm of the youth and of the church: for whence, I ask, come these dissensions, if not from that mire? He returns to the vomit who now again calls back and introduces scholastic theology into the academies, mixing that bread which proceeds from the mouth of God alone with the leaven of the philosophers. These men turn the simplicity of believing into the curiosity and subtlety of disputing, daily devising new questions, most of which are useless, unknown of old to the prophets and apostles, which nevertheless increase the number of the disputants who today fill the Christian world." Thus far he. Let the author also be heard of the book entitled The Burden of the Church: "The pagans," he says, "seek to destroy the evangelical norm; therefore the devil instigates that the doctrine of Christians be propped up with pagan authorities, and that the dogmas of the Gentiles be mixed with the principles of the faith, so that at last the evangelical truth may be blown away from the midst through sophistical deceits." With which agrees that saying of Erasmus: "All things promise that the matter will succeed most happily. Yet one scruple holds my soul, lest under the pretense of ancient literature Paganism should attempt to lift up its head." But "from the beginning it was not so" [Greek: ap' arches ou gegonen houto]. That philosophical mode of teaching spiritual things is alien from the gospel. The first Christians after the times of the apostles also were utterly ignorant of it. Let the writings of the most ancient Christians be consulted; it will be found that they did not treat theological topics [Greek: theologoumena] in the manner of the more recent writers. And in every kind, that is best which is most ancient.

XII. Next, there is in spiritual truth, shining in this its native and heavenly light, something divine [Greek: theion ti], which seizes and compels the minds of men into admiration of it. With a certain ineffable glory and splendor it insinuates itself into the hearts of men and penetrates their consciences. It has a fullness, an amplitude, or a liberty, accompanied by an unutterable efficacy. In these consists the life and soul of true theology. But that the sense, force, and energy of all these are in wondrous ways obscured, weakened, and diminished, when theology, loaded with the chains of philosophical notions and terms, ensnared in their nooses, is forced to walk along, is the complaint of almost all who would wish to use it as the sole norm of cultivating holy communion with God in Christ. That in this way, I say, that spiritual efficacy or supreme authority over the consciences of men is lost, together with the heavenly and spiritual amplitude, which are wont to be the inseparable companions of evangelical truth delivered purely and untouched, all would readily feel and even confess, were not the minds of very many occupied by insuperable prejudices. Hence in the place of spiritual wisdom there is substituted I know not what sterile and arid set of opinions; with the highest acquaintance with and expertise in which there are often imbued men impious, unbelieving, carnal, worldly, and strangers to all saving knowledge of God in Christ. This also we see happening daily: namely, that the spiritual nature of evangelical things is most miserably obscured, while learned and sharp-witted little men spend much labor on teaching them accurately, methodically, and philosophically.

XIII. Further: that very many errors of old rushed into the church out of this philosophy, as out of the Trojan horse, we have shown from Tertullian and others. Likewise, whatever the sophists still babble about many articles of the faith has proceeded from the Peripatetic philosophy.

XIV. That the common philosophy also supplies in abundance the matter of endless brawls and useless disputations, the thing itself cries aloud. Relying on it, sharp and glib-tongued men "assail the estate of almighty God with calumnious lawsuits. They dissect the faith with minute circumlocutions, each one as his tongue is the more wicked. They loose and bind the chains of questions through plaited syllogisms" (Prudentius, Apotheosis, Hymn against the Unbelievers), which he once sang of the ancient philosophers. And they everywhere mix in many things which they who use them scarcely understand themselves. "I should be ashamed to say I do not understand, if they themselves understood who have treated these things," says Melchior Cano, Common Places, book 2, ch. 7. Most worthy of note also is the objection [Greek: enstasis] and complaint of the most learned Vives, founded on book 18, ch. 18 of Augustine's City of God. "The theologians," he says, "teach with great consent that it belongs to God alone to create something out of nothing. On which Thomas says much, whose arguments Scotus weakens in order to confirm his own, which Ockham strives to overthrow in order to fix [ p. 467 ] his own; but these too Peter of Ailly dislocates; and thus they either play in a serious matter, or force the heavenly thing to be subservient to their passions and factions. What morals can be amended, what depraved affections calmed and removed, what, in short, can come of a doctrine agitated and dragged about by dissensions, turned up and down at the pleasure of human passions, shaken by the engines of such obstinate litigants?"

XV. Hence theological quarrels take their origin, and are made eternal; all of which could without doubt easily be settled, if men who are Christians (one is ashamed to say it), at least as far as profession goes, would give themselves up in faith and for direction to the one divine word, and to the Holy Spirit speaking powerfully in it. But while almost all the wisdom of all men who care for and busy themselves with matters of religion is thoroughly infected with this philosophy, if it is not this very philosophy itself, controversies are perpetuated, and the reconciliation of those who disagree, even in matters of the least moment, is rendered impossible. Hence the theological libraries are full of enormous books and disputations about things, assertions, and terms, of all of which the Christian world had never heard anything, unless by chance they had come into the mind of Aristotle. But what a forest of evils and disturbances has come forth from thence, it is not the place here to expound.

XVI. Most truly and most gravely had Dr. Hoornbeek written much to this purpose not long ago, treating of the doctrine of the Trinity (Confutation of Socinianism, book 2, ch. 4, sect. 3). "With what terms, I beseech you," he says, "or notions, intruding [Greek: embateuontes] into a matter inaccessible [Greek: aprositos] to us by its profundity, should we attempt to explain it fully and plainly, with phrases or distinctions lately born in the school? Especially since all the logical or metaphysical terms which we are accustomed to use in this matter are formed from inferior things, and from their consideration and comparison through the operation of our intellect; whereas of that mystery there is no exemplar among these things. Nor do the customary terms seem sufficiently proportioned or apt for explaining it; from which we abstain the more religiously for this reason, that as every day new and yet newer ones are discovered, in proportion as new inventions are always made, or new kinds of distinctions framed, so we may not undeservedly doubt whether here also some more convenient notion of distinguishing might not be constructed. At least to suspend one's step seems to be outside of danger. Nor do we easily believe that the ancients were the less wise here, although they were ignorant of our recent formulas of distinctions. Besides, we also see that, some kind of distinction being granted, presently there is sharp disputing about it, the sum of the matter often dismissed, and to the prejudice of the truth, often more boldly and emptily than more certainly." So it is: quarrel begets quarrel, distinction begets distinction; and he who has seemed to himself to hold them all is no whit wiser than those who hold none. Hence it has come to pass, as Jansen of Ypres most gravely warns, "that in truth, for most Christians, the science of the more recent theology serves almost no purpose, except that, by learning and following the divergent senses of divergent men, suited to their own desires, they put off Christian simplicity and become more crafty, and in probity more wicked" (Augustinus, vol. 2, col. 22).

XVII. But I do not wish to linger longer on these things. Perhaps in the forbearance of God an opportunity will be given in which I may treat more fully of the true use and restoration of philosophy; here, therefore, I shall finish in a few words.

XVIII. Those who have investigated, with hatred far removed, and likewise with love and partisan zeal set aside, the origins, occasions, and causes of the contentions and brawls which are everywhere agitated among the professors of the Christian religion, with such uproar, with such commotions of minds, hatreds, exasperations, and with such reproach to the most holy name of Jesus and scandal to the gospel, have been exceedingly few: "they are scarcely as many in number as the gates of Thebes or the mouths of the rich Nile" (Juvenal, Satire 13.26-27). Inveterate prejudices, which the calculations of the age have begotten, divert the minds of men elsewhere. And those who have attempted to compose the dissensions and quarrels between the parties by a mutual relaxation of the rigor of opinions, and by concession, have long been in a position to understand that they have wasted their labor and their oil. Some ascribe this most deadly evil to the most just judgment of God, delivering over men full of themselves to the vanity of these contentions, because they would not submit themselves to the simplicity and purity of the gospel; yet in such a way that they assert that the darkness of the men themselves, their prejudices, affections, and depraved lusts are the immediate and culpable cause of them all. But supposing the just judgment of God, the sins of men deserving it, they affirm that the principal means of bringing into the church of Christ those most hideous errors which swarm everywhere, and of perpetuating quarrels about them and about other matters, has been that mixture of which we are treating, of philosophy, both Platonic and Peripatetic, with the doctrine of the gospel. In the opinion of these men, until Christians, shaking off this dust of Babylonian confusion and returning to evangelical simplicity, strive solely to be made wise through "the Spirit of revelation in the mystery of the gospel," any hope or expectation of deliverance from that vanity of fighting and brawling with which they are everywhere exercised will be in vain. This, they say, is the thing which has so nefariously set the worshippers of Jesus against one another, and which is the fountain of all separations and schisms. Hence they say that theology has been changed into a kind of thorny and perplexed art, or science, which depraved men learn, pursuing it just like other arts, without any aid of the Holy Spirit or communication of spiritual light. And they complain that by the study of that theology the minds of students are turned away from the divinely instituted pursuit of true wisdom, through faith, prayers, assiduous meditation on the word of God, and the holy practice of evangelical obedience. They hold, therefore, that evangelical theology ought to be absolutely separated from all mixture of philosophy. But to free the minds of men from the inveterate prejudices in whose bonds they are held, and to pluck up by the roots that "falsely named" [Greek: pseudonymos] wisdom which constitutes a great part of that erudition in the vain conceit of which the crowd of the learned so greatly exalts itself, they affirm to be the work of the Holy Spirit alone, which he will at last accomplish by the effectual putting forth of his power.
On the study of theology, or of the Scriptures

I. We have now set forth, according to the plan of our undertaking, the nature, origin, progress, and various enlargement of theology, that is, of the doctrine concerning God, his will, and the obedience we owe to him. We have also explained openly, and, as we hope, according to the mind of the Holy Spirit, the habit of mind or understanding by which we rightly perceive that doctrine, a habit which directs the whole man in its saving use. Concerning the study of theology, and how anyone may become an evangelical theologian, we have determined to inquire briefly in this last place. Now we have shown at length above that he alone is properly called a theologian who, besides being savingly wise in the mystery of the gospel, is furthermore so furnished with spiritual gifts that he is able to instruct others in the knowledge of the same mystery, to the glory of God and the praise of the grace of Christ.

II. With this end set before them, we see that very many fall short, since a method badly framed can find no issue. I have no wish in this place to harry or attack the commonly received course and method of studies. Those who persist in them have respectable authorities for what they do. That a professorship in theological schools and academies, which should teach that philosophical manner of expounding spiritual truth which we have described above, is of great usefulness, and that it could be of greater usefulness than it commonly is, we freely allow. To examine the directions, ways, and methods of studying and handling theology which many men celebrated for learning have published, though they very often contradict and obstruct one another, does not belong to our purpose. We are not all equally wise in all things. In a course of study in which some would suffer loss, others profess that they reap a rich harvest. But whatever in any of them gives occasion or fuel to that darkness, those quarrels, disputations, vain opinions, and prejudices in which almost every one of us who have enrolled ourselves in the study of theology is entangled, ought, I hope all will judge, to be cast away.

III. We have elsewhere rejected the attempt to hew and plane down spiritual truth, or theology, to the Lesbian and arbitrary rules of other arts and sciences. This doctrine contains something divine [Greek: theion ti], which refuses to be confined by such bars. Theology demands in those who cultivate it a different liberty and light of the understanding, different principles and purposes of mind, different ends, and different affections of heart, from any that the secular or philosophical arts and sciences either know or care for. Those who would be imbued with this spiritual wisdom must of necessity cultivate most holy communion with God in the mystery of the gospel through Jesus Christ, and must exercise their souls in experiencing the spiritual power and efficacy of saving truths, truths which are neither implanted in the mind by nature, nor presented to it through those things which lie open to its rational consideration and examination. The illumination of the heart; the communication of the spirit of wisdom; [ p. 470 ] the revelation and manifestation [Greek: phanerosis] of the mysteries of the kingdom of Christ through that Spirit; the translation out of darkness into the marvelous light of Christ; the participation of the treasures of wisdom and knowledge which are hidden in Christ: all these things, at which philosophy shudders, and which to its devotees have always been folly, mere empty words and nothing more, are what beget and constitute our theology.

IV. Do not, therefore, expect, reader, that I should here review a catalogue of books, or prescribe a method of reading them; others have long since done both, with much judgment, and with no small reputation for wide reading [Greek: polyanagnosia]; especially Gisbert Voetius in his Theological Library, a man of the greatest learning [Greek: polymathestatos], never to be praised enough for his rare erudition, singular piety, and love of the truth.

V. But what I am attempting tends in another direction. I do not in any way sustain the office of a professor of theology; nor does the care of directing others in the plan or method of their studies lie upon me. I hate the din and sophistry of the schools. By God's gracious disposal I live at home and to myself. My business is this, to see whether in any way I may serve the interests of the household of faith. There are therefore but a few things I have to add concerning the study of theology, things which may help forward the minds of pious students in the search after that heavenly truth whose nature we have expounded. But that we may arrive where we are heading, certain things must be premised.

VI. First, then, let the reader observe that I do not here understand those words or names, "theology" and "theologians," otherwise than as I have shown they ought to be taken, according to the principles laid down and confirmed above. In this discussion of ours they do not denote a certain science, and a certain class of men furnished with it. All believing Christians are theologians; since, namely, the saving light of the gospel has been bestowed upon them all. The difference that is found among them is one of degrees. For the distribution of spiritual gifts among believers by the Holy Spirit is plainly arbitrary: "He works in all as he wills [Greek: kathos bouletai]" (1 Cor. 12:11). On this hangs every difference of theologians among themselves. The same saving light is in them all. Into it they are daily "transformed from glory to glory by the Lord the Spirit." But the gifts of grace [Greek: charismata], variously communicated to them by the same Spirit, make a vast difference among these theologians. Theology, therefore, so far as it denotes a saving intellectual habit, or a spiritual light renewing the mind and transforming it into the image of Christ, who is light, belongs to all believers and to believers only. But those things which pertain to the increase of that light, to the confirmation of the habit, and thus to the spiritual elevation of the understanding, to the clear perception, in the due manner, of the saving object, and to the spiritually effectual narration or declaration of the truth once known, in which things all the difference among theologians consists, these are what we chiefly endeavor to promote in the study of theology.

VII. Those free gifts of the Spirit, which, superadded to the saving light, variously distinguish evangelical theologians among themselves, produce a twofold effect in the minds of the men furnished with them. For, first, [ p. 471 ] since all adult believers, by virtue of the saving light infused into them in their conversion to God (for each one of them is made light in the Lord), possess a radical and fundamental knowledge of the whole counsel of God concerning the demonstration of his glory in their obedience and salvation, seeing they are capable of all spiritual things and can perceive them savingly, those gifts render that knowledge more certain, clearer, fuller, and more distinct, in the apprehension of the truth as it is revealed by God and set forth in the holy Scriptures.

VIII. On them depends, I say, a distinct, express, fixed, and solid knowledge of the spiritual things set forth in the Scriptures. Of the same gifts also there is this further effect, a sound and solid judgment, which is able to discern things true from things false, and to dispose the articles of truth in that order which the natural relation they bear to one another requires.

IX. Secondly, these gifts alone render men apt to teach [Greek: didaktikoi]; for by their virtue we are made sufficient [Greek: hikanoi] to instruct others in that good knowledge of Christ with which we ourselves have been imbued. Now he in whom these things are present, that is, the saving light elevating the mind and disposing it to a saving perception of evangelical truth, and those gifts of the Holy Spirit which stir up, increase, and inflame that light, and distinctly implant in the mind the truth set forth in the holy Scriptures, and moreover render him fit to impart to others that knowledge of God in Christ which he himself has graciously attained, he is the theologian of whom we speak. The study of theology, therefore, is nothing else than the endeavor, ordered according to the rule of the divine word, to promote in the mind of the theologian the saving light and the spiritual gifts in which this heavenly wisdom consists. This the Scripture itself teaches us everywhere: 1 Cor. 14:12; 2 Cor. 3:5, 6, 18; 4:6; Eph. 1:17-19; 3:7, 16-19; 4:15, 16; Col. 1:26, 27; 2:2, 3, 7; 1 Tim. 4:12-16; 2 Tim. 3:14-17; 1 Pet. 4:10, 11; 2 Pet. 3:18.

X. Thirdly, we propose that the use of theology is chiefly to be considered in the ministry of the gospel. The knowledge of it, indeed, by itself alone constitutes no man a minister of the gospel, properly [Greek: kyrios] so called. There is further required in any theologian, for the right undertaking of that office, a solemn calling through election and ecclesiastical separation. But that a man should be apt and fit to discharge that office comes from theology. Although, therefore, its nature is such that everyone who has any care either for the glory of God or for his own salvation ought to give himself to it, yet the duty of promoting and cultivating it lies in a peculiar manner upon those who are engaged in the work of instructing others in the knowledge of the divine will. For if those who set their minds upon the sacred office of the ministry would seriously weigh with themselves what light, and what spiritual gifts, it has pleased the Lord Jesus in these last days graciously to bestow upon the Christian people, that is, upon very many of the ordinary body of believers, they would without doubt conclude that it concerns them to take diligent care, with all their strength and the utmost effort of their soul, for the increase of those "spiritual gifts" which they hold as their peculiar stock. That they have learned arts and sciences, [ p. 472 ] that they are furnished with the knowledge of philosophy and languages, that they can wear out whole days in wrangling and disputing, will scarcely suffice to bring their ministry out of contempt, or even to have it held in any esteem at all. For these are not the things about which, in their immediate dealings with the churches, their business lies. If a man who hired out his labor to another for sowing and harvesting should boast that he was skilled in the science of navigation or of war, he would not escape being a laughingstock and an object of scorn to his employer. Nor will it ever go better with him who, puffed up with confidence in secular learning and scholastic trifles, empty of spiritual gifts [Greek: charismata], a stranger to the mystery of the gospel, should thrust himself into the office of teaching the Christian people. There go out daily into the vineyard of the Lord (a thing to be lamented) "new orators, foolish, mere striplings," who, though they have scarcely saluted good letters from the threshold, yet, relying on a vain conceit of knowledge built on cold and worthless reasonings, believe themselves able to stun rustic and unlettered folk with the din of foot-and-a-half-long words, and easily to hold them to their duty. But when it comes to the actual discharge of the work of the ministry, being destitute of all true theology, that is, of saving light and spiritual gifts, they prove so miserably cold and conduct themselves so ineptly that to all those in whose hearts are the ways of the Lord, and who know what that eternal life is which consists in the knowledge of God and of Christ, they are at once worthless and despised. Hence arise angers, quarrels, dissensions, and schisms; and both our duty and the times demand that these be met by the ministers of the gospel not with force and arms, but with the study of true theology and the practice of piety.
XI. These things being premised according to the plan of our undertaking, it remains that we briefly review those things which seem necessary for making useful progress in the study of theology.

XII. First, I hung for some time in doubt of mind whether it was necessary that I should make mention of the common arts and sciences with which all the learned agree that students ought to come furnished to the study of theology, or not. For why should I bring in here things about which no one is in doubt, things which are known to everyone? Yet reasons are not lacking why I should do it, however often it has been done; I will do it, therefore. These arts are grammar, to which I refer skill in the original languages, logic, and rhetoric. For although without the knowledge of these, that is, as they are technically [Greek: technikos] taught, a man may become wise in the mystery of the gospel, yet, since that manner of delivering and expounding the heavenly doctrine which is observed in the Scriptures has many things in common with the other branches of knowledge which these arts serve, we judge them at least useful for promoting that wisdom, and for making progress in it in that peculiar way which we have proposed. The other parts and ornaments of secular erudition we do not pass over in silence as if we were banishing them altogether from theological study into other fields. Let each man give his attention to them according to his own bent and ability, especially to history of every kind. Now writing and vocal speech are the modes and means of all communication of rational conceptions. But whatever is said, whatever [ p. 473 ] is written, by which any conceptions of a rational mind are uttered, rests upon the fundamental rules and principles of those common arts. Every utterance that falls away from them either falls away from reason itself, or pours darkness and mist over reason. Indeed, those very arts are nothing else than certain delineations of general rules which the nature of things teaches us to employ in the communication of rational conceptions through words. The things themselves, therefore, namely the conceptions of the rational mind uttered in words, by which men set forth their thoughts, debate doubtful matters, and persuade one another of what seems true, are prior to these arts. And since no mortal can comprehend all the ways and modes which men use in uttering the sense of their minds, any more than he can conceive all the rational conceptions of all men, whose diversity begets the differences of utterance, those observations of very many wise men through so many ages which are contained in these arts cannot but be exceedingly useful. Further, skill in those common sciences, or the exercise of the mind in them, assists the mind in forming its conceptions, strengthens all its faculties, confirms the judgment of the understanding, frees the very things about which mind and reason are occupied from ambiguity and deceptions, and sets and presents their natures bare, clear, and distinct before contemplation.

XIII. It were indeed to be wished that those to whom falls the province of instructing studious youth would employ greater care, diligence, and prudence than is commonly used in the schools and academies, so that students might attain solid learning in these arts and a useful proficiency. For it is most certain that a superficial and undigested knowledge of them, though it may suffice for the shadowy wrestling-ground of the schools, is very often the greatest prejudice to good minds, and of no advantage or use. In truth, those among students who attain any useful proficiency in these common arts "appear as scattered swimmers in the vast deep" (Virgil, Aeneid 1.118). Sloth forestalls all praiseworthy endeavors in not a few. They refuse to endure the difficulties and pains of study. For some obstinately shun all labor of mind and wit, who nevertheless, led by I know not what folly, do not blush to enroll their names among the candidates for learning. But at last they themselves, and those whose concern it is that they should pursue better things, taught by experience all too late, come to understand how much harm it has done to have occupied places destined for study with such a spirit. Then, very many, when they ought to be giving diligent labor to the learning of these arts, having no fixed end of their labors set before them, wander hither and thither in their studies through miscellaneous reading, and so in the end attain no useful progress in any kind of learning. Others, destitute of the benefit of skilled instructors, wasting their time in feeble attempts, are always beginning something, and never finish anything.

XIV. For when the things that are truly useful appear hedged about with excessive difficulty and darkness, growing weary of the work they have begun, they are overcome and cast down their minds to those trivial and profitless things which the vast abundance of futile books supplies. Now that hindrance which renders the serious and praiseworthy endeavors of very many vain and fruitless is ignorance of the nature and true end of all arts and sciences. Their first inventors had this purpose before them, to contend against the curse which lies upon the human race because of the first sin, and to free themselves, one and all, from that penalty of ignorance of the truth and of darkness by which we are naturally oppressed. For when the human soul perceives itself weighed down by blindness and ignorance of all things, it strives in every way to extricate itself from their snares. But we all know that the whole encyclopedia of the sciences is by no means sufficient to attain this end. And those who are ignorant of the saving light of the gospel can have no other general end set before them in the study of the arts and sciences. By their help they would emerge from their natural darkness. Here, therefore, the minds of very many, miserably subject to the power and dominion of inbred vanity, fluctuate hither and thither in the search after truth. For all Christians confess that no one can attain the end at which they aim except through the saving grace of Christ. Until, therefore, all sciences and arts are restored, as we wished above, to the rule and the use of infallible truth, it cannot be but that the study of them will turn out very unhappily for most men.

XV. Secondly, the plan of studies being settled, let students attentively weigh what that mark or scope is at which they aim with mind and eye. For since the end of the whole work is the rule in the inquiry after and use of the means, it must always be carefully attended to, unless we wish to wander perpetually from the way. But what and of what sort that end is, or ought to be, is sufficiently clear from what has been said before. There are those who think it enough to have turned over theological systems, in which the doctrine of the gospel is reduced to certain heads of matters, with hand by night and by day, until, being molded into their pattern [Greek: eis typon], they seem to themselves to conceive in their mind a kind of digest of theology. To read, to know, and to hold in memory what the ancient fathers, what the schoolmen, what the more recent theologians have thought or written in matters of faith and in the business of religion, this is what completes the purpose of others. Very many strive with all their might to attain a philosophical proficiency in some sort of theological science. But all these ends we have already banished far from the ends of theology.

XVI. That we may become "wise and understanding" in the mystery of the gospel, in the knowledge of God in Christ, in the secrets of the divine counsel and covenant, in the hidden things of spiritual worship and the obedience of faith, this is the something toward which we tend in this study. Let this remain fixed and settled, the mark at which the eye of the mind aims in the choice and use of means. If "wisdom comes from above, from the Father of lights," if understanding in the mystery is something with which we must be endowed by the Holy Spirit, then with respect to that theology [ p. 475 ] into which we wish to be initiated it would without doubt be the greatest folly to use any means for obtaining the knowledge of it apart from those alone which, being prescribed by God himself, are moreover crowned with the communication of the Holy Spirit. He who sets this scope before himself with due reverence toward God, with denial of himself and of his natural powers, leaning by faith upon the help and benefit of the Spirit of Christ, and who uses no means except in order to that end, and therefore in that manner in which it is fitting that such means be used for obtaining such an end, that is, piously and religiously, his studies, by God's favor, will arrive at the issue that was proposed. But while students waver hither and thither in the use of most uncertain means, pursuing at random I know not what ends set before themselves, although they "cast the gold into the fire," yet for the most part there "comes out a calf" (Exod. 32:24). Let those, therefore, who would not lose their labor and their oil in the study of theology, hold this as a fixed purpose, that the something toward which they tend, and which they go to seek with all their might, is spiritual and saving wisdom in the mystery of the gospel.

XVII. Thirdly, we have proved above that all supernatural truth revealed by God is contained in didactic propositions in the holy Scripture. In the spiritual and saving knowledge of that truth our wisdom consists. All will therefore readily grant that diligent reading of the Scriptures, and assiduous meditation in them, is absolutely necessary for all candidates of theology; but in truth, so far as appears from the event, however much they may grant it in words, those who apply themselves to that matter with due thought and care will be found exceedingly few. And the neglect of this brings upon very many in this study not merely loss but destruction. Nor can it fall out otherwise, where men profess to aim at any end whatever while the means are openly neglected. They spend little labor in a desultory and sluggish reading of the Scriptures, joined to a pernicious slackness of mind; less in the hearing of the word and in solemn preaching; in meditation, none; yet none of them doubt that they will become most consummate theologians: a most foolish race of men, and one that deserves to be despised. Searching after the noble and heavenly wisdom, to which nothing in human life is comparable or equal, they count as worthless, or at least put in the last place, that means, almost the only one, which is destined to that end. They are wise, if indeed this is wisdom, to will what cannot come to pass. But perhaps they are wholly taken up in reading the writings of the older and the more recent theologians, who of set purpose expound the sense of the Scriptures? So be it; and we praise that diligence. But this is not the study of the Scriptures. It is plainly one thing, with ardent vows poured out through faith in Christ, and with the help of the Holy Spirit implored, to read the Scriptures themselves and to meditate in them, so that we may drink in the Spirit who lives in them; it is another thing to turn over the writings of other men, however learned and however fragrant with heavenly truth. Let them hear Acosta the Jesuit. "He," says he, "who reads the Scriptures with purity of soul will profit more than if he should attempt to unravel the mysteries with many commentaries." And he adds something almost fanatical, as it will seem to some, [ p. 476 ] namely, "that he has seen many unlearned men, who scarcely understood Latin, who have drawn so much light from the holy Scriptures that he himself was astonished, while professors, with all the assiduity of their studies, could teach nothing like it." It is well that he was a Jesuit, and a learned man. Hear also Erasmus, in his Preface to the New Testament: "These things, I say, and things of this kind" (that is, faith, charity, holiness, contempt of the world according to the word of God), "if anyone, breathed upon by the Spirit of Christ, should preach, inculcate, exhort to, invite to, and animate toward, he at last is truly a theologian, even if he were a ditcher or a weaver; and if anyone exhibits these things in his very manner of life, he in the end is a great doctor."

XVIII. But since there are several things which frighten the minds of very many away from the study of the Scriptures themselves, and so bring upon them most imminent ruin, it will not be amiss to review them briefly in this place.

XIX. First, then, the hidden unbelief of the human mind persuades very many that the holy Scriptures, because they are obscure and entangled with difficulties, or because they contain mysteries with which human ingenuity would gladly have nothing in common, can scarcely be rightly and clearly understood. And they do not think it their duty to inquire more diligently into that which they feel themselves unequal to understanding. Accordingly, with the exception of certain histories, and of those things which that revelation of the mind of God has in common with other sciences, they scarcely advance, in the study of it, beyond the sound of the words and the bark of the letter. The profound mysteries, and the rays of heavenly wisdom which flash in it, terrify sluggish and earthly souls. They consult also, perhaps, certain texts out of which they think commonplaces can be put together, and which serve to support the opinions of this or that sect. But truly, as to attaining spiritual wisdom, they are far enough removed from any pure, bare, immediate searching out of the mind and sense of the Holy Spirit in the Scriptures.

XX. Nay, for I know not what causes, a great part of students, if they do not despise the reading of the Scriptures, at least neglect it. Some are repelled by the style, some by the matters themselves. Well known is what Jerome relates as having happened to himself, in his Epistle to Eustochium on the keeping of virginity. "After frequent night watches," he says, "after tears which the remembrance of my past sins drew from my inmost bowels, Plautus would be taken into my hands; if at any time, returning to myself, I had begun to read the prophets, the uncultivated speech filled me with horror; and because with blind eyes I did not see the light, I thought the fault was not in my eyes, but in the sun." Likewise it is in everyone's mouth how the impious Politian professed that he shrank from the Scriptures because of the barbarism, as he calls it, of their style. Still more impious are the things which Caspar Peucer relates of him, in his Epistle to Christopher Carlowitz. When Politian was asked, he says, what he judged of the Psalms of David, he replied, "I see that those ancient poems are full of wisdom. There are there precepts of all the virtues, there are admonitions concerning providence, threatenings concerning the punishments of the wicked, and promises concerning the rewards [ p. 477 ] and protection of the just. There are also inserted the old histories of that nation, both that the memory of antiquity might be handed down to posterity, and that at the same time examples of punishments and of protection might be in view. This wisdom I value highly, as useful for life and morals, and I consider the figures to be elegant in their own language; and of old, harmonies were added to them, fitted to stir the motions of the soul. But in Pindar the same things are more sweetly narrated, and more splendidly illustrated with examples." So far Politian. Thus the impious manikin, as if he had been appointed arbiter between the Holy Spirit and a godless [Greek: atheos] heathen, decks David with garlands, so that he might fall a more illustrious victim to the praise of Pindar. Would indeed that there were none who hoped they could draw the doctrine of virtue better, if not from Pindar, at least from Plato, Plutarch, Seneca, Epictetus, Arrian, Plotinus, and the like, than from the books of the Old and New Testaments; an atheism which we daily see God avenging with horrible blindness.

XXI. Secondly, they scarcely believe that those notices of truth with which they are only too eager to be imbued, and which the most learned writings of learned men supply in abundance, are contained in the Scriptures. The most profound notions, that is, the subtle arguments, the acute distinctions, by which men are both made learned and delighted, they can learn from other books; the contemplation of this book is humble, dry, and barren. Such, forsooth, is the fatuity of trifling manikins. For the holy Scripture is not only the rule and standard of all that spiritual truth of which we are treating, so far that nothing in that kind is true, or ought to be admitted in the place of truth, unless it is contained in it; but it is also its sole fountain and origin, so that no notion or conception of the mind about the things of religion has any sublimity, nor any arguments subtlety, unless they are drawn out of it; as we have shown elsewhere.

XXII. Thirdly, since Christ has promised his Spirit to lead all believers into the truth through the word of God, those who, according to that promise, do not look to him alone, nor flee to his help, are in very deed destitute of that aid and support without which no one has ever been engaged in the study of the Scriptures rightly, cheerfully, and with fruit. Other studies, indeed, can be both undertaken and accomplished by one's own effort. But in the sacred letters there is something hidden, for the saving perception of which the human mind is unequal, whatever external helps are brought in to its assistance. Therefore the Holy Spirit is promised as a helper in the work. He is received only by faith. It ought therefore to be a wonder to no one if to those who do not know the Spirit, nor can receive him, since they do not have faith, the free and honest searching of those Scriptures is a weariness and almost a hatred. Those who do not know how to trust the promised Spirit, to implore his help and aid with assiduous prayers, and to give themselves over to the leading of that anointing which teaches all things, will in vain bend the edge of their mind and the sharpness of their wit upon this study.

XXIII. Fourthly, there clings deep in the souls of many an aversion to cultivating any immediate communion with God. This is the fruit of indwelling sin. Where that reigns, which is the condition [ p. 478 ] of the unregenerate, a universal enmity against God prevails. It retains some little of its strength even in the regenerate. Now in the writings of all men the native vanity of the human mind has something in which to delight itself: although theological matters are handled, the mind nevertheless dwells on things which are common to other sciences as well. In the holy Scripture the soul has to do with God himself. And since the mind subject to its native vanity cannot bear this, it flees the study of the word itself with a certain secret aversion.

XXIV. Until these things, and others of the same kind, which not only greatly hinder those entering upon the study of theology, but also drive them into strange frustrations, are removed, it ought not to seem wonderful if, after much time spent in the study of theology, there come forth more philosophers, or men who know only how to envy the praise of others, than theologians, that is, men wise in the mystery of Christ.
XXV. Let us return to the work we have undertaken, so that the things we further require in that study of the Scriptures which we are commending may be added.

First, then, he who intends to give his labor to the sacred letters ought to keep firmly fixed in his soul that the most holy God is in a peculiar manner near to him as he reads and meditates, so that, being moved with due reverence for the divine majesty and authority, as becomes a most abject sinner, he may carry himself in that study with the utmost submission of soul. For in the holy Scriptures God himself addresses the reader no less immediately than if he were using a voice sounding from heaven. He himself is despised wherever fear and godly caution [Greek: eulabeia] do not occupy the hearts of readers. With those alone is he pleased to dwell who tremble at his word. A light and desultory reading of the Scriptures is the plague of souls, and opens a window to atheism. He who approaches the reading of the Scriptures ought to keep that most weighty apostolic caution in Hebrews 12:28, 29 fixed in his memory as with a beam-nail, as they say: "Let us have grace, whereby we may serve God acceptably, with reverence and godly fear; for our God is a consuming fire." "The fear of the Lord is the beginning of this wisdom"; and unless it is present in the study of the word, it will be present nowhere.

XXVI. Secondly, let the student seriously weigh what his own concern is in every truth in whose drawing forth from the mystery of the divine word he is engaged, and how he may be advanced by it in the knowledge and worship of God. Let this always be the primary and chief end of all studies and meditations in the holy Scriptures. We must always come to the study of them so that we may learn from them our duty; that in them we may cultivate most holy communion with God; that we may experience, in the inmost senses of our souls, the power and efficacy of the Spirit mightily putting forth his grace and light in them; and that we may know how our consciences stand toward the authority of God himself and of Christ our Lord which is eminent in them. He will wait in vain for any progress, saving to himself or useful to the church, who, despising this end of sacred meditation, has nothing else in mind than to furnish his understanding with a bare knowledge of the truth. The word is the food of the soul. Now there are two things which are nourished and cherished by it: namely, the saving light, [ p. 479 ] and the spiritual gifts superadded to it. The first is graciously granted to us that it may be saving to ourselves; the others, that we ourselves may be bearers of salvation to others. But he who, in the study of the sacred letters, does not aim, in the first place, at the increase of the former, will either make no harvest of the latter, or will himself receive no fruit from their harvest.

XXVII. Thirdly, a practical acknowledgment of the plainly divine authority of the holy Scripture, and a religious subjection of conscience to it in all things, ought always to be present with its reader. For the word of God, and it alone, immediately discharges its office among us in the name of God himself. It therefore holds supreme dominion over us, to which we are bound to bring our minds and understandings into captivity. For we ought so to set our consciences before it, as men about to receive from its sentence the judgment concerning ourselves and all those things which look toward eternity.

XXVIII. Fourthly, the study of the Scriptures in those languages in which they were written by the Holy Spirit himself will be a great help in the investigation of the truth. For besides the fact that from those fountains alone can we draw the genuine force and signification of the words and phrases of the text, there is also a peculiar emphasis in those languages, especially in the Hebrew, an energy [Greek: energeia] also, by which it effectually insinuates the force and sense of the words into the mind, and which can be communicated to no other language.

XXIX. Fifthly, let assiduous and ardent prayers lead, close, and accompany all these things, and whatever others there may be of that kind. This is the greatest means, appointed by God, of obtaining both him who is the author of all true wisdom, and that very wisdom which we are seeking. We have taught above that the author of this wisdom is the Holy Spirit. He it is who alone searches out and lays open those deep things of God, which he learns and teaches. He it is through whom the Son of God gives us understanding, that we may know him who is true, and who shows us the things that are Christ's, and so leads us into all truth. The Lord Jesus teaches us that he is received by faith, but obtained by prayers. God bestows him upon us as the Spirit of supplications; so that, furnished with his grace and aided by his power, we may ask for that very Spirit of direction and consolation. This our wisdom, too, is the gift of God. "If any of you," says James, "lacks wisdom, let him ask of God, who gives" (James 1:5). Hence, therefore, all hope of profiting in theological study depends upon God. Rightly says Justin to Trypho, "But do you, before all things, pray that the gates of light may be opened to you; for these things are not seen nor understood by all, but only by him to whom God and his Christ have granted to understand" [Greek: Euchou de soi pro panton photos anoichthenai pylas]. And that saying of Plato, in the Epinomis appended to the books of the Laws, when he was about to expound the nature of God and theology, is almost divinely spoken; for the Athenian Stranger, being bidden by Clinias to expound, trusting in the kindness of God and praying to him, the things that were agreeable to his nature, adds, "These things shall be, [ p. 480 ] if God himself guides us on the way; only join your prayers with mine" [Greek: Estai tauta, an autos ho Theos hemin hyphegetai; xyneuchou monon].

XXX. Why do we stand amazed that so many elegant wits grow weary and faint in the study and search after heavenly truth? They do not know how to pray. They have never received the Spirit of supplication; nay, alas, they more often spurn him; nay, wittily and jestingly, but wickedly, but blasphemously, they harass him; nor are they anywhere more delightful to themselves and to others than when they insolently mock him and lash him with petulant pleasantries. They are earthly, and savor nothing but earthly things; and while they look down on all others and carry themselves most magnificently, as if they alone were wise, in truth they sink below the lowest of the Christian common people in the knowledge of the gospel.

XXXI. Further, the believing soul, by the assiduous pouring out of those prayers which that Spirit suggests, whom God graciously bestows on us for the relieving of our infirmities, obtains communion with God himself in the greatest mysteries of the gospel, and thereby, through that exercise, is daily increased in the knowledge of them. For while the soul experiences in itself by faith the power and efficacy of the saving doctrine, so far as it has advanced, being admitted little by little into all the secrets of the covenant of God entered into in Christ, it "grows up into him who is the head, the fullness of him who fills all in all."

XXXII. To these studies let there be added a constant and religious intimacy with those who holily cultivate the practice of piety according to the rule of evangelical theology. The antiquity of the Gentiles everywhere relates that the greatest philosophers traversed almost the whole world in order to enjoy the counsel and companionship of those who had been initiated into the mysteries of that wisdom which they were investigating. We, for the most part, despise such helps of true wisdom of this kind as may be had at home. Some hold that no account at all is to be taken of those in whom the power, energy [Greek: energeia], and life of that wisdom which they seek effectually put themselves forth. Nay, and what madness this is, from none do they dissent with more hostile hatred or envy than from those who endeavor to express that wisdom by true piety, by faith unfeigned, by sincere evangelical obedience, by the assiduous and spiritual worship of God, and by holiness of life. If anyone should contend with all his might that that article concerning the communion of saints, which is contained in what they call the Apostles' Creed, should be expunged from the things to be believed, the crowd of students would at once fly in his face; while in the meantime they themselves either are utterly ignorant of what that communion of saints is, or wickedly despise it.

XXXIII. Now that intimacy entered into with the saints and the faithful will beget the assiduous exercise of those spiritual gifts in which this wisdom consists; and by that exercise, from the very nature of the thing and by virtue of the institution of Christ, the gifts themselves are increased.
END OF THE WORK.
